topics.title: Elements of Information Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elements of Information Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information Security (InfoSec)** is the discipline of protecting information by mitigating risks. It involves the processes and methodologies designed and implemented to prevent unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, recording, or destruction of data, whether in storage, processing, or transit.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCCT604)**, *Elements of Information Security* refers to the foundational pillars (also called the **security services**) upon which any secure system, network, or IoT device is built. The **three core elements** form the **CIA Triad**: **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability**. Extended elements include **Authentication, Authorization, Accountability (Auditing), and Non-Repudiation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Vault House"

Imagine your most valuable possessions (jewelry, documents, family photos) are stored inside a high-security house. Information Security works the same way:

| Real-World House | Digital Equivalent | Security Element |
|---|---|---|
| **Locked doors and tinted windows** — only family can see inside | Encrypted data — only authorized users can read | **Confidentiality** |
| **Tamper-evident seals on the safe** — you can detect if someone opened it | Hash values and digital signatures — any change is detected | **Integrity** |
| **Backup generator and fire-resistant walls** — your valuables survive any crisis | Redundant servers and DDoS protection — data is always accessible | **Availability** |
| **Doorbell camera and ID card** — confirms who is at the door | Passwords, biometrics, MFA — confirms who the user is | **Authentication** |
| **Keys to specific rooms** — even family can only access certain rooms | Access Control Lists (ACL), Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) | **Authorization** |
| **CCTV footage** — every visit is recorded | Audit logs — every action is traceable | **Accountability** |
| **Signed receipt for parcel delivery** — courier cannot deny delivery | Digital signatures — sender cannot deny sending | **Non-Repudiation** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Every question in the Ethical Hacking paper ultimately tests whether you can identify *which* element of the CIA Triad is being violated by a given attack. For example, a **man-in-the-middle attack** violates **Confidentiality + Integrity**, while a **DDoS attack** violates **Availability**.

## 1.3 The Seven Core Elements at a Glance

1. **Confidentiality** — Privacy of data
2. **Integrity** — Trustworthiness and accuracy of data
3. **Availability** — Accessibility of data when needed
4. **Authentication** — Verification of identity
5. **Authorization** — Granting of appropriate access rights
6. **Accountability (Auditing)** — Tracking user actions
7. **Non-Repudiation** — Inability to deny an action

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The CIA Triad — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Confidentiality
- **Goal:** Ensure that sensitive information is *not* disclosed to unauthorized individuals, entities, or processes.
- **How it works:** Information is protected through access controls, encryption, and steganography.
- **Threats it counters:** Eavesdropping, sniffing, social engineering, shoulder surfing, dumpster diving.
- **Why it matters:** Breaches in confidentiality lead to privacy violations, identity theft, and corporate espionage.

### 2.1.2 Integrity
- **Goal:** Maintain the accuracy, consistency, and trustworthiness of data across its entire lifecycle.
- **How it works:** Hashing algorithms (MD5, SHA-256), digital signatures, checksums, and version control.
- **Threats it counters:** Tampering, data corruption, man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks, viruses.
- **Why it matters:** A single bit flipped in a financial transaction can transfer millions to the wrong account.

### 2.1.3 Availability
- **Goal:** Ensure that information and systems are *accessible* to authorized users whenever needed.
- **How it works:** Redundancy, failover clusters, backups, RAID, DDoS mitigation, UPS systems.
- **Threats it counters:** Denial-of-Service (DoS), Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS), ransomware, hardware failure.
- **Why it matters:** A 2024 Gartner report estimates that **downtime costs enterprises an average of $5,600 per minute**.

## 2.2 The Extended Elements — AAA + Non-Repudiation

### 2.2.1 Authentication (The "Who are you?" check)
- **Definition:** The process of verifying the identity of a user, process, or device.
- **Factors of Authentication (MFA model):**
  - **Something you know** — Password, PIN
  - **Something you have** — Smart card, OTP token
  - **Something you are** — Fingerprint, retina scan, face ID
  - **Something you do** — Typing pattern, gait
  - **Somewhere you are** — Geolocation

### 2.2.2 Authorization (The "What can you do?" check)
- **Definition:** The process of granting or denying specific permissions to an authenticated user.
- **Models:** DAC (Discretionary), MAC (Mandatory), RBAC (Role-Based), ABAC (Attribute-Based).
- **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP):** Users should be granted *only* the minimum access necessary to perform their job.

### 2.2.3 Accountability (The "What did you do?" check)
- **Definition:** The ability to trace actions to a specific user or system.
- **Tools:** Audit logs, SIEM systems, log management platforms.
- **Key requirement:** Every user must have a *unique* identifier so actions can be tied back to a single entity.

### 2.2.4 Non-Repudiation (The "You cannot deny this" guarantee)
- **Definition:** Ensures that a party in a communication *cannot* deny the authenticity of their signature on a document or the sending of a message.
- **Mechanism:** Digital signatures (RSA, ECDSA), cryptographic receipts, timestamped logs.
- **Difference from Authentication:** Authentication proves identity at the *start*; non-repudiation provides *irrefutable proof* of the action after it occurs.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The table below is the single most important reference for Module 1. Memorize the *Threats countered* column — examiners love asking "Which element of InfoSec is violated by attack X?"

| **Element** | **Primary Goal** | **Core Technique** | **Threats Countered** | **Example Real-World Attack** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Privacy of data | AES, RSA, TLS/SSL | Eavesdropping, Sniffing | Wireshark packet capture on open Wi-Fi |
| **Integrity** | Data trustworthiness | SHA-256, HMAC, Digital Signatures | Tampering, MITM | SQL injection altering database records |
| **Availability** | Continuous access | RAID, Backups, CDNs | DoS, DDoS | Mirai botnet attack on Dyn DNS (2016) |
| **Authentication** | Identity verification | Passwords, Biometrics, MFA | Impersonation, Spoofing | Phishing email with fake login page |
| **Authorization** | Permission enforcement | ACL, RBAC, ABAC | Privilege Escalation | Zero-day in Linux sudo (CVE-2021-3156) |
| **Accountability** | Action traceability | Audit Logs, SIEM | Repudiation, Insider threats | Edward Snowden data exfiltration |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Irrefutable proof | Digital Signatures, PKI | Denial of action | Email forgery without DKIM/SPF |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **In Banking:** CIA Triad + Non-Repudiation form the foundation of UPI, SWIFT, and credit card transactions.
- **In Healthcare (IoT Medical Devices):** Integrity is critical — a tampered insulin pump dosage could kill a patient.
- **In IoT (relevant to PBCCT604):** Availability is the hardest element to guarantee in resource-constrained devices like smart sensors.
- **In Cloud Computing:** Multi-tenancy makes confidentiality a top concern, addressed by encryption-at-rest and encryption-in-transit.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

## 3.1 Mathematical Foundation: Hash Functions and Integrity

Integrity in modern systems is mathematically guaranteed through **cryptographic hash functions**. A hash function $H$ maps an input of arbitrary length to a fixed-length output.

$$H : \{0, 1\}^{*} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}^{n}$$

where $n$ is the output bit-length (e.g., $n = 256$ for SHA-256).

### 3.1.1 Three Core Properties of a Secure Hash Function

**Step 1: Pre-image Resistance**

Given a hash value $h$, it should be computationally infeasible to find any input $m$ such that:

$$H(m) = h$$

**Step 2: Second Pre-image Resistance**

Given an input $m_1$, it should be computationally infeasible to find a *different* input $m_2$ such that:

$$H(m_1) = H(m_2)$$

**Step 3: Collision Resistance**

It should be computationally infeasible to find *any* two distinct inputs $m_1 \neq m_2$ such that:

$$H(m_1) = H(m_2)$$

### 3.1.2 The Pigeonhole Principle Applied to Hashing

> [!NOTE]
> **Why collisions MUST exist:** This is a classic KTU long-answer derivation question. The proof uses the Pigeonhole Principle from combinatorics.

**Step 1 — Define the input space:**

The set of all possible messages $M$ is infinite, since $M = \{0, 1\}^{*}$.

**Step 2 — Define the output space:**

The set of all possible hash values is finite, with cardinality $\vert O \vert = 2^{n}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Pigeonhole Principle:**

We are mapping an infinite set $M$ into a finite set $O$ via the function $H$. By the **Pigeonhole Principle**, at least one output value must have multiple pre-images.

$$\exists \, h \in O \text{ such that } \vert H^{-1}(h) \vert \geq 2$$

**Step 4 — Formalize the statement:**

Since $\vert M \vert = \infty$ and $\vert O \vert = 2^{n} < \infty$, the mapping $H$ cannot be injective. Therefore, the number of possible collisions $C$ satisfies:

$$C \geq \vert M \vert - 2^{n} = \infty - 2^{n} = \infty$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

Collisions are mathematically guaranteed to exist. The security of a hash function does not come from avoiding collisions, but from making them *computationally infeasible* to find — which requires $n$ to be large enough (e.g., $n \geq 256$).

### 3.1.3 The Birthday Paradox — Expected Collisions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** The "Birthday Attack" uses the Birthday Paradox to find collisions faster than brute force.

**Step 1 — Probability that two hash values differ:**

For a hash with $n$-bit output, the probability that two randomly chosen inputs $m_1, m_2$ produce *different* hashes is:

$$P(\text{different}) = 1 - \frac{1}{2^{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Probability that $k$ random inputs are all distinct:**

$$P(\text{all distinct}) = \prod_{i=1}^{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{2^{n}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Approximate using the exponential identity:**

For small $i$ relative to $2^{n}$, we use $1 - x \approx e^{-x}$:

$$P(\text{all distinct}) \approx \exp\left(-\frac{k(k-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{n}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for 50% collision probability:**

Setting $P(\text{collision}) = 0.5$:

$$1 - \exp\left(-\frac{k(k-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{n}}\right) = 0.5$$

$$\exp\left(-\frac{k(k-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{n}}\right) = 0.5$$

$$\frac{k(k-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{n}} = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $k$:**

Since $k(k-1) \approx k^{2}$ for large $k$:

$$k \approx \sqrt{2 \cdot 2^{n} \cdot \ln(2)} \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$$

**Step 6 — Apply to SHA-256:**

For $n = 256$:

$$k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{128} \approx 2^{128.23}$$

This means a birthday attack on SHA-256 requires approximately $2^{128}$ operations — still computationally infeasible as of 2024.

## 3.2 The Information Security Risk Equation

A foundational KTU concept is the relationship between threats, vulnerabilities, and risk:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

where:
- $R$ = Risk (impact of a successful attack)
- $T$ = Threat (likelihood of an attack occurring)
- $V$ = Vulnerability (weakness in the system)
- $I$ = Impact (business consequence if exploited)

**Interpretation:** Risk is zero if *any* one of the three factors is zero — there is no point having a vulnerability if there is no threat, and no point having a threat if the system has no vulnerability.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Attack-to-Element Mapping

The following table is the **most-asked analytical tool** in KTU Module 1. For any attack, you must identify which CIA element(s) it violates and what countermeasure applies.

| **Attack Type** | **Primary Element Violated** | **Secondary Element Violated** | **Countermeasure** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Packet Sniffing (Wireshark) | Confidentiality | — | TLS/SSL encryption |
| SQL Injection | Integrity | Confidentiality | Parameterized queries, input validation |
| DDoS Attack | Availability | — | Rate limiting, CDN, scrubbing centers |
| Phishing | Authentication | Confidentiality | MFA, security awareness training |
| Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) | Confidentiality | Integrity | Certificate pinning, HTTPS |
| Ransomware | Availability | Integrity | Regular offline backups, EDR |
| Password Brute Force | Authentication | Confidentiality | Account lockout, bcrypt hashing |
| Privilege Escalation | Authorization | Accountability | PoLP, RBAC, patch management |
| Replay Attack | Authentication | Non-Repudiation | Nonces, timestamps, sequence numbers |
| DNS Spoofing | Integrity | Authentication | DNSSEC, certificate validation |
| Keylogger | Confidentiality | Integrity | Antivirus, virtual keyboards, MFA |
| Insider Data Exfiltration | Confidentiality | Accountability | DLP, behavioral analytics, audit logs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define the CIA Triad. Explain why it is considered the foundation of Information Security.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 mark) The **CIA Triad** is a foundational model in Information Security comprising three core elements: **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability**.
- (1 mark) **Confidentiality** ensures data is accessible only to authorized users; **Integrity** ensures data is accurate and unaltered; **Availability** ensures systems are accessible when needed.
- (1 mark) It is the foundation because *every* security control, policy, and countermeasure in any system — from banking to IoT — is designed to protect at least one of these three properties. Any attack can be classified by which element of the CIA Triad it violates.

---

### Question 2
**Q: Differentiate between Authentication and Authorization with a suitable example.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Authentication | Authorization |
|---|---|---|
| Question answered | **Who are you?** | **What can you do?** |
| Order | Occurs **first** | Occurs **after** authentication |
| Mechanism | Passwords, biometrics, OTP, MFA | ACL, RBAC, ABAC |
| Example | Entering username and password to log into KTU portal | After login, a student can only view grades but not modify them |

(1 mark for the definition of each, 1 mark for the contrast, 1 mark for the example.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A

**Q (a): Explain the seven elements of Information Security in detail with examples.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Information Security rests on seven key elements: the three core elements of the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) and four extended elements (Authentication, Authorization, Accountability, Non-Repudiation). Together, they form the complete security framework.

**1. Confidentiality (1 mark):**
Confidentiality ensures that information is accessible *only* to those authorized to view it. It prevents unauthorized disclosure of sensitive data. Example: Patient medical records in a hospital are encrypted so that only the treating doctor can read them. Countermeasures include AES encryption, access controls, and steganography.

**2. Integrity (1 mark):**
Integrity ensures that data remains accurate, consistent, and unaltered during storage or transit. Example: A bank's transaction database must not be modified by an unauthorized party, otherwise funds could be redirected. Countermeasures include SHA-256 hashing, HMAC, and digital signatures.

**3. Availability (1 mark):**
Availability ensures that information and services are accessible to authorized users *whenever needed*. Example: An e-commerce website like Amazon must remain online 24/7, especially during sales. Countermeasures include RAID, redundant servers, load balancers, and DDoS protection.

**4. Authentication (1 mark):**
Authentication verifies the identity of a user, device, or process. It answers the question "Who are you?" Example: Logging into a Google account using a password and an OTP sent to your phone (MFA). Techniques include passwords, biometrics, smart cards, and one-time passwords.

**5. Authorization (1 mark):**
Authorization determines what an *authenticated* user is permitted to do. It answers "What can you do?" Example: In a university, a faculty member can enter marks into the KTU portal, but a student can only view their own marks. Implemented using RBAC and the Principle of Least Privilege.

**6. Accountability (0.5 mark):**
Accountability ensures that every action performed on a system is traced back to a unique, identified user. Example: SIEM systems in enterprises log every login, file access, and configuration change for forensic analysis.

**7. Non-Repudiation (0.5 mark):**
Non-Repudiation ensures that a party *cannot deny* having performed an action. Example: A digitally signed email using S/MIME provides cryptographic proof that the sender actually sent the message, preventing them from later denying it.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Together, these seven elements provide a holistic framework. Authentication and Authorization protect *access*, Confidentiality and Integrity protect *data*, Availability protects *service continuity*, and Accountability and Non-Repudiation provide *forensic and legal* guarantees.

---

**Q (b): With a neat diagram, explain the components of the CIA Triad. Also discuss how each component is implemented in a real-world IoT system.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (2 marks):** [Student should draw the triangle/pyramid with the three vertices labeled Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, and the center labeled "Information"]

**Confidentiality in IoT (1.5 marks):**
In IoT, devices constantly transmit sensor data over wireless networks. Confidentiality is implemented using **TLS 1.3** for data-in-transit and **AES-256** for data-at-rest in the cloud. For example, a smart home camera encrypts the video stream before sending it to the cloud server so that hackers on the same Wi-Fi cannot intercept the footage.

**Integrity in IoT (1.5 marks):**
Integrity is critical in IoT because tampered data can cause physical harm (e.g., wrong dosage in a smart medical device). Implemented through **SHA-256 hashing** of sensor readings, **HMAC** for message authentication codes, and **digital signatures** for firmware updates. Example: A smart energy meter signs its readings with a private key, and the utility verifies the signature using the public key to ensure no tampering.

**Availability in IoT (1 mark):**
IoT devices are often resource-constrained and deployed in remote locations, making availability a challenge. Implemented via **redundant gateway nodes**, **edge computing** to reduce cloud dependency, **OTA (Over-The-Air) updates** to patch vulnerabilities, and **DDoS-resistant cloud backends**. Example: A smart agriculture sensor network uses mesh networking so that if one node fails, data can be routed through another.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The CIA Triad is universally applicable. In IoT — the focus of this course — all three elements are interdependent, and the challenge is to balance them given the limited CPU, memory, and battery resources of edge devices.

---

### Question B

**Q (a): Describe the AAA framework of Information Security. How does it differ from the CIA Triad?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **AAA framework** stands for **Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (or Accountability)**. It is a complementary model to the CIA Triad, focused on *controlling access* to systems rather than protecting the *data itself*.

**1. Authentication (1.5 marks):**
Authentication is the process of *verifying the identity* of a user or device. It is the first step in any access control system.
- **Single-factor:** Username + password
- **Multi-factor (MFA):** Password + OTP + fingerprint
- **Protocols:** RADIUS, TACACS+, Kerberos, OAuth 2.0
- Example: A bank customer logging in with password and a one-time password (OTP).

**2. Authorization (1.5 marks):**
Authorization determines *what an authenticated user is allowed to do*. It enforces the Principle of Least Privilege.
- **Models:** DAC, MAC, RBAC, ABAC
- **Mechanism:** Access Control Lists (ACLs) and policy files
- Example: In a hospital, a doctor has authorization to view and edit patient records, but a receptionist can only view (not edit) them.

**3. Accounting / Accountability (1.5 marks):**
Accounting (also called *Auditing*) tracks *what authenticated and authorized users actually do*. It is essential for forensic investigation and compliance.
- **Tools:** Audit logs, SIEM (Splunk, IBM QRadar), syslog servers
- **Standard:** Every action is timestamped and tied to a unique user ID
- Example: A failed login attempt is logged with the username, IP address, and time — allowing the security team to detect brute-force attacks.

**Difference from CIA Triad (1.5 marks):**

| Basis | CIA Triad | AAA Framework |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Protecting the *data* itself | Controlling *access* to the data |
| **Elements** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | Authentication, Authorization, Accounting |
| **Goal** | Data must be private, accurate, and accessible | Only the right people can access the right data |
| **Layer** | Data-centric | Access-control-centric |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
In practice, both frameworks are used together. The CIA Triad defines *what* must be protected, while the AAA framework defines *how* access to that protected data is controlled and monitored.

---

**Q (b): Explain Non-Repudiation in detail. How is it achieved using Digital Signatures? Mention its importance in e-commerce and IoT.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Non-Repudiation (1 mark):**
Non-Repudiation is a security property that ensures a party in a communication *cannot deny* the authenticity of their signature on a document, the sending of a message, or the performance of an action. It provides *irrefutable proof* of origin, receipt, and integrity.

**Two Types of Non-Repudiation (1 mark):**
- **Non-Repudiation of Origin (NRO):** The sender cannot deny having sent the message.
- **Non-Repudiation of Receipt (NRR):** The receiver cannot deny having received the message.

**Achieving Non-Repudiation via Digital Signatures (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Key Generation:** The sender generates a key pair — a *private key* $K_{priv}$ and a *public key* $K_{pub}$.

**Step 2 — Hashing:** The sender computes a hash of the message $M$:

$$h = H(M)$$

**Step 3 — Signing:** The sender encrypts the hash with their *private key*:

$$S = E_{K_{priv}}(h)$$

**Step 4 — Sending:** The signature $S$ is sent along with the message $M$.

**Step 5 — Verification by Receiver:** The receiver decrypts the signature using the sender's *public key*:

$$h' = D_{K_{pub}}(S)$$

**Step 6 — Comparison:** The receiver independently computes $H(M)$ and checks:

$$H(M) = h' \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Signature is valid}$$

**Why this works:** Only the sender knows $K_{priv}$, so only the sender could have produced $S$. The receiver (or any third party with $K_{pub}$) can independently verify it. The sender therefore *cannot later deny* the action.

**Importance in E-Commerce (1 mark):**
In online transactions, digital signatures provide legal proof equivalent to a handwritten signature. India's **Information Technology Act, 2000** legally recognizes digital signatures. Used in e-filing of income tax, e-tendering, and digital contracts.

**Importance in IoT (1 mark):**
In IoT, firmware updates must be digitally signed by the manufacturer. When a smart device receives an OTA update, it verifies the signature using the manufacturer's public key. This prevents attackers from pushing malicious firmware onto millions of devices — as was attempted in the **Mirai botnet** attack.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Non-Repudiation is essential for legal, financial, and operational trust in digital systems. It bridges the gap between physical-world signatures and the digital realm through asymmetric cryptography.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in PBCCT604 Module 1:**
> 1. **Conflating Authentication with Authorization** — This is the #1 mistake. Remember: *Authentication = "Who are you?"* and *Authorization = "What can you do?"* Examiners specifically test this distinction. (Lose up to 2 marks per question.)
> 2. **Forgetting Non-Repudiation** — Many students answer only CIA Triad and skip the 4 extended elements. If the question says "elements of InfoSec", you MUST list all **seven**, not just three.
> 3. **No real-world examples** — KTU 2024 scheme uses **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. Every theoretical answer must be supported by a *practical example* (e.g., banking, IoT, cloud) to score full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** — In a 7-mark question, a missing or poorly labeled diagram costs at least 1–2 marks. Always draw the CIA Triad as a triangle with the three vertices and label each.
> 5. **Spelling "Authentification"** — It is **Authentication**, not "Authentification". Spelling errors in technical terms are penalized in the KTU board valuation key.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Elements of Information Security**

- [x] **Information Security (InfoSec)** = the practice of protecting information from unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction.
- [x] **The CIA Triad** is the foundation: **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability.
- [x] **Confidentiality** is protected by **encryption** (AES, RSA, TLS).
- [x] **Integrity** is protected by **hashing** (SHA-256) and **digital signatures**.
- [x] **Availability** is protected by **redundancy, backups, and DDoS mitigation**.
- [x] **Authentication** answers *"Who are you?"* and uses passwords, biometrics, MFA.
- [x] **Authorization** answers *"What can you do?"* and uses ACL, RBAC, and the **Principle of Least Privilege**.
- [x] **Accountability** is enforced through **audit logs and SIEM systems**.
- [x] **Non-Repudiation** is provided by **digital signatures** using asymmetric cryptography (PKI).
- [x] **5 Factors of Authentication:** Something you **know**, **have**, **are**, **do**, or **somewhere** you are.
- [x] **Hash function** $H : \{0, 1\}^{*} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}^{n}$ provides three security properties: **pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, and collision resistance**.
- [x] **Collisions are mathematically guaranteed** to exist (Pigeonhole Principle), but should be *computationally infeasible* to find.
- [x] **Birthday Attack** reduces the complexity of finding collisions from $2^{n}$ to approximately $2^{n/2}$.
- [x] **Risk Equation:** $R = T \times V \times I$. If any one factor is zero, the risk is zero.
- [x] **Defense in Depth** uses 7 layers: Policy, Physical, Perimeter, Network, Host, Application, and Data.
- [x] **KTU Mantra:** For every attack, identify *which* element(s) of InfoSec it violates, and name *one specific countermeasure*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authenticity and Non-Repudiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Authenticity and Non-Repudiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Information Security and Ethical Hacking, **Authenticity** and **Non-Repudiation** are two of the foundational pillars of the **CIA Triad extension (often called the Parkerian Hexad)**. They govern the integrity of identity and the enforceability of digital actions within a networked system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authenticity** is the property by which the claimed identity of a principal (user, device, process, or message origin) is verifiably confirmed to be genuine, untampered, and trustworthy at the moment of communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Repudiation** is the assurance that a party involved in a digital transaction cannot subsequently deny having performed the action — neither the **origin** (sending) nor the **receipt** (acknowledgment) of the message can be refuted.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCCT604)** syllabus, these concepts bridge the gap between symmetric cryptography (speed) and asymmetric cryptography (identity binding), forming the cryptographic backbone of secure IoT ecosystems.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Wax Letter"

Imagine a king sending a royal decree across his kingdom:

| Real-World Element | Cryptographic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Royal Seal with unique insignia | **Digital Signature** (binds identity to message) |
| Wax that cracks if tampered | **Cryptographic Hash Function** (e.g., SHA-256) |
| Trusted scribe who verifies the seal | **Certificate Authority (CA)** |
| The sealed envelope itself | **Digital Certificate (X.509)** |
| Royal messenger demanding a signed receipt | **Acknowledgment / Receipt Token** |

- **Authenticity** is like the king's seal — it *proves who* sent the message.
- **Non-Repudiation** is like the king's seal combined with a notarized registry — it *proves who* sent it AND prevents the king from later saying "I never sent that."

In cybersecurity, **a Digital Signature provides BOTH authenticity AND non-repudiation simultaneously**, which is why it is considered the cornerstone of legally binding electronic communications (e.g., India's IT Act 2000, eIDAS in EU).

---

### 1.3 Why These Concepts Matter in Ethical Hacking

> [!NOTE]
> An ethical hacker must understand authenticity and non-repudiation because:
> 1. **Attackers forge identity** — spoofing MAC addresses, IP headers, emails, certificates.
> 2. **Attackers repudiate actions** — denying they launched an attack by manipulating logs.
> 3. **Defenders need forensic evidence** — digital signatures and audit trails provide court-admissible proof.

---

### 1.4 Distinction Between Authentication and Non-Repudiation (KTU Board Favorite)

| Property | Authentication | Non-Repudiation |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Verify identity *at the time of access* | Provide *undeniable proof* of an action later |
| **When Valid** | Real-time / session-based | Persistent (long-term legal weight) |
| **Achieved By** | Passwords, biometrics, tokens, certificates | Digital signatures, timestamps, trusted third parties |
| **Symmetric Crypto Use** | MACs (Message Authentication Codes) | **Cannot** be achieved alone with symmetric crypto |
| **Asymmetric Crypto Use** | Challenge-response with private key | **Digital signatures** are the gold standard |
| **Repudiable?** | Yes (sender could share secret key) | **No** (only sender has private key) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** A **MAC provides authentication but NOT non-repudiation**, because the symmetric key is shared. Either party could have generated the MAC. Only an **asymmetric digital signature** provides non-repudiation because the private key is mathematically bound to one party.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Strength Parameters (Bolded)

The strength of authenticity/non-repudiation mechanisms is measured in **bits of security**:
- **RSA-2048** ≈ **112 bits of security** (NIST recommended since 2013).
- **ECDSA-256** ≈ **128 bits of security**.
- **SHA-256** hash output: **256 bits**.
- **RSA-1024** (now deprecated) ≈ **80 bits** — broken by NSA-grade adversaries.
- **Biometric False Acceptance Rate (FAR)**: typically $\mathbf{0.001\%}$ to $\mathbf{0.1\%}$ for commercial fingerprint systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars Supporting Authenticity & Non-Repudiation

#### **Pillar A — Cryptographic Hash Functions**
A hash function $H(\cdot)$ maps arbitrary input $m$ to a fixed-length digest $h$:

$$h = H(m), \quad \text{where } h \in \{0,1\}^{n}$$

For cryptographic use, $H$ must satisfy:
1. **Pre-image resistance**: Given $h$, finding $m$ such that $H(m) = h$ is computationally infeasible.
2. **Second pre-image resistance**: Given $m_1$, finding $m_2 \neq m_1$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$ is infeasible.
3. **Collision resistance**: Finding any $m_1 \neq m_2$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$ is infeasible.

Standard algorithms: **SHA-256**, **SHA-3 (Keccak)**, **SHA-384**, **SHA-512**, and the legacy (broken) **MD5** and **SHA-1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** MD5 produces a **128-bit** digest and SHA-1 produces a **160-bit** digest — both are considered cryptographically broken and should NEVER be used in new deployments.

---

#### **Pillar B — Message Authentication Codes (MAC)**
A MAC uses a **symmetric key** $K$ shared between sender and receiver:

$$\text{MAC}_{K}(m) = f(K, m)$$

The receiver recomputes the MAC and compares. If they match, the message is authentic.

**Variants:**
- **HMAC** (Hash-based MAC) — combines a hash function with two derived keys:
$$\text{HMAC}_{K}(m) = H\Big((K_{out} \oplus \text{opad}) \;\Vert\; H((K_{in} \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert m)\Big)$$

- **CMAC** — MAC based on block ciphers (AES-CMAC).
- **GMAC** — MAC mode for GCM authenticated encryption.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation of MACs:** Since both parties share $K$, a MAC **does NOT provide non-repudiation**. Either party could have generated it. MACs provide authentication only.

---

#### **Pillar C — Digital Signatures (The Crown Jewel)**
A digital signature uses **asymmetric cryptography**. The sender signs with their **private key** $SK$, and anyone can verify with the **public key** $PK$:

$$\sigma = \text{Sign}_{SK}(H(m))$$

$$\text{Verify}_{PK}(\sigma, m) = \text{True} \;\text{or}\; \text{False}$$

**Standard Algorithms:**
- **RSA-PSS** (Probabilistic Signature Scheme based on RSA).
- **RSA-PKCS#1 v1.5** (legacy, still widely deployed).
- **ECDSA** (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm — preferred for IoT due to short signatures).
- **EdDSA** (Ed25519 — modern, fast, deterministic).
- **DSA** (Digital Signature Algorithm — older NIST standard).

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Hash output size | $n$ bits → $2^n$ possible digests | SHA-256 → $n = 256$ |
| Birthday paradox collision | $\approx 1.177 \times \sqrt{2^n}$ | SHA-256 → ~$2^{128}$ ops to find collision |
| RSA signature generation | $s = m^d \bmod n$ | $d$ = private exponent, $n = p \cdot q$ |
| RSA signature verification | $m' = s^e \bmod n$ | Check $m' \stackrel{?}{=} H(M)$ |
| ECDSA signature | $(r, s)$ where $r = (kG)_x \bmod n$ | $k$ = random nonce, $G$ = base point |
| ECDSA verification | $u_1 = H(m) s^{-1} \bmod n$, $u_2 = r s^{-1} \bmod n$ | Check $(u_1 G + u_2 PK)_x \stackrel{?}{=} r$ |
| HMAC block size | $\text{ipad} = 0x36$ repeated, $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ repeated | Standard for SHA family |
| RSA key strength | $\log_2(\sqrt{n})$ bits | $n$ ≈ 2048 bits → ~112-bit security |
| HMAC security | $\min(n, L)$ bits | $n$ = hash output, $L$ = key length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical:** In all equations above, never use the literal `|` character inside markdown tables. Use LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value or division.

---

### 2.3 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) — The Trust Framework

Authenticity and non-repudiation do not exist in a vacuum — they require a **trust hierarchy**.

**Components of PKI:**
1. **Certificate Authority (CA)** — trusted entity that issues certificates.
2. **Registration Authority (RA)** — verifies identity before CA issues cert.
3. **X.509 Digital Certificate** — binds a public key $PK$ to an identity $ID$.
4. **Certificate Revocation List (CRL)** — list of revoked certificates.
5. **OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol)** — real-time cert validity check.
6. **Trust Anchor / Root CA** — top of the chain, self-signed.

A simplified X.509 certificate structure:
$$\text{Cert} = \{\text{Version}, \text{SerialNo}, \text{Issuer}_{ID}, \text{Validity}, \text{Subject}_{ID}, PK_{Subject}, \text{Extensions}, \text{Sig}_{CA}\}$$

---

### 2.4 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) — Defensive Depth

| Factor | Description | Example | Attack Vector |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge (Factor 1)** | Something you know | Password, PIN | Phishing, brute force |
| **Possession (Factor 2)** | Something you have | Smart card, OTP token | Theft, SIM swap |
| **Inherence (Factor 3)** | Something you are | Fingerprint, iris, face | Spoofing, deepfakes |
| **Location (Factor 4)** | Somewhere you are | GPS, IP geolocation | VPN bypass |
| **Behavior (Factor 5)** | Something you do | Keystroke dynamics, gait | ML evasion |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus emphasizes MFA as a primary defense against credential theft — a top attack vector in IoT environments where devices share weak default passwords.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Banking (NEFT/RTGS/UPI)** | Digital signatures on transaction messages ensure non-repudiation for legal disputes. |
| **e-Governance (DSC in India)** | Class 2/Class 3 Digital Signature Certificates under IT Act 2000. |
| **IoT Firmware Updates** | Manufacturer signs firmware; device verifies before flashing — prevents malicious OTA injection. |
| **TLS/HTTPS** | Server certificate proves website authenticity to client. |
| **Code Signing** | Developers sign executables; OS verifies publisher authenticity. |
| **Blockchain** | Each transaction signed by sender's private key — inherent non-repudiation. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: How RSA Digital Signature Provides Non-Repudiation

**Step 1 — Key Generation by User A (Alice):**
Alice chooses two large primes $p$ and $q$, computes:

$$n = p \cdot q$$

$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$$

Alice picks public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$, then computes private exponent $d$:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Public Key}_{Alice} = (n, e), \quad \text{Private Key}_{Alice} = (n, d)$$

---

**Step 2 — Signing a Message $M$:**
Alice first computes the hash of the message:

$$h = H(M), \quad h \in \mathbb{Z}_n$$

She then computes the signature:

$$s = h^{d} \bmod n$$

She transmits $(M, s)$ to Bob.

---

**Step 3 — Verification by Bob:**
Bob independently computes the hash:

$$h' = H(M)$$

Bob uses Alice's public key $(n, e)$ to recover:

$$h'' = s^{e} \bmod n$$

By Euler's theorem, since $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$:

$$s^{e} \bmod n = (h^{d})^{e} \bmod n = h^{d \cdot e} \bmod n = h^{1 + k\phi(n)} \bmod n = h \cdot (h^{\phi(n)})^{k} \bmod n$$

Since $h^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ (Euler's theorem for $\gcd(h, n) = 1$):

$$h'' = h \bmod n$$

Therefore Bob checks:

$$h'' \stackrel{?}{=} h'$$

If equal → **signature is valid** → Alice is authenticated → **non-repudiation achieved** (only Alice knows $d$).

---

**Step 4 — Why This Guarantees Non-Repudiation:**
Suppose Alice later denies signing. A judge demands Alice produce $d$. Since only she had it, and $\sigma = h^d$ is publicly verifiable, the math is **undeniable**. This is the legal foundation of e-signatures worldwide.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Authenticity? | Non-Repudiation? | Confidentiality? | Speed | Key Management |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plain Hash $H(M)$ | ❌ No identity | ❌ | ❌ | Very fast | None |
| MAC (HMAC) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (shared key) | ❌ (unless encrypted too) | Fast | Symmetric |
| MAC + Encryption | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Fast | Symmetric |
| RSA Signature | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ (signature only) | Slow for large $M$ | Asymmetric |
| ECDSA Signature | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ | Fast | Asymmetric |
| Signed + Encrypted | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Moderate | Both |
| Digital Certificate | ✅ Yes (third-party verified) | ✅ Yes | ❌ | Moderate | PKI chain |

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: HMAC Construction

HMAC is defined as:

$$\text{HMAC}_{K}(M) = H\Big((K_{0} \oplus \text{opad}) \;\Vert\; H((K_{0} \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M)\Big)$$

Where:
- $K_{0} = K$ if $\text{len}(K) = B$ (block size), else $K_{0} = H(K)$ padded to block size.
- $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ repeated $B$ times (outer padding).
- $\text{ipad} = 0x36$ repeated $B$ times (inner padding).
- $\Vert$ = concatenation.
- $B = 64$ bytes for SHA-1/SHA-256, $B = 128$ bytes for SHA-384/SHA-512.

**Why double hashing?** The construction defends against length-extension attacks on raw $H(K \Vert M)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If a question asks "Why not just $H(K \Vert M)$?" — answer: vulnerable to length extension where attacker appends data and computes valid MAC without knowing $K$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Workflow — Full Signed Message Exchange

**Step-by-step trace of Alice sending signed message to Bob:**

1. Alice composes plaintext $M$.
2. Alice computes $h = \text{SHA-256}(M)$.
3. Alice signs: $s = \text{Sign}_{SK_A}(h) = h^{d_A} \bmod n_A$.
4. Alice transmits $(M, s, \text{Cert}_A)$ to Bob.
5. Bob validates $\text{Cert}_A$ via CA chain → obtains verified $PK_A$.
6. Bob computes $h' = \text{SHA-256}(M)$.
7. Bob verifies: $s^{e_A} \bmod n_A \stackrel{?}{=} h'$.
8. If match → Bob accepts → **authentication + integrity + non-repudiation** achieved.

**Attack surface at each step:**
- Step 3: If $SK_A$ leaked → attacker can impersonate.
- Step 5: If CA compromised → fake certificates.
- Step 7: If $H$ broken (collision) → attacker forges.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Digital Signature Workflow (Sign → Verify)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Alice Plaintext M] --> B[SHA-256 Hash]
    B --> C[Hash h]
    C --> D[Sign with Private Key SK]
    D --> E[Signature sigma]
    E --> F[Transmit M plus sigma]
    F --> G[Bob receives]
    G --> H[Bob computes SHA-256 of M]
    H --> I[Hash h prime]
    F --> J[Extract Public Key PK from CA cert]
    J --> K[Verify sigma using PK]
    I --> K
    K --> L{Match check}
    L -->|Yes| M[Authenticated and Non Repudiable]
    L -->|No| N[Reject Message]
```

---

### 4.2 PKI Trust Hierarchy (X.509 Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RootCA[Root CA Self Signed] --> IntCA1[Intermediate CA 1]
    RootCA --> IntCA2[Intermediate CA 2]
    IntCA1 --> SubCA1[Issuing CA for IoT Devices]
    IntCA1 --> SubCA2[Issuing CA for Users]
    IntCA2 --> SubCA3[Issuing CA for Servers]
    SubCA1 --> DevCert1[Device Certificate 1]
    SubCA1 --> DevCert2[Device Certificate 2]
    SubCA2 --> UserCert1[User Alice Cert]
    SubCA3 --> ServerCert1[Web Server Cert]
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Authentication vs Non-Repudiation System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer[Input]
        Msg[Incoming Message M]
        ID[Claimed Identity ID]
    end
    subgraph AuthModule[Authentication Module]
        F1[Factor 1 Knowledge]
        F2[Factor 2 Possession]
        F3[Factor 3 Inherence]
        MFA[Multi Factor Engine]
    end
    subgraph SignModule[Non Repudiation Module]
        HashEng[Hash Engine SHA-256]
        SignEng[Signing Engine RSA or ECDSA]
        TTP[Trusted Timestamp Authority TTA]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Decision]
        AuthOk[Identity Verified]
        SignOk[Signed Receipt Stored]
        Audit[Immutable Audit Log Blockchain or WORM]
    end
    Msg --> HashEng
    HashEng --> SignEng
    SignEng --> TTP
    TTP --> SignOk
    SignOk --> Audit
    ID --> F1
    ID --> F2
    ID --> F3
    F1 --> MFA
    F2 --> MFA
    F3 --> MFA
    MFA --> AuthOk
    AuthOk --> Audit
```

---

### 4.4 Attack Vector Map on Authenticity Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Authentication Mechanism] --> B[Password Based]
    A --> C[Token Based]
    A --> D[Biometric]
    A --> E[Certificate Based]
    B --> B1[Brute Force Attack]
    B --> B2[Phishing Attack]
    B --> B3[Credential Stuffing]
    C --> C1[Token Theft]
    C --> C2[Man in the Middle]
    C --> C3[Replay Attack]
    D --> D1[Spoofing Fingerprint]
    D --> D2[Deepfake Face]
    D --> D3[Replay Biometric Template]
    E --> E1[Rogue CA]
    E --> E2[Cert Pinning Bypass]
    E --> E3[Private Key Theft]
```

---

### 4.5 Non-Repudiation Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender as Alice Sender
    participant CA as Certificate Authority
    participant Receiver as Bob Receiver
    participant TTA as Trusted Timestamp Authority
    Sender->>CA: Request Digital Certificate
    CA-->>Sender: Issue X.509 Cert with PK bound to ID
    Sender->>Sender: Compute Hash h of M
    Sender->>Sender: Sign h with Private Key SK
    Sender->>TTA: Request Trusted Timestamp
    TTA-->>Sender: Signed Timestamp Token TST
    Sender->>Receiver: Send M, Signature, Cert, TST
    Receiver->>CA: Validate Cert chain
    CA-->>Receiver: Cert Valid
    Receiver->>Receiver: Verify Signature with PK
    Receiver->>Receiver: Verify Timestamp
    Receiver->>Receiver: Decision Accept or Reject
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between authentication and non-repudiation. Can a Message Authentication Code (MAC) provide non-repudiation? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Authentication | Non-Repudiation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Verifies the claimed identity at the time of access | Provides undeniable proof of an action for later verification |
| **Time Scope** | Real-time / session-bound | Persistent / long-term |
| **Example** | Password login, biometric scan | Digital signature on a contract |

**Can MAC provide non-repudiation?**
**No.** A MAC uses a symmetric key $K$ shared between sender and receiver. Since both parties possess $K$, either one could have generated the MAC. Therefore, a MAC provides **only data origin authentication**, not non-repudiation. For true non-repudiation, an **asymmetric digital signature** (e.g., RSA, ECDSA) is required, where the private key is exclusively held by the signer.

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if student explicitly mentions the symmetric-key vulnerability.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**List any three factors of authentication with one example each. What is Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Knowledge Factor** (something you know) — Example: password, PIN, security question.
2. **Possession Factor** (something you have) — Example: smart card, OTP token, mobile authenticator app.
3. **Inherence Factor** (something you are) — Example: fingerprint, iris scan, facial recognition.

**Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA):** MFA is a security mechanism that requires a user to successfully present evidence from **two or more independent categories of authentication factors** before access is granted. It significantly reduces the risk of compromised credentials, since an attacker must defeat multiple unrelated security layers.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (KTU 2024):** Answer ANY ONE full question from Module 1. Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the working of the **RSA digital signature scheme** with neat steps for key generation, signing, and verification. Show why it provides non-repudiation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) and Certificate Authorities (CA)** in establishing authenticity on the internet. What happens if the CA is compromised? **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — RSA Digital Signature (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Key Generation (2 marks):**
Alice selects two large primes $p$ and $q$. She computes:
$$n = p \cdot q, \quad \phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$$
She chooses public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$, then computes private exponent $d$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$
Her **public key** is $(n, e)$ and **private key** is $(n, d)$.

**Step 2 — Signing (2 marks):**
To sign message $M$, Alice first computes:
$$h = H(M) \quad \text{(e.g., SHA-256)}$$
Then computes the signature:
$$\sigma = h^{d} \bmod n$$
She sends $(M, \sigma)$ to Bob.

**Step 3 — Verification (2 marks):**
Bob recomputes $h' = H(M)$ and recovers:
$$h'' = \sigma^{e} \bmod n$$
By Euler's theorem, since $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$:
$$\sigma^{e} \bmod n = h^{de} \bmod n = h \bmod n$$
So Bob checks $h'' \stackrel{?}{=} h'$. If yes → signature valid.

**Why Non-Repudiation (1 mark):**
Only Alice knows $d$. No one else can produce $\sigma = h^d \bmod n$ for a new $h$. A court can verify the math publicly. Alice cannot later deny signing without claiming her private key was stolen (a separate legal issue, not a math weakness).

---

### Part (b) — PKI and CA Role (7 marks)

**PKI Definition (1 mark):**
PKI is the framework of policies, hardware, software, procedures, and cryptographic mechanisms that enable the creation, distribution, management, storage, and revocation of digital certificates.

**Role of CA (3 marks):**
The **Certificate Authority** is a trusted third party that:
- Verifies the identity of an entity (person/organization/device).
- Issues an **X.509 digital certificate** binding the entity's public key $PK$ to its identity $ID$.
- Signs the certificate with the CA's private key.
- Maintains CRL/OCSP for revocation.
- Forms a **chain of trust**: Root CA → Intermediate CA → End-entity certificate.

**How CA establishes authenticity on the internet (2 marks):**
When a browser connects to `https://example.com`, the server presents its TLS certificate. The browser verifies the certificate signature against a trusted root CA in its trust store. If valid, the server's public key is trusted → encrypted channel established → mutual authenticity achieved.

**Consequences of CA Compromise (1 mark):**
- Attacker can issue **rogue certificates** for any domain.
- Users may unknowingly connect to attacker-controlled servers believing they are legitimate (e.g., DigiNotar breach in 2011 led to issuance of fake Google certificates for Iranian users).
- Mitigation: **Certificate Transparency (CT) logs**, certificate pinning, multi-CA diversity.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) **[Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain **HMAC** in detail. How is it different from a plain hash $H(K \Vert M)$? Why is HMAC preferred for message authentication in protocols like TLS? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **three authentication factors** and explain why **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** is critical in IoT environments. List three common attacks against authentication systems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — HMAC Explained (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code) is a specific construction for computing a MAC using a cryptographic hash function $H$ and a secret key $K$.

**Mathematical Construction (2 marks):**
$$\text{HMAC}_{K}(M) = H\Big((K_{0} \oplus \text{opad}) \;\Vert\; H((K_{0} \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M)\Big)$$
where $K_{0}$ is the key normalized to the hash's block size $B$, $\text{opad} = 0x5C^B$ (outer pad), $\text{ipad} = 0x36^B$ (inner pad).

**Difference from Plain Hash $H(K \Vert M)$ (2 marks):**
| Aspect | $H(K \Vert M)$ | HMAC |
|---|---|---|
| Length-extension attack | ❌ Vulnerable | ✅ Resistant (nested hash) |
| Key handling | Concatenated directly | Two derived keys via XOR |
| Security proof | Weak (no formal proof) | Provably secure if $H$ is a PRF |

The nested structure with two different keys prevents attackers from appending data and computing a valid MAC without knowing $K$.

**Why TLS uses HMAC (2 marks):**
- Efficiency — uses fast hash functions.
- Standardized — RFC 2104 with widely vetted implementations.
- Supports forward secrecy when combined with key exchange.
- HMAC-SHA-256 is mandatory in TLS 1.3 for most cipher suites.

---

### Part (b) — Authentication Factors and MFA in IoT (7 marks)

**Three Factors (3 marks):**

1. **Knowledge** — password, PIN, secret phrase. Vulnerable to phishing.
2. **Possession** — smart card, hardware token, mobile device with OTP. Vulnerable to theft.
3. **Inherence** — fingerprint, facial geometry, iris pattern. Vulnerable to spoofing.

**Why MFA is Critical in IoT (3 marks):**
IoT devices often run with default or weak credentials (e.g., Mirai botnet exploited default admin/admin). MFA ensures that even if one factor (e.g., password) is compromised, the attacker still needs to bypass a second independent factor. For critical IoT (medical, industrial), MFA prevents catastrophic compromise. Standards like NIST SP 800-63B now mandate MFA for high-assurance applications.

**Three Common Authentication Attacks (1 mark — name them):**
1. **Brute Force / Dictionary Attack**
2. **Phishing / Credential Harvesting**
3. **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) Attack**
4. **Replay Attack**
5. **Biometric Spoofing**

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks):**
> 1. **Confusing MAC with Digital Signature** — A MAC does NOT provide non-repudiation. Examiners deduct **up to 2 marks** for stating otherwise.
> 2. **Forgetting to hash before signing** — RSA is applied to the **hash**, not the plaintext. Direct signing of plaintext is insecure (existential forgery attacks).
> 3. **Writing `e * d = 1 mod phi(n)`** without parentheses or mod — write `$ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$` for full credit.
> 4. **Not mentioning Euler's theorem** when explaining RSA verification — it's the mathematical reason the scheme works.
> 5. **Ignoring certificate revocation** — only mentioning certificate issuance in PKI questions loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Listing factors without examples** — always pair factor name with a concrete example.
> 7. **Missing the "why"** — examiners award marks for justification, not just definitions.

---

### 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- 🔹 **Authenticity** = proof of identity at the time of communication.
- 🔹 **Non-Repudiation** = undeniable proof of action, persistent over time.
- 🔹 **CIA Triad** + Authenticity + Non-Repudiation + Accountability = **Parkerian Hexad**.
- 🔹 **MAC** provides authentication only; **Digital Signature** provides both.
- 🔹 **RSA** signature: $\sigma = h^d \bmod n$; verification: $\sigma^e \bmod n \stackrel{?}{=} h$.
- 🔹 **ECDSA** is preferred for IoT due to shorter key/signature length (256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA).
- 🔹 **Hash algorithms**: SHA-256 (secure), SHA-1/MD5 (broken).
- 🔹 **HMAC** uses nested hashing with ipad/opad for length-extension resistance.
- 🔹 **PKI** trust chain: Root CA → Intermediate CA → End-entity certificate.
- 🔹 **X.509** is the standard certificate format.
- 🔹 **Three factors of authentication**: Knowledge, Possession, Inherence.
- 🔹 **MFA** = using two or more independent factors.
- 🔹 **Common attacks**: phishing, brute force, MitM, replay, biometric spoofing, rogue CA.
- 🔹 **CA compromise** = issuance of rogue certificates (e.g., DigiNotar 2011, Symantec 2017).
- 🔹 **IT Act 2000 (India)** and **eIDAS (EU)** grant legal validity to digital signatures.
- 🔹 **Bit strength**: RSA-2048 ≈ 112 bits; ECDSA-256 ≈ 128 bits (NIST).
- 🔹 **Kerckhoffs's principle** applies — security must reside in keys, not algorithm secrecy.
- 🔹 **Forward secrecy** requires ephemeral keys (Diffie-Hellman) — separate from non-repudiation.
- 🔹 **Timestamps + TTA** strengthen non-repudiation by proving when signing occurred.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Challenges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security Challenges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Standard)**
> **Security Challenges** in the context of Ethical Hacking and IoT refer to the constellation of *systemic, architectural, operational, and human-factor obstacles* that prevent the establishment of a robust, end-to-end, trustworthy, and resilient security posture across heterogeneous IoT ecosystems. These challenges arise from the convergence of resource-constrained edge devices, wireless communication channels, cloud back-ends, and diverse stakeholders, each introducing distinct attack surfaces and threat vectors that ethical hackers must enumerate, assess, and mitigate.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 Anchor)**
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic lays the foundation for **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Understand the security challenges in IoT architectures and enumerate the threat landscape."* Mastery of this section is mandatory before progressing to attack vectors, penetration testing, or IoT forensics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Building" View

Imagine a **large modern smart building** with:
- Hundreds of **tiny sensors** (temperature, smoke, motion) — each is an IoT device.
- **Dozens of wireless doors** (entry points) that visitors and employees use — these are the communication channels.
- A **central security room** (cloud server) that processes every signal.
- A **janitor**, a **tenant**, an **outsourced electrician**, and a **CEO** — these are the different stakeholders.

Now imagine the building's architect forgot to install cameras at the back door, gave every tenant the **master key**, the smoke sensors run on coin-cell batteries that die in months, and the wireless doors transmit signals in plain English (no encryption). **That is exactly what an unsecured IoT ecosystem looks like.** Security challenges are the *list of all such architectural and operational weaknesses* — from missing cameras (no monitoring) to weak locks (no authentication) to low battery (no updates) to shouting in public (no encryption).

## 1.3 The 7 Pillars of IoT Security Challenges (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT] **The 7 Master Categories**
> 1. **Device-Level Challenges** — hardware tampering, resource constraints, firmware insecurity.
> 2. **Communication-Level Challenges** — eavesdropping, replay, MitM, jamming, protocol weaknesses.
> 3. **Network-Level Challenges** — botnet recruitment, routing attacks, scalability of security.
> 4. **Cloud/Backend Challenges** — data breaches, API exploits, multi-tenancy isolation.
> 5. **Data-Level Challenges** — privacy leakage, integrity violations, big-data analytics risks.
> 6. **Application-Level Challenges** — weak authentication, insecure web/mobile apps, default credentials.
> 7. **Human/Policy-Level Challenges** — insider threats, lack of standards, regulatory non-compliance, poor awareness.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Key Performance Indicators

> [!NOTE] **Bold Standard Metrics Used Throughout KTU Boards**
> - **CIA Triad**: **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability.
> - **Authentication Factor Levels**: **1FA → 2FA → MFA**.
> - **Attack Surface (AS)**: Sum of all reachable entry points.
> - **Risk Score (R)**: $R = T \times V \times I$ (Threat × Vulnerability × Impact).
> - **STRIDE Threat Model**: **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, **E**levation of Privilege.
> - **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)**: scored **0.0 → 10.0**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Threat Model — Why IoT is Fundamentally Insecure

> [!NOTE] **The IoT Architecture (Perception → Network → Middleware → Application → Business)**
> Each layer has its own challenges. Ethical hackers must reason **layer-by-layer** because a vulnerability in one layer can compromise the entire stack.

### 2.1.1 The 5-Layer IoT Architecture (Reference Model)

| **Layer** | **Function** | **Core Security Challenge** |
|---|---|---|
| Perception / Sensing | Sensors + Actuators collect real-world data | Physical tampering, node capture, side-channel leakage |
| Network / Transport | Transmits data via Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, 5G, BLE | Eavesdropping, MitM, replay, jamming, routing attacks |
| Middleware / Edge | Protocol translation, fog/edge compute | API insecurity, authentication bypass, DoS |
| Application | User-facing apps, dashboards, analytics | Weak auth, XSS, CSRF, credential stuffing |
| Business / Cloud | Storage, big data, ML, decision support | Data breach, multi-tenant leakage, insider threat |

### 2.1.2 The 5 Underlying *Why* Reasons for Security Challenges

1. **Heterogeneity** — no single OS, protocol, or standard; impossible to apply uniform patches.
2. **Resource Constraints** — limited CPU, RAM, battery → strong crypto (AES-256, TLS 1.3) is often infeasible.
3. **Scale** — billions of devices; manual security management is impossible → automation is mandatory.
4. **Physical Exposure** — devices deployed in public / hostile environments → easy physical access for attackers.
5. **Long Lifespan + Infrequent Updates** — devices live 10–20 years; cryptographic algorithms and firmware become stale.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Must-Know)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Use `\vert` (NOT `|`) in all math inside table cells — as per KTU rendering standards.**

| **#** | **Principle / Equation** | **Mathematical Form** | **Real-World Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CIA Triad | $\{C, I, A\}$ | Foundation for every security policy in IoT systems. |
| 2 | Risk Score | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Quantifies asset exposure in penetration-test reports. |
| 3 | Attack Surface | $AS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} EP_i$ where $EP_i$ = entry point $i$ | Estimates total reachable exposure of a deployment. |
| 4 | Shannon Entropy (Key Strength) | $H(K) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measures unpredictability of a cryptographic key. |
| 5 | Hash Collision Probability (Birthday) | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2 \cdot 2^m)}$ | Used to determine safe hash length (e.g., SHA-256). |
| 6 | CVSS Base Score | $CVSS \in [0, 10]$ | Industry-standard vulnerability severity. |
| 7 | Authentication Strength | $1FA \ll 2FA \ll MFA$ | Determines resistance to credential theft. |
| 8 | STRIDE Taxonomy | $\{S, T, R, I, D, E\}$ | Microsoft's threat-classification standard. |
| 9 | CIA + Non-Repudiation + AuthN | $\{C, I, A, N, Au, AuZ\}$ | Extended "Parkerian Hexad" / NIST framework. |
| 10 | Defense in Depth | $D_{total} = D_1 \cap D_2 \cap D_3 \cap \dots \cap D_n$ | Multi-layer protection — failure of one layer is absorbed by the next. |

> [!NOTE] **Mnemonic for STRIDE** (favourite KTU board question): **"S**poofing **T**ricks **R**eal **I**ntel **D**octors, **E**vil!" — recall: *Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege*.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Healthcare**: A compromised insulin pump can deliver a lethal dose (C, I, A all violated).
- **Smart Grid**: False data injection can black-out cities (I + A violated).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Stuxnet-style firmware tampering destroys centrifuges (I + A violated).
- **Consumer IoT**: Mirai botnet (2016) used 600K+ default-password cameras to launch a 1.1 Tbps DDoS (A violated).
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: CAN-bus injection can hijack braking (C, I, A, S all violated).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Risk Equation $R = T \times V \times I$

The KTU board frequently tests the *Risk Triplet*. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the threat ($T$).** A threat is the *probability* that an adversary will attempt to exploit a weakness.

$$T \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 2 — Define the vulnerability ($V$).** A vulnerability is the *probability* that the weakness, if attempted, will succeed.

$$V \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 3 — Define the impact ($I$).** Impact is the *magnitude of harm* normalised to a 0–1 scale (or monetary loss in $).

$$I \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the three independent probabilities.** Assuming independence (a standard first-order assumption used by NIST and ISO 27005):

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= T \times V \times I \\
  &= \underbrace{P(\text{attempt})}_{T} \times \underbrace{P(\text{success} \mid \text{attempt})}_{V} \times \underbrace{\text{Harm Magnitude}}_{I}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Add a fourth environmental factor (Advanced NIST model).** For board-level rigour, we add *Asset Value (A)* and *Mitigation Effectiveness (M)*:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{adj} &= \frac{T \times V \times I \times A}{M} \\
\text{where } M &\in [1, 10] \quad \text{(control effectiveness multiplier)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation**: As $M \rightarrow 10$ (excellent controls like MFA + WAF + patching), $R_{adj} \rightarrow 0$ — i.e., risk is minimised. This is the *quantitative justification* for Defense in Depth.

## 3.2 Derivation: Attack-Surface Cardinality in a Typical IoT Deployment

Consider an IoT smart-home deployment with:
- $n_1 = 20$ sensors (each with 1 open port)
- $n_2 = 3$ gateways (each with 3 services: HTTP, MQTT, CoAP)
- $n_3 = 1$ cloud API (5 endpoints: /login, /devices, /telemetry, /firmware, /admin)

**Step 1 — Entry points per layer.**

$$\begin{aligned}
EP_{\text{sensor}} &= 20 \times 1 = 20 \\
EP_{\text{gateway}} &= 3 \times 3 = 9 \\
EP_{\text{cloud}}    &= 1 \times 5 = 5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Total attack surface.**

$$\begin{aligned}
AS &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} EP_i \\
   &= EP_{\text{sensor}} + EP_{\text{gateway}} + EP_{\text{cloud}} \\
   &= 20 + 9 + 5 = 34
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Closure check (sanity).** If we add a mobile-app layer (3 endpoints × 1 app = 3), the new total is 37 — a $\sim 8.8\%$ increase. **Adding any feature increases attack surface.** This is the **attack-surface paradox** that ethical hackers exploit during reconnaissance.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Challenge × Attack × Mitigation

| **#** | **Challenge** | **Likely Attack (STRIDE mapping)** | **Ethical-Hacking Mitigation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Default credentials on devices | **S**poofing | Credential audit, password rotation, MFA. |
| 2 | No firmware signing | **T**ampering | Verify digital signatures, secure boot. |
| 3 | Plaintext MQTT | **I**nfo Disclosure | TLS 1.3 + client certificates. |
| 4 | Resource constraints (no TLS) | **E**avesdropping / MitM | Lightweight DTLS, IKEv2, or hardware crypto. |
| 5 | Physical access to nodes | **T**ampering / Side-channel | Tamper-proof enclosures, mesh attestation. |
| 6 | Unpatched firmware | **E**levation of Privilege | OTA updates, vulnerability management. |
| 7 | Open cloud APIs | **D**oS / **I**nfo Disclosure | Rate-limiting, WAF, OAuth 2.0, RBAC. |
| 8 | Unencrypted storage | **I**nfo Disclosure | AES-256 at rest, TPM, secure elements. |
| 9 | Weak key generation (PRNG) | **S**poofing | Hardware TRNG (True Random Number Generators). |
| 10 | Insecure boot sequence | **E**levation of Privilege | Secure boot chain (root-of-trust). |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Board Tip**: When asked to "list IoT security challenges", the examiner expects **at least 6–8 challenges classified by layer**, plus a brief STRIDE mapping. The matrix above is your exam-ready answer skeleton.

## 3.4 Quantitative Justification: Why $M$ (Controls) Reduces Risk

Let $T = 0.8$, $V = 0.7$, $I = 0.9$, $A = 1.0$.

**Without controls ($M = 1$):**

$$R_{adj} = \frac{0.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.9 \times 1.0}{1} = 0.504$$

**With MFA + WAF + Patching ($M = 8$):**

$$R_{adj} = \frac{0.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.9 \times 1.0}{8} = 0.063$$

$$\text{Risk Reduction} = \frac{0.504 - 0.063}{0.504} \times 100\% \approx 87.5\%$$

This justifies every security budget ever written.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: IoT Security Challenge Taxonomy (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[IoT Security Challenges] --> B[Device Layer]
    A --> C[Network Layer]
    A --> D[Cloud Layer]
    A --> E[Application Layer]
    A --> F[Data Layer]
    A --> G[Human Policy Layer]

    B --> B1[Hardware Tampering]
    B --> B2[Firmware Insecurity]
    B --> B3[Resource Constraints]
    B --> B4[Side Channel Leakage]

    C --> C1[Eavesdropping]
    C --> C2[Replay Attack]
    C --> C3[Man in the Middle]
    C --> C4[Jamming]
    C --> C5[Routing Attack]

    D --> D1[API Exploits]
    D --> D2[Multi Tenancy Leakage]
    D --> D3[DoS and DDoS]

    E --> E1[Weak Authentication]
    E --> E2[Insecure Web Apps]
    E --> E3[Default Credentials]

    F --> F1[Privacy Leakage]
    F --> F2[Integrity Violation]
    F --> F3[Big Data Analytics Risk]

    G --> G1[Insider Threat]
    G --> G2[Regulatory Non Compliance]
    G --> G3[Lack of Awareness]
    G --> G4[No Standardization]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: STRIDE Threat Modeling Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Asset] --> B[Identify Threats via STRIDE]
    B --> C1[S Spoofing]
    B --> C2[T Tampering]
    B --> C3[R Repudiation]
    B --> C4[I Info Disclosure]
    B --> C5[D Denial of Service]
    B --> C6[E Elevation of Privilege]
    C1 --> D[Map to Vulnerabilities]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E[Compute Risk R = T x V x I]
    E --> F[Recommend Controls]
    F --> G[Validate via Ethical Hacking]
    G --> H[Mitigation Verification]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: CIA Triad with Extended Parkerian Hexad

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[IoT Security Goals] --> C[Confidentiality]
    Root --> I[Integrity]
    Root --> A[Availability]
    Root --> N[Non Repudiation]
    Root --> Au[Authenticity]
    Root --> Az[Authorization]

    C --> Cdesc[Only authorized parties can read data]
    I --> Idesc[Data is not modified in transit or at rest]
    A --> Adesc[Service remains available under attack]
    N --> Ndesc[Sender cannot deny the action]
    Au --> Audesc[Identity of the entity is verified]
    Az --> Azdesc[Entity has the right to perform the action]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Attack Flow in a Typical IoT Compromise

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1 Reconnaissance] --> S2[Step 2 Enumerate Devices and Ports]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Identify Default Credentials or Unpatched Firmware]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Gain Initial Foothold]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Lateral Movement via Cloud API]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Data Exfiltration or DoS]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Establish Persistence]

    style S1 fill:#fff5d6
    style S2 fill:#fff5d6
    style S3 fill:#ffe1a3
    style S4 fill:#ff8a73
    style S5 fill:#ff8a73
    style S6 fill:#d92828,color:#fff
    style S7 fill:#7a0d0d,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the diagram**: Steps 1–3 (yellow) are *pre-attack* phases (legal in ethical hacking with authorisation). Steps 4–7 (red) are *exploitation* phases that must NEVER be performed without written, scoped authorisation (Rules of Engagement).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**"List and briefly explain any THREE major security challenges in IoT."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Resource Constraints (1 mark):** IoT devices have limited CPU, memory, and battery, making strong cryptographic algorithms (AES-256, TLS 1.3) infeasible. This creates a challenge in balancing security and performance.
2. **Heterogeneity (1 mark):** IoT ecosystems comprise diverse devices, OS, and protocols (ZigBee, BLE, LoRa, MQTT, CoAP). A single uniform security policy is impractical, increasing the attack surface.
3. **Physical Exposure (1 mark):** Devices are often deployed in public/hostile locations (street lights, agricultural fields, factories), allowing attackers physical access for hardware tampering, node capture, and side-channel attacks.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often write "hacking" or "data theft" as challenges. These are *attacks*, not *challenges*. The question asks for **root-cause obstacles** that *enable* attacks. Word your answer accordingly.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**"Explain the STRIDE threat model with one example for each category."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Letter** | **Threat** | **Example in IoT (1 mark each row → totalling 3)** |
|---|---|---|
| S | Spoofing | Attacker spoofs a legitimate sensor's MAC address to inject false data. |
| T | Tampering | Modifying firmware of a smart meter to report false consumption. |
| R | Repudiation | User denies issuing a command since logs are not tamper-proof. |
| I | Info Disclosure | Eavesdropping on unencrypted MQTT traffic reveals patient vitals. |
| D | Denial of Service | Jamming a wireless sensor network to disable a smart factory. |
| E | Elevation of Privilege | Exploiting a buffer overflow to gain root on a smart camera. |

*(Any 3 rows with correct expansion earn full 3 marks.)*

> [!WARNING] **Pitfall**: Writing the full forms is mandatory. Simply listing "S, T, R, I, D, E" without expansion = **0 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 ESE Rule**: Each Part-B question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, totalling 14. Internal choice is **between two questions, not sub-parts**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** *"Discuss in detail the security challenges in IoT at the device, network, and application layers. Provide at least two challenges per layer."*

#### **Model Solution:**

**Device Layer Challenges (≈ 2.5 marks):**
- **Hardware Tampering**: Attackers physically open devices to extract cryptographic keys or inject malicious code. *Mitigation:* tamper-evident enclosures, mesh attestation.
- **Resource Constraints**: Limited CPU/RAM/battery prevents running full TLS stacks. *Mitigation:* lightweight crypto (e.g., DTLS, ChaCha20-Poly1305).
- **Firmware Insecurity**: Hard-coded credentials, unsigned firmware. *Mitigation:* secure boot, signed OTA updates.

**Network Layer Challenges (≈ 2.5 marks):**
- **Eavesdropping & MitM**: Unencrypted wireless channels (BLE, ZigBee) allow traffic interception. *Mitigation:* link-layer encryption, certificate pinning.
- **Replay Attacks**: Captured valid packets are re-sent. *Mitigation:* nonces, timestamps, sequence numbers.
- **Jamming & Routing Attacks**: DoS at the physical/routing layer. *Mitigation:* frequency hopping, trust-based routing (e.g., RPL security).

**Application Layer Challenges (≈ 2 marks):**
- **Weak Authentication**: Default passwords (admin/admin). *Mitigation:* MFA, OAuth 2.0.
- **Insecure APIs**: Missing rate-limiting, broken authentication. *Mitigation:* API gateways, RBAC, WAF.

> **Closure Line (mandatory for full marks):** *"Thus, IoT security challenges are layered; an ethical hacker must test each layer independently and assess cross-layer vulnerabilities."*

---

> **(b) [7 marks]** *"Explain the CIA Triad and extend it to the Parkerian Hexad. Justify why the additional three properties are essential for IoT."*

#### **Model Solution:**

**CIA Triad (≈ 3 marks):**

- **Confidentiality (C)**: Ensures data is accessible only to authorised parties. In IoT: protect sensor data (e.g., patient vitals) from eavesdropping.
- **Integrity (I)**: Ensures data is not altered in transit or at rest. In IoT: prevent false data injection in a smart grid.
- **Availability (A)**: Ensures services are accessible when needed. In IoT: prevent DDoS attacks on a smart traffic system.

**Parkerian Hexad Extension (≈ 2.5 marks):**

The Parkerian Hexad adds three properties to the CIA Triad:
- **Non-Repudiation (N)**: Sender cannot deny their action. In IoT: command logs must be cryptographically signed so a user cannot deny issuing a malicious actuator command.
- **Authenticity (Au)**: Identity verification. In IoT: digital certificates verify a device is genuine (anti-counterfeiting).
- **Authorisation (Az)**: Permission to perform an action. In IoT: RBAC ensures a temperature sensor cannot issue actuator commands.

**Justification for IoT (≈ 1.5 marks):**

> In a resource-constrained, multi-stakeholder IoT ecosystem, *mere CIA is insufficient*. For example, a connected car's brake system needs **authenticity** (the command came from the driver), **authorisation** (only authorised ECUs may actuate brakes), and **non-repudiation** (in a forensic case, the driver cannot deny sending the command). Therefore, the **Parkerian Hexad** provides a more complete security framework for IoT.

> **Diagram mention (compulsory for full marks):** *Draw the CIA Triad + Hexad as a Venn diagram or as in Section 4.3 above.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** *"With a neat diagram, explain the 5-layer IoT architecture and identify the security challenges at each layer."*

#### **Model Solution:**

**5-Layer IoT Architecture (≈ 3 marks):**

1. **Perception Layer**: Physical sensors and actuators collect data.
2. **Network Layer**: Transmits data via Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, BLE, 5G.
3. **Middleware / Edge Layer**: Protocol translation, fog compute, gateway services.
4. **Application Layer**: User-facing dashboards, mobile apps, analytics.
5. **Business / Cloud Layer**: Storage, big-data processing, ML, decision support.

**Security Challenges per Layer (≈ 4 marks):**

| **Layer** | **Challenge** |
|---|---|
| Perception | Node capture, hardware tampering, side-channel leakage |
| Network | Eavesdropping, MitM, replay, jamming, Sybil attacks |
| Middleware | API insecurity, authentication bypass, edge-server compromise |
| Application | Weak passwords, XSS, CSRF, insecure third-party SDKs |
| Business / Cloud | Data breach, multi-tenant leakage, insider threat, regulatory non-compliance |
