- Produces a score between **0.0 and 10.0**, classified into **None (0.0), Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), and Critical (9.0–10.0)**.

**2. Base Metrics (3 marks):**
These describe the **intrinsic** properties of a vulnerability that remain constant across time and environment. They are sub-divided into **Exploitability** and **Impact**.

| Metric | Options | Score |
|--------|---------|-------|
| Attack Vector (AV) | Network (0.85), Adjacent (0.62), Local (0.55), Physical (0.20) | |
| Attack Complexity (AC) | Low (0.77), High (0.44) | |
| Privileges Required (PR) | None (0.85), Low (0.62), High (0.27) | |
| User Interaction (UI) | None (0.85), Required (0.62) | |
| Scope (S) | Unchanged, Changed | |
| Confidentiality (C) | None (0.00), Low (0.22), High (0.56) | |
| Integrity (I) | None (0.00), Low (0.22), High (0.56) | |
| Availability (A) | None (0.00), Low (0.22), High (0.56) | |

**3. Temporal Metrics (1.5 marks):**
These capture the **current state** of exploit techniques and patch availability.
- **Exploit Code Maturity (E)** — Not Defined, Unproven, Proof-of-Concept, Functional, High
- **Remediation Level (RL)** — Official Fix, Temporary Fix, Workaround, Unavailable
- **Report Confidence (RC)** — Unknown, Reasonable, Confirmed

**4. Environmental Metrics (1.5 marks):**
These allow **organization-specific** customization.
- **Confidentiality Requirement (CR)** — Low, Medium, High
- **Integrity Requirement (IR)** — Low, Medium, High
- **Availability Requirement (AR)** — Low, Medium, High
- **Modified Base Metrics** — allowing override of the original Base values

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — 7 marks

**Given Metrics:**
- AV:N (0.85), AC:L (0.77), PR:N (0.85), UI:N (0.85), S:C, C:H (0.56), I:H (0.56), A:H (0.56)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISS) for S:C.

$$ ISS = 1 - \left[ (1-C) \times (1-I) \times (1-A) \right] $$

$$ ISS = 1 - \left[ (1-0.56)^3 \right] = 1 - (0.44)^3 $$

$$ ISS = 1 - 0.085184 = 0.914816 $$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the Impact value.

$$ Impact = 7.52 \times (ISS - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (ISS \times 0.9731 - 0.02)^{13} $$

$$ Impact = 7.52 \times 0.885816 - 3.25 \times (0.890377 - 0.02)^{13} $$

$$ Impact = 6.6613 - 3.25 \times (0.870377)^{13} $$

$$ Impact = 6.6613 - 3.25 \times 0.1478 $$

$$ Impact = 6.6613 - 0.4804 = 6.1809 $$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the Exploitability (E) sub-score.

$$ E = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI $$

$$ E = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 = 3.887 $$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the Base Score.

$$ BaseScore = \text{RoundUp}\left[ \min\left( (Impact + E), 10 \right) \right] $$

$$ BaseScore = \text{RoundUp}\left[ \min(6.1809 + 3.887, 10) \right] $$

$$ BaseScore = \text{RoundUp}\left[ \min(10.0679, 10) \right] = \text{RoundUp}(10) = 10.0 $$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Classify the severity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CVSS v3.1 Base Score = 10.0 → CRITICAL Severity**

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Remediation Timeline.

| Severity | Patch Window | Action |
|----------|--------------|--------|
| **Critical (9.0–10.0)** | **24–72 hours** | Emergency patch; take device offline if no patch available |
| High (7.0–8.9) | 7–14 days | Scheduled patching with compensating WAF rules |
| Medium (4.0–6.9) | 30 days | Routine patch cycle |
| Low (0.1–3.9) | 90 days | Backlog patch |

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Specific IoT Recommendations.

1. **Take the camera offline** immediately or segment it on an isolated VLAN.
2. **Push a signed firmware update** to all affected devices.
3. **Audit network traffic** for any signs of exploitation (CISA/NIST advisory search).
4. **Replace** if the vendor has issued an End-of-Life notice with no patch forthcoming.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing VA with Pentesting** — A 3-mark question asking for the difference is the most-failed item. Memorize the table in Section 5.1.
> 2. **Forgetting units in ALE** — Always write "₹X per year" or "₹X per incident." Naked numbers lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the RoundUp()** — CVSS scores are always integers in the published metric; forgetting the rounding step loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up DREAD components** — "D" appears twice (Damage and Discoverability). Spelling out the full names in your answer is mandatory.
> 5. **Not stating the CVSS version** — Always write "CVSS v3.1" not just "CVSS." The v2 and v3 formulas differ significantly.
> 6. **Skipping interpretation in numerical problems** — A computation-only answer without a business interpretation loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Wrong tool attribution** — Saying "Nessus is open source" is a factual error. Nessus Essentials is a free *tier* of a *commercial* product. OpenVAS is the truly open-source scanner.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Vulnerability Assessment**
> 
> - **Definition:** Systematic identification, classification, and prioritization of security weaknesses in systems.
> - **Fundamental Equation:** $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$.
> - **VA vs. Pentest:** VA = find & report (broad, automated); Pentest = find & exploit (deep, manual).
> - **VA Lifecycle:** 5 phases — Planning → Asset Discovery → Scanning → Analysis & Prioritization → Reporting & Verification.
> - **CVSS v3.1 Score Range:** 0.0 (None) → 10.0 (Critical), maintained by **FIRST.org**.
> - **CVSS Metric Groups:** **Base** (intrinsic), **Temporal** (changes over time), **Environmental** (org-specific).
> - **Base Score Formula:** $RoundUp[\min(Impact + Exploitability, 10)]$.
> - **ALE Formula:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ where $SLE = AV \times EF$.
> - **DREAD Formula:** Average of Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected Users, Discoverability (each 1–10).
> - **Top Tools:** Nessus (commercial), Qualys (SaaS), OpenVAS (open source), Nmap (free), OWASP ZAP (web).
> - **Key Databases:** **CVE** (MITRE), **NVD** (NIST), **CVSS** (FIRST).
> - **IoT-Specific VA Steps:** Firmware extraction (Binwalk), hardware interface (JTAG/UART), network protocol fuzzing, credential analysis.
> - **Severity Bands:** 0.1–3.9 Low, 4.0–6.9 Medium, 7.0–8.9 High, 9.0–10.0 Critical.
> - **Remediation SLOs:** Critical = 24–72 hrs, High = 7–14 days, Medium = 30 days, Low = 90 days.
> - **Compliance Hooks:** PCI-DSS 11.3, ISO 27001 A.12.6.1, HIPAA Security Rule, DPDPA 2023.
> - **Exam Rule:** Always mention the **tool, the formula version, and the interpretation** in any numerical VA question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools for Foot Printing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Tools for Foot Printing** # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Footprinting** is the **first and most critical phase of the Reconnaissance / Information Gathering stage** in the Ethical Hacking methodology. It is defined as the systematic process of collecting exhaustive information about a target system, network, or organization from publicly accessible sources (Open Source Intelligence - **OSINT**) **without directly interacting** with the target's internal infrastructure in a intrusive manner. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for PBCCT604, footprinting establishes the foundational **attack surface map** before any active scanning or penetration testing is attempted. The deliverables of footprinting include: - **Network Range** (IP blocks, ASN numbers) - **Domain Names**, Sub-domains, and DNS records - **Operating System fingerprinting clues** - **Employee email patterns, organizational structure** - **Physical locations** and infrastructure details - **Defense mechanisms** (Firewalls, IDS/IPS) in place ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Detective Before the Heist":** > Imagine a detective planning to infiltrate a highly secure museum. Before kicking down doors, he spends weeks doing homework: reading public newspaper articles about the museum, checking Google Maps for the back alley, looking at employee LinkedIn profiles to see who works the night shift, checking the county registrar for the building's blueprint filings, and reading the museum's own promotional brochures. He gathers *every piece of publicly available paper* and stitches them together into a complete map of the building — guard rotations, door types, alarm vendors. This is **Footprinting**. The detective never actually touched the museum yet. When the real "heist" (penetration test) begins, he already knows where every camera is blind. The same logic applies to an ethical hacker. **Tools for Footprinting** are the detective's magnifying glass, binoculars, and public-records database, all rolled into software. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Metrics Used in Footprinting:** > - **TTL (Time-To-Live)** default values: **64** (Linux), **128** (Windows), **255** (Network Devices) > - **WHOIS Port:** TCP **43** > - **DNS Port:** UDP **53** (queries) / TCP **53** (zone transfers) > - **HTTP/HTTPS Ports:** TCP **80** and **443** > - **OSI Layer:** Footprinting primarily operates at Layers **2 through 7** > - **RFC 3912:** WHOIS protocol specification > - **RFC 1035:** DNS protocol specification ## 1.4 Two Classifications of Footprinting > [!NOTE] > **Passive Footprinting** → Gathering data **without the target ever knowing** (e.g., searching Google, reading job postings, Wayback Machine). > **Active Footprinting** → Directly interacting with the target's systems (e.g., pinging the host, traceroute, DNS zone transfer requests). The target's logs *may* record your activity. Tools exist for both classes, and Module 1 focuses on enumerating these utilities. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Footprinting Tools Footprinting tools follow a **4-Stage Operational Logic**, regardless of whether they are web-based, CLI, or GUI utilities: ### Stage 1 — **Domain & Registrar Intelligence** - Querying domain registration databases (**WHOIS**) - Discovering **name servers, registrant email, admin contact** - Identifying **domain creation / expiry date** (a freshly registered domain is a red flag for phishing) ### Stage 2 — **Network Range & IP Enumeration** - Discovering the **Autonomous System Number (ASN)** owned by the organization - Mapping all **IP blocks (CIDR ranges)** allocated to the target - Identifying the **Internet Service Provider (ISP)** and **colocation data centers** ### Stage 3 — **DNS Record Mining** - Querying **A, AAAA, MX, NS, TXT, SOA, CNAME, PTR** records - Attempting **DNS Zone Transfer (AXFR)** — a classic misconfiguration - Discovering **sub-domains** (mail.target.com, dev.target.com, vpn.target.com) ### Stage 4 — **OSINT Correlation & Cross-Verification** - Cross-checking email addresses on social media - Pulling historical snapshots from the **Wayback Machine** - Identifying exposed documents (PDFs, DOCX) via **Google Dorks** ## 2.2 Comprehensive Footprinting Tool Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **single most important high-yield reference** for KTU exams on this topic. Memorize the **Tool → Purpose → Category → Output** mapping. | Tool Name | Primary Purpose | Category | Output Data | License | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **WHOIS** (whois.iana.org) | Domain registrar lookup | Passive | Registrant, NS, dates | Free / RFC 3912 | | **Nslookup / dig** | DNS record query | Active (non-intrusive) | A, MX, NS, TXT records | BIND utility | | **theHarvester** | Email & subdomain harvesting | Passive OSINT | Emails, subdomains, hosts | Open Source (Kali) | | **Maltego** | Visual link analysis | Passive OSINT | Relationship graphs | Commercial / Community | | **Recon-ng** | Web recon framework | Passive OSINT | Modular reports | Open Source | | **Shodan** | IoT & device search engine | Passive | Open ports, banners, CVEs | Subscription / Free tier | | **Censys** | Internet-wide scanner data | Passive | Hosts, certificates | Free / Academic | | **Google Dorks** | Advanced Google operators | Passive | Exposed files, logins | Free (browser-based) | | **Wayback Machine** | Historical website snapshots | Passive | Deleted pages, old code | Free | | **Traceroute / Tracert** | Network path mapping | Active | Hop-by-hop routers | OS built-in | | **Netcraft** | Web technology profiler | Passive | Server OS, CMS, SSL info | Free tier | | **OSINT Framework** | Meta-directory of OSINT tools | Passive | Tool catalog | Free / Web | | **SpiderFoot** | Automated OSINT automation | Passive / Active | 200+ data points | Open Source | | **FOCA** | Metadata extraction from docs | Passive | Author, software, paths | Free / Pro | | **Sublist3r** | Subdomain enumeration | Passive | Subdomains via many engines | Open Source | | **dnsmap** | Subdomain brute-forcing | Active | Discovered subdomains | Open Source | ## 2.3 High-Yield Conceptual Formulas & Decision Rules > [!NOTE] > While footprinting is largely qualitative, KTU examiners expect students to know these **decision rules** and **protocol constants**. ### Decision Rule 1 — Identifying OS from TTL $$TTL_{observed} \approx \begin{cases} 64 & \text{if } \text{OS} = \text{Linux/Unix} \\ 128 & \text{if } \text{OS} = \text{Windows} \\ 255 & \text{if } \text{OS} = \text{Network Device (Cisco/Juniper)} \end{cases}$$ ### Decision Rule 2 — CIDR Range Coverage $$\text{Number of usable hosts} = 2^{(32 - n)} - 2$$ where $n$ is the CIDR prefix length. The "$-2$" accounts for the **network address** and the **broadcast address** which are reserved. ### Decision Rule 3 — Email Pattern Inference $$\text{Pattern} = f(\text{FirstName}, \text{LastName}, \text{Domain})$$ Common corporate patterns observed during footprinting: - `firstname.lastname@domain.com` (most common) - `firstinitial.lastname@domain.com` - `firstname@domain.com` - `flastname@domain.com` ### Decision Rule 4 — DNS Zone Transfer Success Test $$\text{AXFR}_{result} = \begin{cases} \text{REFUSED} & \text{Secure (good config)} \\ \text{Transfer Successful} & \text{Vulnerable (full zone leaked)} \end{cases}$$ ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > Footprinting is **not** a theoretical exercise. It is used by: > - **Red Teams** — to plan authorized penetration tests > - **Blue Teams** — to audit their own organization's external exposure > - **Bug Bounty Hunters** — to find hidden subdomains & exposed assets > - **Threat Intelligence Analysts** — to map adversary infrastructure > - **IoT Security Auditors** — to find exposed Shodan-indexed devices (very relevant for this course's IoT focus) --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — WHOIS Lookup Process **Scenario:** An ethical hacker wants to footprint the domain `ktu.edu.in` and determine the registrar, creation date, and name servers. ### Step 1 — Query the IANA Root WHOIS Server The hacker first queries the **Root WHOIS server** to identify which **Registrar WHOIS server** is authoritative for the `.in` TLD. $$\text{Query: } \text{whois.iana.org} \rightarrow \text{whois.inregistry.net}$$ ### Step 2 — Query the Authoritative Registrar The tool then queries the registrar's WHOIS server on **TCP Port 43** (per RFC 3912): $$\text{Query: } \text{whois.inregistry.net} \rightarrow \text{Response (TCP/43)}$$ ### Step 3 — Parse the Output The output fields typically include: | Field | Example Value (Illustrative) | | :--- | :--- | | Domain Name | KTU.EDU.IN | | Registrar | ERNET India / Affiliated Registrar | | Creation Date | 2009-08-24 | | Expiry Date | 2028-08-24 | | Name Server | NS1.KTU.EDU.IN | | Name Server | NS2.KTU.EDU.IN | | Registrant Org | APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University | | Registrant State | Kerala | | Admin Email | admin@ktu.edu.in | ### Step 4 — Cross-Verification The hacker takes the **Name Server (NS)** records and uses them in the next stage for **DNS enumeration** (dig/nslookup), thereby chaining the tools in a logical kill-chain. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Insight:** If a student is asked in KTU exams *"What protocol port does WHOIS use?"*, the answer is **TCP 43**. This is a frequently tested 1-mark fact. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Subdomain Enumeration with theHarvester **Scenario:** Use theHarvester to enumerate subdomains and emails of `target-org.com`. ### Step 1 — Command Syntax The general syntax follows the pattern: $$\texttt{theHarvester -d -b -l }$$ ### Step 2 — Sample Invocation $$\texttt{theHarvester -d target-org.com -b google -l 500}$$ Where: - $-d$ → target domain - $-b$ → data source (google, bing, linkedin, shodan, censys) - $-l$ → result count limit ### Step 3 — Tool Workflow (Logical Steps) **Step 3.1** — theHarvester sends crafted **Google Dork queries** such as: $$\text{Query: } \texttt{site:target-org.com -www -mail}$$ **Step 3.2** — It scrapes the first 500 search results and extracts: - Email addresses matching `*@target-org.com` - Hostnames / subdomains **Step 3.3** — It verifies each discovered host by performing a **DNS resolution**: $$\text{Host} \rightarrow \text{gethostbyname()} \rightarrow \text{IP Address}$$ **Step 3.4** — It outputs a structured report: | Type | Discovered Asset | | :--- | :--- | | Email | admin@target-org.com | | Email | hr@target-org.com | | Subdomain | mail.target-org.com | | Subdomain | vpn.target-org.com | | Subdomain | dev.target-org.com | | IP | 203.0.113.45 | | IP | 203.0.113.46 | ### Step 4 — Pivot to Next Phase Each discovered subdomain becomes a **target for active scanning** in Module 2 (Scanning Networks). This is the **chain-of-tools methodology** that KTU examiners love to test. > [!IMPORTANT] > **theHarvester is included by default in Kali Linux** at `/usr/bin/theHarvester`. Knowing its flag set ($-d$, $-b$, $-l$, $-f$) is a high-yield 3-mark question item. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — DNS Zone Transfer Test (Active Footprinting) **Scenario:** Test if `ns1.target-org.com` allows unauthorized zone transfers. ### Step 1 — Identify Authoritative NS $$\texttt{dig NS target-org.com} \rightarrow \text{ns1.target-org.com}$$ ### Step 2 — Attempt Zone Transfer The command syntax is: $$\texttt{dig @ns1.target-org.com target-org.com AXFR}$$ ### Step 3 — Interpret the Output Flags The dig response header contains critical flags: | Flag | Meaning | Security Implication | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `XFR size: X records` | Transfer succeeded | **CRITICAL VULNERABILITY** | | `; Transfer failed.` | REFUSED | **Securely configured** | | `status: SERVFAIL` | Server error | May be misconfigured | ### Step 4 — Why This Matters (Mathematical View) A successful AXFR reveals **every single hostname** in the zone — internal servers, dev environments, backup hosts. The exposure is: $$E_{zone} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i$$ where $H_i$ is the $i$-th host record and $N$ is the total record count. Even one misconfigured NS disclosing $N=200$ records is a complete organizational map leak. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Passive vs Active Footprinting Tools > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently asks: *"Differentiate between passive and active footprinting with tools."* Use this exact comparison. | Parameter | Passive Footprinting | Active Footprinting | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Target Awareness** | Target is **unaware** | Target's logs **may record** activity | | **Detection Risk** | **Very Low / Zero** | **Moderate to High** | | **Tools Examples** | theHarvester, Maltego, Shodan, Google Dorks, Wayback Machine | Nmap, DNS zone transfer, traceroute | | **Legal Risk** | Generally **lower** (uses public data) | **Higher** (direct probing) | | **Information Depth** | Broad and high-level | Technical and deep | | **Stage in Pentest** | Early / Module 1 | Later / Module 2 onwards | | **OSI Layer Touched** | Layers 5–7 (mostly) | Layers 2–4 (often) | --- ## 3.5 Tool-by-Tool Operational Walkthrough Summary > [!NOTE] > **Use this 5-tool chain as the canonical Module 1 answer** when KTU asks *"Explain the tools used for footprinting with examples."* **Step 1 → WHOIS Lookup** Identify the domain owner, registrar, and contact information. **Step 2 → DNS Enumeration (dig / nslookup)** Resolve all subdomains to IPs and find mail servers. **Step 3 → theHarvester / Sublist3r** Harvest emails and subdomains using search engine scraping. **Step 4 → Shodan / Censys** Search for Internet-exposed devices, open ports, and software banners (very **IoT-relevant** for PBCCT604). **Step 5 → Maltego / Recon-ng** Visually correlate all collected data points into a relationship graph for the final reconnaissance report. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Footprinting Tool Taxonomy (Hierarchical Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[FOOTPRINTING TOOLS]:::root A --> B[DOMAIN INTELLIGENCE]:::cat A --> C[NETWORK DISCOVERY]:::cat A --> D[OSINT COLLECTION]:::cat A --> E[VISUAL CORRELATION]:::cat B --> B1[WHOIS Lookup]:::tool B --> B2[DNS Tools dig nslookup]:::tool B --> B3[Sublist3r]:::tool B --> B4[dnsmap]:::tool C --> C1[Traceroute]:::tool C --> C2[Shodan]:::tool C --> C3[Censys]:::tool C --> C4[Netcraft]:::tool D --> D1[theHarvester]:::tool D --> D2[Google Dorks]:::tool D --> D3[Wayback Machine]:::tool D --> D4[FOCA Metadata]:::tool D --> D5[SpiderFoot]:::tool E --> E1[Maltego]:::tool E --> E2[Recon-ng]:::tool E --> E3[OSINT Framework]:::tool classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:3px classDef cat fill:#16213e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f3460,stroke-width:2px classDef tool fill:#0f3460,color:#e94560,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Footprinting Workflow / Kill Chain (Sequential Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[STEP 1
DOMAIN REGISTRAR]:::step --> S2[STEP 2
DNS RECORDS]:::step S2 --> S3[STEP 3
IP AND ASN MAPPING]:::step S3 --> S4[STEP 4
SUBDOMAIN ENUMERATION]:::step S4 --> S5[STEP 5
EMAIL AND EMPLOYEE OSINT]:::step S5 --> S6[STEP 6
IOT DEVICE DISCOVERY
SHODAN]:::step S6 --> S7[STEP 7
RECONNAISSANCE REPORT]:::step S7 --> S8[HANDOFF TO
MODULE 2 SCANNING]:::step classDef step fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Passive vs Active Footprinting Tool Distribution ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[TOOL CLASSIFICATION BY DETECTION RISK]:::root Q --> P[PASSIVE TOOLS
ZERO DETECTION RISK]:::passive Q --> R[ACTIVE TOOLS
TARGET LOGS ACTIVITY]:::active P --> P1[WHOIS]:::ptool P --> P2[Google Dorks]:::ptool P --> P3[theHarvester]:::ptool P --> P4[Shodan]:::ptool P --> P5[Maltego]:::ptool P --> P6[Wayback Machine]:::ptool P --> P7[Netcraft]:::ptool R --> R1[dig nslookup]:::atool R --> R2[DNS Zone Transfer AXFR]:::atool R --> R3[Traceroute]:::atool R --> R4[dnsmap Brute Force]:::atool R --> R5[SpiderFoot Active Mode]:::atool classDef root fill:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#22d3ee,stroke-width:3px classDef passive fill:#064e3b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#10b981,stroke-width:2px classDef active fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff,stroke:#f97316,stroke-width:2px classDef ptool fill:#065f46,color:#d1fae5,stroke:#34d399,stroke-width:1px classDef atool fill:#9a3412,color:#fed7aa,stroke:#fb923c,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Data Flow Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER PUBLIC OSINT SOURCES"] I1[DNS SERVERS
PORT 53]:::src I2[WHOIS SERVERS
PORT 43]:::src I3[SEARCH ENGINES
GOOGLE BING]:::src I4[WAYBACK ARCHIVES]:::src I5[SHODAN CENSYS DATABASES]:::src end subgraph PROC["PROCESSING LAYER TOOL ENGINES"] P1[WHOIS CLIENT]:::proc P2[dig ENGINE]:::proc P3[theHarvester SCRAPER]:::proc P4[MALTEGO TRANSFORMS]:::proc P5[SHODAN API PARSER]:::proc end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER INTELLIGENCE REPORT"] O1[IP AND ASN LIST]:::out O2[DNS RECORDS]:::out O3[EMAIL AND EMPLOYEE LIST]:::out O4[SUBDOMAIN MAP]:::out O5[EXPOSED IOT DEVICES]:::out O6[VISUAL RELATIONSHIP GRAPH]:::out end I1 --> P2 --> O2 I2 --> P1 --> O1 I3 --> P3 --> O3 I3 --> P3 --> O4 I4 --> P4 --> O6 I5 --> P5 --> O5 classDef src fill:#1e293b,color:#67e8f9,stroke:#06b6d4 classDef proc fill:#312e81,color:#c7d2fe,stroke:#6366f1 classDef out fill:#3f3f46,color:#fde047,stroke:#facc15 ``` ## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Tool Chain in a Realistic Engagement (Sequential Process Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ETHICAL HACKER]:::actor --> B[1 WHOIS]:::t1 B --> C[2 dig]:::t2 C --> D[3 theHarvester]:::t3 D --> E[4 Sublist3r]:::t4 E --> F[5 Shodan]:::t5 F --> G[6 Maltego]:::t6 G --> H[RECON REPORT]:::report classDef actor fill:#831843,color:#fce7f3,stroke:#f472b6,stroke-width:3px classDef t1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#60a5fa classDef t2 fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3b82f6 classDef t3 fill:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2563eb classDef t4 fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1d4ed8 classDef t5 fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e40af classDef t6 fill:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a classDef report fill:#fbbf24,color:#1a1a2e,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:3px ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Q1. Define Footprinting. List any four tools used for footprinting.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Footprinting is the process of gathering maximum information about the target system, network, or organization from publicly available sources (OSINT) during the reconnaissance phase of ethical hacking. It is the **first step** in the pre-attack phase and aims to create a complete profile of the target's external presence **without directly probing** the internal network. > **Four Tools (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each, any 4):** > 1. **WHOIS** — for domain registrar information > 2. **theHarvester** — for email and subdomain harvesting > 3. **Shodan** — for IoT and exposed device discovery > 4. **Maltego** — for visual relationship analysis > 5. **dig / nslookup** — for DNS record enumeration > 6. **Traceroute** — for network path mapping --- > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]** **Q2. Differentiate between Passive and Active Footprinting. Give two tool examples for each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Passive Footprinting | Active Footprinting | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Target Awareness | Target is **unaware** | Target's logs may record activity | | Tool Example 1 | **theHarvester** (1 Mark) | **Traceroute** (1 Mark) | | Tool Example 2 | **Shodan** (1 Mark) | **dig with AXFR** (1 Mark) | --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 1 ESE Pattern]** ### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Option 1) **Q.A.(a) Explain the concept of Footprinting in Ethical Hacking. With a neat block diagram, describe the various tools used for footprinting. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. Concept of Footprinting (3 Marks):** Footprinting is the **first phase of the Reconnaissance stage** in the ethical hacking methodology (as defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus for PBCCT604). It is the structured collection of information about a target — including **domain names, IP addresses, subdomains, email addresses, employee details, OS types, and network topology** — using **publicly available Open Source Intelligence (OSINT)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > The objective is to build a complete **attack surface map** of the target *before* engaging in any active scanning. Footprinting reduces the attacker's uncertainty from approximately **100%** (blind attack) to **near zero** (informed attack). **2. Tool Categories with Examples (4 Marks):** | Category | Tools (with one-line purpose) | Marks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Domain Intelligence** | WHOIS (registrar data), dig / nslookup (DNS records) | 1 | | **Network Discovery** | Traceroute (path), Shodan (IoT), Censys (certs) | 1 | | **Email & Subdomain** | theHarvester, Sublist3r, FOCA | 1 | | **Visual & Automated** | Maltego, Recon-ng, SpiderFoot | 1 | **Block Diagram (1 Mark — for diagram relevance):** ```mermaid flowchart TD A[FOOTPRINTING TOOLS]:::root A --> B[DOMAIN
WHOIS dig]:::c1 A --> C[NETWORK
Traceroute Shodan]:::c2 A --> D[OSINT
theHarvester Maltego]:::c3 A --> E[VISUAL
Maltego Recon-ng]:::c4 classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,color:#fff,stroke:#e94560 classDef c1 fill:#0f3460,color:#fff classDef c2 fill:#16213e,color:#fff classDef c3 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#fff classDef c4 fill:#312e81,color:#fff ``` **Conclusion (Bonus):** Footprinting is the **low-risk, high-value** foundation of every ethical hack; tools like WHOIS and theHarvester automate the otherwise tedious manual OSINT. --- **Q.A.(b) With suitable examples, explain the working of WHOIS lookup and theHarvester as footprinting tools. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. WHOIS Lookup (3.5 Marks):** - **Definition (1 Mark):** WHOIS is a **TCP-based query/response protocol (Port 43)** defined in **RFC 3912** that retrieves the **registration data** of a domain name, IP address block, or Autonomous System Number. - **Working Steps (1.5 Marks):** 1. Client queries the **IANA root WHOIS server** (`whois.iana.org`) 2. IANA redirects to the **authoritative TLD WHOIS server** (e.g., `.com` → `whois.verisign-grs.com`) 3. The TLD server redirects to the **registrar WHOIS server** 4. Registrar returns full record: **Registrant, Admin/Tech contact, Name Servers, Creation/Expiry dates** - **Example Output (1 Mark):** | Field | Value | | :--- | :--- | | Domain Name | EXAMPLE.COM | | Registrar | Example Registrar Inc. | | Name Server | NS1.EXAMPLE.COM | | Creation Date | 2000-01-01 | **2. theHarvester (3.5 Marks):** - **Definition (1 Mark):** theHarvester is a **Python-based OSINT tool** pre-installed in **Kali Linux** at `/usr/bin/theHarvester`. It harvests **emails, subdomains, hosts, employee names, and open ports** from multiple public search engines and SHODAN. - **Command Syntax (1 Mark):** $$\texttt{theHarvester -d target.com -b google -l 500 -f output.html}$$ - **Working Logic (1 Mark):** It issues multiple search engine queries (e.g., `site:target.com`, `inurl:target.com`) using **Google Dorks**, parses HTML responses using **regex patterns**, validates each host with **DNS resolution (`gethostbyname`)**, and exports a structured report (XML, HTML, JSON). - **Example Output (0.5 Mark):** | Type | Discovered | | :--- | :--- | | Email | ceo@target.com | | Subdomain | mail.target.com | | IP | 192.0.2.10 | --- ### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Option 2 / Internal Choice) **Q.B.(a) Discuss DNS footprinting in detail. Explain the role of dig, nslookup, and DNS zone transfers in the reconnaissance process. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. DNS Footprinting Overview (2 Marks):** DNS Footprinting is the process of querying **Domain Name System (Port UDP/TCP 53)** servers to extract information about a target's **domain names, IP addresses, mail servers, name servers, and subdomains**. It is one of the most **information-rich** active footprinting techniques. **2. The `dig` Utility (1.5 Marks):** - `dig` (Domain Information Groper) is a **BIND-utility CLI tool** used to query DNS name servers. - **Syntax:** `dig @nameserver domain type` (e.g., `dig @ns1.target.com target.com MX`) - **Capabilities:** Queries any record type (A, AAAA, MX, NS, TXT, SOA, CNAME, PTR) and supports **AXFR (zone transfer)**. **3. The `nslookup` Utility (1.5 Marks):** - Older than `dig`, available natively on **Windows and Linux**. - **Interactive mode:** `nslookup` → `server ns1.target.com` → `set type=any` → `target.com` - Primarily used for **quick A and MX lookups**; less verbose than dig. **4. DNS Zone Transfer (AXFR) (2 Marks):** - A **zone transfer** is a mechanism where a **secondary DNS server** synchronizes its zone data from the **primary server**. - **Command:** `dig @ns1.target.com target.com AXFR` - **Security Risk:** If misconfigured (i.e., `allow-transfer { any; }` in BIND config), **any external attacker** can request and receive the **entire zone file**, exposing every subdomain. - **Secure Configuration:** `allow-transfer { ; };` — restricted to authorized secondaries only. **Diagram (Bonus 0.5 Mark):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Attacker] -->|dig AXFR| B[Primary NS ns1] B -->|REFUSED Secure| A B -.->|Success Vulnerable| C[Full Zone File Leaked] classDef def fill:#1a1a2e,color:#fff,stroke:#e94560 ``` --- **Q.B.(b) Explain the role of Shodan, Maltego, and Google Dorks in passive footprinting. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. Shodan (2.5 Marks):** - **Definition (1 Mark):** Shodan is often called the **"search engine for hackers"** or the **"IoT search engine."** Unlike Google, which indexes web pages, Shodan indexes **Internet-connected devices, their banners, open ports, and software versions**. - **Working (1 Mark):** Shodan continuously scans the entire IPv4 Internet, captures **banner responses** (service identification strings), and indexes them. A query like `port:23 "IoT"` returns all **Telnet-exposed IoT devices** globally. - **Relevance to IoT Security (0.5 Mark):** In PBCCT604, Shodan is **critical** for identifying **exposed IoT devices** (cameras, routers, SCADA) — exactly the syllabus mandate for IoT Security. **2. Maltego (2.5 Marks):** - **Definition (1 Mark):** Maltego is a **commercial OSINT and graphical link-analysis tool** developed by **Paterva** (now part of Maltego Technologies). It is pre-installed in Kali Linux in a **Community Edition**. - **Working (1 Mark):** Maltego uses **"Transforms"** — small scripts that query public APIs (WHOIS, DNS, Shodan, social media) and represent the results as **nodes in a graph**. A typical investigation starts with a **domain entity** and expands outward into **emails, IPs, ASN, and social media profiles**. - **Output (0.5 Mark):** A **visual relationship graph** showing all linked entities — this is the deliverable of the footprinting phase. **3. Google Dorks (2 Marks):** - **Definition (1 Mark):** **Google Dorking** (a.k.a. Google Hacking) refers to the use of **advanced search operators** in Google/Bing to find **sensitive information that was unintentionally exposed** on the public Internet. - **Common Operators (0.5 Mark):** - `site:` — restrict to a domain - `filetype:` — find specific file types (e.g., `filetype:pdf`) - `inurl:` / `intitle:` — keyword in URL/title - `intext:` — keyword in body text - **Example Dorks (0.5 Mark):** - `site:target.com filetype:pdf "confidential"` → finds leaked PDFs - `inurl:admin login site:target.com` → finds exposed admin panels - `intitle:"index of" "password"` → finds open directory listings --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Tool Port Confusion:** Students often write "WHOIS uses Port 53" — WRONG. **Port 43 (TCP)** for WHOIS, **Port 53 (UDP/TCP)** for DNS. Mixing these costs 1 full mark. > 2. **Passive vs Active Confusion:** Writing "theHarvester is an active tool" is wrong — it uses **Google's public search** and never touches the target, hence **passive**. > 3. **Missing Diagram:** KTU awards **1–2 marks** for neat block diagrams in 7-mark sub-questions. Always include a **Mermaid/flowchart** showing tool categories. > 4. **No Examples:** Listing tools without a single **command syntax** or **output sample** will cap you at ~60% of marks. Always pair every tool with **at least one command or output snippet**. > 5. **Forgetting IoT Context:** This is **PBCCT604 — Ethical Hacking AND IoT Security**. Whenever discussing Shodan or Censys, explicitly tie it back to **IoT device exposure**. Examiners reward IoT-context answers. > 6. **Protocol Layer Mismatch:** Don't say "WHOIS works at Layer 3." It is an **application-layer (Layer 7)** protocol. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Read Before Every Exam:** - ✅ **Footprinting** = Phase 1 of Reconnaissance; uses **OSINT** (public data). - ✅ **Two types:** **Passive** (target unaware) vs **Active** (target may log). - ✅ **WHOIS** uses **TCP Port 43** (RFC 3912). Output: registrant, NS, dates. - ✅ **DNS** uses **UDP/TCP Port 53**. **AXFR** = zone transfer; misconfiguration = full zone leak. - ✅ **`dig`** is preferred over `nslookup` for advanced queries. - ✅ **theHarvester** flag set: `$-d$` (domain), `$-b$` (source), `$-l$` (limit), `$-f$` (file output). - ✅ **Maltego** = visual OSINT graph; uses **Transforms**. - ✅ **Recon-ng** = modular web-recon framework. - ✅ **Shodan** = IoT / device search engine — **crucial for PBCCT604's IoT Security angle**. - ✅ **Censys** = similar to Shodan, with stronger **certificate indexing**. - ✅ **Google Dorks** = advanced operators (`site:`, `filetype:`, `inurl:`, `intitle:`, `intext:`). - ✅ **Wayback Machine** = historical website snapshots. - ✅ **Sublist3r** = subdomain enumeration via search engine scraping. - ✅ **FOCA** = metadata extraction from documents (PDF, DOCX, XLSX). - ✅ **SpiderFoot** = automated OSINT across **200+ data sources**. - ✅ **Netcraft** = web technology fingerprinting (server OS, CMS, SSL). - ✅ **Traceroute** = maps network path; outputs TTL and hop IPs. - ✅ **TTL clue:** 64 = Linux, 128 = Windows, 255 = Cisco/Network. - ✅ **CIDR formula:** Usable hosts = $2^{(32-n)} - 2$ where $n$ = prefix length. - ✅ **Email patterns** to test: `firstname.lastname@`, `flastname@`, `firstname@`. - ✅ **Default tool location in Kali:** `/usr/bin/theHarvester`, `/usr/bin/maltego`, `/usr/bin/recon-ng`. - ✅ **Footprinting output feeds Module 2 (Scanning Networks)** — mention the handoff in answers. - ✅ **IoT Context:** Always link Shodan/Censys findings to **IoT device exposure** for PBCCT604.

topics.title: Conducting Competitive Intelligence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conducting Competitive Intelligence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Competitive Intelligence (CI)** is the systematic, ethical, and legal collection, analysis, and dissemination of information about a target organization's competitors, products, customers, technologies, and overall business environment to support strategic decision-making and threat modeling in the context of ethical hacking and penetration testing.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context of *Ethical Hacking & IoT Security*, Competitive Intelligence is positioned as a **passive reconnaissance** technique under **Module 1 (Footprinting & Reconnaissance)**. It involves gathering actionable intelligence about rival organizations, market players, and the broader threat landscape **without directly probing the target**, using only **Open Source Intelligence (OSINT)** channels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PBCCT604 – Module 1):**
> Competitive Intelligence is grouped alongside *Footprinting*, *Google Hacking*, and *People Search* as a **passive information-gathering methodology**. It is strictly **bounded by legal and ethical frameworks** — any breach converts it into *industrial espionage* or *unauthorized reconnaissance*, both of which fall under the **IT Act 2000 (India) / CFAA (US)** and are examinable as examiner-favorite pitfalls.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> 🕵️ **Real-World Analogy — "The Chess Grandmaster's Pre-Match Study"**
>
> Imagine a chess grandmaster preparing for a championship match. Before moving a single piece, the grandmaster studies the opponent's previous **500 games**, notes their **opening preferences**, identifies **recurring weaknesses** (e.g., weak kingside defence), and analyzes the **coach's strategic philosophy**. The grandmaster never breaks into the opponent's hotel room to read their diary — that would be cheating and illegal. Instead, every insight comes from **publicly broadcast tournaments, interviews, and published game records**.
>
> **Competitive Intelligence in Ethical Hacking is exactly this chess preparation applied to cyber targets:**
> - The *opponent* = the target organization and its competitive landscape.
> - The *public tournaments* = the company's website, social media, job postings, patents, press releases.
> - The *chess notation* = structured data on technologies, personnel, and infrastructure.
> - The *cheating* = unauthorized scanning, social engineering for passwords, or insider theft (these are *illegal reconnaissance*, **not** CI).

The grandmaster gains a **strategic edge** without touching the opponent. Similarly, an ethical hacker uses CI to map the **attack surface** and **threat vectors** of a target's ecosystem purely from public data, allowing blue teams to **proactively defend** before adversaries exploit the same publicly visible weaknesses.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundaries

The following standardized terms are **bold-marked** for examiner priority:

| Term | Definition | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **OSINT (Open Source Intelligence)** | Intelligence derived from publicly available sources | Core mechanism of CI |
| **Passive Reconnaissance** | Data collection without direct interaction with the target | CI falls under this category |
| **Attack Surface** | The sum of all points (digital, physical, human) where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter | Product of CI analysis |
| **Threat Vector** | The path or means by which an attacker gains unauthorized access | Output of CI-driven threat modeling |
| **OPSEC (Operational Security)** | Process of preventing observable adversarial intelligence gathering | Defensive counter to CI |
| **Covert Channel** | A hidden, unauthorized communication path | Detected via deep CI analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Legal Boundary Reminder:** Competitive Intelligence is legal **only when** confined to publicly available, ethically obtainable data. The moment CI crosses into *dumpster diving without permission*, *social engineering for credentials*, or *unauthorized system probing*, it becomes **illegal reconnaissance** under **Section 66 of the Indian IT Act 2000** and the **Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of Competitive Intelligence

Competitive Intelligence, as prescribed in the **KTU 2024 ethical hacking framework**, follows a **five-phase structured methodology**. Each phase must be executed in sequence to ensure that the intelligence produced is **accurate, actionable, and legally defensible**.

### Phase 1 — Planning & Direction
- **Why:** Define the *intelligence requirement* — what specific question must CI answer?
- **How:** Identify the target organization, its industry competitors, the scope of intelligence (technical, strategic, personnel), and the Key Intelligence Topics (KITs).
- **Example KITs:** *"What CDN does Company X use?"* / *"Who is Company X's cloud provider?"* / *"What vulnerabilities exist in Company X's recent IoT product?"*

### Phase 2 — Source Identification
- **Why:** Raw data is useless without a reliable source taxonomy.
- **How:** Classify sources into **primary (direct from target)** and **secondary (third-party aggregators)**.
- **Primary sources:** Company website, blog, social media, press releases, patent filings, RFCs, job postings.
- **Secondary sources:** Crunchbase, LinkedIn, Shodan, Censys, WHOIS, Google Dorks, archive.org (Wayback Machine).

### Phase 3 — Data Collection (Ethical & Legal Only)
- **Why:** To assemble raw intelligence while maintaining the *ethical ceiling*.
- **How:** Use OSINT tools, manual browsing, and analyst-driven correlation. **Never** bypass authentication, scrape private data, or interact with target systems in a probing manner.

### Phase 4 — Analysis & Synthesis
- **Why:** Raw data ≠ intelligence. Intelligence is *data with context and meaning*.
- **How:** Apply **SWOT analysis** (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats), **PESTLE analysis** (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental), and **threat-actor profiling** to convert raw data into strategic insight.

### Phase 5 — Dissemination & Feedback
- **Why:** Intelligence that does not reach decision-makers is wasted effort.
- **How:** Produce a **CI Report** with structured findings, confidence levels, and recommended defensive actions. Integrate feedback loops to refine future CI cycles.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While Competitive Intelligence is largely qualitative, several **quantitative scoring models** and **boundary equations** are examinable. All symbols are explicitly defined to avoid ambiguity.

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Attack Surface Score (ASS)** | $ASS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot E_i$ | dimensionless | Quantifies total exposure |
| **Information Confidence Level (ICL)** | $ICL = \frac{V_c}{V_c + V_i}$ | ratio $\in [0, 1]$ | Measures data reliability |
| **Source Reliability Rating (SRR)** | $SRR \in \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ | ordinal | A=Completely reliable, F=Unreliable |
| **Information Content Rating (ICR)** | $ICR \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ | ordinal | 1=Confirmed, 6=Cannot be judged |
| **CI Cycle Time (T_CI)** | $T_{CI} = T_p + T_s + T_c + T_a + T_d$ | hours | Total operational duration |
| **Reconnaissance Risk Index (RRI)** | $RRI = \frac{D_{detected}}{D_{total}} \times 100$ | percentage | Stealth of CI operation |
| **OSINT Coverage Ratio (OCR)** | $OCR = \frac{S_{gathered}}{S_{estimated}}$ | ratio $\in [0, 1]$ | Completeness of collection |

### Definition of Variables

- $w_i$ = weight assigned to exposure category $i$ (e.g., domain $= 0.3$, personnel $= 0.4$, technology $= 0.3$)
- $E_i$ = exposure value for category $i$, normalized to $[0, 1]$
- $V_c$ = number of verified data points
- $V_i$ = number of inconsistent or unverified data points
- $D_{detected}$ = number of CI actions detected by target's defensive systems
- $D_{total}$ = total CI actions performed
- $S_{gathered}$ = sources successfully mined
- $S_{estimated}$ = total estimated relevant sources

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Competitive Intelligence is not an academic exercise — it powers **three critical engineering workflows** in modern cybersecurity:

1. **Red Team Operations:** A red team uses CI to identify *which third-party vendor* the target depends on, then maps supply-chain attack vectors (e.g., the **2020 SolarWinds breach** was facilitated by exactly this kind of CI-derived insight).
2. **Blue Team Threat Modeling:** Blue teams run CI against their *own* organization to discover what adversaries can see. This feeds the **MITRE ATT&CK** pre-attack tactic taxonomy (TA0043: Reconnaissance).
3. **IoT Security Posture Assessment:** For IoT products, CI reveals the *firmware update mechanism*, *default credentials published in manuals*, *open MQTT brokers*, and *vendor support forums* — all of which are high-fidelity attack surface data.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Cross-Reference:** Map the five CI phases against **MITRE ATT&CK T1590–T1595** (Gather Victim Network/Host/Domain Information) to earn extra marks in Part B answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Attack Surface Score (ASS)

The Attack Surface Score quantifies the cumulative exposure of an organization across multiple intelligence categories. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Categorize Exposure Vectors

We partition an organization's total digital exposure into $n$ mutually exclusive categories:

$$n = \{Domain, Network, Personnel, Technology, Physical, Third\text{-}Party\}$$

This gives $n = 6$ canonical categories used in KTU board examinations.

### Step 2 — Assign Normalized Exposure Values

For each category $i$, the analyst assigns $E_i \in [0, 1]$ representing the *relative discoverability* of exploitable information:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{domain} &= \frac{\text{DNS records exposed}}{\text{Total estimated DNS records}} \\
E_{network} &= \frac{\text{Open ports mapped}}{\text{Estimated port range}} \\
E_{personnel} &= \frac{\text{Employees profiled}}{\text{Total headcount}} \\
E_{technology} &= \frac{\text{Tech stack elements identified}}{\text{Total estimated elements}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Strategic Weights

The strategic weight $w_i$ reflects the *impact* of exposure in that category. A standard KTU-aligned weighting is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{domain} &= 0.20 \\
w_{network} &= 0.25 \\
w_{personnel} &= 0.20 \\
w_{technology} &= 0.20 \\
w_{physical} &= 0.05 \\
w_{third\text{-}party} &= 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

The constraint $\sum w_i = 1.0$ ensures normalization.

### Step 4 — Compute the Final ASS

$$
ASS = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot E_i
$$

**Worked Example:**

Suppose an organization's CI assessment yields $E_{domain} = 0.80$, $E_{network} = 0.60$, $E_{personnel} = 0.70$, $E_{technology} = 0.90$, $E_{physical} = 0.30$, $E_{third\text{-}party} = 0.50$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ASS &= (0.20)(0.80) + (0.25)(0.60) + (0.20)(0.70) + (0.20)(0.90) + (0.05)(0.30) + (0.10)(0.50) \\
&= 0.160 + 0.150 + 0.140 + 0.180 + 0.015 + 0.050 \\
ASS &= 0.695
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $ASS = 0.695$ indicates **high exposure** (threshold for concern is typically $ASS \geq 0.50$). The blue team must prioritize remediation of the *Technology* and *Domain* categories first.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — CI Source Classification

The following matrix maps **CI sources** to **intelligence type** and **legal status** — a common KTU 14-mark question structure.

| Source Category | Specific Source | Intelligence Type | Legal Status | Example Finding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Corporate | Company Website | Strategic | ✅ Legal | Product roadmap hints |
| Corporate | Press Releases | Strategic | ✅ Legal | New partnership disclosures |
| Corporate | Patents (USPTO, IPO) | Technical | ✅ Legal | Algorithm details |
| Human | LinkedIn Profiles | Personnel | ✅ Legal (public profiles) | CIO uses Cisco ASA firewalls |
| Human | Conference Talks | Technical | ✅ Legal | Architecture diagrams |
| Technical | WHOIS Lookup | Domain | ✅ Legal | Registrar and admin contact |
| Technical | Shodan / Censys | Network | ✅ Legal | Open port 22 on edge devices |
| Technical | Google Dorks | Cross-domain | ✅ Legal | Exposed admin panels |
| Technical | Wayback Machine | Historical | ✅ Legal | Removed credentials in old pages |
| Third-Party | Crunchbase | Financial | ✅ Legal | Funding rounds and investors |
| Third-Party | Glassdoor | Personnel morale | ✅ Legal (aggregated) | Tech stack mentions in reviews |
| **Forbidden** | **Dumpster Diving (no permit)** | **Physical** | ❌ **Illegal** | Discarded hardware |
| **Forbidden** | **Pretext Calling for passwords** | **Personnel** | ❌ **Illegal** | Social engineering bypass |

## 3.3 Derivation of Information Confidence Level (ICL)

The ICL is the ratio of *verified* to *total* data points. Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ICL &= \frac{V_c}{V_c + V_i} \\
\text{Where: } V_c + V_i &= V_{total} \\
\text{Therefore: } ICL &= \frac{V_c}{V_{total}} \\
\text{Boundary: } ICL &\in [0, 1] \\
ICL = 1 &\Rightarrow \text{All data verified} \\
ICL = 0 &\Rightarrow \text{No data verified}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Examiner Note:** When answering Part B questions, always state the ICL boundary conditions explicitly. Many students lose **2 marks** for omitting these.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Competitive Intelligence Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CI Trigger: Strategic Need] --> B[Phase 1: Planning and Direction]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Source Identification]
    C --> D[Phase 3: Ethical Data Collection]
    D --> E[Phase 4: Analysis and Synthesis]
    E --> F[Phase 5: Dissemination and Feedback]
    F --> G[CI Report and Actionable Insight]
    G --> H{Decision Maker}
    H --> I[Red Team Attack Path]
    H --> J[Blue Team Defense Plan]
    H --> K[IoT Posture Update]
    F --> L[Feedback Loop]
    L --> B
    D --> M{Legal and Ethical Check}
    M -->|Pass| E
    M -->|Fail| N[Halt Operation Illegal Reconnaissance]
    N --> O[Document and Report Violation]
```

## 4.2 CI Source Taxonomy — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PrimarySources[Primary Sources Direct from Target]
        P1[Company Website]
        P2[Social Media Accounts]
        P3[Press Releases]
        P4[Patent Filings]
        P5[Job Postings]
    end
    subgraph SecondarySources[Secondary Sources Third Party Aggregators]
        S1[WHOIS and DNS Databases]
        S2[Shodan and Censys]
        S3[LinkedIn Public Profiles]
        S4[Crunchbase Financial Data]
        S5[Wayback Machine Archives]
        S6[Google Dorks and Search Engines]
    end
    subgraph AnalysisLayer[Analysis Layer]
        A1[SWOT Analysis]
        A2[PESTLE Analysis]
        A3[MITRE ATT and CK Mapping]
        A4[Threat Actor Profiling]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        O1[Attack Surface Score]
        O2[Threat Vector Map]
        O3[CI Confidence Report]
    end
    PrimarySources --> AnalysisLayer
    SecondarySources --> AnalysisLayer
    AnalysisLayer --> OutputLayer
```

## 4.3 CI Ethical Boundary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Competitive Intelligence Activity] --> Q1{Is the data source public?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is access authorized?}
    Q1 -->|No| Illegal1[Illegal: Private Data Access]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Does it bypass security controls?}
    Q2 -->|No| Illegal2[Illegal: Unauthorized Access]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Illegal3[Illegal: Security Bypass]
    Q3 -->|No| Legal[Ethical CI Compliant with IT Act 2000]
    Legal --> Report[Document and Proceed]
    Illegal1 --> Report2[Halt and Report to Compliance]
    Illegal2 --> Report2
    Illegal3 --> Report2
```

## 4.4 CI Phases vs MITRE ATT&CK Tactics Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CI Phase 1 Planning] --> M1[TA0042 Resource Development]
    B[CI Phase 2 Source ID] --> M2[TA0043 Reconnaissance]
    C[CI Phase 3 Collection] --> M3[T1590 Gather Victim Network Info]
    D[CI Phase 4 Analysis] --> M4[T1592 Gather Victim Host Info]
    E[CI Phase 5 Dissemination] --> M5[TA0044 Develop Capabilities]
    C --> M6[T1589 Identify Victim Personnel]
    C --> M7[T1591 Gather Victim Org Info]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Competitive Intelligence in the context of ethical hacking. Mention any two primary sources of CI. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Competitive Intelligence (CI) is the systematic and ethical collection and analysis of publicly available information about an organization's competitors, products, technologies, and personnel to support strategic threat modeling and penetration testing decisions.

> **Two Primary Sources (2 Marks = 1 Mark each):**
> 1. **Company Website and Press Releases** — Reveal strategic direction, product launches, partnership announcements, and occasionally technology stack hints embedded in technical blogs.
> 2. **Patent Filings (USPTO / Indian Patent Office)** — Disclose detailed algorithmic and architectural information, often years before product release, providing high-fidelity technical intelligence.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Competitive Intelligence and Industrial Espionage. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Competitive Intelligence | Industrial Espionage |
|---|---|---|
| **Legality** | Fully legal | Illegal under IT Act 2000, CFAA |
| **Source** | Public OSINT only | Private, stolen, covert |
| **Method** | Ethical, transparent | Deceptive, covert, trespassing |
| **Consent** | No consent needed (public data) | Violates consent and privacy |
| **Example** | Reading published patents | Dumpster diving, pretexting |

*(2 marks for the tabular differentiation + 1 mark for the example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question is split into sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the five phases of Competitive Intelligence methodology. How does each phase contribute to building a complete threat profile of a target organization? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

The five phases of Competitive Intelligence methodology are:

**1. Planning and Direction (1.5 Marks)**
This phase defines the Key Intelligence Topics (KITs). The ethical hacker identifies *what* information is needed (e.g., cloud provider, IoT firmware, employee email patterns). Clear KITs prevent scope creep and ensure that CI remains legally bounded. The output is a **CI Requirements Document** that serves as the operational charter.

**2. Source Identification (1 Mark)**
Sources are classified into *primary* (direct from the target) and *secondary* (third-party aggregators). For example, the company website is primary; Crunchbase and LinkedIn are secondary. The analyst maps each KIT to its optimal source.

**3. Data Collection (1.5 Marks)**
Using only ethical OSINT tools (WHOIS, Shodan, Google Dorks, theHarvester, Maltego), raw data is collected. The cardinal rule: **never probe the target directly**. Every action must be passive and non-intrusive.

**4. Analysis and Synthesis (2 Marks)**
Raw data is converted into intelligence using structured analytical frameworks:
- **SWOT Analysis** — maps internal strengths/weaknesses and external opportunities/threats.
- **PESTLE Analysis** — contextualizes macro-environmental factors.
- **MITRE ATT&CK Mapping** — aligns findings to adversary tactics.
The output is the **Attack Surface Score (ASS)** and prioritized **Threat Vector Map**.

**5. Dissemination and Feedback (1 Mark)**
The final CI Report is delivered to decision-makers (red team lead, CISO, blue team). A feedback loop refines future CI cycles.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the legal and ethical boundaries of Competitive Intelligence. With reference to the IT Act 2000 (India), explain why unauthorized reconnaissance is punishable. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Ethical Boundaries (3 Marks):**
1. **Source Legitimacy:** Only publicly available data is permissible. Sources must be open, legal, and ethically obtainable.
2. **Non-Intrusion:** The CI operator must not interact with target systems in a probing manner. Any port scanning, vulnerability scanning, or social engineering crosses the ethical line.
3. **Data Minimization:** Collect only what is needed for the defined KITs. Avoid hoarding personal data.
4. **Transparency & Accountability:** CI activities must be documented, auditable, and conducted under a formal mandate (e.g., penetration test authorization letter).

**Legal Boundaries under IT Act 2000 (3 Marks):**
- **Section 43:** Damages to computer systems — applies to unauthorized access even for reconnaissance.
- **Section 66:** Computer-related offences — *"Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently does any act referred to in Section 43, shall be punishable."* Unauthorized scanning qualifies.
- **Section 66E:** Violation of privacy — capturing private images or data without consent.
- **Section 72:** Breach of confidentiality — accessing data without authorization.

**Punishment Consequences (1 Mark):**
Penalties include imprisonment up to **3 years** and/or fines up to **₹5 lakh** (Section 66). Civil liability under Section 43A may apply for negligence involving sensitive personal data.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the role of Competitive Intelligence in IoT security assessment. Identify at least four IoT-specific OSINT sources and explain their intelligence value. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Role of CI in IoT Security Assessment (3 Marks):**
IoT devices expand the attack surface through diverse protocols (MQTT, CoAP, Zigbee, LoRaWAN), constrained firmware, and weak update mechanisms. CI enables ethical hackers to **map this surface passively** before active testing. Specifically, CI:
1. Identifies exposed IoT management interfaces (e.g., public MQTT brokers).
2. Discovers default credentials published in vendor manuals.
3. Maps firmware update endpoints and OTA mechanisms.
4. Profiles the vendor's third-party component supply chain.

**Four IoT-Specific OSINT Sources (4 Marks = 1 Mark each):**

| OSINT Source | Intelligence Value |
|---|---|
| **Shodan.io** | Discovers internet-connected IoT devices, exposed ports, banner information, and default credentials. |
| **Censys.io** | Provides certificate transparency data and IoT device fingerprints across the IPv4 space. |
| **MQTT Broker Indexers (e.g., mqtt-broker-finder)** | Identifies open MQTT brokers leaking IoT telemetry. |
| **Vendor FCC Filings** | Reveals internal photographs, block diagrams, and chipset identifiers of IoT hardware. |

Additional valid sources include: **Thingful**, **CVE databases (NVD)**, **GitHub repos for leaked firmware**, and **Insecam** for exposed camera feeds.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A cybersecurity team uses the Attack Surface Score formula to evaluate a target organization. Given: $E_{domain} = 0.75$, $E_{network} = 0.60$, $E_{personnel} = 0.80$, $E_{technology} = 0.85$, $E_{physical} = 0.20$, $E_{third\text{-}party} = 0.55$, with weights $w = \{0.20, 0.25, 0.20, 0.20, 0.05, 0.10\}$. Compute the ASS, interpret the result, and recommend two defensive actions. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Numerical]`**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — State the Formula (1 Mark):**

$$ASS = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot E_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ASS &= (0.20)(0.75) + (0.25)(0.60) + (0.20)(0.80) + (0.20)(0.85) + (0.05)(0.20) + (0.10)(0.55) \\
&= 0.150 + 0.150 + 0.160 + 0.170 + 0.010 + 0.055
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Result (1 Mark):**

$$ASS = 0.695$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 Marks):**
The ASS of **0.695** exceeds the standard high-exposure threshold of **0.50**, indicating that the target organization has a **substantial attack surface** in the digital domain. The highest contributors are the *Technology* ($0.170$) and *Personnel* ($0.160$) categories, which together account for **47.5%** of the total exposure.

**Step 5 — Two Defensive Actions (1 Mark):**
1. **Personnel OPSEC Training:** Since personnel exposure is high, the blue team should implement a security awareness program, sanitize LinkedIn profiles, and train employees against social engineering.
2. **Technology Stack Hardening:** Mask server banners, hide version headers, and use Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) to reduce technology fingerprinting by Shodan and Censys scans.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Competitive Intelligence Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating CI with active scanning (–2 marks):** Students often write that CI includes *Nmap port scans* or *Nessus vulnerability scans*. This is factually wrong — these are **active reconnaissance** techniques, not CI. CI is strictly **passive and OSINT-based**.
>
> 2. **Omitting the legal caveat (–2 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly test whether students can articulate the **IT Act 2000 / CFAA** boundary. A CI definition without the *legal and ethical* qualifier is incomplete.
>
> 3. **Skipping weight justification in numerical problems (–1 mark):** When computing ASS, students must state *why* weights are assigned and verify $\sum w_i = 1$. Skipping this loses easy marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing primary vs secondary sources (–1 mark):** A common error is listing *Shodan* as a primary source. Shodan is a **secondary aggregator**; the target's own website is primary.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the feedback loop (–1 mark):** The fifth phase (Dissemination and Feedback) is frequently omitted. Always mention that CI is a **cyclical, iterative process**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Conducting Competitive Intelligence**

- ✅ CI is a **passive, OSINT-based reconnaissance** method — *never* active scanning.
- ✅ The **five phases** are: *Planning → Source ID → Collection → Analysis → Dissemination + Feedback*.
- ✅ **Legality** is defined by **public accessibility** + **ethical obtainability** + **non-intrusive access**.
- ✅ **Illegal CI** is punishable under **IT Act 2000 Sections 43, 66, 66E, 72** and the **CFAA (US)**.
- ✅ Primary sources: *Website, press releases, patents, job postings, social media.*
- ✅ Secondary sources: *WHOIS, Shodan, Censys, Crunchbase, Wayback Machine, Google Dorks.*
- ✅ The **Attack Surface Score** formula is $ASS = \sum w_i \cdot E_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- ✅ The **ICL** formula is $ICL = V_c / (V_c + V_i)$, bounded in $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ In **IoT contexts**, CI must include *Shodan, Censys, MQTT broker indexers, FCC filings*.
- ✅ Map CI to **MITRE ATT&CK TA0043 (Reconnaissance)** for examiner bonus marks.
- ✅ Key frameworks: **SWOT**, **PESTLE**, **OPSEC**, **CIA Triad** — be ready to name-drop in any CI answer.
- ✅ Always close your answer with: *"CI is bounded by the legal-ethical ceiling; crossing it converts the activity into industrial espionage."*

> **Final Mantra for KTU 2024 Examiners:** *"Show me that you understand the boundary between intelligence and intrusion — that's where the marks live."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Google Hacking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Google Hacking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Google Hacking** (also known as *Google Dorking*) is the practice of using advanced and specially crafted search operators in Google Search Engine queries to locate security vulnerabilities, misconfigured servers, exposed sensitive documents, login portals, default credentials, and other hidden information that is publicly indexed but not easily discoverable through standard search queries.

In the context of **Ethical Hacking**, Google Hacking is classified as a **passive reconnaissance** technique under the *Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT)* umbrella, because the attacker never directly interacts with the target system. Instead, they leverage the vast search index already maintained by Google's web crawlers (Googlebot) to extract intelligence that the target organization has inadvertently exposed to the public internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBCCT604 – Module 1):** Google Hacking refers to the use of advanced search engine operators (Google Dorks) to extract sensitive information, vulnerable targets, and exposed infrastructure. It is a foundational OSINT methodology in the **Information Gathering / Footprinting** phase of ethical hacking.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a giant, ultra-organized public library (the internet) where every book, every diary, every spreadsheet, and every locked filing cabinet (web servers) has been catalogued by a meticulous librarian (Googlebot) into a massive index card system. 

- **Normal User**: Walks in and asks, *"Do you have any books about cats?"*
- **Google Hacker**: Knows the librarian's shorthand code system and asks, *"Do you have any unlocked filing cabinet receipts, where the author name contains 'password', the title starts with 'admin', and the document type is 'XLS', filed after the year 2020?"*

The librarian (Google) says, *"Yes, here are 4,217 matches."* 

**The key insight**: The information was *always* publicly accessible — Google did not break into anything. The Google Hacker simply knew *how to ask* in the search engine's own language. The **vulnerability lies in misconfiguration by the data owner**, not in Google itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The term was popularized by security researcher **Johnny Long** in the early 2000s, who later founded the **Google Hacking Database (GHDB)** at `https://www.exploit-db.com/google-hacking-database`, which catalogues thousands of useful dorks.

## 1.3 Core Search Operators — The Google Hacker's Vocabulary

| Operator | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `site:` | Restricts search to a specific domain | `site:ktu.edu.in` |
| `filetype:` | Searches for a specific file extension | `filetype:pdf` |
| `ext:` | Alias for `filetype:` | `ext:sql` |
| `inurl:` | Searches query term inside the URL string | `inurl:admin` |
| `intitle:` | Searches query term inside the page title | `intitle:"index of"` |
| `intext:` | Searches query term inside the page body | `intext:"password"` |
| `cache:` | Displays Google's cached version of a page | `cache:ktu.edu.in` |
| `link:` | Finds pages linking to a given URL | `link:example.com` |
| `info:` | Shows summary information about a page | `info:example.com` |
| `related:` | Finds similar pages | `related:example.com` |
| `-` (minus) | Excludes a term from results | `admin -login` |
| `*` (wildcard) | Acts as any matching character | `"* password *"` |
| `OR` | Logical OR between terms | `admin OR administrator` |
| `"..."` | Exact phrase matching | `"DB Username"` |

## 1.4 What an Attacker Aims to Find

- 📄 **Sensitive Documents**: `.pdf`, `.doc`, `.xls` containing financial data, employee lists
- 🔐 **Login Portals**: Admin panels, database login screens
- 🛢️ **Database Dumps**: Exposed `.sql`, `.csv` files
- 📹 **IoT Devices**: Open webcams, routers, SCADA systems
- 🗂️ **Directory Listings**: Apache/Nginx `Index of /` pages revealing file structure
- 🔑 **Credentials & Keys**: Leaked API keys, SSH private keys, passwords in config files
- 🖥️ **Vulnerable Servers**: Default installations of known vulnerable software
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of a Google Hacking Attack

Google Hacking is executed in a structured, repeatable methodology. A KTU-style answer should always present it as a phased operation.

### Phase 1: Target Selection & Domain Isolation
- The attacker identifies the target organization's primary domain (e.g., `target.com`).
- Sub-domains are enumerated using the `site:` operator combined with negative search filters:
  - `site:target.com -www` reveals non-www subdomains.

### Phase 2: Operator Chaining (Dork Construction)
- Multiple operators are combined to refine the search.
- The **AND logic** is implicit — space-separated terms must all match.
- The **OR logic** must be explicit using the uppercase `OR` operator.
- Exclusion is achieved with the `-` prefix.

### Phase 3: Result Triage & Validation
- Each result is manually inspected to confirm sensitivity.
- False positives are filtered (e.g., tutorials *discussing* the topic, not actual exposures).

### Phase 4: Evidence Collection & Reporting
- The findings are documented with screenshots, URLs, timestamps, and risk classifications.
- In ethical hacking, this is reported back to the client as part of the penetration test deliverable.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Google Hacking — Information Leakage Theory

The success of Google Hacking depends on three underlying conditions, all of which must be present simultaneously:

1. **Indexability**: The target page or file must be crawlable by Googlebot. This is controlled by the `robots.txt` file, meta tags, and HTTP headers.
2. **Lack of Authentication**: The resource must be accessible without a username/password.
3. **Discoverability via Pattern**: The resource must contain predictable text patterns (e.g., "Index of /", "Login", "password=") that allow an operator-based query to surface it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Insight:** If we model Google's index as a set $I$ containing $N$ documents, and each Google Dork $D_i$ is a filter function $f_i : I \rightarrow I'$, then Google Hacking is the iterative application:
> $$I_{final} = f_n(f_{n-1}(\cdots f_1(I)))$$
> where $I_{final}$ represents the leaked intelligence set.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Google Dork Categories

| Category | Typical Dork Example | What It Finds |
|----------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Directory Listings** | `intitle:"index of" "parent directory"` | Open file directories |
| **Login Portals** | `intitle:"admin login" inurl:admin` | Admin authentication pages |
| **Exposed Documents** | `site:gov.in filetype:pdf confidential` | Leaked PDF documents |
| **Database Files** | `filetype:sql "INSERT INTO" -site:github.com` | Exported SQL dumps |
| **Config Files** | `filetype:env intext:DB_PASSWORD` | Exposed environment configs |
| **Cameras & IoT** | `inurl:"/view.shtml" "Network Camera"` | Unsecured webcams |
| **Email Harvesting** | `intext:"@" site:target.com` | Employee email addresses |
| **Vulnerable Software** | `"Powered by phpMyAdmin" "Welcome to"` | Default phpMyAdmin installs |
| **Backup Files** | `filetype:bak inurl:backup` | Old backup archives |
| **SSH Keys** | `filetype:pem "BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY"` | Exposed private keys |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Penetration Testers**: Use Google Hacking as the *first* reconnaissance step to map a client's external attack surface.
- **Bug Bounty Hunters**: Discover exposed assets that fall within the program's scope for legal disclosure and reward.
- **Red Teams**: Build target lists for further exploitation stages.
- **Blue Teams & Defenders**: Continuously run Google Dorks *against their own organization* (a practice called **Google Dork Self-Assessment**) to detect unintended exposures before adversaries do.
- **OSINT Analysts**: Aggregate leaked information for threat intelligence reports.
- **IoT Security**: Discover default-credentialed routers, smart cameras, and SCADA dashboards that may be reachable from the public internet.

## 2.5 Defense Against Google Hacking (The Ethical Defender's Mandate)

| Defense Layer | Implementation |
|---------------|----------------|
| `robots.txt` | Use `Disallow: /private/` to instruct crawlers (advisory only, not enforced) |
| `noindex` Meta Tag | `<meta name="robots" content="noindex, nofollow">` |
| HTTP Headers | `X-Robots-Tag: noindex` |
| Authentication | Place all sensitive directories behind login walls |
| `.htaccess` | Use `Header set X-Robots-Tag "noindex"` |
| Google Search Console | Use **URL Removal Tool** to request de-indexing |
| Periodic Dorking | Run periodic Google Dork scans against your own domain |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Constructing a Google Dork — Full Walkthrough

A KTU examiner will award full marks only if the student demonstrates the *logical construction* of a dork, not just a memorized one. Below is a systematic 5-step derivation.

### Step 1: Define the Intelligence Goal

Suppose the ethical hacker's goal is:
> *"Find all publicly indexed PDF files on the domain `example.com` that mention the word 'confidential'."*

### Step 2: Map the Goal to Operators

| Goal Component | Operator Mapping |
|----------------|------------------|
| Restrict to a domain | `site:example.com` |
| Restrict to PDF files | `filetype:pdf` |
| Keyword in document body | `intext:confidential` |

### Step 3: Combine Using Google Syntax Rules

Google applies **implicit AND** between space-separated tokens. Therefore:

$$Q = \text{site:example.com} \;\land\; \text{filetype:pdf} \;\land\; \text{intext:confidential}$$

### Step 4: Final Dork String (Valid Google Query)

```
site:example.com filetype:pdf intext:confidential
```

### Step 5: Logical Refinement (Excluding Noise)

To remove developer discussion pages (false positives), we add exclusions:

```
site:example.com filetype:pdf intext:confidential -site:example.com/blog -inurl:tutorial
```

The final logical form is:

$$Q_{refined} = Q \;\land\; \lnot\text{(blog)} \;\land\; \lnot\text{(tutorial)}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Dorks Deconstructed

| # | Dork String | Logical Decomposition | Target Asset |
|---|-------------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | `intitle:"index of" "parent directory"` | Title contains directory-listing string | Apache/Nginx open folders |
| 2 | `inurl:admin intitle:login` | URL contains "admin" AND title contains "login" | Admin login panels |
| 3 | `filetype:sql "INSERT INTO" -github` | SQL file containing INSERT statements, excluding code hosts | Leaked database dumps |
| 4 | `inurl:"/view.shtml" "Network Camera"` | URL has camera endpoint pattern | Unsecured IP cameras |
| 5 | `ext:env intext:DB_PASSWORD` | Environment file containing DB password | Misconfigured Laravel apps |
| 6 | `site:target.com filetype:xls intext:"salary"` | XLS files on target domain mentioning salary | Exposed payroll spreadsheets |
| 7 | `"Powered by" "phpMyAdmin" "Welcome to"` | Default phpMyAdmin install page | Unpatched DB admin tools |
| 8 | `inurl:".git" ext:git` | Exposed `.git` folder | Source code leakage |
| 9 | `filetype:log intext:"HTTP/1.1 200"` | Log files containing success responses | Web server logs |
| 10 | `intitle:"DVR Login" inurl:login` | DVR-specific login page | Unsecured CCTV recorders |

## 3.3 Quantitative Dork Efficiency — Worked Example

Suppose Google indexes 50,000 pages for `site:example.com`. A generic query for `password` returns 800 results. After refining with `filetype:csv intext:password`, only 12 results remain.

**Dork efficiency** can be defined as:

$$\eta = \frac{N_{filtered}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$

Substituting:

$$\eta = \frac{12}{800} \times 100\% = 1.5\%$$

This means the refined dork **retains only 1.5% of the original noisy results**, drastically improving the signal-to-noise ratio for the analyst. Higher efficiency (lower $\eta$) indicates a more surgically precise dork — a desirable property in professional penetration testing.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Scenario (KTU Typical 7-Mark Sub-Question)

**Scenario:** During a penetration test of `university.edu.in`, you are asked to demonstrate how Google Hacking can be used to identify exposed student records.

**Analytical Procedure (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Step A — Domain Enumeration:**
   - `site:university.edu.in` returns the indexed footprint.
   - Combine with `-site:university.edu.in/research` to remove academic publication noise.

2. **Step B — File Type Targeting:**
   - Student records are typically `.xls` or `.csv`.
   - Append: `filetype:xls OR filetype:csv`.

3. **Step C — Content Keyword Targeting:**
   - Add: `intext:"register number" OR intext:"roll no"` to catch enrollment data.

4. **Step D — Exclusion of Legitimate Uses:**
   - `-inurl:results` to filter out official result portals.

5. **Step E — Final Dork:**
   ```
   site:university.edu.in (filetype:xls OR filetype:csv) 
   (intext:"register number" OR intext:"roll no") 
   -inurl:results
   ```

6. **Step F — Triaging:** Each result is examined; the ethical hacker documents URL, file size, sensitivity level, and reports it.

> [!NOTE]
