> **KTU Insight:** Examiners award marks for showing the *chaining logic* (`AND` between groups, `OR` within groups), not just the final string. Always break the dork into its operator-level components in your answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Google Hacking Attack Flow — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker Initiates Reconnaissance] --> B[Identify Target Domain]
    B --> C[Select Initial Google Dork Template]
    C --> D[Execute Search Query in Google]
    D --> E{Results Returned?}
    E -- No --> F[Refine Dork Operators]
    F --> D
    E -- Yes --> G[Triage and Filter Results]
    G --> H[Validate Sensitive Content]
    H --> I{Sensitive Data Found?}
    I -- No --> F
    I -- Yes --> J[Document Evidence with Screenshots]
    J --> K[Report to Client in Pentest Deliverable]
    K --> L[Close Reconnaissance Phase]
```

## 4.2 Google Dork Operator Relationship — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Google Dork Query] --> B[Domain Operators]
    A --> C[File Operators]
    A --> D[Content Operators]
    A --> E[URL Operators]
    A --> F[Boolean Operators]
    B --> B1[site: target domain]
    B --> B2[related: similar sites]
    C --> C1[filetype: file extension]
    C --> C2[ext: alias for filetype]
    D --> D1[intitle: title text]
    D --> D2[intext: body text]
    D --> D3[cache: cached version]
    E --> E1[inurl: URL substring]
    E --> E2[link: backlink search]
    F --> F1[OR inclusive logic]
    F --> F2[minus exclusive logic]
    F --> F3[asterisk wildcard]
```

## 4.3 Phased Penetration Testing Context — Where Google Hacking Fits

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Passive Reconnaissance] --> P2[Phase 2: Active Reconnaissance]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Scanning and Enumeration]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Vulnerability Analysis]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Exploitation]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Post Exploitation]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 7: Reporting]
    P1 --> X1[Google Hacking and OSINT]
    P1 --> X2[WHOIS and DNS Lookup]
    P1 --> X3[Social Media Intelligence]
    X1 --> X4[Uses Public Search Index Only]
    X4 --> X5[No Direct Contact with Target]
    P3 --> Y1[Nmap Port Scanning]
    P3 --> Y2[Service Version Detection]
```

## 4.4 Defense-in-Depth Against Google Hacking

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1[Sensitive Asset on Web Server] --> Z2{Is robots.txt configured?}
    Z2 -- Yes --> Z3[Googlebot obeys Disallow rule]
    Z2 -- No --> Z4[Asset is crawlable]
    Z3 --> Z5[Asset may still be indexed elsewhere]
    Z4 --> Z6{Is noindex meta tag present?}
    Z6 -- Yes --> Z7[Asset is excluded from index]
    Z6 -- No --> Z8[Asset is publicly indexed]
    Z8 --> Z9[Exposed to Google Dork Searches]
    Z5 --> Z9
    Z9 --> Z10[Periodic Self-Dorking Required]
    Z10 --> Z11[Risk Mitigated through Proactive Scanning]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Q: Define Google Hacking. Mention any four commonly used Google Dork operators.**

**Model Answer:**

Google Hacking is a passive reconnaissance technique that uses advanced search engine operators (called Google Dorks) to discover sensitive information, misconfigured servers, and security vulnerabilities that have been inadvertently indexed by Google.

Four commonly used operators are:

1. **`site:`** — restricts the search to a specific domain (e.g., `site:ktu.edu.in`).
2. **`filetype:`** — searches for documents with a specific file extension (e.g., `filetype:pdf`).
3. **`inurl:`** — finds pages where the search term appears in the URL (e.g., `inurl:admin`).
4. **`intitle:`** — finds pages where the search term appears in the HTML title (e.g., `intitle:"index of"`).

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is the Google Hacking Database (GHDB)? Why is it significant for ethical hackers?**

**Model Answer:**

The Google Hacking Database (GHDB) is a public repository of pre-constructed Google Dork queries, originally created by Johnny Long and now hosted on the Exploit-DB platform at `https://www.exploit-db.com/google-hacking-database`. It categorizes dorks by target (e.g., *Files containing passwords*, *Vulnerable servers*, *Login portals*).

**Significance for ethical hackers:**

- It accelerates the reconnaissance phase by providing ready-made, tested dorks.
- It serves as a defensive checklist — security teams can run GHDB dorks *against their own organization* to identify exposed assets.
- It is widely cited in penetration testing methodologies and is recognized in industry standards like OWASP and PTES.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is for **14 marks** and contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Candidates answer **one full question** (either Choice A or Choice B).

### 🔹 Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Style]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of Google Hacking in detail. Discuss its role in the reconnaissance phase of ethical hacking. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct a step-by-step Google Dork to find all publicly indexed `.pdf` and `.doc` files on the domain `example.com` that contain the word "confidential". Explain each operator used. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Choice A — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 marks):** Google Hacking is a passive OSINT technique that leverages advanced Google search operators (Google Dorks) to discover security-sensitive information indexed by Google's web crawlers. Unlike active scanning, it does not send packets to the target.

**Role in Reconnaissance (3 marks):**
- It belongs to **Phase 1: Passive Reconnaissance** of the penetration testing lifecycle.
- It maps the **external attack surface** by identifying subdomains, exposed documents, login portals, and misconfigured services without alerting the target's intrusion detection systems.
- It produces an **intelligence report** listing URLs, file types, and content patterns, which informs subsequent scanning and exploitation phases.

**Advantages and Limitations (2 marks):**
- ✅ *Advantages:* No direct interaction, hard to trace, no logs generated on the target.
- ❌ *Limitations:* Only finds *indexed* content; cannot access the deep web or authenticated areas.

#### Model Solution for Choice A — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Domain Restriction (1 mark):** To restrict results to the target domain, use `site:example.com`.

**Step 2 — File Type Filtering (2 marks):** Use `(filetype:pdf OR filetype:doc)` to include both file formats. The parentheses and uppercase `OR` enforce inclusive logic.

**Step 3 — Content Keyword (1 mark):** Use `intext:confidential` to match documents containing the word "confidential" in their body text.

**Step 4 — Exclusion of False Positives (1 mark):** Append `-inurl:blog` to filter out developer blog discussions about confidentiality policies.

**Step 5 — Final Dork String (2 marks):**

```
site:example.com (filetype:pdf OR filetype:doc) intext:confidential -inurl:blog
```

This dork returns only PDF and DOC files on the `example.com` domain that contain "confidential" but are not hosted under blog subdirectories. The student must explain the implicit AND between the three groups and the explicit OR within the file-type group.

---

### 🔹 Choice B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice]

**Q: (a)** With suitable examples, explain the following Google Dork operators: `site:`, `filetype:`, `inurl:`, `intitle:`, `intext:`, and `cache:`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What countermeasures can an organization implement to prevent its sensitive assets from appearing in Google Hacking searches? Discuss any five defense mechanisms. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Choice B — Part (a) [7 Marks]

| Operator | Function | Example Dork | Result Type |
|----------|----------|--------------|-------------|
| `site:` | Limits search to a domain (1 mark) | `site:ktu.edu.in` | Pages from KTU website only |
| `filetype:` | Filters by file extension (1 mark) | `filetype:pdf` | PDF documents only |
| `inurl:` | Matches a term in the URL (1 mark) | `inurl:admin` | Pages with "admin" in URL |
| `intitle:` | Matches a term in the title (1.5 marks) | `intitle:"index of"` | Open directory listings |
| `intext:` | Matches a term in the body (1.5 marks) | `intext:password` | Pages containing the word |
| `cache:` | Shows Google's cached snapshot (1 mark) | `cache:ktu.edu.in` | Cached KTU homepage |

#### Model Solution for Choice B — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Five key countermeasures (1.4 marks each):

1. **Configure `robots.txt` Properly:** Use `Disallow: /private/` directives to instruct compliant crawlers. However, note that `robots.txt` is *advisory*, not enforced.

2. **Apply `noindex` Meta Tags:** Insert `<meta name="robots" content="noindex, nofollow">` in sensitive HTML pages to request exclusion from the index.

3. **Use HTTP `X-Robots-Tag` Header:** For non-HTML files (PDF, DOC), set the header `X-Robots-Tag: noarchive, noindex` to prevent caching and indexing.

4. **Enforce Authentication:** Place all sensitive directories behind login walls (e.g., HTTP Basic Auth, SSO) so crawlers cannot reach them.

5. **Periodic Self-Dorking & Google Search Console:** Regularly run GHDB-style dorks against your own domain. Submit a **URL Removal Request** via Google Search Console for any leaked sensitive URLs.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating Google Hacking with active hacking:** Examiners deduct marks if you describe Google Hacking as a *break-in*. It is *passive reconnaissance* — the system is never compromised via this technique.
> 2. **Listing operators without examples:** Writing "inurl, intitle, filetype" without a sample dork for each loses 1–2 marks. Always pair an operator with an example.
> 3. **Ignoring defensive countermeasures:** A one-sided answer covering only attack is incomplete. KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **CO4 (Defensive Security)**. Always include a defense section.
> 4. **Forgetting Boolean logic:** Mixing lowercase `or` (ignored by Google) with uppercase `OR` is a common error. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Writing Python/code:** This is *not* a programming question. Do not write `import googlesearch` scripts. Present dorks as search engine strings only.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔍 **Google Hacking = Passive Reconnaissance** using Google search operators (Google Dorks).
- 🧰 **Core operators:** `site:`, `filetype:`, `inurl:`, `intitle:`, `intext:`, `cache:`, `link:`, `related:`.
- ⚙️ **Boolean rules:** Implicit `AND` between tokens, explicit `OR` (uppercase), `-` for exclusion, `"..."` for exact match, `*` for wildcard.
- 🎯 **Targets discovered:** Open directories, login portals, exposed documents, default-credentialed IoT devices, source code leaks, database dumps.
- 📚 **GHDB:** A public catalog of dorks maintained on Exploit-DB, originally by Johnny Long.
- 🛡️ **Defenses:** `robots.txt`, `noindex` meta tags, `X-Robots-Tag` HTTP header, authentication, periodic self-dorking, Google Search Console URL removal.
- ⚖️ **Ethics:** Always obtain *written authorization* before performing Google Hacking against a target. Unauthorized reconnaissance, even passive, may violate computer-misuse laws.
- 📊 **Efficiency metric:** A well-constructed dork achieves a low $\eta = (N_{filtered}/N_{total}) \times 100\%$, meaning high signal-to-noise ratio.
- 🔄 **KTU 2024 CO alignment:** This topic maps to **CO1** (Describe reconnaissance techniques) and partially **CO4** (Apply countermeasures).
- 💡 **Exam mantra:** *Operator + Example + Boolean Logic + Defense* — these four pillars cover most 7-mark and 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scanning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scanning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scanning** is the second phase of the ethical hacking methodology, wherein a penetration tester actively probes the target system or network to gather detailed intelligence about live hosts, open ports, running services, operating system fingerprints, and potential vulnerabilities. It is a **pre-attack reconnaissance activity** that converts passive intelligence (footprinting) into actionable, technical attack-surface data.

According to the **CEH (Certified Ethical Hacker) and EC-Council framework** adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus for *PBCCT604*, scanning is formally classified under the **"Scanning & Enumeration"** stage of the attack lifecycle, succeeding footprinting and preceding system hacking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Scanning encompasses four sub-domains — (1) Network Scanning, (2) Port Scanning, (3) Vulnerability Scanning, and (4) Banner Grabbing / OS Fingerprinting.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a thief casing a jewelry store **before** a heist. They don't break in yet. Instead, they:

| Thief's Action | Ethical Hacking Equivalent |
|----------------|---------------------------|
| Walks around the building noting entry/exit points | **Network Scanning** — maps live hosts, subnets, routers |
| Checks which windows are unlocked | **Port Scanning** — discovers open/closed/filtered TCP/UDP ports |
| Identifies the type of lock (Yale, deadbolt) | **Banner Grabbing** — determines service versions |
| Reads store alarm brand stickers | **OS Fingerprinting** — identifies OS (Windows, Linux, IoT firmware) |
| Looks for "Out of Order" signs on doors | **Vulnerability Scanning** — finds weak service versions or CVEs |

**Key Idea:** Scanning is *knocking on digital doors* to hear what echoes back. The echoes tell you what software is running, how the system is configured, and which doors (vulnerabilities) are left unguarded.

---

## 1.3 Why Scanning Matters in Ethical Hacking & IoT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **IoT (Internet of Things)** devices — which are characterized by constrained resources, default credentials, and rarely-patched firmware — scanning is **the most critical pre-attack phase**. A single open Telnet port (port 23) on an IoT camera can expose an entire smart-home network.

**Three "Golden Questions" answered by Scanning:**

1. **What is alive?** (Host discovery / Ping sweep)
2. **What is open?** (Port scanning — TCP/UDP 0–65535)
3. **What is exploitable?** (Vulnerability mapping)

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bold for Visibility)

- **TCP Port Range:** $0$ to $65535$ (total **$2^{16} = 65{,}536$** ports)
- **Well-Known Ports:** $0$–$1023$ (reserved, RFC 1700)
- **Registered Ports:** $1024$–$49{,}151$
- **Dynamic/Private Ports:** $49{,}152$–$65{,}535$
- **ICMP Echo Request Type:** **$8$**, Echo Reply Type: **$0$**
- **Three-Way Handshake Flags:** **SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK**
- **Default Nmap Scan Speed:** "Polite" — **T3** timing template
- **Common Scan Tool:** **Nmap (Network Mapper)** — Gordon Lyon (Fyodor)
- **OS Fingerprinting TCP Window Size default:** **$65{,}535$ bytes**
- **SYN packet flag bit:** **$0x02$** in the TCP flags byte
- **FIN flag bit:** **$0x01$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Scanning Methodology — Structured Logical Steps

A KTU examiner expects scanning to be presented as a **four-stage process**, each with a distinct goal:

### Step 1 — Host Discovery (Ping Sweep)
- **Goal:** Identify which IP addresses in a subnet are alive.
- **Mechanism:** Send **ICMP Echo Request (Type 8)**; expect **ICMP Echo Reply (Type 0)**.
- **Tools:** `ping`, `fping`, `nmap -sn`, `arping`.
- **Advanced Techniques:** ARP scan (for LANs), TCP SYN to port 80, UDP probe to closed port (ICMP unreachable = host alive).

### Step 2 — Port Scanning
- **Goal:** Determine the state of TCP/UDP ports on a live host.
- **Port States (RFC 793 + Nmap):**
  - `open` — service actively accepting connections
  - `closed` — port responds, no service listening
  - `filtered` — no response (firewall dropping packets)
  - `unfiltered` — responds but state undetermined (used in ACK scan)
  - `open|filtered` — no response, could be open or filtered
  - `closed|filtered` — only used in IP ID idle scan

### Step 3 — Service & Version Detection (Banner Grabbing)
- **Goal:** Identify the application software and version on each open port.
- **Mechanism:** Connect to port, read server banner (e.g., `Apache/2.4.41`, `OpenSSH_7.6`).
- **Tools:** `nmap -sV`, `netcat`, `amap`, `httprint`.

### Step 4 — OS Fingerprinting
- **Goal:** Identify the target's operating system.
- **Two Methods:**
  - **Active:** Send crafted packets, analyze TCP/IP stack fingerprint (TTL, Window Size, DF bit, TOS).
  - **Passive:** Sniff traffic, infer OS from observed packet behavior.
- **Database:** Nmap's `nmap-os-db` with **2,500+** OS signatures.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Scanning Technique

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners frequently ask: *"Why use SYN scan instead of TCP Connect scan?"* The answer lies in **stealth and speed**.

| Reason | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| **Stealth** | SYN scan does **not complete** the 3-way handshake, so it's not logged in most application logs (only IDS-level logs). |
| **Speed** | Half-open scans are faster; no need to tear down connections. |
| **Raw socket access** | Requires root/admin privileges (a clear exam differentiator). |

---

## 2.3 TCP Scanning Techniques — The Core KTU Topic

### 2.3.1 TCP Connect Scan (`-sT`)
- Uses OS's built-in `connect()` system call.
- Completes full **3-way handshake** → easy to detect.
- No raw socket needed (works as unprivileged user).

### 2.3.2 SYN Stealth / Half-Open Scan (`-sS`)
- Sends **SYN**, receives **SYN-ACK**, replies with **RST** (reset) instead of ACK.
- Never completes connection → connection logs absent.
- **Default scan** when run as root in Nmap.

### 2.3.3 FIN Scan (`-sF`)
- Sends packet with **FIN flag only**.
- RFC 793: closed port replies with **RST**; open port silently drops.
- **Stealthy** — bypasses simple stateless firewalls and basic logging.

### 2.3.4 NULL Scan (`-sN`)
- Sends packet with **all flags turned off** (0x00).
- Same RFC 793 logic: open = drop, closed = RST.

### 2.3.5 XMAS Scan (`-sX`)
- Sends packet with **FIN + PSH + URG** flags set (lit up "like a Christmas tree").
- Same open/closed logic as FIN/NULL.

### 2.3.6 ACK Scan (`-sA`)
- Sends **ACK** flag only.
- Used for **firewall rule mapping** (stateful vs stateless).
- RST response = unfiltered; no response = filtered.

### 2.3.7 Idle / Zombie Scan (`-sI`)
- Uses a third-party "zombie" host as a proxy.
- Attacker spoofs IP via zombie, infers port state from zombie's **IP ID sequence increments**.

---

## 2.4 UDP Scanning

- Sends **UDP packets**; closed ports return **ICMP Port Unreachable (Type 3, Code 3)**.
- Open UDP ports often give **no response** → slow and unreliable.
- Tools: `nmap -sU`, `unicornscan`.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD** — This table is a high-frequency 14-mark question source. Memorize the response patterns.

| Scan Type | Nmap Flag | Flags Sent | Response: Open Port | Response: Closed Port | Stealth Level |
|-----------|-----------|------------|--------------------|-----------------------|---------------|
| TCP Connect | `-sT` | SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK | SYN-ACK | RST | **Low** (logged) |
| SYN Stealth | `-sS` | SYN → RST | SYN-ACK | RST | **High** |
| FIN | `-sF` | FIN | No response (drop) | RST | **Very High** |
| NULL | `-sN` | None (0x00) | No response (drop) | RST | **Very High** |
| XMAS | `-sX` | FIN+PSH+URG | No response (drop) | RST | **Very High** |
| ACK | `-sA` | ACK | RST (unfiltered) | RST (unfiltered) / no reply (filtered) | Medium |
| Idle/Zombie | `-sI` | SYN via zombie | IP ID increments by 2 | IP ID increments by 1 | **Maximum** |
| UDP | `-sU` | UDP | No response | ICMP Port Unreachable | Medium |
| Window | `-sW` | ACK | RST with non-zero window | RST with zero window | Medium |
| Maimon | `-sM` | FIN+ACK | Drop | RST | High |

---

## 2.6 Scanning Countermeasures (Defense Perspective)

- **Block ICMP Echo** at the firewall.
- **Use Stateful Inspection Firewalls** to detect half-open patterns.
- **Deploy IDS/IPS** (Snort, Suricata) with SYN-scan signatures.
- **Use Port Scan Detection Tools** (e.g., `portsentry`).
- **Banner Obfuscation** — strip version info from HTTP, SSH, SMTP banners.
- **Honeypots** — decoy services to mislead attackers.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for IoT engineers:**
> - A default **Mikrotik, TP-Link, or Hikvision IoT device** exposes Telnet (23), HTTP (80), and RTSP (554). Scanning identifies them in **seconds** via Nmap.
> - **Mirai Botnet (2016)** used SYN scanning to find IoT devices with default credentials, infecting **600,000+** devices.
> - Forensics teams use the same techniques to **audit corporate networks** for shadow IoT.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Numerical Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Choosing a Scan

The following logic is a KTU-favorite "design-type" question:

**Given a target:** Choose the optimal scanning technique based on stealth requirement, privilege level, and firewall presence.

$$
\text{Optimal Scan} = f(\text{Privilege},\ \text{Stealth},\ \text{Firewall})
$$

Where:
- **Privilege** $\in \{\text{root},\ \text{user}\}$
- **Stealth** $\in \{\text{low},\ \text{medium},\ \text{high}\}$
- **Firewall** $\in \{\text{none},\ \text{stateless},\ \text{stateful}\}$

| Privilege | Stealth Required | Firewall | Recommended Scan |
|-----------|------------------|----------|------------------|
| root | high | none | **SYN (`-sS`)** |
| root | very high | stateless | **FIN/NULL/XMAS** |
| root | maximum | any | **Idle/Zombie** |
| user | low | none | **TCP Connect (`-sT`)** |
| root | medium | stateful | **ACK + fragmentation** |

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — Scan Time vs. Port Count

A common KTU numerical problem:

> **Problem:** Estimate the time to perform a full TCP Connect scan of **$N$ ports** across **$H$ hosts**, given a per-port response time of **$R$ seconds** and parallelization factor **$P$** (max concurrent connections).

**Derivation:**

Total ports to scan = $N \times H$

Time per port (sequential) = $R$ seconds

Sequential total time:
$$
T_{\text{seq}} = N \times H \times R
$$

With parallelization factor $P$ (concurrent connections):
$$
T_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{N \times H \times R}{P}
$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question):**

- $N = 1000$ ports (custom range)
- $H = 254$ hosts (/24 subnet)
- $R = 0.05$ s
- $P = 50$ (Nmap default)

Sequential:
$$
T_{\text{seq}} = 1000 \times 254 \times 0.05 = 12{,}700 \text{ seconds} \approx 3.53 \text{ hours}
$$

Parallel:
$$
T_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{12{,}700}{50} = 254 \text{ seconds} \approx 4.23 \text{ minutes}
$$

This justifies why **Nmap's parallelism** dramatically reduces scan time.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Idle Scan IP ID Logic

The Idle Scan relies on a **zombie host's IP Identification field**, which increments by 1 for every packet the zombie sends.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Probe 1:** Attacker sends SYN-ACK to zombie → zombie replies with **RST**, IP ID = $X$.
2. **Attacker spoofs** source IP as zombie, sends SYN to target port.
3. **Case A — Target port OPEN:** Target sends SYN-ACK to zombie → zombie (unsolicited) replies with RST → zombie's IP ID increments to $X + 2$.
4. **Case B — Target port CLOSED:** Target sends RST to zombie → zombie ignores → IP ID remains $X + 1$ (only its own RST from Step 1 incremented it).
5. **Probe 2:** Attacker sends another SYN-ACK to zombie → zombie replies RST with IP ID.

$$
\text{IP ID delta} =
\begin{cases}
2 & \Rightarrow \text{Port OPEN} \\
1 & \Rightarrow \text{Port CLOSED} \\
0 & \Rightarrow \text{Zombie not idle / not suitable}
\end{cases}
$$

**Algebraic representation:**
$$
\Delta_{\text{IPID}} = \text{IPID}_{\text{Probe2}} - \text{IPID}_{\text{Probe1}}
$$

$$
\text{Port State} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{open} & \text{if } \Delta_{\text{IPID}} = 2 \\
\text{closed} & \text{if } \Delta_{\text{IPID}} = 1 \\
\text{unknown} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.4 OS Fingerprinting — The TCP Stack Signature Equation

OS fingerprinting uses the **TCP ISN (Initial Sequence Number) sampling**, **IP TTL**, **TCP Window Size**, and **DF bit** as a multi-dimensional signature vector.

**Signature Vector Definition:**
$$
\vec{S} = \langle \text{TTL},\ W,\ \text{DF},\ \text{OS}_{\text{guess}} \rangle
$$

**Example signatures (real-world Nmap DB):**

| OS | TTL | Window Size | DF Bit |
|----|-----|-------------|--------|
| Linux 2.6.x | 64 | 5,840 | 1 |
| Windows XP | 128 | 65,535 (chained) | 1 |
| Cisco IOS | 255 | 4,128 | 0 |
| FreeBSD | 64 | 65,535 | 1 |
| IoT (uClinux) | 64 | 2,048 | 1 |

The Nmap algorithm computes a **matching score**:
$$
\text{Match Score} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(S_i = D_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

Where $w_i$ is the weight of feature $i$ and $\mathbb{1}$ is the indicator function. The OS with the highest match score is reported.

---

## 3.5 Banner Grabbing — HTTP Request/Response Analysis

A standard `HEAD` request to a web server:

**Client → Server:**
```
HEAD / HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: target.com\r\n
\r\n
```

**Server → Client:**
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n
Server: Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)\r\n
X-Powered-By: PHP/7.4.3\r\n
```

**Extracted Banner:** `Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)` + `PHP/7.4.3` → queries CVE database for known exploits.

---

## 3.6 Vulnerability Scanning vs. Port Scanning — Analytical Distinction

| Property | Port Scanning | Vulnerability Scanning |
|----------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Question answered** | What ports are open? | What is exploitable? |
| **Depth** | Transport layer (L4) | Application layer (L7) + L4 |
| **Output** | Port state | CVE-ID, CVSS score, exploit path |
| **Tools** | Nmap, Masscan | Nessus, OpenVAS, Qualys, Nikto |
| **Database** | None | 100,000+ CVEs |
| **Active probing** | Yes (crafted packets) | Yes + version-aware requests |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Scanning Methodology — Block-Level Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Target IP Subnet] --> B[Host Discovery - Ping Sweep]
    B --> C{Host Alive?}
    C -- No --> Z1[Mark Host as Down - Skip]
    C -- Yes --> D[Port Scanning - TCP and UDP]
    D --> E[Service Version Detection - Banner Grab]
    E --> F[OS Fingerprinting - Stack Analysis]
    F --> G[Vulnerability Mapping - CVE Lookup]
    G --> H[Generate Scan Report]
    H --> I[End: Attack Surface Documented]

    stepA[ICMP Echo Request] -.-> B
    stepB[SYN Scan -sS] -.-> D
    stepC[Nmap -sV] -.-> E
    stepD[Nmap -O] -.-> F
    stepE[Nessus or OpenVAS] -.-> G
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The solid arrows show the **mandatory sequential flow**.
- The dashed arrows show the **specific tool/protocol** used at each stage.

---

## 4.2 TCP Three-Way Handshake — Normal vs. SYN Scan

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker as Attacker (Kali Linux)
    participant Target as Target Host (Port 80)

    Note over Attacker,Target: Normal TCP Connect Scan
    Attacker->>Target: SYN (Seq=x)
    Target-->>Attacker: SYN-ACK (Seq=y, Ack=x+1)
    Attacker->>Target: ACK (Seq=x+1, Ack=y+1)
    Note over Attacker,Target: Connection ESTABLISHED - Logged

    Note over Attacker,Target: SYN Stealth Scan
    Attacker->>Target: SYN (Seq=a)
    Target-->>Attacker: SYN-ACK (Seq=b, Ack=a+1)
    Attacker->>Target: RST (Reset - Tears Down)
    Note over Attacker,Target: Half-Open - Not Logged in App Logs
```

---

## 4.3 Idle/Zombie Scan — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Attacker] -- 1.Probe IP ID --> Z[Zombie Host]
    A -- 2.Spoofed SYN from Zombie IP --> T[Target Host]
    Z -. 3a.RST to Target if Port Open .-> T
    T -. 3b.Unsolicited SYN-ACK to Zombie .-> Z
    Z -- 4.Zombie sends RST to Target --> T
    A -- 5.Re-probe IP ID --> Z
    Z -- 6.Returns New IP ID --> A
```

**Logic encoded:** By comparing the two IP ID samples, the attacker infers the target port state without ever sending a packet from their own IP to the target.

---

## 4.4 Scan Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Scanning Technique] --> Q1{User Privilege?}
    Q1 -- Root / Admin --> Q2{Need Stealth?}
    Q1 -- Unprivileged User --> Conn[TCP Connect Scan -sT]
    Q2 -- Yes, Maximum --> Idle[Idle Scan -sI zombie]
    Q2 -- Yes, High --> Stealth[SYN Scan -sS]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Firewall Present?}
    Q3 -- Yes, Stateful --> Ack[ACK Scan -sA for Rule Mapping]
    Q3 -- No --> Stealth
    Q2 -- Yes, Evasion --> Evade{FIN/NULL/XMAS}
    Evade --> FIN[FIN Scan -sF]
    Evade --> NULL[NULL Scan -sN]
    Evade --> XMAS[XMAS Scan -sX]
```

---

## 4.5 IoT Scanning Architecture — Real-World View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LAN1[Home IoT Network 192.168.1.0/24]
        IoT1[Smart Camera]
        IoT2[Smart Bulb]
        IoT3[Smart Speaker]
        IoT4[WiFi Router]
    end

    Attacker[Attacker Laptop - Kali Linux] -- ARP Scan --> LAN1
    IoT1 -- Response: Port 23 Open Telnet --> Attacker
    IoT2 -- Response: Port 80 Open HTTP --> Attacker
    IoT3 -- Response: Port 443 Open HTTPS --> Attacker
    IoT4 -- Response: Port 22 and 80 Open --> Attacker

    Attacker --> NmapDB[Nmap OS Database Lookup]
    NmapDB -- Match Score --> Report[OS Report: uClinux 4.9 on IoT Camera]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> Differentiate between **active scanning** and **passive scanning**. Give one example tool for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Scanning | Passive Scanning |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Definition** | Sends packets to the target to elicit responses | Monitors/sniffs existing traffic without injecting packets |
| **Detection Risk** | High — target is aware of probing | Almost zero — invisible to target |
| **Information Obtained** | Port state, OS, versions | Services, protocols, OS inference from observed traffic |
| **Example Tool** | Nmap (`nmap -sS target`) | Wireshark, P0f, Kismet |

**1-mark breakdown:** Definition (1), Detection characteristic (1), Example (1).

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
> What is **banner grabbing**? Why is it considered a critical step in vulnerability assessment?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Banner grabbing is the process of connecting to an open port and reading the service-identifying string (banner) returned by the application, e.g., `Apache/2.4.41` or `OpenSSH_7.6p1`.
- **Purpose (1 mark):** It reveals the exact software version, which can then be cross-referenced with CVE databases (e.g., NVD) to identify known vulnerabilities.
- **Criticality (1 mark):** It converts an abstract "open port" into a concrete, exploitable target. For example, `vsftpd 2.3.4` on port 21 immediately flags the **vsftpd backdoor CVE-2011-2523**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do NOT confuse** the XMAS scan flags (FIN+PSH+URG) with the SYN scan flags (SYN). Many students write "XMAS uses SYN+ACK" — this costs 3–4 marks.
> - **Always state the prerequisite privilege** for SYN scan (root) — examiners specifically look for this.
> - **Avoid generic answers** like "scanning is used to find vulnerabilities." Use exact terminology: "port state determination," "service version detection," "OS fingerprinting."
> - In numerical problems, **show all substitution steps** — partial marks are awarded for correct setup even if arithmetic slips.
> - For diagrams, **label every arrow** and **distinguish request/response** with different line styles.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **TCP three-way handshake** and describe how the **SYN Stealth Scan** deviates from it. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the following scanning techniques: **FIN Scan, NULL Scan, XMAS Scan, and Idle Scan**. Use a suitable table and state the underlying RFC logic. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Normal TCP Handshake (2 marks):**
The TCP three-way handshake establishes a connection via three steps:
1. **Client → Server:** `SYN` (Seq = x) — "I want to connect, my initial sequence number is x."
2. **Server → Client:** `SYN-ACK` (Seq = y, Ack = x+1) — "I acknowledge your sequence, here's mine."
3. **Client → Server:** `ACK` (Seq = x+1, Ack = y+1) — "Connection established."

The connection state transitions: `CLOSED → SYN_SENT → SYN_RECEIVED → ESTABLISHED`.

**Step 2 — SYN Stealth Deviation (2 marks):**
In SYN Stealth Scan (`nmap -sS`):
1. Attacker sends `SYN`.
2. Target responds `SYN-ACK` (indicating an open port).
3. **Attacker replies with `RST` (Reset)** instead of `ACK`, tearing down the half-open connection.

The connection is **never established**, leaving no entry in the application server's connection logs.

**Step 3 — Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Bypasses basic application-layer logging.
- Faster than full TCP Connect (no teardown overhead).
- Clear distinction between open (SYN-ACK) and closed (RST).

**Step 4 — Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Requires **root/administrator privileges** (raw socket access).
- Detected by modern stateful firewalls and IDS/IPS.
- Does not work against stateless firewalls that drop SYN packets (filtered state).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Underlying RFC 793 Logic (2 marks):**

> "A TCP segment with no flags set, or with FIN/URG/PSH flags, sent to a **closed port** MUST result in an `RST`. A **listening (open) port** will silently **drop** the packet (no response), as the segment does not correspond to an established connection."

This differential response forms the basis of FIN, NULL, and XMAS scans.

**Comparative Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | FIN Scan (`-sF`) | NULL Scan (`-sN`) | XMAS Scan (`-sX`) | Idle Scan (`-sI`) |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Flags Set** | FIN only | None (0x00) | FIN+PSH+URG | SYN (spoofed via zombie) |
| **Open Port Response** | No reply (drop) | No reply (drop) | No reply (drop) | IP ID increment = 2 |
| **Closed Port Response** | RST | RST | RST | IP ID increment = 1 |
| **Stealth** | High | High | High | **Maximum** (attacker invisible) |
| **Bypasses Stateless FW** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Bypasses Stateful FW** | No | No | No | Yes |
| **Privilege Required** | Root | Root | Root | Root |
| **Drawback** | Microsoft's stack violates RFC 793 (sends RST regardless) | Same as FIN | Same as FIN | Requires idle zombie; slower |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**  
> **(a)** What is **OS fingerprinting**? Explain **active and passive fingerprinting** with examples. Describe any two **TCP stack signature parameters** used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A security analyst needs to scan a **/24 subnet (254 hosts)** with **1000 custom TCP ports** per host. If the average per-port response time is **40 ms** and Nmap runs with **100 parallel probes**, calculate the total scan time. Justify how **scan parallelism** and **timing templates** (T0–T5) affect the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** OS fingerprinting is the process of determining the target host's operating system by analyzing the unique behavioral characteristics of its TCP/IP stack.

**Active Fingerprinting (2 marks):**
- The attacker sends **crafted, non-standard packets** to the target and analyzes the responses.
- Tools: **Nmap `-O`**, Xprobe2.
- **Example:** Nmap sends a series of 9 probes (SYN, ACK, ICMP, etc.) and compares replies to its `nmap-os-db`.

**Passive Fingerprinting (2 marks):**
- The attacker **sniffs existing network traffic** without injecting packets.
- Tools: **p0f, Ettercap, NetworkMiner**.
- **Example:** Observing a TCP SYN packet with `TTL=64, Window=5840, DF=1` → strongly suggests **Linux 2.6/3.x/4.x**.

**Two TCP Stack Signature Parameters (2 marks):**

1. **IP Time-To-Live (TTL):** Linux typically uses **TTL=64**, Windows **TTL=128**, Cisco/network devices **TTL=255**. The attacker notes the TTL of the first packet from the target.
2. **TCP Window Size:** Linux uses 5,840 (older) or 65,535 (newer). Windows often uses 65,535 with window scaling. FreeBSD uses 65,535. This field is highly OS-specific.

*(Other valid parameters: DF bit, IP options, ICMP echo reply TOS, TCP options ordering.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Subnet = /24 → $H = 254$ hosts
- Ports per host = $N = 1000$
- Response time per port = $R = 40 \text{ ms} = 0.04 \text{ s}$
- Parallelism factor = $P = 100$

**Step 1 — Total Port Probes (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Total probes} = N \times H = 1000 \times 254 = 254{,}000 \text{ probes}
$$

**Step 2 — Sequential Time (1 mark):**
$$
T_{\text{seq}} = 254{,}000 \times 0.04 = 10{,}160 \text{ seconds} \approx 2.82 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Parallel Time (2 marks):**
$$
T_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{10{,}160}{100} = 101.6 \text{ seconds} \approx 1 \text{ minute } 41.6 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Effect of Nmap Timing Templates T0–T5 (3 marks):**

| Template | Name | Parallelism | Delay | Use Case |
|----------|------|-------------|-------|----------|
| T0 | Paranoid | 1, very slow | 5 min | IDS evasion, maximum stealth |
| T1 | Sneaky | 1 | 15 s | Stealthy scans |
| T2 | Polite | 10 | 400 ms | Reduce network load |
| T3 | **Normal** (default) | 100 | 0 ms | Balanced speed/stability |
| T4 | Aggressive | 1,000 | 0 ms | Fast scans on stable networks |
| T5 | Insane | 10,000 | 0 ms | Maximum speed, may lose accuracy |

**Justification:** Increasing parallelism ($P$) from 100 to 1,000 (T4) would reduce the time to **10.16 seconds**, but increases the risk of triggering IDS alarms and packet drops on congested networks. Reducing to T1 (P=1) would inflate the time to **10,160 seconds (~2.82 hours)** but ensures stealth.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU 2024 Module 1 — Scanning Checklist**

- [x] **Scanning = 2nd phase** of ethical hacking (after Footprinting, before System Hacking).
- [x] **Four sub-stages:** Host Discovery → Port Scanning → Banner Grabbing → Vulnerability Mapping.
- [x] **TCP Connect (`-sT`)** — full handshake, logged, no root needed.
- [x] **SYN Stealth (`-sS`)** — half-open, **root required**, default Nmap scan.
- [x] **FIN/NULL/XMAS** — based on **RFC 793**; closed port = RST, open port = drop.
- [x] **XMAS flags = FIN + PSH + URG** (NOT SYN).
- [x] **Idle Scan (`-sI`)** — uses zombie host's IP ID; maximum stealth.
- [x] **UDP Scan (`-sU`)** — open = no reply, closed = ICMP Type 3 Code 3.
- [x] **Port states:** open, closed, filtered, unfiltered, open|filtered, closed|filtered.
- [x] **Port range:** $0$–$65{,}535$ ($2^{16}$); well-known = $0$–$1023$.
- [x] **Nmap is the de-facto tool** for port scanning and OS detection.
- [x] **OS fingerprinting uses** TTL, Window Size, DF bit, IP options ordering.
- [x] **Linux TTL = 64, Windows = 128, Cisco = 255** (default initial values).
- [x] **Mirai Botnet (2016)** used SYN scanning to find IoT devices — classic IoT scanning case study.
- [x] **Countermeasures:** Stateful firewalls, IDS/IPS, banner stripping, honeypots.
- [x] **Numerical formula:** $T = \frac{N \cdot H \cdot R}{P}$
- [x] **Nmap timing templates** T0–T5 control parallelism and delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Mile Tip:** When the KTU question uses the phrase *"Discuss scanning methodology"*, the examiner expects a 4-step answer (Discovery → Port → Service → Vulnerability) with at least **one tool name** and **one flag/response** example per step. Always close with a **countermeasure** paragraph for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enumeration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Enumeration # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Enumeration** is the process of establishing an active connection with a target host (or system) to extract detailed information such as **user names, group names, network shares, service banners, routing tables, SNMP details, and protocol-specific data** by directly querying the system using legitimate protocols and services. In the formal KTU ethical hacking framework, enumeration sits as the **third sequential phase** of a penetration test, immediately after **Footprinting (passive reconnaissance)** and **Scanning (active reconnaissance)**. While scanning only reveals open ports and live hosts, enumeration dives deeper by exploiting the inherent trust relationships in network protocols to extract *granular, exploitable intelligence*. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > Enumeration is classified under the *Information Gathering & System Hacking* cluster. The CO mapping is typically **CO1** (Understand the phases of ethical hacking and perform footprinting/scanning/enumeration). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are a detective investigating a large office building (the target network): - **Footprinting** = Looking at the building from the outside, checking the company website, reading public news, and noting the address and the number of floors. - **Scanning** = Walking around the building, peeking through windows, checking which doors are unlocked, and identifying which rooms have lights on. - **Enumeration** = *Walking into the unlocked doors*, knocking on specific rooms, asking the receptionist (the system services) "Who works here? What are their names? Which room belongs to whom? Can I see the company directory?" In technical terms, enumeration is the phase where the attacker **talks directly to the system** using its own native language (the protocols) and tricks it into revealing sensitive internal information it would not normally share with outsiders. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundaries > [!NOTE] > **Core Metrics Used in Enumeration:** > - **Service Banners:** Plain-text identification strings returned by services (e.g., `Apache/2.4.41`, `OpenSSH_8.2`). > - **User Account Lists:** Valid login names discovered via RPC, LDAP, or SMB enumeration. > - **SNMP Strings:** Community strings (default = `public` for read, `private` for read/write). > - **Port Range Scanned:** Typically **0–65535** (full TCP/UDP range). > - **NetBIOS Name Resolution:** 16-character identifier (15 chars name + 1 char suffix). --- ## 1.4 Why Enumeration is the Most Critical Phase | Property | Footprinting | Scanning | **Enumeration** | |---|---|---|---| | **Interaction** | Indirect / Passive | Active (probes) | **Direct (queries)** | | **Source of Data** | Public databases | Network behavior | **System services** | | **Information Type** | Domain, IP ranges | Open ports, OS | **Users, shares, services** | | **Risk Level** | Very Low | Medium | **High (logged/detected)** | Enumeration crosses the line from **silent observation to active dialogue** with the target. This is why it carries the highest detection risk and provides the highest value intelligence. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Foundational Steps of Enumeration Every enumeration attack, regardless of the protocol used, follows the same logical triad: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1 — Identify the Active Services** > Before enumeration, the attacker must know *which service is listening*. This is determined from the scanning phase. For example: Port 135 = MSRPC, Port 139 = NetBIOS, Port 161 = SNMP, Port 389 = LDAP, Port 25 = SMTP. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 2 — Probe the Service with Native Protocol Calls** > Instead of sending random packets, the attacker sends *legitimate protocol-specific requests* that the service is *designed to answer*. For example, an LDAP query asking for the directory tree, or an SNMP GET request asking for system parameters. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 3 — Harvest and Correlate the Responses** > The replies contain the gold — usernames, software versions, shared folders, routing data, and more. The attacker correlates this data to build an *attack map* (e.g., target specific user accounts for password guessing). --- ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step - **Why Step 1?** Sending an SNMP query to a system that has SNMP disabled wastes time and triggers IDS alerts. Service identification *narrows the attack surface*. - **Why Step 2?** Network protocols are built on a *request–response model*. If the attacker speaks the same protocol language, the server will reply without suspicion. - **Why Step 3?** A single username is useless. But *correlating* usernames + group memberships + shared drives = a complete picture of the internal trust structure. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 EXAM CHEAT SHEET — ENUMERATION PROTOCOL MAP** | Protocol | Default Port(s) | Information Extracted | Risk Level | |---|---|---|---| | **SMTP** | 25, 465, 587 | Valid user accounts via `VRFY`, `EXPN`, `RCPT TO` | Medium | | **DNS** | 53 | Zone transfers, host records, mail servers | Low–Medium | | **SNMP** | 161, 162 | System info, routing tables, ARP tables, community strings | **Very High** | | **NetBIOS** | 137, 138, 139 | Host names, user accounts, shared folders | High | | **LDAP** | 389, 636 | Active Directory users, groups, OUs | **Very High** | | **SMB/CIFS** | 445 | File shares, user lists, OS details | High | | **MSRPC** | 135, 593 | Domain controllers, user lists | High | | **FTP** | 21, 2121 | Anonymous login, banner, user accounts | Medium | | **NFS** | 2049 | Exported file systems, mount points | High | | **IPSec** | 500, 4500 | VPN encryption parameters | Medium | | **VoIP (SIP)** | 5060, 5061 | Internal extensions, user agents | Medium | | **RPCbind** | 111 | Registered RPC program numbers | High | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Enumeration is not only an attack technique — its methodologies underpin: - **Network Asset Management:** IT teams use SNMP enumeration to auto-discover every printer, router, and IoT sensor on a corporate network. - **Active Directory Auditing:** Security teams use LDAP enumeration to find stale user accounts, excessive privileges, and misconfigured groups. - **Vulnerability Management:** Tools like Nessus, Qualys, and OpenVAS internally perform enumeration to map the attack surface before running vulnerability checks. - **Incident Response:** When a breach is suspected, responders enumerate compromised accounts by analyzing login patterns harvested during enumeration logs. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of Enumeration Techniques ## 3.1 SMTP Enumeration — Step-by-Step Logic Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP) supports three built-in commands designed for mail delivery but abused by attackers for user enumeration. > [!NOTE] > **The Three SMTP Enumeration Commands:** > 1. `VRFY ` — Verifies if a username is valid. > 2. `EXPN ` — Expands a mailing list to reveal members. > 3. `RCPT TO:` — Used during mail transaction to validate recipient. ### Logical Flow of SMTP Enumeration: ``` Step 1: Telnet to the target on port 25 Step 2: Receive the SMTP banner (e.g., "220 mail.target.com ESMTP") Step 3: Issue "HELO attacker.com" Step 4: For each guessed username, send "VRFY username" Step 5: Observe the response code → 252 "Cannot VRFY user" = user MAY exist → 550 "User unknown" = user does NOT exist → 250 "OK" = user CONFIRMED Step 6: Compile the list of confirmed valid users ``` ### Worked Example — Deriving Valid Users: Suppose an attacker tests three usernames against the mail server at `192.168.1.10`: - `VRFY admin` → Server replies `252 2.1.5 Cannot VRFY user, will try to forward` - `VRFY john` → Server replies `550 5.1.1 User unknown` - `VRFY root` → Server replies `250 2.1.5 root... Recipient OK` **Result:** The attacker now has **two high-value valid accounts**: `admin` and `root`. These will be the immediate targets for the *Password Cracking* module later. --- ## 3.2 SNMP Enumeration — Step-by-Step Logic SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) uses a *community string* as a password. Devices ship with the default `public` string enabled for read access. ### Logical Flow of SNMP Enumeration: ``` Step 1: Identify if UDP port 161 is open on the target. Step 2: Send an SNMP GET request using the community string "public". Step 3: If the device responds, query the Management Information Base (MIB). Step 4: Extract data from key MIB objects: → sysDescr (System Description — OS and version) → sysName (Hostname) → sysUpTime (System uptime — age of device) → ifNumber (Number of interfaces) → ipRouteTable (Routing table) → tcpConnTable (Active TCP connections) → arpTable (ARP cache — other IPs in the LAN) Step 5: Build a complete map of the network from the routing/ARP tables. ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **The MIB Tree — Why SNMP Enumeration is So Powerful:** > The Management Information Base (MIB) is a hierarchical database. The **root** is `iso.org.dod.internet.mgmt.mib-2`. Every numeric object has a unique OID (Object Identifier). For example, system description is `1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1`. By walking this tree (using `snmpwalk`), the attacker can extract *hundreds* of data points from a single query. --- ## 3.3 NetBIOS / SMB Enumeration — Step-by-Step Logic NetBIOS over TCP/IP uses ports **137** (name service), **138** (datagram), and **139** (session). SMB typically uses port **445**. ### Logical Flow of NetBIOS Enumeration: ``` Step 1: Issue an NBSTAT query to port 137 (e.g., nbtstat -a ) Step 2: The target responds with its NetBIOS name table containing: → Unique name (the computer itself) → Group name (domain/workgroup) → Messenger name (user logged in) → File server service running? (hex code 0x20) Step 3: Cross-reference the 16th byte suffix to identify roles: → 0x00 = Workstation → 0x20 = File Server → 0x1C = Domain Controller → 0x1E = Browser/election service Step 4: Attempt a NULL session to port 139/445 to enumerate shares: → net view \\target /user:"" "" Step 5: If NULL session succeeds, enumerate users and groups. ``` > [!NOTE] > **The NULL Session Attack — A Classic Enumeration Vector:** > A NULL session is a connection to an SMB share *without providing any credentials*. On legacy Windows systems (NT, 2000, XP without hardening), this was enabled by default and provided a treasure trove of user lists, group lists, and share lists. Modern Windows versions disable it, but it remains a favorite exam topic. --- ## 3.4 LDAP Enumeration — Step-by-Step Logic Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP) is the backbone of Active Directory. Port **389** for unencrypted LDAP, **636** for LDAPS. ### Logical Flow of LDAP Enumeration: ``` Step 1: Connect to the LDAP server using an anonymous bind (if allowed). Step 2: Request the naming contexts (the directory roots). → Example response: DC=company,DC=com Step 3: Issue an LDAP search filter to enumerate all user objects. → Filter: (objectClass=user) → This returns every username, display name, and login script path. Step 4: Enumerate group memberships. → Filter: (objectClass=group) Step 5: Map the trust relationships between organizational units (OUs). ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why LDAP Enumeration is the Crown Jewel for Active Directory Attacks:** > A single anonymous LDAP query can return *every user, every group, every computer account, and every service principal name* in the entire domain. This is the foundational step for **Kerberoasting**, **AS-REP Roasting**, and **Golden Ticket** attacks in advanced penetration testing. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Position of Enumeration in the Ethical Hacking Kill Chain This diagram illustrates where Enumeration sits in the complete attack lifecycle, and how its outputs feed into the next phase. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Phase 1: Footprinting
Passive Reconnaissance] --> scanB[Phase 2: Scanning
Identify Live Hosts and Open Ports] scanB --> enumC[Phase 3: ENUMERATION
Active Query of Services] enumC --> gainD[Phase 4: Gaining Access
Password Cracking and Exploits] gainD --> privE[Phase 5: Privilege Escalation
Become Administrator or Root] privE --> coverF[Phase 6: Covering Tracks
Clear Logs and Hide Presence] enumC -. Harvests .-> dataX[Users, Shares, Banners
SNMP Strings, AD Objects] dataX -. Feeds .-> gainD style enumC fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff style dataX fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style startA fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1864ab,color:#ffffff style scanB fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1864ab,color:#ffffff style gainD fill:#b197fc,stroke:#5f3dc4,color:#ffffff style privE fill:#b197fc,stroke:#5f3dc4,color:#ffffff style coverF fill:#b197fc,stroke:#5f3dc4,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.2 The Internal Architecture of an Enumeration Operation This block-level diagram shows how enumeration tools interact with multiple target services simultaneously to harvest different categories of information. ```mermaid flowchart LR attackerNode[Attacker Workstation
Running enum4linux, snmpwalk, nmap, ldapsearch] subgraph targetNetwork[Target Network Perimeter] smtpSrv[Mail Server
Port 25
SMTP] snmpDev[Network Devices
Port 161
SNMP] dcSrv[Domain Controller
Port 389
LDAP] fileSrv[File Server
Port 445
SMB] dnsSrv[DNS Server
Port 53
DNS] end attackerNode -->|VRFY commands| smtpSrv attackerNode -->|SNMP GET requests| snmpDev attackerNode -->|Anonymous LDAP bind| dcSrv attackerNode -->|NULL session| fileSrv attackerNode -->|AXFR zone transfer| dnsSrv smtpSrv -. Usernames .-> harvested[Harvested Intelligence Database] snmpDev -. Routing Tables, OS Info .-> harvested dcSrv -. AD Users, Groups, OUs .-> harvested fileSrv -. File Shares, Local Users .-> harvested dnsSrv -. Host Records, Subdomains .-> harvested harvested --> attackMap[Attack Map for Next Phase
Gaining Access] style attackerNode fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style harvested fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 style attackMap fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style targetNetwork fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1864ab,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Enumeration Technique ```mermaid flowchart TD qStart{Which port
is open?} qStart -->|Port 25 / 587| smtpPath[SMTP Enumeration
Use VRFY, EXPN, RCPT TO] qStart -->|Port 53| dnsPath[DNS Enumeration
Try Zone Transfer AXFR] qStart -->|Port 161| snmpPath[SNMP Enumeration
Test community string public] qStart -->|Port 137 / 139 / 445| netbiosPath[NetBIOS / SMB Enumeration
Use NULL session, RID cycling] qStart -->|Port 389 / 636| ldapPath[LDAP Enumeration
Anonymous bind and search AD] qStart -->|Port 21| ftpPath[FTP Enumeration
Test anonymous login] smtpPath --> outputA[Output: Valid User Accounts] dnsPath --> outputB[Output: Network Host Map] snmpPath --> outputC[Output: Device Configurations] netbiosPath --> outputD[Output: Shares and Users] ldapPath --> outputE[Output: AD Object Database] ftpPath --> outputF[Output: File System Access] style qStart fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1864ab,stroke-width:2px style outputA fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#ffffff style outputB fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#ffffff style outputC fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#ffffff style outputD fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#ffffff style outputE fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#ffffff style outputF fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#ffffff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q1. Define Enumeration. How is it different from Scanning? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Enumeration is the process of establishing an active connection with a target host to extract detailed information such as usernames, group names, network shares, and service banners by directly querying network protocols. The key difference from Scanning is: - **Scanning** only identifies *live hosts* and *open ports* using probe packets. It does not log in or extract application-layer data. - **Enumeration** actively interacts with the *services* listening on those ports using their native protocol commands (VRFY, LDAP bind, SNMP GET) to harvest granular intelligence like user accounts and shares. In short, scanning finds the *door*; enumeration asks *who lives behind it*. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2. List any four techniques used for enumeration with their default ports. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Technique | Default Port | |---|---| | SMTP Enumeration | **25** | | SNMP Enumeration | **161** | | NetBIOS Enumeration | **137, 139** | | LDAP Enumeration | **389** | | SMB Enumeration | **445** | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern 2024 Scheme]** **Q (a). Explain in detail the SMTP Enumeration technique. List the SMTP commands used and explain how an attacker can extract valid user accounts using this technique. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Connect to the mail server:** The attacker opens a Telnet or raw TCP connection to port **25** of the mail server. ``` telnet 192.168.1.10 25 ``` The server responds with its banner: ``` 220 mail.target.com ESMTP Postfix ``` **Step 2 — Announce the attacker:** ``` HELO attacker.com ``` Server replies: `250 mail.target.com` **Step 3 — Use the VRFY command:** The `VRFY` (Verify) command asks the mail server whether a specific mailbox exists. ``` VRFY admin ``` Possible responses: | Response Code | Meaning | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | 250 OK | Mailbox confirmed | **Valid user** | | 252 Cannot VRFY | Server will forward | **Likely valid** | | 550 User unknown | Mailbox does not exist | **Invalid user** | **Step 4 — Use the EXPN command:** ``` EXPN users-list ``` This expands a mailing list and reveals *all members* of that list, even if the individual addresses are hidden. **Step 5 — Use RCPT TO during MAIL FROM:** The attacker starts a mail transaction: ``` MAIL FROM: RCPT TO: ``` If the server replies `250 OK`, the user exists. If it replies `550 User not local`, the user does not exist. **Step 6 — Automation:** The attacker automates this using dictionaries (wordlists) to test thousands of usernames in minutes. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > Students often write only the commands (VRFY, EXPN, RCPT TO) but forget to explain the *response codes* and their meanings. Without explaining what 250, 252, and 550 mean, you lose 2–3 marks. **Always show the response-to-meaning mapping.** --- **Q (b). Describe SNMP Enumeration in detail. Explain the role of MIB and community strings. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — SNMP Architecture:** SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) is used for managing network devices like routers, switches, printers, and servers. It operates over **UDP port 161** for queries and **port 162** for traps. **Step 2 — Community Strings:** A community string functions as a *plain-text password*. Two default strings exist: - **public** — grants **read** access to device information. - **private** — grants **read/write** access (allows configuration changes). Devices shipped with default community strings are trivially enumerable. An attacker simply sends: ``` snmpwalk -v1 -c public 192.168.1.1 ``` If the device responds, it reveals its entire MIB tree. **Step 3 — The MIB (Management Information Base):** The MIB is a hierarchical database of objects that describe the device. It is organized as a tree: ``` iso (1) └── org (3) └── dod (6) └── internet (1) └── mgmt (2) └── mib-2 (1) ├── system (1) │ ├── sysDescr (1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1) │ ├── sysUpTime (1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3) │ └── sysContact (1.3.6.1.2.1.1.4) ├── interfaces (2) ├── ip (4) │ └── ipRouteTable (1.3.6.1.2.1.4.21) └── tcp (6) ``` **Step 4 — Information Harvested:** By walking the MIB, the attacker extracts: - **sysDescr** → Operating system and device model - **sysName** → Hostname - **ipRouteTable** → Full routing table of the device - **arpTable** → ARP cache (other devices on the LAN) - **tcpConnTable** → Active TCP connections **Step 5 — Countermeasures:** - Change default community strings. - Use SNMPv3 with authentication and encryption. - Block UDP port 161 at the firewall. - Disable SNMP on devices that do not need it. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write "SNMP uses port 161" but fail to explain the difference between **SNMPv1, v2c, and v3**. v1 and v2c use clear-text community strings (vulnerable). v3 adds authentication (MD5/SHA) and encryption (DES/AES). Mentioning this version-wise difference guarantees 1–2 extra marks. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern 2024 Scheme]** **Q (a). Explain NetBIOS Enumeration and the NULL Session attack. How can an attacker enumerate users and shares using this technique? (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — NetBIOS Architecture:** NetBIOS (Network Basic Input/Output System) is a legacy Microsoft protocol that allows applications on separate computers to communicate over a LAN. It operates over: - **Port 137** — Name service (NBNS) - **Port 138** — Datagram service - **Port 139** — Session service In modern networks, NetBIOS is encapsulated inside **SMB (Server Message Block)** on **port 445**. **Step 2 — NetBIOS Name Table:** Every NetBIOS-enabled machine maintains a name table with a unique 16-byte identifier (15 characters for the name + 1 byte for the service suffix). The attacker queries port 137 using tools like `nbtstat` or `nbtscan`: ``` nbtstat -a 192.168.1.20 ``` Response includes: | Name | Type | Suffix | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | WORKSTATION01 | UNIQUE | 0x00 | The computer itself | | CORPORATE | GROUP | 0x00 | Domain/workgroup name | | ADMIN | UNIQUE | 0x03 | Currently logged-in user | | WORKSTATION01 | UNIQUE | 0x20 | File server service | **Step 3 — The NULL Session Attack:** A NULL session is an *unauthenticated* SMB session. The attacker connects without providing any username or password: ``` net use \\192.168.1.20\IPC$ "" /u:"" ``` If the server permits this, the attacker can issue: - `net view \\192.168.1.20` → Lists all shared folders. - `netset.exe` or RID cycling → Extracts user accounts. **Step 4 — RID Cycling for User Enumeration:** The Relative Identifier (RID) is the last part of a Windows SID (Security Identifier). By enumerating RIDs from 500 upwards, the attacker maps usernames: ``` Administrator → RID 500 Guest → RID 501 User1 → RID 1001 User2 → RID 1002 ``` Tools like `enum4linux` or `rpcclient` automate this entire process. **Step 5 — Countermeasures:** - Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP on network adapters. - Block ports 137, 138, 139, 445 at the perimeter firewall. - Disable NULL sessions via Group Policy: *Network access: Restrict anonymous access*. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention **port 445** and only list 137/139. Since modern Windows uses 445 for direct SMB (without NetBIOS), omitting it loses 1 mark. Also, do not forget the **16th byte suffix interpretation table**. --- **Q (b). Write a detailed note on LDAP Enumeration and its significance in Active Directory attacks. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — LDAP Fundamentals:** LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) is the protocol used to access directory services like **Active Directory**, OpenLDAP, and Oracle Internet Directory. It operates on: - **Port 389** — Unencrypted LDAP - **Port 636** — LDAPS (LDAP over SSL/TLS) **Step 2 — Directory Structure:** The LDAP directory is a tree structure called the **DIT (Directory Information Tree)**: ``` Root └── dc=company,dc=com ├── ou=Users │ ├── cn=John │ ├── cn=Alice │ └── cn=Admin ├── ou=Computers └── ou=Groups ├── cn=Domain Admins └── cn=HR Team ``` **Step 3 — The Anonymous Bind Attack:** If the LDAP server allows anonymous binds (no credentials required), the attacker connects: ``` ldapsearch -x -H ldap://192.168.1.30 -b "dc=company,dc=com" ``` This returns *every object* in the directory. **Step 4 — Targeted Object Enumeration:** The attacker uses LDAP filters to extract specific data: | Filter | Returns | |---|---| | `(objectClass=user)` | All user accounts | | `(objectClass=computer)` | All domain-joined machines | | `(objectClass=group)` | All groups | | `(memberOf=CN=Domain Admins,...)` | Privileged users | | `(servicePrincipalName=*)` | Accounts with SPNs (Kerberoasting targets) | **Step 5 — Significance in Modern Attacks:** LDAP enumeration is the *gateway* to the most devastating Active Directory attacks: - **Kerberoasting** — Cracking service account passwords. - **AS-REP Roasting** — Attacking accounts without pre-authentication. - **Golden Ticket / Silver Ticket** — Forging Kerberos tickets using harvested KRBTGT credentials. - **Lateral Movement** — Identifying privileged accounts to pivot across the network. **Step 6 — Countermeasures:** - Disable anonymous LDAP binds. - Enforce LDAPS on port 636. - Implement LDAP signing and channel binding. - Apply the principle of least privilege on directory objects. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When writing about LDAP, students often confuse **LDAP** (port 389) with **LDAPS** (port 636). Mixing them up costs a mark. Also, mentioning **Active Directory-specific terms** like *Domain Controller*, *Global Catalog*, and *Service Principal Name (SPN)* shows depth and earns bonus marks. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General) > [!WARNING] > **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks in Enumeration Questions:** > 1. **Writing only tools without explaining the protocol logic.** Naming "enum4linux" or "snmpwalk" without explaining *what protocol they speak* and *what responses they expect* loses half the marks. > 2. **Forgetting the response codes.** SMTP (250/550/252) and SMB error codes are easy marks. Always include them. > 3. **Skipping countermeasures.** Every KTU question has a 1–2 mark component asking "How can this attack be prevented?" Students ignore this. > 4. **Mixing up ports.** Port 137 (NetBIOS Name) is different from Port 139 (NetBIOS Session), which is different from Port 445 (SMB Direct). Be precise. > 5. **Not mentioning real-world impact.** Connecting the technique to a *named real attack* (e.g., Kerberoasting from LDAP enumeration) demonstrates higher-order thinking per **Bloom's Taxonomy L4 (Analyze)**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Enumeration (Module 1)** - **Definition:** Enumeration = *active, direct querying* of network services to extract user accounts, shares, banners, and configuration data. - **Position:** Phase 3, after Footprinting and Scanning, before Gaining Access. - **Core Principle:** It abuses *legitimate protocol features* (VRFY, LDAP bind, SNMP GET) to harvest data the server is designed to share with authenticated peers. - **SMTP (Port 25):** Uses `VRFY`, `EXPN`, `RCPT TO`. Response codes 250 (valid), 550 (invalid), 252 (uncertain). - **SNMP (Port 161/162):** Uses community strings (`public`, `private`). Walks the **MIB tree** to extract device info, routing, and ARP tables. - **NetBIOS (Port 137/139) and SMB (Port 445):** Exposes NetBIOS name table with the **16-byte suffix**. NULL sessions allow unauthenticated share and user enumeration. RID cycling maps usernames. - **LDAP (Port 389/636):** The *most powerful* enumeration target. Anonymous binds reveal the entire Active Directory. Foundation for Kerberoasting and Golden Ticket attacks. - **DNS (Port 53):** Zone transfer (`AXFR`) misconfiguration reveals complete network topology. - **FTP (Port 21):** Anonymous login (`anonymous:anonymous`) grants unauthenticated file access. - **Countermeasures:** Disable anonymous binds, change default credentials, use encrypted protocol versions (SNMPv3, LDAPS, SMB3 with signing), enforce firewall rules, and apply the principle of least privilege. - **Detection:** Enumeration generates *auditable logs* in SIEM systems — unlike passive footprinting, it leaves a forensic trail. - **KTU Exam Focus:** Always pair each technique with its **port number**, **command/query used**, **typical output**, and **countermeasure**.

topics.title: Trojans and Backdoors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Trojans and Backdoors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Trojan Horse (Trojan)

A **Trojan Horse** is a type of malicious software (malware) that disguises itself as a legitimate, useful, or entertaining program to deceive users into installing it. Once executed, the hidden malicious payload is delivered, granting the attacker unauthorized access, data theft, or system control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Cybersecurity Glossary):** *A Trojan is a non-self-replicating malicious program that relies entirely on social engineering and user interaction for its propagation, and contains a hidden payload designed to compromise the CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) triad of the victim host.*

**Key Properties (Bolded Metrics):**
- **Propagation Type:** Non-replicating (Relies on user deception)
- **Primary Vector:** Social Engineering, Email Attachments, Fake Software
- **Default Communication Ports:** **TCP 4444**, **TCP 5555**, **TCP 9999**
- **Obfuscation Technique:** Packing, Encryption, Polymorphic Code

### Intuitive Analogy: The Greek Horse of Troy
Imagine a magnificent wooden horse offered as a "gift" to the city of Troy. The soldiers accept it, bring it inside their fortified walls, and at night, enemy soldiers hidden inside climb out and destroy the city from within. A **Trojan** works exactly like this — it looks like a gift (a free game, a PDF, a software update) but carries a hidden destructive payload. The victim willingly lets the attacker **inside their own firewall**!

---

## 1.2 Backdoor

A **Backdoor** is a covert method of bypassing standard authentication, encryption, or security mechanisms to gain remote, often undocumented, access to a computer system or application. Backdoors can be **intentionally created** by developers for maintenance or **maliciously implanted** by attackers post-compromise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** *A backdoor is a hidden entry point in a software, hardware, or network system that allows unauthorized access while evading standard logging and authentication procedures, thereby violating the principle of complete mediation.*

**Key Properties (Bolded Metrics):**
- **Stealth Level:** High (Often undetectable by standard antivirus)
- **Persistence:** Survives reboots via registry edits, scheduled tasks, or rootkits
- **Common Protocol:** **HTTPS over port 443**, **DNS tunneling over port 53**
- **Trust Violation:** Breaks the **Principle of Complete Mediation**

### Intuitive Analogy: The Secret Tunnel
Think of a bank vault with a heavy front door, guards, and cameras. A backdoor is a **secret tunnel** dug from the alley behind the bank, unknown to the guards and missing from the official blueprints. The thief can walk in anytime — quietly and without tripping alarms. In computing, a backdoor is exactly this: an **undocumented shortcut** that bypasses every defensive wall the system has.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Trojans and Backdoors are studied together because **most modern Trojans install backdoors** as their primary payload. The Trojan is the *delivery vehicle*; the Backdoor is the *weapon inside*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Table

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Trojan: The Operational Logic

A Trojan operates in a strict client-server architecture, divided into two logical components:

### Step 1: The Server Component (Victim Side)
- Embedded inside the seemingly legitimate program (the "gift").
- The hidden payload is the *actual* malicious code.

### Step 2: The Client Component (Attacker Side)
- The attacker's control console.
- Sends commands to the server component and receives stolen data.

### Step 3: The Infection Mechanism
- Victim executes the trojanized file.
- The trojan installs itself silently, often dropping itself into system directories like `C:\Windows\System32`.

### Step 4: The Command & Control (C2) Handshake
- The server component opens a **listening port** (e.g., TCP 4444) or connects *outbound* to the attacker's C2 server.
- Establishes persistent communication channel.

### Step 5: Payload Execution
- Attacker issues commands via the established channel.
- Actions include keylogging, file exfiltration, webcam access, and more.

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Trojans (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Trojan Type | Primary Function | Real-World Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Remote Access Trojan (RAT)** | Grants full remote control over victim machine | **njRAT**, **DarkComet**, **Remcos** |
| **Backdoor Trojan** | Opens a covert entry point for future access | **Back Orifice (1998)**, **NetBus** |
| **Banking Trojan** | Steals financial credentials via form grabbing | **Zeus**, **Emotet**, **TrickBot** |
| **DDoS Trojan** | Converts victim into a bot for DDoS attacks | **Mirai variants**, **BlackEnergy** |
| **Downloader Trojan** | Downloads additional malware post-infection | **Emotet (initial stage)** |
| **Rootkit Trojan** | Hides malicious processes at kernel level | **Alureon**, **ZeroAccess** |
| **Mailfinder Trojan** | Harvests email contacts for spam campaigns | **Lover Letter** |

---

## 2.3 Backdoor Types: A Deep Dive

Backdoors can exist at multiple layers of the computing stack:

| Layer | Backdoor Type | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hardware** | Firmware Backdoor | Hidden code in UEFI, BIOS, or router firmware |
| **Kernel / OS** | Rootkit Backdoor | Operates at Ring 0; nearly undetectable |
| **Application** | Code Backdoor | Hardcoded secret login (e.g., `admin/admin`) |
| **Network** | Protocol Backdoor | Covert channel via DNS, ICMP, or HTTP tunnels |
| **Cryptographic** | Algorithm Backdoor | Weakened encryption with attacker-known key |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Table (Formula / Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Key Term | Value / Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Default RAT Port** | TCP Port | **4444** |
| **Back Orifice Default Port** | UDP Port | **31337** |
| **NetBus Default Port** | TCP Port | **12345** |
| **DDoS Amplification Factor** | Metric | **$\vert \text{Traffic}_{\text{reflected}} \vert / \vert \text{Request}_{\text{original}} \vert$** |
| **Trojan Lifecycle Phases** | Stages | **5** (Creation, Propagation, Execution, C2, Payload) |
| **CIA Triad** | Security Model | **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability** |
| **Principle Violated** | Security Principle | **Complete Mediation** |
| **Reverse Shell Standard** | Connection | **Outbound from victim to attacker** |
| **Bind Shell Standard** | Connection | **Inbound to victim from attacker** |
| **Persistence Mechanism** | Technique | **Registry Run Keys, Scheduled Tasks, Services** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Understanding Trojan/Backdoor architecture is critical for designing **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)**, **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** platforms, and **IoT firmware integrity validators** — all core areas of ethical hacking in 2024+ smart infrastructure.

---

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Trojan Design

- **Why Social Engineering?** It is the *weakest link* in any security chain — the human.
- **Why Non-Replicating?** Replication triggers network-level anomaly detection; Trojans stay stealthy by spreading *slowly and silently*.
- **Why C2 Architecture?** It allows **one-to-many** attacker control, enabling botnet scale (e.g., Mirai IoT botnet).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Lifecycle Derivation

## 3.1 The Trojan Attack Lifecycle (5-Stage Derivation)

The Trojan attack follows a deterministic sequence. Each stage is mathematically/operationally distinct:

### Stage 1: Trojan Creation & Binding
The attacker takes a legitimate executable (e.g., a free game) and binds the malicious server payload to it using a *binder tool* (e.g., Yoda's Crypter, Themida).

### Stage 2: Distribution via Social Engineering
The trojanized file is distributed via email attachments, torrent sites, USB drops, or fake software updates. The success rate can be modeled as:

$$
P_{\text{infection}} = P_{\text{delivery}} \times P_{\text{open}} \times P_{\text{execute}}
$$

Where $P_{\text{delivery}}$ is the probability the file reaches the victim, $P_{\text{open}}$ is the chance they open it, and $P_{\text{execute}}$ is the chance they bypass any warning prompt.

### Stage 3: Silent Execution & Installation
Upon execution, the wrapper code runs the legitimate program (to avoid suspicion) **in parallel** with the hidden payload. The payload typically:
1. Drops itself into `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\`.
2. Adds a **registry persistence key** under `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`.
3. Disables Windows Defender real-time scanning (UAC bypass).

### Stage 4: C2 Channel Establishment
The server component initiates either a **reverse shell** (victim → attacker) or a **bind shell** (attacker → victim). The handshake can be represented as:

$$
\text{Handshake} = \{\text{SYN} \rightarrow \text{SYN-ACK} \rightarrow \text{ACK}\} \cup \text{Encrypted Auth Token}
$$

The encrypted auth token validates the attacker as the legitimate controller, preventing other attackers from hijacking the bot.

### Stage 5: Payload Delivery
The attacker now has a **persistent foothold** and can issue commands. Common payload functions include:

| Command | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| `keylog_start` | Begin recording keystrokes to a log file |
| `screenshot` | Capture and exfiltrate desktop screen |
| `webcam_on` | Activate victim's webcam covertly |
| `download <url>` | Fetch and execute additional malware |
| `ddos <target_ip>` | Launch distributed denial-of-service |

---

## 3.2 Reverse Shell vs. Bind Shell: Analytical Derivation

The choice of connection model is a strategic decision driven by **firewall topology**:

$$
\text{If } \text{Firewall}_{\text{inbound}} = \text{Blocked} \Rightarrow \text{Use Reverse Shell}
$$

$$
\text{If } \text{Firewall}_{\text{outbound}} = \text{Blocked} \Rightarrow \text{Use Bind Shell}
$$
