### Step-by-Step Logic of a Reverse Shell

1. Victim's trojan server connects *outbound* to attacker's IP on port **443** (mimics HTTPS — appears legitimate).
2. The outbound connection is permitted by the firewall (most allow HTTP/S).
3. The attacker's listener receives the connection and now has a shell.
4. All commands flow over the established TLS-encrypted tunnel, defeating DPI (Deep Packet Inspection).

### Step-by-Step Logic of a Bind Shell

1. Victim's trojan server binds and listens on a local port (e.g., **4444**).
2. Attacker connects *inbound* to victim's IP on port 4444.
3. Requires firewall to allow inbound traffic — rarely works on hardened networks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Trojan vs. Backdoor (Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Trojan | Backdoor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Malware disguised as legitimate software | Covert method to bypass authentication |
| **Replication** | Does NOT self-replicate | Does NOT self-replicate |
| **Primary Role** | Delivery vehicle | Payload / Persistent access |
| **Trigger** | Requires user execution | Runs silently in background |
| **Detection** | Signature + heuristic based | Behavioral / network anomaly based |
| **Relationship** | Often *delivers* a backdoor | Often *installed by* a trojan |
| **Persistence** | Limited (one-time) | High (survives reboots) |
| **Example** | **ILOVEYOU (2000)** | **Back Orifice (1998)** |
| **Port Reference** | Variable | Often **31337, 4444, 12345** |

---

## 3.4 The Defense Derivation: Why Detection is Hard

The probability of detecting a modern trojan/backdoor can be modeled as:

$$
P_{\text{detect}} = 1 - (1 - P_{\text{sig}})(1 - P_{\text{heur}})(1 - P_{\text{behavior}})
$$

Where $P_{\text{sig}}$ is signature detection, $P_{\text{heur}}$ is heuristic detection, and $P_{\text{behavior}}$ is behavioral sandbox detection. Since each method has **independent** failure modes, attackers only need to bypass **one** layer to succeed. This is why multi-layered (defense-in-depth) security is mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trojan Attack Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Attacker Creates Trojanized File] --> A2[Distribute via Email USB Torrent]
    A2 --> A3[Victim Downloads and Executes File]
    A3 --> A4[Wrapper Runs Legit Program]
    A4 --> A5[Hidden Payload Drops Server]
    A5 --> A6[Server Adds Registry Persistence]
    A6 --> A7[Server Initiates Reverse Shell to C2]
    A7 --> A8[Attacker Gains Full Remote Control]
    A8 --> A9[Steal Data Keylog DDoS Spy]
```

## 4.2 Trojan Client-Server Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph attackerSide[Attacker System]
        B1[Client Console GUI]
        B2[Command Builder]
        B3[C2 Listener on Port 4444]
    end
    subgraph victimSide[Victim System]
        B4[Trojan Server Payload]
        B5[Registry Persistence Hook]
        B6[Stealth Process Hider]
    end
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 -- Encrypted Channel over Port 443 or 4444 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B4 --> B6
```

## 4.3 Backdoor Types Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Backdoors] --> C2[Hardware Layer]
    C1 --> C3[Software Layer]
    C1 --> C4[Network Layer]
    C1 --> C5[Cryptographic Layer]
    C2 --> C2A[Firmware Backdoor]
    C2 --> C2B[Supply Chain Implant]
    C3 --> C3A[Code Backdoor Hardcoded Credentials]
    C3 --> C3B[Rootkit Backdoor Kernel Level]
    C4 --> C4A[DNS Tunneling]
    C4 --> C4B[ICMP Tunneling]
    C4 --> C4C[HTTP Covert Channel]
    C5 --> C5A[Weak Random Number Generator]
    C5 --> C5B[Backdoored Encryption Standard]
```

## 4.4 Reverse Shell vs Bind Shell Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph reverse[Reverse Shell Victim Initiates]
        D1[Victim Server] -- Outbound Connection on Port 443 --> D2[Attacker Listener]
    end
    subgraph bind[Bind Shell Attacker Initiates]
        D3[Victim Server Listening on Port 4444] -- Inbound Connection Required --> D4[Attacker Client]
    end
```

## 4.5 Trojan Infection Lifecycle (State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant
    Dormant --> Deployed: Social Engineering
    Deployed --> Executed: User Clicks
    Executed --> Installed: Dropper Payload
    Installed --> Active: C2 Connection Established
    Active --> Payload: Attacker Issues Command
    Payload --> Active: Awaiting Next Command
    Active --> [*]: Detected and Removed
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a **Trojan Horse** and a **Backdoor**. List any **three** well-known Trojans with their default ports.

**Model Answer:**
A **Trojan Horse** is a non-replicating malicious program that disguises itself as legitimate software to trick users into executing it. A **Backdoor** is a covert mechanism that bypasses normal authentication to provide persistent unauthorized access. Trojans are the *delivery vehicle*; Backdoors are often the *payload*.
- **njRAT** → Default port **5552**
- **DarkComet** → Default port **1604**
- **Back Orifice** → Default port **31337**

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** What is a **Reverse Shell**, and why do attackers prefer it over a Bind Shell in modern networks?

**Model Answer:**
A **Reverse Shell** is a connection where the victim machine initiates an *outbound* connection to the attacker's listener. Attackers prefer it because:
1. Most firewalls **block inbound** but allow **outbound** traffic.
2. It uses ports like **443** (HTTPS), blending with normal web traffic.
3. It bypasses **NAT** without port forwarding.
4. It evades many **IDS/IPS** signature rules.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **different types of Trojans** with suitable examples. Discuss the architecture of a **Remote Access Trojan (RAT)** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Trojan** is a malicious program disguised as legitimate software. The major types are:

1. **Remote Access Trojan (RAT):** Provides the attacker full remote control. Example: **njRAT**, **Remcos**.
2. **Backdoor Trojan:** Opens a covert entry point. Example: **Back Orifice**.
3. **Banking Trojan:** Steals financial credentials. Example: **Zeus**, **TrickBot**.
4. **DDoS Trojan:** Uses victim as a bot. Example: **Mirai**.
5. **Downloader Trojan:** Fetches additional malware. Example: **Emotet**.

**RAT Architecture:**
The RAT has two components: a **Client** (attacker-side GUI console) and a **Server** (victim-side payload). The server is delivered via social engineering, installs silently with **registry persistence** at `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`, and initiates a **reverse shell** to the attacker's C2 server on port **4444** or **443**. The attacker can then issue commands for keylogging, screen capture, file exfiltration, and webcam spying.

**(b)** Describe the **various methods of Trojan detection and prevention**. Mention the role of **Tripwire**, **Integrity Checkers**, and **Behavioral Analysis** in modern EDR systems. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Detection Methods:**
1. **Signature-Based Detection:** Compares file hashes against known malware databases (AV engines).
2. **Heuristic Analysis:** Examines code structure for suspicious patterns (e.g., packed executables, API call anomalies).
3. **Behavioral Analysis (Sandboxing):** Executes the file in an isolated VM and monitors system calls, network connections, and registry modifications.
4. **Integrity Checkers:** Tools like **Tripwire** monitor critical system files and registry keys for unauthorized changes.
5. **Network-Based Detection:** Monitors outbound C2 traffic on suspicious ports (4444, 31337, 1604).

**Prevention Methods:**
1. **User Education** — Most important defense against social engineering.
2. **Least Privilege** — Run applications without admin rights.
3. **Application Whitelisting** — Only signed/approved software can execute.
4. **Patching & Updates** — Close known backdoor vulnerabilities.
5. **Firewall Rules** — Block known malicious IPs and C2 domains.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Supplementary Exam 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture and working of a Trojan Horse attack**. Describe the **5 lifecycle stages** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Trojan Horse attack** follows a strict 5-stage lifecycle:

1. **Creation & Binding:** Attacker takes a legitimate executable (e.g., a PDF reader) and binds the malicious server payload to it using a binder/crypter tool.
2. **Distribution (Social Engineering):** The trojanized file is spread via phishing emails, fake downloads, or USB drops.
3. **Execution & Installation:** When the victim runs the file, the wrapper executes the legitimate program in parallel with the hidden payload. The payload drops itself into `%APPDATA%` and creates **registry persistence**.
4. **Command & Control (C2) Establishment:** The server component opens a **reverse shell** to the attacker's C2 server, usually on port **443** (HTTPS) to evade firewalls.
5. **Payload Execution:** The attacker issues commands — keylogging, data exfiltration, DDoS, ransomware deployment.

**Diagram (ASCII for Exam Sheet):**
```
Attacker --> [C2 Server on Port 4444] <-- Internet <-- [Victim PC running Trojan Server]
                                              |
                                              v
                                    Steal Data / Keylog / DDoS
```

**(b)** What are **Backdoors**? Explain their **types, working mechanism, and defense strategies** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Backdoor** is a hidden method of bypassing normal authentication, granting persistent unauthorized access. It violates the **Principle of Complete Mediation**.

**Types:**
- **Code Backdoors:** Hardcoded credentials or hidden admin accounts (e.g., `admin/backdoor`).
- **Rootkit Backdoors:** Operate at kernel level (Ring 0), hiding malicious processes.
- **Firmware Backdoors:** Embedded in UEFI/BIOS/router firmware — survive OS reinstallation.
- **Network Backdoors:** Covert channels via DNS tunneling, ICMP, or HTTP.
- **Cryptographic Backdoors:** Weaknesses in encryption algorithms known only to attackers.

**Working Mechanism:**
1. Backdoor is implanted post-exploitation (often *by a Trojan*).
2. Registers a **service** or **scheduled task** for persistence.
3. Listens on a covert port or beacons outbound periodically.
4. Authenticates attacker via encrypted token.
5. Grants shell or remote control without logging.

**Defense Strategies:**
- **Regular integrity checks** (Tripwire, OSSEC).
- **Disable unused services and ports.**
- **BIOS/UEFI firmware verification** (TPM-based measured boot).
- **Outbound traffic monitoring** for C2 beacons.
- **Patch management** to remove known vulnerabilities.
- **Application whitelisting and least privilege.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Virus/Worm with Trojan.** Examiners specifically test whether you know Trojans **do NOT self-replicate**. Writing "Trojan replicates itself" costs full marks.
> 2. **Always mention the port number.** Writing "RAT uses port 4444" shows technical depth; omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Reverse Shell vs Bind Shell — draw the direction of connection.** Many students write the wrong direction (attacker → victim for reverse shell). Correct: **Victim → Attacker**.
> 4. **Distinguish Trojan (delivery) from Backdoor (payload).** Examiners award marks specifically for this conceptual clarity.
> 5. **Mention the CIA Triad and Complete Mediation** in definition questions — these are KTU 2024 favorite keywords.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ☐ **Trojan = Non-replicating malware disguised as legitimate software.**
- ☐ **Backdoor = Covert persistent access mechanism bypassing authentication.**
- ☐ **Trojan is the VEHICLE; Backdoor is often the PAYLOAD.**
- ☐ **Key Ports:** 4444 (RAT), 31337 (Back Orifice), 12345 (NetBus), 1604 (DarkComet), 5552 (njRAT).
- ☐ **Reverse Shell:** Victim → Attacker (outbound, preferred, evades firewall).
- ☐ **Bind Shell:** Attacker → Victim (inbound, rarely works on hardened networks).
- ☐ **5 Lifecycle Stages:** Creation → Distribution → Execution → C2 → Payload.
- ☐ **5 Detection Methods:** Signature, Heuristic, Behavioral, Integrity, Network-based.
- ☐ **5 Prevention Methods:** User education, Least privilege, Whitelisting, Patching, Firewall.
- ☐ **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (always mention in defs).
- ☐ **Principle of Complete Mediation** is violated by every backdoor.
- ☐ **Examples to memorize:** Back Orifice, NetBus, njRAT, DarkComet, Zeus, Emotet, Mirai, ILOVEYOU.
- ☐ **RAT Architecture:** Client (attacker) ↔ Server (victim), persistent via Registry Run keys.
- ☐ **Defence-in-Depth** — multiple independent detection layers required because:
$$P_{\text{detect}} = 1 - (1 - P_{\text{sig}})(1 - P_{\text{heur}})(1 - P_{\text{behavior}})$$

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw the **C2 architecture diagram** for any Trojan-related 14-mark question. It is worth **2–3 easy marks** and is often the difference between a B-grade and an A-grade.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viruses and Worms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Viruses and Worms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Computer Virus** is a malicious software program (malware) that, when executed, replicates itself by modifying other legitimate computer programs and inserting its own code. The execution of the infected host program is what triggers the virus, making it a form of **parasitic malware** that requires a host file or boot sector to propagate.

A **Computer Worm** is a standalone malicious software program that replicates itself across computer networks without requiring a host file, human interaction, or a trigger program. Worms exploit vulnerabilities in operating systems and network protocols to self-propagate autonomously, consuming bandwidth and system resources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The 2024 Scheme explicitly categorizes viruses and worms under the broader umbrella of **Malware** (Malicious Software). Students must clearly distinguish between a virus (host-dependent) and a worm (host-independent, network-aware).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Biological Analogy:**
> - **Virus** behaves like the **biological influenza virus**. A flu virus cannot travel by itself; it needs a host cell to inject its genetic material and hijack cellular machinery to reproduce. Similarly, a computer virus attaches itself to a `.exe`, `.doc`, or boot sector (the "host cell") and uses the host's execution to replicate. You must "open" the infected file to get "sick."
> - **Worm** behaves like an **airborne airborne pathogen**, such as measles spreading through a ventilation system. It does not need a host file — it crawls independently through network connections (LAN, internet, email servers) and infects any vulnerable machine it encounters, like air spreading through ductwork.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Standard Metrics

- **Payload**: The malicious action portion of the malware (data deletion, backdoor installation, DDoS trigger). Measured in **lines of code (LOC)**.
- **Replication Vector**: The medium of spread (file, email, network port, USB). Measured in **propagation speed (systems/hour)**.
- **Trigger Condition**: The event that activates the payload (date, user action, network availability). Measured in **boolean state (dormant/active)**.
- **Detection Evasion Rate**: Polymorphic engines can mutate the **signature** (a unique byte pattern) every **few seconds**, rendering signature-based antivirus ineffective.
- **Bandwidth Consumption**: Worms like Code Red generated up to **2.4 GB per second** of HTTP traffic during outbreaks.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** "Virus = Vehicle needs Driver" (host + user). "Worm = Autonomous Drone" (self-replicating over network).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Parameters

### 2.1 Operational Lifecycle — Phase-by-Phase Breakdown

A malware analyst must understand that both viruses and worms follow a **four-phase lifecycle**, originally formalized by **Fred Cohen (1986)** in his seminal paper *"Computer Viruses: Theory and Experiments."*

#### **Phase 1: Dormant Phase**
- The malware lies inactive in the system. The virus is attached to a host file, but the host has not yet been executed. The worm waits for network availability or a scheduled time.
- **Why it matters**: Allows the malware to evade initial scans and time-based detection heuristics.

#### **Phase 2: Propagation Phase**
- The malware replicates itself. A virus copies its code into other host files. A worm scans the network for vulnerable IP addresses, brute-forces ports, and dispatches copies of itself.
- **Why it matters**: This is the exponential-growth stage. A single infected host can become 1,000 infected hosts within **24 hours** under ideal conditions.

#### **Phase 3: Triggering Phase**
- The condition for payload activation is met. Common triggers include:
  - A specific date/time (e.g., **Michelangelo Virus** activates on March 6)
  - A user action (opening a file)
  - A count threshold (after N replications)
  - Remote command from a C2 (Command-and-Control) server
- **Why it matters**: This separation of replication and destruction is a tactical design that maximizes spread before detection.

#### **Phase 4: Execution Phase (Payload Delivery)**
- The malicious action is performed:
  - **Data Corruption/Deletion** (e.g., CIH/Chernobyl Virus)
  - **Backdoor Installation** (e.g., Mydoom.A opened port 3127)
  - **DDoS Trigger** (e.g., Mydoom launched DDoS against www.sco.com)
  - **Credential Theft** (keyloggers, screen scrapers)
  - **Cryptocurrency Mining** (modern cryptojacking worms)
- **Why it matters**: The payload is what causes financial and operational damage.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **single most exam-relevant** artifact for Module 1. Memorize the dichotomy between host-dependence, replication vector, and trigger mechanism.

| Parameter | Computer Virus | Computer Worm |
|---|---|---|
| Host Dependency | **Mandatory** (attaches to .exe, .doc, .mbr) | **None** (standalone executable) |
| Primary Vector | File sharing, USB, email attachments | Network protocols, OS vulnerabilities |
| Human Intervention | **Required** (must open the file) | **Not required** (autonomous) |
| Replication Speed | Slow (user-dependent) | Very fast (network-speed, exponential) |
| Detection Difficulty | Moderate (signature-based works initially) | High (polymorphic worms mutate) |
| Primary Damage | Data corruption, file deletion | Bandwidth exhaustion, DDoS, lateral spread |
| Detection Method | Heuristic + signature scanning | Anomaly-based IDS/IPS, network behavior analysis |
| Carrier File | Yes (寄生 / parasitic) | No (独立 / autonomous) |
| Example | ILOVEYOU (2000), CIH (1998) | Code Red (2001), Conficker (2008), Stuxnet (2010) |
| Self-Contained | No | Yes |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding viruses and worms is foundational for:

- **Antivirus Engine Development**: Signature databases, heuristic engines, and sandboxing rely on lifecycle knowledge.
- **Network Security Architecture**: Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS) use worm-propagation signatures (e.g., Snort rules for Code Red's `GET /default.ida?` payload).
- **Incident Response (IR)**: First responders use lifecycle phase identification to choose between **containment**, **eradication**, and **recovery** strategies.
- **IoT Security**: Modern IoT botnets (e.g., **Mirai (2016)**) are worm variants that exploit default credentials in cameras, DVRs, and routers. The KTU 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on this intersection in Module 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 2016 **Mirai worm** infected over **600,000 IoT devices** within **20 hours** by scanning Telnet ports (23) and using a dictionary of **61 default username/password pairs**. This is now a mandatory case-study for IoT security courses.

### 2.4 The Cohen Three-State Model (Foundational Theorem)

Fred Cohen's formal model defines the system state transitions:

$$
S = \{S_{clean}, S_{infected}, S_{payload\_active}\}
$$

The transition function $\delta$ maps a state to the next state under a triggering event $T$:

$$
\delta(S_{clean}, V_{execute}) \rightarrow S_{infected}
$$

$$
\delta(S_{infected}, T_{trigger}) \rightarrow S_{payload\_active}
$$

Where $V_{execute}$ represents the event of running the infected host file, and $T_{trigger}$ is the payload activation condition.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Detailed Case-Study

### 3.1 Detailed Lifecycle Derivation for a Polymorphic Worm

We will mathematically trace the propagation of a self-replicating polymorphic worm across a Class B-sized network of size $N = 65{,}534$ vulnerable hosts. The worm uses **half-interval scanning** with mutation rate $m$.

**Step 1: Define the propagation rate per time unit.**

Let $I(t)$ = number of infected hosts at time $t$. The classic **SIR (Susceptible-Infected-Recovered)** epidemiological model applies:

$$
\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot I(t) \cdot \left(\frac{N - I(t)}{N}\right)
$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = transmission coefficient (probes/second a single infected host generates)
- $N$ = total vulnerable population
- $I(t)$ = infected population at time $t$

**Step 2: Substitute realistic values for a typical Internet worm.**

Let $\beta = 10$ probes per second (a conservative value; Slammer achieved $\beta \approx 4000$). Let $N = 65{,}534$ (a single Class B subnet). At $t = 0$, $I(0) = 1$.

$$
\frac{dI}{dt} = 10 \cdot I(t) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{I(t)}{65534}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Solve the logistic differential equation.**

The general solution to the logistic equation $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta I(1 - \frac{I}{N})$ is:

$$
I(t) = \frac{N}{1 + \left(\frac{N - I_0}{I_0}\right) e^{-\beta t}}
$$

**Step 4: Apply initial condition $I(0) = 1$.**

$$
I(t) = \frac{65534}{1 + 65533 \cdot e^{-10t}}
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate at $t = 1$ second (first wave).**

$$
I(1) = \frac{65534}{1 + 65533 \cdot e^{-10}}
$$

We compute $e^{-10} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-5}$.

$$
I(1) = \frac{65534}{1 + 65533 \cdot (4.54 \times 10^{-5})}
$$

$$
I(1) = \frac{65534}{1 + 2.976} = \frac{65534}{3.976} \approx 16{,}484 \text{ hosts}
$$

**Interpretation:** In just **1 second**, the worm infects approximately **25.15%** of the entire vulnerable subnet. This is precisely the speed observed in **Slammer (2003)**, which infected **75,000 hosts in 10 minutes**.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Virus vs. Worm

| Axis of Comparison | Virus Operational Detail | Worm Operational Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **Replication Trigger** | User double-clicks infected `.exe` | Worm locates open port automatically |
| **Spread Geometry** | Tree-based, branch-by-branch (user shares file) | Mesh-based, exponential (network broadcast) |
| **Code Size (avg)** | 1 KB to 50 KB (must fit in host slack space) | 50 KB to 500 KB (standalone binary) |
| **Signature Length** | Fixed 32-256 byte pattern | Polymorphic: mutates per generation |
| **Persistence Mechanism** | Modifies registry/host file | Installs as service/daemon/rootkit |
| **Network Awareness** | None (file-bound) | Built-in TCP/IP stack utilization |
| **Payload Common Examples** | File overwrite, MBR corruption | DDoS, ransomware dropper, crypto mining |
| **Defense Evasion** | Stealth techniques (intercept INT 13h) | Code mutation, encrypted payload, rootkit cloaking |
| **Removal Difficulty** | Moderate (clean host files + restore backups) | High (must patch vulnerability + remove backdoor) |
| **Economic Damage (Top Event)** | Mydoom: $38 billion (2004) | ILOVEYOU: $15 billion (2000) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table 3.2:** A common student error is to claim ILOVEYOU is a "worm" because it spread via email. KTU examiners classify ILOVEYOU as a **virus** because it required the user to open the `.vbs` attachment (host + human trigger). True worms (like Code Red) did not require any user action.

### 3.3 Case Study: The Stuxnet Worm (2010) — The Most Engineered Worm in History

Stuxnet is the gold-standard exam case study because it combined virus-like and worm-like behaviors. We decompose it into its **5 propagation modules**:

**Module A: Windows Vulnerability Exploitation**
- Exploited **CVE-2010-2568** (LNK file vulnerability) and **CVE-2010-2729** (Print Spooler).
- Allowed zero-day remote code execution.

**Module B: Network Spreading Engine**
- Used **SMB (Server Message Block)** protocol on port 445 to propagate laterally.
- Targeted **Siemens Step 7** industrial control systems.

**Module C: USB Propagation Module**
- Used a signed driver to write `.lnk` files on USB drives — a virus-like delivery.

**Module D: Rootkit Cloaking**
- Installed a Windows rootkit to hide its DLLs from the process list.

**Module E: PLC Payload**
- Modified the **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** ladder logic to spin centrifuges at destructive speeds while reporting normal values to operators.

**Why this case study matters for IoT Security:** Stuxnet demonstrated that worms can target **cyber-physical systems** (IoT/OT convergence), making it a direct prerequisite topic for Module 1 of PBCCT604.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "Differentiate virus and worm with a real-world example," answer: "**Stuxnet** is a worm with virus-like features; it used both network propagation and USB host files to cross an air-gapped network in the Natanz nuclear facility."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Virus Lifecycle Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host File Executed] --> B[Virus Code Triggered]
    B --> C{Check Trigger Condition}
    C -- Not Met --> D[Replicate into Other Hosts]
    C -- Met --> E[Execute Payload]
    D --> F[Return to Dormant]
    F --> A
    E --> G[Damage: Data Loss, Backdoor, Corruption]
    G --> H[End of Lifecycle]
```

### 4.2 Worm Autonomous Propagation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Worm Activated on Host] --> B[Scan Network Ports]
    B --> C{Vulnerable Host Found}
    C -- No --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Exploit Vulnerability]
    D --> E[Transfer Worm Copy]
    E --> F[Execute on New Host]
    F --> G[Mutate Signature]
    G --> B
    F --> H[Trigger Payload Module]
    H --> I[Bandwidth Exhaustion or DDoS]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Virus vs. Worm Interaction Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VS[Host File Environment]
        V1[Legitimate .exe]
        V2[Infected .exe]
        V3[Boot Sector]
    end
    subgraph WS[Network Environment]
        W1[Worm Instance 1]
        W2[Worm Instance 2]
        W3[Worm Instance N]
    end
    subgraph PS[Payload Engine]
        P1[Data Corruption]
        P2[Backdoor]
        P3[DDoS Module]
        P4[Keylogger]
    end
    V2 --> P1
    V3 --> P1
    W1 --> P2
    W1 --> P3
    W2 --> P4
    W3 --> P1
    W1 -.propagates via SMB.-> W2
    W2 -.propagates via Telnet.-> W3
```

### 4.4 Detection-Defense Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Signature-Based AV] --> L2[Layer 2: Heuristic Engine]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Behavior-Based Sandbox]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Network IDS/IPS]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Anomaly Detection via ML]
    L5 --> L6[Quarantine and Forensics]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Diagram 4.1 shows the closed-loop nature of a virus (must return to a host). Diagram 4.2 shows the open-loop, exponential nature of a worm. Diagram 4.3 maps the architectural separation between virus and worm operating environments. Diagram 4.4 presents the **defense-in-depth** principle that security architects use to counter both threat classes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define a computer virus. List any four types of viruses.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **computer virus** is a malicious program that, when executed, replicates by inserting its code into other legitimate programs (host files), requiring a host and a trigger event (usually human-initiated) to propagate.

Four types of viruses (1/2 mark each):
1. **File Virus** — infects executable files (`.exe`, `.com`).
2. **Boot Sector Virus** — infects the Master Boot Record (MBR).
3. **Macro Virus** — infects documents with macro languages (e.g., `.doc` with VBA).
4. **Polymorphic Virus** — encrypts/mutates its code to evade signature detection.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a virus and a worm in terms of host dependency, propagation speed, and trigger mechanism.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Virus | Worm |
|---|---|---|
| Host Dependency | Requires host file | Standalone |
| Propagation Speed | Slow (user-mediated) | Very fast (network-speed) |
| Trigger Mechanism | User executes host | Autonomous (vulnerability exploit) |

(1 mark for each correctly contrasted criterion.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

**Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]** With neat diagrams, explain the lifecycle phases of a computer virus. Discuss the four operational phases with a real-world example. (14 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Lifecycle Explanation — 7 Marks**

The computer virus lifecycle consists of four operational phases as formalized by Fred Cohen:

1. **Dormant Phase:** The virus is attached to a host file but is inactive. The host program functions normally. Example: A `.doc` file with embedded macro virus does nothing until the user opens it in Word.

2. **Propagation Phase:** The virus copies itself into other programs on the host and across storage media (USB, network shares). It may use slack space, append its code to executables, or insert itself at the entry point.

3. **Triggering Phase:** The virus waits for a specific event — date/time (Michelangelo: March 6), user count (after N file opens), or external command. The payload remains inactive until this condition is met.

4. **Execution (Payload) Phase:** The malicious action is performed. In the **CIH/Chernobyl virus (1998)**, the payload overwrote the first 2048 sectors of the hard drive and corrupted the BIOS flash memory, rendering the motherboard unbootable.

**(b) Diagram and Real-World Example — 7 Marks**

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dormant: Virus in Host File] --> B[Propagation: Copy to Other Files]
    B --> C[Triggering: Condition Met]
    C --> D[Execution: Payload Delivered]
    D --> E[Damage: Data Loss, System Crash]
```

**Real-World Example: CIH/Chernobyl Virus (June 1998)**
- Created by **Chen Ing-Hau** (Taiwan).
- Payload activated on **April 26** (Chernobyl anniversary).
- Estimated damage: **$1 billion**; bricked millions of PCs.
- **Lesson for IoT Security:** Modern CIH-like malware targets firmware/BIOS of IoT devices, demonstrating the need for signed firmware updates (Module 1 of PBCCT604 emphasizes this).

---

**Q3 (B). [Internal Choice Alternative — 14 Marks]** Explain the propagation mechanism of a computer worm. Compare and contrast a worm with a virus. Describe a recent worm attack that targeted IoT systems. (14 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Worm Propagation Mechanism — 7 Marks**

A computer worm propagates through the following autonomous mechanism:

1. **Network Scanning:** The worm generates IP addresses (sequentially, randomly, or via a hit-list). It probes common ports (445/SMB, 23/Telnet, 80/HTTP, 22/SSH).
2. **Vulnerability Exploitation:** Once a vulnerable host is found, the worm exploits a known CVE (e.g., buffer overflow in a network daemon) to gain remote code execution.
3. **Self-Transfer:** The worm transfers a copy of itself (often via TFTP, HTTP, or SMB) to the new host.
4. **Execution & Resumption:** The copy executes on the new host, then begins scanning the network from there — creating exponential growth.

**Example: Conficker Worm (2008)** — used **MS08-067** vulnerability in the Windows Server service, infecting **9 million** PCs across **200 countries** by generating **250 domain names per day** for its C2 infrastructure.

**(b) Comparison and IoT Case Study — 7 Marks**

Comparison (as per Section 2.2 table above) — 4 marks.

**IoT Worm Case Study: Mirai (October 2016)** — 3 marks.

- **Author:** Anonymous (later identified as Paras Jha).
- **Vector:** Telnet port 23 with default credentials dictionary.
- **Target:** IoT devices (cameras, DVRs, routers) — 61 default username/password pairs.
- **Scale:** 600,000+ devices infected in 20 hours; launched a **1.2 Tbps DDoS** against Dyn DNS, disrupting Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and GitHub.
- **Lesson:** Highlights why Module 1 of PBCCT604 mandates the study of worms — IoT security is impossible without understanding self-propagating malware.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (read carefully before submitting):**
> 1. **Calling ILOVEYOU a "worm"**: It is a **virus** (VBScript attachment requiring user action). Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this misclassification.
> 2. **Omitting the host file from virus definition**: A virus without a host is conceptually a worm. Always state the host-dependency.
> 3. **Confusing "polymorphic" with "metamorphic"**: Polymorphic viruses **encrypt** with varying keys (signature changes); metamorphic viruses **rewrite their own code** (no constant signature).
> 4. **Skipping the diagram**: In a 14-mark question, the **diagram is worth 3–4 marks**. Drawing only a text flowchart without arrows/markers forfeits partial credit.
> 5. **Forgetting the IoT angle**: In the 2024 Scheme, Module 1 explicitly bridges malware to IoT. Failing to mention Mirai, Stuxnet, or a similar IoT-relevant worm loses **at least 2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Virus = Host + Trigger + User Action.** No host = not a virus.
- **Worm = Standalone + Network + Autonomous.** No network = worm cannot spread.
- **Lifecycle has 4 phases:** Dormant → Propagation → Triggering → Execution.
- **Polymorphic** = encryption-based mutation. **Metamorphic** = code-rewriting mutation.
- **Top historical events to remember for KTU 2024:**
  - **CIH (1998)** — BIOS-killer virus.
  - **ILOVEYOU (2000)** — email virus, $15B damage.
  - **Code Red (2001)** — IIS web server worm.
  - **Slammer (2003)** — fastest spread (75,000 hosts in 10 min).
  - **Conficker (2008)** — domain-flux C2 worm.
  - **Stuxnet (2010)** — first cyber-physical weapon (worm + virus hybrid).
  - **Mirai (2016)** — IoT DDoS worm, 1.2 Tbps.
- **Defense-in-Depth**: Signature AV → Heuristic → Sandbox → IDS/IPS → ML Anomaly.
- **For KTU 2024 Module 1**: Always bridge your answer back to **IoT security** — the course is *Ethical Hacking & IoT Security*, not generic malware analysis.
- **The Cohen Model** ($S = \{S_{clean}, S_{infected}, S_{payload\_active}\}$) is the formal mathematical foundation; cite it in 14-mark answers for full marks.
- **The Logistic Growth Equation** $I(t) = \frac{N}{1 + (\frac{N-I_0}{I_0})e^{-\beta t}}$ explains why worms exhibit S-curve saturation rather than unbounded growth (vulnerability patching eventually throttles the worm).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** "**V**iruses need **V**ehicles; **W**orms need **W**ires. Both end with **W**reckage."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proxy and Packet Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Proxy and Packet Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Proxy Server — The "Middleman of the Internet"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exam-Ready):**
> A **Proxy Server** is an intermediary gateway application/software that sits between an end-user (client) and a destination server, evaluating, forwarding, and mediating requests on behalf of the client while providing functions such as caching, anonymity, access control, content filtering, and traffic logging.

In simpler terms — a proxy is **not the destination itself**, but a *controlled checkpoint* that you deliberately insert into the path so that every request is inspected, modified, or anonymized before reaching the target.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Reception Desk

Imagine a corporate office where no employee can directly meet a visitor. Every visitor must report to the **reception desk** first. The receptionist:
- Checks the visitor's identity (access control).
- Forwards the message to the correct employee (request forwarding).
- Keeps a record of who came and when (logging).
- Sometimes responds on behalf of the employee (caching).
- Hides the employee's actual cabin location (anonymity).

The **reception desk = Proxy Server**, the **visitor = Client**, and the **employee = Destination Web Server**.

### 1.1.1 Types of Proxy Servers (KTU High-Yield)

| Proxy Type | Direction of Operation | Primary Purpose | KTU Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Proxy** | Client → Internet | Anonymity, content filtering, caching | "Client-side middleman" |
| **Reverse Proxy** | Internet → Internal Server | Load balancing, SSL offload, WAF | "Server-side middleman" |
| **Transparent Proxy** | Client unaware of proxy | ISP-level filtering, caching | "Invisible gatekeeper" |
| **Anonymous Proxy** | Hides client IP | Privacy | "Identity mask" |
| **SOCKS Proxy (Layer 5)** | Any protocol at Session layer | Versatile tunneling | "Protocol-agnostic tunnel" |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** For Ethical Hacking, the **Forward Proxy** is most relevant — it is used in penetration testing for **anonymity, request redirection, and bypassing IP-based blacklists**.

---

## 1.2 Packet Filtering — The "Smart Bouncer at the Door"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exam-Ready):**
> **Packet Filtering** is a network-layer security mechanism (typically implemented on a router, firewall, or Layer-3 switch) that inspects the header of every traversing packet — specifically source IP, destination IP, source port, destination port, and protocol — and **decides to ALLOW, DROP, or REJECT** the packet based on a predefined rule set (Access Control List / ACL).

### Conceptual Analogy — Airport Security Checkpoint

Picture an airport where only certain passengers are allowed to board:
- A security officer checks your **passport** (Source IP), your **boarding pass destination** (Destination IP), your **flight number** (Port number), and your **airline** (Protocol).
- If your details match a "no-fly" list, you are **denied entry (DROP)** or sent to a holding area (**REJECT** with notification).
- If all details are legitimate, you **pass through (ALLOW)**.

The **security officer = Packet Filter**, the **rule list = Access Control List (ACL)**, and the **passenger = IP Packet**.

### 1.2.1 Where Packet Filtering Operates in the OSI Model

| OSI Layer | Header Field Inspected | Example Filter Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 3 (Network) | Source / Destination IP Address | Block traffic from 10.0.0.5 |
| Layer 4 (Transport) | TCP/UDP Source & Destination Port | Block Telnet (port 23) |
| Layer 4 (Flags) | TCP Flags (SYN, ACK, FIN) | Block unsolicited SYN packets |
| Layer 2 (Datalink) | MAC Address (rare in pure filtering) | MAC binding |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Packet filtering works **ONLY on header information**, never on the packet's payload (data content). If you need to inspect content (e.g., URLs, keywords, malware signatures), you need a **Proxy**, **Stateful Inspection Firewall**, or **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)**.

---

## 1.3 Why Both Topics Are Joined in KTU Module 1

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Reasoning:** Proxies and Packet Filters are the **two foundational traffic-interception technologies** that every ethical hacker must understand because:
> 1. They are the **first line of defense** in any network.
> 2. They are also the **first targets** for bypass attacks (e.g., tunneling, fragmentation, protocol abuse).
> 3. The **CO (Course Outcome) mapping** for PBCCT604 Module 1 includes CO1: *Understand network security primitives* — and both proxy and packet filtering are direct primitives.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Proxy Server — Deep Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Working of a Forward Proxy (Step-by-Step Logic)

A forward proxy intercepts outbound traffic from internal clients. The operational flow is:

1. **Client initiates a request** — e.g., `GET http://example.com/page.html` is sent by the browser.
2. **Request is redirected to the proxy** — This redirection happens via:
   - Manual browser configuration (proxy IP + port).
   - Automatic Proxy Auto-Configuration (PAC) file.
   - Transparent redirection by a gateway (WPAD — Web Proxy Auto-Discovery).
3. **Proxy receives and inspects the request** — It checks:
   - Destination URL (allowed domains list).
   - Authentication credentials of the client.
   - Internal policy (e.g., no social media during work hours).
4. **Proxy generates an outbound request on behalf of the client** — The packet leaving the proxy has the **proxy's own IP** as source, not the client's.
5. **Destination server processes the request and responds to the proxy.**
6. **Proxy relays the response back to the client**, optionally caching the response for future identical requests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Exam:** The *destination server never directly sees the client*. From the server's perspective, the request originated from the proxy. This provides **anonymity for the client**.

### 2.1.2 Working of a Reverse Proxy

The reverse proxy is placed in front of one or more **internal servers**. Clients send requests to a single public-facing URL (the reverse proxy), which then:
- Determines which backend server should handle the request (load balancing).
- Caches responses from slow backends.
- Performs SSL termination (decrypts HTTPS, reducing load on backends).
- Acts as a Web Application Firewall (WAF).

**Example:** When you access `www.google.com`, you are actually talking to a Google reverse proxy (e.g., GFE — Google Front End), which forwards your query to one of thousands of backend servers.

### 2.1.3 Proxy in Ethical Hacking Context

In penetration testing, the attacker deploys a proxy to:
- **Evade IP-based detection** — The target logs see the proxy's IP, not the attacker's.
- **Chain requests (proxy chains)** — Use multiple proxies in series (e.g., via Tor or `proxychains`).
- **Intercept and modify** traffic using tools like **Burp Suite** or **mitmproxy**.

---

## 2.2 Packet Filtering — Deep Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Three-Action Decision Model

Every packet filter must take one of three actions when a packet arrives:

$$A(p) = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT} & \text{if } p \text{ matches an ALLOW rule first} \\ \text{DROP} & \text{if } p \text{ matches a DENY rule first, silently discarded} \\ \text{REJECT} & \text{if } p \text{ matches a DENY rule, ICMP unreachable sent back} \end{cases}$$

- **ACCEPT** — Packet is forwarded normally.
- **DROP** — Packet is silently discarded; the sender receives **no notification**. This is preferred in security to avoid revealing the firewall's existence.
- **REJECT** — Packet is discarded and the sender is **notified** via an ICMP message (e.g., "Destination Unreachable"). Useful in debugging.

### 2.2.2 Two Major Variants of Packet Filtering

| Feature | **Stateless (Simple) Packet Filter** | **Stateful Packet Inspection (SPI)** |
|---|---|---|
| Inspection basis | Each packet inspected **independently** | Tracks **connection state** in a state table |
| Memory/Resources | Very low | High (state table must be maintained) |
| Defeats | Easily defeated by **IP spoofing**, fragmentation | Can detect **out-of-order packets**, half-open scans |
| Speed | Extremely fast | Slightly slower |
| Example Tool | Basic router ACL, `iptables` (stateless mode) | `iptables` with `-m state`, modern firewalls, Cisco ASA |
| OSI Layer | Layer 3/4 | Layer 3/4/5 (with state tracking) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Tip:** Stateless = "Each packet is a stranger." Stateful = "I remember our conversation."

### 2.2.3 The ACL Rule Set — A Formal View

An **Access Control List (ACL)** is an **ordered list** of rules. A packet filter evaluates rules **top-to-bottom** and executes the **first match** (first-match semantics).

For a packet with header fields:
- Source IP = $S_{ip}$
- Destination IP = $D_{ip}$
- Source Port = $S_{p}$
- Destination Port = $D_{p}$
- Protocol = $\Pi$ (TCP, UDP, ICMP)

The decision function is:

$$f(p) = R_1 \text{ if } (S_{ip}, D_{ip}, S_p, D_p, \Pi) \in \sigma_1$$
$$\text{else } f(p) = R_2 \text{ if } (S_{ip}, D_{ip}, S_p, D_p, \Pi) \in \sigma_2$$
$$\vdots$$
$$\text{else } f(p) = R_n \text{ if } (S_{ip}, D_{ip}, S_p, D_p, \Pi) \in \sigma_n$$
$$\text{else } f(p) = R_{\text{default}}$$

Where:
- $R_i$ is the action (ACCEPT / DROP / REJECT) for rule $i$.
- $\sigma_i$ is the predicate (condition set) for rule $i$.
- $R_{\text{default}}$ is the implicit or explicit default action (usually DROP for security).

### 2.2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Packet Decision Function** | $f(p) \in \{\text{ACCEPT, DROP, REJECT}\}$ | Core of every firewall |
| **Implicit Deny** | If no rule matches → DROP | Security best practice |
| **Rule Match Predicate** | $\sigma_i : (S_{ip}, D_{ip}, S_p, D_p, \Pi, \text{flags}) \to \{\text{true, false}\}$ | Building blocks of ACL |
| **First-Match Semantics** | $f(p) = R_i$ where $i = \min\{j : \sigma_j(p) = \text{true}\}$ | Order matters in ACL |
| **State Table Entry (SPI)** | $(S_{ip}, S_p, D_{ip}, D_p, \Pi, \text{state}, \text{timeout})$ | Tracks TCP connections |
| **Throughput of Stateless Filter** | $\approx \dfrac{P}{L_h}$ packets/sec where $L_h$ = header parse time | High-speed backbone routers |
| **Anonymity Hop Count (Proxy Chain)** | $H = n$ for $n$ chained proxies | Privacy vs. latency tradeoff |

### 2.2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Edge Routers** — Cisco, Juniper use stateless filtering for high throughput at the perimeter.
- **Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFW)** — Palo Alto, Fortinet use stateful + DPI + proxying.
- **Cloud Security** — AWS Security Groups and NACLs are packet-filter-based.
- **IoT Gateways** — Since IoT devices are resource-constrained, lightweight stateless packet filtering is preferred.
- **Penetration Testing** — Tools like `nmap` must be aware of filtering to interpret scan results (`filtered` vs `open` vs `closed`).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Decision Walkthrough: Packet Filter ACL

Let us solve a classic KTU-style scenario with an explicit numerical ACL evaluation.

**Scenario:** A Linux server uses `iptables` with the following rule set (top to bottom):

| Rule # | Action | Source IP | Dest IP | Source Port | Dest Port | Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ACCEPT | 192.168.1.0/24 | 10.0.0.5 | ANY | 22 | TCP |
| 2 | ACCEPT | 10.0.0.0/8 | 10.0.0.5 | ANY | 80 | TCP |
| 3 | DROP | ANY | 10.0.0.5 | ANY | ANY | ANY |

**Question:** Determine the action for the following incoming packets:
- **Packet A:** Src=192.168.1.10, Dst=10.0.0.5, SrcPort=5000, DstPort=22, Proto=TCP
- **Packet B:** Src=172.16.5.4, Dst=10.0.0.5, SrcPort=6000, DstPort=443, Proto=TCP
- **Packet C:** Src=10.0.0.50, Dst=10.0.0.5, SrcPort=4000, DstPort=80, Proto=TCP

### Step-by-Step Evaluation (Packet A)

We evaluate predicates $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ in order.

**Step 1:** Test rule 1 predicate $\sigma_1$:
- $S_{ip} = 192.168.1.10 \in 192.168.1.0/24$ ? **TRUE**
- $D_{ip} = 10.0.0.5 = 10.0.0.5$ ? **TRUE**
- $D_p = 22 = 22$ and $\Pi = \text{TCP}$ ? **TRUE**

Therefore $\sigma_1 = \text{TRUE}$ and first match occurs at $i=1$.

$$f(p_A) = R_1 = \text{ACCEPT}$$

**Result:** Packet A is **ACCEPTED**.

### Step-by-Step Evaluation (Packet B)

**Step 1:** Test $\sigma_1$:
- $S_{ip} = 172.16.5.4 \notin 192.168.1.0/24$ ? **FALSE**

$\sigma_1 = \text{FALSE}$, move to next rule.

**Step 2:** Test $\sigma_2$:
- $S_{ip} = 172.16.5.4 \notin 10.0.0.0/8$ ? **FALSE**

$\sigma_2 = \text{FALSE}$, move to next rule.

**Step 3:** Test $\sigma_3$:
- $S_{ip} = 172.16.5.4 \in \text{ANY}$ ? **TRUE**
- $D_{ip} = 10.0.0.5 \in \text{ANY}$ ? **TRUE**
- $\sigma_3 = \text{TRUE}$, first match at $i=3$.

$$f(p_B) = R_3 = \text{DROP}$$

**Result:** Packet B is **DROPPED** (silently — no ICMP reply).

### Step-by-Step Evaluation (Packet C)

**Step 1:** Test $\sigma_1$:
- $S_{ip} = 10.0.0.50 \notin 192.168.1.0/24$ ? **FALSE**

**Step 2:** Test $\sigma_2$:
- $S_{ip} = 10.0.0.50 \in 10.0.0.0/8$ ? **TRUE**
- $D_{ip} = 10.0.0.5 = 10.0.0.5$ ? **TRUE**
- $D_p = 80 = 80$, $\Pi = \text{TCP}$ ? **TRUE**
- $\sigma_2 = \text{TRUE}$, first match at $i=2$.

$$f(p_C) = R_2 = \text{ACCEPT}$$

**Result:** Packet C is **ACCEPTED**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Takeaway:** Notice how Packet B to port 443 (HTTPS) is dropped because no specific rule allows it. This is the principle of **"Default Deny"** — explicitly allow only known-good traffic, block everything else.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Proxy vs. Packet Filter (Exhaustive Comparison)

This matrix is a **must-know** for KTU Part B questions.

| Comparison Axis | **Proxy Server** | **Packet Filter** |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 7 (Application) primarily, also SOCKS at L5 | Layer 3 & 4 |
| Inspection Depth | Can inspect **payload** (full content) | Header only |
| Anonymity | Provides **IP masking** for the client | No anonymity — sees real IPs |
| Caching | **Yes** — reduces bandwidth & latency | No caching |
| Authentication | Can require user login (per-user policy) | Generally IP/port-based only |
| Logging Granularity | URL, username, payload metadata | Source/dest IP, port, protocol |
| Performance Impact | **High** (full payload processing) | **Low** (header-only) |
| Complexity of Configuration | High | Low to Medium |
| Defeats | Bypassed by **direct IP access** if proxy is optional | Bypassed by **IP spoofing, tunneling, fragmentation** |
| Typical Tools | Squid, HAProxy, Nginx (reverse), Burp Suite | iptables, pf, Cisco ACL |
| State Awareness | Usually stateful at app level (e.g., HTTP session) | Stateless or stateful depending on variant |
| Application Protocol Awareness | **Yes** (HTTP, FTP, SMTP) | **No** |
| Cost | Higher (CPU, memory) | Lower |
| Use in IoT | Limited (resource-heavy) | **Highly preferred** (lightweight) |
| Example in Ethical Hacking | `Burp Suite` intercepting browser → server | `iptables` blocking nmap SYN scan |

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Modeling of Proxy Anonymity (Derivation)

Assume an attacker uses a chain of $n$ proxy servers, each adding one "hop" of indirection. The probability that the attacker can be deanonymized if a single proxy is compromised is:

$$P_{\text{deanonymize}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

Where $p_i$ is the probability that proxy $i$ is compromised AND cooperates with the investigator.

**Derivation (logic):**
- To trace back to the original client, **all** $n$ proxies in the chain must be compromised.
- If we assume each proxy has an independent compromise probability $p_i$, then by the **product rule of independent events**:

$$P(\text{all } n \text{ compromised}) = p_1 \times p_2 \times \cdots \times p_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

**For a uniform compromise probability** $p$ across all $n$ proxies:

$$P_{\text{deanonymize}} = p^n$$

This shows the **exponential decay** of deanonymization probability as the chain length $n$ grows. However, **latency** increases linearly:

$$L_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \approx n \cdot \bar{L}$$

This is the classic **anonymity-vs-latency tradeoff** in proxy chain design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This derivation is rarely required in full, but mentioning the tradeoff `anonymity ↑ ⇒ latency ↑` earns bonus marks in long-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forward Proxy Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientA[Client Browser] -->|HTTP Request| proxyA[Forward Proxy Server]
    proxyA -->|Policy Check| ruleSetA[Access Policy and Auth]
    ruleSetA -->|Allowed| cacheA[Cache Lookup]
    cacheA -->|Cache Miss| fwA[Internet Gateway]
    cacheA -->|Cache Hit| clientA
    fwA --> webA[Destination Web Server]
    webA -->|HTTP Response| proxyA
    proxyA -->|Response to Client| clientA
```

**Explanation of the flow:**
- The client does not talk directly to the destination.
- Every outbound request is first evaluated by the proxy's policy engine.
- Cache lookup occurs to serve repeated requests instantly.
- Only on a cache miss does the proxy forward the request to the internet.

---

## 4.2 Reverse Proxy with Load Balancing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userR[End User] -->|HTTPS Request| revProxy[Reverse Proxy and SSL Terminator]
    revProxy --> lb{Load Balancer Engine}
    lb -->|Round Robin| srv1[Backend Server 01]
    lb -->|Least Connections| srv2[Backend Server 02]
    lb -->|IP Hash| srv3[Backend Server 03]
    srv1 --> revProxy
    srv2 --> revProxy
    srv3 --> revProxy
    revProxy -->|Aggregated Response| userR
```

**Explanation of the flow:**
- The reverse proxy terminates the SSL connection (decrypts HTTPS).
- A load-balancing engine decides which backend handles the request.
- All backends respond to the reverse proxy, which returns a single response to the user.
- This architecture is used by Google, Amazon, Netflix at the edge.

---

## 4.3 Packet Filtering Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pktStart[Packet Arrives at Filter] --> parseA[Parse L3 and L4 Header]
    parseA --> lookupT[Lookup in State Table if Stateful]
    lookupT -->|No State Entry| ruleLoop[Evaluate ACL Rules Top to Bottom]
    lookupT -->|Existing State and Allowed| acceptA[ACCEPT and Forward]
    ruleLoop -->|First Match ACCEPT| acceptA
    ruleLoop -->|First Match DROP| dropA[DROP Silently]
    ruleLoop -->|First Match REJECT| rejectA[REJECT and Send ICMP]
    ruleLoop -->|No Match| defaultA[Apply Default Action Usually DROP]
    defaultA --> dropA
    acceptA --> pktOut[Forward Packet]
    dropA --> pktLogA[Log Discarded Packet]
    rejectA --> pktLogA
```

**Explanation of the flow:**
- The filter always starts by parsing the header (no payload).
- A stateful filter checks an internal state table first — if the packet belongs to an established, allowed connection, it is accepted instantly without re-evaluating rules.
- For a stateless filter, every rule in the ACL is checked in order.
- A "no-match" situation falls back to the default action (best practice: implicit DROP).

---

## 4.4 Proxy Chain for Anonymity in Ethical Hacking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    attacker[Attacker Machine] --> p1[Proxy 1 Entry Node]
    p1 --> p2[Proxy 2 Middle Relay]
    p2 --> p3[Proxy 3 Exit Node]
    p3 --> target[Target Server]
    target --> p3
    p3 --> p2
    p2 --> p1
    p1 --> attacker
```

**Explanation:**
- The attacker is hidden behind three proxies.
- The target server only sees the IP of Proxy 3 (exit node).
- Even if Proxy 3 is seized, the investigator still needs to compromise Proxy 2 and Proxy 1 to reach the attacker.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define proxy server. List any four types of proxies.
> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, Module 1]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A proxy server is an intermediary application that sits between a client and a destination server, forwarding client requests on the client's behalf while providing additional services such as caching, access control, anonymity, and content filtering.

**Any four types (½ mark each):**
1. Forward Proxy
2. Reverse Proxy
3. Transparent Proxy
4. Anonymous Proxy
5. SOCKS Proxy
6. Distorting Proxy

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between stateless and stateful packet filtering.
> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, Module 1]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Stateless Packet Filter | Stateful Packet Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Inspection Basis | Each packet evaluated independently in isolation | Tracks connection state via a state table |
| Resource Usage | Low | High |
| Resistance to Spoofing | Weak | Strong |
| Example | Basic `iptables` rule on header fields | `iptables -m state` or Cisco ASA |

(Any three valid contrasting points earn 3 marks.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Module 1 contains internal choice questions. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent alternatives — a student may be asked to answer either.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Proxy and Packet Filtering

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the architecture and working of a Forward Proxy server with a neat block diagram. How does it provide anonymity to clients?**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture (3 marks):**
A forward proxy is positioned at the network edge. Internal clients (browsers, apps) are configured — manually, via PAC file, or via transparent redirection (WPAD) — to send all outbound requests to the proxy. The proxy has two network interfaces: one facing the internal LAN and one facing the internet (or upstream gateway).

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Client sends a request (e.g., HTTP GET) to the proxy.
2. Proxy applies the **access policy** (allowed domains, time-of-day rules, user authentication).
3. If allowed, the proxy establishes a connection to the destination server using **its own IP** as the source.
4. The response is cached and returned to the client.
5. Subsequent identical requests are served from the cache (cache hit) without contacting the destination.

**Anonymity Mechanism (1 mark):**
Because the destination server sees only the proxy's IP address, the **client's real IP is never exposed**. The proxy may also strip identifying headers (e.g., `X-Forwarded-For`) from outgoing requests, providing true network-layer anonymity.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the three actions (ACCEPT, DROP, REJECT) of a packet filter. Design an ACL for the following policy:**
- Allow HTTP (port 80) and HTTPS (port 443) from any source to the web server at 203.0.113.10.
- Allow SSH (port 22) only from the admin subnet 198.51.100.0/24 to the same server.
- Block all other traffic to 203.0.113.10.

**Model Answer:**

**Three Actions (3 marks):**
- **ACCEPT** — packet is forwarded to its destination.
- **DROP** — packet is silently discarded; sender receives no response. Preferred for security (hides filter existence).
- **REJECT** — packet is discarded and the sender is informed via an ICMP "Destination Unreachable" message. Useful for diagnostics.

**ACL Design (4 marks):**

| Rule # | Action | Source | Destination | Protocol | Dest Port |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ACCEPT | ANY | 203.0.113.10 | TCP | 80 |
| 2 | ACCEPT | ANY | 203.0.113.10 | TCP | 443 |
| 3 | ACCEPT | 198.51.100.0/24 | 203.0.113.10 | TCP | 22 |
| 4 | DROP | ANY | 203.0.113.10 | ANY | ANY |

**Explanation:**
- Rules 1 and 2 allow public HTTP/HTTPS access.
- Rule 3 allows admin SSH only from the trusted subnet.
- Rule 4 applies the **default-deny** principle: any packet not matched by rules 1–3 is silently dropped.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Proxy and Packet Filtering (Alternative)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast Proxy Server and Packet Filter across any seven parameters. When would you choose one over the other?**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Proxy Server | Packet Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OSI Layer | Application (L7) | Network/Transport (L3/L4) |
| 2 | Inspection | Inspects payload + header | Inspects header only |
| 3 | Anonymity | Provides IP masking | No anonymity |
| 4 | Caching | Yes | No |
| 5 | Performance | Slower (high processing) | Faster (low overhead) |
| 6 | Configuration Complexity | High | Low to Medium |
| 7 | Logging | URL, user, content metadata | IP, port, protocol |
| 8 | Suitability for IoT | Limited (resource-heavy) | Highly suitable |

**When to choose (1 mark):**
- Use **packet filter** for high-throughput perimeter security, IoT gateways, and as a first line of defense.
- Use **proxy server** when content inspection, user authentication, caching, or anonymity are required.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**What is Stateful Packet Inspection (SPI)? Explain how it defeats a TCP SYN scan. Construct a state table for a normal TCP 3-way handshake.**

**Model Answer:**

**SPI Definition (2 marks):**
Stateful Packet Inspection is a filtering technique that maintains a **state table** tracking every active connection's status (e.g., NEW, ESTABLISHED, RELATED, INVALID). Packets are evaluated not only against static ACL rules but also against their relationship to existing connections.

**Defeating SYN Scan (2 marks):**
A TCP SYN scan sends a single SYN packet to a target port and waits for SYN-ACK (open) or RST (closed). A stateful filter:
- Marks the unsolicited SYN as `NEW`.
- If the filter does not allow NEW connections on that port, the SYN is dropped before any SYN-ACK is generated.
- The scanner receives **no response** and reports the port as `filtered` — denying reconnaissance information.

**State Table for TCP 3-Way Handshake (3 marks):**

| Step | Packet | Source → Dest | State Transition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SYN | Client → Server | Client: `SYN_SENT`; Server: `NEW` |
| 2 | SYN-ACK | Server → Client | Server: `SYN_RECEIVED`; Connection marked `ESTABLISHED` upon receipt of ACK |
| 3 | ACK | Client → Server | Both sides: `ESTABLISHED` |

**State Table Representation:**

| Source IP | Src Port | Dest IP | Dst Port | State | Timeout |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 203.0.113.5 | 5000 | 203.0.113.10 | 80 | ESTABLISHED | 3600s |

The stateful filter allows all subsequent packets in this connection without re-checking the ACL, drastically improving throughput.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DROP and REJECT** — Many students write that DROP sends a reply. **WRONG.** DROP is silent; REJECT sends an ICMP reply.
> 2. **Forgetting "Default Deny"** — When designing an ACL, students often stop at "permit" rules and forget the final "deny all" rule. Always include it.
> 3. **Claiming packet filters inspect content** — This is the #1 factual error. Packet filters **only** inspect headers. If content is needed, it's a proxy/IDS/IPS.
> 4. **Confusing Forward and Reverse Proxy** — Forward Proxy is for *clients* (egress); Reverse Proxy is for *servers* (ingress).
> 5. **Rule Order** — ACLs are first-match. Putting the "deny all" rule at the top makes the entire ACL useless. Always put specific allows first, general denies last.
> 6. **No diagram** — KTU Part B questions on architecture (especially proxy) **require** a diagram. Not drawing one typically costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Proxy Server** = Application-layer intermediary providing anonymity, caching, and content filtering.
- **Forward Proxy** sits on behalf of *clients*; **Reverse Proxy** sits on behalf of *servers*.
- **Packet Filter** = Network/Transport-layer mechanism inspecting only **packet headers**.
- Three actions: **ACCEPT, DROP, REJECT**. DROP is silent; REJECT replies with ICMP.
- **Stateless** = independent per-packet inspection (fast, weak). **Stateful** = uses a state table (slower, strong).
- ACLs follow **first-match** semantics; default-deny is a security best practice.
- Packet filters are preferred for **IoT** and high-speed links; proxies are preferred when content inspection or user-level auth is needed.
- In penetration testing: proxy chains provide anonymity; packet filters are obstacles (filtered ports in nmap output).
- Key math: Deanonymization probability in an $n$-hop proxy chain = $p^n$ (product of independent compromise probabilities).
- Always draw a **block diagram** for proxy questions — it is expected and rewarded.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Denial of Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Denial of Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Denial of Service (DoS)** is a malicious cyber-attack in which an adversary intentionally disrupts the normal functioning of a targeted server, service, or network by overwhelming it with a flood of illegitimate requests, exhausting its critical computational resources (CPU cycles, memory, bandwidth, or socket buffers), thereby rendering the service unavailable to legitimate, authorized users.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBCCT604, Module 1), a Denial of Service attack is classified under the broader domain of **Availability Attacks** — one of the three pillars of the classic CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) security triad. The fundamental objective is **not data theft**, but the **suspension of service uptime** and degradation of Quality of Service (QoS) metrics.

When the attack is launched **simultaneously from multiple distributed sources** (often compromised machines called *zombies* or *bots*), it escalates into a **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS)** attack. A more recent variant, the **Distributed Reflection Denial of Service (DRDoS)**, exploits legitimate third-party servers as unwitting reflectors/amplifiers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Availability Metric (KTU Definition):** Service availability is often expressed as a percentage, e.g., the **"Five Nines" (99.999%)** standard, which permits a maximum downtime of approximately **5.26 minutes per year**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Jam" Model

Imagine a four-lane highway (the network) leading to a single toll booth (the target server).

- **Normal Traffic:** Cars arrive at a steady rate, pay the toll, and pass through. Service is available.
- **DoS Attack:** A single attacker parks a bulldozer sideways across all four lanes. The toll booth is fine, the road is fine — but no legitimate car can reach the booth.
- **DDoS Attack:** Thousands of attackers, parked in hundreds of trucks, block every approach road within a 50 km radius. Blocking them all simultaneously is nearly impossible.

The **bulldozer is the attacker's bandwidth/computational payload**. The **toll booth is the server**. The **legitimate cars are real users**. The "system" is technically healthy but **practically unreachable**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Victim / Target** | The end host, server, or network whose service is being denied. |
| **Attacker** | The malicious entity orchestrating the DoS event. |
| **Bot / Zombie / Agent** | A compromised machine (part of a *botnet*) used to launch DDoS. |
| **Botnet** | A coordinated network of bots under the control of a single *bot herder*. |
| **Command & Control (C2)** | The infrastructure the attacker uses to remotely instruct bots. |
| **Reflector / Amplifier** | A legitimate third-party server abused to multiply attack traffic. |
| **SYN Flood** | A connection-table exhaustion attack exploiting the TCP 3-way handshake. |
| **Bandwidth Depletion** | DoS subtype that consumes network link capacity. |
| **Resource Depletion** | DoS subtype that exhausts host-level resources (CPU/RAM/sockets). |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of DoS Attacks (KTU High-Yield Taxonomy)

A clean way to think about DoS attacks is to ask two questions:
**"What resource is being exhausted?"** and **"How is the attack delivered?"**

### 2.1.1 By Resource Targeted

1. **Bandwidth Depletion Attacks**
   - The attacker floods the network link (e.g., a 1 Gbps link) with traffic exceeding its capacity.
   - Result: Legitimate packets are dropped at the router due to queue overflow.
   - Example: **UDP Flood, ICMP Flood, Amplification attacks.**

2. **Resource Depletion Attacks**
   - The attacker targets *host-level* finite resources such as:
     - TCP connection table (SYN cookies are a defense).
     - Application threads/processes (Slowloris).
     - Disk space (log-flooding).
     - CPU / RAM (cryptographic exhaustion).
   - Example: **SYN Flood, HTTP GET Flood, Slowloris.**

### 2.1.2 By Attack Mechanism (the 3-Generation Model)

| Generation | Mechanism | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gen-I (Single-source)** | One attacker exhausts the link. | Ping of Death, Teardrop |
| **Gen-II (Agent-based)** | Botnet of hundreds of agents. | Trinoo, TFN, Stacheldraht |
| **Gen-III (Reflective/Amplified)** | Public servers (DNS, NTP) are tricked into flooding the victim. | DNS Amplification, NTP Amplification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Hybridization:** Today's real-world attacks frequently blend all three generations. A single incident may combine an HTTP GET flood, a UDP amplification layer, and a slow-rate application-layer probe.

## 2.2 The Three TCP-State Attack Surfaces

DoS attacks on TCP-based services exploit the **TCP state machine** in three classic ways:

1. **State Exhaustion (SYN Flood):** Never complete the 3-way handshake, leaving the server in `SYN_RECEIVED`.
2. **State Reset Exploitation:** Send forged RST packets to tear down legitimate connections.
3. **State-Consumption (Slowloris):** Open many partial HTTP requests that *never* finish downloading, pinning application threads.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the most frequently tested numerical concepts for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

| Symbol / Concept | Equation / Definition | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Availability Percentage** | $$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures; MTTR = Mean Time To Repair. |
| **Maximum Annual Downtime (for A)** | $$D_{max} = 525600 \cdot (1 - A)$$ minutes/year | 525600 = total minutes in a 365-day year. |
| **Amplification Factor (AF)** | $$AF = \frac{\text{Bytes reflected to victim}}{\text{Bytes sent by attacker}}$$ | A core metric for DDoS amplification. |
| **Effective Attack Bandwidth** | $$B_{eff} = N_{bots} \cdot B_{per\,bot} \cdot AF$$ | $N_{bots}$ = number of bots; $B_{per\,bot}$ per-bot upload. |
| **SYN Backlog Saturation Rate** | $$t_{sat} = \frac{L_{backlog}}{R_{SYN} - R_{SYN\_ACK\_timeout}}$$ | Time to fill TCP backlog log. |
| **Attack Power Ratio (vs. defense)** | $$R_p = \frac{B_{attacker}}{B_{link\,capacity}}$$ | When $R_p \geq 1$, DoS is trivial. |

**Important boundary rule for the table:** Use the `\\` line-break inside math cells only when written in LaTeX display mode. In plain markdown tables, math is rendered as-is. **Absolute value bars and conditional bars are written as `\vert x \vert` or `P(A \mid B)` — never with the raw pipe character.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DoS study is not just academic. The principles directly inform:

- **Network Capacity Planning:** Engineers provision **over-subscription ratios** (e.g., 1:4) on the assumption that not all users are simultaneously active — but DoS attackers intentionally *invalidate* that assumption.
- **Cloud Auto-Scaling Economics:** AWS, Azure, and GCP all charge per elastic resource; a DoS that triggers auto-scaling can inflict **financial damage** even if it never causes downtime (a *resource-exhaustion financial DoS*).
- **IoT Security (Module-Specific Relevance):** With billions of low-power, often-unpatched IoT devices (cameras, routers, smart bulbs), the **Mirai botnet** showed that the IoT ecosystem itself *is* the DDoS ammunition. KTU Module 1 explicitly references IoT-driven DDoS in PBCCT604.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** India's IT Act, 2000 (amended 2008), Section 43(f) and Section 66-F (read with the IT Rules, 2021) criminalize DoS as a form of **cyber-terrorism** if it affects critical infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The TCP 3-Way Handshake — The Foundation of the SYN Flood

Before deriving the SYN flood attack, we must establish the **legitimate** state transition.

### 3.1.1 Legitimate Handshake (3 messages)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:}\quad & \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} : \text{SYN} \mid \text{seq} = x \\
\text{Step 2:}\quad & \text{Server} \rightarrow \text{Client} : \text{SYN-ACK} \mid \text{seq} = y, \text{ ack} = x+1 \\
\text{Step 3:}\quad & \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} : \text{ACK} \mid \text{seq} = x+1, \text{ ack} = y+1 \\
\text{Result:}\quad & \text{Connection enters ESTABLISHED state.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each side allocates a Transmission Control Block (TCB) entry only after the final ACK. The server, however, allocates a TCB **immediately** upon sending the SYN-ACK (Step 2) and waits for the client's ACK. This **half-open allocation** is the vulnerability.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of SYN-Flood Saturation

Let:
- $L_{backlog}$ = size of the server's SYN-pending queue (typical Linux: 256 to 1024 entries).
- $R_{SYN}$ = rate of incoming SYN packets.
- $T_{timeout}$ = SYN-receive timeout (typical 75 seconds on Linux).
- $R_{clean}$ = rate at which legitimate clients complete the 3-way handshake (clearing an entry).

The **net queue growth rate** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{net} &= R_{SYN} - R_{clean} \\
\text{Backlog size at time } t :\quad Q(t) &= Q_0 + R_{net} \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

Saturation occurs when $Q(t) = L_{backlog}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{backlog} &= Q_0 + R_{net} \cdot t_{sat} \\
t_{sat} &= \frac{L_{backlog} - Q_0}{R_{net}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 1 Gbps attacker sending 64-byte SYNs at line rate generates approximately $1.95 \times 10^6$ SYNs/second. Against a server with $L_{backlog} = 1024$ and no legitimate traffic, saturation is achieved in:

$$
t_{sat} = \frac{1024}{1{,}950{,}000} \approx 5.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ seconds} \approx 0.525 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The queue fills in under one millisecond. The attacker's efficiency is bounded only by the **attacker→server link bandwidth**.

## 3.2 DDoS Amplification Factor — Full Derivation

The **Amplification Factor (AF)** is the heart of a reflection-based DDoS. Let's derive it for a classic **DNS amplification**.

### 3.2.1 Setup

1. Attacker sends a DNS query of size $S_{query}$ to a public recursive resolver.
2. The **source IP of the query is spoofed** to be the victim's IP.
3. The resolver, believing the victim asked, replies with the full DNS answer of size $S_{response}$ to the victim.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes sent by attacker} &= S_{query} \\
\text{Bytes received by victim} &= S_{response} \\
AF &= \frac{S_{response}}{S_{query}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a `ANY` query against an open resolver with DNSSEC enabled:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{query} &\approx 60 \text{ bytes (UDP, with IP/UDP headers)} \\
S_{response} &\approx 4000 \text{ bytes} \\
AF &= \frac{4000}{60} \approx 66.7
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Effective Attack Bandwidth

If the attacker has $B_{attacker} = 100$ Mbps of upstream capacity and recruits $N_{reflectors}$ open resolvers, each delivering the amplified response:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{eff} &= B_{attacker} \cdot AF \cdot N_{reflectors} \\
\text{With } B_{attacker} = 100 \text{ Mbps},\ AF = 66.7,\ N_{reflectors} = 1000 :\quad \\
B_{eff} &= 100 \cdot 66.7 \cdot 1000 \text{ Mbps} = 6.67 \times 10^6 \text{ Mbps} \approx 6.67 \text{ Tbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Real-world sanity check:** The 2018 GitHub DDoS peaked at **1.7 Tbps** — well within the modeled range.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Attack vs. Defense Mapping (for KTU)

| Attack | Layer (OSI) | Resource Targeted | Best-Practice Defense |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SYN Flood** | L4 (Transport) | TCP connection table | SYN cookies, SYN proxies, firewalls |
| **UDP Flood** | L4 | Bandwidth | Rate-limiting, uRPF filtering |
| **ICMP Flood / Smurf** | L3 | Bandwidth | Disable IP-directed broadcasts |
| **DNS Amplification** | L7 (via L3/L4) | Bandwidth | Response Rate Limiting (RRL), BCP-38 |
| **HTTP GET/POST Flood** | L7 | App threads/sockets | CAPTCHA, WAF, JS challenge |
| **Slowloris** | L7 | App threads | Connection timeouts, min. request rate |
| **Ping of Death** | L3 | IP reassembly buffer | OS-level patch (historical) |
| **NTP Amplification** | L3/L4 | Bandwidth | Disable `monlist`, upgrade NTP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OSI Layer Insight:** The further **up the stack** the attack operates (L7), the **harder it is to detect** because traffic patterns resemble legitimate users. Slowloris, for instance, is fully RFC-compliant HTTP.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DDoS Architecture (Botnet-Driven)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["ATTACKER HERDER"]:::actorNode
    B["C2 COMMAND SERVER"]:::infraNode
    C["BOT NODE 1"]:::botNode
    D["BOT NODE 2"]:::botNode
    E["BOT NODE 3"]:::botNode
    F["BOT NODE N"]:::botNode
    G["VICTIM SERVER"]:::victimNode
    H["LEGITIMATE USER"]:::legitNode

    A -- "Issue attack command" --> B
    B -- "Push attack script" --> C
    B -- "Push attack script" --> D
    B -- "Push attack script" --> E
    B -- "Push attack script" --> F
    C -- "Flood traffic" --> G
    D -- "Flood traffic" --> G
    E -- "Flood traffic" --> G
    F -- "Flood traffic" --> G
    H -- "Genuine request DROPPED" --> G

    classDef actorNode fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef infraNode fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef botNode fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef victimNode fill:#118ab2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef legitNode fill:#cccccc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```
