> **Reading the diagram:** A single human attacker ("herder") controls a Command-and-Control (C2) server. The C2 pushes attack code to hundreds or thousands of compromised bots. All bots simultaneously send flood traffic to the victim, while legitimate users find the service unreachable.

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — TCP SYN Flood vs. Legitimate Handshake

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT : Client sends SYN
    SYN_SENT --> SYN_RCVD : Server sends SYN-ACK
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED : Client sends ACK
    SYN_RCVD --> SYN_FLOODED : Timeout with no ACK
    SYN_FLOODED --> NEW_SYN_DROPPED : Backlog full, new SYNs rejected
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT : Data exchange ends
    NEW_SYN_DROPPED --> SYN_FLOODED : Attacker keeps queue saturated
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSED : Connection closed normally
    FIN_WAIT --> CLOSED : Final ACK received

    note right of SYN_FLOODED
        Half-open TCB pinned.
        Legit SYNs may be
        silently dropped here.
    end note
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reflection/Amplification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["ATTACKER"]:::actorNode
    R1["REFLECTOR DNS"]:::refNode
    R2["REFLECTOR NTP"]:::refNode
    R3["REFLECTOR SSDP"]:::refNode
    V1["VICTIM"]:::victimNode

    A1 -- "Spoofed query 60 bytes" --> R1
    A1 -- "Spoofed query 60 bytes" --> R2
    A1 -- "Spoofed query 60 bytes" --> R3
    R1 -- "Reply 4000 bytes to VICTIM IP" --> V1
    R2 -- "Reply 500 bytes to VICTIM IP" --> V1
    R3 -- "Reply 3000 bytes to VICTIM IP" --> V1

    classDef actorNode fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef refNode fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef victimNode fill:#118ab2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The attacker never sends packets directly to the victim. Instead, it sends small (60-byte) queries to public servers (DNS, NTP, SSDP) with the **victim's IP** in the source field. Those servers faithfully send their much larger responses to the victim. The result is **traffic amplification** without ever exposing the attacker's location.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Defense-in-Depth Layering

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["L1 ISP SCRUBBING CENTER"]:::defNode
    L2["L2 EDGE FIREWALL uRPF + RATE LIMIT"]:::defNode
    L3["L3 WAF / L7 INSPECTION"]:::defNode
    L4["L4 APPLICATION TIMEOUTS"]:::defNode
    L5["L5 ANYCAST GEO-DISPERSION"]:::defNode
    V1["VICTIM ORIGIN SERVER"]:::victimNode

    A0["ATTACK TRAFFIC"]:::attNode --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> V1
    B0["LEGITIMATE TRAFFIC"]:::legitNode --> L5
    L5 --> V1

    classDef defNode fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef victimNode fill:#118ab2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef attNode fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef legitNode fill:#cccccc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Real-world DoS defense is **layered**, not single-point. Traffic passes through ISP scrubbing, edge filtering, WAF, application timeouts, and Anycast geo-dispersion. Attack traffic is dropped at the earliest layer possible; legitimate traffic is routed to the nearest healthy origin.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students most frequently lose marks on DoS questions by **(a)** confusing *DoS* with *DDoS* in definitions, **(b)** forgetting to mention **resource exhaustion vs. bandwidth exhaustion** as two separate subtypes, and **(c)** skipping the **mitigation step** when asked to "explain" an attack (KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates 2–3 marks for the countermeasure in 7-mark sub-parts). Always close a DoS answer with a defense.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Set A)]
**Define Denial of Service. Differentiate between DoS and DDoS in two points.**

**Model Answer:**
Denial of Service (DoS) is a cyber-attack in which an attacker renders a service unavailable to legitimate users by exhausting its resources or bandwidth.
| Aspect | DoS | DDoS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sources** | Single source | Multiple distributed sources (botnet) |
| **Detection** | Easier (single IP) | Harder (many IPs, often spoofed) |
| **Mitigation** | IP block | Traffic scrubbing + filtering |
| **Volume** | Limited by one machine's bandwidth | Multiplied by $N_{bots}$ |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for table.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Syllabus-Aligned)]
**List any three DoS attack techniques and the OSI layer each targets.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **SYN Flood** — Layer 4 (Transport), exhausts TCP connection table.
2. **DNS Amplification** — Layer 7 / Layer 3-4, exhausts bandwidth via reflection.
3. **Slowloris** — Layer 7 (Application), exhausts application threads by holding partial HTTP requests.
*(3 marks: 1 per correct pair.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the TCP SYN Flood attack with a neat diagram of the 3-way handshake. Show how the attacker exploits the half-open connection state.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recap the 3-way handshake:**
The client sends SYN, server replies SYN-ACK and **immediately allocates a TCB (Transmission Control Block)**, and the client sends ACK to establish.

**Step 2 — Identify the flaw:**
The server commits its connection state **before** the third ACK arrives. The TCB sits in a *SYN-pending queue* of finite size $L_{backlog}$ (Linux default ≈ 256).

**Step 3 — The attack:**
The attacker (often using a spoofed, unreachable source IP) sends a flood of SYN packets but **never sends the final ACK**. Each SYN pins one TCB slot. The half-open entries accumulate until the backlog is full, at which point the server **stops accepting new SYNs — including from legitimate users.**

**Step 4 — Saturation time:**
With $R_{SYN}$ SYN/s and $T_{timeout} = 75$ s, the queue fills in $t_{sat} = L_{backlog} / R_{SYN}$ seconds. A 1 Gbps attacker saturates a 1024-entry queue in milliseconds.

**Step 5 — Mitigation:**
- **SYN Cookies:** The server encodes the connection state into the SYN-ACK sequence number and only allocates a real TCB on receipt of the final ACK.
- **Firewall SYN proxying** and reducing `tcp_synack_retries`.

*(7 marks: 1 for diagram + 2 for mechanism + 2 for impact + 2 for mitigation.)*

**(b) [7 Marks] With relevant equations, derive the Amplification Factor for a DNS reflection attack. If an attacker has 50 Mbps upstream, an AF of 50, and recruits 2000 open resolvers, calculate the effective attack bandwidth in Gbps.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
The Amplification Factor (AF) is the ratio of response bytes sent to the victim to the query bytes sent by the attacker:

$$AF = \frac{S_{response}}{S_{query}}$$

**Step 2 — Typical values for DNS ANY query:**
$S_{query} \approx 60$ bytes; $S_{response} \approx 3000$ bytes ⇒ $AF = 50$.

**Step 3 — Effective attack bandwidth:**

$$
B_{eff} = B_{attacker} \cdot AF \cdot N_{reflectors}
$$

$$
B_{eff} = 50 \text{ Mbps} \cdot 50 \cdot 2000
$$

$$
B_{eff} = 50 \cdot 50 \cdot 2000 = 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ Mbps} = 5 \text{ Tbps} = 5000 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
A modest 50 Mbps home link (e.g., a single fiber connection) can be turned into a 5 Tbps weapon against the victim — roughly **three times the size of the 2018 GitHub attack.**

*(7 marks: 2 for equation + 1 for substitution + 2 for answer + 2 for interpretation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Classify DoS attacks based on the resource targeted. Provide two examples for each class.**

**Model Solution:**

**Class 1 — Bandwidth Depletion Attacks:**
The attacker floods the network link, exceeding its capacity so that legitimate packets are dropped.
Examples: **UDP Flood, ICMP Flood (Smurf), DNS Amplification.**

**Class 2 — Resource Depletion Attacks:**
The attacker targets host-level finite resources.
Examples: **SYN Flood (TCP table), Slowloris (application threads), HTTP GET Flood (worker pool).**

**Class 3 — Configuration / Logical DoS:**
The attacker exploits software bugs or misconfigurations.
Examples: **Ping of Death (oversized ICMP), Teardrop (malformed IP fragments), Land attack (source = destination IP).**

| Class | Target | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bandwidth | Network link | UDP Flood |
| Resource | Host (CPU/RAM/socket) | SYN Flood |
| Logical | Software bug | Ping of Death |

*(7 marks: 2 per class with examples + 1 for the summary table.)*

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the architecture of a Botnet-based DDoS attack. Include the role of the herder, the C2 server, and the bots. List any two famous botnet incidents.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Herder (Attacker):**
The human operator who designs the attack campaign. They do *not* send attack traffic directly, preserving anonymity.

**Step 2 — Command & Control (C2) Server:**
The herder communicates with bots via a C2 channel — historically IRC, later HTTP/HTTPS, increasingly Tor-based or peer-to-peer. The C2 pushes instructions, payload updates, and target lists.

**Step 3 — Bots (Zombies):**
Thousands of machines previously infected with malware (Mirai used default Telnet credentials on IoT devices). Bots lie dormant until activated, then simultaneously flood the target.

**Step 4 — Architecture diagram (textual):**
`Herder ⇄ C2 ⇄ Bots → Victim (with legitimate users blocked).`

**Step 5 — Two famous botnet incidents:**
- **Mirai (2016):** Compromised ~600,000 IoT devices (cameras, DVRs, routers) using default credentials. Launched a **620 Gbps** attack on Brian Krebs' blog and a **1.1 Tbps** attack on OVH (French hosting).
- **Mirai-derived variant (Dyn, October 2016):** Brought down Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and GitHub by attacking the Dyn DNS provider, demonstrating collateral damage at Internet scale.

**Step 6 — Defense:**
- Sinkholeting C2 domains, takedowns, ISP-level filtering, and *BCP-38* (anti-spoofing at the network edge).

*(7 marks: 1 architecture step + 2 architecture narrative + 2 incidents + 2 defense.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (read the night before the KTU exam):**

- ✅ **DoS vs. DDoS:** DoS = single source; DDoS = multiple distributed sources (botnet).
- ✅ **DRDoS:** Uses *reflectors/amplifiers* (DNS, NTP, SSDP) to multiply traffic without ever directly targeting the victim.
- ✅ **Two depletion axes:** *Bandwidth* (network link) and *Resources* (host memory, sockets, CPU).
- ✅ **SYN Flood** is the **most-tested** DoS in KTU exams — always pair it with **SYN cookies** as the defense.
- ✅ **3-Way Handshake:** `SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`. Vulnerability lies in the **half-open state** between SYN-ACK and ACK.
- ✅ **Amplification Factor (AF) = Response Size / Query Size.** DNS ANY ≈ 50×, NTP monlist ≈ 556×, SSDP ≈ 30×.
- ✅ **Effective DDoS Bandwidth:** $B_{eff} = B_{attacker} \cdot AF \cdot N_{reflectors}$.
- ✅ **Mirai (2016)** is the canonical *IoT-driven botnet* — directly relevant to **PBCCT604 IoT Security**.
- ✅ **Five Nines (99.999%)** = ~5.26 min/year downtime. Use $D_{max} = 525600 \cdot (1-A)$.
- ✅ **Layer-up attacks (L7) are hardest to detect** because traffic looks legitimate (Slowloris, HTTP flood).
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth:** ISP scrubbing → uRPF → WAF → App timeouts → Anycast.
- ✅ **BCP-38** is the IETF best-practice for ingress filtering to *prevent IP spoofing* (root cause of DRDoS).
- ✅ **India-specific:** IT Act §66-F treats DoS against critical infrastructure as **cyber-terrorism** (high-marks answer in law questions).
- ✅ **MTBF/MTTR/Availability formula** is a generic engineering metric — expect it on **any** paper, not just DoS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sniffer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sniffer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Packet Sniffer** (also known as a **Network Analyzer**, **Protocol Analyzer**, or **Packet Analyzer**) is a software or hardware tool that captures, logs, decodes, and analyzes the raw data packets traversing a computer network. Operating primarily at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI Model, a sniffer intercepts traffic by placing the host's Network Interface Card (NIC) into **promiscuous mode**, bypassing the default filtering behavior that discards frames not destined for the local machine.

In the context of the KTU PBCCT604 syllabus, sniffers are categorized as a **reconnaissance and eavesdropping** class of attack tools used by both ethical penetration testers (defensively, for diagnostics) and black-hat attackers (offensively, for credential theft, session hijacking, and traffic analysis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A sniffer does NOT generate, modify, or inject traffic. It is fundamentally a **passive eavesdropping instrument** — its presence is often invisible to the communicating endpoints.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wiretap on a Telephone Line"

Imagine two people, **Alice** and **Bob**, speaking on a landline telephone inside a sealed room. A sniffer is equivalent to a **covert wiretap** that a third party, **Eve**, has secretly soldered onto the copper wire between them:

- **Alice and Bob** are unaware that the call is being monitored.
- **Eve** does not need to interrupt or alter the conversation — she simply **listens** and **records**.
- However, if Eve wanted to be *active*, she could also use a **"man-in-the-middle"** tap (like ARP poisoning) to *redirect* Alice's and Bob's calls through her own extension, allowing her to modify the conversation.

In the digital world:
- **Alice and Bob** = any two networked devices (e.g., a laptop and a web server).
- **The telephone wire** = a shared Ethernet segment, Wi-Fi channel, or network switch port.
- **Eve's wiretap** = a packet sniffer running on a connected host (or a hardware tap on the physical cable).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A packet sniffer is **not a virus, worm, or trojan**. It is a *dual-use* diagnostic utility. Tools like **Wireshark** are pre-installed by default in Kali Linux precisely because they are indispensable for legitimate network troubleshooting and security auditing.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Technical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Where raw frames are captured |
| NIC Mode | Promiscuous Mode | Disables MAC address filtering |
| Default Encapsulation | Ethernet II (IEEE 802.3) | Frame format captured |
| Common Capture Format | **PCAP / PCAPNG** (.pcap, .pcapng) | Standard libpcap file format |
| Default Port for Remote Capture | **UDP 5555** (e.g., rpcapd) | Remote sniffing service port |
| Maximum MTU (Ethernet) | **1500 bytes** | Largest single-frame payload |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The "How" Behind a Sniffer

A packet sniffer operates through a structured sequence of interactions with the host operating system's networking stack and the physical NIC. The complete data flow is described below:

1. **NIC Initialization in Promiscuous Mode**
   - The driver instructs the NIC hardware to forward **all** received frames to the CPU, regardless of the destination MAC address.
   - The default behavior of a NIC is *non-promiscuous mode*, where it discards frames whose destination MAC does not match its own.

2. **Raw Frame Capture (Kernel Buffer)**
   - Captured frames are copied from the NIC's hardware ring buffer into a kernel-space circular buffer (e.g., `pfring`, `NAPI`, or `libpcap` ring).
   - The buffer size directly impacts the number of packets that can be held before overflow.

3. **Filtering (BPF — Berkeley Packet Filter)**
   - The user applies a **BPF expression** (e.g., `tcp port 80 and host 192.168.1.1`) to pre-filter packets at the kernel level, dramatically reducing CPU load.
   - The BPF compiler translates human-readable filters into a small virtual machine bytecode executed per packet.

4. **Decoding (Protocol Dissection)**
   - Each captured frame is passed up the protocol stack: Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application (HTTP, DNS, FTP, etc.).
   - Tools like Wireshark use a library of **dissectors** to parse application-layer payloads and present them in human-readable form.

5. **Storage / Display**
   - Packets are either displayed live in a real-time scrolling view or saved to a `.pcap` file for offline forensic analysis.

## 2.2 Classification of Sniffers (High-Yield for KTU)

### 2.2.1 By Placement Architecture
- **Passive Sniffer:** Placed on a hub or a mirrored SPAN port. It only listens; it does not inject frames. Indistinguishable from normal traffic.
- **Active Sniffer:** Injects crafted frames (e.g., ARP replies) to manipulate switch CAM tables and force traffic redirection. **Traceable** under forensic analysis.

### 2.2.2 By Physical Implementation
- **Software Sniffer:** Application-level tool running on a general-purpose OS (Wireshark, Tcpdump, Ettercap).
- **Hardware Sniffer (Network Tap):** A physical inline device with separate TX and RX ports that copies optical/electrical signals for analysis. Used in high-security environments.

## 2.3 Why Hubs vs. Switches Change Everything

| Network Device | Sniffing Feasibility | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Hub** | Trivial — all ports share one collision domain | Frames are broadcast to every connected host |
| **Switch** | Difficult natively — each port is a separate collision domain | Frames are forwarded only to the destination MAC's port |
| **Switch with SPAN/Mirror Port** | Possible — port mirroring copies traffic to a designated monitor port | Legitimate enterprise diagnostic feature |
| **Switch + ARP Poisoning** | Possible — actively poison the CAM table to redirect victim's traffic | The "active sniffer" attack vector |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum Frame Size (Ethernet) | $S_{max} = 1500 \text{ bytes (payload)} + 18 \text{ bytes (header/FCS)}$ | Total $\approx 1522$ bytes with VLAN tag |
| Promiscuous Mode Activation | $\text{NIC}_{mode} = \neg \text{MAC}_{filter}$ | Boolean condition |
| Capture Buffer Throughput | $B = N \times L$ | $N$ = packets, $L$ = avg frame length (bytes) |
| Bandwidth Utilization | $U = \frac{\text{Captured Bytes}}{\text{Time Window}}$ | Measured in B/s or bps |
| BPF Filter Example | `tcp dst port 443 and src net 10.0.0.0/8` | Kernel-level filter |
| DNS Cache Poison Target | $\text{RR}_{TTL} \to 0$ | Shorter TTL = larger poisoning window |
| ARP Cache Poison | $\text{IP}_{victim} \to \text{MAC}_{attacker}$ | CAM table manipulation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Packet sniffers are the **cornerstone tool** of network forensics, IDS/IPS signature development, VoIP quality monitoring, application-layer debugging (e.g., HTTP/HTTPS troubleshooting), and compliance auditing (PCI-DSS, HIPAA).

## 2.5 Active Sniffing Attack Vectors (KTU High-Yield)

1. **ARP Poisoning / ARP Spoofing** — Attacker sends gratuitous ARP replies to poison a victim's ARP cache, redirecting traffic through the attacker's machine.
2. **MAC Flooding** — Attacker floods the switch CAM table with bogus MAC addresses, causing the switch to fail-open into hub-like broadcast behavior.
3. **DNS Cache Poisoning** — Attacker injects forged DNS responses into a resolver's cache, redirecting domain name resolution.
4. **DHCP Starvation / Rogue DHCP** — Exhausts legitimate DHCP pool; attacker issues malicious IP configuration (rogue gateway/DNS) to clients.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Matrices

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Why Hubs Allow Universal Sniffing

Consider a classic 10BASE-T Ethernet hub with $N$ connected hosts, operating in a single **collision domain**. Let each frame transmitted by host $H_i$ be a broadcast signal $S_{ij}$ intended for host $H_j$.

In a hub, the physical layer repeats the incoming electrical signal on **all other ports** with no MAC-based filtering. Mathematically, for any transmitted frame $F$:

$$
\forall \, k \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}, \; k \neq i \implies \text{Hub}_{output_k} = F
$$

This means **every host** $H_k$ (where $k \neq i$) receives the frame. If host $H_k$ has its NIC in **promiscuous mode**, it accepts the frame regardless of the destination MAC field. The capture probability for any single frame is therefore:

$$
P_{capture} = \frac{1}{1} = 1.0 \quad (\text{on a hub segment})
$$

By contrast, on a managed switch with a populated CAM table, the switch has already learned the binding $\text{MAC}_{H_j} \to \text{Port}_p$. The frame is forwarded **only** to $\text{Port}_p$, yielding:

$$
P_{capture} = \frac{1}{N} \quad (\text{on a switched segment, naive sniff})
$$

This $N$-fold reduction in capture probability is precisely why **active attacks** (ARP poisoning) are required to defeat switch isolation.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: ARP Poisoning Attack (Active Sniffing)

The attacker $A$ wishes to intercept traffic between victim $V$ and gateway $G$. The attack proceeds in five discrete steps:

**Step 1 — Reconnaissance:**
Attacker identifies $V$'s IP $\text{IP}_V$, $G$'s IP $\text{IP}_G$, and the broadcast MAC address $\text{MAC}_{BC}$ using a tool like `arping` or `netdiscover`.

$$
\text{Target} = \{ \text{IP}_V, \text{IP}_G, \text{MAC}_{BC} \}
$$

**Step 2 — Crafting Forged ARP Replies:**
The attacker constructs a gratuitous ARP reply of the form:

$$
\text{ARP}_{\text{reply}} = \{ \text{Sender IP} = \text{IP}_G, \; \text{Sender MAC} = \text{MAC}_A, \; \text{Target IP} = \text{IP}_V, \; \text{Target MAC} = \text{MAC}_V \}
$$

**Step 3 — Injection:**
This forged packet is broadcast on the LAN. The victim $V$'s OS updates its ARP cache such that:

$$
\text{ARP}_{V}: \text{IP}_G \to \text{MAC}_A
$$

**Step 4 — Traffic Redirection:**
When $V$ now sends a packet destined for the gateway, it encapsulates the frame with:

$$
\text{Ethernet Header}: \text{Dest MAC} = \text{MAC}_A
$$

The switch dutifully forwards this to the **attacker's port** instead of the gateway's port.

**Step 5 — Forwarding & Sniffing:**
Attacker enables IP forwarding (`sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1`) so that captured traffic is *relayed* to the real gateway, making the attack transparent to the victim. The attacker can now run a sniffer (e.g., `urlsnarf`, `dsniff`) on this forwarded stream.

## 3.3 Detection Probability Matrix

| Detection Method | Detects Passive Sniffer? | Detects Active Sniffer? | False Positive Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| ARP Watch / ARPON | No | **Yes** | Low |
| Promiscuous Mode Detection (e.g., `Nmap -sn`, Sentinel) | **Yes** | No | Medium |
| DNS Round-Trip Latency Analysis | No | **Yes** | High |
| Switch Port Statistics (MAC FLAP) | No | **Yes** | Low |
| IDS Signature (e.g., Snort `arp:sPoof`) | No | **Yes** | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Counterintuitive Insight:** A truly passive sniffer on a hub is **mathematically undetectable** by the communicating endpoints — there is no packet signature, no timing anomaly, and no extra traffic. Detection is only possible *out-of-band* (e.g., by an IDS with port mirroring).

## 3.4 Decision Matrix: Choosing a Sniffing Countermeasure

| Environment | Recommended Defense | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Small Office (Hub-based) | Replace hub with managed switch | Eliminates broadcast sniffing |
| Enterprise Switched Network | Port Security + DHCP Snooping + DAI | Blocks MAC flooding and ARP poisoning |
| Wi-Fi Network | Use **WPA3** with **Management Frame Protection (802.11w)** | Encrypts traffic; prevents session hijack via disassoc |
| Sensitive Application | End-to-End Encryption (TLS 1.3, IPsec) | Even if captured, payload is unreadable |
| Critical Infrastructure | Hardware Tap + Encrypted Backhaul | Air-gapped forensic capture |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Passive Sniffer Architecture on a Hub

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Host A] -- Frame F --> HUB[Ethernet Hub]
    HUB -- Broadcast F --> B[Host B]
    HUB -- Broadcast F --> S[Host S - Sniffer]
    HUB -- Broadcast F --> C[Host C]
    S --> CAP[Capture Buffer - libpcap]
    CAP --> DEC[Protocol Dissector]
    DEC --> UI[Wireshark UI - Hex / Tree View]
    DEC --> FILE[PCAP File Storage]
```

**Interpretation:** The hub repeats every frame on all ports. The sniffer host S captures all traffic indiscriminately because its NIC is in promiscuous mode.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Active Sniffer via ARP Poisoning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Victim V] -- Step 1: Send to Gateway MAC --> SW[Switch]
    SW -- Step 2: Lookup CAM table --> ATK[Attacker A]
    ATK -- Step 3: Sniff in pcap --> LOG[Credentials, URLs, Cookies]
    ATK -- Step 4: IP Forward to Real Gateway --> GW[Real Gateway]
    GW -- Step 5: Reply to Victim --> V
    ATK -. Step 0: Gratuitous ARP Reply .-> V
```

**Interpretation:** The attacker (A) poisons V's ARP cache to redirect V's traffic. A logs everything, then forwards it transparently to the real gateway (G). V is unaware.

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Sniffer Operation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NIC[NIC in Promiscuous Mode] --> RAW[Raw Frame Buffer - Ring]
    RAW --> BPF{BPF Filter Match?}
    BPF -- Yes --> DECODE[Protocol Dissection Layer]
    BPF -- No --> DROP[Drop Packet]
    DECODE --> REC[Reassembly: TCP Stream, TLS Decrypt]
    REC --> VIEW[Live View / Hex Dump]
    REC --> STORE[Save to PCAP File]
    VIEW --> EXP[Export Objects - HTTP files, IMAGES]
    STORE --> FOR[Forensic Offline Analysis]
```

**Interpretation:** This shows the complete internal pipeline of a software sniffer like Wireshark, from raw NIC capture through filtering, dissection, reassembly, and storage.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Countermeasure Stack (Defense in Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Replace Hubs with Switches] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2: Port Security - Limit MACs per port] --> L3
    L3[Layer 3: DHCP Snooping - Block rogue DHCP] --> L4
    L4[Layer 4: Dynamic ARP Inspection DAI] --> L5
    L5[Layer 5: 802.1X Port Authentication] --> L6
    L6[Layer 6: TLS 1.3 - Encrypt application payload] --> L7
    L7[Layer 7: IDS/IPS - Snort signature for ARP spoof]
```

**Interpretation:** A robust enterprise defense against sniffers employs layered controls. Even if one layer is bypassed, subsequent layers protect the confidentiality of the data.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain what is meant by "promiscuous mode" in the context of a packet sniffer. Why is it required?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Promiscuous mode is a NIC operational mode in which the network interface card forwards **every** frame it receives to the operating system kernel, regardless of the destination MAC address. By default, a NIC operates in non-promiscuous mode and discards frames not addressed to its own MAC, which would make packet sniffing impossible on a switched network.

A sniffer requires promiscuous mode to capture traffic not destined for the local host — for example, when running on a hub or a SPAN port to analyze an entire network segment. (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for default behavior contrast, 1 for sniffer requirement justification.)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between passive and active sniffing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passive Sniffing | Active Sniffing |
|---|---|---|
| Traffic Modification | Does not inject packets | Injects forged packets (e.g., ARP) |
| Detectability | Virtually undetectable | Detectable by IDS/ARP monitors |
| Environment | Hubs, SPAN ports, Wi-Fi | Switched LANs |
| Example Tool | Wireshark on a hub | Ettercap in ARP-poison mode |

---

---

## PART B — Full-Question Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. The structure below (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks mirrors the actual KTU End-Semester Exam paper template.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and working of a packet sniffer**. Describe the role of the Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF) in the capture pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A packet sniffer consists of four major subsystems:

1. **Capture Engine** — Places the NIC into promiscuous mode and copies raw frames from the hardware ring buffer into a kernel-space buffer using a library like `libpcap` (Linux) or `WinPcap`/`Npcap` (Windows).

2. **Filter Engine (BPF)** — Applies user-defined filter expressions (e.g., `tcp port 80`) at the kernel level using a small register-based virtual machine. Only matching packets are copied to user space, drastically reducing CPU overhead.

3. **Protocol Dissector** — Decodes the captured frames layer by layer: Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application (HTTP, DNS, FTP, SMTP). Each dissector parses the relevant fields and constructs a human-readable protocol tree.

4. **Presentation / Storage Layer** — Displays packets in a three-pane view (packet list, protocol tree, hex dump) and optionally saves them to a `.pcap` file using the standard `pcap` file format defined in RFC 4127.

**BPF Role:** BPF filters run in kernel space before the packet is copied to user space, which means a 1 Gbps link with 90% unwanted traffic can be filtered down to a manageable stream without context-switching overhead. (7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for four subsystems, 2 for BPF role.)

---

**(b)** Explain the **ARP poisoning attack** with a stepwise description. How does an attacker use it to perform active sniffing on a switched network? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

ARP poisoning exploits the stateless and unauthenticated nature of the ARP protocol. The attacker sends **gratuitous ARP replies** that falsely bind the gateway's IP address to the attacker's MAC address in the victim's ARP cache.

**Step-by-step procedure:**
1. **Identify targets:** Use tools like `arping` or `netdiscover` to find victim IP $\text{IP}_V$, gateway IP $\text{IP}_G$, and the broadcast MAC.
2. **Enable IP forwarding:** The attacker runs `sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1` so that intercepted traffic is relayed to the real gateway, keeping the victim's network access intact (stealth).
3. **Craft and send gratuitous ARP:** The attacker broadcasts a forged ARP reply claiming `IP_G → MAC_A`.
4. **Victim updates ARP cache:** The victim's OS trusts the gratuitous reply and updates its cache: `IP_G → MAC_A`.
5. **Traffic redirection:** All victim traffic meant for the gateway is now sent to the attacker's MAC. The switch's CAM table reflects this, and traffic flows to the attacker's port.
6. **Sniff and forward:** The attacker runs Wireshark/Tcpdump on the redirected stream, capturing credentials, URLs, cookies, etc., while transparently forwarding packets onward to maintain connectivity.

This converts a switched network (which is normally immune to passive sniffing) into a hub-like environment from the victim's perspective. (7 marks — 2 for ARP protocol weakness, 3 for step-by-step attack, 2 for switched network defeat mechanism.)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **different types of sniffers** based on their implementation (hardware vs. software) and architecture (passive vs. active). Give **two examples** for each category. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**By Implementation:**
- **Hardware Sniffers (Network Taps):** Physical inline devices that copy optical or electrical signals for out-of-band monitoring. Examples: **Net Optics Tap**, **Garland Technology Tap**, **Viavi Solutions** taps. Used in data centers and compliance-driven environments where software compromise is a concern.
- **Software Sniffers:** Application programs running on a standard OS. Examples: **Wireshark** (GUI-based, cross-platform), **Tcpdump** (CLI, Linux/Unix), **Ettercap** (active MITM suite), **Kismet** (Wi-Fi sniffer).

**By Architecture:**
- **Passive Sniffers:** Only listen; do not inject traffic. Examples: **Wireshark on a hub**, **Kismet in monitor mode**, **Darkstat**.
- **Active Sniffers:** Inject traffic to manipulate the network. Examples: **Ettercap in ARP-poison mode**, **Cain & Abel**, **bettercap**.

**Distinguishing factors:** Passive sniffers are virtually undetectable and require no special privileges beyond root. Active sniffers are traceable, generate visible artifacts (MAC FLAPS, gratuitous ARP alerts), and are detected by modern IDS/IPS systems. (7 marks — 2 for hardware, 2 for software, 2 for passive/active, 1 for examples.)

---

**(b)** Describe the **countermeasures** that can be deployed to prevent sniffing attacks in a switched Ethernet network. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A defense-in-depth strategy is required, with the following layered controls:

1. **Replace Hubs with Managed Switches** — Eliminates the trivial broadcast-sniffing vector. *(1 mark)*

2. **Port Security (IEEE 802.1X)** — Limits the number of MAC addresses allowed per switch port, blocking MAC flooding attacks. *(1 mark)*

3. **DHCP Snooping** — Prevents rogue DHCP servers from issuing malicious IP configurations. *(1 mark)*

4. **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** — Validates ARP packets against a trusted DHCP snooping database, blocking gratuitous ARP replies that do not match. *(1 mark)*

5. **MAC Address Sticky / Static CAM Entries** — Pin critical servers' MAC-to-port mappings to prevent CAM poisoning. *(1 mark)*

6. **Encryption (TLS 1.3, IPsec, WPA3)** — Even if a packet is captured, the payload remains confidential. This is the **ultimate** defense against sniffing. *(1 mark)*

7. **IDS/IPS Deployment (e.g., Snort, Suricata)** — Detects signature-based indicators of ARP spoofing, MAC FLAP events, and DNS poisoning in real time. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - Writing only "use encryption" without mentioning **specific protocols (TLS 1.3, IPsec, WPA3)** loses 1 mark.
> - Confusing **DHCP Snooping** with **DAI** — these are separate but complementary controls; DAI depends on the DHCP snooping binding table.
> - Forgetting to mention **port security's role in MAC flooding prevention** costs 1 mark.
> - Listing "antivirus" as a countermeasure — sniffers are not malware; AV cannot detect them.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Sniffer Definition:** Tool that captures and analyzes network packets by placing the NIC in **promiscuous mode**. Operates at OSI Layer 2.
- **Two Major Categories:** Passive (listen-only) vs. Active (inject-and-redirect). Hardware (taps) vs. Software (Wireshark, Tcpdump).
- **Hub vs. Switch:** Hubs broadcast to all ports (sniffing trivial). Switches isolate ports (sniffing requires active attack).
- **ARP Poisoning:** Most common active sniffing attack; redirects victim traffic by poisoning the ARP cache with gratuitous replies.
- **MAC Flooding:** Overwhelms the switch CAM table to force fail-open into hub behavior.
- **DNS Cache Poisoning:** Injects forged DNS records into a resolver's cache.
- **Standard Capture Format:** **PCAP / PCAPNG** (libpcap-based, RFC 4127).
- **Key Defense Layers:** Managed switches, Port Security, DHCP Snooping, DAI, TLS 1.3, WPA3, IDS/IPS.
- **BPF Role:** Kernel-level pre-filtering of captured packets for performance.
- **Detection:** ARP watch tools, promiscuous-mode detectors, switch MAC FLAP logs, IDS signatures.
- **Ultimate Countermeasure:** **End-to-End Encryption** — even captured traffic is unreadable without keys.
- **Tools to Remember for KTU Exam:** Wireshark, Tcpdump, Ettercap, Cain & Abel, Kismet, bettercap, NetworkMiner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Social Engineering: Shoulder Surfing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Social Engineering: Shoulder Surfing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shoulder Surfing** is a low-tech, non-invasive form of **social engineering attack** in which an adversary directly or indirectly observes a victim's private information — typically credentials such as passwords, PINs, OTP codes, credit card numbers, or confidential data — by physically or remotely watching the victim enter that information into a system, terminal, or interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard):**
> "Shoulder surfing is a social engineering technique where an attacker covertly observes a target's screen, keyboard, or keypad to obtain sensitive information such as passwords, personal identification numbers (PINs), and other confidential data, often executed in public places like ATMs, airports, or coffee shops."

In the context of **Ethical Hacking**, shoulder surfing is classified under the **Human-Based Social Engineering** category of the *Information Gathering & Human Exploitation* phase of a penetration test (Reconnaissance → Enumeration → Exploitation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at an **ATM kiosk** in a crowded mall. You insert your card, cover the keypad with one hand (good practice!), and type your 4-digit PIN. The person in the queue behind you, who appears to be casually scrolling on their phone, is actually recording a **silent video** of your fingertips pressing the keys — by simply holding their phone camera at a tilted angle beneath the gap of your hand.

That silent video is later analyzed frame-by-frame. The **DISTANCE between successive keypresses** and the **pressure pattern** reveal the PIN sequence.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Hook:** Shoulder surfing is essentially *"eavesdropping with the eyes instead of the ears."* The attacker doesn't need to *hack* the system — they simply let **you** enter the secret, while they **watch you do it.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Benchmarks

In KTU board evaluations and penetration testing literature, the following metrics are standard:

- **Observation Distance:** Typically **0.5 m to 3 m** for direct eye-based attacks; up to **15–30 m** with binoculars or zoom lenses.
- **Standard PIN Length Targeted:** **4-digit numeric PINs** (banking standard) — brute-force reduced from 10,000 to a handful of possibilities via shoulder surfing.
- **Average Success Time:** An experienced attacker can memorize an **8-character alphanumeric password** from a single glance within **2–4 seconds** (cited in Kevin Mitnick's *The Art of Deception*).
- **Common Target Environments:** **ATMs, airport check-in kiosks, coffee shop Wi-Fi logins, corporate reception areas, mobile phone unlock screens.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Shoulder surfing is a *direct observation attack* and does **not** require any technical intrusion into the target system. It is therefore classified as a *non-technical / physical-layer social engineering* vector in the **OWASP Social Engineering Top 10** framework.

## 1.4 Classification of Shoulder Surfing

| Class | Method | Tool Required | Skill Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Observation** | Looking over the shoulder with naked eyes | None | Low |
| **Remote Optical** | Using binoculars, telescopes, or zoom lenses | Optical device | Low–Medium |
| **Surveillance Camera** | CCTV or hidden camera recording | Camera | Medium |
| **Mobile Phone Camera** | Covert video/audio recording via phone | Smartphone | Low |
| **Reflective Surface** | Watching reflections on glasses, windows, or screens | Surface | Medium |
| **Thermal / Heat Mapping** | Analyzing residual heat on keypads | Thermal camera | High |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Caution:** When asked to *classify* shoulder surfing, students often confuse it with **phishing** (digital) or **dumpster diving** (offline data recovery). Shoulder surfing is **strictly a real-time, observation-based** technique — there is no digital footprint.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — The "How & Why"

The shoulder surfing attack lifecycle can be broken down into **six distinct phases**, each of which is a high-yield point for KTU board questions:

### Phase 1: Target Selection & Reconnaissance
- The attacker identifies a high-value target (e.g., a corporate executive entering credentials, an ATM user, a smartphone user typing a banking password).
- Reconnaissance includes identifying **less-crowded locations**, **single-occupancy workstations**, and **users with poor security hygiene** (e.g., users who don't shield their keyboards).

### Phase 2: Positioning & Approach
- The attacker positions themselves at an **angle of 15°–45°** relative to the target's screen or keypad — close enough to read, far enough to avoid suspicion.
- Common pretexts: *"Sorry, is this the queue?"*, pretending to be on a phone call, posing as a maintenance worker.

### Phase 3: Observation Capture
- Information is captured via the **visual channel** (eyes), **photographic channel** (camera/phone), or **reflective channel** (mirror/glasses).
- Critical visual data includes:
  - **Keystroke order** (sequence of characters).
  - **Keystroke spacing** (relative position on a virtual keyboard).
  - **Screen reflections** (visible on glasses or windows).

### Phase 4: Information Reconstruction
- If recorded, the attacker replays the video at **0.25x–0.5x speed** to recover keystrokes.
- For PIN pads, attackers often use **post-attack statistical analysis** — most users press keys in *clusters* (e.g., 1-2-3-5 is more common than 7-8-9-0).

### Phase 5: Validation & Exploitation
- The captured credentials are validated by **attempting login** (for online systems) or **direct use at an ATM**.
- In **ethical hacking engagements**, this phase is replaced by documentation and reporting to the client.

### Phase 6: Covering Tracks
- The attacker deletes any captured media, exits the area, and resumes normal behavior.

## 2.2 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In penetration testing, shoulder surfing is used during the **physical security assessment** phase. Ethical hackers perform "**red team**" exercises that include:

- **Office walkthroughs** to identify users who leave sticky notes with passwords on monitors.
- **Public Wi-Fi hotspot audits** to identify users entering credentials over unencrypted channels.
- **Corporate training** to demonstrate how easily credentials can be lifted in a 5-second window.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The 2024 scheme places heavy emphasis on the *human factor* in cybersecurity. Shoulder surfing, phishing, and tailgating collectively form the **"Big Three"** of social engineering vectors — and shoulder surfing is the *simplest to execute* of the three.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Value / Standard** | **Relevance** |
|---|---|---|
| Attack Category | Human-Based Social Engineering | KTU Module 1 |
| Attack Vector | Direct / Indirect Visual Observation | KTU Module 1 |
| Primary Targets | Passwords, PINs, OTPs, Card Numbers | KTU Module 1 |
| Common Locations | ATMs, Airports, Cafés, Offices | KTU Module 1 |
| Optimal Attack Angle | 15°–45° to target's screen | Conceptual Q |
| Standard PIN Search Space | $10^4 = 10{,}000$ combinations | 3-Mark Q |
| Standard Password Search Space (8-char) | $94^8 \approx 6.09 \times 10^{15}$ | Conceptual Q |
| Effective Search Space Reduction | Up to **95%** with shoulder surfing | 3-Mark Q |
| Countermeasure Class | Behavioral + Technical | KTU Module 1 |
| Best Defense | **Privacy Filters + Biometric Auth** | KTU Module 1 |
| Detection Difficulty | **Very High** (no digital logs) | Conceptual Q |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Note for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> A 4-digit numeric PIN has a search space of:
> $$ N_{\text{PIN}} = 10^4 = 10{,}000 $$
> When shoulder surfing reduces candidate PINs to roughly **5–10** possibilities (e.g., by observing 3 of 4 digits), the **effective brute-force effort** drops by a factor of $\frac{10^4}{10} = 10^3$ — a **1000x reduction** in attack time.

## 2.4 Countermeasure Architecture

Countermeasures are classified into **three layers**:

| Layer | Countermeasure | Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|
| **Behavioral** | Shielding keypad with hand, awareness training | Medium |
| **Environmental** | Privacy screen filters, anti-glare, position of ATM in wall | High |
| **Technical** | Biometric authentication, OTP-based 2FA, token-based auth | Very High |
| **Policy** | Clean desk policy, no sticky notes, mandatory screen locks | High |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** A privacy screen filter only protects against **direct line-of-sight attacks**. It does **not** prevent a thermal imaging attack on a keypad 30 seconds *after* use, nor a hidden camera recording the user's hand movements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Information Theoretic Cost of Shoulder Surfing

The following analytical treatment allows a student to answer **mathematical and analytical** questions in KTU exams that test the **entropy reduction** caused by shoulder surfing.

### Step 1: Define the Initial Information Entropy

For a password of length $L$ drawn from an alphabet of size $A$, the **Shannon entropy** (in bits) of the password space is:

$$
H_{\text{initial}} = \log_2(A^L) = L \cdot \log_2(A)
$$

For a 4-digit numeric PIN:
$$
A = 10, \quad L = 4
$$

$$
H_{\text{initial}} = 4 \cdot \log_2(10)
$$

$$
H_{\text{initial}} = 4 \cdot 3.3219
$$

$$
H_{\text{initial}} \approx 13.29 \text{ bits}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A uniform 4-digit PIN carries approximately **13.29 bits of entropy** — meaning a brute-force attacker needs, on average, $\frac{10{,}000}{2} = 5{,}000$ attempts.

### Step 2: Define the Information Leaked via Shoulder Surfing

Suppose the attacker successfully observes $k$ of the $L$ characters of the password. The **information leaked** $I_{\text{leaked}}$ is:

$$
I_{\text{leaked}} = k \cdot \log_2(A)
$$

For a 4-digit PIN where the attacker observes 3 of 4 digits:

$$
I_{\text{leaked}} = 3 \cdot \log_2(10) = 3 \cdot 3.3219 \approx 9.97 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Residual Entropy

The **residual entropy** of the password after shoulder surfing is:

$$
H_{\text{residual}} = H_{\text{initial}} - I_{\text{leaked}} = (L - k) \cdot \log_2(A)
$$

Substituting $L = 4$ and $k = 3$:

$$
H_{\text{residual}} = (4 - 3) \cdot \log_2(10)
$$

$$
H_{\text{residual}} = 1 \cdot 3.3219
$$

$$
H_{\text{residual}} \approx 3.32 \text{ bits}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The attacker now faces only $\frac{10^1}{2} = 5$ average attempts to brute-force the final digit.

### Step 4: Compute the Search Space Reduction Factor

$$
R = \frac{A^L}{A^{L-k}} = A^k
$$

For our example:

$$
R = 10^3 = 1{,}000
$$

This means shoulder surfing reduced the attacker's workload by a factor of **1000x**.

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style)

**Question:** A 6-character password uses lowercase alphabets only (A=26). The attacker shoulders 4 of the 6 characters. Calculate: (i) initial entropy, (ii) residual entropy, (iii) search space reduction.

**Solution:**

**(i) Initial Entropy:**

$$
H_{\text{initial}} = 6 \cdot \log_2(26) = 6 \cdot 4.7004 \approx 28.20 \text{ bits}
$$

**(ii) Residual Entropy:**

$$
H_{\text{residual}} = (6 - 4) \cdot \log_2(26) = 2 \cdot 4.7004 \approx 9.40 \text{ bits}
$$

**(iii) Search Space Reduction:**

$$
R = 26^4 = 456{,}976
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the *entropy loss* is **linear in $k$** but the *search space reduction* is **exponential in $k$**. This is why even partial observation of a password is catastrophic — a single additional character observed can multiply attack efficiency by **26x to 94x** depending on the alphabet.

## 3.2 KTU Analytical Matrix: Attack vs. Defense

| **Attack Variant** | **Information Captured** | **Effective Entropy Loss** | **Best Defense** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single direct glance at PIN entry | 1–2 digits | $1 \cdot \log_2(10) \approx 3.32$ bits | Hand shielding |
| Slow-motion video analysis | All digits, full sequence | $\approx 13.29$ bits (full) | Biometric auth |
| Reflection on glasses/window | Partial screen content | $30\%–70\%$ of password entropy | Privacy filter |
| Thermal residual (30s post-use) | Last 1–3 digits | $\approx 3.32\text{–}9.97$ bits | Random keypad layout |
| Zoom-lens from 10m | Full screen content | $\approx 100\%$ entropy loss | Position-based privacy |

## 3.3 Decision Tree: When is Shoulder Surfing Most Effective?

A high-yield KTU question style is asking students to **identify the conditions** under which shoulder surfing is most likely to succeed. The answer is:

1. **High-traffic public locations** (anonymity of the attacker).
2. **Targets with low security awareness** (uncovered keypads).
3. **Slow typing** (more observation time).
4. **Predictable input patterns** (e.g., left-to-right PIN entry).
5. **Fixed-angle terminals** (e.g., wall-mounted ATMs in narrow corridors).
6. **Absence of privacy filters or biometric alternatives**.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked *"Why is shoulder surfing still relevant in 2024 despite biometric authentication?"*, the correct answer is that **biometrics are not yet universal** — ATMs, legacy systems, password managers, and corporate VPNs still rely on typed credentials, keeping shoulder surfing a viable attack vector.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Attack Lifecycle Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Attacker Identifies Target"]:::attackNode --> B["Positioning at 15-45 Degree Angle"]
    B --> C["Covert Observation Initiated"]
    C --> D["Keystrokes / Screen Captured"]
    D --> E{"Recording Method?"}
    E -- "Direct Eyes" --> F["Memory Based Capture"]
    E -- "Phone Camera" --> G["Video Stored on Device"]
    E -- "Optical Aid" --> H["Binocular / Telescope Capture"]
    F --> I["Information Reconstruction"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Credential Validation"]
    J --> K["Exploitation or Reporting"]
    K --> L["Cover Tracks and Exit"]

```

## 4.2 Defense-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
    L4 --> L5["Layer 5: Organizational Policy Audit"]

```

## 4.3 Attack Surface Map: Shoulder Surfing Vectors

```mermaid

    end

```

## 4.4 Entropy Reduction Schematic

