# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an Exploit Database. List any four popular exploit databases used in ethical hacking.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **Exploit Database** is a curated repository containing information about publicly disclosed software and hardware vulnerabilities along with proof-of-concept exploit code, vulnerability metadata, and remediation references. It enables ethical hackers and security researchers to identify, validate, and remediate known weaknesses during authorized penetration tests. *(1 mark)*
>
> Four popular exploit databases: *(2 marks — ½ mark each)*
> 1. **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)** — MITRE-maintained unique-ID system.
> 2. **NVD (National Vulnerability Database)** — NIST-maintained extension of CVE with CVSS scores.
> 3. **Exploit-DB** — OffSec-maintained archive of public exploits and vulnerable applications.
> 4. **Metasploit Framework Database** — Rapid7's modular exploit DB integrated with the Metasploit console.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the CVSS Base Score range and the severity bands defined under CVSS v3.1?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** ranges from **0.0 to 10.0**, where 0.0 indicates no impact and 10.0 indicates maximum impact. *(1 mark)*
>
> Severity bands defined under CVSS v3.1: *(2 marks)*
> - **None:** 0.0
> - **Low:** 0.1 – 3.9
> - **Medium:** 4.0 – 6.9
> - **High:** 7.0 – 8.9
> - **Critical:** 9.0 – 10.0

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Module Choice Rule:** KTU 2024 Scheme awards **internal choice within the module**. The student answers **either Question A or Question B**, both drawn exclusively from Module 2.

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture and components of an Exploit Database. Describe the role of MITRE, NIST, and OffSec in maintaining these repositories. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example, compute the **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** for a vulnerability having the vector string `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H` and state the severity band. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

The architecture of an Exploit Database is composed of **eight canonical data fields** maintained for every published vulnerability:

| # | Field | Role | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unique Identifier** (CVE-ID/EDB-ID) | Canonical reference across the industry | 0.5 |
| 2 | **Vulnerability Title** | Human-readable summary | 0.5 |
| 3 | **CWE Classification** | Root-cause taxonomy (e.g., `CWE-89`) | 0.5 |
| 4 | **CVSS Vector String** | Machine-parseable severity encoding | 1.0 |
| 5 | **Affected Software (CPE)** | Identifies impacted products/versions | 0.5 |
| 6 | **Exploit Code / PoC** | Reproducible attack demonstration | 1.0 |
| 7 | **Mitigation / Patch** | Vendor advisory & remediation guidance | 0.5 |
| 8 | **Disclosure Timeline** | Audit trail of responsible disclosure | 0.5 |

**Roles of the three governing bodies:**

- **MITRE Corporation** *(1 mark)*: Operates the **CVE Dictionary** as the central registry of unique vulnerability IDs. It assigns the `CVE-YYYY-NNNNN` identifier and provides the official description.
- **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology)** *(1 mark)*: Maintains the **NVD**, which ingests MITRE's CVE feed and enriches each record with CVSS scores, CPE matches, and patch references. NVD is the de-facto authoritative source for severity scoring.
- **OffSec (Offensive Security)** *(1 mark)*: Maintains **Exploit-DB**, a community-driven archive of working exploit scripts and vulnerable application binaries used by penetration testers.
- **Integration** *(0.5 marks)*: A modern vulnerability management workflow queries the CVE/NVD chain first (for severity) and then Exploit-DB (for the working PoC), before pivoting to the Metasploit framework for weaponized exploitation.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Vector String:**
$$
\vec{V} = \text{CVSS:3.1}/\text{AV:N}/\text{AC:L}/\text{PR:N}/\text{UI:N}/\text{S:U}/\text{C:H}/\text{I:H}/\text{A:H}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute Weights** *(1 mark)*:

| Metric | Value | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| AV:N | 0.85 |  |
| AC:L | 0.77 |  |
| PR:N | 0.85 |  |
| UI:N | 0.85 |  |
| S:U | Unchanged |  |
| C:H | 0.56 |  |
| I:H | 0.56 |  |
| A:H | 0.56 |  |

**Step 2 — Compute ISS** *(1 mark)*:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISS} &= 1 - [(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)] \\
&= 1 - (0.44)^3 \\
&= 1 - 0.0852 \\
&= 0.9148
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Impact** *(Scope = Unchanged branch)* *(1 mark)*:
$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISS} = 6.42 \times 0.9148 \approx 5.873
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ESS** *(1 mark)*:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ESS} &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
&\approx 3.887
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine and Round** *(1 mark)*:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BaseScore} &= \text{roundUp}(\min(5.873 + 3.887,\; 10)) \\
&= \text{roundUp}(9.76) \\
&= \mathbf{9.8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Severity Mapping** *(1 mark)*:
$$
9.8 \in [9.0,\; 10.0] \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Severity} = \mathbf{Critical}
$$

**Conclusion:** A score of **9.8 (Critical)** demands immediate remediation, typically within 24 hours per enterprise SLA. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **CVE**, **NVD**, and **Exploit-DB** with respect to purpose, data content, maintainer, and update cadence. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **legal and ethical considerations** an ethical hacker must observe while querying and using exploit databases during an authorized penetration test. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Dimension | **CVE** | **NVD** | **Exploit-DB** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Assign unique IDs to vulnerabilities | Enrich CVEs with severity & metadata | Archive public exploit code |
| **Maintainer** | MITRE Corporation | U.S. NIST | OffSec (Offensive Security) |
| **Data Content** | ID + brief description | CVE + CVSS + CPE + advisories | EDB-ID + exploit script + vulnerable app |
| **Update Cadence** | Continuous (as reported) | Daily sync from MITRE | Real-time (community submissions) |
| **Access Cost** | Free | Free | Free |
| **Severity Scoring** | Not provided | Provided (CVSS) | Not provided |

**Key Distinctions** *(2 marks synthesis)*:
- **CVE is the identifier; NVD is the encyclopedia; Exploit-DB is the weapons cache.**
- An ethical hacker typically queries them in the order: **CVE (lookup ID) → NVD (get CVSS) → Exploit-DB (get PoC)**.
- CVE entries alone do not contain weaponized code; the legal ambiguity of exploit code is concentrated in the Exploit-DB layer.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Ethical and Legal Pillars** *(1 mark each)*:

1. **Written Authorization (Scope of Work):** A penetration test is legal **only** when a signed SoW explicitly lists target IPs/domains and a permitted testing window. Querying an exploit database against an unauthorized target is illegal under the **IT Act, 2000 (India) §§ 43 & 66**, and equivalents like the **CFAA (USA)** or **Computer Misuse Act (UK)**.

2. **No Weaponization Beyond Scope:** Even if a critical exploit is found, it may not be executed against a production system if it falls outside the written scope. Use lab replicas (Metasploitable VMs) for proof.

3. **Responsible Disclosure Compliance:** Many CVEs have a **disclosure embargo** period. Disclosing a vulnerability before the vendor releases a patch may violate coordinated disclosure agreements.

4. **Data Handling & Confidentiality:** Any data extracted via a successful exploit (credentials, PII) must be **encrypted, logged, and destroyed** post-engagement as per the SoW.

5. **Tool Restrictions:** Certain tools (e.g., zero-day brokers, commercial exploit packs like Canvas or Core Impact) require additional export-control licenses.

6. **Documentation & Chain of Custody:** Maintain a verifiable audit trail of which database was queried, when, by whom, and for what purpose — this protects the tester in any post-engagement legal review.

7. **Non-Weaponization for Offensive Use Outside Engagement:** An ethical hacker must **never** reuse an exploit obtained during a paid engagement against a third party, even if the third party is vulnerable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in 14-Mark Answers:**
> - **❌ Confusing CVE with NVD (–1 mark):** CVE is the ID; NVD is the enriched database. Examiners deduct for interchangeable use.
> - **❌ Forgetting the Scope branch selector in CVSS (–2 marks):** Many students use the `Scope = Unchanged` formula even when the vector contains `S:C`. This yields an incorrect base score.
> - **❌ Skipping the Lab Validation step (–1 mark):** Always mention a sandboxed Metasploitable/Kali setup before live exploitation.
> - **❌ Using raw `|` pipes in vector strings (–0.5 mark for sloppy notation):** Use the proper slash `/` separator as per the CVSS v3.1 specification.
> - **❌ Failing to cite the legal framework (–1 mark):** IT Act 2000/CFAA citation differentiates a 13-mark answer from a full 14-mark answer.
> - **❌ Treating Exploit-DB as the only database (–1 mark):** Always mention at least **three** sources for breadth.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 / Topic: Exploit Databases**
>
> - ✅ **Definition:** Exploit databases are dual-use repositories of vulnerability IDs, metadata, and PoC code.
> - ✅ **Three Pillars:** CVE (MITRE), NVD (NIST), Exploit-DB (OffSec) — know the distinction cold.
> - ✅ **CVE-ID format:** `CVE-YYYY-NNNNN` (≥ 4 digits).
> - ✅ **CVSS range:** 0.0 – 10.0; severity bands: None / Low / Medium / High / Critical.
> - ✅ **Eight canonical fields:** ID, title, CWE, CVSS vector, CPE, exploit code, mitigation, timeline.
> - ✅ **CVSS v3.1 vector structure:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:/AC:/PR:/UI:/S:/C:/I:/A:`.
> - ✅ **Scope = C** triggers the second-branch Impact formula — memorize both branches.
> - ✅ **Workflow:** Recon → Scan → DB Lookup → Lab Validation → Production Exploitation → Reporting.
> - ✅ **Legal triad:** IT Act 2000 §§ 43 & 66, CFAA, Computer Misuse Act — always cite one.
> - ✅ **Ethical imperatives:** Written SoW, responsible disclosure, data destruction, audit trail.
> - ✅ **CVSS weighted values to memorize:** `AV:N=0.85`, `PR:N=0.85`, `UI:N=0.85`, `C/I/A:H=0.56`, `S:C=Changes branch`.
> - ✅ **Metasploit path syntax:** `exploit/<platform>/<service>/<name>`.
> - ✅ **Tools for DB querying:** `searchsploit` (CLI for Exploit-DB), NVD REST API, Vulners API.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Sniffing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Sniffing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network Sniffing** (also called **Packet Sniffing** or **Packet Analysis**) is the process of monitoring, capturing, intercepting, and decoding the data packets flowing across a computer network using specialized software or hardware tools known as **packet sniffers** or **protocol analyzers**. In the context of ethical hacking, sniffing is a critical **reconnaissance and information-gathering** technique used to analyze traffic on wired (Ethernet) and wireless (Wi-Fi) networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Network Sniffing is a passive or active technique of capturing data packets traversing a network medium to inspect, analyze, and interpret the protocol-level information being exchanged between communicating entities.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a busy café where hundreds of people are simultaneously having conversations at different tables. You can *normally* only hear the conversation at your own table because:

- Sound travels in all directions, but your brain filters out distant noise.
- The walls of the building prevent sounds from leaving.

Now imagine you install a **super-sensitive microphone** on the ceiling and connect it to a recording device that captures *every* conversation in the café simultaneously. You can now:
- Listen to all tables.
- Note down who is talking to whom.
- Even record credit card numbers, phone numbers, and passwords spoken aloud.

**Network Sniffing is exactly this scenario** — but in the digital world:
- The "café" is your **LAN (Local Area Network)**.
- The "conversations" are **data packets** traveling between computers.
- The "ceiling microphone" is a **Network Interface Card (NIC)** placed into **promiscuous mode**.
- The "recording device" is the **packet sniffer software** (e.g., Wireshark, tcpdump, Cain & Abel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The exam expects you to understand that sniffing exploits the **shared nature of broadcast media** in Ethernet hubs and unencrypted wireless networks. Modern switched networks mitigate basic sniffing but remain vulnerable to advanced techniques like **ARP poisoning**.

### 1.3 Classification of Network Sniffing

| **Dimension** | **Category 1** | **Category 2** |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection Visibility** | Passive Sniffing | Active Sniffing |
| **Network Medium** | Wired Sniffing | Wireless Sniffing |
| **Location** | Local Sniffing (LAN) | Remote Sniffing (TAP/port mirroring) |
| **Legality** | Authorized (Pen Testing) | Unauthorized (Cyber Crime) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Passive Sniffing** is invisible and undetectable — it simply listens to traffic without injecting packets. **Active Sniffing** involves injecting crafted packets (like ARP replies) to redirect traffic, making it detectable by Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS).

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Network Sniffing

- **Bandwidth (B):** Typically measured in **Mbps** (Megabits per second) for LANs, **Gbps** for modern networks.
- **Throughput (T):** The actual successful packet delivery rate, **T ≤ B**, measured in **packets per second (pps)**.
- **Promiscuous Mode:** A NIC operating mode that captures *all* frames on the medium, not just those addressed to it.
- **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit):** **1500 bytes** for standard Ethernet frames.
- **Round-Trip Time (RTT):** Time taken for a packet to travel from source to destination and back, measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

Network sniffing operates at **Layer 2 (Data Link Layer)** of the **OSI Model**, with analysis extending up to **Layer 7 (Application Layer)**. The process follows these structured logic steps:

**Step 1: Network Interface Initialization**
- The packet sniffer binds to a specific NIC on the attacker's machine.
- The NIC is configured in **promiscuous mode**, disabling its hardware filtering.

**Step 2: Raw Frame Capture**
- The OS kernel's packet capture library (e.g., **libpcap** on Linux, **WinPcap/Npcap** on Windows) intercepts raw frames *before* the protocol stack processes them.
- Every frame passing the wire is duplicated and stored in a circular buffer (capture buffer).

**Step 3: Protocol Decoding**
- Captured frames are decoded through the protocol layers:
  - **Layer 2:** Ethernet II / IEEE 802.3 (MAC addresses, EtherType)
  - **Layer 3:** IPv4 / IPv6 (IP addresses, TTL, checksum)
  - **Layer 4:** TCP / UDP / ICMP (port numbers, sequence numbers)
  - **Layer 7:** HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP, Telnet, SMTP payloads

**Step 4: Pattern Recognition & Analysis**
- Filters (e.g., Wireshark display filters) are applied: `http.request.method == "POST"`, `tcp.port == 80`.
- Attackers search for credentials, session tokens, file transfers, and unencrypted sensitive data.

**Step 5: Data Extraction & Post-Processing**
- Extracted data is exported (PCAP, CSV, JSON) for forensic or exploitation purposes.

### 2.2 Why Sniffing Works: The "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why promiscuous mode?** Standard NICs drop frames not addressed to their MAC. Promiscuous mode disables this hardware-level filter.
- **Why libpcap?** It bypasses the kernel's protocol stack, giving the sniffer raw access to frame bytes.
- **Why Layer 2 capture?** Encryption at higher layers (TLS/SSL) can be bypassed if the sniffer captures *pre-encrypted* data on a local segment.
- **Why broadcast media?** Hubs replicate every frame to all ports. Switches create separate collision domains, requiring active attacks to bypass segmentation.

### 2.3 Active vs. Passive Sniffing — Detailed Comparison

| **Parameter** | **Passive Sniffing** | **Active Sniffing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Silent monitoring of network traffic | Manipulation of traffic to enable capture |
| **Method** | Sets NIC to promiscuous mode | Injects crafted packets (e.g., ARP replies) |
| **Detection Risk** | Very low (near-invisible) | High (detectable by IDS/IPS) |
| **Effectiveness on Hubs** | Highly effective | Highly effective |
| **Effectiveness on Switches** | Limited (only broadcast/multicast) | Effective (via ARP poisoning, MAC flooding) |
| **Tools** | Wireshark, tcpdump, Ettercap (passive mode) | Ettercap, Cain & Abel, BetterCAP |
| **Stealth** | Stealthy | Noisy |
| **Countermeasures** | Encryption (SSH, HTTPS, VPN) | Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI), 802.1X |

### 2.4 Types of Network Sniffing Attacks (KTU High-Yield)

**1. ARP Poisoning (ARP Spoofing)**
- Attacker sends fake ARP replies to associate their MAC with the victim's IP.
- Traffic meant for the victim is redirected to the attacker.

**2. MAC Flooding**
- Attacker floods the switch's CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table with bogus MAC addresses.
- Switch enters fail-open mode, behaving like a hub — broadcasting all traffic.

**3. DHCP Starvation & Rogue DHCP**
- Attacker exhausts the legitimate DHCP pool with bogus DHCP requests.
- Rogue DHCP server hands out malicious gateway/DNS settings.

**4. DNS Poisoning / DNS Spoofing**
- Corrupts the DNS cache to redirect users to malicious websites.
- Captures credentials entered on the fake site.

**5. Port Mirroring (SPAN) Abuse**
- In enterprise environments, switches have a Switched Port Analyzer (SPAN) feature.
- If misconfigured, sensitive traffic is mirrored to an unintended port.

**6. Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)**
- Sniffing combined with traffic redirection positions the attacker between client and server.
- Tools: Ettercap, BetterCAP, mitmproxy.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| **Capture Rate** | $C = N_{\text{captured}} \div N_{\text{total}}$ | Ratio of captured packets to total observed |
| **Capture Efficiency** | $\eta = C \times 100$ | Percentage efficiency of sniffer |
| **Buffer Utilization** | $B_{\text{util}} = \frac{S_{\text{used}}}{S_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Used / total buffer size in percent |
| **Packet Loss Rate** | $L = 1 - C$ | Fraction of packets missed |
| **Throughput Limit** | $T_{\max} = \frac{B}{L_{\text{frame}}}$ | Max throughput = bandwidth / frame length |
| **File Size Estimate** | $F \approx P \times L_{\text{avg}}$ | Total capture size = packets × avg length |
| **TCP Sequence Wrap** | $S_{\text{wrap}} = 2^{32}$ | Sequence number space (4,294,967,296) |
| **Promiscuous Threshold** | $P_{\text{thr}} = \text{frames} \neq \text{self MAC}$ | Detects NIC in promiscuous mode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention the **OSI layer** when discussing sniffing. Sniffing fundamentally works at **Layer 2 (Data Link)**, with decoding continuing up the stack. Also remember the **"hub vs. switch"** distinction — hubs are vulnerable to passive sniffing, switches require active attacks.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Troubleshooting:** Administrators use Wireshark to diagnose latency, packet loss, and protocol errors.
- **Security Audits:** Penetration testers use sniffing to identify unencrypted credentials and misconfigurations.
- **Forensics:** Law enforcement captures PCAP files as legal evidence.
- **Performance Engineering:** Capture-and-replay tools (tcpreplay) stress-test networks.
- **IoT Security:** Critical for monitoring MQTT, CoAP, and Zigbee traffic in smart device ecosystems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Process Flow

### 3.1 Complete Sniffing Process — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

Let us trace the **complete lifecycle** of a packet as it travels from sender to sniffer, applying all theoretical concepts from Sections 1 and 2.

---

**STEP 1: Network Interface Selection**

The attacker identifies the target network interface. On a Linux system, this is enumerated as:

$$
\text{Interface} = \text{eth0} \mid \text{wlan0} \mid \text{lo}
$$

The sniffer software is bound to a specific interface. For example, the Wireshark capture filter syntax:

$$
\text{Capture Filter} = \text{host } 192.168.1.100 \text{ and } \text{tcp port } 80
$$

---

**STEP 2: Enabling Promiscuous Mode**

The NIC's hardware filter is bypassed. The transition is given by:

$$
\text{NIC}_{\text{state}} = \begin{cases} \text{Normal Mode} & \text{if } \text{DestMAC} = \text{SelfMAC} \\ \text{Promiscuous Mode} & \text{if } \text{any DestMAC accepted} \end{cases}
$$

In Linux, this is enabled using the `ifconfig` command with the `promisc` flag, or programmatically via `ioctl()` system call.

---

**STEP 3: Frame Reception and Buffer Storage**

Each frame arriving at the NIC is copied into a kernel-level **ring buffer**:

$$
B_{\text{ring}} = \{ F_1, F_2, F_3, \ldots, F_n \}
$$

where each frame $F_i$ is a structured byte sequence following the Ethernet II format:

$$
F = \underbrace{\text{DestMAC}}_{6B} + \underbrace{\text{SrcMAC}}_{6B} + \underbrace{\text{EtherType}}_{2B} + \underbrace{\text{Payload}}_{46\text{-}1500B} + \underbrace{\text{FCS}}_{4B}
$$

The total frame size is:

$$
L_{\text{frame}} = 6 + 6 + 2 + L_{\text{payload}} + 4 = L_{\text{payload}} + 18 \text{ bytes}
$$

---

**STEP 4: Protocol Decoding (Layer-by-Layer)**

The sniffer peels back each protocol layer:

**Layer 2 — Ethernet:**
$$
\text{EtherType} = 0x0800 \Rightarrow \text{IPv4} \mid 0x86DD \Rightarrow \text{IPv6} \mid 0x0806 \Rightarrow \text{ARP}
$$

**Layer 3 — IPv4 Header:**
$$
\text{IP Header} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{Version} \mid \text{IHL} \\ \text{TOS} \mid \text{Total Length} \\ \text{Identification} \mid \text{Flags} \mid \text{Fragment Offset} \\ \text{TTL} \mid \text{Protocol} \mid \text{Header Checksum} \\ \text{Source IP Address} \\ \text{Destination IP Address} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Layer 4 — TCP Header (excerpt):**
$$
\text{TCP Header} \supset \{ \text{SrcPort}, \text{DstPort}, \text{SeqNum}, \text{AckNum}, \text{Flags} \}
$$

**Layer 7 — Application Payload:**
$$
\text{Payload} = \text{Decoded Application Data (HTTP, FTP, DNS, etc.)}
$$

---

**STEP 5: Filtering and Pattern Matching**

The sniffer applies display filters. The Wireshark filter language uses **Boolean expressions**:

$$
F_{\text{filter}} = \text{protocol} \cdot \text{field} \cdot \text{operator} \cdot \text{value}
$$

For example, to capture all HTTP POST requests (often containing credentials):

$$
F_{\text{filter}} = \text{http.request.method} == \text{"POST"}
$$

To detect cleartext passwords in Telnet sessions:

$$
F_{\text{filter}} = \text{telnet and } \text{data contains "password"}
$$

---

**STEP 6: Data Extraction and Exfiltration**

Captured data is exported in standard formats:

$$
\text{Export Formats} = \{\text{PCAP}, \text{PCAPNG}, \text{CSV}, \text{JSON}, \text{Plain Text}\}
$$

The total size of a capture session is estimated as:

$$
S_{\text{capture}} = N_{\text{frames}} \times \overline{L_{\text{frames}}}
$$

where $\overline{L_{\text{frames}}}$ is the average frame length in bytes.

---

**STEP 7: Counter-Detection Awareness**

A sophisticated sniffer must account for detection. The probability of detection by an IDS is given by:

$$
P_{\text{detect}} = 1 - (1 - p_{\text{signature}})^N
$$

where $p_{\text{signature}}$ is the per-packet detection probability and $N$ is the number of packets injected (for active sniffing).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Comparison Points

| **Sniffing Type** | **Trigger Mechanism** | **Target Network** | **Countermeasure** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Passive Hub Sniffing | Set NIC promiscuous | Hubs, Wi-Fi (open) | Encryption (WPA2/3) |
| ARP Poisoning | Send fake ARP replies | Switched LANs | Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) |
| MAC Flooding | Overflow CAM table | Switched LANs | Port security, 802.1X |
| DNS Spoofing | Inject false DNS responses | Any network using DNS | DNSSEC, DNS over HTTPS |
| DHCP Starvation | Exhaust IP pool | Switched LANs | DHCP snooping |
| Port Mirroring Abuse | Misconfigured SPAN | Enterprise switches | Proper SPAN configuration |
| MITM | Combined techniques | Any network | Certificate pinning, HSTS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matrix matters:** KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask comparison-style questions in the 14-mark section. Memorizing this matrix gives you 7 marks of high-quality content in under 60 seconds of writing time.

### 3.3 Engineering Trade-off Analysis

There is a fundamental trade-off between **stealth** and **coverage** in sniffing:

$$
\text{Stealth} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Coverage}}
$$

This means:
- **Passive sniffing** on a hub: high coverage, high stealth, but limited to hub-based legacy networks.
- **Active sniffing** via ARP poisoning: high coverage on modern switches, but easily detected by modern IDS.
- **Remote sniffing** via port tapping: high stealth, high coverage, but requires physical access.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Network Sniffing Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Medium Ethernet or Wi-Fi] --> B[Attacker NIC in Promiscuous Mode]
    B --> C[Kernel Ring Buffer via libpcap]
    C --> D[Frame Parser Layer 2]
    D --> E[Network Parser Layer 3]
    E --> F[Transport Parser Layer 4]
    F --> G[Application Parser Layer 7]
    G --> H[Display Filter Engine]
    H --> I[Pattern Matcher credentials tokens]
    I --> J[Export Module PCAP CSV JSON]
    J --> K[Analyst Workstation]
    L[Intrusion Detection System] -.monitors.-> B
    M[Switch CAM Table] -.vulnerable to MAC Flood.-> A
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- **Block A** represents the physical network medium where all traffic flows.
- **Block B** is the attacker's NIC, which must be placed in promiscuous mode to capture all frames.
- **Blocks C through G** form the protocol decoding pipeline, peeling back layers of the OSI stack.
- **Block H** applies user-defined display filters (e.g., `http`, `ftp`, `telnet`).
- **Block I** performs pattern matching to extract sensitive data such as credentials and session tokens.
- **Block J** exports the filtered data in standard forensic formats.
- **Block L (dashed line)** represents the IDS, which monitors for signs of sniffing (e.g., unusual ARP traffic).
- **Block M (dashed line)** indicates the switch's CAM table, which is vulnerable to MAC flooding attacks.

### 4.2 Active Sniffing Attack Flow — ARP Poisoning Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Victim Host
    participant N as Network Switch
    participant A as Attacker Sniffer
    participant S as Server Gateway
    V->>N: ARP Request Who has 192.168.1.1
    A->>V: Fake ARP Reply 192.168.1.1 is at AttackerMAC
    A->>S: Fake ARP Reply 192.168.1.100 is at AttackerMAC
    V->>A: Traffic to Gateway now flows via Attacker
    S->>A: Gateway to Victim traffic also routed to Attacker
    A->>A: Capture and forward packets MITM
    A->>S: Forward to real destination
    A->>V: Forward back to victim transparently
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

This sequence diagram illustrates the **classic ARP poisoning MITM attack**:
1. The victim broadcasts an ARP request asking for the gateway's MAC.
2. The attacker (not the real gateway) sends a forged ARP reply, claiming the gateway's IP is at the attacker's MAC.
3. The attacker performs the same deception to the real gateway, claiming the victim's IP is at the attacker's MAC.
4. All traffic from the victim intended for the internet is now redirected to the attacker.
5. The attacker captures the packets, then forwards them transparently to maintain the illusion — a true **Man-in-the-Middle** scenario.

### 4.3 Sniffing Defense-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Layer1 [Layer 1 Physical Security]
        A1[Locked Server Rooms]
        A2[Port Security on Switches]
    end
    subgraph Layer2 [Layer 2 Network Segmentation]
        B1[VLAN Separation]
        B2[802.1X Authentication]
        B3[Dynamic ARP Inspection]
    end
    subgraph Layer3 [Layer 3 Encryption]
        C1[HTTPS TLS Everywhere]
        C2[SSH instead of Telnet]
        C3[VPN for Remote Access]
        C4[IPSec for Site to Site]
    end
    subgraph Layer4 [Layer 4 Monitoring]
        D1[IDS Snort Suricata]
        D2[Network Behavior Analysis]
        D3[Regular PCAP Audits]
    end
    Layer1 --> Layer2 --> Layer3 --> Layer4
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

This block diagram shows the **defense-in-depth model** for mitigating network sniffing. Each layer adds a barrier:
- **Layer 1** prevents physical access to network ports.
- **Layer 2** segments the network to limit broadcast domains.
- **Layer 3** encrypts traffic so even if captured, it is unreadable.
- **Layer 4** detects and responds to active sniffing attempts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define network sniffing. Differentiate between passive and active sniffing with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Network sniffing is the process of monitoring, capturing, and analyzing data packets flowing across a network using specialized tools called packet sniffers (e.g., Wireshark, tcpdump) to inspect protocol-level information.

**Passive Sniffing (1 mark):** A non-intrusive technique where the attacker silently captures traffic by placing the NIC in promiscuous mode. It does not inject any packets into the network. **Example:** Capturing traffic on an Ethernet hub or open Wi-Fi network using Wireshark.

**Active Sniffing (1 mark):** An intrusive technique where the attacker manipulates network traffic by injecting crafted packets to redirect flows through their machine. **Example:** ARP poisoning, where fake ARP replies are sent to redirect a victim's traffic to the attacker's machine.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is promiscuous mode? Why is it required for network sniffing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Promiscuous mode is a NIC operating mode in which the network interface card accepts **all** frames passing through the network medium, regardless of the destination MAC address. By default, a NIC discards frames not addressed to its own MAC address.

**Requirement (1.5 marks):** Promiscuous mode is required because standard NICs perform hardware-level filtering and drop any frame whose destination MAC does not match their own. To sniff traffic intended for other hosts on the same network segment (especially on hubs and wireless networks), the attacker must disable this filtering so that all frames — including unicast frames to other machines — are delivered to the sniffer software for capture and analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **OSI layer architecture** relevant to network sniffing. Describe how a packet sniffer decodes traffic layer by layer using the Wireshark tool. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **ARP poisoning** in detail. Explain the attack mechanism, the consequences, and the countermeasures using Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question A (a):**

**OSI Layer Architecture for Sniffing (3 marks):**

Network sniffing operates primarily at **Layer 2 (Data Link Layer)** of the OSI model, with decoding extending to all upper layers:

- **Layer 1 (Physical):** Defines the electrical/optical signals and connectors (Ethernet cables, radio waves). Sniffing requires physical or wireless access to this medium.
- **Layer 2 (Data Link):** Frames are captured here. The sniffer reads MAC addresses (source and destination) and the EtherType field to determine the next protocol.
- **Layer 3 (Network):** IP headers are parsed. Source IP, destination IP, TTL, and protocol number are extracted.
- **Layer 4 (Transport):** TCP/UDP headers reveal port numbers (e.g., 80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS), sequence numbers, and flags (SYN, ACK, FIN).
- **Layer 7 (Application):** The actual payload is interpreted as HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP, or other application protocols.

**Wireshark Layer-by-Layer Decoding (4 marks):**

When a packet is captured in Wireshark, the interface displays three hierarchical panes:

**1. Frame Pane (top):** Shows the raw frame with summary — frame number, timestamp, source/destination, protocol, length, and info.

**2. Protocol Detail Pane (middle):** Expandable tree showing each protocol layer:
$$
\text{Frame} \rightarrow \text{Ethernet II} \rightarrow \text{Internet Protocol} \rightarrow \text{Transmission Control Protocol} \rightarrow \text{Hypertext Transfer Protocol}
$$

**3. Raw Bytes Pane (bottom):** Hexadecimal representation of the actual frame bytes. Selecting a field in the middle pane highlights the corresponding bytes in the bottom pane.

For example, an HTTP request packet is decoded as:
- **Ethernet II:** DestMAC, SrcMAC, EtherType = 0x0800
- **IPv4:** SrcIP = 192.168.1.100, DstIP = 93.184.216.34, TTL = 64, Protocol = 6 (TCP)
- **TCP:** SrcPort = 54321, DstPort = 80, Seq = 1, Flags = PSH/ACK
- **HTTP:** `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: example.com\r\n`

This layer-by-layer breakdown allows analysts and attackers to reconstruct entire sessions, identify cleartext credentials, and diagnose network issues.

---

**Model Answer — Question A (b):**

**ARP Poisoning — Attack Mechanism (3.5 marks):**

**Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** maps Layer 3 IP addresses to Layer 2 MAC addresses within a local network. ARP operates on a **trust-on-first-use** basis — hosts cache ARP replies without authentication.

**Attack Steps:**

1. **Reconnaissance:** The attacker scans the LAN using tools like `arp -a` or Nmap to identify the IP and MAC addresses of the victim and the gateway.
2. **Crafted ARP Reply:** The attacker sends a **gratuitous ARP reply** (unsolicited) to the victim:
   - `Sender IP: 192.168.1.1` (gateway's IP)
   - `Sender MAC: AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF` (attacker's MAC)
3. **Cache Poisoning:** The victim's ARP cache is updated. It now believes the gateway's IP is at the attacker's MAC.
4. **Traffic Redirection:** All packets from the victim to the internet are sent to the attacker's NIC.
5. **Bidirectional Poisoning:** The attacker simultaneously poisons the gateway's ARP cache to claim the victim's IP, achieving full **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)**.
6. **Capture and Forward:** The attacker uses a tool like Ettercap to log all traffic while transparently forwarding it to the real destination.

**Consequences (1.5 marks):**

- **Credential Theft:** Captures cleartext HTTP, FTP, Telnet, SMTP, POP3 logins.
- **Session Hijacking:** Steals session cookies to impersonate the user.
- **Data Manipulation:** Attacker can modify packets in transit (e.g., inject malware into downloads).
- **Network Disruption:** ARP storms can degrade network performance.

**Countermeasures — Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) (2 marks):**

DAI is a Layer 2 security feature on managed switches (Cisco, HP, Juniper):

- **Trusted Ports:** Connect to DHCP servers; ARP packets are forwarded without inspection.
- **Untrusted Ports:** Connect to end hosts; all ARP packets are validated.

**Validation Logic:**
$$
\text{ARP}_{\text{valid}} = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } (\text{IP} \rightarrow \text{MAC}) \in \text{DHCP Snooping Binding Table} \\ \text{False} & \text{if mismatch detected} \end{cases}
$$

Invalid ARP packets are dropped, and the port can be err-disabled. Combined with **DHCP Snooping**, **Port Security**, and **802.1X**, DAI provides robust protection against ARP-based sniffing attacks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain **MAC flooding** as an active sniffing technique. Describe how an attacker exploits the CAM table limitation and what countermeasures switch administrators can implement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **passive sniffing** and **active sniffing** across at least **six parameters**. Justify why **encryption** is the ultimate defense against sniffing, with examples of encrypted protocols. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question B (a):**

**MAC Flooding — Concept (3 marks):**

A network switch maintains a **CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table** that maps MAC addresses to physical switch ports. The CAM table has a **finite size** (e.g., 4,096 to 32,768 entries depending on the switch model).

**Attack Mechanism:**

1. The attacker uses a tool like **macof** (part of the Dsniff suite) to generate thousands of **bogus ARP replies** with random source MAC addresses.
2. These packets flood the switch from a single port:
$$
N_{\text{entries}} = \{ \text{MAC}_1, \text{MAC}_2, \text{MAC}_3, \ldots, \text{MAC}_n \}
$$
3. The CAM table fills to capacity. When a new MAC address arrives, the oldest entry is evicted (FIFO replacement).
4. The switch **enters fail-open mode** — since it cannot learn the destination MAC, it **floods the frame out of all ports**, behaving like a hub.
5. The attacker's NIC, now in promiscuous mode, receives **all traffic** on the network, including unicast traffic intended for other hosts.

**Detection and Impact (1.5 marks):**

- The switch's CPU utilization spikes as it processes the flood of MAC learning frames.
- Network performance degrades due to flooding.
- The attack is **noisy** and easily detected by monitoring CAM table size or port-level traffic.

**Countermeasures (2.5 marks):**

1. **Port Security:** Limit the number of MAC addresses allowed per port:
$$
\text{MAC}_{\text{limit}} = 1 \text{ (typical for user ports)}
$$
Violations can trigger shutdown, restrict, or protect modes.

2. **802.1X Authentication:** Require devices to authenticate before being granted network access. Unauthenticated MAC addresses are denied.

3. **DHCP Snooping:** Prevents rogue DHCP servers; indirectly limits the attack surface.

4. **CAM Table Flood Detection:** Modern switches alert when CAM table utilization exceeds thresholds (e.g., 80%).

5. **Network Segmentation (VLANs):** Limit broadcast domains so flooding affects fewer hosts.

6. **Managed Switches with ACLs:** Block MAC-flooding patterns at the switch level.

---

**Model Answer — Question B (b):**

**Comparison Table — Passive vs. Active Sniffing (3.5 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Passive Sniffing** | **Active Sniffing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Traffic Manipulation** | None — silently observes | Injects crafted packets |
| **Detection Risk** | Very low, near-invisible | High, IDS/IPS can detect |
| **Skill Required** | Low — run sniffer on hub/Wi-Fi | Medium — requires protocol knowledge |
| **Network Effect** | No impact on network | Can disrupt network (storms) |
| **Works on Hubs** | Yes, very effective | Yes, but unnecessary |
| **Works on Switches** | Limited (only broadcast) | Yes (via ARP poisoning, MAC flood) |
| **Tools** | Wireshark, tcpdump, Kismet | Ettercap, BetterCAP, Cain & Abel |
| **Countermeasure** | Encryption | DAI, Port Security, 802.1X |

**Why Encryption is the Ultimate Defense (3.5 marks):**

Even if an attacker successfully captures packets, **encryption renders the payload unreadable** without the decryption key. This shifts the security boundary from the network layer to the cryptographic layer.

**Examples of Encrypted Protocols vs. Their Cleartext Counterparts:**

| **Cleartext (Vulnerable)** | **Encrypted (Secure)** | **Encryption Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP (port 80) | HTTPS (port 443) | TLS 1.3 |
| Telnet (port 23) | SSH (port 22) | RSA / ECDH key exchange |
| FTP (port 21) | SFTP / FTPS (port 22 / 990) | SSH / TLS |
| SMTP (port 25) | SMTPS (port 465) | TLS |
| POP3 (port 110) | POP3S (port 995) | TLS |
| SNMPv1/v2 (port 161) | SNMPv3 | AES + authentication |
| DNS (port 53) | DoH (DNS over HTTPS) | HTTPS tunneling |

**Engineering Justification:**

- **Confidentiality:** AES-256 encryption ensures brute-force attacks are computationally infeasible.
- **Integrity:** HMAC and digital signatures detect tampering.
- **Authentication:** Certificates (X.509) prevent MITM impersonation.
- **Forward Secrecy:** Ephemeral keys (ECDHE) protect past sessions even if long-term keys are compromised.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "switch" and "hub" terminology.** A hub broadcasts everything; a switch segments. Many students incorrectly say "sniffing works on a switch in promiscuous mode" without explaining ARP poisoning. Always clarify the difference.
> 2. **Always mention the OSI layer.** If you write about sniffing without referencing Layer 2, expect to lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Distinguish ARP (Layer 2) from DNS (Layer 7).** ARP poisoning is not the same as DNS poisoning. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Do NOT claim Wireshark is an "attack tool."** In KTU's ethical hacking context, describe it as a **defensive analysis tool** that can be misused for attacks.
> 5. **Forgetting countermeasures.** A 14-mark question on sniffing must include both attack and defense. Listing only attacks will cap you at ~9 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Network Sniffing (Module 2):**

- **Definition:** Sniffing = capture + decode network packets using NIC in promiscuous mode.
- **OSI Layer:** Operates at **Layer 2 (Data Link)**, decoding up to Layer 7.
- **Key Tool Categories:** libpcap, Wireshark, tcpdump (passive) | Ettercap, BetterCAP, Cain (active).
- **Promiscuous Mode:** Disables MAC filtering; captures all frames on the medium.
- **Passive Sniffing:** Hub-based or open Wi-Fi; silent; undetectable.
- **Active Sniffing:** ARP poisoning, MAC flooding, DNS spoofing, DHCP starvation.
- **ARP Poisoning:** Forged ARP replies redirect traffic to attacker; counter = Dynamic ARP Inspection.
- **MAC Flooding:** CAM table overflow → switch behaves like hub; counter = Port Security + 802.1X.
- **Hub vs. Switch:** Hub = single collision domain, all traffic broadcast. Switch = separate collision domains, MAC learning.
- **Best Countermeasure:** **End-to-end encryption** (TLS, SSH, VPN, IPsec) — even if captured, payload is unreadable.
- **Key Ports:** HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, SSH=22, Telnet=23, FTP=21, DNS=53.
- **MTU:** Standard Ethernet frame payload = **1500 bytes**.
- **Detection Methods:** ARP watch, CAM table monitoring, promiscuous mode detection (e.g., sending frames to non-existent MACs).
- **KTU 2024 Focus Areas:** Comparison tables, attack-countermeasure pairs, Wireshark layer decoding, encryption-based defenses.
- **Real-world Tools to Know:** Wireshark, tcpdump, Ettercap, BetterCAP, Cain & Abel, Kismet (wireless), NetworkMiner (forensics).
- **Ethics Reminder:** Unauthorized sniffing is illegal under **IT Act 2000 (India)** Section 66 — and equivalent international laws (CFAA, Computer Misuse Act UK).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Sniffing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Sniffing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sniffing** is the process of **monitoring, intercepting, and capturing data packets** as they traverse a computer network using specialized software or hardware tools known as **packet analyzers** or **network sniffers** (e.g., Wireshark, Tcpdump, Ettercap).

In the context of **Ethical Hacking & IoT Security**, sniffing is classified as a **Network Reconnaissance / Information Gathering** attack technique. It is formally categorized under the **Pre-Attack Phase** of the Cyber Kill Chain, where the attacker passively or actively observes traffic to extract sensitive information such as credentials, session tokens, email content, and IoT device telemetry.

The **"Types of Sniffing"** specifically refers to the **methodological classification** of sniffing attacks based on:
- The **network infrastructure** (Hub vs. Switch)
- The **engagement level** of the attacker with the network
- The **protocol layer** being targeted (Layer 2, Layer 3, etc.)

### 1.2 Primary Classification: The Two Pillars

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus and the standard CEH (Certified Ethical Hacker) framework, sniffing is broadly classified into **two primary types**:

| Type | Engagement | Network Type | Detectability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive Sniffing** | Silent observation | Hub / Non-switched | Very Difficult |
| **Active Sniffing** | Active manipulation | Switched | Easier to detect |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Mailroom Spying Scenario"**
>
> Imagine a large office building where everyone sends letters to each other through a **central mailroom**.
>
> - **Passive Sniffing** is like a mailroom worker who **quietly opens every letter and reads it without touching or modifying anything**. The senders never know their mail was read. This works when the building uses a **"copy-everyone" system** (a hub), where every letter is automatically duplicated to all desks. The attacker just sits and reads.
>
> - **Active Sniffing** is like a mailroom worker who **actively tricks the postal system** by forging a "change of address" form (ARP poisoning), redirecting another person's mail through their own desk. Now they can read, copy, and even alter the letters before forwarding them. This is required in modern buildings that use a **"smart routing" system** (a switch), where letters only go to the intended recipient.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Ethical Hacking

- **Penetration Testing** begins with sniffing to map live hosts, protocols, and credentials.
- **IoT Security** relies heavily on sniffing to detect rogue devices, rogue gateways, and unencrypted telemetry leaks.
- **Defense Design** requires understanding sniffing types to deploy the correct countermeasures (port security, ARP inspection, encryption).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric Highlight**: A modern switched Ethernet network has an estimated **~95%** of enterprise traffic routed through switches. This makes **passive sniffing alone insufficient**, forcing attackers to use **active sniffing techniques** — a critical exam distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Sniffing Types

The complete taxonomy of sniffing types in the KTU syllabus can be broken down hierarchically:

#### **Type 1: Passive Sniffing**

- **Operational Mode**: Read-only, silent observation.
- **Network Dependency**: Works only on **non-switched** networks (Hubs, Wireless mediums).
- **How it works**:
  - In a **Hub-based network**, every packet sent by any device is broadcast to **every port** on the hub.
  - The sniffer places the Network Interface Card (NIC) into **Promiscuous Mode**, instructing it to accept ALL frames, not just those addressed to it.
  - The attacker captures and analyzes the traffic silently.
- **Why it works**: The IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame does not prevent a NIC from accepting frames destined for other MAC addresses when in promiscuous mode.
- **Real-world scenarios**:
  - Public Wi-Fi hotspots (radio waves are inherently broadcast).
  - Legacy hub-based office LANs.
  - Network TAPs (Test Access Points) used legitimately in monitoring.
- **Detection difficulty**: **Extremely difficult** because no traffic is generated or modified by the attacker.

#### **Type 2: Active Sniffing**

- **Operational Mode**: Active injection and manipulation of network traffic.
- **Network Dependency**: Required for **switched** networks (which dominate modern IT/IoT).
- **How it works**: The attacker injects crafted packets to **redirect traffic** through their machine, then captures it.
- **Sub-categories of Active Sniffing**:

| Active Sniffing Sub-Type | Target Protocol | Attack Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **ARP Poisoning / ARP Spoofing** | ARP (Layer 2) | Sends fake ARP replies to map attacker's MAC to victim's IP |
| **MAC Flooding** | CAM Table (Layer 2) | Overloads switch's CAM table forcing it into hub mode |
| **DHCP Spoofing / Starvation** | DHCP (Layer 7/Application) | Rogue DHCP server assigns malicious gateway/DNS |
| **DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning** | DNS (Layer 7) | Injects false DNS records to redirect traffic |
| **STP (Spanning Tree) Manipulation** | STP (Layer 2) | Becomes root bridge to intercept VLAN traffic |
| **Port Stealing / Mirroring** | Switch internals | Fills CAM table with attacker's MAC on victim's port |

#### **Type 3: Protocol-Based Sniffing (Cross-Layer Classification)**

This is an **alternative classification** based on which protocol layer the sniffer targets:

- **Layer 2 (Data Link) Sniffing**: Captures Ethernet frames, MAC addresses, ARP requests.
- **Layer 3 (Network) Sniffing**: Captures IP packets, ICMP, routing protocols.
- **Layer 4 (Transport) Sniffing**: Captures TCP/UDP segments, port numbers, flags (SYN, ACK, FIN).
- **Layer 7 (Application) Sniffing**: Captures HTTP, FTP, Telnet, SMTP, MQTT, CoAP (critical for IoT).

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT-Specific Sniffing Note**: In IoT environments, sniffing is particularly dangerous because protocols like **MQTT (port 1883)** and **CoAP (port 5683)** often transmit **without TLS encryption**, exposing device telemetry, firmware versions, and control commands in plaintext.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **formulas, constants, and technical metrics** frequently tested in KTU examinations for this topic.

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Promiscuous Mode Filter Bypass | $\text{Frame Accept} = \forall \text{frames, regardless of } \text{DstMAC}$ | NIC accepts all frames | Logical flag |
| ARP Cache Poisoning Equation | $\text{IP}_{\text{victim}} \rightarrow \text{MAC}_{\text{attacker}}$ in victim's ARP cache | Replaces real MAC mapping | MAC/ARP entry |
| MAC Flooding Threshold | $\text{CAM}_{\text{overflow}} \approx 4{,}000 \text{ to } 65{,}000$ entries | Switch memory exhaustion limit | Entries |
| Promiscuous Mode Detection (Packet Ratio) | $R_p = \dfrac{N_{\text{received}}}{N_{\text{destined}}}$ | If $R_p \gg 1$, NIC is in promiscuous mode | Ratio (unitless) |
| Bandwidth Consideration (Wireshark Capture) | $C_{\text{cap}} \leq B_{\text{link}} \times 0.8$ | Practical capture limit | Mbps / Gbps |
| Default MQTT Port | $\text{TCP/1883}$ | Unencrypted IoT messaging | Port number |
| Default CoAP Port | $\text{UDP/5683}$ | Constrained IoT protocol | Port number |
| Default HTTPS Port | $\text{TCP/443}$ | Encrypted (sniffing yields encrypted data) | Port number |
| ARP Aging Timer | $T_{\text{ARP}} \approx 15$ to $60$ seconds | Time before cache refresh needed | Seconds |
| Wireless Sniffing Range (typical) | $r \approx 100$ m (indoor) / $\approx 300$ m (outdoor) | Wi-Fi sniffing radius | Meters |

> **Usage Note**: For any formula, never use the vertical pipe symbol `\|` inside the table. Use `\vert` for absolute value if needed. Here, ratio $R_p$ uses plain notation to remain table-safe.

### 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering

- **Promiscuous Mode** is the foundational concept — without it, no sniffing is possible.
- **ARP Cache Equation** is the heart of active sniffing — every active attack rewrites an ARP cache.
- **MAC Flooding Threshold** explains *why* modern switches use TCAM (Ternary CAM) with **ASIC-based** lookup for performance.
- **Promiscuous Mode Detection Ratio** $R_p$ is used by IDS systems (like Snort) to identify rogue sniffers.
- **Port Numbers** (1883, 5683) are **high-yield KTU facts** for IoT security questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Promiscuous Mode Detection Ratio ($R_p$)

This derivation explains how a Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) mathematically detects whether a host is in **promiscuous mode** by analyzing traffic patterns.

**Step 1: Define the baseline traffic count for a normal NIC**

In a normal (non-promiscuous) NIC, the number of frames received and processed is approximately equal to the number of frames actually addressed to the host.

$$N_{\text{destined}} = \sum_{i=1}^{T} \mathbb{1}\left[\text{DstMAC}_i = \text{HostMAC}\right]$$

Where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function that equals 1 if the condition is true, 0 otherwise, and $T$ is the observation time window.

**Step 2: Define the captured frame count for a promiscuous NIC**

A NIC in promiscuous mode captures **every** frame that physically arrives at the interface, regardless of the destination MAC.

$$N_{\text{received}} = \sum_{i=1}^{T} \mathbb{1}\left[\text{Frame}_i \text{ arrived at NIC}\right]$$

**Step 3: Formulate the ratio**

$$R_p = \frac{N_{\text{received}}}{N_{\text{destined}}}$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result with boundary conditions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &\approx 1 \quad &&\text{(Normal NIC — receives only destined frames)} \\
R_p &\gg 1 \quad &&\text{(Promiscuous Mode — receives all frames)} \\
R_p &= 1 \quad &&\text{(Even in promiscuous, if attacker filters out non-relevant traffic)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Practical consideration**

In a fully switched network with unicast traffic, $N_{\text{destined}} \to 0$ for the attacker, making $R_p$ approach infinity — which is why **active sniffing is needed on switches**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — ARP Cache Poisoning Logic

**Step 1: Normal ARP Cache State**

Initially, the victim's ARP cache contains the correct mapping:

$$\text{Victim ARP Cache: } \text{IP}_{\text{Gateway}} \rightarrow \text{MAC}_{\text{Gateway}}$$

**Step 2: Attacker injects a gratuitous (unsolicited) ARP reply**

The attacker sends a crafted ARP reply containing:

$$\text{Reply Payload: } \text{IP}_{\text{Gateway}} \rightarrow \text{MAC}_{\text{Attacker}}$$

**Step 3: Victim's OS updates its cache (statelessly, in many implementations)**

$$\text{Victim ARP Cache: } \text{IP}_{\text{Gateway}} \rightarrow \text{MAC}_{\text{Attacker}}$$

**Step 4: Traffic redirection occurs**

All packets the victim sends to the gateway are now sent to the attacker's MAC, which the switch delivers to the attacker's physical port.

**Step 5: Attacker forwards traffic to maintain invisibility (Man-in-the-Middle)**

The attacker enables **IP forwarding** and re-sends the packets to the real gateway, so the victim does not notice the loss of connectivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why gratuitous ARP works**: Many operating systems (especially older Windows versions) accept ARP replies **without verification**, making this attack trivial. This is why **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** is a critical defense.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Passive vs. Active Sniffing)

| Analytical Parameter | Passive Sniffing | Active Sniffing |
|---|---|---|
| **Attacker Action** | None (silence) | Inject packets |
| **Network Type** | Hub / Wireless | Switched |
| **NIC State** | Promiscuous Mode | Promiscuous + Traffic Injection |
| **Detection Risk** | Very Low | Moderate to High |
| **Tools Used** | Wireshark, Tcpdump | Ettercap, BetterCAP, Cain & Abel |
| **Performance Impact on Network** | None | Noticeable (extra packets) |
| **Countermeasure Difficulty** | High (radio-based) | Medium (DAI, port security) |
| **Modern Relevance** | Declining (switches dominate) | Increasing |
| **Exam Weightage (KTU)** | 2–3 marks definition | 7–10 marks sub-types |

### 3.4 Decision Logic for Sniffing Type Selection

The following algorithm (presented as logic flow, NOT code) guides an attacker in selecting the right sniffing type:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify Network Type} &\rightarrow \text{Hub or Switch?} \\
\text{If Hub} &\rightarrow \text{Use Passive Sniffing (set NIC to promiscuous)} \\
\text{If Switch} &\rightarrow \text{Proceed to Step 2} \\
\text{Step 2: Choose Active Sub-Technique} &\rightarrow \text{Based on switch capability} \\
\text{If CAM table size small} &\rightarrow \text{MAC Flooding} \\
\text{If DAI disabled} &\rightarrow \text{ARP Poisoning} \\
\text{If DHCP snooping absent} &\rightarrow \text{DHCP Spoofing} \\
\text{If VLAN trunking present} &\rightarrow \text{STP Manipulation} \\
\text{Step 3: Begin Capture} &\rightarrow \text{Save to .pcap file for offline analysis}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Complete Taxonomy of Sniffing Types

The following Mermaid block renders a hierarchical tree showing the full classification of sniffing types as required by the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["SNIFFING TYPES"] --> B1["PASSIVE SNIFFING"]
    A1 --> B2["ACTIVE SNIFFING"]

    B1 --> C1["Hub Based Capture"]
    B1 --> C2["Wireless Capture"]
    B1 --> C3["Promiscuous Mode Only"]

    B2 --> D1["ARP Poisoning"]
    B2 --> D2["MAC Flooding"]
    B2 --> D3["DHCP Spoofing"]
    B2 --> D4["DNS Spoofing"]
    B2 --> D5["STP Manipulation"]

    B3 --> E1["Layer 2 Ethernet Sniffing"]
    B3 --> E2["Layer 3 IP Sniffing"]
    B3 --> E3["Layer 4 TCP UDP Sniffing"]
    B3 --> E4["Layer 7 Application Sniffing"]
    B3 --> E5["IoT Protocol MQTT CoAP Sniffing"]

    D1 --> F1["MITM Attack Enabler"]
    D2 --> F2["Switch to Hub Downgrade"]

    D5 --> F5["VLAN Traffic Hijack"]
```

This block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end operational sequence** of both sniffing types, highlighting the key divergence point (network type).

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Attacker Machine"] --> P2["Set NIC to Promiscuous Mode"]
    P2 --> P3{"Network Type?"}

    P3 -->|"Hub or Wireless"| P4["PASSIVE PATH"]
    P3 -->|"Switched"| P5["ACTIVE PATH"]

    P4 --> P6["Silently Capture All Broadcast Frames"]

    P7 --> P8["Extract Credentials or Data"]
    P8 --> P9["No Detection Risk"]

    P5 --> P6A["Identify Switch Weakness"]

    P6B --> P6C["Redirect Victim Traffic to Attacker"]
    P6C --> P6D["Enable IP Forwarding for MITM"]
    P6D --> P6E["Capture and Analyze Packets"]
    P6E --> P6F["Moderate Detection Risk via IDS"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: ARP Poisoning Process (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Since ARP poisoning is the most tested active sniffing technique, this block diagram maps its **functional architecture** showing all participating components and message flows.

```mermaid
graph TD
    VICTIM["VICTIM HOST IP 192.168.1.10"] -->|"ARP Request Who has Gateway"| NET["LOCAL NETWORK BROADCAST"]
    NET --> ATTACKER["ATTACKER HOST 192.168.1.50"]
    NET --> GATEWAY["REAL GATEWAY 192.168.1.1"]
    GATEWAY -->|"Legitimate ARP Reply"| VICTIM
    ATTACKER -->|"FAKE ARP REPLY Gateway is at ATTACKER MAC"| VICTIM
    VICTIM -->|"UPDATES CACHE Poisoned"| POISONED["POISONED CACHE Gateway MAC equals ATTACKER MAC"]
    POISONED -->|"All Traffic to Gateway"| ATTACKER
    ATTACKER -->|"Re forwards via IP Forwarding"| GATEWAY
    GATEWAY -->|"Reply to Victim"| VICTIM
    ATTACKER -->|"Captures all Data"| CAPTURED["PCAP FILE WITH CREDENTIALS"]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4: IoT Sniffing Attack Surface (KTU-Critical)

This diagram maps the IoT-specific sniffing landscape, which is a key focus of this course.

```mermaid
graph TD
    IOT["IoT DEVICE SENSOR OR ACTUATOR"] -->|"Plaintext MQTT TCP 1883"| BROKER["MQTT BROKER"]
    IOT -->|"Plaintext CoAP UDP 5683"| COAPSRV["CoAP SERVER"]
    IOT -->|"Plaintext HTTP TCP 80"| CLOUD["CLOUD API"]
    BROKER --> SNIFFER["SNIFFER ATTACKER"]
    COAPSRV --> SNIFFER
    CLOUD --> SNIFFER
    SNIFFER --> EXTRACT["EXTRACT Device ID Telemetry Control Commands Credentials"]
```
