
> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams**: In the KTU board exam, drawing any of these diagrams with **labels for protocols and ports** will fetch **2 bonus marks** under the "diagrammatic representation" evaluation criteria. Always label the arrows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Define sniffing. Differentiate between passive and active sniffing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Sniffing** is the process of capturing and analyzing network packets traversing a network using tools like Wireshark or Tcpdump, in order to extract sensitive information such as usernames, passwords, and session tokens.

> **Passive Sniffing** involves silently monitoring traffic on a non-switched network (hub or wireless medium) by placing the NIC in **promiscuous mode**. It does not inject any traffic and is very difficult to detect.

> **Active Sniffing** is required on switched networks where the attacker actively injects packets (e.g., ARP replies) to redirect traffic through their machine. Techniques include **ARP poisoning, MAC flooding, and DHCP spoofing**. It is detectable by IDS systems.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for passive, 1 mark for active)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is promiscuous mode? Why is it essential for sniffing?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Promiscuous mode** is a NIC configuration in which the network interface accepts **all incoming frames** regardless of the destination MAC address, rather than filtering out frames not addressed to it.

> It is essential for sniffing because, under normal operation, a NIC discards frames where `DstMAC ≠ HostMAC`. To capture other users' traffic, the sniffer must override this filter and process every frame. Without promiscuous mode, sniffing on a hub would still capture broadcast frames, but sniffing on a switch or capturing unicast traffic destined for other hosts would be impossible.

> Modern NIDS tools (like Snort) detect rogue promiscuous mode by observing the ratio $R_p = N_{\text{received}} / N_{\text{destined}} \gg 1$.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark definition, 1 mark necessity, 1 mark detection note)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the different types of active sniffing attacks. List the protocols they target and the tools used to perform them. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Active Sniffing** is the class of sniffing techniques used on switched networks, where the attacker must actively inject traffic to redirect the victim's data flow through their machine. The major sub-types are:

**1. ARP Poisoning / ARP Spoofing (2 Marks)**
- **Target Protocol**: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol, Layer 2)
- **Mechanism**: Attacker sends gratuitous ARP replies to victim, mapping the gateway's IP to the attacker's MAC address. All traffic meant for the gateway is redirected to the attacker.
- **Enables**: Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack on the local subnet.
- **Tool**: Ettercap, BetterCAP, Cain & Abel.

**2. MAC Flooding (1.5 Marks)**
- **Target Protocol**: CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table inside the switch
- **Mechanism**: Attacker sends thousands of fake source MAC addresses to overflow the switch's CAM table, forcing it to fail open and behave like a hub (broadcasting all frames to all ports).
- **Tool**: Macof (from Dsniff suite).

**3. DHCP Spoofing / Starvation (1.5 Marks)**
- **Target Protocol**: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol)
- **Mechanism**: Attacker runs a rogue DHCP server that responds to DHCP requests faster than the real server, handing out malicious default gateway and DNS settings. DHCP starvation exhausts the IP pool.
- **Tool**: Yersinia, DHCPig.

**4. DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning (1 Mark)**
- **Target Protocol**: DNS (Domain Name System)
- **Mechanism**: Attacker injects fake DNS records into a resolver's cache, redirecting users to malicious websites.
- **Tool**: Ettercap (dns_spoof plugin), Dnsspoof.

**5. STP Manipulation (1 Mark)**
- **Target Protocol**: Spanning Tree Protocol (802.1D)
- **Mechanism**: Attacker sends superior BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit) frames to become the root bridge, intercepting all VLAN traffic.

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain how ARP poisoning works as a Man-in-the-Middle attack. What is the role of IP forwarding in this attack? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ARP Poisoning MITM Process (5 Marks):**

1. **Initial State**: Victim (192.168.1.10) has correct ARP entry — `Gateway IP (192.168.1.1) → Real MAC`.
2. **Attack Initiation**: Attacker (192.168.1.50) sends a **gratuitous ARP reply** to the victim, claiming `192.168.1.1 is at MAC_Attacker`.
3. **Cache Update**: Victim's OS, without verifying the ARP reply, overwrites its cache with the poisoned entry.
4. **Traffic Redirection**: All packets victim sends to the gateway (e.g., web requests) are now physically delivered to the attacker's NIC.
5. **IP Forwarding Role**: The attacker enables **IP forwarding** (a kernel/IP stack setting) to re-transmit the captured packets to the real gateway. This keeps the victim's network functional, preventing suspicion.
6. **Bidirectional Capture**: The same poisoning is performed against the gateway (telling it that `192.168.1.10 is at MAC_Attacker`), so return traffic also flows through the attacker.
7. **Outcome**: The attacker can now **read, log, and modify** all traffic between victim and gateway in real time.

**Diagram (2 Marks)** — refer to Section 4.3 of these notes for the complete block diagram. (The student should redraw it in the exam with proper labels for Host, Attacker, Gateway, ARP Request, Fake Reply, and IP Forwarding arrow.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Internal Choice]

**Q. (a)** What are the different types of sniffing in the context of IoT networks? Explain with examples how MQTT and CoAP protocols are vulnerable to sniffing attacks. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**IoT Sniffing Types (3 Marks):**

In IoT environments, sniffing is classified based on the **target protocol and device class**:

1. **Gateway-to-Cloud Sniffing**: Capturing traffic between the IoT gateway and cloud APIs (often HTTP/MQTT over TLS — but many use plaintext).
2. **Device-to-Gateway Sniffing**: Capturing traffic from constrained sensors to the local gateway (MQTT/CoAP over Wi-Fi/Zigbee).
3. **Wireless Air Sniffing**: Capturing over-the-air signals using SDR (Software Defined Radio) tools like HackRF, especially for Zigbee, Z-Wave, LoRa.
4. **Bluetooth/BLE Sniffing**: Capturing BLE advertisements and GATT communications (e.g., using Ubertooth).
5. **Backbone Sniffing**: Capturing on the wired backhaul connecting IoT controllers.

**MQTT Vulnerability (2 Marks):**
- MQTT by default runs on **TCP port 1883 in plaintext**.
- An attacker on the same Wi-Fi network places NIC in promiscuous mode and captures every `PUBLISH`, `SUBSCRIBE`, and `CONNECT` packet.
- **Extracted data**: Topic names (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`), client IDs, username/password (if sent in `CONNECT`), and payload values.
- **Tool**: Wireshark with `mqtt` display filter.

**CoAP Vulnerability (2 Marks):**
- CoAP runs on **UDP port 5683 in plaintext** (DTLS-secured variant uses 5684).
- Constrained devices often skip DTLS to save CPU and battery.
- An attacker captures `CON`, `NON`, `ACK` messages and can read sensor readings or replay them.
- **Tool**: Wireshark with `coap` filter.

---

**(b)** Compare and contrast passive and active sniffing. List at least 6 distinguishing parameters in a table format. Explain why passive sniffing is considered nearly impossible to detect. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | Passive Sniffing | Active Sniffing |
|---|---|---|
| Network Type | Hub / Wireless | Switched |
| Attacker Action | Read-only, no injection | Injects crafted packets |
| NIC Mode | Promiscuous | Promiscuous + Injection |
| Traffic Visibility | Broadcast only | All unicast via MITM |
| Tools | Wireshark, Tcpdump | Ettercap, BetterCAP, Cain |
| Detection Risk | Very Low | Moderate |
| Countermeasure | Encryption (WPA2, TLS) | DAI, Port Security, 802.1X |
| Data Integrity | Untouched | Attacker can modify |

**Why Passive Sniffing is Hard to Detect (3 Marks):**

1. **No Traffic Generation**: The attacker does not send any packets, so network logs, flow records, and SIEM systems see no anomaly.
2. **Broadcast Nature**: On a hub, the attacker's capture is indistinguishable from a legitimate user receiving legitimate broadcast traffic (e.g., ARP broadcasts, DHCP offers).
3. **Promiscuous Mode is Silent**: The NIC does not announce its mode change via any standard protocol.
4. **Wireless Invisibility**: On Wi-Fi, the radio medium is inherently shared — capturing frames is physically undetectable to other radios.
5. **Encryption is the Only Defense**: Without WPA2/TLS, there is no protocol-level signal that sniffing is occurring.
6. **Indirect Detection Only**: The only reliable detection is via out-of-band methods (NIDS monitoring host behavior for promiscuous mode using the $R_p$ ratio or DNS ping methods).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Definition Mistake**: Students often write "sniffing is hacking" — this fetches **0 marks**. Always state it as "monitoring/intercepting packets for information gathering."
> 2. **Passive = Hub Misconception**: Some students think passive sniffing also works on switches — **it does not** (without MAC flooding, which converts it to active).
> 3. **MQTT Port Confusion**: The default unencrypted MQTT port is **1883** and encrypted (MQTTS) is **8883**. Mixing these up loses marks.
> 4. **Diagram Labels**: Drawing a diagram without **protocol labels and arrows** loses 1–2 marks. Always annotate.
> 5. **Promiscuous Mode Definition**: Do NOT confuse with "Monitor Mode" (used in wireless). Promiscuous = wired/wireless frame acceptance; Monitor = full wireless 802.11 frame capture including management frames.
> 6. **Forgetting IP Forwarding**: In ARP poisoning answers, students often forget to mention **IP forwarding**, which is the key to keeping the victim unaware. This is a **2-mark loss**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for "Types of Sniffing"**

- **Sniffing = packet capture for information extraction** (NOT packet injection).
- **Two main types**: Passive (silent, hub-based) and Active (injection-based, switch-based).
- **Promiscuous mode** is the foundational requirement for any sniffer.
- **Passive sniffing** is undetectable by conventional means; only **encryption** prevents it.
- **Active sniffing** sub-types (must memorize all 5):
  - ARP Poisoning → rewrites IP-to-MAC mapping
  - MAC Flooding → exhausts switch CAM table
  - DHCP Spoofing → rogue DHCP server
  - DNS Spoofing → fake DNS records
  - STP Manipulation → rogue root bridge
- **Default MQTT port = TCP/1883** (plaintext), **8883** (TLS).
- **Default CoAP port = UDP/5683** (plaintext), **5684** (DTLS).
- **CAM table threshold ≈ 4,000 to 65,000** entries (depends on switch model).
- **ARP aging timer ≈ 15 to 60 seconds** (active poisoning must be refreshed).
- **Detection ratio formula**: $R_p = N_{\text{received}} / N_{\text{destined}}$; if $R_p \gg 1$, host is in promiscuous mode.
- **Modern enterprise traffic ≈ 95% goes through switches** → makes **active sniffing the dominant real-world technique**.
- **IoT-specific risk**: MQTT and CoAP are often unencrypted by default → high sniffing risk.
- **Tools to remember**: Wireshark, Tcpdump, Ettercap, BetterCAP, Cain & Abel, Macof, Yersinia.
- **Key defense**: Encryption (TLS/DTLS), Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI), DHCP Snooping, Port Security, 802.1X authentication.
- **IP forwarding is the silent enabler** of MITM attacks — it keeps the victim connected while the attacker intercepts.
- **Kill Chain placement**: Sniffing belongs to the **Reconnaissance / Weaponization** phase.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MITM Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** MITM Attacks # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **MITM (Man-in-the-Middle) Attack** is a sophisticated cyberattack in which an adversary secretly intercepts, relays, and potentially alters communication between two parties (Client ↔ Server) who falsely believe they are communicating directly with each other. The attacker positions themselves logically in the middle of the data exchange path, breaking the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability) and violating the **AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting)** security model. In the context of **KTU PBCCT604 (Ethical Hacking & IoT Security)**, MITM attacks fall under the **Network & Communication Layer Threats** classification, specifically targeting Layers 2 (Data Link), 3 (Network), and 7 (Application) of the **OSI Reference Model**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **🗣️ "The Forgotten Letter Interceptor" Analogy:** > > Imagine **Alice** and **Bob** are two friends who exchange sealed love letters through a trusted postman (the network). Now, an **evil postman named Eve** intercepts the mailbox, opens every letter (reads the data), rewrites the contents (modifies the data), reseals the envelope (repackages the packet), and then forwards it to the real recipient. Neither Alice nor Bob ever knows their private conversation was read and tampered with. ### 🎯 Key Intuitive Takeaways: - **Interception** → Eve *reads* the letter (Sniffing/Eavesdropping) - **Modification** → Eve *rewrites* the letter (Packet Injection/Tampering) - **Impersonation** → Eve *pretends* to be the other party (Session Hijacking) - **Stealth** → The communication appears "normal" to Alice and Bob ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Classifications > [!NOTE] > **Common MITM Attack Vectors (Bolded for KTU Importance):** > - **ARP Spoofing / ARP Poisoning** (Layer 2) > - **DNS Spoofing / DNS Cache Poisoning** (Layer 7) > - **IP Spoofing** (Layer 3) > - **SSL/TLS Stripping** (Layer 7) > - **Wi-Fi Evil Twin Attack** (Layer 1/2) > - **Email Hijacking / SMB Relay** (Layer 7) > - **Rogue DHCP Server Attack** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Cryptographic Metric:** Modern defense against MITM relies on **Digital Certificates** validated through **X.509 PKI (Public Key Infrastructure)** chains. The standard TLS handshake timeout is **60 seconds**, and certificates have a typical validity of **365 days (1 year)** for DV/OV certificates and up to **825 days (≈ 27 months)** as per CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements v2.1. --- # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Phases of a MITM Attack > [!NOTE] > A successful MITM attack follows a strict **4-Phase Operational Kill Chain** that KTU examiners frequently test: ### 🔹 **Phase 1: Reconnaissance (Information Gathering)** - Attacker identifies the **target subnet** using tools like `Nmap`, `Netdiscover`, or `Wireshark`. - Discovers the **Default Gateway IP** and **MAC addresses** of all active hosts. - Identifies open ports, active services, and operating system fingerprints. ### 🔹 **Phase 2: Positioning (Network Injection)** - Attacker exploits a vulnerability in the **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** or **Domain Name System (DNS)** to insert themselves into the communication path. - Uses one of these mechanisms: - **ARP Reply Spoofing** (Gratuitous ARP) - **DNS Cache Poisoning** - **Rogue Access Point Deployment** - **ICMP Redirect** abuse ### 🔹 **Phase 3: Interception (Traffic Capture)** - **IP Forwarding** is enabled in the kernel (`echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward`) to silently relay traffic. - A **packet sniffer** (e.g., Wireshark, Tcpdump) is used in **promiscuous mode** to capture all data flowing through the attacker's NIC. - Sensitive data is harvested: credentials, session tokens, cookies, plaintext passwords (HTTP, FTP, Telnet). ### 🔹 **Phase 4: Exploitation (Tampering & Session Hijacking)** - Attacker can now: - **Read** unencrypted payloads - **Modify** data in transit (e.g., inject malicious JavaScript, change transaction amounts) - **Steal session cookies** to impersonate the victim - **Strip SSL/TLS** to downgrade HTTPS → HTTP ## 2.2 Detailed Mechanism of ARP Spoofing (Most Tested Topic) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The ARP Protocol Flaw Exploited by MITM:** > - ARP is a **stateless protocol** with **no authentication mechanism**. > - Any host can send an unsolicited ARP reply (called **Gratuitous ARP**). > - Hosts blindly update their ARP cache with the latest reply → **TOCTOU (Time of Check to Time of Use) vulnerability**. **Attack Logic Table:** | Step | Victim's ARP Cache (Before) | Attacker Sends | Victim's ARP Cache (After) | |------|---------------------------|----------------|---------------------------| | 1 | Gateway IP `192.168.1.1` → Real MAC `AA:BB:CC:11:22:33` | "192.168.1.1 is at Attacker MAC `AA:BB:CC:99:88:77`" | Gateway IP `192.168.1.1` → **Attacker MAC** `AA:BB:CC:99:88:77` | | 2 | Victim IP `192.168.1.50` → Real MAC `AA:BB:CC:44:55:66` | "192.168.1.50 is at Attacker MAC `AA:BB:CC:99:88:77`" | Victim IP `192.168.1.50` → **Attacker MAC** `AA:BB:CC:99:88:77` | | 3 | — | Attacker enables IP Forwarding | Traffic from Victim ↔ Gateway now flows through Attacker | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Cryptographic & Network Formulas Relevant to MITM Defense (CRITICAL for KTU Board Exams):** | Concept | Formula / Equation | Description | Real-World Utility | |---------|-------------------|-------------|-------------------| | **ARP Cache Poisoning Rate** | $T_{poison} = \frac{P_{reply} \times N_{hosts}}{R_{cache\_update}}$ | Time to poison N hosts based on gratuitous ARP replies | Used to estimate attack speed in LANs | | **TLS Handshake Latency** | $L_{TLS} = 2 \times RTT + T_{crypto}$ | Total TLS 1.3 handshake delay (1-RTT resumed) | Sizing CDNs and load balancers | | **Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret** | $K = (g^b \mod p)^a \mod p = (g^a \mod p)^b \mod p$ | Both parties compute same secret K | Foundation of key exchange in TLS | | **RSA Digital Signature Verification** | $V = M^e \mod n$ (Verify with sender's public key) | Validates message integrity + sender authenticity | Defeats MITM forgery | | **Hash-Based Message Auth Code** | $HMAC(K, M) = H((K \oplus opad) \mid\mid H((K \oplus ipad) \mid\mid M))$ | Creates keyed hash to prevent tampering | Used in JWT, API authentication | | **Certificate Chain Validation Depth** | $Chain = Root_{CA} \rightarrow Intermediate_{CA} \rightarrow End_{Entity}$ | PKI chain of trust verification | Detects rogue CAs in MITM | | **Round Trip Time (RTT) SSL Strip** | $RTT_{strip} \approx RTT_{normal} + \Delta_{inject}$ | Extra latency introduced by SSL stripping proxy | Network forensics signature | | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Wi-Fi MITM)** | $SNR = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | dB metric for detecting Evil Twin interference | Wireless IDS detection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > - **Banking Sector:** MITM detection modules are deployed in **SWIFT gateways** to prevent fraudulent wire transfers. > - **IoT Security:** MITM attacks on **MQTT brokers** can compromise smart homes, SCADA systems, and industrial PLCs. > - **Cloud Security:** **SSL Inspection Proxies** (e.g., Zscaler, Blue Coat) perform legitimate MITM-like deep packet inspection to scan for malware. > - **5G Networks:** MITM prevention is built into **SUCI (Subscription Concealed Identifier)** in 5G-AKA authentication. --- # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (Foundation of MITM Defense) > [!NOTE] > **Why This Derivation Matters for KTU:** > The Diffie-Hellman (DH) key exchange is the **cryptographic primitive** that allows two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel. The original 1976 DH protocol was **vulnerable to MITM** because it had no authentication — this led to the development of **Station-to-Station (STS) protocol** using **digital signatures**. Examiners love asking "Why is unauthenticated DH vulnerable to MITM?" ### 🔹 **Derivation: Computing the Shared Secret** Let: - $p$ = A large public prime number (typically **2048-bit** for modern security) - $g$ = A public primitive root modulo $p$ (generator) - $a$ = Alice's private key (random integer, $1 < a < p-1$) - $b$ = Bob's private key (random integer, $1 < b < p-1$) **Step 1: Public Parameter Exchange (over insecure channel)** $$ \text{Alice publishes: } A = g^a \mod p $$ $$ \text{Bob publishes: } B = g^b \mod p $$ **Step 2: Each Party Computes the Shared Secret Independently** $$ K_{Alice} = B^a \mod p $$ $$ K_{Bob} = A^b \mod p $$ **Step 3: Proof That Both Computed Values Are Equal (Exponentiation Laws)** $$ \begin{aligned} K_{Alice} &= B^a \mod p \\ &= (g^b \mod p)^a \mod p \\ &= g^{b \cdot a} \mod p \quad \text{(since } (x^y)^z = x^{y \cdot z}\text{)} \\ &= g^{a \cdot b} \mod p \quad \text{(commutativity of multiplication in exponent)} \\ &= (g^a \mod p)^b \mod p \\ &= A^b \mod p \\ &= K_{Bob} \end{aligned} $$ ✅ **Both parties now share the identical secret $K$** without ever transmitting it. **Step 4: Computational Security Argument (Discrete Logarithm Problem)** An eavesdropper (Eve) sees $\{p, g, A, B\}$ over the channel. To recover the secret $K$, she must solve: $$ a = \log_g A \mod p $$ This is the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**, which is computationally **infeasible** for large primes (e.g., 2048-bit). The hardness assumption is: $$ T_{break} \approx O(\sqrt{p}) \approx 2^{1024} \text{ operations} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Unauthenticated DH Fails to MITM:** > Since DH provides **confidentiality** of the key but **no authentication of identity**, an attacker **Mallory** can: > 1. Intercept $A$ from Alice and send her own $M_A$ to Bob. > 2. Intercept $B$ from Bob and send her own $M_B$ to Alice. > 3. Establish two separate DH sessions: one with Alice (sharing $K_{AM}$), one with Bob (sharing $K_{BM}$). > 4. Decrypt, read, re-encrypt, and relay all messages transparently. > > **Solution:** Use **STS (Station-to-Station) protocol** where each party signs their DH public value with their **RSA/ECDSA private key**. The receiver verifies with the **public key from a trusted X.509 certificate**. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: MITM Attack vs. Defense Comparison > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table (Frequently asked in 14-mark questions):** | Attack Vector | OSI Layer | Protocol Exploited | Detection Method | Primary Defense | |---------------|-----------|-------------------|------------------|-----------------| | **ARP Spoofing** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | ARP (RFC 826) | Arpwatch, XArp, IDS | Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI), Static ARP entries | | **DNS Spoofing** | Layer 7 (Application) | DNS (UDP/53) | DNSSEC validation, IDS | DNS over HTTPS (DoH), DNSSEC, Response Rate Limiting | | **IP Spoofing** | Layer 3 (Network) | IP, TCP | uRPF (Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding) | Ingress/Egress filtering (BCP 38/RFC 2827) | | **SSL Stripping** | Layer 7 (Application) | HTTP, TLS | HSTS preload list | HSTS, HPKP (deprecated), TLS 1.3 | | **Wi-Fi Evil Twin** | Layer 1/2 (Physical/Datalink) | 802.11 | WIDS, RSSI analysis | WPA3-Enterprise, 802.1X EAP-TLS | | **Email MITM** | Layer 7 (Application) | SMTP, IMAP | SPF, DKIM, DMARC | End-to-end PGP/SMIME encryption | | **IoT MQTT MITM** | Layer 7 (Application) | MQTT (TCP/1883) | Certificate pinning | MQTTS (8883), TLS mutual auth, X.509 client certs | | **Bluetooth MITM** | Layer 2 (Datalink) | LMP, L2CAP | Out-of-band pairing verification | SSP (Secure Simple Pairing) with ECDH | ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Analysis: SSL Stripping Attack Mechanics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Moxie Marlinspike's SSLstrip (2009) — Classic KTU Case Study** **Scenario:** Victim `V` (192.168.1.50) wants to visit `https://www.bank.com` from a coffee shop Wi-Fi. **Step 1: Victim Types URL** - Browser: `http://www.bank.com` (note: even if user types `https://`, the **302 redirect** from server sends them to `https://`, which the attacker can intercept) **Step 2: Attacker Proxies the Request** - Attacker's tool maintains a **secure HTTPS connection** to `www.bank.com` (real server) using the server's valid certificate. - Attacker returns a **plaintext HTTP response** to the victim with the same content (stripped images, links also rewritten from `https://` → `http://`). **Step 3: Victim Sees Normal Website** - No padlock icon ✗ (warning) — but most users ignore browser warnings. - All form submissions (login credentials, credit card) are sent as **plaintext HTTP POST** to the attacker's proxy. **Step 4: Attacker Harvests and Forwards** - Attacker logs credentials, modifies transaction data, then establishes a clean TLS tunnel to the real bank. **Step 5: Mathematical Description of the Downgrade** $$ \text{Original: } V \xrightarrow{HTTPS} \text{Bank} $$ $$ \text{After MITM: } V \xrightarrow{HTTP} \text{Attacker} \xrightarrow{HTTPS} \text{Bank} $$ **Defense Equation — HSTS Enforcement:** $$ \text{HSTS Header: } \text{Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload} $$ This forces the browser to **rewrite** all `http://` requests to `https://` **before** they leave the user's machine, bypassing the attacker's proxy. --- # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: MITM Attack Architecture (Network Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Victim Client
192.168.1.50] -->|1 HTTP Request to bank.com| B[Attacker Machine
192.168.1.99
MITM Proxy] B -->|2 Forward HTTPS to real server| C[Legitimate Bank Server
www.bank.com] C -->|3 Encrypted Response| B B -->|4 Stripped HTTP Response| A A -->|5 Plaintext Login POST| B B -->|6 Replay Encrypted Login| C B -.->|7 Logged Credentials| D[Attacker Database
Credential Store] style A fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style B fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style C fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style D fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#660066,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: ARP Spoofing Cache Poisoning Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Initial State
Victim ARP Cache Has Real Gateway MAC] --> stepB[Attacker Sends
Gratuitous ARP Reply] stepB[Attacker Sends
Gratuitous ARP Reply] --> stepC[Victim Updates ARP Cache
Now Trusts Attacker MAC] stepC[Victim Updates ARP Cache
Now Trusts Attacker MAC] --> stepD[Victim Sends All Traffic
to Gateway via Attacker] stepD[Victim Sends All Traffic
to Gateway via Attacker] --> stepE[Attacker Enables
IP Forwarding] stepE[Attacker Enables
IP Forwarding] --> stepF[Full MITM Established
Interception Active] style stepA fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000000 style stepB fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900,color:#000000 style stepC fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style stepD fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style stepE fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#660066,color:#ffffff style stepF fill:#ff0000,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of MITM Defense Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph USER["USER APPLICATION LAYER"] appA[Web Browser] appB[Mobile App] appC[IoT Device MQTT Client] end subgraph LAYER7["LAYER 7 APPLICATION DEFENSE"] d1[HSTS Preload Enforcement] d2[Certificate Pinning] d3[DNSSEC Validation] end subgraph LAYER4["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT DEFENSE"] d4[TLS 1.3 Mutual Authentication] d5[AES-256-GCM Encryption] end subgraph LAYER3["LAYER 3 NETWORK DEFENSE"] d6[BCP 38 Ingress Filtering] d7[Unicast RPF Check] end subgraph LAYER2["LAYER 2 DATALINK DEFENSE"] d8[Dynamic ARP Inspection DAI] d9[Port Security 802.1X] end subgraph LAYER1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL DEFENSE"] d10[WPA3 Enterprise] d11[Wireless IDS WIDS] end appA --> d1 appB --> d2 appC --> d3 d1 --> d4 d2 --> d4 d3 --> d4 d4 --> d5 d5 --> d6 d6 --> d7 d7 --> d8 d8 --> d9 d9 --> d10 d10 --> d11 style USER fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style LAYER7 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style LAYER4 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000000 style LAYER3 fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style LAYER2 fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#660066,color:#000000 style LAYER1 fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#990044,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: MITM Attack Detection & Response Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR p1[Network Traffic Monitoring] --> p2{Signature Match?} p2 -->|Yes - ARP Anomaly| p3[Alert: Possible ARP Spoof] p2 -->|Yes - SSL Strip Detected| p4[Alert: TLS Downgrade] p2 -->|Yes - Rogue AP| p5[Alert: Evil Twin] p2 -->|No| p1 p3 --> p6[Quarantine Attacker Port] p4 --> p6 p5 --> p6 p6 --> p7[Block MAC at Switch Port] p6 --> p8[Log Incident to SIEM] p7 --> p9[Forensic Analysis] p8 --> p9 p9 --> p10[Update IDS Signatures] style p1 fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style p2 fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900,color:#000000 style p3 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style p4 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style p5 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style p6 fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style p7 fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#660066,color:#ffffff style p8 fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#660066,color:#ffffff style p9 fill:#99ffcc,stroke:#006633,color:#000000 style p10 fill:#99ffcc,stroke:#006633,color:#000000 ``` --- # 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** ### **Q1. Define Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack. List any four common types of MITM attacks.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** is a type of cyberattack where an adversary secretly intercepts and possibly alters the communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly with each other. The attacker positions themselves logically in the middle of the data path, breaking the **confidentiality and integrity** of the exchange. **Four Common Types of MITM Attacks:** 1. **ARP Spoofing / ARP Poisoning** — Manipulating Layer 2 MAC-to-IP mappings. 2. **DNS Spoofing / DNS Cache Poisoning** — Redirecting domain name resolutions to attacker-controlled IPs. 3. **SSL/TLS Stripping** — Downgrading HTTPS connections to plaintext HTTP. 4. **Wi-Fi Evil Twin Attack** — Creating a rogue access point mimicking a legitimate SSID. --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** ### **Q2. What is ARP Spoofing? Explain the role of Gratuitous ARP in MITM attacks.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **ARP Spoofing** (also called ARP Poisoning) is a Layer 2 MITM attack in which an attacker sends falsified **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** messages onto a Local Area Network (LAN). The goal is to associate the attacker's **MAC address** with the **IP address of the legitimate default gateway** (or another host), causing victim devices to forward all their traffic through the attacker's machine. **Role of Gratuitous ARP:** A **Gratuitous ARP** is an unsolicited ARP reply broadcast by a host **without being prompted by an ARP request**. It is normally used to announce an IP-to-MAC mapping change (e.g., when a NIC is replaced). However, attackers **exploit this mechanism** to: - Bypass the standard ARP request-reply cycle - Force victims to update their ARP cache immediately - Inject false mappings faster than legitimate network devices can defend Because ARP is a **stateless protocol with no authentication**, this attack is highly effective in unmonitored LANs. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **📛 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **forget to draw network diagrams** in MITM questions — losing **2 marks** guaranteed. > - Confusing **ARP (Layer 2)** with **IP (Layer 3) spoofing** is a **fatal error** that invigilators penalize heavily. > - Writing only **defense** without **mechanism** = 0 marks for the attack explanation part. > - Forgetting to mention **PKI / Digital Certificates** in SSL stripping defense = -2 marks. --- ### **Question A: 14 Marks (Option 1)** > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]** **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **operational phases of a Man-in-the-Middle attack** with a suitable network diagram. Discuss how an attacker performs **ARP Spoofing** to intercept traffic on a LAN. *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** is executed in **four operational phases**: **Phase 1: Reconnaissance** The attacker scans the target network to identify active hosts, their IP/MAC addresses, and the default gateway using tools like `Nmap`, `Wireshark`, or `Netdiscover`. **Phase 2: Positioning (ARP Cache Poisoning)** ``` Normal ARP Cache (Victim): 192.168.1.1 (Gateway) → AA:BB:CC:11:22:33 (Real MAC) After Attacker Sends Gratuitous ARP: 192.168.1.1 (Gateway) → AA:BB:CC:99:88:77 (Attacker MAC) ``` The attacker broadcasts a **Gratuitous ARP reply** claiming: "The IP `192.168.1.1` is at MAC `AA:BB:CC:99:88:77`". The victim blindly updates its ARP cache. **Phase 3: Interception** The attacker enables **IP Forwarding** (`echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward`) to silently relay packets between victim and gateway, preventing a denial-of-service detection. A **packet sniffer** in **promiscuous mode** captures all traffic. **Phase 4: Exploitation** The attacker harvests plaintext credentials, session cookies, and can inject malicious payloads (e.g., malicious JavaScript via HTTP response injection). **Network Diagram:** ``` [Victim 192.168.1.50] ←--ARP Poisoned--→ [Attacker 192.168.1.99] ←--→ [Gateway 192.168.1.1] ←--→ [Internet] ↓ logs ↓ captures [Unaware User] [Wireshark / Ettercap] ``` **(b)** Describe the **SSL Stripping attack** with a real-world example. What defenses can prevent this attack? *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **SSL Stripping** (popularized by Moxie Marlinspike in 2009 via the `sslstrip` tool) is an MITM technique that **downgrades a secure HTTPS connection to an unencrypted HTTP connection** transparently. **Mechanism:** 1. Victim types `https://www.bank.com` in browser. 2. The legitimate server sends a `301/302 redirect` from `http://` → `https://`. 3. The **attacker intercepts this redirect** and instead serves the content over **plaintext HTTP**, while maintaining a **legitimate HTTPS connection** with the real bank server in the background. 4. The victim sees no padlock icon but the page loads normally. All credentials (username, password, OTP) are sent as **plaintext HTTP POST** to the attacker. **Mathematical Representation:** $$ \text{Normal: } V \xrightarrow{HTTPS} \text{Bank} $$ $$ \text{MITM: } V \xrightarrow{HTTP} \text{Attacker} \xrightarrow{HTTPS} \text{Bank} $$ **Real-World Example:** The **2010 Firesheep attack** at coffee shops demonstrated that session cookies transmitted over HTTP could be hijacked to impersonate users on Facebook, Twitter, etc. **Defenses:** 1. **HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security):** Server sends header `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; preload` instructing browsers to **never** use HTTP. 2. **HSTS Preload List:** Browser-level hardcoded list of domains that must always use HTTPS. 3. **Certificate Pinning:** Browser hardcodes the expected server certificate. 4. **TLS 1.3 Adoption:** Eliminates many legacy downgrade attacks. 5. **User Education:** Warn users about missing padlock icons. --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks (Option 2)** > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q. (a)** What is **DNS Spoofing**? Explain how it is used to perform a MITM attack on web traffic. Discuss the role of **DNSSEC** in preventing such attacks. *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **DNS Spoofing** (also called **DNS Cache Poisoning**) is a Layer 7 MITM attack in which an attacker injects **malicious DNS records** into a DNS resolver's cache, causing domain names (e.g., `www.bank.com`) to resolve to an **attacker-controlled IP address**. **Mechanism of DNS Spoofing MITM:** 1. Victim opens browser and types `www.bank.com`. 2. The **local DNS resolver** has been poisoned (or the attacker's rogue DNS server is used). 3. The resolver returns the **attacker's IP** (e.g., `203.0.113.99`) instead of the real bank's IP. 4. Victim's browser connects to the **attacker's fake bank website**, which has a valid-looking interface. 5. Victim enters credentials, which the attacker logs and reuses. **Network Flow:** ``` [Victim Browser] → [Compromised DNS Resolver] → [Returns Fake IP] ↓ [Attacker Phishing Server] ↓ [Logs Credentials] ``` **Why DNS Is Vulnerable:** - **UDP-based** (no handshake, no authentication) - **Query ID** is only 16-bit (only 65,536 possibilities) → brute-force possible - **No cryptographic verification** of source authenticity - **Birthday Attack** probability: $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2/2^{17}}$ — high success with ~200 forged packets **DNSSEC (DNS Security Extensions) Defense:** DNSSEC adds **cryptographic digital signatures** (using **RSA / ECDSA** keys in **chain of trust** from the **Root Zone** to authoritative servers) to every DNS response. The resolver: 1. Validates the **RRset signature (RRSIG)** using the public key from the **DNSKEY** record. 2. Verifies the **chain of trust** from root → TLD → authoritative server. 3. Rejects any response with an invalid or missing signature. **Mathematical Verification:** $$ \text{Verify}(DNSKEY, RRSIG, RRset) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } RRSIG \equiv Sig_{KSK}(Hash(RRset)) \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise (poisoning detected)} \end{cases} $$ --- **(b)** Discuss the **defense mechanisms against MITM attacks** at different layers of the OSI model. Compare at least **three detection tools** used by network administrators. *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Defense Mechanisms at Different OSI Layers:** | OSI Layer | Defense Mechanism | Purpose | |-----------|-------------------|---------| | **Layer 2 (Data Link)** | Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI), Port Security | Prevents ARP spoofing in switched networks | | **Layer 3 (Network)** | uRPF, BCP 38 Ingress Filtering | Blocks IP spoofing at router | | **Layer 4 (Transport)** | TLS 1.3, Mutual Authentication | Encrypts data + verifies both endpoints | | **Layer 7 (Application)** | HSTS, DNSSEC, Certificate Pinning | Enforces HTTPS and validates DNS responses | **Three Detection Tools Comparison:** 1. **Wireshark** — Open-source packet analyzer; captures packets in promiscuous mode. Can detect duplicate IP-MAC mappings (sign of ARP poisoning). Limitation: Manual analysis required. 2. **Arpwatch** — Lightweight Unix tool that monitors ARP traffic and emails alerts on **new MAC-to-IP pairings** or **flapping** (rapid changes indicative of poisoning). Excellent for LAN monitoring. 3. **XArp** — Advanced Windows/Linux tool with active and passive detection modes; uses heuristic analysis and **OS fingerprinting** to identify attackers. Provides real-time alerts. 4. **Snort / Suricata** (Bonus) — Network IDS that uses signature-based detection rules for known MITM patterns (e.g., Ettercap signatures, unusual ARP broadcast rates). **IoT-Specific Defenses:** - Use **MQTTS (port 8883)** with **X.509 client certificates** - Implement **certificate pinning** in firmware - Use **device identity attestation** in 5G networks (SUCI) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** ### ✅ **Core Concepts** - MITM = **Interception + Modification + Impersonation** of legitimate communication - Affects **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) - Targets **Layers 2, 3, and 7** of the OSI model - Most common attack vector in **LANs and Wi-Fi networks** ### ✅ **Attack Types to Memorize** - **ARP Spoofing** (Layer 2, exploits stateless ARP) - **DNS Spoofing** (Layer 7, exploits unauthenticated UDP DNS) - **SSL Stripping** (Layer 7, exploits HTTP → HTTPS redirect) - **IP Spoofing** (Layer 3, exploits unverified source IP) - **Evil Twin Wi-Fi** (Layer 1/2, exploits SSID trust) - **Email Hijacking** (Layer 7, exploits SMTP trust) ### ✅ **Key Tools to Remember** - **Wireshark** — Packet capture & analysis - **Ettercap** — Automated MITM framework - **Bettercap** — Modern MITM tool (successor to Ettercap) - **Arpwatch / XArp** — ARP anomaly detection - **sslstrip** — HTTPS downgrade tool - **Snort / Suricata** — NIDS for MITM signatures ### ✅ **Defense Keywords for 14-Mark Answers** - **PKI / X.509 Digital Certificates** (Authentication) - **TLS 1.3 with Mutual Authentication** (Encryption + Auth) - **HSTS Preload List** (HTTPS enforcement) - **DNSSEC** (DNS authentication) - **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** (Layer 2 defense) - **WPA3-Enterprise + 802.1X** (Wireless defense) - **Certificate Pinning** (Mobile/IoT defense) - **End-to-End Encryption (PGP, Signal Protocol)** (Application defense) ### ✅ **Mathematical Foundations to Remember** - **Diffie-Hellman:** $K = g^{ab} \mod p$ (vulnerable to MITM without auth) - **Discrete Logarithm Problem:** $O(2^{1024})$ complexity for 2048-bit prime - **HSTS Header:** `max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload` - **TLS 1.3 Handshake:** 1-RTT (0-RTT resumption) - **DNSSEC Chain:** Root → TLD → Authoritative (RRSIG verification) ### ✅ **Real-World Case Studies to Cite** - **2009:** Moxie Marlinspike's SSLstrip at Black Hat - **2010:** Firesheep Wi-Fi session hijacking - **2011:** DigiNotar CA breach (rogue certificates for Google) - **2014:** Heartbleed (allowed MITM via memory disclosure) - **2017:** KRACK attack on WPA2 4-way handshake - **2018:** VPNFilter router MITM (500K devices compromised) - **2023:** Volt Typhoon (state-sponsored MITM on critical infrastructure) ### ✅ **IoT-Specific MITM Considerations** - **MQTT** (port 1883) is plaintext — must use **MQTTS** (8883) - **CoAP** (port 5683) — use **DTLS** for security - **LoRaWAN** uses **AppSKey** and **NwkSKey** to prevent MITM - **Zigbee** uses **APS layer encryption** with **Trust Center** keys - **Bluetooth** uses **LE Secure Connections** with **ECDH P-256** > **🎯 Final KTU Exam Tip:** When answering MITM questions, always follow the **"Mechanism → Example → Defense"** trifecta. Examiners reward students who connect **theory to real-world impact** and **IoT relevance** (as per Module 2's emphasis on IoT Security). ---

topics.title: ARP Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** ARP Attacks # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 ARP — The Address Resolution Protocol (Foundation Concept) **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** is a critical Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) communication protocol operating within the Local Area Network (LAN) boundaries, defined under **IEEE 802.3** and **RFC 826**. Its primary function is to dynamically map a known **32-bit IPv4 logical address** to its corresponding **48-bit MAC (Media Access Control) physical address**, enabling frame delivery on Ethernet networks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCCT604 — Module 2):** > ARP attacks form the foundation of **Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) exploitation** in ethical hacking. Every KTU 2024 Scheme question on this topic expects students to demonstrate the **"request-reply asymmetry"** of ARP — a stateless, trust-based protocol that attackers ruthlessly exploit. ## 1.2 ARP Attacks — Formal Definition **ARP Attacks** constitute a family of **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** and **Denial-of-Service (DoS)** exploits that target the inherent trust-based, stateless design of the ARP protocol. By injecting **malicious ARP packets** (crafted replies) into a LAN segment, an attacker can **poison** the ARP cache of legitimate hosts, redirecting traffic through a machine controlled by the adversary without the knowledge of either the sender or the receiver. > [!NOTE] > **Core Exploited Vulnerability:** ARP has *no authentication mechanism*. Any host on a LAN can claim to own any IP-MAC pair, and all other hosts will accept and cache the claim. This is the "Achilles heel" that ARP attacks weaponize. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Post Office Letter Forwarding Trick" Imagine a college campus where students (hosts) communicate by writing letters. Every student maintains a **personal phonebook** (ARP cache) that maps "Roll Number → Hostel Room Number" (IP → MAC). - **Normal ARP:** A new student (Say, *Rahul*, Roll No. 104) wants to send a letter to *Priya* (Roll No. 203). He shouts in the common room: *"Hey everyone! Who has Roll No. 203? Tell me your room number!"* Priya replies: *"It's Room 207!"* Rahul writes it down. - **ARP Attack:** A mischievous student (*Mallory*) runs into the common room *uninvited* and whispers to Rahul: *"Hey, Priya moved to Room 666. That's me."* Rahul, trusting the whisper, updates his phonebook. Now all letters meant for Priya are delivered to Mallory. Mallory can read them, modify them, and re-send to Priya (or not) — **a perfect Man-in-the-Middle**. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Exam:** "ARP is *trusting, stateless, and broadcast* — three vulnerabilities that make it attackable." Memorize this trio — examiners love it. ## 1.4 Classification of ARP Attacks (KTU Board Perspective) | Attack Type | Objective | KTU Weightage | |-------------|-----------|---------------| | **ARP Spoofing / Poisoning** | Traffic redirection (MITM) | High ⭐ | | **ARP Cache Poisoning** | Corrupt ARP tables of victims | High ⭐ | | **ARP Flooding** | CAM table exhaustion → DoS | Medium | | **Gratuitous ARP Abuse** | Identity takeover / IP conflict | Medium | | **RARP Spoofing** | Reverse ARP manipulation | Low | **Key Performance Metric:** ARP operates with a **~60 second default cache timeout** in Windows and **~20 seconds** in Linux — meaning attacks must be persistent or refreshed automatically (a feature in tools like `ettercap`). # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Normal ARP Workflow — The Baseline For an attack to be understood, the normal flow must be crystal clear: 1. **Host A** (IP: `192.168.1.10`, MAC: `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`) wants to communicate with **Host B** (IP: `192.168.1.20`, MAC: `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`). 2. Host A checks its **ARP cache** (in-memory table). 3. If no entry exists, Host A broadcasts an **ARP Request**: *"Who has 192.168.1.20? Tell AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA"*. 4. The ARP request is sent to the **broadcast MAC address** `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`. 5. Only Host B (matching IP) unicasts an **ARP Reply**: *"192.168.1.20 is at BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB"*. 6. Host A caches the mapping and proceeds with communication. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Design Flaw:** ARP is **stateless** — it accepts replies *even if it never sent a request* (this is the basis of **Unsolicited ARP / Gratuitous ARP** abuse). ## 2.2 ARP Packet Structure (RFC 826 — Exam Essential) The ARP message is encapsulated directly inside an **Ethernet Frame** (EtherType = `0x0806`). | Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose | |-------|--------------|---------| | Hardware Type | 2 | `0x0001` for Ethernet | | Protocol Type | 2 | `0x0800` for IPv4 | | Hardware Address Length | 1 | `6` for MAC | | Protocol Address Length | 1 | `4` for IPv4 | | **Opcode** | 2 | `1`=Request, `2`=Reply, `3`=RARP Request, `4`=RARP Reply | | Sender Hardware Address (SHA) | 6 | Attacker's MAC (or victim's, if spoofing) | | Sender Protocol Address (SPA) | 4 | Forged IP | | Target Hardware Address (THA) | 6 | Victim's MAC (or `00:00:00:00:00:00` in broadcast) | | Target Protocol Address (TPA) | 4 | Target IP | **Total ARP payload: 28 bytes** ## 2.3 Operational Mechanics of ARP Spoofing — Step-by-Step The "Why" and "How" of the attack: - **Why it works:** ARP has no authentication, accepts unsolicited replies, and updates cache entries **without validation**. - **How it proceeds:** 1. **Reconnaissance:** Attacker uses tools like `arpspoof`, `ettercap`, or `Cain & Abel` to scan the LAN (`arp -a`, `nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24`). 2. **Packet Crafting:** Attacker generates **forged ARP Reply packets** with: - `SPA` = Gateway's IP (e.g., `192.168.1.1`) - `SHA` = Attacker's MAC (e.g., `CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC`) 3. **Flooding:** These packets are broadcast continuously to victims. 4. **Cache Update:** Victims update their ARP cache → Gateway's IP now points to attacker's MAC. 5. **Traffic Interception:** Victim's traffic meant for the gateway is now sent to attacker. 6. **Forwarding (Optional):** Attacker enables **IP forwarding** to relay packets to real gateway, maintaining stealth. 7. **Action:** Sniff credentials (using Wireshark, `dsniff`), modify payloads, or drop packets (DoS). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet Since ARP is a protocol (not a physics/math concept), the "formulas" are **behavioral metrics, packet ratios, and detection thresholds**: | Parameter | Formula / Value | Engineering Use | |-----------|-----------------|-----------------| | **ARP Cache Timeout (Windows)** | $T_{cache} = \text{15--45 seconds (avg.)}$ | Determines attack refresh interval | | **ARP Cache Timeout (Linux)** | $T_{cache} \approx \text{60 seconds}$ | Default `gc_stale_time` | | **ARP Packet Size** | $S_{arp} = 28 \text{ bytes (payload)} + 18 \text{ bytes (Ethernet header)} = 46 \text{ bytes}$ | Bandwidth calculation | | **Required Forged Packets/sec** | $P_{rate} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cache}}$ | Sustained attack rate | | **MAC Address Space** | $2^{48} = 281,474,976,710,656$ | Theoretical uniqueness | | **Gratuitous ARP Detection Rate** | $D_{gARP} = \dfrac{N_{gARP}}{T_{window}}$ | IDS threshold (e.g., > 5/sec = suspicious) | | **MITM Bandwidth Overhead** | $O_{mitm} = 2 \times B_{legitimate}$ | Doubled traffic (in + out) | | **CAM Table Exhaustion Threshold** | $N_{CAM} = 4096 \text{ entries (typical switch)}$ | DoS saturation point | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** ARP attacks are the **gateway attack** for IoT networks (Module 2 in PBCCT604) because most IoT devices (smart bulbs, IP cameras) use **plain ARP with no encryption** and have **no host-based firewall** — making them trivially exploitable. ## 2.5 Why Layer 2 Attacks Are Devastating - **Encryption bypass:** Even HTTPS/TLS can be undermined if the attacker forces the victim to a **rogue proxy** (SSL stripping via `sslstrip` post-ARP-spoof). - **Persistence:** ARP poisoning is **silent** — victims see no warnings. - **Scale:** One attacker on a `/24` LAN can poison **254 hosts simultaneously**. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: ARP Cache State Transition Let the **ARP Cache State** of a victim host be represented as a tuple $C = \{(IP_i, MAC_i)\}$. A normal entry and a poisoned entry can be modeled as: **Initial (Legitimate) State:** $$C_{legit} = \{ (192.168.1.1 \rightarrow BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB), (192.168.1.20 \rightarrow DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:DD) \}$$ **After Attack (Poisoned) State:** $$C_{poison} = \{ (192.168.1.1 \rightarrow CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC), (192.168.1.20 \rightarrow DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:DD) \}$$ Where `CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC` is the attacker's MAC. The transition is governed by the **unsolicited ARP reply acceptance function**: $$ \text{CacheUpdate} : C_{t+1} = \begin{cases} C_t \cup \{ (IP_{attacker}, MAC_{attacker}) \} & \text{if } \text{Opcode} = 2 \text{ (Reply)} \\ C_t & \text{otherwise (in secure ARP)} \end{cases} $$ > **Logic Explanation:** Secure ARP variants (like **S-ARP** or **Dynamic ARP Inspection — DAI**) add an authentication check `f(Reply) = \text{verify}(Signature)`, blocking the update if invalid. The vulnerability arises because the default function accepts *all* replies unconditionally. ## 3.2 Derivation: Attack Bandwidth Consumption For a sustained ARP poisoning attack across a LAN segment with $N$ victim hosts and refresh interval $T_{cache}$: $$ \text{Forged Packets Required} = N \times \frac{T_{attack}}{T_{cache}} $$ **Step-by-step evaluation for a typical scenario:** - Given: $N = 50$ victim hosts, $T_{cache} = 60$ seconds, $T_{attack} = 3600$ seconds (1 hour). - Substitute: $$ \text{Forged Packets} = 50 \times \frac{3600}{60} $$ - Compute the inner division first: $$ \frac{3600}{60} = 60 \text{ (refreshes per host per hour)} $$ - Multiply by victim count: $$ \text{Forged Packets} = 50 \times 60 = 3000 \text{ packets/hour} $$ - Convert to bytes (each ARP packet ≈ 46 bytes including Ethernet header): $$ \text{Total Bandwidth} = 3000 \times 46 = 138{,}000 \text{ bytes/hour} \approx 134.77 \text{ KB/hour} $$ > **Engineering Insight:** This is *negligible bandwidth* (≈ 0.3 kbps), which is why ARP attacks are **virtually undetectable by bandwidth monitoring** — they are stealthy, volumetrically minor attacks. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Detection Heuristics | Detection Method | Mechanism | False Positive Rate | KTU Relevance | |------------------|-----------|---------------------|---------------| | **Rate-based IDS** | Alert if gARP > threshold (e.g., 10/sec) | Low (≈ 2%) | High ⭐ | | **ARP Watch (Linux tool)** | Logs IP-MAC pair changes | Very Low | High ⭐ | | **DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection)** | Switch-level validation via DHCP snooping | Negligible | Very High ⭐⭐ | | **XArp (Windows tool)** | Heuristic + signature based | Moderate | Medium | | **Static ARP Entries** | Manual `arp -s` pinning | Zero (but unscalable) | High ⭐ | | **802.1X Port Authentication** | Authenticate before ARP allowed | Very Low | Medium | ## 3.4 Derivation: MITM Packet Latency Overhead When an attacker performs MITM, each frame traverses the attacker's machine. The additional latency introduced is: $$ \Delta t_{mitm} = 2 \times t_{process} + t_{forward} $$ Where: - $t_{process}$ = attacker's NIC processing time (≈ 0.01 ms) - $t_{forward}$ = kernel IP forwarding latency (≈ 0.05 ms) Substituting: $$ \Delta t_{mitm} = 2 \times 0.01 + 0.05 = 0.07 \text{ ms} $$ > **Logic Explanation:** This sub-millisecond overhead is why victims **do not perceive any performance degradation** during ARP MITM attacks — making them even more dangerous. ## 3.5 Attack Tree (Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure) A complete attack tree for **"Compromise LAN traffic via ARP poisoning"**: 1. **Phase 1 — Reconnaissance** - Identify subnet: `192.168.1.0/24` - Discover hosts: `nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24` - Identify gateway IP (via `ip route` or `traceroute`). 2. **Phase 2 — Weaponization** - Choose tool: `ettercap -T -q -M arp:remote /192.168.1.1// /192.168.1.20//` 3. **Phase 3 — Delivery** - Enable IP forwarding: `echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward` - Launch ARP poisoning (continuous forged replies). 4. **Phase 4 — Exploitation** - Sniff: `wireshark` or `tcpdump -i eth0` - SSL strip: `sslstrip -l 8080` + iptables redirect. 5. **Phase 5 — Persistence** - Cron job to re-inject every 30 seconds. 6. **Phase 6 — Cover Tracks** - Flush iptables modifications, kill processes. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Normal ARP Communication Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Host A
IP 192.168.1.10
MAC AA AA AA AA AA AA] nodeB[Host B
IP 192.168.1.20
MAC BB BB BB BB BB BB] nodeSwitch[LAN Switch
Layer 2 Hub] nodeA -->|1 ARP Request Broadcast| nodeSwitch nodeSwitch -->|2 Forward to all ports| nodeB nodeB -->|3 ARP Reply Unicast| nodeA nodeA -->|4 Data Frame to BB BB BB BB BB BB| nodeB ``` > **Diagram Logic:** Step 1 is a broadcast (everyone receives), Step 3 is a unicast (only the requester gets the reply), Step 4 is data transmission using the resolved MAC. ## 4.2 ARP Spoofing / MITM Attack Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeVictim[Victim Host
IP 192.168.1.20
MAC DD DD DD DD DD DD] nodeAttacker[Attacker Machine
IP 192.168.1.55
MAC CC CC CC CC CC CC] nodeGateway[Real Gateway
IP 192.168.1.1
MAC BB BB BB BB BB BB] nodeInternet((Internet)) step1[1 Attacker sends forged ARP Reply to Victim
Claiming 192.168.1.1 is at CC CC CC CC CC CC] step2[2 Attacker sends forged ARP Reply to Gateway
Claiming 192.168.1.20 is at CC CC CC CC CC CC] step3[3 Victim updates ARP cache
Gateway entry now poisoned] step4[4 Gateway updates ARP cache
Victim entry now poisoned] step5[5 Victim traffic to Internet goes to Attacker first] step6[6 Attacker forwards to real Gateway via IP Forwarding] nodeAttacker -->|step1| nodeVictim nodeAttacker -->|step2| nodeGateway nodeVictim -->|step3| nodeVictim nodeGateway -->|step4| nodeGateway nodeVictim -->|step5 step6| nodeInternet nodeAttacker -.Sniff Modify Drop.-> nodeInternet ``` > **Visualization Note:** Dashed lines indicate the *attacker's malicious actions* (sniffing/modifying). Solid arrows show the *redirected traffic flow*. ## 4.3 ARP Cache State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> EmptyCache EmptyCache --> BroadcastRequest : Need to send packet to unknown IP BroadcastRequest --> WaitForReply : ARP Request sent to FF FF FF FF FF FF WaitForReply --> CachePopulated : Valid ARP Reply received WaitForReply --> CachePopulated : Unsolicited ARP Reply ACCEPTED CachePopulated --> CachePoisoned : Attacker injects forged reply CachePoisoned --> CachePopulated : Legitimate reply overrides (race condition) CachePopulated --> ExpiredEntry : Timeout T cache ExpiredEntry --> BroadcastRequest : New packet triggers refresh ``` > **KTU Insight:** The transition `WaitForReply → CachePopulated` (via *unsolicited reply*) is the **exact vulnerability** — the protocol permits the cache to populate *without* ever sending a request. ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ARP Attack Toolchain ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RECON[Phase 1 Reconnaissance Module] r1[Network Scanner nmap arp scan] r2[Gateway IP Identifier ip route] end subgraph CRAFT[Phase 2 Packet Crafter] c1[ARP Reply Generator scapy] c2[Opcode set to 2 Reply] end subgraph DELIVER[Phase 3 Delivery Engine] d1[Continuous Broadcast Loop] d2[IP Forwarding Enabler] end subgraph EXPLOIT[Phase 4 Exploitation Suite] e1[Packet Sniffer tcpdump] e2[SSL Strip Module] e3[Credential Harvester dsniff] end subgraph STEALTH[Phase 5 Stealth and Persistence] s1[ARP Refresh Cron Job] s2[Log Wiper] end RECON --> CRAFT CRAFT --> DELIVER DELIVER --> EXPLOIT EXPLOIT --> STEALTH ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define ARP Spoofing. List any two tools used to perform it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (2 marks):** ARP Spoofing is a Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) attack in which an attacker sends forged ARP Reply packets onto a Local Area Network to associate the attacker's MAC address with the IP address of a legitimate host (typically the default gateway). This misleads the victim's ARP cache, redirecting network traffic through the attacker's machine, enabling Man-in-the-Middle, session hijacking, or Denial-of-Service attacks. - **Tools (1 mark — name any two):** `ettercap`, `arpspoof` (part of dsniff suite), `Cain & Abel`, `Scapy` (Python-based packet crafter). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is a Gratuitous ARP? How is it abused in ARP attacks?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (2 marks):** A Gratuitous ARP (gARP) is an ARP Reply packet that is broadcast by a host **without being prompted by an ARP Request**. It is normally used by a host to announce its own IP-MAC binding to all other hosts on the LAN (e.g., after an IP change or failover). The source and target IP addresses in the packet are both the sender's own IP. - **Abuse (1 mark):** Attackers abuse gARP to **falsely announce** that the gateway's IP is now associated with the attacker's MAC. Every host on the LAN that receives this unsolicited broadcast updates its ARP cache, instantly poisoning all victims without any need for an initial request — making gARP the **fastest vector** for ARP poisoning. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) in detail with a neat diagram of the ARP packet format. Discuss why ARP is inherently vulnerable to attacks. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the working of an **ARP Spoofing attack** with a suitable diagram. How does it lead to a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack? Explain the role of IP forwarding in maintaining the attack. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (a): **ARP Overview (2 marks):** ARP is a Layer 2/3 protocol (RFC 826) used to map a 32-bit IPv4 address to a 48-bit MAC address on a local network. When a host wants to send an IP packet, it consults its ARP cache. If the destination IP is unresolved, it broadcasts an **ARP Request** (`FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`) asking "Who has IP X.X.X.X?". The owner of that IP unicasts an **ARP Reply** with its MAC address. **ARP Packet Format (3 marks):** | Field | Bytes | |-------|-------| | Hardware Type | 2 | | Protocol Type | 2 | | Hw Addr Len | 1 | | Proto Addr Len | 1 | | Opcode | 2 | | Sender MAC | 6 | | Sender IP | 4 | | Target MAC | 6 | | Target IP | 4 | **Vulnerabilities (2 marks):** 1. **Stateless nature** — accepts replies without requests. 2. **No authentication** — any host can claim any IP-MAC pair. 3. **Broadcast trust** — all hosts accept updates blindly. 4. **Cache overwrite** — newer replies override older entries (race condition). #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (b): **ARP Spoofing Workflow (3 marks):** The attacker sends forged ARP Reply packets to the victim, claiming that the **gateway's IP** (`192.168.1.1`) is at the **attacker's MAC** (`CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC`). Simultaneously, the attacker sends another forged reply to the gateway, claiming the **victim's IP** is also at the attacker's MAC. This **double-poisoning** places the attacker as the relay for all traffic between victim and gateway. **MITM Establishment (2 marks):** - Victim → Gateway traffic now goes: Victim → Attacker → Gateway. - Gateway → Victim traffic now goes: Gateway → Attacker → Victim. - The attacker can **sniff, modify, or drop** packets — a complete MITM. **Role of IP Forwarding (2 marks):** The attacker enables `echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward` so that the kernel forwards received packets to their legitimate destination. This prevents the victim from losing connectivity (which would alert them), keeping the attack **stealthy and persistent**. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Differentiate between **ARP Spoofing** and **ARP Flooding**. Explain how ARP Flooding leads to a **CAM Table Exhaustion** Denial-of-Service attack. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the following ARP attack mitigation techniques: **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)**, **Static ARP Entries**, and **Encryption at higher layers (e.g., HTTPS/TLS)**. Which is the most effective and why? **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (a): **Difference Table (3 marks):** | Parameter | ARP Spoofing | ARP Flooding | |-----------|--------------|--------------| | Objective | Traffic interception (MITM) | Switch resource exhaustion (DoS) | | Mechanism | Forged ARP replies with false IP-MAC | Massive unique (random) ARP packets | | Victim | Specific host(s) | The switch itself | | Outcome | Cache poisoning | CAM table overflow → broadcast storm | | Stealth | High | Low (visible network slowness) | **CAM Table Exhaustion Explained (4 marks):** A managed switch maintains a **CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table** mapping MAC addresses to physical ports (capacity ≈ 4,096 entries). In ARP flooding, the attacker generates thousands of ARP packets, each containing a **unique random source MAC address**. The switch, trusting these ARP packets, populates its CAM table until it overflows. Once full, the switch enters **fail-open mode**, behaving like a hub — forwarding *all* frames out *all* ports. This creates a **broadcast storm**, degrading the entire LAN to a denial-of-service state. Tools: `macof` (part of dsniff), `yersinia`. #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (b): **Dynamic ARP Inspection — DAI (2.5 marks):** DAI is a switch-level security feature that intercepts all ARP packets on **untrusted ports** and validates them against a trusted database (typically built via **DHCP Snooping**). If an ARP packet has an IP-MAC binding not present in the DHCP snooping binding table, DAI **drops** the packet, preventing poisoning. **Static ARP Entries (2.5 marks):** Administrators can manually pin IP-to-MAC mappings using `arp -s `. This prevents cache poisoning for that specific entry. However, it is **unscalable** for large networks and **impractical for dynamic environments** like IoT (where devices join/leave frequently). **Encryption (HTTPS/TLS) (1 mark):** TLS encrypts payload but does **not** protect ARP itself — an attacker can still redirect traffic, but the data inside remains confidential. Useful as a *complementary* layer, not a primary ARP defense. **Most Effective (1 mark):** **DAI is the most effective** because it provides **automated, scalable, and centralized** protection at the infrastructure layer, without requiring per-host configuration, and it works seamlessly with DHCP-enabled networks (including IoT). --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Forgetting IP forwarding** in MITM explanation → lose 2 marks (examiners test this!). > 2. **Confusing ARP Flooding with MAC Flooding** — they are different but related. ARP flooding uses *ARP packets with random MACs*; MAC flooding uses *non-ARP frames with random MACs*. > 3. **Drawing ARP packet format without labeling Opcode field** → lose 1 mark. > 4. **Not mentioning "stateless" or "no authentication"** as vulnerabilities → lose 2 marks. > 5. **Forgetting to state the default cache timeout** (15–60 sec) in attack persistence explanation → lose 1 mark. > 6. **Stating "ARP is encrypted"** — FALSE. This is a guaranteed zero. --- ## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ ARP = **Address Resolution Protocol**, maps **IP (Layer 3) → MAC (Layer 2)**, defined by **RFC 826**. - ✅ ARP packet size = **28 bytes** payload, **46 bytes** total with Ethernet header; EtherType = **0x0806**. - ✅ Opcode values: **1 = Request, 2 = Reply, 3 = RARP Request, 4 = RARP Reply**. - ✅ ARP is **stateless, trust-based, and unauthenticated** — the *triple vulnerability*. - ✅ **ARP Spoofing** = forged reply poisoning victim's cache; primary goal = **MITM**. - ✅ **Gratuitous ARP (gARP)** = unsolicited broadcast reply announcing own IP-MAC; abused for instant poisoning. - ✅ **IP Forwarding** must be enabled by attacker to keep MITM stealthy and avoid alerting victim. - ✅ **ARP Flooding** = random MAC ARP packets → **CAM table exhaustion (≈ 4,096 entries)** → DoS. - ✅ Tools: `ettercap`, `arpspoof`, `Cain & Abel`, `scapy`, `macof`, `yersinia`. - ✅ Default cache timeouts: **Windows ≈ 15–45 sec**, **Linux ≈ 60 sec** — dictates attack refresh rate. - ✅ **Mitigation Hierarchy (most to least effective):** **DAI > Port Security + 802.1X > Static ARP > Encryption > IDS monitoring**. - ✅ **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** validates ARP against **DHCP Snooping Binding Table** — *the gold standard* for enterprise LANs. - ✅ **IoT Relevance:** Most IoT devices lack host firewalls → highly vulnerable to ARP attacks; this is why KTU Module 2 emphasizes it. - ✅ ARP is **LAN-only** — it does not cross routers, so attacks are **localized to the broadcast domain**. - ✅ **Mnemonic:** "**A**RP **A**ttacks = **A**uthentication-**A**bsent **A**nomaly" — remember the four A's. - ✅ **Real-World Impact:** ARP MITM can defeat SSL via `sslstrip` (downgrade attack), intercept HTTP cookies, and steal IoT credentials. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, questions on ARP Attacks are most often framed as **"Explain X attack with diagram" (14 marks)**. Always include: **(i) packet format OR attack flow diagram, (ii) the 'why it works' vulnerability, and (iii) at least one mitigation**. Missing any of these three is a guaranteed mark loss.

topics.title: Denial of Service Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Denial of Service Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Denial of Service (DoS) Attack** is a malicious cyber-attack technique in which an adversary deliberately disrupts the normal functioning of a target machine, server, network resource, or web application by **flooding** it with superfluous requests, malformed packets, or by **exploiting** a specific software/hardware vulnerability, thereby making the legitimate service unavailable to its intended users.

A **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS)** attack is the scaled, coordinated variant of a DoS attack, where the attack traffic originates from a **botnet** — a large network of geographically distributed, compromised machines (called *zombies* or *bots*) — under the centralized command of an attacker (the *botmaster* or *Command & Control (C2) server*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology Mandate:** Always distinguish between **DoS** (single source) and **DDoS** (multiple distributed sources) explicitly. Examiners allocate marks specifically for this distinction in module-2 questions.

## 1.2 Key Metrics (Industry-Standard Units)

The severity and scale of a DoS attack is quantified using three industry-standard metrics:

| Metric | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth/Throughput** | **Gbps (Gigabits per second)** | Measures volumetric attack capacity |
| **Packet Rate** | **Mpps (Million packets per second)** | Measures protocol/state-exhaustion attacks |
| **Request Rate** | **RPS (Requests per second)** | Measures application-layer attacks |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant & Doorway" Intuition

Imagine a popular restaurant (the **target server**) that has only **one small revolving door** (the **network bandwidth**).

- A **legitimate customer** walks in, places an order, eats, and leaves.
- A **DoS attack** is like **one bully** standing in that revolving door, refusing to move. *All* genuine customers are blocked because of *one* attacker.
- A **DDoS attack** is like the bully **calling 10,000 friends** (the *botnet*) to all rush the same door at once. The revolving door gets jammed, and even though most "friends" are innocent, the door cannot serve any real customer.

The restaurant doesn't get robbed — it just becomes **unable to serve**. That is precisely what a DoS attack does to a server.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Viva:** *"DoS = 1 bully; DDoS = 10,000 bullies. The Goal is NOT theft, but BLOCKAGE of service."*

## 1.4 The CIA Triad Impact

> [!WARNING]
> DoS attacks primarily violate the **Availability** pillar of the classic **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability). In KTU 2024 questions, frame DoS answers around the **loss of availability** — *never* data theft.

---

## 1.5 Canonical Classification of DoS/DDoS Attacks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The industry standard model (and the one KTU examiners expect) classifies attacks into **three categories**:

1. **Volumetric Attacks** — Saturate the link bandwidth. Example: *UDP Flood, ICMP Flood (Ping Flood), Amplification Attacks (DNS, NTP, Memcached)*.
2. **Protocol (State-Exhaustion) Attacks** — Exhaust server resources, connection state tables, or intermediate devices (firewalls, load balancers). Example: *SYN Flood, Ping of Death, Smurf Attack, Fraggle Attack*.
3. **Application-Layer Attacks** — Target the top of the OSI stack (Layer 7). They are low-bandwidth but devastating. Example: *HTTP GET/POST Flood, Slowloris, RUDY (R-U-Dead-Yet)*.

---

## 1.6 Quick Definition Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Botnet:** A collection of internet-connected devices (PCs, IoT devices, servers) infected with malware and remotely controlled by an attacker to launch coordinated attacks.
>
> **Command & Control (C2):** The centralized server/communication channel through which the botmaster commands the botnet.
>
> **Zombie/Bot:** An individual compromised machine in a botnet.
>
> **Reflection/Amplification Attack:** An attack that uses legitimate third-party servers to reflect and amplify attack traffic toward the victim.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a DoS Attack — Operational Logic Flow

A typical DDoS attack lifecycle consists of **four distinct phases**:

1. **Recruitment Phase** — The attacker scans the internet for vulnerable hosts and infects them with malware. These become **bots/zombies**.
2. **Command Phase** — The botmaster communicates with the botnet through a **C2 channel** (IRC, HTTP, HTTPS, P2P, Tor hidden services).
3. **Execution Phase** — The botmaster issues the attack command (e.g., *"Flood target X with SYN packets"*). All bots simultaneously send attack traffic.
4. **Post-Attack / Cover Phase** — Attack trails are erased; logs are wiped; bots await next command.

## 2.2 Detailed Attack Mechanism Breakdown

### 2.2.1 SYN Flood Attack (Protocol-Layer, Most Tested in KTU)
- **How it works:** Exploits the **TCP 3-Way Handshake** (*SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK*).
- The attacker sends a flood of **SYN packets** with **spoofed source IP addresses**.
- The target server allocates a **TCP connection state** (memory in the *SYN queue/half-open connection table*) and replies with `SYN-ACK`.
- Since the spoofed source never sends the final `ACK`, the half-open connection **times out** (typically after ~75 seconds).
- The attacker sends millions of these → server's connection table fills up → **legitimate users are refused service**.

### 2.2.2 UDP Flood (Volumetric)
- Sends a massive volume of **User Datagram Protocol (UDP)** packets to random ports on the target.
- The target checks for applications on those ports, finds none, replies with `ICMP Destination Unreachable`.
- Resource exhaustion occurs at the **OS kernel and network stack** level.

### 2.2.3 ICMP Flood (Ping Flood)
- Sends an overwhelming number of `ICMP Echo Request` packets.
- Target must allocate resources to process and reply with `Echo Reply`.

### 2.2.4 Smurf Attack (Amplification)
- Attacker sends `ICMP Echo Request` packets to a **network's broadcast address** with the **victim's IP as the spoofed source**.
- Every host on the network replies to the victim → victim is flooded with replies.

### 2.2.5 Ping of Death
- Sends an **oversized ICMP packet** (> 65,535 bytes, fragmented).
- Vulnerable systems crash when reassembling → **buffer overflow / DoS**.

### 2.2.6 Slowloris (Application-Layer)
- Opens a legitimate TCP connection, then sends the HTTP headers **very, very slowly** (one byte at a time, never completing the request).
- Server keeps the connection open, waiting → connection-pool exhaustion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following mathematical relationships are the **core equations** used in KTU 2024 DoS numerical/analytical questions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bandwidth Saturation** | $R_{attack} \gg R_{link}$ | **Gbps** | Attack rate must exceed link capacity to succeed. |
| 2 | **Amplification Factor (AF)** | $AF = \dfrac{S_{response}}{S_{request}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of response size to request size in reflection attacks. |
| 3 | **Bandwidth Amplification** | $B_{victim} = AF \times B_{attacker}$ | **Gbps** | Effective bandwidth seen by victim = AF × attacker's bandwidth. |
| 4 | **Connection Exhaustion** | $N_{open} \le T_{max}$ | **connections** | Number of open half-connections cannot exceed TCP state table. |
| 5 | **SYN Flood Success Condition** | $\lambda_{SYN} \times \tau_{timeout} > N_{max}$ | req/s × s | SYN arrival rate × timeout > max half-open slots. |
| 6 | **Mean Time To Exhaustion (MTTE)** | $MTTE = \dfrac{R_{available}}{\lambda_{attack} - \lambda_{legit}}$ | **seconds** | Time to fill the buffer when attack rate exceeds legitimate rate. |
| 7 | **Required Bot Count** | $N_{bots} = \dfrac{R_{target}}{R_{per\_bot}}$ | **bots** | Number of bots needed to generate required attack rate. |
| 8 | **Application-layer request flooding** | $R_{requests} > R_{server\_capacity}$ | **RPS** | Request rate must exceed server's max processing capacity. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Rule:** All formulas use the LaTeX `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table renderer.

## 2.4 Botnet Architecture — Hierarchical Model

A modern botnet uses a **multi-tier C2 architecture**:

- **Tier 0 — Botmaster:** The human attacker.
- **Tier 1 — C2 Servers:** Command relay nodes (often on bullet-proof hosting, Tor, or compromised cloud instances).
- **Tier 2 — Bots / Agents:** The infected endpoints (PCs, IoT devices, mobile phones).
- **Tier 3 — Target(s):** The victim(s).

The most notorious real-world example of a botnet-driven DDoS attack was the **Mirai Botnet (2016)**, which used compromised **IoT devices** (CCTV cameras, DVRs, routers) to launch a record **~1.2 Tbps** attack against Dyn DNS, taking down Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and GitHub.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Beyond exam marks:** The same DoS/DDoS analytical framework is used by **SRE (Site Reliability Engineering)** teams to:
> - **Capacity planning** — calculating server headroom to survive N× normal traffic.
> - **Stress testing** — tools like *LOIC, HOIC, hping3, Slowloris, Mausezahn* are also used legitimately for **penetration testing** with written authorization.
> - **Designing scrubbing centers** — ISP-level facilities that filter malicious traffic before it reaches customers.

## 2.6 Defense & Mitigation — Layered Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 expects you to write defense measures mapped to the **attack category** in SECTION 1.5.

| Defense Layer | Technology | Mitigates |
|---|---|---|
| **Network Edge / ISP** | **BGP Blackholing, Sinkholing, Rate-Limiting, Upstream Scrubbing** | Volumetric attacks |
| **Firewall / IDS / IPS** | **Stateful Inspection, SYN Cookies, Connection Limits** | Protocol attacks |
| **WAF (Web App Firewall)** | **Signature & Anomaly Detection, CAPTCHA, JS Challenges** | Application-layer attacks |
| **Application / Server** | **CDN (Cloudflare, Akamai), Load Balancers, Caching, Anycast Routing** | All categories |
| **IoT-specific** | **Default Password Reset, Firmware Updates, Network Segmentation** | Mirai-style botnets |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing *"install antivirus"* as the primary DoS defense. Antivirus prevents *bot infection*, not the *attack itself*. The defense of an *ongoing* attack is a network/perimeter function.
