# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **exhaustive, KTU-style analytical derivations** of the core DoS calculations. Every algebraic transition is written out, with explanatory text rows as required by the V10 protocol.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — SYN Flood Connection Table Exhaustion

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A web server has a TCP SYN queue of size **$N_{max} = 1024$ half-open connections**. Each half-open connection remains in the queue for a timeout period of **$\tau_{timeout} = 75$ seconds**. An attacker sends SYN packets at a rate of **$\lambda_{SYN} = 20$ SYN/second** with spoofed source IPs (so the ACK never arrives). Determine whether the attack is successful, and if so, calculate the **Mean Time To Exhaustion (MTTE)**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Success Condition

The server's queue fills up if the arrival rate of new half-open connections exceeds the rate at which old ones time out.

**Success Condition for SYN Flood:**

$$
\lambda_{SYN} \times \tau_{timeout} > N_{max}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
20 \; \text{SYN/s} \times 75 \; \text{s} = 1500 \; \text{half-open connections}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with the Queue Limit

$$
1500 \; > \; 1024
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion of Success Test

The inequality is satisfied. Therefore, the attack **succeeds** — the half-open queue fills up completely, and legitimate users cannot establish TCP connections.

### Step 5 — Compute the Mean Time to Exhaustion (MTTE)

The MTTE formula for connection-pool exhaustion is:

$$
MTTE = \dfrac{N_{max}}{\lambda_{SYN} - \lambda_{timeout}}
$$

where the timeout rate is:

$$
\lambda_{timeout} = \dfrac{N_{active}}{\tau_{timeout}}
$$

For a fully-filled queue just before exhaustion, $N_{active} \approx N_{max}$:

$$
\lambda_{timeout} = \dfrac{1024}{75} \approx 13.65 \; \text{connections/second}
$$

Substituting into the MTTE formula:

$$
MTTE = \dfrac{1024}{20 - 13.65} = \dfrac{1024}{6.35}
$$

$$
\boxed{MTTE \approx 161.26 \; \text{seconds} \approx 2 \; \text{minutes} \; 41 \; \text{seconds}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Within roughly **2 minutes 41 seconds**, the server's SYN queue is full, and all new legitimate TCP handshakes are silently dropped. This is exactly the result KTU examiners want you to articulate.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DNS Amplification Attack Bandwidth Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style):**
An attacker has access to a single compromised server with **$B_{attacker} = 100$ Mbps** outbound bandwidth. The attacker exploits an open DNS resolver that has an **Amplification Factor (AF) of 50**. Calculate:
- (a) The effective attack bandwidth seen by the victim.
- (b) The number of bots required to achieve **$B_{target} = 50$ Gbps** of attack traffic.

### Part (a) — Effective Bandwidth at Victim

The bandwidth amplification formula is:

$$
B_{victim} = AF \times B_{attacker}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
B_{victim} = 50 \times 100 \; \text{Mbps}
$$

$$
\boxed{B_{victim} = 5000 \; \text{Mbps} = 5 \; \text{Gbps}}
$$

### Part (b) — Number of Bots Required

The required number of bots is given by:

$$
N_{bots} = \dfrac{B_{target}}{AF \times B_{attacker}}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
N_{bots} = \dfrac{50 \; \text{Gbps}}{5 \; \text{Gbps per bot}} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{3} \; \text{Mbps}}{5 \times 10^{3} \; \text{Mbps per bot}}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{bots} = 10 \; \text{bots}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **10 bots**, each with 100 Mbps upstream, are sufficient to launch a **50 Gbps** attack against the victim — a clear illustration of why amplification attacks are catastrophic.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Application-Layer HTTP Flood (RPS Analysis)

**Problem (KTU-style):**
An e-commerce web server has a maximum processing capacity of **$R_{server} = 5000$ RPS** (Requests per second). Under normal conditions, it serves **$R_{legit} = 3000$ RPS**. A Slowloris-style application-layer attack generates **$R_{attack} = 2500$ RPS** of slow, hold-open HTTP requests. Determine:
- (a) Whether the server reaches saturation.
- (b) The percentage of legitimate requests that will be served during the attack.

### Part (a) — Saturation Test

The total demand on the server is:

$$
R_{total} = R_{legit} + R_{attack}
$$

$$
R_{total} = 3000 + 2500 = 5500 \; \text{RPS}
$$

Compare with server capacity:

$$
5500 \; > \; 5000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Server is SATURATED}
$$

### Part (b) — Percentage of Legitimate Requests Served

Under saturation, the server typically processes requests in proportion to their arrival rate (assuming equal weight). The fraction of capacity allocated to legitimate traffic is:

$$
f_{legit} = \dfrac{R_{legit}}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{3000}{5500} \approx 0.5455
$$

The percentage of legitimate requests actually served:

$$
P_{legit\_served} = f_{legit} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{legit\_served} \approx 54.55\%}
$$

This means **~45% of legitimate users experience a service failure** — a devastating outage for an e-commerce platform during peak hours.

---

| **SYN Flood** | L4 (Transport) | Protocol | TCP 3-way handshake | **SYN Cookies**, firewalls |
| **UDP Flood** | L4 (Transport) | Volumetric | Stateless UDP | Rate-limiting |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following question bank is modeled precisely on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: Part A (3-mark short-answer) and Part B (14-mark descriptive with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Denial of Service (DoS) attack. Differentiate between DoS and DDoS.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 60–80 words):**

A **Denial of Service (DoS) attack** is a deliberate attempt to make a server, service, or network resource unavailable to its legitimate users by flooding it with malicious traffic or by exploiting a vulnerability that causes resource exhaustion.

The key **differences** are:

| Aspect | DoS | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| **Sources** | Single attacker machine | Multiple distributed machines (botnet) |
| **Volume** | Limited by single source bandwidth | Scaled by hundreds–thousands of bots |
| **Traceability** | Easier to trace | Harder; attacks come from many sources |
| **Mitigation** | Block single IP | Requires distributed scrubbing/CDN |

---

### **Question 2: What is a Botnet? Mention its role in a DDoS attack.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q1]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 60–80 words):**

A **Botnet** is a network of internet-connected devices (PCs, IoT devices, servers) that have been infected with malware and are remotely controlled by a central attacker known as the **botmaster** via a **Command & Control (C2)** channel.

In a **DDoS attack**, the botnet provides the **distributed attack infrastructure**. The botmaster issues a single command, and all bots simultaneously flood the victim with attack packets, generating traffic volumes (e.g., **1.2 Tbps** in the Mirai attack) impossible from a single source. **Example:** Mirai botnet (2016) used IoT devices to take down Dyn DNS.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions are split into two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The model answers below are written to the **exact word-count and depth** expected by KTU board examiners.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q12]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the SYN Flood attack in detail. Describe how it exploits the TCP three-way handshake and the consequences on the target server.**

**Model Answer:**

The **SYN Flood** is a **protocol-layer (Layer 4) Denial of Service attack** that exploits the **TCP three-way handshake**, the connection-establishment mechanism of the Transmission Control Protocol.

The normal **TCP three-way handshake** proceeds as follows:
1. The **client** sends a `SYN` (synchronize) packet to the server.
2. The **server** allocates a TCP state entry (a "half-open connection") in its **SYN queue** and replies with `SYN-ACK`.
3. The **client** completes the handshake by sending `ACK`, after which the connection becomes fully established.

**In a SYN Flood attack:**
- The attacker transmits a massive volume of `SYN` packets to the target server, but with **spoofed (forged) source IP addresses**.
- The server, behaving per protocol, allocates a state entry for each `SYN` and sends back `SYN-ACK` to the *spoofed* address.
- Since the spoofed source never sends the final `ACK`, the half-open connection **remains pending** until it **times out** (typically **75 seconds**).
- The attacker generates millions of these half-open connections. Eventually, the server's **SYN queue overflows**.
- Once the queue is full, the server **refuses all new TCP connections**, including those from legitimate users.

**Consequences on the target server:**
- **Resource exhaustion** — memory, CPU, and connection-state table slots are consumed by fake half-open connections.
- **Service unavailability** — genuine users receive connection failures or timeouts.
- **Cascading failure** — if the server is behind a load balancer or firewall, the entire upstream device's state table can also be exhausted.
- **Financial and reputational loss** — e-commerce platforms lose transactions; SLA penalties apply.

**Countermeasures** include:
- **SYN Cookies** — instead of storing the connection state, the server encodes state into the `SYN-ACK`'s sequence number.
- **Firewall rate-limiting** of incoming `SYN` packets.
- **Increasing the SYN queue size** and reducing the timeout.
- **RST cookies** and **TCP intercept** on Cisco IOS devices.

*(Word count: ~280 — appropriately scaled for 7 marks.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the three categories of DDoS attacks with one example of each. Briefly explain the role of botnets in amplifying these attacks.**

**Model Answer:**

DDoS attacks are canonically classified into **three categories** based on the **target layer** and **attack vector**:

**1. Volumetric Attacks (Bandwidth Saturation):**
- **Goal:** Saturate the target's network link bandwidth.
- **Mechanism:** Generate a massive volume of packets to consume all available bandwidth.
- **Example:** **UDP Flood** — the attacker sends large volumes of UDP packets to random ports on the target. The target, finding no application listening, replies with `ICMP Destination Unreachable` packets, further consuming its own resources. **DNS Amplification** is a more powerful variant, achieving an **amplification factor of 28–50×**.

**2. Protocol (State-Exhaustion) Attacks:**
- **Goal:** Exhaust server or intermediate-device resources such as connection state tables, routing tables, or CPU.
- **Mechanism:** Exploit weaknesses in protocol implementations (e.g., incomplete TCP handshakes, fragmented packets).
- **Example:** **SYN Flood** — floods the server with half-open TCP connections until the SYN queue is full. **Ping of Death** sends oversized IP packets that crash the target during reassembly.

**3. Application-Layer Attacks (Layer 7):**
- **Goal:** Crash or slow down a specific web application or service.
- **Mechanism:** Send seemingly legitimate but resource-intensive requests. They are **low-bandwidth but high-impact**.
- **Example:** **HTTP GET/POST Flood** — sends thousands of legitimate-looking HTTP requests. **Slowloris** opens many connections and sends HTTP headers one byte at a time, exhausting the server's connection pool.

**Role of Botnets in Amplification:**

A **botnet** is a network of compromised devices (called *zombies* or *bots*) under the remote control of a **botmaster** via a **Command & Control (C2)** infrastructure. Botnets **amplify** DDoS attacks in three critical ways:

- **Volume amplification:** A single attacker with 100 Mbps can be scaled to **Tbps-level** attacks by orchestrating thousands of bots.
- **Geographical distribution:** Bots are spread globally, making source-based blocking ineffective.
- **Anonymity:** The botmaster's IP is hidden behind layers of bots, complicating forensic attribution.

The **Mirai Botnet (October 2016)** infected **~600,000 IoT devices** (CCTV cameras, DVRs, home routers) by exploiting default factory passwords and launched a record **~1.2 Tbps** attack against Dyn DNS, taking down major platforms including Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and GitHub.

*(Word count: ~330 — appropriately scaled for 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q13]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of an Amplification Attack. With a suitable diagram description, explain the DNS Amplification attack. Calculate the amplification factor if a 60-byte DNS query generates a 3,000-byte response.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Amplification Attack** is a special class of volumetric DDoS attack in which the attacker exploits **publicly accessible, misconfigured third-party servers** (e.g., DNS, NTP, Memcached, SSDP) to **reflect** and **magnify** the attack traffic directed at a victim.

The attacker sends a small request to the third-party server using the **victim's IP address as the spoofed source**. The third-party server, thinking it is responding to the legitimate victim, sends a much larger response. The result is a *multiplicative increase* in bandwidth hitting the victim.

**DNS Amplification Attack — Description:**

In a **DNS Amplification attack**, the attacker exploits **open DNS resolvers** — DNS servers that accept recursive queries from any source on the internet.

1. The attacker, controlling a **botnet**, sends a small DNS query (e.g., `"ANY example.com"`) to thousands of **open DNS resolvers**.
2. The source IP in each query is **spoofed** to the **victim's IP address**.
3. Each resolver, believing the query is from the victim, sends a large DNS response to the victim.
4. The victim is overwhelmed by the combined, amplified traffic.

**Amplification Factor (AF) Calculation:**

The amplification factor is the ratio of the response size to the request size:

$$
AF = \dfrac{S_{response}}{S_{request}}
$$

Substitute the given values:

$$
AF = \dfrac{3000 \; \text{bytes}}{60 \; \text{bytes}}
$$

$$
\boxed{AF = 50}
$$

This means **every 1 byte of attacker traffic produces 50 bytes of traffic at the victim** — a 50× multiplication. With thousands of open resolvers, the attack can reach **hundreds of Gbps** from a modest botnet.

**Why DNS Amplification is Dangerous:**
- Open resolvers are abundant on the internet.
- The amplification factor (28–50×) is very high.
- The attack traffic appears to come from legitimate DNS servers, complicating filtering.

**Mitigation** includes **BCP-38** (source-address validation at ISP level) and **Response Rate Limiting (RRL)** on DNS servers.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the various defense mechanisms against DoS/DDoS attacks at the network, host, and application layers. Mention at least two tools used for DoS testing in ethical hacking.**

**Model Answer:**

Defense against DoS/DDoS attacks follows the **defense-in-depth** principle, with measures applied across multiple layers:

**1. Network-Layer Defenses:**

- **BGP Blackholing:** Announces the victim's IP prefix with a `blackhole` community, causing upstream ISPs to drop all traffic to that IP. Simple but causes collateral damage — legitimate users are also blocked.
- **Sinkholing:** Redirects malicious traffic to a "sinkhole" server for analysis and blacklisting.
- **Rate-Limiting & Traffic Shaping:** Caps the number of packets/second accepted from any source.
- **Upstream Scrubbing Centers:** ISPs analyze and filter traffic in the cloud before forwarding to the customer.
- **Anycast Routing (e.g., CDN):** Distributes attack traffic across many geographically dispersed data centers, diluting its impact.

**2. Host/Server-Layer Defenses:**

- **SYN Cookies:** Encode TCP state into the sequence number of the `SYN-ACK`, eliminating the need for a SYN queue entry until the `ACK` arrives.
- **Connection Limits & Timeouts:** Cap the number of half-open connections and reduce the timeout.
- **Firewall Rules (e.g., iptables, Cisco ACLs):** Drop suspicious traffic patterns.
- **Disabling Unused Services:** E.g., disable ICMP echo-reply to mitigate ping flood.
- **Operating System Hardening:** Apply patches, disable IP-directed broadcasts (mitigates Smurf attack).

**3. Application-Layer Defenses:**

- **Web Application Firewall (WAF):** Detects and blocks malicious HTTP/S patterns.
- **CAPTCHA and JavaScript Challenges:** Distinguish humans from bots.
- **Rate-Limiting per User / IP / API Key:** Prevents credential-based flooding.
- **Content Delivery Network (CDN):** Caches content at edge nodes, reducing origin server load.
- **Load Balancers:** Distribute traffic across multiple servers.

**4. IoT-Specific Defenses (Modern Requirement):**

- **Reset default factory credentials** on all IoT devices.
- **Firmware updates** to patch known exploits.
- **Network segmentation** to isolate IoT devices from critical infrastructure.
- **Disable Universal Plug and Play (UPnP)** on routers.

**Tools Used in Ethical DoS Testing (with authorization):**

| Tool | Purpose | Layer |
|---|---|---|
| **LOIC (Low Orbit Ion Cannon)** | UDP/TCP/HTTP flood | L3/L4/L7 |
| **HOIC (High Orbit Ion Cannon)** | HTTP flood with booster scripts | L7 |
| **hping3** | Crafted packet flooding, SYN flood | L3/L4 |
| **Slowloris** | Slow HTTP header attack | L7 |
| **Mausezahn** | High-speed packet generator | L2/L3 |
| **Metasploit** (auxiliary modules) | SYN flood, HTTP DoS | L4/L7 |
| **Nmap** (reconnaissance) | Identify open ports for attack surface | L3/L4 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT skip this!**
> - Many students confuse **BGP Blackholing** (drops *all* traffic) with **Sinkholing** (redirects to analysis). These are **not** the same. Marks are deducted if you use them interchangeably.
> - Students often write *"antivirus"* as a DoS defense. **Mark-loss alert:** Antivirus prevents *initial bot infection*, not the *active DoS attack*. The defense of an *ongoing* attack is a *network* and *perimeter* function.
> - Listing tools **without** a one-line description of *what they do* results in a **−1 to −2 mark deduction** on a 7-mark sub-question.
> - Always explicitly state that ethical DoS testing requires **written authorization** under **penetration testing contracts**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield, Last-Minute Revision Notes — KTU 2024 Module 2**

- [x] **DoS = 1 attacker; DDoS = many (botnet)** — *always* state the distinction first.
- [x] DoS violates the **Availability** pillar of the **CIA Triad**, not Confidentiality or Integrity.
- [x] **Three attack categories:** Volumetric (Gbps), Protocol (state exhaustion), Application-Layer (RPS).
- [x] **SYN Flood** exploits the **TCP 3-way handshake** by leaving connections *half-open* with **spoofed source IPs**.
- [x] **Amplification Factor (AF)** = Response Size ÷ Request Size. DNS AF ≈ 28–50, NTP AF ≈ 556, Memcached AF ≈ **51,000** (the most dangerous).
- [x] **Botnet hierarchy:** Botmaster → C2 Server → Bots (Zombies) → Target.
- [x] **Mirai (2016)** = 1.2 Tbps attack via IoT default passwords → Dyn DNS → Twitter, Netflix, GitHub down.
- [x] **Slowloris** is the canonical **Application-Layer (L7)** attack — sends HTTP headers *one byte at a time*.
- [x] **SYN Cookies** are the canonical defense for SYN Flood — encode state in the sequence number.
- [x] **BGP Blackholing** drops *all* traffic (collateral damage); **Sinkholing** redirects to a *sandbox* for analysis.
- [x] **Defense-in-Depth:** ISP scrubbing → Firewall/IDS → WAF → CDN/Load Balancer → Application hardening.
- [x] **MTTE** for SYN flood: $MTTE = \dfrac{N_{max}}{\lambda_{SYN} - \lambda_{timeout}}$ — *memorize this formula*.
- [x] **Required bot count:** $N_{bots} = \dfrac{B_{target}}{AF \times B_{attacker}}$ — *memorize this formula*.
- [x] **Tools to remember:** LOIC, HOIC, hping3, Slowloris, Mausezahn, Metasploit.
- [x] **Ethical rule:** *All* DoS testing in ethical hacking requires **written authorization** — unauthorized testing is a criminal offense under the **IT Act 2000 (India)** and equivalent global laws.
- [x] **Three-letter acronym to remember for OSI layer:** **V**olumetric = L3/L4, **P**rotocol = L4, **A**pplication = L7 → **"VPA: Very Painful Attack"** 💀.
- [x] **Always mention IoT** in 2024 answers — Mirai-style IoT botnets are a hot KTU 2024 trend.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Denial of Service Attacks**
> Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | Course: PBCCT604 | Module 2
> Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Protocol | For Educational Use Only

topics.title: Hijacking Session with MITM Attack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hijacking Session with MITM Attack # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Session Hijacking — Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Session Hijacking** is the exploitation of a valid, established computer session (a semi-authenticated conversation channel between two communicating endpoints) by an attacker who steals, predicts, or replays the session token/identifier in order to impersonate the legitimate user and gain unauthorized access to the target system. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (under *PBCCT604 — Ethical Hacking & IoT Security*), session hijacking is grouped under **Network-Level and Application-Level attacks** and is considered a *post-authentication* threat — i.e., the victim has already passed the login wall, but the ongoing session is being commandeered. ### 1.2 Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack** is a cyberattack in which the attacker secretly intercepts, relays, and potentially alters the communication between two parties (e.g., client ↔ server, IoT device ↔ cloud broker) who *believe* they are communicating directly with each other. The attacker's machine is positioned as an invisible relay/proxy in the data path. MITM is a **pre-condition enabler** for several session hijacking variants because the attacker needs visibility into session tokens (cookies, JWTs, TCP sequence numbers) to perform the hijack. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postman Impersonation" Model | Concept | Real-World Analogy | |---|---| | **Client ↔ Server** | Alice (sender) writes letters to Bob (receiver) via a trusted postman. | | **Session Token** | A sealed envelope containing Alice's handwritten signature. | | **Attacker (MITM)** | A corrupt postman who opens the envelope in transit, copies Alice's signature, and forwards a fake letter. | | **Session Hijack** | The corrupt postman walks into Bob's office and *says* he is Alice, showing the copied signature — Bob lets him in. | | **ARP/DNS Spoofing** | The corrupt postman tricks Alice and Bob into believing *he* is the official post office. | | **SSL Stripping** | He secretly downgrades Alice's "registered & sealed" letter to a normal postcard so he can read it. | The **intuition** is simple: MITM *enables visibility*; Session Hijacking *exploits visibility* to *steal the identity of the session*. --- ## 1.4 Core Metrics & Standard Terminology - **Session Token Length:** Industry standard is **128 bits** (e.g., ASP.NET `SessionID` is 120-bit; modern JWTs are 256-bit HS256). - **Cookie Flags:** `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, `SameSite=Strict` are mandatory mitigation flags. - **HTTPS Port:** **TCP 443**; HTTP is **TCP 80**; SSL Stripping downgrades 443 → 80. - **ARP Cache Timeout:** typically **60 seconds** in Windows; ARP poisoning must refresh continuously. - **Common MITM Tools:** **Ettercap, Bettercap, Cain & Abel, Wireshark, MITMf, Burp Suite, sslstrip.** > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU exams, always explicitly mention **whether the attack is Network-layer (TCP/ARP/DNS) or Application-layer (Cookie/JWT/Token)**, as this distinction is the most frequently tested discriminator. --- ## 1.5 The Three Logical Phases of a Session Hijack 1. **Phase 1 — Footprinting & Positioning:** Attacker sniffs the network, identifies the victim and the gateway, and positions itself in the data path (via ARP/DNS spoofing). 2. **Phase 2 — Token Capture / Session ID Theft:** Session ID, cookie, JWT, or TCP sequence number is captured live or predicted. 3. **Phase 3 — Impersonation & Action:** The attacker replays the captured token from their own machine, hijacking the live session. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** Session hijacking is a **5-stage process** in classical models (EC-Council CEH): *Footprinting → Session ID prediction / theft → Session ID brute-force / sniffing → Session poisoning → Takeover*. MITM fits inside *Stage 3* as the **delivery vector**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Attack Architecture — Layered View Session hijacking and MITM attacks operate at different layers of the **TCP/IP stack**. The table below is the *most important* reference for KTU 2024 theory questions. | Layer | Attack Vector | Tool Examples | Token Captured | |---|---|---|---| | **Network / Link (L2)** | ARP Spoofing / ARP Cache Poisoning | Ettercap, Bettercap | All unencrypted traffic | | **Network (L3)** | ICMP Redirect, IP Spoofing | Nemesis, hping3 | TCP Sequence Numbers | | **Transport (L4)** | TCP Session Hijacking, UDP Hijack | Hunt, Shijack | TCP SEQ + ACK numbers | | **Application (L7)** | Cookie Theft, JWT Sniffing, XSS-based steal | Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP | Session Cookies, JWTs | | **Crypto (SSL/TLS)** | SSL Stripping, SSL Sniffing (downgrade) | sslstrip, sslsniff | Pre-encryption tokens | --- ## 2.2 TCP Session Hijacking — The Mathematical Core TCP session hijacking exploits the **predictable sequence numbers** of the Transmission Control Protocol. To hijack, the attacker must predict the next `SEQ` and `ACK` numbers. ### 2.2.1 TCP Sequence Number Prediction Modern OS kernels use RFC 1948 / RFC 6528 compliant **ISN (Initial Sequence Number) randomization**, but a *classical mathematical model* is still expected in KTU exam answers. Let: - $C$ = Client's ISN - $S$ = Server's ISN - $B_{next}$ = Server's next expected byte from client (server's $ACK$ field value) - $D$ = Total data bytes sent by client - $W$ = TCP window size (typical **16384 bytes** for Ethernet, **65535 bytes** theoretical max) - $R$ = Number of retransmissions / dropped packets (attacker injects silence to keep $B_{next}$ predictable) The **predictable server-side ACK** at time of hijack is: $$B_{next} = C + 1 + D + (W \cdot R)$$ The **client-side SEQ** the attacker must forge is: $$SEQ_{forged} = S + 1 + D' + (W \cdot R)$$ where $D'$ is data sent by the server to the client. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this works:** Older BSD stack ISNs incremented by a *fixed constant* $\Delta$ every microsecond. $ISN = \Delta \cdot \mu s$. This allowed attackers to predict $C$ and $S$ after sampling a few packets — the basis of the **Mitnick attack (1994)** on Tsutomu Shimomura's machine. ### 2.2.2 Desynchronization (ACK Storm) Model When the attacker injects a packet with forged SEQ, the legitimate client is now desynchronized. Every subsequent packet from the server produces a **duplicate ACK** to the client, generating an **ACK storm**. The number of duplicate ACKs per second: $$ACK_{storm} = \frac{N_{packets}}{RTT}$$ where: - $N_{packets}$ = packets in flight - $RTT$ = round-trip time (typical LAN: **0.5 ms** to **5 ms**) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often say "the attacker desyncs both sides." Actually, only **one side** is desynced (the *real client* becomes confused with duplicate ACKs); the **server remains perfectly synchronized with the attacker** — that is the *point* of the attack. --- ## 2.3 MITM Attack — The "Invisible Relay" Formalism Consider three entities: **Alice (A)**, **Bob (B)**, and **Mallory (M)**, where M is the attacker. 1. **A → M** (instead of A → B): Mallory receives Alice's message $M_A$. 2. **M → B**: Mallory forwards $M_A$ (optionally modified to $M_A'$) to Bob. 3. **B → M**: Bob's reply $M_B$ arrives at Mallory. 4. **M → A**: Mallory forwards $M_B$ (optionally $M_B'$) to Alice. Both $A$ and $B$ believe they are talking *directly* to each other. The attacker is **transparent**. The **Mathematical Condition for MITM Success:** $$Latency_{observed\ by\ A} \approx Latency_{legitimate} + 2 \cdot T_{M}$$ where $T_M$ = Mallory's processing time per packet. If $T_M$ is too high, the attack is **detectable** by latency analysis. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas and definitions — at least one appears in every KTU ESE question on this topic.** | # | Formula / Definition | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $B_{next} = C + 1 + D + (W \cdot R)$ | Predicted next server ACK number | | 2 | $ISN = \Delta \cdot t_{\mu s}$ | Old-style predictable ISN generation | | 3 | $P_{success} = 1 - (1 - p)^k$ | Probability of cracking session ID of $k$-bit length with success per trial $p$ | | 4 | $T_{hijack} \leq (T_{session} - T_{elapsed})$ | Time available to hijack before session expires | | 5 | $AR_{poison} = \frac{2}{T_{cache}}$ | ARP spoofing refresh rate ($T_{cache}$ ≈ 60 s) | | 6 | $H_{cookie} = SHA_{256}(R_{server} \mid R_{client} \mid S_{key})$ | Secure session cookie (HMAC construction) | | 7 | **SSL Stripping:** 443 → 80 downgrade | Why HTTPS-only sites are mandatory | | 8 | **Same-Site Cookie:** `SameSite=Strict` | Mitigates CSRF + cross-origin session theft | | 9 | **HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)** | Prevents SSL stripping via `Strict-Transport-Security` header | | 10 | **Session Token Bit Length:** **128 bits minimum** | Modern secure session ID | > **CRITICAL LATEX NOTE:** The vertical pipe in formulas like $H_{cookie}$ uses the `\mid` (or `\vert`) LaTeX symbol to avoid breaking the markdown table — never use a raw `|` inside table cells. ### 2.4.1 The Brute-Force Session ID Probability If a session ID is $k$ bits long, the attacker's probability of guessing it in $n$ trials is: $$P = 1 - (1 - 2^{-k})^{n}$$ For $k = 128$ bits and $n = 10^{6}$ attempts: $$P \approx 1 - (1 - 2.94 \times 10^{-39})^{10^{6}} \approx 2.94 \times 10^{-33}$$ This is *astronomically small* — proving why **token length matters** in real engineering. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **IoT Context (KTU 2024 Module 2):** IoT devices (smart cameras, MQTT brokers, CoAP servers) frequently use **short, unencrypted session tokens** to save CPU/battery, making them *prime MITM targets*. An attacker positioned via ARP poisoning can capture **MQTT CONNECT packets** carrying `clientID` + credentials in plaintext (default MQTT port **1883** is unencrypted). - **Enterprise Web Security:** Modern banking portals use **HSTS, certificate pinning, and `SameSite=Strict` cookies** — all *direct counters* to MITM + session theft. - **5G/Edge:** gNB ↔ UPF links rely on **SUCI/SUPI** encryption to prevent IMSI catchers (which are MITM attacks at the radio layer). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU ESE:** The Module-2 question will almost certainly ask you to **link an IoT-specific protocol (MQTT, CoAP, Zigbee, BLE pairing) to a MITM or session-hijack threat** and propose a mitigation. Memorize the **plaintext defaults** of these protocols. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Full Derivation — TCP Session Hijack via Sequence Number Prediction We derive the conditions under which an attacker can successfully predict a server's next-expected-sequence number and inject a forged packet that the server accepts as legitimate. ### Step 1 — Establish the TCP Three-Way Handshake (legitimate) The client (C) and server (S) open a TCP session: 1. **C → S:** SYN, $SEQ = x$ 2. **S → C:** SYN-ACK, $SEQ = y$, $ACK = x + 1$ 3. **C → S:** ACK, $SEQ = x + 1$, $ACK = y + 1$ After Step 3, both sides are synchronized. Initial sequence numbers $x$ and $y$ were generated via $ISN$ algorithm. ### Step 2 — Attacker Sniffs One Full Data Exchange Suppose the client sends $D = 100$ bytes to the server, and the server sends $D' = 250$ bytes in reply. The attacker observes: $$ACK_{server \to client} = y + 1 + D = y + 1 + 100 = y + 101$$ $$SEQ_{server \to client} = y + 1$$ The attacker also knows the client's ISN $x$ (from the original handshake it sniffed). ### Step 3 — Attacker Computes the Next Expected ACK If the attacker remains silent for $R$ retransmission windows (each window is $W$ bytes, default $W = 65535$), the server's next expected byte number is: $$ \begin{aligned} B_{next} &= x + 1 + D + (W \cdot R) \\ &= x + 1 + 100 + (65535 \cdot R) \\ &= x + 101 + 65535 \cdot R \end{aligned} $$ For $R = 0$ (no retransmission delay): $$B_{next} = x + 101$$ ### Step 4 — Attacker Crafts Forged Packet The attacker now constructs a packet with: $$SEQ_{forged} = B_{next} = x + 101$$ $$ACK_{forged} = (\text{server's last SEQ}) + D' = (y + 250) + 1 = y + 251$$ This packet is sent to the **server** with the **attacker's source IP** (which has been spoofed via ARP poisoning to impersonate the real client). ### Step 5 — Server Accepts the Packet Because $B_{next}$ exactly matches the server's expectation, the server's TCP state machine transitions to *ESTABLISHED* with the attacker's connection. The **real client is now desynchronized** and begins receiving **duplicate ACKs** (the ACK storm). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Server window: } & W_{s} \geq D_{attacker} \quad \text{(injected payload fits in window)} \\ \text{Client window: } & W_{c} \text{ still expects } SEQ = y + 101 \text{ from server} \\ \text{Result: } & \text{ACK storm until client times out} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Success Condition The hijack succeeds if and only if: $$ \begin{aligned} &(\text{i}) \quad \vert SEQ_{forged} - B_{next} \vert = 0 \\ &(\text{ii}) \quad \vert SEQ_{forged} - B_{next} \vert \le W_{s} \quad (\text{window-tolerance fallback}) \\ &(\text{iii}) \quad \text{Attacker IP} = \text{Victim IP} \quad (\text{via ARP poisoning}) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Modern Reality:** ISN randomization (RFC 1948) makes strict equality (i) infeasible. Attackers rely on (ii) — the **window-tolerance fallback** — which still works because TCP receivers accept any sequence number inside the current receive window ($\pm 2^{16}$ bytes typically). --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Detection vs. Evasion of MITM | Defender's Detection Vector | Attacker's Evasion Tactic | Outcome | |---|---|---| | ARP rate-limit switches | Spread ARP replies over time | Attacker wins silently | | Static ARP entries on hosts | Use **DHCP spoofing** instead | Switches to L2.5 attack | | HTTPS HSTS header | Use **HSTS preload bypass** via NTP trick | Partial success | | Latency-based detection (RTT spikes) | Use **Kernel-level packet forwarding** | Latency stays < 1 ms | | Certificate Pinning | Use **trusted root CA injection** on victim | Full bypass on rooted/Windows machines | | `SameSite=Strict` cookies | Force victim to issue cross-origin request via **XSS** | Token still exfiltrable | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 nuance:** A *mitigation is only as strong as its weakest assumption*. For example, HSTS assumes the user visited the site at least once over HTTPS — *first-visit* is still vulnerable. The strongest defense is **HSTS Preload + Certificate Pinning + End-to-End Application-Layer Encryption (e.g., Signal Protocol).** --- ## 3.3 Sequence-of-Events Diagram for a Complete MITM + Session Hijack $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Step} & \quad \textbf{Action} \\ 1 & \quad \text{Attacker enables IP forwarding: } \texttt{cat 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip\_forward} \\ 2 & \quad \text{Attacker runs ARP spoof: victim} \leftarrow \text{attacker's MAC for gateway IP} \\ 3 & \quad \text{Victim's ARP cache is poisoned: } \text{gateway IP} \mapsto \text{attacker's MAC} \\ 4 & \quad \text{Victim's HTTP GET to bank.com: } \text{Victim} \to \text{Attacker} \to \text{bank.com} \\ 5 & \quad \text{Attacker runs sslstrip: } \text{HTTPS (443) is rewritten to HTTP (80)} \\ 6 & \quad \text{Victim's browser sends } \text{Set-Cookie: sessionid=ABC123} \text{ in cleartext} \\ 7 & \quad \text{Wireshark on attacker captures: } \text{Cookie: sessionid=ABC123} \\ 8 & \quad \text{Attacker replays } \text{Cookie: sessionid=ABC123} \text{ from their own browser} \\ 9 & \quad \text{Bank server trusts cookie → grants access to attacker's browser} \\ 10 & \quad \text{Hijack complete. Original victim is desynced by ARP storm.} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **canonical 10-step MITM-to-Session-Hijack kill chain** the KTU 2024 examiner expects in a 14-mark answer. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 MITM Attack — Network Positioning Diagram (Mermaid) The diagram below shows how the attacker (Mallory) is positioned **logically** between Alice and Bob after ARP poisoning, even though physically all three are on the same LAN. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Alice
Real Client
IP 192.168.1.10] -- "1. Sends HTTPS request
Destination: Gateway 192.168.1.1" --> ARP[Poisoned ARP Cache
Alice believes 192.168.1.1 = MAC of Mallory] ARP -- "2. Packet physically lands
at Mallory's NIC" --> M[Mallory
Attacker / MITM
IP 192.168.1.105
Runs sslstrip + Wireshark] M -- "3. Mallory relays to real gateway
after stripping SSL" --> G[Real Gateway
192.168.1.1
Forwards to bank.com] G -- "4. Response: Set-Cookie: sessionid=XYZ" --> M M -- "5. Mallory forwards stripped reply
to Alice" --> A M -- "6. Mallory captures sessionid=XYZ
and replays in own browser" --> BANK[Bank Server
www.bank.com
Trusts sessionid=XYZ] BANK -- "7. Grants access to Mallory
as if she were Alice" --> M classDef victim fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 classDef attacker fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 classDef server fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 class A victim class M attacker class BANK,G server ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MITM + Session Hijack Pipeline This is the **engineer's view** of the attack: each block represents a processing stage inside the attacker's machine. Use this in the KTU 14-mark answer if a network diagram is too risky to draw. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NET["L2/L3 NETWORK ACCESS"] N1[Wi-Fi / Ethernet Sniffer] N2[ARP Spoof Engine
ettercap -M arp] N3[IP Forwarder
ip_forward=1] end subgraph CRYPTO["CRYPTO DOWNGRADE LAYER"] C1[SSL Strip Proxy
sslstrip -l 80] C2[HTTPS → HTTP Rewriter] C3[Plaintext Extractor] end subgraph TOKEN["SESSION TOKEN EXTRACTION"] T1[HTTP Header Parser] T2[Cookie Filter
Set-Cookie / Cookie] T3[JWT Decoder
base64 payload] T4[Token Database
SQLite / txt] end subgraph REPLAY["SESSION REPLAY / HIJACK"] R1[Browser Replayer
curl --cookie] R2[Burp Suite Sequencer] R3[Token Injector] end N1 --> N2 --> N3 N3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 C3 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 T4 --> R1 T4 --> R2 T4 --> R3 classDef network fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 classDef crypto fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef token fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 classDef replay fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 class N1,N2,N3 network class C1,C2,C3 crypto class T1,T2,T3,T4 token class R1,R2,R3 replay ``` --- ## 4.3 TCP Session Hijack — State-Machine View ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> CLOSED CLOSED --> SYN_SENT : Client sends SYN SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED : SYN-ACK received and ACK sent ESTABLISHED --> ESTABLISHED : Data exchange
Attacker sniffs SEQ ESTABLISHED --> DESYNC : Attacker forges packet
with predicted SEQ DESYNC --> ATTACKER_OWNED : Server accepts forged packet
Real client gets duplicate ACKs ATTACKER_OWNED --> SESSION_HIJACKED : Attacker replays cookie/TCP-stream SESSION_HIJACKED --> [*] ``` --- ## 4.4 MITM Mitigation Defense-in-Depth Stack ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[L1 - Network
Wired Ethernet preferred
WPA3 for Wi-Fi] L2[L2 - Switching
Dynamic ARP Inspection DAI
DHCP Snooping] L3[L3 - IP / Routing
Static ARP entries
Private VLANs] L4[L4 - Transport
TLS 1.3 only
Reject TLS 1.0/1.1] L5[L5 - Application
HSTS preload
Certificate Pinning] L6[L6 - Session Mgmt
SameSite=Strict cookies
Short session TTL
Regenerate ID on login] L7[L7 - User Awareness
Padlock icon check
Avoid public Wi-Fi for banking] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** When asked to *"explain the prevention strategy for session hijacking"*, **draw the layered defense-in-depth diagram above** in your answer script. It is worth full marks because it demonstrates the *defensive mindset* the KTU 2024 OBE pattern rewards. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1 **Define Session Hijacking. List any four techniques used to perform session hijacking.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Session hijacking is the process of **taking over an already-authenticated active session** between a client and a server by stealing, predicting, or replaying the session token, thereby impersonating the legitimate user without needing their credentials. - **Four Techniques (2 marks, 0.5 each):** 1. **Session Sniffing** — capturing the session ID over an unencrypted network using tools like Wireshark. 2. **Session Prediction** — guessing valid session IDs by exploiting weak or sequential ID generation. 3. **Session Fixation** — forcing the victim to use a pre-known session ID set by the attacker. 4. **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** — injecting JavaScript that exfiltrates the victim's session cookie. 5. *(Any other valid technique: Session Brute-Force, MITM via ARP poisoning, Man-in-the-Browser, Session Riding/CSRF.)* --- ### Question 2 **What is a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack? How is it related to session hijacking?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **MITM Definition (1.5 marks):** A Man-in-the-Middle attack is a **network-based eavesdropping and tampering attack** in which the attacker secretly intercepts and relays communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly. Tools such as **Ettercap, Bettercap, sslstrip** are commonly used. - **Relation to Session Hijacking (1.5 marks):** MITM is an **enabler** (or *delivery vector*) for many session hijacking attacks. By positioning itself in the data path, the attacker can: - **Capture unencrypted session cookies** traveling between client and server (e.g., via SSL stripping). - **Predict and inject TCP sequence numbers** once visible traffic is observed. - **Replay captured session tokens** to impersonate the victim. - In short: **MITM provides the visibility, session hijacking provides the exploitation.** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part-B questions in Module 2 carry **14 marks**, usually with sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Both Part-A and Part-B questions are compulsory with internal choice. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[a] With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack. List the common tools used. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** MITM is an attack where a malicious actor secretly intercepts and relays communication between two endpoints, both of which believe they are communicating directly with each other. **Working — Step-by-Step (4 marks, 1 each step):** 1. **Network Positioning:** The attacker joins the same LAN (or rogue Wi-Fi hotspot) as the victim and uses **ARP Spoofing** to associate the gateway's IP address with the attacker's MAC address in the victim's ARP cache. 2. **Traffic Interception:** All victim traffic destined for the gateway (and hence the Internet) now flows through the attacker's machine. The attacker has IP forwarding enabled so the victim still has Internet. 3. **Cryptographic Downgrade (SSL Stripping):** The attacker rewrites all `https://` links in HTML responses to `http://`, so the victim unwittingly uses plaintext HTTP. The attacker maintains a separate HTTPS session with the real server. 4. **Token Capture & Replay:** The attacker sniffs plaintext headers, extracts the `Set-Cookie` session ID, and replays it from their own browser to impersonate the victim. **Tools (2 marks, any 4):** Ettercap, Bettercap, Cain & Abel, sslstrip, Wireshark, MITMf, Burp Suite, Driftnet. **Diagram (already drawn in SECTION 4.1):** Alice ↔ Mallory ↔ Bob with ARP poisoning at the center. --- **[b] Explain the concept of TCP Session Hijacking with sequence number prediction. How is it prevented in modern systems? (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` **Model Answer:** **Concept (1 mark):** TCP session hijacking exploits the **TCP three-way handshake's predictable initial sequence numbers (ISNs)** to inject forged packets into an established connection, desynchronizing the legitimate client and taking over the session. **Working with Sequence Number Prediction (3 marks):** 1. Attacker sniffs the **three-way handshake** between victim and server, capturing ISNs $x$ (client) and $y$ (server). 2. Attacker observes the next several data packets to learn how the server's expected ACK number $B_{next}$ evolves. 3. Attacker computes: $$B_{next} = x + 1 + D + (W \cdot R)$$ where $D$ = data bytes from client, $W$ = window, $R$ = retransmission rounds. 4. Attacker sends a forged packet with $SEQ_{forged} = B_{next}$ from a spoofed IP (the victim's IP via ARP poisoning). The server's TCP stack accepts it because it lies inside its receive window. 5. The **real client** is now desynchronized and starts flooding **duplicate ACKs (the ACK storm)**. **Prevention in Modern Systems (3 marks, 1 each):** 1. **ISN Randomization (RFC 1948 / RFC 6528):** Modern OS kernels use cryptographically strong random ISNs, making pure prediction infeasible. 2. **TCP MD5 Signature Option / TCP-AO (RFC 5925/5926):** Per-segment integrity protection prevents forged packet injection. 3. **TLS 1.3 + Certificate Pinning:** Application-layer encryption ensures that even if TCP is hijacked, the attacker cannot decrypt or inject meaningful payload. 4. *(Bonus: HSTS, VPN, encrypted DNS — DoH/DoT.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative **[a] Discuss the different types of session hijacking attacks at the application level and the network level. Give one example of each. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]` **Model Answer:** **Application-Level Session Hijacking (3.5 marks):** The attacker targets the session management layer (cookies, JWT, session IDs) used by the web application. - **Examples:** 1. **Session Sniffing (Cookie Theft):** Capturing `Set-Cookie` over plaintext HTTP via MITM. Tool: Wireshark filter `http.cookie`. 2. **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS):** Injecting `` into a vulnerable page to exfiltrate cookies. 3. **Session Fixation:** Attacker sends victim a pre-set session ID via a phishing link; victim logs in using that ID, then attacker uses the same ID. 4. **Session Brute-Force:** Guessing a weak session ID via `Burp Suite Sequencer`. **Network-Level Session Hijacking (3.5 marks):** The attacker targets the TCP/IP layer to take over the live connection. - **Examples:** 1. **TCP Session Hijacking:** Forged packet injection using predicted SEQ/ACK (as in Question A.b). 2. **ARP Cache Poisoning + Sniffing:** L2 attack used to capture all traffic (the MITM enabler). 3. **DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning:** Redirecting victim to a rogue server. Tool: `ettercap -M dns`. 4. **ICMP Redirect / Route Manipulation:** Forcing victim packets through attacker host. > **Distinction to remember:** Application-level needs *application-layer visibility* (cookie, JWT); Network-level needs *packet-level visibility* (TCP, ARP, IP). --- **[b] What is the role of session tokens in web security? Explain any four session-token-attack mitigation techniques. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]` **Model Answer:** **Role of Session Tokens (2 marks):** Session tokens are **opaque, server-issued identifiers** (typically 128+ bit random strings, sometimes wrapped in JWTs) that the browser sends on every subsequent request after login. The server uses them to **map the incoming HTTP request to the authenticated user state stored in its session table**, without requiring the user to re-enter credentials. They are the *primary target* of session hijackers because possessing a valid token = possessing the user's identity. **Four Mitigation Techniques (5 marks, 1.25 each):** 1. **Use of Secure Cookie Flags:** - `Secure` — cookie sent only over HTTPS. - `HttpOnly` — JavaScript cannot read the cookie (mitigates XSS-based theft). - `SameSite=Strict` — cookie not sent on cross-origin requests (mitigates CSRF-based forwarding). 2. **Regenerate Session ID on Login (Session Migration):** - Issue a *new* session ID immediately after successful authentication to prevent **Session Fixation**. 3. **Short Session Timeouts and Idle Expiry:** - Idle timeout of **15–30 minutes**; absolute timeout of **8 hours** for high-value applications. - Use sliding-window expiration only for low-risk apps. 4. **HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) + Certificate Pinning:** - `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload` header. - Pin the server's public key hash in the mobile app to prevent rogue CA acceptance. 5. *(Bonus: Server-side token binding via Token Binding Protocol RFC 8471.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students LOSE marks in KTU 2024 ESE (Ethical Hacking & IoT Security):** > > 1. **Confusing MITM with Session Hijacking as the same attack.** They are *related* but *different*. MITM is a *positioning/traffic visibility attack*; session hijacking is a *token exploitation attack*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for using them interchangeably. > 2. **Forgetting the "How is it detected?" angle.** A 14-mark answer that *only* describes the attack and *omits* detection (e.g., latency spikes, ARP rate-limit, certificate warnings) is treated as incomplete. > 3. **Missing IoT context.** Module 2 of PBCCT604 is **Ethical Hacking + IoT Security**. Always tie the answer back to an IoT protocol: **MQTT (port 1883 plaintext), CoAP (UDP 5683), Zigbee, BLE pairing, LoRaWAN.** A pure "web session hijack" answer is marked down ~2 marks. > 4. **Wrong formula for $B_{next}$.** Writing $B_{next} = x + D$ without the $+1$ or the window term is a common error. The correct form is $B_{next} = C + 1 + D + (W \cdot R)$ — see SECTION 2.2.1. > 5. **Not drawing a diagram.** KTU 14-mark questions carry an **implicit 1–2 marks for a "neat labelled diagram"**. Always include at least the Mermaid-style block diagram from SECTION 4.1 or 4.2. > 6. **Forgetting the ACK-storm side-effect.** TCP hijack is *noisy* — the desynced client keeps emitting duplicate ACKs. Modern IDS/IPS detect this. Mentioning it shows depth. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Final 60-second revision checklist — read this on the morning of the exam.** - **Session Hijacking** = stealing/exploiting a *valid active session token*; classified as **Application-Level** or **Network-Level** hijack. - **MITM** = invisible *relay positioning* between two communicating parties; the *enabler* for many session hijacks. - **3 Phases of Session Hijack:** Footprinting → Token Capture → Impersonation. - **5-Stage EC-Council model:** Footprinting → Session ID Generation Weakness → Session ID Theft → Session Poisoning → Takeover. - **TCP Hijack Formula:** $B_{next} = C + 1 + D + (W \cdot R)$. Predict SEQ, forge packet, desync the real client. - **The Mitnick Attack (1994):** Historical example of ISN prediction — always quote this for context-rich answers. - **MITM Tools to memorize:** Ettercap, Bettercap, sslstrip, Wireshark, Burp Suite, Cain & Abel, MITMf. - **Default IoT plaintext ports:** MQTT = **1883**, CoAP = **5683/UDP**, Modbus TCP = **502** — all MITM-vulnerable. - **Cookie Flags trio:** `Secure` + `HttpOnly` + `SameSite=Strict` — mandatory for any KTU mitigation answer. - **HSTS** prevents SSL stripping; **Certificate Pinning** prevents rogue-CA MITM; **ISN Randomization** prevents TCP hijack. - **ARP Poisoning** is the most common L2 enabler; defend with **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** + **DHCP Snooping** on managed switches. - **SSL Stripping:** downgrades 443 → 80; the attacker sees plaintext even if the server supports HTTPS. - **Session ID length must be ≥ 128 bits**; $P_{guess} = 1 - (1 - 2^{-k})^n$ — for 128 bits this is effectively zero. - **Modern SSL/TLS Version:** Use only **TLS 1.3**; older versions are MITM-vulnerable to POODLE/BEAST. - **Defense-in-Depth layers:** Network → Switching → IP → Transport (TLS) → Application (HSTS/Pinning) → Session Mgmt → User Awareness. - **Defense in IoT context:** Use **DTLS** (Datagram TLS) for CoAP, **MQTT over TLS (port 8883)**, **X.509 certs per device**, and **LoRaWAN AppKey per device**. > [!TIP] > **One-line exam mantra to write at the start of your answer:** > *"MITM is the eye that watches; session hijacking is the hand that steals."*

topics.title: DNS Spoofing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DNS Spoofing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — DOMAIN NAME SYSTEM (DNS) SPOOFING

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **DNS Spoofing** (also referred to as **DNS Cache Poisoning** or **DNS Redirection**) is a sophisticated Man-In-The-Middle (MITM) attack vector in which an adversary injects forged or malicious DNS resolution data into the cache of a DNS resolver. The objective is to redirect the victim from a legitimate website (e.g., a banking portal) to a rogue, attacker-controlled server that visually mimics the original site, thereby harvesting credentials, deploying malware, or executing phishing campaigns.

In the context of **IoT (Internet of Things) Security**, DNS Spoofing is particularly catastrophic because the vast majority of low-power IoT devices (smart cameras, smart bulbs, industrial sensors, wearables, and connected appliances) lack robust built-in cryptographic certificate validation. They perform a *lightweight* DNS lookup to reach their manufacturer's cloud API — making them prime targets for redirection, botnet recruitment (e.g., **Mirai botnet**), and lateral network pivoting.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **DNS Resolver** | A server that translates human-readable domain names (e.g., `www.ktu.ac.in`) into machine-readable IP addresses (e.g., `103.43.112.45`). |
| **Cache** | A temporary storage mechanism within the resolver that stores recent DNS lookups to reduce latency. |
| **Transaction ID (TxID)** | A 16-bit identifier used to match DNS queries with their corresponding responses. |
| **Rogue Server** | An attacker-controlled server impersonating a legitimate web service. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tampered Phone Book" Attack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine the following real-world scenario:**
>
> You are looking for the phone number of "Apollo Hospital" in a printed city phone book. However, a malicious person has stealthily **scratched out** the original number and **handwritten** their own number next to the listing. When you dial "Apollo Hospital," you actually reach the criminal — perhaps someone pretending to be a hospital staff member asking for your credit card details.
>
> **DNS Spoofing works identically in cyberspace.** The DNS system is essentially the *Internet's phone book*. An attacker tampers with the entries (IP address mappings) so that when your browser asks "Where is `www.mybank.com`?", the poisoned DNS resolver replies with the IP address of the *attacker's* server instead of the genuine bank's server.

### Why this matters for IoT
Your smart home camera, when booting, automatically queries a DNS server to locate `cloud-camera-vendor.com`. If the DNS response has been spoofed, the camera will obediently connect to the attacker's server — handing over live video feeds, microphone audio, and even the home Wi-Fi credentials stored on the device. Because the device "trusts" whatever DNS says, the compromise is **silent and transparent** to the end user.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Normal DNS Resolution (Pre-Attack)

To understand the attack, we must first understand the legitimate flow. When a user types `www.example.com` into a browser, the following sequence occurs:

1. **Local Check** — The operating system inspects its *local DNS cache* for a previously resolved record.
2. **Recursive Query to Resolver** — If no local record exists, the query is forwarded to the ISP's *recursive DNS resolver*.
3. **Iterative Query to Root, TLD, and Authoritative Servers** — The resolver queries the DNS hierarchy (`.` → `.com` → `example.com`'s authoritative server) to obtain the authoritative IP address.
4. **Caching** — The resolver stores the response in its cache for a duration defined by the **TTL (Time-To-Live)** field.
5. **Response to Client** — The IP address is returned to the user's machine, which then initiates the actual TCP/HTTP connection.

> [!NOTE]
> The **vulnerability** lies in Step 4. The resolver *trusts* whichever response arrives first and matches the TxID — without cryptographic authentication of the source.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Attack Methodology (The Poisoning Sequence)

The attacker systematically corrupts the trust model of the DNS:

- **🔍 Reconnaissance:** Attacker identifies the target DNS resolver (e.g., `8.8.8.8` or an internal corporate resolver `10.0.0.1`) and maps the network topology.
- **🎯 Query Triggering:** Attacker coerces the target resolver to issue a DNS query for a domain they control (e.g., `evil-domain.com`) or a popular target domain.
- **🔢 TxID Prediction:** The attacker *races* the legitimate authoritative server by predicting (or brute-forcing) the 16-bit Transaction ID. The probability of guessing correctly in a single attempt is $\frac{1}{65536}$.
- **💉 Forged Response Injection:** The attacker floods the resolver with **forged UDP packets** on port 53 containing:
   * The correct TxID (or a guessed one)
   * The attacker's IP address mapped to the target domain
   * A long TTL value (to *persist* the poison)
- **🏁 Race Condition Win:** If the forged packet arrives **before** the legitimate response, the resolver accepts it and writes the malicious entry into its cache.
- **🔁 Persistence:** The poisoned entry remains cached for the entire TTL duration (often hours or days), redirecting *all* users of that resolver.
- **💥 Exploitation:** Every subsequent user querying the resolver for the target domain is now redirected to the attacker's server.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Comparison Table

### Table 2.3.1 — DNS Spoofing vs. DNS Cache Poisoning vs. DNS Hijacking

| Feature | DNS Spoofing | DNS Cache Poisoning | DNS Hijacking |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Attack Vector** | Modifies DNS responses in transit | Poisons the resolver's cache | Modifies DNS configuration on client or registrar |
| **Target** | Live network traffic | DNS resolver's stored data | Client device or registrar account |
| **Persistence** | Transient (until cache flush) | Long-lived (TTL duration) | Permanent until manually fixed |
| **Sophistication** | High (requires TxID prediction) | High (requires race condition win) | Medium (requires access to client) |
| **Common Tool** | `ettercap`, `scapy` | `Kaminsky exploit`, `dnsmasq-sploit` | Router compromise, rogue DHCP |

### Table 2.3.2 — Probability of Brute-Force TxID Guess

The probability $P$ of correctly guessing the 16-bit Transaction ID in a single forged packet is:

$$P = \frac{1}{2^{16}} = \frac{1}{65536} \approx 1.525 \times 10^{-5}$$

However, an attacker sending $n$ forged packets per second has a cumulative success probability within time $t$ given by:

$$P_{success} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{65536}\right)^{n \cdot t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners frequently award marks to students who explicitly state that modern DNS Spoofing attacks often **bypass** the TxID guess altogether by exploiting **source port prediction** (reducing randomness) or by abusing the **Kaminsky Vulnerability (CVE-2008-1447)**, which allows an attacker to poison a *random* domain rather than a fixed one — making the attack far more scalable.

### Table 2.3.3 — Essential DNS Record Types Targeted

| Record Type | Purpose | Spoofing Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **A Record** | Maps domain to IPv4 address | Direct website redirection |
| **AAAA Record** | Maps domain to IPv6 address | Direct website redirection (IPv6) |
| **CNAME** | Alias of one domain to another | Chains redirection to attacker |
| **MX** | Mail server routing | Email interception |
| **NS** | Authoritative name server | Full DNS zone takeover |
| **TXT** | Verification records | SPF/DKIM bypass for email spoofing |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DNS Spoofing is not merely an academic concept — it is a **defensive knowledge domain** critical for:

- **Network Security Engineers** — Configuring secure recursive resolvers (e.g., Unbound, BIND with Response Rate Limiting).
- **IoT Firmware Developers** — Hardcoding *trusted* DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) endpoints to prevent device-level spoofing.
- **Penetration Testers** — Demonstrating the risk in red-team engagements using `ettercap -T -q -M arp:remote /gateway// /victim//` combined with `dnsspoof`.
- **SOC Analysts** — Detecting anomalous DNS traffic patterns via SIEM correlation rules.
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 3. EXHAUSTIVE STEP-BY-STEP ATTACK DERIVATION & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Mathematical Model of the Kaminsky-Style DNS Spoofing Attack

In the classical **Kaminsky exploit (2008)**, the attacker abuses the fact that DNS resolvers will re-issue queries if a response is not received within a timeout window. The attack transforms a low-probability "guess" into a *deterministic* race.

Let us derive the **time-window model** for a successful cache poisoning attempt.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

Let:
- $T_{auth}$ = Round-trip time (RTT) from resolver to authoritative server (in milliseconds)
- $T_{attacker}$ = RTT from resolver to attacker-controlled server (in milliseconds)
- $T_{injection}$ = Time taken by attacker to generate and inject one forged response (in milliseconds)
- $N$ = Number of forged packets sent by the attacker during the race window
- $P_{per\_packet}$ = Probability of guessing the correct TxID per packet
- $W$ = The "race window" in milliseconds during which a forged response will be accepted

The race window is the period between the resolver sending the query and receiving the *legitimate* response:

$$W = T_{auth} - \epsilon$$

where $\epsilon$ represents the network jitter (typically a few milliseconds).

### Step 2 — Attacker Packet Budget per Race Window

The maximum number of forged packets an attacker can inject within the race window $W$ is bounded by:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{W}{T_{injection}} \right\rfloor$$

If the attacker uses high-performance tools (e.g., custom Scapy scripts on a 10 Gbps link), $T_{injection}$ can be as low as **0.1 ms**, yielding:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{100 \text{ ms}}{0.1 \text{ ms}} \right\rfloor = 1000 \text{ forged packets}$$

### Step 3 — Cumulative Probability of Successful Poisoning

The probability that *at least one* of the $N$ forged packets correctly guesses the TxID is the complement of all of them failing:

$$P_{success} = 1 - (1 - P_{per\_packet})^{N}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Numerical Values

Given $P_{per\_packet} = \frac{1}{65536}$ and $N = 1000$:

$$P_{success} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{65536}\right)^{1000}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Binomial Approximation

For small probabilities and moderate $N$, we use the approximation $(1 - x)^n \approx e^{-nx}$:

$$P_{success} \approx 1 - e^{-\frac{N}{65536}}$$

$$P_{success} \approx 1 - e^{-\frac{1000}{65536}}$$

$$P_{success} \approx 1 - e^{-0.01526}$$

$$P_{success} \approx 1 - 0.9849$$

$$P_{success} \approx 0.0151 = 1.51\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 1.51% success rate per race window may appear low, but recall that the **Kaminsky attack** allows the attacker to *trigger a new race* for a *new random subdomain* (e.g., `xyz123.bank.com`) on every failed attempt. Across $K = 1000$ such race attempts:

$$P_{total} = 1 - (1 - 0.0151)^{1000} \approx 1 - e^{-15.1} \approx 99.999\%$$

This is why the Kaminsky attack was deemed **catastrophic** and forced the global rollout of **DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions)**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a DNS Spoofing Mitigation Strategy

The following table guides a security architect in selecting the appropriate defense based on the operational environment:

| Mitigation Strategy | Effectiveness Against TxID Guessing | Effectiveness Against Kaminsky Attack | Overhead | IoT Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Randomize Source Port** (instead of fixed 53) | Medium | Low | Negligible | High |
| **Increase TxID Entropy** (32-bit) | High | Medium | Low | High |
| **DNSSEC (Cryptographic Signing)** | Very High | Very High | Moderate | Medium (CPU cost) |
| **DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH)** | High | High | Moderate | Low (battery cost) |
| **DNS-over-TLS (DoT)** | High | High | Moderate | Medium |
| **Response Rate Limiting (RRL)** | Low | High | Low | High |
| **0x20 Bit Encoding Randomization** | Medium | Medium | Negligible | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When the question asks *"Why is DNSSEC the gold standard?"*, always mention three pillars: (1) **Origin Authentication** of DNS data, (2) **Data Integrity** via digital signatures (RSA/SHA-256), and (3) **Authenticated Denial of Existence** via NSEC3 records.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — IoT Camera Redirection Scenario

> **Problem Statement:** An attacker wishes to redirect a fleet of 500 IoT security cameras (each configured to use a public DNS resolver `8.8.8.8`) to a rogue command-and-control (C2) server. The attacker has access to a script capable of injecting 2000 forged DNS responses per race window.

Using the formula derived in Step 5:

$$P_{success} = 1 - e^{-\frac{2000}{65536}} = 1 - e^{-0.03052} \approx 0.0301 = 3.01\%$$

Across $K = 500$ devices, each with independent race attempts, the expected number of compromised devices $E[C]$ is:

$$E[C] = K \times P_{success} = 500 \times 0.0301 \approx 15.05$$

Thus, approximately **15 cameras** are statistically expected to fall under attacker control within a single attack burst — a non-trivial number for a botnet expansion.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Legitimate DNS Resolution Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Browser on IoT Device] --> B{Local DNS Cache Hit?}
    B -- Yes --> Z[Return Cached IP]
    B -- No --> C[Query ISP Recursive Resolver]
    C --> D[Query Root Server .]
    D --> E[Query TLD Server .com]
    E --> F[Query Authoritative Server]
    F --> G[Receive Authoritative IP]
    G --> H[Resolver Stores in Cache with TTL]
    H --> I[Return IP to Client]
    I --> J[Client Connects to Legitimate Server]
```

## 4.2 DNS Spoofing Attack Flow (Adversary Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Attacker Sets Up Rogue DNS Server] --> B1[Attacker Monitors Target Resolver]
    B1 --> C1[Attacker Triggers DNS Query for Target Domain]
    C1 --> D1[Resolver Sends Query to Authoritative Server]
    D1 --> E1[Attacker Floods Forged UDP Responses]
    E1 --> F1{Which Response Arrives First?}
    F1 -- Forged Response Wins --> G1[Resolver Caches Attacker's IP]
    F1 -- Legitimate Response Wins --> H1[Attack Retried with New Subdomain]
    G1 --> I1[Subsequent Users Redirected to Rogue Server]
    I1 --> J1[Credential Harvesting / Malware Drop]
    H1 --> C1
```

## 4.3 IoT-Specific DNS Spoofing Attack Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAN[Local IoT Network]
        DEV1[Smart Camera]
        DEV2[Smart Bulb]
        DEV3[Industrial Sensor]
        ROUTER[Wi-Fi Router]
    end
    ATTACKER[Attacker on Same Network] -->|ARP Poisoning / Packet Injection| ROUTER
    ROUTER -->|Forged DNS Response| DEV1
    ROUTER -->|Forged DNS Response| DEV2
    ROUTER -->|Forged DNS Response| DEV3
    DEV1 -->|HTTPS to cloud-vendor.com| ROGUE[Rogue C2 Server]
    DEV2 -->|HTTPS to cloud-vendor.com| ROGUE
    DEV3 -->|MQTT to telemetry-server.com| ROGUE
    ROGUE --> EXFIL[Data Exfiltration / Botnet Recruitment]
```

## 4.4 Mitigation Architecture (Defensive Layered Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CLIENT[IoT Client] --> DOH[DNS-over-HTTPS Stub Resolver]
    DOH -->|Encrypted Query| VALIDATOR[DNSSEC-Aware Recursive Resolver]
    VALIDATOR -->|Signed Response Verification| CACHE[Authenticated Cache]
    CACHE --> CLIENT
    VALIDATOR -.->|RRL: Rate Limit Suspicious Responses| VALIDATOR
    VALIDATOR -.->|0x20 Encoding Randomization| VALIDATOR
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define DNS Spoofing. List any two techniques used to perform it.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
DNS Spoofing is a cyber attack in which an attacker injects forged DNS responses into a DNS resolver's cache to redirect users from a legitimate website to a malicious one. Two common techniques are: **(1) TxID Prediction / Brute-Forcing**, where the attacker guesses the 16-bit transaction identifier to win the race against the legitimate response, and **(2) Kaminsky Exploitation (CVE-2008-1447)**, which abuses non-existent subdomain queries to force repeated race attempts, making the attack deterministic. *(Examiner-acceptable: ARP poisoning + DNS interception via `ettercap`.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between DNS Spoofing and DNS Hijacking.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DNS Spoofing | DNS Hijacking |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Tampers with DNS responses in transit (network layer attack) | Tampers with DNS configuration on the client device or registrar (system layer attack) |
| **Scope** | Affects all users of the poisoned resolver | Affects only the compromised client/router |
| **Example Tool** | `ettercap dns_spoof` plugin | Router admin password compromise, malicious DHCP server |
| **Mitigation** | DNSSEC, DoH, source port randomization | Router firmware updates, strong admin credentials |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Model Paper — Module 2 Bundle]**

---

### ✅ **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the step-by-step working of a DNS Spoofing attack. How does the Kaminsky exploit improve upon the classical cache poisoning technique? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Reconnaissance (1 mark):** The attacker maps the network, identifies the target resolver, and selects a target domain whose traffic they wish to redirect.
2. **Query Forcing (1 mark):** The attacker sends a query to the resolver for a non-existent subdomain (e.g., `random12345.bank.com`) of the target domain, which forces the resolver to query the authoritative server.
3. **Forged Response Flood (2 marks):** The attacker floods the resolver with UDP packets on port 53 containing:
   * The guessed TxID
   * The attacker's IP address as the A-record response
   * An NS record pointing to the attacker's authoritative server (this is the **Kaminsky trick** — it makes the malicious NS record persistent across cache flushes)
4. **Race Condition Win (1 mark):** If the forged response arrives before the legitimate one, the resolver caches the attacker's IP.
5. **Persistence via NS Record (1 mark):** Unlike classical cache poisoning (which expires with TTL), the Kaminsky attack poisons the *NS (Name Server) record*, which delegates the entire zone to the attacker's server — giving the attacker **complete control** over all future resolutions for the domain.
6. **Exploitation (1 mark):** All users of the resolver are now silently redirected to the attacker's server.

**Why Kaminsky is superior:** The classical attack had a single shot per domain per TTL expiry. The Kaminsky attack creates *fresh* race windows for *fresh* random subdomains, transforming a probabilistic attack into a near-deterministic one. After a successful poison, the attacker controls the entire domain's NS delegation.

---

#### (b) Discuss the role of DNSSEC in preventing DNS Spoofing. Why is DNSSEC adoption still limited in IoT environments? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Role of DNSSEC (4 marks):**

1. **Origin Authentication (1 mark):** DNSSEC uses public-key cryptography (typically RSA-2048 or ECDSA-P256) where each DNS zone is signed by a private key. The corresponding public key is published in the parent zone, forming a **chain of trust** from the root down to the target domain.
2. **Data Integrity (1 mark):** Each DNS response is accompanied by an **RRSIG (Resource Record Signature)**. The resolver cryptographically verifies the signature against the published **DNSKEY** record.
3. **Authenticated Denial of Existence (1 mark):** Using **NSEC3** records, DNSSEC proves cryptographically that a domain does *not* exist, preventing attackers from forging "no such domain" responses to hide malicious redirects.
4. **Chain of Trust (1 mark):** The root zone is signed by a trusted key held by ICANN, and every parent signs the child's key, creating a verifiable trust hierarchy from `.` to `example.com`.

**Why DNSSEC is Limited in IoT (3 marks):**

1. **Computational Overhead (1 mark):** Signature verification consumes CPU cycles — costly for battery-powered IoT devices with sub-100 MHz microcontrollers.
2. **Memory Footprint (1 mark):** DNSSEC responses are larger (due to RRSIG + DNSKEY + NSEC3 chains), exceeding the memory of constrained IoT firmware.
3. **Operational Complexity (1 mark):** DNSSEC requires careful **key management**, **rollover procedures**, and **signed parent zones** — many IoT manufacturers lack the operational maturity to implement this correctly, leading to broken validation paths.

---

### ✅ **QUESTION B (ALTERNATIVE) — (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Describe the architecture of a DNS Spoofing attack against an IoT smart home network. Include a labelled block diagram in your explanation. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Network Reconnaissance (1 mark):** Attacker connects to the home Wi-Fi (e.g., via a compromised guest network or WPS brute-force). Uses `nmap` to identify IoT devices and the router's default gateway IP.
2. **ARP Spoofing Setup (1 mark):** The attacker uses `ettercap` or `bettercap` to poison the ARP tables of the router and the IoT devices, positioning themselves as the **Man-In-The-Middle**.
3. **DNS Interception (2 marks):** All DNS queries from IoT devices pass through the attacker's machine. The attacker intercepts queries (e.g., `connect.smartcamera.com`) and crafts forged responses pointing to a rogue IP (e.g., `203.0.113.55`).
4. **Rogue Server Deployment (1 mark):** The attacker hosts a fake cloud server on `203.0.113.55` that mimics the camera vendor's authentication API. The IoT camera, trusting the DNS response, connects and transmits credentials.
5. **Botnet Recruitment (1 mark):** The compromised camera is now a zombie node, awaiting commands from the attacker's C2 server (often via the **Mirai** botnet protocol).
6. **Persistence (1 mark):** The attacker may also compromise the router's DNS settings via the default admin panel (default credentials), making the spoofing persistent across reboots.

**Labelled Block Diagram (to be drawn by student — expected in KTU answer sheet):**

```
[IoT Devices] → [Compromised Router] → [Attacker MITM] → [Rogue DNS Server]
                          ↓
                  [Legitimate Cloud] (blocked)
```

---

#### (b) Compare and contrast the following DNS attack types: (i) DNS Spoofing, (ii) DNS Amplification, (iii) DNS Tunneling. Mention at least one mitigation for each. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Attack Type | Objective | Mechanism | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) DNS Spoofing** | Redirection of victim to malicious site | Forged DNS responses injected into resolver cache | **DNSSEC** + DoH + source port randomization |
| **(ii) DNS Amplification** | Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) | Spoofed source IP amplifies small query into large response via open resolvers | **Response Rate Limiting (RRL)** + BCP-38 (ingress filtering) |
| **(iii) DNS Tunneling** | Data exfiltration / C2 channel bypass | Encapsulates non-DNS data (e.g., HTTP, SSH) within DNS queries/responses (e.g., via `iodine`, `dnscat2`) | **Deep packet inspection** + anomalous traffic volume monitoring + egress filtering |

**Award marks** for correctly identifying the *primary objective* of each attack and citing **at least one specific, technically valid mitigation**. KTU examiners reward precision over breadth.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU VALUATION WARNING — COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing DNS Spoofing with ARP Spoofing:** They are *complementary* but distinct. DNS Spoofing operates at the **application layer** (DNS protocol); ARP Spoofing operates at **Layer 2**. Writing "DNS Spoofing is the same as ARP Spoofing" guarantees partial mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting the TxID:** Any DNS Spoofing explanation that omits the **Transaction ID (16-bit)** is incomplete. The TxID is the linchpin of the attack.
> 3. **Claiming DNSSEC is a "firewall":** DNSSEC is a *cryptographic validation* mechanism, NOT a firewall. Examiners penalize this mischaracterization.
> 4. **Ignoring IoT context:** This course is **Ethical Hacking & IoT Security**. Pure DNS answers without IoT relevance (smart cameras, industrial sensors) miss the course's specific focus and may lose up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the math:** For 7-mark derivations, *quantitative* reasoning (as shown in Section 3.1) is often expected. Students who write only prose typically score 4/7 instead of 7/7.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> 📌 **IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (KTU Module 2 — DNS Spoofing):**

- **Definition:** DNS Spoofing = injecting forged DNS responses to corrupt a resolver's cache and redirect users.
- **Core Vulnerability:** The DNS protocol is **stateless** and historically unauthenticated — it trusts the first matching UDP response on port 53.
- **Transaction ID (TxID):** A 16-bit identifier. Probability of correct guess per packet = $\frac{1}{65536}$.
- **Kaminsky Exploit (CVE-2008-1447):** Transforms probabilistic cache poisoning into a deterministic one by exploiting random subdomains and **NS-record poisoning**.
- **Primary Tool:** `ettercap` (with `dns_spoof` plugin), `scapy` (for custom packet crafting), `bettercap`.
- **DNSSEC:** The cryptographic gold standard. Uses **RRSIG, DNSKEY, DS, and NSEC3** records to provide origin authentication and data integrity.
- **DoH (DNS-over-HTTPS):** Encrypts DNS queries in TLS, preventing on-path spoofing.
