In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (course PBCCT604), exploit writing is studied as a **defensive, offensive-security research discipline** — meaning ethical hackers (often called *Red Teamers* or *Vulnerability Researchers*) author exploits in order to: 1. **Prove** that a vulnerability is real (Proof-of-Concept exploitation). 2. **Assess** the actual risk and impact of the vulnerability. 3. **Develop** patches, mitigations, signatures, and detection rules. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PBCCT604 – Module 2):** Exploit writing is the culmination of vulnerability analysis. It sits at the intersection of *reverse engineering*, *assembly language*, *memory corruption research*, and *secure protocol design* — making it the most technically rigorous topic in ethical hacking. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are a **locksmith hired by a bank to test their vault door**. You are not a criminal; you are *certified* and *authorized*. Your job is to: 1. Study the **blueprint of the vault** (static / dynamic analysis of the binary). 2. Identify a *theoretical weakness* — say, a door hinge that bends under **> 500 Newtons** of lateral force (vulnerability analysis). 3. Craft a **tool** — a hydraulic jack — that applies **1,200 N** at exactly the right angle, popping the hinge and opening the door **without destroying the vault's contents** (exploit development). 4. Hand the bank a **report** showing exactly how you got in, so they can upgrade the hinge (responsible disclosure / patching). **Exploit writing is exactly this — but in software.** | Vault Analogy Component | Software Security Equivalent | | :--- | :--- | | Vault blueprint | Source code / disassembled binary | | Hinge weakness | Buffer overflow, format string, use-after-free | | Hydraulic jack | Shellcode + payload (the exploit) | | Bank report | CVE submission + vendor advisory | | Locksmith (you) | Ethical Hacker / Vulnerability Researcher | > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Exploit writing is *not* about *destroying* the target. The **professional exploit author** always ensures the exploit is **reliable, repeatable, and stealthy** — leaving minimal forensic artifacts, just like the locksmith leaves no broken glass. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Definitions in Exploit Writing - **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures):** Globally unique ID assigned to a public vulnerability (e.g., `CVE-2021-44228` — Log4Shell). - **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System):** Severity score from **0.0 to 10.0** based on impact and exploitability. - **PoC (Proof-of-Concept):** Minimal code that demonstrates a vulnerability is exploitable. - **Shellcode:** Position-independent machine code (typically in assembly) injected as a payload, traditionally spawning a command shell. - **NOP Sled (No-Operation Slide):** A sequence of `0x90` bytes that "slides" execution into the actual shellcode when the exact address is unknown. - **ROP (Return-Oriented Programming):** A modern, **NX/DEP-bypass** technique that chains together existing code "gadgets" ending in `ret` instructions. - **ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization):** Mitigation that randomizes memory addresses, making exploitation harder. - **DEP/NX (Data Execution Prevention / No-Execute):** Mitigation that marks stack/heap memory as non-executable. > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Examiners frequently test whether students can **distinguish between a vulnerability and an exploit**. A *vulnerability* is the *flaw*; an *exploit* is the *weaponized code that abuses the flaw*. Confusing these two terms guarantees a loss of marks. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Exploit Development Lifecycle (Conceptual Framework) Professional exploit writing follows a rigorous, repeatable lifecycle. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to enumerate, in order, the following seven phases: ### Phase 1 — Vulnerability Discovery / Intelligence Gathering - Source code auditing (white-box), fuzzing (black-box), or hybrid taint analysis. - Tools: **AFL++**, **LibFuzzer**, **Burp Suite**, manual code review. - Output: A confirmed *bug* (e.g., a `strcpy` with no bounds check). ### Phase 2 — Vulnerability Analysis & Triage - Determine **exploitability**: is the bug reachable? does it corrupt memory? is there a control-flow hijack primitive? - Assign **CVSS v3.1** base score. - Tools: **GDB**, **WinDbg**, **IDA Pro**, **Ghidra**, **Binary Ninja**. ### Phase 3 — Reverse Engineering - Disassemble the vulnerable binary. - Identify the **function prologue**, **stack frame layout**, and **control-flow graph (CFG)**. - Locate gadgets (for ROP), IAT entries, and any anti-debug checks. ### Phase 4 — Exploit Primitive Development - Build the memory-corruption primitive. For a stack-based buffer overflow, this means controlling the **Instruction Pointer (EIP/RIP)** through overwriting the saved return address. ### Phase 5 — Payload (Shellcode) Construction - Write or generate the shellcode (often via **msfvenom**, **pwntools**, or hand-crafted assembly). - Apply **encoders** to evade bad-character filters (e.g., null bytes, newlines). - For modern targets, build **ROP chains** to bypass NX/DEP. ### Phase 6 — Reliability & Evasion Engineering - Convert the PoC into a **reliable, position-independent** exploit. - Bypass **ASLR** via info leaks, **DEP** via ROP, **Stack Canaries** via brute-force or leaks, and **CFI** via creative gadget selection. - Add **anti-forensic / anti-sandbox** features only when legally and ethically justified (pentest engagements). ### Phase 7 — Documentation & Responsible Disclosure - Write a clear advisory. - Submit **CVE** via MITRE or directly to the vendor. - Coordinate **public disclosure** timeline (commonly **90 days** post-notification — Google Project Zero's standard). --- ## 2.2 Classification of Exploits (High-Yield for KTU) | Class of Exploit | Memory Region Abused | Typical Bug Type | Modern Mitigation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Stack Buffer Overflow** | Stack | `strcpy`, `gets`, `sprintf` overrun | Stack canary, NX, ASLR | | **Heap Overflow / UAF** | Heap | `free()` use-after-free, double-free | Heap metadata hardening, CFI | | **Format String** | Stack/Heap | `printf(user_input)` | Compiler warnings, FORTIFY_SOURCE | | **Integer Overflow** | Arithmetic | Signed/unsigned wraparound | Sanitizers (UBSan) | | **Race Condition / TOCTOU** | File system, kernel | Time-of-check to time-of-use | Locking primitives | | **ROP / JOP / COP** | Code segment | Reuse of existing gadgets | CFI, CET (Intel CET, ARM BTI) | | **Web Exploit (SQLi/XSS)** | Application tier | Improper input sanitization | WAF, parameterization | | **IoT-Specific Exploit** | Firmware / UART / JTAG | Hard-coded keys, open ports | Secure boot, signed firmware | --- ## 2.3 Memory Layout Refresher (Critical for Stack-Based Exploits) A 32-bit Linux process memory layout (relevant to classic stack overflow teaching): ``` [ Low Address 0x00000000 ] | .text (code, RX) <- shellcode injected here if NX disabled | .rodata (read-only data) | .data (initialized RW) | .bss (uninitialized RW) | Heap (grows UP towards high addresses) | ... (unmapped) | Stack (grows DOWN towards low addresses) <- overflow buffer lives here [ High Address 0xFFFFFFFF ] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why does the stack grow downward?** Historically so that the **heap and stack can share the same address space and grow toward each other** without prior knowledge of program size, maximizing RAM utilization on early UNIX systems. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **The following table is the most important rapid-revision tool for KTU university exams on this topic.** Memorize the *relationships* (not the literal values) — they are how examiners frame 14-mark "design an exploit for X" questions. | Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Engineering Utility | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Buffer offset to EIP** | $D = A_{buf} - A_{ret}$ | Distance (bytes) from buffer start to saved return address | Tells you how many padding bytes to send | | **NOP sled length** | $L_{NOP} \geq \sigma$ | NOP length $\geq$ address-jitter $\sigma$ | Compensates for ASLR / imprecise jumps | | **CVSS Base Score** | $S = f(AV, AC, PR, UI, S, C, I, A)$ | Weighted function of 8 base metrics | Industry-standard severity rating | | **Exploit reliability** | $R = \dfrac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Success percentage across $N$ runs | Measures PoC $\rightarrow$ weaponized exploit maturity | | **Stack canary detection probability** | $P_{detect} = 1 - 2^{-k}$ | $k$ = canary byte randomness | Forces attacker to **leak** the canary first | | **Entropy of ASLR (32-bit)** | $H \approx 8 \text{ bits}$ (stack), $H \approx 8 \text{ bits}$ (libc) | Bits of randomness in base address | Why 32-bit ASLR is brute-forceable | | **Entropy of ASLR (64-bit)** | $H \approx 28 \text{ bits}$ (Linux mmap) | Far stronger, brute-force impractical | Mandates info-leak primitive | | **Shellcode size budget** | $\vert s \vert \leq B$ | Payload size $\leq$ buffer/frame size $B$ | Drives choice of *connect-back* vs *bind* shell | --- ## 2.5 Why Exploit Writing Matters in Engineering (Real-World Utility) 1. **Defense & Hardening:** Every major OS hardening feature (DEP, ASLR, CFI, Stack Canary) was created *because* someone wrote an exploit first. 2. **IoT Security:** Hard-coded credentials, unsigned firmware, and exposed UART/JTAG on embedded devices are routinely weaponized by botnets (e.g., **Mirai**). Exploit-writing skills underpin IoT penetration testing. 3. **Bug Bounty Industry:** Platforms like **HackerOne** and **Bugcrowd** paid out over **$300 million** cumulatively to ethical exploit authors (2020–2024 data). 4. **National Security & CTF:** Government agencies and Capture-The-Flag competitions (DEF CON, PlaidCTF) employ exploit writing as the *gold standard* of offensive capability. 5. **Firmware / SCADA Security:** Critical infrastructure exploits (e.g., **Stuxnet**, **Industroyer**) demonstrated that exploit-writing expertise is essential for protecting power grids and industrial control systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Linkage:** This topic directly maps to **CO2** of PBCCT604 — *"Apply vulnerability analysis techniques to design exploits and recommend mitigations for systems and IoT devices."* When you write exam answers, **always end with the mitigation recommendation** — this is where KTU examiners award the "Apply / Evaluate" Bloom's-level marks. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Classic Stack-Based Buffer Overflow (Linux x86, No Protections) > [!NOTE] > The following derivation is the **canonical "stack overflow" scenario** that appears in almost every KTU question paper as either a 7-mark or 14-mark question. We assume a *deliberately vulnerable* C program compiled with `gcc -fno-stack-protector -z execstack -m32 -g vuln.c` — i.e., **all protections OFF** — purely for teaching. Real-world exploitation is far more complex. ### Step 1: The Vulnerable Source Code ```c // vuln.c (for teaching only — DO NOT deploy) #include #include void win(void) { printf("Congratulations! You have hijacked control flow.\n"); // In a real exploit: execve("/bin/sh", ...) shellcode } void vulnerable(char *input) { char buf[64]; // 64-byte stack buffer strcpy(buf, input); // <- unbounded copy (the bug) } int main(int argc, char **argv) { if (argc != 2) { fprintf(stderr, "Usage: %s \n", argv[0]); return 1; } vulnerable(argv[1]); return 0; } ``` ### Step 2: Identify the Stack Frame Geometry When `vulnerable()` is called, the stack frame (low-to-high address order) is: ``` | ... main's frame above ... | +----------------------------------+ | saved EBP (4 bytes) | <-- frame pointer (EBP) +----------------------------------+ | return addr (4 bytes) | <-- TARGET: overwrite this +----------------------------------+ | buf[63] | | ... | | buf[0] (64 bytes total) | <-- strcpy writes here +----------------------------------+ | ... lower addresses ... | ``` ### Step 3: Determine the Offset to EIP (Symbolic Derivation) Let: - $B = 64$ bytes = size of `buf`. - $S_{EBP} = 4$ bytes = saved EBP (32-bit). - $A_{ret}$ = address of the saved return address. - $A_{buf}$ = address of `buf[0]`. By the geometric stack layout: $$ A_{ret} = A_{buf} + B + S_{EBP} $$ The **number of padding bytes** $D$ the attacker must send *before* overwriting the return address is therefore: $$ D = B + S_{EBP} = 64 + 4 = 68 \text{ bytes} $$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** After exactly **68 bytes** of junk input, the next 4 bytes the attacker writes will land **on the saved return address** and be popped into **EIP** when `vulnerable()` executes `ret`. ### Step 4: Verify the Offset Empirically (Conceptual Pattern) The standard KTU-style pattern is to send a **cyclic pattern** of unique 4-byte de Bruijn sequences as input. A crash will reveal the value of EIP at fault, which can be looked up in the pattern to compute $D$ exactly. (Tools: **GDB**, **pwntools** `cyclic`/`cyclic_find`.) This step validates the geometric derivation. ### Step 5: Construct the Payload (Symbolic + Binary Form) The final exploit payload $P$ has the symbolic form: $$ P = \underbrace{\text{pad}}_{D \text{ bytes}} \; \Vert \; \underbrace{\text{new\_EIP}}_{4 \text{ bytes}} \; \Vert \; \underbrace{\text{shellcode}}_{\vert s \vert \text{ bytes}} $$ Where: - $\text{pad}$ = 68 bytes of non-destructive filler (e.g., `'A' * 68`). - $\text{new\_EIP}$ = the **little-endian** 4-byte address we want to redirect execution to. Choices: - $A_{win}$ = address of `win()` (for educational PoC). - $A_{buf}$ = address of `buf[0]` (for real shellcode execution, since `-z execstack` makes the stack executable). - $\text{shellcode}$ = position-independent machine code. For a *bind-shell* or *connect-back* shell. ### Step 6: Little-Endian Encoding Worked Example Suppose $A_{win} = 0x080491A6$. The 4 raw bytes the attacker must place at offset $D = 68$ are written in **little-endian** order: $$ \text{new\_EIP bytes} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{A6} & \text{91} & \text{04} & \text{08} \end{bmatrix} $$ That is, the **least significant byte (0xA6) is stored first** at the lowest address, because the x86 architecture reads multi-byte values least-significant-byte-first. > [!WARNING] > **Most Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write the address in big-endian form (`08 04 91 A6`) and are puzzled when the exploit crashes. Always verify endianness — *x86 is little-endian, network byte order is big-endian*. ### Step 7: Full Exploit Expression (Symbolic End-to-End) $$ P = \underbrace{\text{0x41} \times 68}_{\text{padding}} \; \Vert \; \underbrace{\text{0xA6, 0x91, 0x04, 0x08}}_{\text{new EIP} \to win()} \; \Vert \; \underbrace{\text{shellcode}}_{\text{position-independent}} $$ ### Step 8: Reliability Calculation For a network-based exploit over a lossy channel, the reliability $R$ is empirically: $$ R = \frac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% $$ For a perfect local PoC with all mitigations disabled: $R = 100\%$. For a real-world exploit against an ASLR-protected binary without an info-leak primitive: $R \approx 0\%$ (the NOP sled + brute-force can be used, but is rarely reliable on 64-bit). ### Step 9: Mitigation Mapping (Mandatory KTU Exam Closure) | Mitigation | Defeats Which Exploit Step? | Bypass Strategy | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Stack Canary (`-fstack-protector`)** | Phase 4 — overwriting return address | Brute-force (32-bit), leak via info-disclosure bug | | **NX / DEP (`-z execstack` off)** | Phase 5 — executing shellcode on stack | ROP, JOP, ret2libc, mprotect syscall | | **ASLR (`-fpie -pie`, `randomize_va_space=2`)** | Phase 5 — predicting code/data addresses | Info-leak, partial overwrite, brute-force (32-bit) | | **CFI (Clang `-fsanitize=cfi`)** | Phase 4–5 — invalid control transfers | Type confusion, COOP, speculative execution abuse | | **Integer Overflow Sanitizer (UBSan)** | Phase 1 — silent wraparound bugs | Compiled-out at runtime in production builds | ### Step 10: KTU Analytical Matrix — Mapping Question Type to Answer Structure | Question Wording | Expected Answer Length | Key Elements Examiner Looks For | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | "Explain the exploit development lifecycle." | 7 marks | All 7 phases in order, 1-line tool/example per phase | | "Differentiate between vulnerability, exploit, and payload." | 3 marks | Crisp 3-bullet table; CVSS / CVE reference | | "Demonstrate a buffer overflow exploit for a given C program." | 14 marks | Code + stack frame diagram + offset derivation + payload + mitigation | | "How does ASLR defeat a stack overflow?" | 7 marks | Explain entropy, mention 32 vs 64 bit, mention info-leak bypass | | "Discuss ROP as a DEP bypass technique." | 7 marks | Define gadget, give example chain, mention CET countermeasure | | "IoT-specific exploit vectors and mitigations." | 14 marks | UART/JTAG, hard-coded creds, unsigned firmware, Mirai case study, secure boot mitigation | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Exploit Development Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phase 1: Vulnerability Discovery
Fuzzing / Code Review / CVE Intel] --> B[Phase 2: Triage and Analysis
GDB / IDA / Ghidra] B --> C[Phase 3: Reverse Engineering
Disassembly / CFG / Gadget ID] C --> D[Phase 4: Exploit Primitive
Control EIP RIP / Hijack CFG] D --> E[Phase 5: Payload Construction
Shellcode / ROP Chain / Encoder] E --> F[Phase 6: Reliability and Evasion
Bypass ASLR DEP Canary CFI] F --> G{Exploit Reliable
and Reproducible?} G -- No --> D G -- Yes --> H[Phase 7: Responsible Disclosure
CVE Submission / Vendor Patch] H --> I[Post-Patch: Validate Mitigation
Re-test / Update Signatures] I --> J[Archive and Lessons Learned] classDef phase fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef gate fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef terminal fill:#064e3b,stroke:#34d399,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff class A,B,C,D,E,F,H phase class G gate class I,J terminal ``` > **Reading Guide:** Boxes 1–7 are sequential **phases** (blue). The orange diamond is the **gating decision** — if reliability is not yet acceptable, loop back to primitive development. Green boxes represent **post-exploitation hygiene**, which is what separates an *ethical* exploit author from a malicious one. --- ## 4.2 Stack Frame Block Diagram — Pre- and Post-Overflow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Pre["BEFORE OVERFLOW (normal strcpy)"] direction TB P1["main() return address"] P2["saved EBP (4 bytes)"] P3["buf[63]"] P4["buf[62]"] P5["... (60 more bytes) ..."] P6["buf[0] <- strcpy writes here"] end subgraph Post["AFTER OVERFLOW (attacker payload)"] direction TB Q1["main() return address = 0xDEADBEEF (overwritten!)"] Q2["saved EBP = 'BBBB' (corrupted)"] Q3["buf[63] = NOP 0x90"] Q4["buf[62] = NOP 0x90"] Q5["... NOP SLED ... (60 bytes)"] Q6["buf[0] = NOP 0x90"] end Pre -. "strcpy of 100+ bytes" .-> Post classDef beforeNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff classDef afterNode fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,color:#ffffff class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6 beforeNode class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4,Q5,Q6 afterNode ``` > **Engineering Reading:** The **red column** shows what an attacker *sees and controls* after a successful overflow. Notice how the **saved return address is the highest address on the stack** — that is why the NOP sled (or direct overwrite) lands *at the end* of the payload, not the beginning. --- ## 4.3 ROP Chain Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Attacker Buffer] -->|pop| B[ROP Gadget 1
pop rdi; ret] B --> C[Argument Value
address of /bin/sh string] C --> D[ROP Gadget 2
pop rsi; ret] D --> E[Argument Value
NULL pointer] E --> F[ROP Gadget 3
pop rdx; ret] F --> G[Argument Value
NULL pointer] G --> H[Address of PLT Entry
execve@libc] H --> I[Kernel Syscall Dispatched
shell spawned] classDef gadget fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,color:#ffffff classDef arg fill:#581c87,stroke:#c084fc,color:#ffffff classDef pivot fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,color:#ffffff classDef sink fill:#064e3b,stroke:#34d399,color:#ffffff class A,B,D,F gadget class C,E,G arg class H pivot class I sink ``` > **Why this works under DEP/NX:** Every blue/purple/orange block is **already executable code** in the binary or its libraries. The attacker only *chains* their return addresses — never writes new executable memory. This is the *exact* reason CET (Control-flow Enforcement Technology) was designed: to detect and break ROP chains by validating that `ret` targets land on legitimate function entries, not mid-instruction gadgets. --- ## 4.4 IoT-Specific Exploit Attack-Surface Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Surface["IoT Device Attack Surface"] S1[Network Interfaces
Wi-Fi / BLE / Zigbee / Cellular] S2[Cloud APIs
REST / MQTT / CoAP] S3[Mobile Companion App
Android / iOS] S4[Web Admin Console
often default creds] S5[Local Hardware
UART / JTAG / SPI] S6[Firmware Image
unencrypted / unsigned] end S1 --> E1[Exploit 1: Protocol Fuzzing
MQTT / CoAP parser bugs] S2 --> E2[Exploit 2: API Auth Bypass
Broken Object Level Authz] S3 --> E3[Exploit 3: Hard-coded API Key
Extracted from APK] S4 --> E4[Exploit 4: Default Credentials
admin:admin] S5 --> E5[Exploit 5: UART Shell Access
Root shell via debug port] S6 --> E6[Exploit 6: Firmware Extraction
Squashfs / Binwalk dump] E1 --> C[Compromise] E2 --> C E3 --> C E4 --> C E5 --> C E6 --> C C --> M{Mitigations} M --> M1[Secure Boot and Signed Firmware] M --> M2[Disable JTAG UART in Production] M --> M3[Per-Device Unique Credentials] M --> M4[Network Segmentation and VLAN] M --> M5[Regular Firmware OTA Updates] classDef surface fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff classDef exploit fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,color:#ffffff classDef miti fill:#064e3b,stroke:#34d399,color:#ffffff class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6 surface class E1,E2,E3,E4,E5,E6,C exploit class M,M1,M2,M3,M4,M5 miti ``` > **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** This exact "attack-surface-then-mitigation" structure is the **highest-scoring 14-mark answer format** for any "Discuss IoT security" question. The diagram demonstrates CO2 (Apply) and CO4 (Analyze/Evaluate) attainment in a single figure. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Differentiate between a *vulnerability*, an *exploit*, and a *payload* (shellcode).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Term | Definition (1 mark each) | | :--- | :--- | | **Vulnerability** | A *flaw or weakness* in software, hardware, or configuration that *could* be abused to violate the system's security policy. Example: an unbounded `strcpy` in a C program. Identified by a **CVE** ID. | | **Exploit** | The *weaponized code, input, or sequence of actions* that *actively abuses* a vulnerability to achieve an attacker objective (e.g., code execution, info disclosure). Example: a Python/assembly script that sends 100 bytes to overflow `buf`. | | **Payload / Shellcode** | The *attacker-supplied code* that runs *after* the exploit has successfully hijacked control flow. It performs the actual malicious action: spawning a shell, downloading malware, exfiltrating data. Example: `execve("/bin/sh")` in x86 assembly. | > The vulnerability is the *lock*, the exploit is the *lockpick*, the payload is what the thief *does* once the door is open. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **What is a NOP sled and why is it used in exploit writing?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A **NOP sled** is a sequence of **NOP** (No-Operation, opcode `0x90` on x86) instructions placed immediately *before* the actual shellcode in a buffer-overflow payload. (1 mark) - Its purpose is to **increase the probability of successful execution** when the attacker does not know the *exact* address of the shellcode in memory — a common situation under **ASLR** or imprecise pointer arithmetic. (1 mark) - If execution lands *anywhere* inside the NOP sled, the CPU "slides" through the NOPs (which do nothing) and eventually reaches the shellcode at the end, executing it. (1 mark) > Effective sled length $L_{NOP}$ should exceed the address jitter $\sigma$ of the target: $L_{NOP} \geq \sigma$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each module typically offers *one* 14-mark question with internal choice (a) or (b). The complete solution to a 14-mark question must include a **labeled diagram**, **derivation where applicable**, **example**, and **concluding mitigation paragraph**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Module 2 Choice A]** **(a)** With the help of a labeled stack-frame diagram, explain the **complete exploit development lifecycle** for a stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in a 32-bit Linux binary with **all protections disabled**. Your answer must include the offset calculation $D = B + S_{EBP}$, payload construction $P = \text{pad} \Vert \text{new\_EIP} \Vert \text{shellcode}$, and the corresponding mitigation strategy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe **Return-Oriented Programming (ROP)** as a technique for bypassing **DEP/NX**. In your answer, define a *gadget*, draw a ROP chain that invokes `execve("/bin/sh", NULL, NULL)`, and explain how **Intel CET** attempts to defeat ROP. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks] 1. **(1 mark) Lifecycle overview:** State all 7 phases — Discovery → Triage → Reverse Engineering → Primitive Development → Payload Construction → Reliability Engineering → Disclosure. 2. **(2 marks) Stack-frame diagram** (must be drawn, see Section 4.2 of these notes). The diagram must clearly label: - `buf[0..63]` (64 bytes). - Saved EBP (4 bytes). - Return address (4 bytes) — *the target of the overwrite*. - Arrow showing `strcpy` writing from low to high addresses. 3. **(2 marks) Offset derivation:** - $B = 64$, $S_{EBP} = 4$. - $D = B + S_{EBP} = 68$ bytes of padding required *before* the return address overwrite. - Justify: by the x86 stack-growth convention and the saved-EBP slot between `buf` and the return address. 4. **(1 mark) Payload construction:** - $P = \text{0x41} \times 68 \; \Vert \; \text{little-endian}(A_{target}) \; \Vert \; \text{shellcode}$. - Note: little-endian encoding of EIP (e.g., `0x080491A6` $\to$ bytes `A6 91 04 08`). 5. **(1 mark) Mitigation closure:** - Enable **Stack Canary** (`-fstack-protector-strong`). - Enable **NX** (drop `-z execstack`). - Enable **ASLR** (`randomize_va_space=2`). - Enable **PIE** (`-pie -fPIE`). - Use **FORTIFY_SOURCE** and **address sanitizers** in development. --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks] 1. **(1 mark) Define ROP:** ROP is a code-reuse technique that chains together short instruction sequences (gadgets) already present in the binary or its linked libraries, each ending in a `ret` instruction, to perform arbitrary computation **without injecting new executable code**. This is the *only* way to bypass DEP/NX in modern exploits. 2. **(1 mark) Define gadget:** A *gadget* is a short sequence of instructions ending in `ret` (opcode `0xC3`). Example: `pop rdi ; ret` (5 bytes total) — pops the next 8 bytes from the stack into `RDI` (the first syscall argument on x86-64). 3. **(3 marks) ROP chain diagram** (see Section 4.3 of these notes). The chain must: - Set `RDI = &"/bin/sh"`. - Set `RSI = 0` (NULL argv). - Set `RDX = 0` (NULL envp). - Jump to the PLT entry of `execve` (or a `syscall` gadget with `RAX = 59`). - Result: shell spawned with no injected executable code. 4. **(1 mark) Intel CET countermeasure:** Control-flow Enforcement Technology introduces **Shadow Stack** (a hardware-protected second copy of return addresses) and **IBT (Indirect Branch Tracking)**. When `ret` pops an address, the CPU compares it against the shadow stack — any mismatch causes a `#CP` exception, killing the ROP chain. ARM offers an analogous feature called **BTI (Branch Target Identification)**. 5. **(1 mark) Modern bypass research:** Mention that researchers have published *speculative execution* and *gadget synthesis* techniques (COOP, SROP, JIT-ROP) that attempt to defeat CET — illustrating the **arms race** between offense and defense. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Module 2 Choice B (Alternative)]** **(a)** Discuss the **IoT-specific attack surface** that an ethical hacker must consider when auditing a smart-home device. Include at least **four distinct vectors** (e.g., firmware, hardware debug ports, cloud APIs, default credentials) and their corresponding mitigations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **ASLR mitigation** in detail. Cover the following in your answer: (i) the memory regions randomized, (ii) the entropy budget on 32-bit vs 64-bit Linux, (iii) how an attacker can bypass ASLR using an *information-leak* vulnerability, and (iv) the reliability formula $R = N_{success}/N_{total} \times 100\%$ for a brute-force bypass. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks] **(2 marks — diagram + setup):** Draw the IoT attack-surface block diagram (see Section 4.4). **(4 marks — four vectors, 1 mark each):** 1. **Unsigned / Unencrypted Firmware:** The attacker downloads the firmware (often a public URL), unzips it with `binwalk`, and extracts a root filesystem (e.g., Squashfs). Hard-coded credentials, private keys, and backdoor accounts are routinely found this way. *Mitigation:* Signed firmware, secure boot, encrypted at rest. 2. **Exposed UART / JTAG / SPI Debug Ports:** Many IoT PCBs leave 4-pin UART headers on the production board, providing a root shell with no authentication. *Mitigation:* Disable or remove debug headers in production; enable secure-boot fuses. 3. **Hard-Coded / Default Credentials:** The infamous Mirai botnet (2016) scanned the entire IPv4 Internet and successfully breached **> 600,000 devices** using a dictionary of **60+ default username/password pairs** (e.g., `admin:admin`, `root:xc3511`). *Mitigation:* Per-device unique credentials enforced at first-boot setup. 4. **Insecure Cloud APIs (Broken Object Level Authorization):** The mobile companion app often sends a request like `GET /api/v1/devices/{deviceID}/unlock` with an *unauthenticated or weakly-authenticated* token — an attacker can iterate device IDs. *Mitigation:* OAuth 2.0 with per-device scoped tokens, server-side authorization checks. **(1 mark — closing):** Add a one-line summary emphasizing **defense-in-depth**: no single mitigation is sufficient, and *secure-by-design* is the KTU-recommended architectural principle. --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks] 1. **(1 mark) What ASLR randomizes:** ASLR randomizes the base addresses of the **stack**, **heap**, **mmap region** (where shared libraries are loaded), and the **executable itself** (when compiled with PIE). On Linux, this is controlled by `/proc/sys/kernel/randomize_va_space` (0=off, 1=conservative, 2=full). 2. **(1 mark) Entropy comparison:** - 32-bit Linux stack: $H \approx 8$ bits (256 possible positions) $\to$ **brute-forceable in seconds.** - 32-bit libc mmap: $H \approx 8$ bits. - 64-bit Linux mmap: $H \approx 28$ bits $\to$ **brute-force impractical.** - 64-bit PIE binary: $H \approx 28$ bits. 3. **(2 marks) Info-leak bypass:** An attacker first triggers a separate, *less-severe* vulnerability that discloses a memory address — e.g., a format-string bug (`%p %p %p`) or a use-after-free that lets them read a freed pointer. The leaked address is used to **defeat the randomization** for the second-stage exploit. This is why **even "minor" info-disclosure bugs are rated critical** in CVSS scoring. 4. **(2 marks) Brute-force reliability:** For a 32-bit target, an attacker can re-run the exploit in a loop, hoping the child process (e.g., a `fork()`-based network daemon) re-uses a different ASLR layout. Reliability is: $$ R = \frac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% $$ For $H = 8$ bits, expected $N_{total} \approx 128$ attempts to achieve 50% success (geometric distribution). For $H = 28$ bits, $N_{total} \approx 2^{27} \approx 1.34 \times 10^{8}$ — infeasible. 5. **(1 mark) Modern counter-countermeasures:** Mention that **Linux v5.18+** added per-`mm_struct` randomization that defeats fork-based brute-forcing, and that **ARMv8.5-A MTE** (Memory Tagging Extension) provides probabilistic heap safety. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 reasons KTU students lose marks on Exploit Writing questions:** > > 1. **Confusing vulnerability with exploit.** The *flaw* is the vulnerability; the *weaponized code* is the exploit. Examiners deduct 1 mark immediately if these terms are swapped. > 2. **Forgetting little-endian byte order** when writing out the EIP-overwrite bytes for a buffer overflow. This is the #1 source of "my exploit doesn't work" answers on paper. > 3. **Omitting the diagram.** A 14-mark exploit question *without* a labeled stack frame is capped at 10 marks — diagrams are non-negotiable. > 4. **No mitigation paragraph.** Always close with the corresponding defense (ASLR, NX, canary, CFI, CET, secure boot, signed firmware). This is the "Apply / Evaluate" Bloom's-level mark. > 5. **Mixing up NOP sled and shellcode.** A NOP sled is `0x90` filler; shellcode is the *actual payload*. Examiners will mark these as separate items in your answer. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Final 60-Second Revision Block — Read This Before the Exam.** - ☐ **Vulnerability ≠ Exploit ≠ Payload.** Memorize the distinction (1 mark saved on every Q). - ☐ The **exploit development lifecycle** has **7 phases**: Discovery → Triage → Reverse Engineering → Primitive → Payload → Reliability → Disclosure. - ☐ The **stack frame** grows *downward*; the buffer is at the *bottom*; the return address is at the *top*. Offset $D = B + S_{EBP}$. - ☐ x86 is **little-endian** — write EIP bytes **LSB-first**. - ☐ **NOP sled** (`0x90` × $L_{NOP}$) compensates for address uncertainty; $L_{NOP} \geq \sigma$. - ☐ **DEP/NX** is bypassed by **ROP** (code reuse via `ret` gadgets). **Intel CET / ARM BTI** defeats ROP via shadow stack / branch-target identification. - ☐ **ASLR entropy**: 32-bit $\approx 8$ bits (brute-forceable); 64-bit $\approx 28$ bits (infeasible). Bypass requires an **info-leak** primitive. - ☐ **CVSS** = 0.0–10.0 industry-standard severity score; **CVE** = globally unique vulnerability ID. - ☐ **IoT exploit vectors** (Module 2, KTU): unsigned firmware, exposed UART/JTAG, hard-coded credentials, insecure cloud APIs, default creds (Mirai case study). - ☐ **Defenses** to always mention in mitigation paragraph: stack canary, NX, ASLR, PIE, CFI, CET, secure boot, signed firmware, network segmentation, per-device credentials. - ☐ **Reliability formula**: $R = N_{success} / N_{total} \times 100\%$. - ☐ **Reverse engineering tools** to know by name: **GDB, Ghidra, IDA Pro, Binary Ninja, WinDbg, pwntools, radare2**. - ☐ **Fuzzing tools**: **AFL++, LibFuzzer, Boofuzz** — these drive Phase 1 of the lifecycle. - ☐ **CVE-2021-44228 (Log4Shell)** and **Mirai (2016)** are excellent 14-mark case-study anchors. - ☐ **Always close** an exploit question with a **mitigation recommendation** — this earns the Bloom's *Apply/Evaluate* marks. - ☐ **Anti-Forensics / Anti-Sandbox** techniques are *out of syllabus* for KTU 2024 — do not waste time on them. ---

topics.title: Buffer Overflow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Buffer Overflow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Buffer Overflow** is a critical software vulnerability (classified under **CWE-120** in the Common Weakness Enumeration) that occurs when a program writes data beyond the allocated boundaries of a fixed-size memory buffer. The excess data overwrites adjacent memory locations, corrupting valid data, crashing the program, or enabling arbitrary code execution by a malicious actor.

In the context of **Ethical Hacking**, a buffer overflow is exploited during the **Exploitation (Gaining Access)** phase of the Cyber Kill Chain to subvert program control flow by overwriting the **return address** on the program stack with an address pointing to attacker-supplied **shellcode**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Buffer overflow is treated as a foundational vulnerability under the broader theme of "Vulnerability Analysis & Exploitation." Students must understand the difference between **Stack-based**, **Heap-based**, and **Format String** buffer overflows, alongside corresponding mitigation strategies (Stack Canaries, DEP, ASLR).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Overfilled Water Glass"

Imagine a row of water glasses arranged on a shelf:

- **Glass 1** = Your allocated buffer (e.g., a 64-byte character array).
- **Glass 2 (next to it)** = Adjacent memory containing critical variables (e.g., a "user is admin" flag, or the function's return address).
- **Water poured in** = The input data supplied by the user.

If a user pours **100 mL** into a glass that only holds **64 mL**, the overflow spills into Glass 2. A buffer overflow attack is analogous to deliberately over-pouring water to **flip a switch** (e.g., the admin flag becomes TRUE) or **redirect a railway track** (overwrite the return address to point to attacker code).

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Buffer Overflow Vulnerabilities

| Vulnerability Type | Memory Region Targeted | Attack Surface | Severity (CVSS) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stack-Based Buffer Overflow** | Program Call Stack | Local arrays, function buffers | **Critical (9.8)** |
| **Heap-Based Buffer Overflow** | Dynamic Heap Memory | `malloc()`/`new` allocations | **High (8.1)** |
| **Format String Attack** | Stack (format specifiers) | `printf(user_input)` misuse | **High (7.5)** |
| **Integer Overflow** | Arithmetic registers | Math operations on bounded ints | **Medium (6.5)** |
| **Unicode Buffer Overflow** | Stack (wide chars) | `wchar_t` arrays | **High (7.8)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **stack-based buffer overflow** is the most academically emphasized variant in KTU Module 2 because it is the classic vehicle for the "**Smashing the Stack for Fun and Profit**" exploit paradigm introduced by Aleph One (1996).

## 1.4 Why Buffer Overflows Remain Relevant in 2024+

Despite decades of mitigation research, buffer overflows persist because:

1. **Legacy Codebase**: A massive amount of C/C++ code in OS kernels, IoT firmware, and embedded systems still uses unsafe functions like `strcpy()`, `gets()`, and `sprintf()`.
2. **Memory-Safety Trade-offs**: Performance-critical systems (RTOS, microcontrollers in IoT) often disable safety features like ASLR and stack canaries to save **CPU cycles and ROM footprint**.
3. **Off-by-One Errors**: Even a single-byte overflow can hijack control flow when targeting function pointers or vtable pointers in C++.

> [!WARNING]
> **IoT Security Implication:** Approximately **70% of IoT device breaches** involve memory corruption bugs, with buffer overflows being the dominant class. The Mirai botnet (2016) exploited buffer overflows in DVRs and home routers to recruit over **600,000 devices** into a DDoS army.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Process Memory Architecture — The Foundation

To understand buffer overflows, one must first understand the **virtual memory layout** of a Linux/Windows process. The address space is divided into distinct regions:

| Memory Region (High → Low Address) | Purpose | Growth Direction | Protection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Kernel Space** | OS Kernel, device drivers | Reserved | Ring 0 / Supervisor |
| **Stack** | Local variables, return addresses, frames | Grows **downward** (toward 0x00000000) | RW, NX (with DEP) |
| **Memory Mapping Segment** | `mmap()` regions, shared libraries, heap | Grows **upward** | RW, NX |
| **Heap** | Dynamic allocations (`malloc`, `new`) | Grows **upward** | RW, NX |
| **BSS Segment** | Uninitialized global/static variables | Fixed | RW |
| **Data Segment** | Initialized global/static variables | Fixed | RW |
| **Text Segment** | Executable machine code (`.text`) | Fixed (Read-Only) | **R-X** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The **Stack grows downward** (from high to low addresses), while the **Heap grows upward**. This is the central reason why a stack-based buffer overflow can overwrite the saved return address — the buffer sits at a *lower* address than the return address, so overflowing it upward climbs toward the return address.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Stack Frame

When a function is called, the CPU pushes a **Stack Frame (Activation Record)** containing the following layout (addresses increase upward):

| Offset from EBP | Content | Size (x86) | Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `EBP + 8` | Function Arguments | 4 bytes each | Passed-in parameters |
| `EBP + 4` | **Return Address (RET)** | **4 bytes** | Where `EIP` jumps after `RET` |
| `EBP + 0` | **Saved EBP (SFP)** | **4 bytes** | Previous frame pointer |
| `EBP - 4` | Local variable 1 | 4 bytes | e.g., `int x` |
| `EBP - 8` | Local variable 2 | 4 bytes | e.g., `int y` |
| `EBP - n` | **Buffer (vulnerable)** | n bytes | e.g., `char buf[64]` |

> [!NOTE]
> The classic exploit targets the `EBP + 4` slot (the **return address**). By writing past the buffer's boundary, the attacker overwrites the return address with a pointer to their shellcode.

## 2.3 The Mechanics of a Stack Smashing Attack — Step-by-Step

A stack-based buffer overflow attack follows a precise 7-step sequence:

1. **Identify the Vulnerability**: Find a function that copies user input into a fixed-size stack buffer without bounds checking (e.g., `strcpy(buf, user_input)`).
2. **Determine Buffer Size**: Use fuzzing or static analysis to learn the buffer's length (e.g., **64 bytes**).
3. **Locate the Return Address Offset**: Compute the exact number of padding bytes required to reach the saved EIP on the stack (typically `buffer_size + 8` for x86).
4. **Craft Malicious Payload**: Construct an input string structured as:  
   `[NOP Sled] + [Shellcode] + [Padding] + [New Return Address]`
5. **Bypass Memory Protections** (if any): Address the DEP (NX bit), ASLR, and stack canaries with techniques like `ret2libc` or Return-Oriented Programming (ROP).
6. **Deliver the Payload**: Send the crafted input to the vulnerable program via the network, file input, or command-line argument.
7. **Hijack Control Flow**: When the function executes `RET`, the CPU pops the overwritten address into **EIP**, redirecting execution to the attacker's shellcode.

## 2.4 The "Why" — Why the CPU Behaves This Way

The x86 `RET` instruction is implemented in hardware as:

$$EIP \leftarrow M[ESP]$$
$$ESP \leftarrow ESP + 4$$

Where `M[ESP]` is the 4-byte value at the top of the stack. There is **no integrity check** on this value. If the attacker can write an arbitrary 4-byte value at the `ESP` position when `RET` executes, they fully control the next instruction executed by the CPU. This is the fundamental "Why" behind the exploit.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet (Cheat Table)

| Concept | Formal Definition | Typical Value / Unit | Exploit Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Buffer Size** | Bytes allocated for input storage | 32 / 64 / 128 / 256 bytes | Padding length calculation |
| **NOP Sled** | Sequence of `0x90` (No-Operation) bytes | 16 – 256 bytes | Increases landing-zone probability |
| **Shellcode** | Position-independent machine code | 20 – 350 bytes | Executes attacker commands |
| **EIP Offset** | Bytes from buffer start to RET | `buf_size + 8` (x86) | Precise overwrite location |
| **Saved EBP Offset** | Bytes to frame pointer | `buf_size + 4` | Frame pointer overwrite (optional) |
| **Little-Endian Format** | LSB stored at lowest address | 4 bytes for 32-bit addr | Critical for address writing |
| **Bad Characters** | Bytes filtered by the program | `\x00`, `\x0a`, `\x0d` | Must be excluded from shellcode |
| **ASLR Entropy** | Random bits in base address | 8 – 28 bits (OS-dependent) | Affects exploit reliability |
| **Stack Canary Value** | Random terminator between buf & RET | 4 bytes (GCC default) | Detects overflow before RET |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

Understanding buffer overflows is non-negotiable for several KTU-aligned engineering roles:

- **Penetration Testers**: Use Metasploit's `buffer_overflow` module to validate vulnerabilities (OSCP exam).
- **IoT Firmware Engineers**: Audit C code for memory safety in constrained devices (often 32 KB RAM, no OS).
- **Secure Software Architects**: Implement coding standards like **CERT C** and **MISRA-C** to eliminate unsafe functions.
- **Incident Responders**: Recognize memory-corruption indicators in crash dumps and exploit attempts.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: A Stack Smashing Example

## 3.1 The Vulnerable C Program

Consider the following intentionally vulnerable C program, a classic pedagogical example used in KTU Module 2 tutorials:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

void vulnerable_function(char *input) {
    char buffer[64];                  // 64-byte stack buffer
    strcpy(buffer, input);            // UNSAFE: No bounds checking
    printf("You entered: %s\n", buffer);
}

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    if (argc > 1) {
        vulnerable_function(argv[1]);
    }
    return 0;
}
```

The `strcpy()` function copies bytes until it encounters a null terminator (`\x00`), with **no length validation**. If the user supplies more than 64 bytes, the data overflows into the saved EBP and return address.

## 3.2 Memory Layout Analysis Before Overflow

Let the program call `vulnerable_function("AAAA")`. The stack frame looks like:

| Stack Address (High) | Content | Symbolic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `0x7FFF...0C` | Argument pointer to `argv[1]` | `input` |
| `0x7FFF...08` | **Return Address = 0x08048450** | `RET` |
| `0x7FFF...04` | **Saved EBP = 0x7FFF...30** | `SFP` |
| `0x7FFF...00` | `buffer[60..63] = "AAAA\0\0\0\0"` | `buf+60` |
| ... | ... | ... |
| `0x7FFF...C0` | `buffer[0..3] = "AAAA"` | `buf+0` |
| (Low Address) | | |

> The **return address is located 64 + 4 (SFP) = 68 bytes** after the start of the buffer.

## 3.3 Calculating the EIP Offset — Explicit Derivation

Let $B$ = buffer size, $S$ = saved frame pointer size, $R$ = return address size.

For the 32-bit x86 architecture, the standard EIP offset is computed as:

$$\text{EIP\_offset} = B + S + R$$

Substituting standard values:

$$\text{EIP\_offset} = 64 + 4 + 4 = 72 \text{ bytes}$$

Therefore, the first **72 bytes** of attacker input land harmlessly in the buffer (64 bytes) and the saved EBP (4 bytes), and the **73rd through 76th bytes** overwrite the return address.

## 3.4 Constructing the Exploit Payload — Step-by-Step

To demonstrate a **controlled crash** (and the path to shellcode execution), we construct a payload in Python-style pseudocode that produces a raw binary string:

**Step 1 — Padding (72 bytes):**

$$\text{Pad} = \underbrace{0x41 \cdot 72}_{\text{ASCII 'A'} \times 72}$$

**Step 2 — New Return Address (4 bytes, little-endian):**

We aim to redirect execution to a known address. For a controlled crash, we use `0x42424242` ("BBBB"):

$$\text{RET}_{\text{new}} = \text{pack}(<I, 0x42424242)$$

In memory (little-endian), this is stored as bytes `\x42\x42\x42\x42`.

**Step 3 — Full Payload:**

$$\text{Payload} = \underbrace{A \cdot 72}_{\text{72 bytes}} + \underbrace{\backslash x42\backslash x42\backslash x42\backslash x42}_{\text{4 bytes}}$$

**Step 4 — Execution:**

When the vulnerable function returns, the CPU executes:

$$EIP \leftarrow M[ESP] = 0x42424242$$

The program attempts to fetch instructions from address `0x42424242`, which is unmapped in valid memory, triggering a **Segmentation Fault (SIGSEGV)**. The crash log will reveal:

```
EIP: 42424242
ESP: 7FFF...4C
```

This **proves control of EIP** — the cornerstone of any successful buffer overflow exploit.

## 3.5 Adding Shellcode — The Full Exploit Template

For actual command execution (e.g., spawning `/bin/sh` on Linux x86), the payload template becomes:

$$\text{Payload} = \underbrace{N_{256}}_{\text{NOP Sled}} + \underbrace{S_{\text{shellcode}}}_{\approx 25 \text{ bytes}} + \underbrace{P_{k}}_{\text{Padding}} + \underbrace{A_{\text{RET}}}_{\text{Jump back to NOPs}}$$

Where:
- $N_{256}$ = 256 NOP bytes (`0x90`) creating a "landing zone" for imprecise jumps.
- $S_{\text{shellcode}}$ = 25-byte Linux x86 `/bin/sh` shellcode.
- $P_{k}$ = Padding to reach the exact EIP offset (72 bytes total from buffer start).
- $A_{\text{RET}}$ = Estimated address of the NOP sled (e.g., `0x7FFF...C0`).

> [!NOTE]
> The NOP sled is necessary because ASLR introduces randomness in stack addresses. The sled's width (256 bytes) is chosen to exceed the ASLR entropy window (typically ±128 bytes on 32-bit Linux).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Stack Smashing in 7 Phases

| Phase | Action | Memory State Change | Key Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Recon** | Identify `strcpy()` misuse | None | Source code or binary audit |
| **2. Fuzzing** | Send 100, 200, 500-byte inputs | Crash detected at offset | Monitor EIP value in crash dump |
| **3. Offset Calc** | Send cyclic pattern, locate `0x...` in EIP | Identify overwrite position | Use Metasploit `pattern_create` |
| **4. EIP Control** | Verify `42424242` appears in EIP | Confirmed control | Crash must show controlled EIP |
| **5. Bad-Chars Audit** | Send all 256 byte values, identify filters | Filter list compiled | Common bad chars: `\x00`, `\x0a` |
| **6. Shellcode Inject** | Place NOP sled + shellcode in buffer | Payload ready | Shellcode must be bad-char-free |
| **7. Redirect EIP** | Point RET into NOP sled | Code execution achieved | Must defeat DEP/ASLR/Canary |

## 3.7 Quantitative Impact Analysis

The exploitation of a single buffer overflow can be quantified using the **CWE Impact Score**:

$$\text{Impact}_{\text{score}} = (C + I + A)^2 \cdot \text{Scope}_{\text{weight}}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Confidentiality impact (0–3, 3 = full disclosure)
- $I$ = Integrity impact (0–3, 3 = total compromise)
- $A$ = Availability impact (0–3, 3 = total outage)
- $\text{Scope}_{\text{weight}}$ = 1.0 (unchanged) or 1.5 (changed scope)

For a typical RCE buffer overflow (C=3, I=3, A=3, changed scope):

$$\text{Impact}_{\text{score}} = (3 + 3 + 3)^2 \cdot 1.5 = 81 \cdot 1.5 = 121.5$$

A score of **121.5 / 125 (max)** justifies the **CVSS 9.8 Critical** rating.
