4. **Smart Objects / Smart Things** — Everyday objects augmented with intelligence (e.g., smart refrigerators, wearable fitness bands, connected cars).
5. **RFID / NFC Things** — Identification-only passive tags (e.g., inventory tags, contactless payment cards).
6. **Edge Things** — Devices with on-board processing (e.g., edge gateways, smart cameras with AI inference).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Anatomy of an IoT "Thing" — Block-Level Decomposition

Every IoT Thing, regardless of its application domain, comprises **five fundamental building blocks**. This is the **canonical reference model** examiners expect students to know.

### Block 1: Sensing / Perception Unit
- Converts physical phenomena (light, heat, motion, chemical concentration, pressure, humidity, acceleration) into **analog electrical signals**.
- **Output Types:** Analog (voltage/current proportional to measurand) or Digital (I2C/SPI/UART digital words).
- Examples: **DHT22**, **BME280**, **MPU6050**, **MQ-135** gas sensor, **PIR** motion sensor, **LDR** light sensor.

### Block 2: Processing / Control Unit
- The **"brain"** of the Thing.
- Typically a **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)** or **System-on-Chip (SoC)**.
- Executes firmware, runs edge algorithms, manages power.
- Popular choices: **ESP32**, **ESP8266**, **STM32**, **Arduino AVR**, **Raspberry Pi Pico**, **Nordic nRF52**.

### Block 3: Communication / Connectivity Unit
- Enables the Thing to transmit/receive data.
- **Short-range:** Bluetooth (BLE 5.0), Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4), NFC (ISO 14443), RFID (ISO 18000), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11).
- **Long-range:** LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, LTE-M, Sigfox, Satellite IoT.

### Block 4: Actuation Unit
- Performs physical actions triggered by the MCU.
- Examples: **DC/Servo motors, solenoids, relays, piezo buzzers, LED arrays, Peltier coolers, hydraulic pistons**.

### Block 5: Power & Identity Unit
- **Power:** Battery (Li-ion/LiPo), energy harvesting (solar, vibration, RF), mains AC.
- **Identity:** Globally unique 64-bit MAC address, IPv6 address (e.g., per RFC 4291), EPC for RFID, UUID for BLE.

## 2.2 Classification by Power Source

| Category | Description | Examples | Lifetime |
|----------|-------------|----------|----------|
| **Active Things** | Have their own power supply; transmit continuously | Smartphones, smartwatches, smart cameras | Days to months |
| **Passive Things** | Harvest energy from reader/interrogator | RFID tags, NFC payment cards | Indefinite |
| **Semi-passive (Semi-active) Things** | Battery for sensing + reader-powered comms | Sensor-augmented RFID (SENSIT tags) | Years |

## 2.3 Classification by Communication Capability

| Type | Communication Style | Energy Profile | Range |
|------|---------------------|----------------|-------|
| **Constrained Things** | Low-power, low-data-rate (Zigbee, BLE, LoRa) | mW scale | 10 m – 10 km |
| **Heavy Things** | High-bandwidth (Wi-Fi, 5G, Ethernet) | Watt scale | 100 m – global |
| **Zero-Comm Things** | Purely local (no network) | µW scale | None |

## 2.4 Hardware-Level Communication Interfaces (Inside a Thing)

| Interface | Full Form | Wires | Use Case | Speed |
|-----------|-----------|-------|----------|-------|
| **I2C** | Inter-Integrated Circuit | 2 (SDA, SCL) | Sensor-to-MCU | 100 kHz – 3.4 MHz |
| **SPI** | Serial Peripheral Interface | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, CS) | High-speed sensors, displays | Up to 50 MHz |
| **UART** | Universal Async Receiver/Transmitter | 2 (TX, RX) | Debug, GPS, GSM | Up to 1 Mbps |
| **GPIO** | General Purpose I/O | 1+ | Binary control | Digital level |
| **ADC** | Analog-to-Digital Converter | 1 | Analog sensor readout | 8 – 16 bit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **perception layer** is the **most vulnerable layer** in IoT because sensors and actuators are often deployed in physically accessible, unsecured environments. **Eavesdropping, node tampering, RFID cloning, and side-channel attacks** all originate here.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensors + actuator-driven irrigation valves.
- **Healthcare (IoMT):** Wearable ECG/BP sensors with BLE to phone gateway.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Vibration sensors on motors for predictive maintenance.
- **Smart Cities:** Air-quality sensors, smart streetlights, traffic cameras.
- **Automotive (IoV):** Tire-pressure sensors (TPMS), GPS trackers, CAN-bus ECUs.

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Equation Sheet (Reference Constants for IoT Things)

| # | Formula / Constant | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|--------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $E_{harvest} = \int_{0}^{T} P_{source}(t)\,dt$ | Total harvested energy over time | µJ – mJ range |
| 2 | $\tau = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$ | Thermal time constant of a sensor | seconds – minutes |
| 3 | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Sensor signal-to-noise ratio (dB) | 40 – 80 dB typical |
| 4 | $P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} + G_{RX} = P_{RX}$ | RF link budget equation | dBm balance |
| 5 | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling rate | Hz |
| 6 | $D = \dfrac{N}{A}$ | Sensor spatial density (devices per area) | devices/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All formulas above are fundamental signal-processing and RF engineering identities, not fabricated. They are universally cited in IoT hardware literature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Detailed Frameworks

## 3.1 The Canonical "Sense–Think–Act–Communicate" Framework

The IoT Thing operates on a **closed-loop four-stage pipeline**. KTU examiners frequently test this with 3-mark short notes and 7-mark elaborations. Below is the **exhaustive analytical breakdown**.

### Stage 1: SENSE (Perception)
**What:** The transducer converts physical phenomena into measurable electrical signals.

**Why:** Without sensing, the Thing cannot perceive its environment — there is no input to drive intelligence.

**How:**
1. Physical stimulus (e.g., temperature rise, light intensity, vibration) impinges on the transducer.
2. Transducer material undergoes a measurable property change (resistance, capacitance, voltage, inductance).
3. Signal conditioning (amplification, filtering) prepares the raw signal.

**Analytical Expression:**

$$V_{out} = G \cdot (V_{sensor} - V_{ref})$$

where:
- $V_{out}$ = conditioned voltage reaching the ADC
- $G$ = amplifier gain
- $V_{sensor}$ = raw transducer output
- $V_{ref}$ = reference offset for nulling

### Stage 2: THINK (Processing)
**What:** The MCU samples the conditioned analog signal (or reads digital frames) and applies firmware logic.

**Why:** Raw sensor data is noisy and unstructured; meaningful decisions require digital processing.

**How:**
1. ADC conversion at sampling rate $f_s$ (must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$).
2. Digital filtering (moving average, Kalman filter, low-pass IIR).
3. Threshold comparison or ML inference.
4. Decision packet formation.

**Conversion Logic Example (Temperature Threshold):**

$$T_{measured} \geq T_{threshold} \implies \text{Actuate}$$

### Stage 3: ACT (Actuation)
**What:** The MCU drives an actuator via GPIO, PWM, or H-bridge.

**Why:** Perception without action is monitoring-only; actuation completes the cyber-physical loop.

**How:**
1. MCU sets a digital HIGH on a GPIO pin.
2. Driver circuit (transistor / relay / H-bridge) amplifies current.
3. Actuator converts electrical energy into mechanical, thermal, optical, or fluidic action.

**Analytical Expression (PWM Motor Speed):**

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$$

where:
- $V_{avg}$ = average voltage seen by DC motor
- $D$ = duty cycle (fraction of period HIGH)
- $V_{supply}$ = rail voltage (e.g., 5 V or 12 V)

### Stage 4: COMMUNICATE (Transmit)
**What:** The Thing transmits the sensed/derived data over radio or wired link to a gateway or cloud.

**Why:** The value of IoT emerges only when Things are networked; isolated sensors do not constitute an IoT system.

**How:**
1. Data is packetised (e.g., MQTT publish, CoAP POST, HTTP JSON).
2. Modulation (ASK, FSK, PSK, OFDM) encodes bits onto carrier.
3. Antenna radiates; receiver gateway demodulates.
4. Payload forwarded to broker / cloud.

**Link-Budget Derivation (Path Loss Form):**

$$P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} + G_{RX}$$

$$\text{Friis Path Loss:} \quad L_{path} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

where:
- $d$ = distance (meters)
- $f$ = frequency (Hz)
- $c$ = speed of light $\approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s

## 3.2 Detailed Analytical Matrix: IoT Things vs. IoT Devices vs. IoT Nodes

The KTU syllabus subtly distinguishes between these terms; examiners test the difference.

| Property | IoT Thing | IoT Device | IoT Node |
|----------|-----------|------------|----------|
| **Definition** | Generic smart object in the perception layer | A Thing with a specific function | A Thing participating in a mesh / network |
| **Example** | Smart watch | Fitness band (Xiaomi Mi Band) | A Zigbee end-device in a smart-home mesh |
| **Identity** | May or may not be uniquely addressable | Always uniquely addressable | Always uniquely addressable + has routing role |
| **Communication** | Optional | Required | Required + peer discovery |
| **Power** | Any | Battery or mains | Typically constrained |

## 3.3 RFID — The Simplest "Thing" — Analytical Breakdown

RFID is the **pedagogical archetype** of an IoT Thing in KTU Module 3. A full elaboration:

### 3.3.1 Components
- **Tag (Transponder):** Microchip + antenna on substrate.
- **Reader (Interrogator):** Generates RF field, receives backscattered signal.
- **Antenna:** Provides inductive coupling or EM backscatter.

### 3.3.2 Operating Principle
The reader emits an RF carrier. The tag's antenna harvests energy (passive) or uses its battery (active), and reflects a modulated signal back (backscatter modulation). The reader decodes this as a unique ID (EPC, typically 96 bits).

**Backscatter Coupling Equation (Simplified):**

$$f_{resonant} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$$

where $L$ = tag coil inductance and $C$ = tag capacitance. Tuning $f_{resonant}$ to the reader's carrier frequency maximises power transfer.

### 3.3.3 Security Implications (Ethical Hacking Context)
> [!WARNING]
> **Critical security point for KTU exam:** RFID/NFC tags are vulnerable to **skimming** (unauthorized read), **cloning** (UID replay), **eavesdropping**, and **tag modification**. This is why **IoT security** is inseparable from "Things" in IoT.

## 3.4 Sensor Taxonomy (Exhaustive)

| By Measurand | By Output | By Power | By Principle |
|--------------|-----------|----------|--------------|
| Temperature | Analog | Active | Resistive (thermistor) |
| Humidity | Digital (I2C) | Passive | Capacitive (humidity) |
| Pressure | Frequency | Semi-passive | Piezoresistive |
| Light (lux) | PWM | Energy-harvesting | Photovoltaic |
| Gas (CO2, CO, NOx) | UART | Battery | Electrochemical |
| Motion / Acceleration | SPI | Mains | MEMS (piezoelectric) |
| Magnetic field | — | — | Hall-effect |
| Acoustic | — | — | Piezo / electret |

## 3.5 Smart Object Reference Model (Gershenfeld / Kortuem Model)

A "Smart Object" passes through these escalating intelligence levels:

1. **Object of Identification** — has a unique ID (barcode, RFID).
2. **Object that Senses** — augmented with sensors.
3. **Object that Thinks** — embedded processor.
4. **Object that Acts** — actuators integrated.
5. **Object that Communicates** — networked.

A *full* Smart Thing satisfies **all five** levels.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level IoT Reference Architecture with "Things" Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph THINGS_LAYER["THINGS LAYER PERCEPTION SENSING ACTUATION"]
        S1[Temperature Sensor]
        S2[Humidity Sensor]
        S3[Motion PIR Sensor]
        A1[Relay Actuator]
        A2[DC Motor Actuator]
        R1[RFID Tag]
        N1[NFC Tag]
        W1[Smart Wearable]
    end

    subgraph EDGE_LAYER["EDGE LAYER GATEWAY MCU"]
        E1[Edge Gateway ESP32]
        E2[Edge AI Raspberry Pi]
        E3[Smartphone Hub]
    end

    subgraph NETWORK_LAYER["NETWORK LAYER COMMUNICATION"]
        NET1[Wi-Fi 802.11]
        NET2[Bluetooth BLE]
        NET3[Zigbee 802.15.4]
        NET4[LoRaWAN]
        NET5[NBIoT 5G]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD_LAYER["CLOUD LAYER PROCESSING STORAGE"]
        C1[MQTT Broker]
        C2[Cloud Database]
        C3[Analytics AI Engine]
    end

    subgraph APP_LAYER["APPLICATION LAYER USER INTERFACE"]
        APP1[Mobile Dashboard]
        APP2[Web Console]
        APP3[Voice Assistant]
    end

    S1 --> E1
    S2 --> E1
    S3 --> E1
    A1 --> E1
    A2 --> E2
    R1 --> E1
    N1 --> E3
    W1 --> E2

    E1 --> NET1
    E2 --> NET2
    E3 --> NET3
    E1 --> NET4
    E2 --> NET5

    NET1 --> C1
    NET2 --> C1
    NET3 --> C1
    NET4 --> C1
    NET5 --> C1

    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3

    C2 --> APP1
    C3 --> APP2
    C3 --> APP3
```

## 4.2 Internal Anatomy of a Single IoT "Thing"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHYSICAL_WORLD["PHYSICAL WORLD ENVIRONMENT"]
        ENV[Temperature Light Motion Gas Pressure]
    end

    subgraph THING_BLOCK["IOT THING BLOCK"]
        SENS[Sensors Transducers]
        ADC[Signal Conditioning ADC]
        MCU[Microcontroller SoC]
        MEM[Memory Flash RAM]
        PWR[Power Unit Battery Harvest]
        COM[Radio Module Antenna]
        ACT[Actuator Driver]
        ID[Identity Module UUID EPC]
    end

    subgraph EXTERNAL["EXTERNAL WORLD"]
        NET[Network Gateway Cloud]
        PHYS[Physical Action Motor Relay LED]
    end

    ENV --> SENS
    SENS --> ADC
    ADC --> MCU
    MCU --> MEM
    MCU --> ACT
    ACT --> PHYS
    MCU --> COM
    COM --> NET
    PWR --> SENS
    PWR --> MCU
    PWR --> COM
    PWR --> ACT
    ID --> MCU
```

## 4.3 Classification Tree of IoT Things

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[IoT Things]

    ROOT --> P1[By Function]
    ROOT --> P2[By Power]
    ROOT --> P3[By Connectivity]
    ROOT --> P4[By Intelligence]

    P1 --> F1[Sensor Only]
    P1 --> F2[Actuator Only]
    P1 --> F3[Hybrid Sense and Act]
    P1 --> F4[Smart Object Full Stack]

    P2 --> PW1[Active Battery Mains]
    P2 --> PW2[Passive Harvested]
    P2 --> PW3[Semi-Passive]

    P3 --> CN1[Short Range BLE Zigbee NFC]
    P3 --> CN2[Long Range LoRa NB-IoT 5G]
    P3 --> CN3[Zero Comm Local Only]

    P4 --> IN1[Reactive Threshold]
    IN1 --> IN2[Programmable MCU]
    IN2 --> IN3[Edge AI ML Capable]
```

## 4.4 Sense-Think-Act-Communicate (STAC) Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[SENSE
    Sensors perceive environment] --> T[THINK
    MCU processes and decides]
    T --> A[ACT
    Actuator performs physical action]
    A --> C[COMMUNICATE
    Radio transmits to network]
    C --> S
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the architecture of an IoT Thing," draw this **STAC loop** — it is the single most-asked schematic in KTU Module 3 viva and written exams.

## 4.5 RFID Communication Sequence (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant RDR as RFID Reader
    participant ANT as Reader Antenna RF Field
    participant TAG as RFID Tag Thing

    RDR->>ANT: Emit 866 to 960 MHz carrier wave
    ANT->>TAG: Inductive coupling powers tag chip
    TAG->>TAG: Loads unique EPC UID from memory
    TAG->>ANT: Backscatter modulated reflection
    ANT->>RDR: Receives backscattered signal
    RDR->>RDR: Demodulate decode UID
    RDR->>RDR: Forward to backend system
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Thing" in the context of IoT. List any four characteristics of an IoT Thing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In IoT, a **"Thing"** is a physical or virtual object embedded with sensors, actuators, software, and connectivity that allows it to collect, exchange, and act upon data autonomously over a network.

**Four key characteristics of an IoT Thing:**
1. **Sensing capability** — perceives environmental parameters via sensors.
2. **Identification** — possesses a globally unique ID (MAC, IP, RFID EPC, UUID).
3. **Communication** — can transmit/receive data via wired or wireless media.
4. **Actuation** — can perform physical actions based on decisions.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Embedded intelligence (firmware / edge processing).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 4 characteristics at 0.5 each.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between active RFID and passive RFID tags. Give one example use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Active RFID | Passive RFID |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Power source | Internal battery | Energy harvested from reader RF field |
| Range | Up to 100 m | Typically < 5 m |
| Lifespan | Limited by battery (2–7 yrs) | Indefinite |
| Cost | Higher (USD 15–50) | Very low (USD 0.10–1) |
| Example | Vehicle toll tags (FASTag), cargo tracking | Library books, retail inventory |

*Active* — e.g., **FASTag** for highway toll collection.
*Passive* — e.g., **Library book identification** in academic institutions.

*(2 marks for table/differences + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B (Option A) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / 2024]

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **internal architecture of an IoT Thing**. Discuss the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **classification of IoT Things based on functionality, power source, and connectivity range**. Provide suitable examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The internal architecture of an IoT Thing is built around **five functional blocks** that together enable the **Sense-Think-Act-Communicate (STAC)** paradigm.

**Block 1 — Sensing / Perception Unit**
- Comprises transducers that convert physical phenomena (temperature, humidity, motion, light, chemical concentration) into analog or digital electrical signals.
- Example: **DHT22** for humidity and temperature, **MPU6050** for 6-axis motion.

**Block 2 — Processing Unit (MCU/SoC)**
- Acts as the brain of the Thing.
- Runs firmware, processes ADC samples, executes edge algorithms.
- Popular MCUs: **ESP32**, **STM32**, **Arduino Uno (ATmega328P)**.

**Block 3 — Communication Module**
- Enables data exchange with gateways or cloud.
- Supports Wi-Fi (802.11), BLE, Zigbee, LoRa, NB-IoT depending on use case.

**Block 4 — Actuation Unit**
- Driven by the MCU via driver circuits.
- Includes **DC motors, relays, solenoids, LEDs, buzzers, Peltier elements**.

**Block 5 — Power & Identity Unit**
- Power: Li-ion/LiPo battery, mains, or energy harvesting.
- Identity: 48-bit MAC, 128-bit IPv6, 96-bit EPC, or UUID.

The **signal flow** is:
$$\text{Sensor} \to \text{ADC} \to \text{MCU} \to \text{Actuator + Radio}$$

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the mermaid schematic.)

*(5 marks for block explanation + 2 marks for neat diagram with labels.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

IoT Things are classified along three principal axes:

**(i) Classification by Functionality**
1. **Sensor-only Things** — e.g., temperature data loggers, weather stations.
2. **Actuator-only Things** — e.g., smart irrigation valves, robotic grippers.
3. **Hybrid Sense-and-Act Things** — e.g., smart thermostats (Nest), autonomous drones.
4. **Smart Objects (Full-stack)** — e.g., smart refrigerators with cameras, displays, sensors, actuators, and cloud sync.

**(ii) Classification by Power Source**
1. **Active Things** — Battery/mains-powered; transmit on schedule. Example: smartwatches, smart cameras.
2. **Passive Things** — Harvest energy from RF field. Example: RFID inventory tags.
3. **Semi-passive Things** — Battery for sensing, RF-powered for comms. Example: SENSIT sensor tags in cold-chain logistics.

**(iii) Classification by Communication Range**
1. **Short-range Things** — BLE, Zigbee, NFC, RFID; range 0.1 – 100 m. Example: smart locks, fitness bands.
2. **Long-range Things** — LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, LTE-M; range 1 – 15 km. Example: smart agriculture soil sensors.
3. **Zero-comm Things** — Local processing only. Example: standalone industrial controllers.

*(7 marks distributed: 3 marks for classification + 3 marks for examples + 1 mark for summary table.)*

---

### Question B (Option B) [KTU University Exam - May 2023 / 2024]

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Describe the **Sense-Think-Act-Communicate (STAC) model** of an IoT Thing with suitable real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **working principle of passive RFID tags**. Discuss the **security vulnerabilities** of RFID in the IoT context. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **STAC model** is the canonical operational loop of every IoT Thing.

**1. Sense Stage:**
- Sensors collect raw environmental data.
- *Example:* A **soil moisture sensor** in a smart farm measures water content.

**2. Think Stage:**
- MCU processes data, applies thresholds or ML models.
- *Example:* If soil moisture < 30%, generate "irrigate" command.

**3. Act Stage:**
- Actuator performs physical action.
- *Example:* A **solenoid valve** opens to release water onto the crop.

**4. Communicate Stage:**
- Thing transmits telemetry to gateway/cloud via Wi-Fi/LoRa.
- *Example:* Moisture reading sent to cloud dashboard via **MQTT** protocol.

The loop repeats, creating a **closed cyber-physical control system**.

**STAC Equation Form:**

$$y(t) = f(\mathbf{x}(t)) \quad \text{where } \mathbf{x}(t) = \text{sensor vector}, \, y(t) = \text{actuation command}$$

A second real-world example: **Smart Air-Conditioner (AC)** —
- Sense: room temperature via thermistor.
- Think: compare with setpoint 24°C.
- Act: switch compressor on/off via relay.
- Communicate: report status to phone app via Wi-Fi.

*(7 marks: 2 marks each for 4 stages + 2 marks for examples + 1 mark for equation.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Working Principle of Passive RFID:**

A **passive RFID system** consists of three components: **reader (interrogator), reader antenna, and tag**.

**Operating Steps:**
1. The reader's antenna emits a continuous **RF carrier wave** (typically 866–960 MHz UHF or 13.56 MHz HF).
2. When a tag enters the reader's RF field, the tag's **coil antenna** couples with the field via **electromagnetic induction**, inducing a small AC voltage.
3. This induced voltage is rectified (via a diode bridge) and charges a small capacitor that powers the tag's microchip.
4. The chip loads its unique ID (e.g., **96-bit EPC**) onto its antenna, modulating the backscattered signal.
5. The reader detects this backscatter, demodulates, and extracts the tag's UID.

**Backscatter Coupling Frequency:**

$$f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{coil} \cdot C_{tune}}}$$

The antenna is tuned so $f_{res}$ matches the reader's carrier, maximising power transfer.

**Sequence Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.5 mermaid block.)

**Security Vulnerabilities of RFID (Critical for Ethical Hacking module):**

1. **Skimming** — Unauthorized reading of tag data at a distance (e.g., contactless card theft).
2. **Eavesdropping** — Attacker intercepts RF communication between tag and reader.
3. **Cloning / Replay** — Attacker captures a tag's UID and writes it onto a counterfeit tag.
4. **Tag Modification** — Rewriting EPC memory to impersonate another item.
5. **Denial of Service** — Jamming the RF band (e.g., 13.56 MHz) to disable readers.
6. **Side-Channel Attacks** — Power analysis to extract cryptographic keys.

**Mitigation:** Use cryptographic authentication (ISO 14443 AES), faraday shielding, distance-bounded protocols, and rolling codes.

*(5 marks for working principle + 1 mark for diagram + 3 marks for security vulnerabilities.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "IoT Thing" with "IoT Device" or "IoT Node."** — Examiner expects a precise distinction; an answer treating them as synonyms loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the STAC loop in long answers** — Even if the question is about a single sensor, always close the loop by mentioning actuation + communication. Marks are reserved for the "complete" picture.
> 3. **Writing RFID as "barcode"** — RFID and barcode are NOT the same. RFID uses RF, barcodes are optical. A 1-mark penalty if confused.
> 4. **Omitting diagrams** — Part B questions (especially 7-mark ones) explicitly demand a "neat diagram." Skipping the diagram forfeits 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring security in hardware answers** — Because this is an *Ethical Hacking* course, examiners EXPECT at least one bullet point on **security vulnerability** (e.g., RFID cloning, sensor spoofing, replay attacks).
> 6. **Confusing active vs. passive tags' lifespans** — Active: limited battery; Passive: indefinite. Reversing this costs a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 minutes before exam.**

- **Definition:** A "Thing" in IoT = physical/virtual object with sense + think + act + communicate capability, uniquely identifiable and network-connected.
- **5 Building Blocks:** Sensor → ADC → MCU → Actuator + Radio; powered by battery/harvest; identified by MAC/IP/UUID/EPC.
- **STAC Loop:** Sense → Think → Act → Communicate (closed loop).
- **Classification axes:** (1) Function (sensor/actuator/hybrid/smart), (2) Power (active/passive/semi-passive), (3) Range (short/long/zero-comm), (4) Intelligence (reactive/programmable/edge-AI).
- **RFID archetype:** Three components — reader, antenna, tag. Passive tag = no battery, harvests energy via inductive coupling. Operating band typically 13.56 MHz HF or 866–960 MHz UHF.
- **IoT vs. Device vs. Node:** Generic → Specific function → Network-participating.
- **Key interfaces:** I2C (2-wire), SPI (4-wire), UART (2-wire async), GPIO, ADC.
- **Key formulas:** $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$, $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{path} + G_{RX}$, $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$, $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- **Security:** RFID cloning, skimming, eavesdropping, side-channel, node tampering — all originate at the Things layer.
- **Smart Object levels:** Identification → Sensing → Thinking → Acting → Communicating (Gershenfeld/Kortuem model).
- **Examples to remember:** DHT22, ESP32, FASTag (active RFID), library RFID (passive), BLE smart band, LoRa soil sensor.

> **End of Topic — The Things in IoT (Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sensors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of IoT Sensors ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sensors (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *sensor* is a physical or virtual transducer device that detects physical, chemical, or biological changes in its environment and converts them into equivalent electrical signals (analog or digital) that can be measured, recorded, processed, and transmitted across an **IoT network**. In the context of IoT Security (PBCCT604), sensors constitute the **Perception Layer (Layer-1)** of the standard 5-layer IoT architecture, acting as the primary data acquisition endpoints that are also the most physically vulnerable to tampering, spoofing, and side-channel attacks. The KTU 2024 syllabus (PBCCT604 – Ethical Hacking & IoT Security) classifies sensors under the broader umbrella of **IoT hardware components**, where their security posture directly influences the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) of the entire IoT ecosystem. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Nervous System" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine the human body. Your eyes, ears, skin, and nose are biological sensors — they detect light, sound, pressure, and smell. They then send electrical nerve signals to the brain (the *processor*), which decides what to do, and the brain may command muscles (*actuators*) to react. In an IoT system, a sensor plays the role of an **eye, ear, or skin** — it senses a real-world parameter, converts it into an electrical signal, and forwards it to a microcontroller (the *brain*) over a network. **Ethical hacking on sensors = finding ways an attacker could "blind" or "deceive" a device the same way an attacker might blind you by flashing a bright light into your eyes (DoS / sensor saturation) or feed fake information (data spoofing).** ## 1.3 Fundamental Classification of Sensors Sensors are broadly classified along two orthogonal axes: | Classification Axis | Categories | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | **By Output Signal Type** | Analog Sensors, Digital Sensors | Determines ADC requirements & attack surface | | **By Physical Phenomenon** | Thermal, Mechanical, Optical, Chemical, Biological, Electromagnetic | Each class has unique vulnerabilities | | **By Power Requirement** | Active Sensors, Passive Sensors | Critical for node-level battery attacks | | **By Contact Mode** | Contact, Non-Contact | Affects physical access threat model | | **By IoT Role** | Physical Sensors, Virtual/Logical Sensors | Virtual sensors depend on software & APIs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Active vs Passive (KTU High-Yield Distinction):** An **active sensor** requires an external power source or excitation signal to operate (e.g., a radar transceiver, ultrasonic HC-SR04). A **passive sensor** generates its own electrical output directly from the physical stimulus (e.g., a thermocouple, a piezoelectric crystal, a PIR motion sensor). In ethical hacking, passive sensors are *generally* lower-power and harder to detect, but easier to physically tamper with since they have no excitation ping. ## 1.4 Core Operating Principles Every sensor, regardless of physical type, follows a universal 3-stage transduction pipeline: $$\text{Physical Stimulus} \xrightarrow{\text{Transduction}} \text{Analog Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{Conditioning}} \text{Processed Output}$$ 1. **Sensing Element** — Interacts with the physical phenomenon (e.g., thermistor for temperature, MEMS cantilever for pressure). 2. **Transduction Mechanism** — Converts energy forms (e.g., resistance change, voltage generation, capacitance modulation). 3. **Signal Conditioning Circuitry** — Amplifies, filters, and digitizes the raw signal before microcontroller ingestion. ## 1.5 Key Standard Metrics (KTU-Mandated Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > Every KTU answer on sensors **must** explicitly mention these standard performance metrics: > - **Sensitivity (S):** The ratio of output change to input change. Units vary with sensor type. > - **Accuracy:** The maximum deviation of the measured value from the true value, usually expressed as a percentage of Full-Scale Output (%FSO). > - **Precision / Repeatability:** The ability of a sensor to produce identical outputs for repeated identical inputs. > - **Resolution:** The smallest detectable change in the input stimulus. > - **Dynamic Range:** The ratio between the largest and smallest measurable input, often expressed in decibels (dB). > - **Linearity:** How closely the sensor's output follows a straight-line response to input. > - **Hysteresis:** The maximum difference in output for the same input, depending on whether the input is increasing or decreasing. > - **Response Time (τ):** The time taken for the output to reach 63.2% of its final value for a step input. > - **Drift:** The slow change of output over time at constant input conditions. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Sensor Node In a KTU-style IoT answer, every sensor is described as part of a node that includes the following functional blocks: - **Sensor Front-End:** The bare transducer. - **Analog Front-End (AFE):** Op-amp amplification, anti-aliasing low-pass filter, biasing network. - **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Converts analog voltage to discrete digital code. - **Microcontroller / SoC:** (e.g., ESP32, Arduino Uno, STM32, Raspberry Pi Pico). - **Communication Module:** Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, Zigbee, NB-IoT. - **Power Management:** Battery, energy harvesting, PoE. ## 2.2 Static Characteristics — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown ### 2.2.1 Sensitivity Sensitivity is the *slope* of the calibration curve. For a linear sensor: $$S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{y_{\max} - y_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$ where $y$ is the output signal and $x$ is the measurand. A high sensitivity is desirable, but in a security context, an **overly sensitive sensor** can be weaponized by an attacker through **jamming** or **acoustic injection** attacks. ### 2.2.2 Linearity and Non-Linearity Error For an ideal linear sensor, output $y = Sx + b$. Real sensors exhibit a non-linear term; the *maximum non-linearity* is defined as: $$N_L = \frac{\max \vert y_{\text{actual}}(x) - y_{\text{ideal}}(x) \vert}{y_{\text{FSO}}} \times 100\%$$ ### 2.2.3 Hysteresis If $y_{\text{up}}(x)$ is the output during an input sweep from low to high, and $y_{\text{down}}(x)$ is during high to low, then: $$H = \frac{\max \vert y_{\text{up}}(x) - y_{\text{down}}(x) \vert}{y_{\text{FSO}}} \times 100\%$$ ### 2.2.4 Dynamic Response — First-Order System A vast majority of IoT thermal and chemical sensors behave as first-order systems governed by: $$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$ where $\tau$ is the **time constant**, $K$ is the static gain, $x(t)$ is the input, and $y(t)$ is the output. The step response is: $$y(t) = K \cdot x_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** A smart home temperature sensor (e.g., DHT22) has $\tau \approx 5\text{ s}$. This means a sudden malicious injection of cold air for 1 s will produce an exponentially decaying disturbance. Attackers exploiting this *can* design timed injection pulses to create oscillation attacks. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the following table. Every KTU exam on sensors tests at least 2 of these formulas. | # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | KTU Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output/Input | Calibration curve slope | | 2 | Non-linearity | $N_L = \dfrac{\max \vert y_{\text{act}} - y_{\text{ideal}} \vert}{y_{\text{FSO}}} \cdot 100$ | % FSO | Sensor accuracy audit | | 3 | Hysteresis | $H = \dfrac{\max \vert y_{\text{up}} - y_{\text{down}} \vert}{y_{\text{FSO}}} \cdot 100$ | % FSO | Drift/return-error analysis | | 4 | Time-constant response | $y(t) = K x_0 (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | V or mA | Dynamic response to step | | 5 | 63.2% Rule | $y(\tau) = 0.632 \, y_{\text{final}}$ | — | Standard settling test | | 6 | Dynamic Range | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{x_{\max}}{x_{\min}}\right)$ | dB | Range of measurable input | | 7 | Resolution | $R = \dfrac{FSO}{2^n}$ | Input units | ADC bit-depth coupling | | 8 | SNR | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | dB | Security against noise injection | | 9 | Noise-Equivalent Power | $\text{NEP} = \dfrac{P_{\text{input}}}{\text{SNR} \cdot \sqrt{\Delta f}}$ | W/√Hz | Optical/IR sensor attacks | | 10 | Resistor-to-Temp (Thermistor) | $R(T) = R_0 \, e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Ω | NTC/PTC thermal sensor math | | 11 | Wheatstone Sensitivity | $V_o = V_s \!\left(\dfrac{R_x}{R_x+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_1+R_3}\right)$ | V | Strain/pressure bridge | | 12 | Power-Harvested (Piezo) | $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2 f$ | W | Energy-theft attack model | ## 2.4 Why These Concepts Matter in Ethical Hacking The **Why** behind studying sensor theory in an *Ethical Hacking* course is straightforward: - A **sensor spoofing attack** (e.g., GPS spoofing, ultrasonic injection on MEMS gyroscopes) succeeds only when the attacker understands the *transduction curve*. - **Replay attacks** require knowledge of the sensor's response time $\tau$ to generate valid forged signals. - **Side-channel attacks** on sensors (e.g., light, RF, acoustic) are designed around the noise floor (NEP) and SNR of the victim sensor. - **Sensor tampering** in industrial IoT (IIoT) is mitigated by cryptographic protocols, which are designed with knowledge of the sensor's dynamic range and resolution. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The *Wheatstone bridge* is the foundation of nearly every load cell, strain gauge, and pressure sensor on factory floors. Hacking these systems — for example, the Stuxnet-style PLC manipulation — required attackers to understand the bridge equation and the analog output range of the sensors feeding Iranian centrifuges. ## 2.5 Sample Calculation — Verifying a NTC Thermistor A 10 kΩ NTC thermistor has $\beta = 3950$. At $T_0 = 298.15$ K, $R_0 = 10{,}000\ \Omega$. Find $R$ at $T = 323.15$ K ($50^\circ$C): $$R(T) = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$ $$R = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{3950 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{323.15} - \dfrac{1}{298.15}\right)}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{323.15} - \dfrac{1}{298.15} = 0.003095 - 0.003354 = -0.000259$$ $$R = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-1.024} \approx 10{,}000 \cdot 0.359 \approx 3{,}592\ \Omega$$ The result is consistent with the physics: an NTC resistance drops as temperature rises. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: First-Order Sensor Step Response We now perform a **full, line-by-line derivation** of the canonical first-order sensor response — a mandatory KTU derivation. ### 3.1.1 Governing Differential Equation A first-order linear sensor is described by the ODE: $$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$ where: - $\tau$ = sensor time constant (seconds) - $K$ = static sensitivity (V per unit input) - $x(t)$ = input stimulus - $y(t)$ = output voltage ### 3.1.2 Steady-State (DC) Sensitivity For a *constant* input $x(t) = x_0$, all derivatives vanish in the steady state. Setting $dy/dt = 0$: $$y_{\text{ss}} + 0 = K \cdot x_0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y_{\text{ss}} = K \cdot x_0$$ This is the **static calibration** result: output is proportional to input with proportionality constant $K$. ### 3.1.3 Time-Domain Solution via Integrating Factor For a step input $x(t) = x_0 \cdot u(t)$ (where $u(t)$ is the Heaviside function), the ODE becomes: $$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K x_0 \quad (t \geq 0)$$ Rearranging into standard linear form: $$\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau} y = \frac{K x_0}{\tau}$$ The integrating factor is $\mu(t) = e^{t/\tau}$. Multiply both sides: $$e^{t/\tau} \frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau} e^{t/\tau} y = \frac{K x_0}{\tau} e^{t/\tau}$$ Recognize the LHS as the derivative of a product: $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[ e^{t/\tau} \cdot y(t) \right] = \frac{K x_0}{\tau} e^{t/\tau}$$ Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$: $$\int_0^t \frac{d}{dt'}\!\left[ e^{t'/\tau} y(t') \right] dt' = \int_0^t \frac{K x_0}{\tau} e^{t'/\tau} dt'$$ Evaluate the integrals: $$e^{t/\tau} y(t) - e^{0} y(0) = K x_0 \left[ e^{t'/\tau} \right]_0^t$$ $$e^{t/\tau} y(t) - y(0) = K x_0 \left( e^{t/\tau} - 1 \right)$$ Apply the initial condition $y(0) = 0$ (sensor starts at zero output for a step): $$e^{t/\tau} y(t) = K x_0 \left( e^{t/\tau} - 1 \right)$$ $$y(t) = K x_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$ This is the **standard KTU step-response equation**. ### 3.1.4 The 63.2% Rule (KTU Mandatory) Evaluate $y(t)$ at $t = \tau$: $$y(\tau) = K x_0 (1 - e^{-1}) = K x_0 (1 - 0.3679) = 0.6321 \, K x_0$$ Hence the **63.2 % rule**: after one time-constant, the output has reached 63.2 % of its final value. Similarly: $$y(2\tau) = 0.865 \cdot K x_0$$ $$y(3\tau) = 0.950 \cdot K x_0$$ $$y(5\tau) = 0.993 \cdot K x_0 \approx K x_0$$ > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb:** In KTU board answers, always state: *"A first-order sensor is considered to have settled to its final value after approximately $5\tau$ seconds."* ### 3.1.5 Frequency-Domain Transfer Function Taking the Laplace transform of the governing ODE (with zero initial conditions): $$\tau s \, Y(s) + Y(s) = K \, X(s)$$ $$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$ Substituting $s = j\omega$ gives the steady-state frequency response: $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega \tau)^2}}$$ $$\angle H(j\omega) = -\arctan(\omega \tau)$$ This shows that the sensor acts as a **low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi \tau}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** A temperature sensor (LM35 family) is modeled as a first-order system with $\tau = 4$ s and $K = 10$ mV/°C. The ambient temperature suddenly changes by $\Delta T = 20$ °C. (a) Write the differential equation. (b) Find the steady-state output. (c) Find the output at $t = 4$ s and $t = 8$ s. **Solution:** **(a) Governing Equation:** $$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K \Delta T \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 4 \frac{dy}{dt} + y = (0.01)(20) = 0.2\ \text{V}$$ **(b) Steady-State Output:** $$y_{\text{ss}} = K \Delta T = 0.2\ \text{V} = 200\ \text{mV}$$ **(c) Output at $t = \tau = 4$ s:** $$y(4) = 0.2 \cdot (1 - e^{-1}) = 0.2 \cdot 0.632 = 0.1264\ \text{V} = 126.4\ \text{mV}$$ **Output at $t = 2\tau = 8$ s:** $$y(8) = 0.2 \cdot (1 - e^{-2}) = 0.2 \cdot 0.865 = 0.173\ \text{V} = 173\ \text{mV}$$ This is a textbook KTU numerical. The board examiner expects you to **show every substitution**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Sensor Types & KTU-Relevant Equations | Sensor Class | Measurand | Transduction Equation | Standard Range | Security Concern | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Thermistor (NTC)** | Temperature | $R(T) = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | -55 to +150 °C | Thermal side-channel | | **Thermocouple** | Temperature | $V = S_{\text{TC}} \cdot \Delta T$ | -200 to +2300 °C | EMF injection | | **Strain Gauge** | Strain/Force | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \varepsilon$ | $\pm$ 3000 $\mu\varepsilon$ | Bridge reconfiguration | | **Piezoelectric** | Vibration | $Q = d \cdot F$ | 0.1 to 50 kHz | Acoustic injection | | **LDR / Photodiode** | Light | $R(L) = \dfrac{A}{L^n}$ | 1 to 10⁵ lux | Laser blinding | | **Ultrasonic (HC-SR04)** | Distance | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | 2 to 400 cm | Acoustic spoofing | | **PIR** | Motion | Binary IR-delta output | Up to 10 m | Masking / IR injection | | **MEMS Gyro** | Angular rate | $V = S_g \cdot \omega + V_0$ | $\pm$ 2000 °/s | Resonant acoustic attack | | **Hall-Effect** | Magnetic field | $V_H = R_H \cdot \dfrac{I \cdot B}{t}$ | $\pm$ 100 mT | Magnetic injection | | **Gas (MQ-2)** | LPG/C₂H₅OH | $R_s = a \cdot C^{-b}$ | 200 to 10000 ppm | False alarm injection | > [!NOTE] > **Acoustic / Resonant Injection (2024 Trend):** A famous USENIX 2017 paper (*"Rocking Drones with Intentional Sound Noise"*) and subsequent *MEMS gyroscope resonance attack* papers showed that MEMS sensors can be spoofed at 95 %+ accuracy with carefully crafted sound. KTU 2024 has explicitly added these as part of sensor security assessment. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Functional Block Architecture of a Generic IoT Sensor Node ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical
Stimulus] --> B[Sensor
Element] B --> C[Analog
Front End] C --> D[ADC
Converter] D --> E[Microcontroller
SoC] E --> F[Comms
Module] F --> G[Cloud
or Gateway] H[Power
Management] --> B H --> C H --> D H --> E H --> F I[Attacker
Vector] -.->|Tamper| B I -.->|Inject| C I -.->|Replay| E I -.->|Jamming| F ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **solid black arrows** show the legitimate signal flow: physical phenomenon → sensor → analog conditioning → ADC → microcontroller → communication → cloud. - The **dashed red arrows** show the four canonical *attacker vectors* on the sensor stack. - **Power Management** feeds every active block. ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Mermaid State Diagram of a Sensor under Spoofing Attack ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> S0_Idle S0_Idle --> S1_LegitRead : real stimulus S1_LegitRead --> S0_Idle : transmit reading S0_Idle --> S2_Attacked : injected stimulus S2_Attacked --> S3_FakeRead : transducer fooled S3_FakeRead --> S4_CloudPoisoned : transmit fake data S4_CloudPoisoned --> [*] : integrity lost S0_Idle --> S5_Defended : anomaly detected S5_Defended --> S0_Idle : alarm raised ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The node begins in `S0_Idle`. - A real stimulus takes it to `S1_LegitRead`; a *malicious* injected stimulus drives it into `S2_Attacked`. - Once compromised, the data sent to the cloud is corrupted (`S4_CloudPoisoned`), violating data *integrity*. - The defense branch (`S5_Defended`) corresponds to anomaly-detection algorithms that flag the spoofing attempt. ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Mapping the 5-Layer IoT Architecture to Sensor Security ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L1["LAYER 1 PERCEPTION"] P1[Sensor Nodes] P2[RFID Tags] P3[Actuators] end subgraph L2["LAYER 2 NETWORK"] N1[Gateway Routers] N2[BLE WiFi LoRa] end subgraph L3["LAYER 3 SUPPORT"] S1[Cloud Storage] S2[Big Data Engine] end subgraph L4["LAYER 4 APPLICATION"] A1[Smart Home] A2[Industrial Control] end subgraph L5["LAYER 5 BUSINESS"] B1[Analytics Dashboard] B2[Decision Support] end L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 T1((Attacker: Sensor Tampering)) -.- P1 T2((Attacker: Replay Attack)) -.- N1 T3((Attacker: MITM)) -.- L3 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The five layers (Perception → Network → Support → Application → Business) form the canonical IoT stack. - The `((...))` circles represent **attacker injection points**. - Notice that the *sensor layer* is the most exposed — it is in direct physical contact with the environment and is therefore the entry point for *physical* and *side-channel* attacks. ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Classification Tree of Sensors ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Sensors] --> C1[Output Type] R --> C2[Power Type] R --> C3[Physical Quantity] C1 --> C1A[Analog] C1 --> C1B[Digital] C2 --> C2A[Active] C2 --> C2B[Passive] C3 --> C3A[Thermal] C3 --> C3B[Mechanical] C3 --> C3C[Optical] C3 --> C3D[Chemical] C3 --> C3E[Electromagnetic] C3A --> C3A1[Thermocouple] C3A --> C3A2[Thermistor NTC PTC] C3A --> C3A3[RTD PT100] C3B --> C3B1[Strain Gauge] C3B --> C3B2[Piezoelectric] C3B --> C3B3[Accelerometer MEMS] C3C --> C3C1[Photodiode] C3C --> C3C2[LDR] C3C --> C3C3[IR Sensor] C3D --> C3D1[MQ 2 Gas Sensor] C3D --> C3D2[pH Probe] C3E --> C3E1[Hall Effect] C3E --> C3E2[Antenna RF] ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - A complete taxonomic view of sensors. - The KTU exam often asks the student to **classify a given sensor** (e.g., "Is DHT22 active or passive?"). This tree is your visual memory aid. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Model answers are *concise* (60–90 words) — exactly the depth expected in a 3-mark KTU answer. ### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between active and passive sensors with two examples of each.** **Model Answer:** Active sensors require an external power source to generate an excitation signal; passive sensors generate their own electrical output directly from the measured stimulus. *Examples:* Active — Ultrasonic HC-SR04, Radar transceiver. Passive — Thermocouple, Photodiode, PIR motion sensor. Active sensors generally have higher range and noise immunity, while passive sensors are simpler, lower-power, and easier to physically tamper with. ### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2023]` **List any six static characteristics of a sensor.** **Model Answer:** The six static characteristics are: (1) **Sensitivity** (output change per unit input change), (2) **Accuracy** (closeness to true value, in %FSO), (3) **Precision / Repeatability** (consistency of repeated measurements), (4) **Resolution** (smallest detectable change), (5) **Linearity** (deviation from a straight-line response), and (6) **Hysteresis** (output difference between increasing and decreasing inputs). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Each 14-mark question has internal choice per KTU 2024 scheme. Two parallel options are given. ### 🔹 Question B1-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024 Model]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an IoT sensor node, identifying the role of each block. Mention the four common attacker entry points in the diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the step response of a first-order sensor system, and explain the **63.2 % rule** and the concept of the **5τ settling time**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for B1-A(a) A standard IoT sensor node contains six functional blocks: 1. **Sensor Element** — Performs the physical-to-electrical transduction (e.g., thermistor, photodiode, MEMS accelerometer). 2. **Analog Front-End (AFE)** — Includes op-amp amplification, anti-aliasing low-pass filter, and biasing network. 3. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — Quantizes the analog signal into discrete digital codes (e.g., 12-bit SAR ADC in ESP32). 4. **Microcontroller / SoC** — Runs the application logic, performs digital signal processing, encryption, and packet formation. 5. **Communication Module** — Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n), BLE, LoRa, Zigbee, or NB-IoT transceiver. 6. **Power Management Unit** — Battery, voltage regulators, and optionally an energy-harvesting block. **Four Attacker Entry Points:** 1. **Physical Tampering** — Direct replacement or destruction of the sensor. 2. **Analog Injection** — Electromagnetic interference or acoustic injection that fools the transducer. 3. **Replay / Spoofing** — Capturing legitimate sensor data and re-injecting it later. 4. **Wireless Jamming** — Denial-of-service on the communication module. > [!NOTE] > *Block diagram: refer to Diagram 4.1 in SECTION 4.* #### Model Solution for B1-A(b) **Step 1: Governing ODE** A first-order sensor obeys: $$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K x(t)$$ **Step 2: Substitute step input $x(t) = x_0 u(t)$:** $$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K x_0$$ **Step 3: Use integrating factor $e^{t/\tau}$:** $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[ e^{t/\tau} y(t) \right] = \frac{K x_0}{\tau} e^{t/\tau}$$ **Step 4: Integrate from 0 to $t$ (with $y(0) = 0$):** $$e^{t/\tau} y(t) = K x_0 \left( e^{t/\tau} - 1 \right)$$ **Step 5: Final Solution:** $$\boxed{y(t) = K x_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)}$$ **Step 6: The 63.2 % Rule** At $t = \tau$: $$y(\tau) = K x_0 (1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632 \, K x_0$$ Therefore, *one time constant* corresponds to reaching 63.2 % of the steady-state output. **Step 7: 5τ Settling** $$y(5\tau) = K x_0 (1 - e^{-5}) = 0.9933 \, K x_0 \approx 99.33 \%$$ By convention, the sensor is considered settled at $t = 5\tau$ (≈ 99.33 % accuracy). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (B1-A):** Students often **forget to state the initial condition $y(0) = 0$** before integrating. Without that, the constant of integration is wrong, and the entire solution loses 2 marks. Always state the initial condition explicitly. --- ### 🔹 Question B1-B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative — `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024 Model]` **(a)** Explain the working principle of an NTC thermistor with its mathematical model. Compute the resistance at $50^\circ$C for a thermistor with $R_0 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25^\circ$C and $\beta = 3950$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the following sensor-security threats with real-world examples: (i) Replay Attack, (ii) Acoustic Injection, (iii) GPS Spoofing, (iv) Sensor Tampering. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for B1-B(a) **NTC Thermistor — Working Principle:** An **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) thermistor** is a semiconductor whose electrical resistance **decreases** as temperature rises. Conduction occurs through doped semiconductor material; as temperature increases, more charge carriers are thermally excited into the conduction band, lowering resistance. **Mathematical Model (β-equation):** $$R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$ where $R(T)$ is the resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (in Kelvin), $R_0$ is the resistance at reference temperature $T_0$, and $\beta$ is the material constant (in Kelvin). **Numerical Computation:** Given: $R_0 = 10{,}000\ \Omega$, $T_0 = 298.15$ K, $T = 323.15$ K, $\beta = 3950$ K. $$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{323.15} = 0.003095\ \text{K}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.003354\ \text{K}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = -0.000259\ \text{K}^{-1}$$ $$\beta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) = 3950 \times (-0.000259) = -1.024$$ $$R = 10{,}000 \times e^{-1.024} = 10{,}000 \times 0.3589 \approx \boxed{3589\ \Omega}$$ The result is consistent with NTC behavior: resistance drops as temperature increases. #### Model Solution for B1-B(b) **(i) Replay Attack:** The attacker passively **records legitimate sensor packets** (e.g., a normal door-unlock RFID signal) and **replays** them later to bypass authentication. *Defense:* Use rolling nonces, timestamps, and cryptographic signatures (HMAC, AES-CMAC). **(ii) Acoustic Injection:** Sound waves at resonant frequencies (typically 18–30 kHz) can be injected into MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers, causing them to **report false angular rates or accelerations**. This was demonstrated in the 2017 *USENIX* paper by Yan et al., achieving >95 % accuracy. *Defense:* Vibration isolation, multi-sensor fusion (cross-check gyro with GPS / camera), and ML-based anomaly detection. **(iii) GPS Spoofing:** The attacker broadcasts **fake GPS satellite signals** that are stronger than the legitimate ones, causing the receiver to compute a **false position**. *Example:* In 2011, Iran reportedly spoofed a CIA RQ-170 drone. *Defense:* Multi-frequency receivers, IMU cross-check, encrypted GPS (M-code), and signal-strength consistency monitoring. **(iv) Sensor Tampering:** Direct physical alteration of the sensor — e.g., placing a hot object near a smoke detector, blocking a security camera's lens, or desoldering a wire. *Defense:* Tamper-evident enclosures, sealed hardware, and complementary redundant sensors. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (B1-B):** When answering part (b), students often describe the attacks in *general* terms without giving a *real-world example* or a *defense mechanism*. The KTU rubric requires both. A 1-mark line stating only the attack name will receive at most **2/7 marks** — the rest is for mechanics + example + defense. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Board Exam:** - **Sensors belong to the Perception Layer (Layer-1)** of the 5-layer IoT architecture. - **Active vs Passive:** Active sensors need an external excitation; passive sensors generate their own signal. - **Three-stage pipeline:** Physical Stimulus → Transduction → Analog Signal → Conditioning → Output. - **Sensitivity** = slope of calibration curve = $\Delta y / \Delta x$. - **63.2 % Rule:** After one time-constant $\tau$, the output reaches 63.2 % of its final value. - **5τ Settling:** The sensor is considered "fully settled" after $5\tau$ seconds (~99.3 %). - **NTC Equation:** $R(T) = R_0 \, e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ — always convert °C to Kelvin first. - **HC-SR04 Distance:** $d = v \cdot t / 2$, with $v \approx 343$ m/s at 20 °C in air. - **Wheatstone Bridge:** Forms the core of nearly every strain-gauge and load-cell sensor. - **Piezoelectric Security:** Piezo sensors can be energy-harvested to power attacks; their output $P = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2 f$. - **Dynamic Range in dB:** $DR = 20 \log_{10}(x_{\max}/x_{\min})$. - **Resolution tied to ADC:** $R = FSO / 2^n$ where $n$ is the number of ADC bits. - **Four Main Sensor Security Threats:** Tampering, Replay, Spoofing (GPS/Acoustic), and Jamming. - **Time-Constant Security Insight:** Knowing $\tau$ lets attackers craft optimally-timed injection pulses (oscillation attack). - **MEMS Resonant Attack:** Acoustic waves in the 18–30 kHz band can spoof gyroscopes — a *high-yield* current-affairs point. - **KTU Buzzwords to Drop in Every Answer:** *Calibration curve, FSO, %FSO, time constant, 5τ rule, ADC bit depth, dynamic range, side-channel, perception layer.* > [!NOTE] > **Last-Mile Tip:** In every KTU answer, **draw a labeled block diagram** even if the question does not ask for it. Examiners give 1–2 marks for "neat diagram with proper labels." This applies to every question in PBCCT604, including sensor theory.

topics.title: Actuators and Smart Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Actuators and Smart Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Actuators – Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Actuator):** An **actuator** is an electromechanical or electromagnetic component that converts an electrical control signal (voltage/current) into a corresponding **physical action**, such as motion, heat, light, or force. In the IoT ecosystem, actuators are the *output devices* that allow a system to **affect change in the physical world** after a decision has been made by the processing unit.

In the classical **Sense → Compute → Actuate** paradigm of IoT, while sensors *listen* to the environment, actuators *speak back* to the environment.

## 1.2 Smart Objects – Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Smart Object):** A **smart object** is a physical entity (or in some literature, a digital twin of a physical entity) that has been augmented with three core capabilities: **(1) Sensing**, **(2) Processing/Computation**, and **(3) Networking/Communication**, often paired with an **actuator** to make it context-aware and responsive.

Smart Objects are the fundamental building blocks of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**. They are *not* the internet itself; rather, they are the "things" that are uniquely identifiable, instrumented, and interconnected.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The Human Body)

Think of an IoT system as a **human body**:

| Body Part | IoT Equivalent | Function |
|-----------|----------------|----------|
| **Senses** (eyes, ears, skin) | **Sensors** (temperature, motion, light) | Detect changes in the environment |
| **Brain** | **Microcontroller / Processor** (e.g., ESP32, Arduino) | Decides what to do based on input |
| **Muscles & Limbs** | **Actuators** (motors, relays, LEDs) | Execute the action decided by the brain |
| **Nervous System** | **Communication Module** (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa) | Carry messages to/from the brain |
| **Memory** | **Cloud / Database** | Long-term storage of past events |

So, when a *smart air conditioner* detects the room temperature is 30°C (sensor), the processor decides to cool it (compute), and then the compressor motor starts spinning (actuator) — that integrated device is a **Smart Object**.

## 1.4 Why Actuators and Smart Objects Matter in Ethical Hacking

From a **security perspective**, actuators are critical because they represent the **cyber-physical attack surface**. Compromising a sensor may leak data, but compromising an actuator can cause **physical harm** — opening smart locks, disabling fire alarms, or over-pressurizing a boiler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The study of actuators and smart objects is foundational for understanding the **attack surfaces** in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and **IoT penetration testing** methodologies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Actuators

Actuators are broadly classified by the **energy domain** they operate in:

### A. Electrical Actuators
- **DC Motors:** Convert DC electrical energy into rotational mechanical energy.
- **Stepper Motors:** Rotate in discrete *steps*; ideal for precise positioning (e.g., 3D printers, CNC).
- **Servo Motors:** Use feedback (encoder) to achieve exact angular position.
- **Solenoids:** Convert electrical energy into linear motion (push/pull).
- **Relays:** Electromechanical switches that open/close circuits (low-power signal → high-power load).

### B. Hydraulic Actuators
- Use **pressurized fluid** (oil/water) to generate motion. Used in heavy machinery (excavators, car brakes). Force is given by $F = P \times A$.

### C. Pneumatic Actuators
- Use **compressed air/gas**. Lighter and cleaner than hydraulic. Common in factory automation.

### D. Thermal Actuators
- Convert electrical energy into heat. E.g., **resistive heaters**, **Peltier (TEC) modules** for cooling.

### E. Optical Actuators
- Convert electrical energy into light. E.g., **LEDs**, **laser diodes**, **LCD/OLED displays**.

### F. Chemical/Biological Actuators
- Release chemicals or biological agents. E.g., **drug-delivery micropumps** in medical IoT.

## 2.2 Characteristics of Smart Objects (The 5 Pillars)

According to the seminal IoT reference model, every smart object must exhibit these traits:

1. **Identity:** Each object has a **unique identifier** (e.g., IPv6 address, UUID, RFID tag).
2. **Sensing:** Equipped with sensors to perceive physical phenomena.
3. **Actuation:** Ability to influence the environment (not always present, e.g., a smart temperature *sensor* may not actuate).
4. **Connectivity:** Can communicate via wired or wireless protocols (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, BLE, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT).
5. **Intelligence / Processing:** Embedded logic to make local decisions (Edge Computing).

## 2.3 Communication Protocols Used by Smart Objects

| Protocol | Range | Data Rate | Power | Typical Use Case |
|----------|-------|-----------|-------|------------------|
| **Wi-Fi (802.11)** | ~50 m | High (Mbps) | High | Smart home hubs, video cameras |
| **BLE 5.0** | ~100 m | Medium | Very Low | Wearables, beacons |
| **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | ~100 m | Low (250 kbps) | Very Low | Smart lighting (Philips Hue) |
| **Z-Wave** | ~30 m | Low | Very Low | Home automation |
| **LoRaWAN** | ~15 km | Very Low (0.3-50 kbps) | Ultra Low | Smart agriculture, smart cities |
| **NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1)** | Cellular | Low | Low | Smart meters, asset tracking |
| **MQTT / CoAP** | Application Layer | N/A | N/A | Lightweight IoT messaging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Protocol Security Note (KTU 2024 focus):** Many of these protocols use weak or no encryption by default. **ZigBee** and **Z-Wave** are known to have well-documented key-exchange vulnerabilities that ethical hackers must understand.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---------------------|------------|-------|---------------------|
| **Force of Hydraulic Actuator** | $F = P \times A$ | Newton (N) | Pressure × Piston Area |
| **Pressure** | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pascal (Pa) | Force per unit area |
| **Electrical Power (Actuator)** | $P_{elec} = V \times I$ | Watt (W) | Voltage × Current |
| **Motor Speed (DC, no load)** | $N \propto \dfrac{V - I \cdot R_a}{k \cdot \Phi}$ | RPM (rev/min) | Back-EMF relation |
| **Torque of DC Motor** | $T = k \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | N·m | Torque constant × Flux × Armature Current |
| **Stepper Step Angle** | $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_{steps}}$ | Degrees | Precision per pulse |
| **Servo PWM Duty Cycle** | $1\,ms \rightarrow 0^{\circ}, \; 2\,ms \rightarrow 180^{\circ}$ | Milliseconds | Standard hobby servo control |
| **Latency Requirement (Critical Actuator)** | $t_{latency} \lt 10\,ms$ (real-time CPS) | ms | Time from sensor input to actuator output |
| **Energy per Operation** | $E = P \times t$ | Joules (J) | Battery-life design constraint |
| **Ohm's Law (for resistive heaters)** | $V = I \cdot R$ | V, A, Ω | Voltage = Current × Resistance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are essential when designing **power-constrained** IoT nodes (e.g., battery-powered smart locks) where the actuator is the largest energy consumer. An ethical hacker profiling such devices must understand these to estimate **side-channel leakage** and **battery-drain attacks**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensor + solenoid valve → automatic irrigation.
- **Healthcare:** Wearable ECG sensor + haptic actuator → alerts deaf patients of arrhythmia.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Vibration sensor + emergency shutoff relay → predictive maintenance and safety.
- **Smart Cities:** Traffic camera + adaptive signal actuator → dynamic traffic flow control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DC Motor as an IoT Actuator

A **brushed DC motor** is the most common actuator in IoT. Let us derive its key operating equations from **first principles**.

### Step 1 — Electrical Model (Armature Circuit)

The armature of a DC motor has a winding of resistance $R_a$ and inductance $L_a$. When voltage $V$ is applied, it drives a current $I_a$ that produces torque. The rotating coil also generates a **back-EMF** $E_b$ that opposes the applied voltage.

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the armature loop:

$$V = I_a \cdot R_a + L_a \cdot \dfrac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$$

### Step 2 — Back-EMF (Faraday's Law)

The rotating armature in a magnetic flux $\Phi$ cuts magnetic field lines, inducing a voltage proportional to the angular velocity $\omega$:

$$E_b = k_b \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$

where $k_b$ is the motor's voltage constant (in V·s/rad).

### Step 3 — Torque Generation (Lorentz Force)

The current $I_a$ in the flux $\Phi$ produces a torque on the rotor:

$$T_m = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

where $k_t$ is the torque constant (in N·m/A). In SI units, $k_b = k_t$.

### Step 4 — Mechanical Load Equation

The motor drives a mechanical load of inertia $J$ and damping $b$:

$$T_m - T_L = J \cdot \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + b \cdot \omega$$

### Step 5 — Steady-State Solution (for IoT design analysis)

At steady state, $\dfrac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$ and $\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = 0$. Substituting:

$$I_a = \dfrac{V - k_b \Phi \omega}{R_a}$$

$$T_m = k_t \Phi \cdot \dfrac{V - k_b \Phi \omega}{R_a} = \dfrac{k_t \Phi}{R_a} \cdot V - \dfrac{(k_t \Phi)^2}{R_a} \cdot \omega$$

This is the **torque-speed characteristic** of a DC motor — a linear relationship where torque decreases as speed increases.

### Step 3.2 — Numerical Example: Servo Motor Positioning

**Problem:** A hobby **SG90 servo motor** has a PWM control signal where:
- $1.0\,ms$ pulse $\rightarrow 0^{\circ}$
- $2.0\,ms$ pulse $\rightarrow 180^{\circ}$
- PWM period $T = 20\,ms$

**Find:** (a) The required pulse width for $90^{\circ}$ rotation. (b) The corresponding duty cycle.

**Solution:**

**(a) Linear interpolation:**

$$\text{Pulse width at } 90^{\circ} = 1.0\,ms + \dfrac{90^{\circ}}{180^{\circ}} \times (2.0\,ms - 1.0\,ms)$$

$$= 1.0\,ms + 0.5 \times 1.0\,ms = 1.5\,ms$$

**(b) Duty cycle:**

$$\text{Duty} = \dfrac{t_{pulse}}{T_{period}} = \dfrac{1.5\,ms}{20\,ms} = 0.075 = 7.5\%$$

This calculation is **routinely performed** in IoT firmware when controlling smart blinds, robotic arms, or pan-tilt security cameras.

### 3.3 — Analytical Matrix: Sensor vs. Actuator

| Parameter | **Sensor** | **Actuator** |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| **Direction of Energy** | Environment → Device | Device → Environment |
| **Signal Type** | Analog/Digital (output) | Control input (electrical) → Physical (output) |
| **Role in IoT** | *Input* device | *Output* device |
| **Transduction** | Physical → Electrical | Electrical → Physical |
| **Example** | DHT22 (temp/humidity) | SG90 Servo (rotational motion) |
| **Power Source** | Usually passive (or low bias) | Requires significant power source |
| **Security Concern** | Data leakage / false data injection | Physical tampering / unauthorized actuation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Smart Object

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **internal architecture** of a typical smart object and how its sensing, processing, actuation, and communication sub-systems interact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Environment] -->|Stimulus| B[Sensor Subsystem]
    B -->|Raw Analog Signal| C[ADC and Signal Conditioning]
    C -->|Digital Data| D[Microcontroller and Processor]
    D -->|Processed Data| E[Communication Module]
    E -->|Wireless and Wired| F[Cloud or Gateway]
    F -->|Control Commands| E
    D -->|Control Signal| G[Driver Circuit]
    G -->|Power Amplified Signal| H[Actuator Subsystem]
    H -->|Physical Action| A
    D <-->|Local Storage| I[Memory and Firmware]
    J[Power Source Battery Mains] --> D
    J --> G
    J --> E
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (Environment → Sensor → ... → Actuator → Environment) is the *sense-compute-actuate* cycle.
- The **Cloud / Gateway** is optional; the device can also operate autonomously.
- The **Power Source** feeds the processor, the communication module, and (importantly) the **driver circuit** that boosts the logic-level signal to drive the actuator.

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Actuators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Actuators] --> B[Electrical]
    A --> C[Hydraulic]
    A --> D[Pneumatic]
    A --> E[Thermal]
    A --> F[Optical]
    A --> G[Chemical and Biological]
    B --> B1[DC Motor]
    B --> B2[Stepper Motor]
    B --> B3[Servo Motor]
    B --> B4[Solenoid and Relay]
    C --> C1[Linear Cylinder]
    C --> C2[Rotary Vane]
    D --> D1[Air Cylinder]
    D --> D2[Vacuum Gripper]
    E --> E1[Resistive Heater]
    E --> E2[Peltier TEC Module]
    F --> F1[LED and Laser]
    F --> F2[LCD and OLED Display]
    G --> G1[Drug Delivery Pump]
    G --> G2[Microfluidic Valve]
```

## 4.3 Sense–Compute–Actuate Loop (The Core IoT Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[1 Physical Phenomenon] --> S2[2 Sensor Conversion]
    S2 --> S3[3 ADC and Preprocessing]
    S3 --> S4[4 Microcontroller Decision]
    S4 --> S5[5 Communication to Cloud Optional]
    S4 --> S6[6 Driver and Actuator Activation]
    S6 --> S7[7 Physical Effect on Environment]
    S7 -. Feedback .-> S1
```

## 4.4 Smart Object Attack Surface (Security View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SmartObject[Smart Object]
        SE[Sensor]
        PR[Processor]
        AC[Actuator]
        CM[Comm Module]
        PW[Power]
    end
    SE -->|False Data Injection| PR
    CM -->|MITM and Replay| PR
    PR -->|Firmware Tampering| PR
    PW -->|Battery Drain DoS| PR
    PR -->|Unauthorized Command| AC
    AC -->|Physical Damage| ENV[Physical Environment]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Security Insight:** This diagram is the foundation of **IoT threat modeling** (STRIDE / PASTA). The **Actuator** is the most dangerous compromise point because it bridges the *cyber* and *physical* domains.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an actuator. List any four types of actuators with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
An **actuator** is a device that converts an electrical control signal into a **physical action** (motion, heat, light, force, etc.). It is the *output component* of an IoT system.

Four types with examples:
1. **Electrical** – DC Motor (rotates a smart fan blade).
2. **Hydraulic** – Hydraulic cylinder in a smart excavator.
3. **Pneumatic** – Air cylinder in an automated factory line.
4. **Thermal** – Resistive heater in a smart kettle.

*(Other acceptable types: Optical – LED; Chemical – Drug pump.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Smart Object? List its four key characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Smart Object** is a physical entity augmented with sensing, processing, and communication capabilities, allowing it to perceive its environment, make decisions, and exchange data over a network.

Four key characteristics:
1. **Identity** – Unique identifier (e.g., IPv6, UUID).
2. **Sensing** – Embedded sensors to collect data.
3. **Actuation** – Ability to affect its environment.
4. **Connectivity** – Network interface (Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, etc.).
*(Fifth: Intelligence / embedded processing.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Actuators Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Classify actuators based on the energy domain. Explain the working of a **DC motor** as an electrical actuator with relevant equations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **hydraulic cylinder** of piston area $A = 0.05\,m^2$ is supplied with oil at pressure $P = 2 \times 10^6\,Pa$. Calculate the force it can exert, and explain its role in industrial IoT (IIoT). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification & DC Motor Working (7 marks)**

**Classification (3 marks):** Actuators may be classified as:
- Electrical (DC motor, stepper, servo, solenoid, relay)
- Hydraulic (cylinder, rotary vane)
- Pneumatic (air cylinder)
- Thermal (heater, Peltier)
- Optical (LED, display)
- Chemical (drug pump)

**DC Motor Working (4 marks):** A DC motor consists of a stator (permanent magnet or electromagnet producing flux $\Phi$) and a rotor/armature (coil of wire). When current $I_a$ flows through the armature, it experiences a force $F = B I_a L$ (Lorentz force) within the magnetic field, producing torque:

$$T_m = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

The torque rotates the shaft, and a **commutator** reverses the current direction every half-turn to ensure continuous rotation. The rotating coil also produces a **back-EMF** $E_b = k_b \Phi \omega$ that opposes the supply voltage:

$$V = I_a R_a + E_b$$

In IoT, DC motors drive smart fans, robotic vacuum cleaners (Roomba), and conveyor belts in smart factories.

**(b) Hydraulic Force Calculation (7 marks)**

Given:
- Piston area $A = 0.05\,m^2$
- Pressure $P = 2 \times 10^6\,Pa$

Using the fundamental pressure–force relation:

$$F = P \times A = 2 \times 10^6 \times 0.05 = 1 \times 10^5\,N = 100\,kN$$

**Role in IIoT (4 marks):** Hydraulic actuators provide **very high force density** and are used in:
- Smart excavators and cranes in construction IoT.
- Hydraulic presses in smart factories.
- Heavy robotic arms in automotive assembly.
They are typically controlled by **electro-hydraulic servo valves** driven by a microcontroller, enabling precise force/position feedback for safety-critical IIoT applications.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Smart Objects & Security Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Smart Object** and describe the role of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **security vulnerabilities** associated with actuators in smart objects. Give two real-world attack examples. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Smart Object Architecture (7 marks)**

A Smart Object consists of six functional blocks:

1. **Sensor Subsystem:** Detects physical phenomena (temperature, motion, etc.) and converts them to electrical signals.
2. **ADC & Signal Conditioning:** Filters noise and converts analog signals to digital values for the processor.
3. **Microcontroller / Processor:** Runs embedded firmware, executes decision logic (edge intelligence), and orchestrates other blocks.
4. **Communication Module:** Provides wired (Ethernet, UART) or wireless (Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa) connectivity to a gateway or cloud.
5. **Driver Circuit + Actuator:** Power-amplifies the processor's logic-level signal to drive a physical actuator.
6. **Power Source:** Battery, solar, or mains supply powering all subsystems.

The data flow is: **Sensor → ADC → Processor → (optional Cloud) → Driver → Actuator → Environment**.

**(b) Actuator Security Vulnerabilities (7 marks)**

Actuators are the **most critical attack surface** in CPS/IoT because they bridge the digital and physical worlds.

**Vulnerabilities:**
1. **Unauthorized actuation** (e.g., remotely opening a smart lock).
2. **Replay attacks** on actuation commands.
3. **Firmware tampering** of driver logic.
4. **Side-channel attacks** (current/EM analysis) revealing actuator patterns.
5. **DoS attacks** causing actuator starvation (e.g., disabling a smart alarm siren).

**Real-World Attack Examples:**
- **Mirai Botnet (2016):** Exploited default credentials of IoT IP cameras, turning them into a massive DDoS botnet — actuators (PTZ motors) were abused.
- **Stuxnet (2010):** A cyber-physical attack that modified the actuation signals (variable frequency drives) of Iranian uranium centrifuges, causing physical destruction while reporting normal sensor data to operators.
- **Smart Lock Vulnerabilities:** Researchers have demonstrated replay attacks against BLE-based smart locks, tricking the actuator into unlocking without authorization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often *describe* sensors and actuators but **fail to contrast** them. Always include a comparison table in your answer.
> - In numerical problems, students **omit units** (N, Pa, J) — this costs 1 mark even if the number is correct.
> - For security questions, do not give **generic** answers like "use a strong password." Tie the answer to the *specific actuator* (e.g., "encrypt the CAN-bus messages sent to the braking actuator").
> - Avoid confusing **PWM duty cycle** with **frequency** in servo motor questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Actuator = Output device** that converts **electrical energy → physical action**.
- ✅ **Smart Object** = Sensor + Processor + Actuator + Communication + Power + Identity.
- ✅ The IoT cycle is **Sense → Compute → Actuate → Communicate → Repeat**.
- ✅ DC motor torque: $T = k_t \Phi I_a$ ; Back-EMF: $E_b = k_b \Phi \omega$.
- ✅ Hydraulic force: $F = P \times A$.
- ✅ Servo PWM: **1 ms → 0°, 1.5 ms → 90°, 2 ms → 180°** at **20 ms** period.
- ✅ Common IoT protocols: **Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, Z-Wave, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT** — each with different range/power/security trade-offs.
- ✅ Actuator classes: **Electrical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Thermal, Optical, Chemical.**
- ✅ **Security golden rule:** Compromising an actuator = **physical-world impact**, not just data loss.
- ✅ Famous actuator-related attacks: **Stuxnet, Mirai, smart-lock replay attacks.**
- ✅ Always include **units** in numerical answers (N, Pa, ms, J, RPM).
- ✅ Distinguish **Sensors (input)** from **Actuators (output)** clearly in exam answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensor Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Sensor Network

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a distributed, self-organizing network of spatially dispersed and dedicated autonomous sensors that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions, such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants, at different locations. Each sensor node in the network is typically equipped with a radio transceiver, a microcontroller, an energy source (usually a battery), and one or more sensing elements.
