The nodes forward their collected data through the network to a central location, known as the **Sink** or **Base Station (BS)**, which acts as a gateway to the outside world (Internet, cloud, or a controlling application server).

In the context of **IoT Security (PBCCT604)**, a sensor network is the foundational *perception layer* of any IoT ecosystem — the layer closest to the physical world. Understanding its architecture, limitations, and vulnerabilities is a mandatory prerequisite for designing secure IoT systems, because every attack on the higher layers (network, application) almost always begins with a compromise of a sensor or a sensor node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 3 focus on Sensor Networks emphasizes their **architectural components, routing protocols, attack surfaces, and security countermeasures** — not on hardware design or low-level electronics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Human Nervous System

Imagine your own body. You have millions of tiny *receptors* (nerve endings) embedded in your skin, eyes, ears, and internal organs. None of these receptors is powerful individually — they only sense a small stimulus. However, they are all *interconnected* through a *nervous system* (spinal cord and peripheral nerves) that carries electrical signals back to a *central brain* for interpretation and response.

A sensor network works in **exactly the same way**:

- **Sensor nodes** = nerve endings (small, cheap, energy-limited, distributed everywhere).
- **Multi-hop radio communication** = peripheral nervous system (relays signals).
- **Sink / Base Station** = brain (collects, fuses, processes, and decides).
- **Internet / Cloud** = higher cognitive functions (long-term memory, decision-making).

Just as a single nerve ending cannot do much, a single sensor node is virtually useless — it is the *collaborative, networked behavior* that produces intelligence. This is why WSNs are also called **"Smart Dust" networks** in advanced research literature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** A WSN shifts the paradigm of computing from *centralized, powerful machines* to *decentralized, tiny, energy-starved, cooperating devices*. This shift is the very reason WSNs are hard to secure — they have to be cheap, low-power, and small, leaving little room for cryptographic horsepower.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Terminology)

The following metrics are *universally* used to characterize any sensor network. They are also the parameters an attacker tries to degrade and a defender tries to preserve.

| Metric | Standard Notation | Typical Magnitude in WSN | Security Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Node Density** | $\rho$ | 10 – 100 nodes / $\text{m}^2$ | Higher density = more attack surface, but also better resilience |
| **Network Lifetime** | $T_L$ | Months to years | Energy depletion attacks try to shorten $T_L$ |
| **Data Latency** | $D_l$ | ms to seconds | Delay attacks (sinkhole, blackhole) inflate $D_l$ |
| **Throughput** | $\Theta$ | kbps | Jamming attacks crush $\Theta$ |
| **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** | $\eta$ | 0.90 – 0.99 | Goal of any routing protocol |
| **Energy per Bit** | $E_b$ | nJ / bit | Determines battery life |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All of the above six metrics appear directly in KTU 14-mark and 3-mark questions. Memorize the symbols, units, and typical magnitudes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Wireless Sensor Network

A WSN architecture is best understood in **five logical layers**, mirroring (but not identical to) the OSI model. Each layer is a *separate attack surface* for an ethical hacker to study.

### Layer 1 — Perception / Physical Layer
- Contains the actual **sensors** (temperature, humidity, PIR, accelerometer, gas, biometric, etc.).
- Performs **analog-to-digital conversion (ADC)**.
- **Physical attacks** happen here: node tampering, node destruction, node replication, side-channel (power, EM) analysis.
- **Radio-layer attacks**: jamming, tampering, radio interference.

### Layer 2 — Data Link / MAC Layer
- Governs *who speaks when* on the shared wireless medium.
- Typical protocols: **S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 (the basis of ZigBee and 6LoWPAN)**.
- **Attacks at this layer**: collision, exhaustion, unfairness, sybil.

### Layer 3 — Network / Routing Layer
- Responsible for **multi-hop path discovery** and **data forwarding** from source nodes to the sink.
- Typical protocols: **LEACH, PEGASIS, SPIN, Directed Diffusion, GPSR, AODV (modified)**.
- **Attacks at this layer**: sinkhole, blackhole, wormhole, selective forwarding, hello flood, sybil.

### Layer 4 — Transport Layer
- Ensures **reliable end-to-end delivery** and **congestion control**.
- Protocols: PSFQ, RMST, CODA, Fusion.
- **Attacks**: flooding, desynchronization.

### Layer 5 — Application Layer
- Houses the **user-facing application**: battlefield surveillance, health monitoring, industrial control, smart agriculture, etc.
- **Attacks**: data corruption, aggregation manipulation, clock skewing.

## 2.2 Components of a Sensor Node (Hardware View)

A single sensor node — the *atom* of a sensor network — has four sub-systems:

1. **Sensing Unit** — sensor(s) + ADC.
2. **Processing Unit** — microcontroller + small memory (often a few KB of RAM).
3. **Communication Unit** — short-range radio transceiver (typically 2.4 GHz ISM band).
4. **Power Unit** — usually a small, non-rechargeable battery (sometimes a solar harvester).

> [!NOTE]
> Because the power unit is *not rechargeable* in most field deployments, **energy efficiency is the single most important design constraint** of a WSN — far more important than speed, throughput, or even security in many legacy designs.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the *core quantitative relations* the KTU examiner expects you to be able to write, derive, and apply. There are no "trick" formulas — every one of these appears in standard WSN textbooks.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First-Order Radio Energy (Transmission) | $E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | $k$ = bits, $d$ = distance (m), $n \in \{2, 4\}$ path-loss exponent, $E_{elec}$ = J/bit, $\epsilon_{amp}$ = J/bit/$\text{m}^{n}$ | Computes transmission energy in LEACH-style protocols |
| 2 | First-Order Radio Energy (Reception) | $E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | $k$ = bits, $E_{elec}$ = J/bit | Reception is *not* free — it costs energy too |
| 3 | Total Energy for Multi-Hop Link of $h$ Hops | $E_{total} = h \cdot \big(E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}/h^{n-1}\big)$ | $h$ = number of hops, $d$ = end-to-end distance | Shows why many short hops save energy |
| 4 | Optimal Number of Hops | $h_{opt} = d \cdot \sqrt[n-1]{\dfrac{\epsilon_{amp}}{(n-1) \cdot E_{elec}}}$ | $d$ = total distance | Minimizes total transmission energy |
| 5 | Network Lifetime (Round-Based) | $T_L = \dfrac{E_{total}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} E_{round}^{(i)}}$ | $E_{total}$ = initial energy, $E_{round}$ = energy/round, $N$ = nodes | Standard LEACH lifetime metric |
| 6 | Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR) | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{recv}}{P_{sent}}$ | $P_{recv}$ = packets received, $P_{sent}$ = packets sent | Universally used routing metric |
| 7 | Average End-to-End Latency | $\bar{D}_l = \dfrac{1}{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} (t_{sink}^{(j)} - t_{src}^{(j)})$ | $M$ = total packets, $t$ = timestamps | Quality-of-Service metric |
| 8 | Node Coverage Probability (Poisson Field) | $P_{cov} = 1 - e^{-\rho \cdot \pi R^{2}}$ | $\rho$ = density, $R$ = sensing radius | Used in coverage attacks |
| 9 | Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR) | $\text{SINR} = \dfrac{P_t \cdot G_{tx} \cdot G_{rx} \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{\alpha} \cdot (N + I)}$ | $P_t$ = transmit power, $G$ = antenna gains, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $\alpha$ = path-loss exponent, $N$ = noise, $I$ = interference | Central to jamming analysis |
| 10 | LEACH Cluster Head Selection Probability | $P_{CH} = \dfrac{k}{N}$ where $k$ = optimal clusters | $N$ = total nodes | Probabilistic rotation, balances energy |

> [!WARNING]
> Do **NOT** confuse $E_{elec}$ (energy per bit *for the circuitry*, e.g., 50 nJ/bit) with $\epsilon_{amp}$ (energy per bit per $\text{m}^{n}$ for the *amplifier*, e.g., 10 pJ/bit/$\text{m}^{2}$). They are two completely different constants and the most common mistake in KTU derivations.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in IoT Security

Every formula above can be **weaponized by an attacker** or **defended by a security engineer**:

- An attacker launching a **jamming attack** aims to push the SINR below the receiver's threshold, effectively setting the *useful throughput* to zero.
- A **sinkhole attack** tricks nearby nodes into routing *all* their data through a malicious node, draining its battery — directly impacting $E_{total}$ and $T_L$.
- A **selective forwarding attack** lowers $\eta$ by silently dropping packets, breaching the integrity goal of the CIA triad.

A security-aware WSN designer therefore does not optimize only for $T_L$ — they optimize for $T_L$ *under attack*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman Model)

The Heinzelman, Chandrakasan, Balakrishnan (HCB) first-order radio model is the single most cited energy model in WSN literature. The KTU examiner frequently asks you to write it from memory and apply it to a small network.

### Step 1 — Define the Assumption
We assume a symmetric channel: energy spent transmitting $k$ bits over distance $d$ equals the energy the receiver consumes to *process* $k$ bits, plus a distance-dependent transmit amplifier term.

### Step 2 — Write the Transmission Energy

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{Tx}(k, d) &= E_{Tx\text{-}elec}(k) + E_{Tx\text{-}amp}(k, d) \\
&= E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n} \\
&= k \cdot \big(E_{elec} + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The first term $E_{elec} \cdot k$ is the *digital* cost (running the radio electronics for $k$ bits). The second term $\epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ is the *analog* cost — the amplifier must pump out more power the farther the receiver is. The exponent $n$ is the **path-loss exponent**: $n = 2$ for free-space line-of-sight, $n = 4$ for multipath urban / foliage environments.

### Step 3 — Write the Reception Energy

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{Rx}(k) &= E_{Rx\text{-}elec}(k) \\
&= E_{elec} \cdot k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Reception does *not* have an amplifier term — it just runs the receiver electronics. This is why, in many textbooks, $E_{Rx} = E_{elec} \cdot k$ alone is given.

### Step 4 — Multi-Hop Extension
Suppose we want to send $k$ bits over a *total* distance $d$ using $h$ equal hops. Each hop covers distance $d/h$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{multi-hop} &= h \cdot \Big[E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot \big(\tfrac{d}{h}\big)^{n}\Big] \\
&= h \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot \dfrac{d^{n}}{h^{n-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** As $h$ increases, the first term grows linearly (more receivers running), while the second term shrinks polynomially (each hop is shorter). The optimum is the *balance point* where the derivative equals zero.

### Step 5 — Derive the Optimal Number of Hops

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dE_{multi-hop}}{dh} &= E_{elec} \cdot k - (n-1) \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot \dfrac{d^{n}}{h^{n}} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{elec} &= (n-1) \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot \dfrac{d^{n}}{h^{n}} \\
h^{n} &= (n-1) \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot \dfrac{d^{n}}{E_{elec}} \\
h &= d \cdot \sqrt[n-1]{\dfrac{(n-1) \cdot \epsilon_{amp}}{E_{elec}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

But the textbook result is usually written more cleanly as:

$$
\boxed{\; h_{opt} = d \cdot \sqrt[n-1]{\dfrac{\epsilon_{amp}}{(n-1) \cdot E_{elec}}} \;}
$$

Wait — careful re-derivation. Equating derivatives correctly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dE_{multi-hop}}{dh} &= E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n} \cdot \big[-(n-1)\big] \cdot h^{-n} \\
&= k \cdot \Big( E_{elec} - (n-1) \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot \dfrac{d^{n}}{h^{n}} \Big) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{elec} &= (n-1) \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot \dfrac{d^{n}}{h_{opt}^{n}} \\
h_{opt}^{n} &= \dfrac{(n-1) \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}}{E_{elec}} \\
h_{opt} &= d \cdot \sqrt[n]{\dfrac{(n-1) \cdot \epsilon_{amp}}{E_{elec}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final clean boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\; h_{opt} = d \cdot \sqrt[n]{\dfrac{(n-1) \cdot \epsilon_{amp}}{E_{elec}}} \;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the exponent is $n$ (not $n-1$) on the outer root. Many students write the wrong root in the exam and lose 1–2 marks. Memorize the *final* form, not the intermediate one.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Network Lifetime Under Periodic Reporting

A common KTU 14-mark question: *"All $N$ nodes have equal initial energy $E_0$. Each node sends one $k$-bit packet per round to the sink at distance $d$ directly. Compute the network lifetime in rounds."*

### Step 1 — Energy Per Round, Per Node
By the first-order model with $n=2$:

$$
E_{round} = E_{Tx}(k, d) = k \cdot (E_{elec} + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{2})
$$

### Step 2 — Total Initial Energy in the Network

$$
E_{total} = N \cdot E_0
$$

### Step 3 — Lifetime (Rounds Until First Node Dies, FND)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_L &= \dfrac{E_{total}}{N \cdot E_{round}} \\
&= \dfrac{N \cdot E_0}{N \cdot k \cdot (E_{elec} + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{2})} \\
&= \dfrac{E_0}{k \cdot (E_{elec} + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{2})}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; T_L = \dfrac{E_0}{k \cdot (E_{elec} + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{2})} \;}
$$

**Logic:** The $N$ cancels — which is the *elegant* insight. Doubling the number of nodes does *not* change the FND lifetime in this symmetric model; it just doubles the total data collected. This is the reason LEACH and other cluster-based protocols exist: to break the symmetry and let some nodes sleep.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Each Concept to a Security Attack

This is the *most exam-relevant* summary you can internalize — it is a direct mapping from a *defensive* concept to its *offensive* counterpart.

| Defensive Concept | Normal Goal | Attacker Action | Resulting Metric Damage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy efficiency | Maximize $T_L$ | Jamming forces retransmission | $T_L \downarrow$, $E_{total} \downarrow$ |
| Routing optimality | Maximize $\eta$ | Blackhole drops all packets | $\eta \to 0$ |
| Path diversity | Avoid single point of failure | Wormhole short-circuits topology | $\eta \downarrow$, security boundary violated |
| Authentication | Verify identity | Sybil injects fake identities | Coverage, voting, aggregation corrupted |
| Time synchronization | Align TDMA slots | Desynchronization shifts slots | Collision rate $\uparrow$, $\eta \downarrow$ |
| In-network aggregation | Reduce traffic | Inject false aggregates | Application-layer integrity broken |
| Localization | Position-tag every reading | Replay / GPS-spoofing | False alarms, wrong actuation |

> [!TIP]
> A 7-mark KTU sub-question often says: *"Explain how a blackhole attack affects the routing layer and name two defenses."* The matrix above is your instant answer template.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level WSN Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the complete functional architecture of a Wireless Sensor Network as it interacts with the broader IoT ecosystem. It maps every component to its corresponding OSI/IoT layer and the type of attack each one is exposed to.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIELD["PHYSICAL FIELD / MONITORING REGION"]
        N1["Sensor Node 1\nTemp + Humidity"]
        N2["Sensor Node 2\nPIR Motion"]
        N3["Sensor Node 3\nGas / Chemical"]
        N4["Sensor Node 4\nVibration"]
        N5["Sensor Node 5\nCamera / Acoustic"]
    end

    subgraph ROUTING["MULTI-HOP MESH ROUTING LAYER"]
        R1["Relay Node A"]
        R2["Relay Node B"]
        R3["Relay Node C"]
    end

    subgraph SINK["SINK / BASE STATION GATEWAY"]
        BS["Base Station BS\nData Fusion + Crypto"]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE / FOG LAYER"]
        EDGE_NODE["Edge Gateway\nLocal Analytics"]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD APPLICATION LAYER"]
        APP["IoT Dashboard\nML Analytics\nActuator Control"]
    end

    subgraph ATTACKS["ATTACK SURFACES"]
        A1["Physical Tampering"]
        A2["Jamming"]
        A3["Sinkhole / Blackhole"]
        A4["Sybil / Hello Flood"]
        A5["Replay / MITM"]
    end

    N1 --> R1
    N2 --> R1
    N3 --> R2
    N4 --> R2
    N5 --> R3
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> BS
    BS --> EDGE_NODE
    EDGE_NODE --> APP

    A1 -.->|targets| N1
    A2 -.->|targets| R1
    A3 -.->|targets| R2
    A4 -.->|targets| N3
    A5 -.->|targets| BS

    classDef fieldNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef relayNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef sinkNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef edgeNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef cloudNode fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#4a235a,color:#000
    classDef attackNode fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

    class N1,N2,N3,N4,N5 fieldNode
    class R1,R2,R3 relayNode
    class BS sinkNode
    class EDGE_NODE edgeNode
    class APP cloudNode
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 attackNode
```

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Single Sensor Node (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS["Physical Sensor\nTemp / Motion / Gas"]
    ADC["Analog to Digital\nConverter ADC"]
    MCU["Microcontroller\nCPU + RAM + ROM"]
    MEM["Local Storage\nEEPROM / Flash"]
    RADIO["Radio Transceiver\n2.4 GHz IEEE 802.15.4"]
    ANT["Antenna"]
    PWR["Power Unit\nBattery + Harvester"]
    SEC["Hardware Crypto\nAES / SHA Engine"]

    SENS --> ADC
    ADC --> MCU
    MCU <--> MEM
    MCU --> RADIO
    RADIO --> ANT
    PWR -.->|energizes| SENS
    PWR -.->|energizes| ADC
    PWR -.->|energizes| MCU
    PWR -.->|energizes| RADIO
    SEC -.->|secures| MCU
    SEC -.->|secures| RADIO
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **power unit (battery)** is drawn in dashed lines because it is *not* a data-flow component — it merely *supplies energy* to every active block. In KTU diagrams, a dashed line almost always denotes "supply" rather than "data".
- The **hardware crypto engine** is a *modern* addition. In legacy MICAz or TelosB motes it was absent, which is why those platforms are now considered insecure.
- The **radio + antenna** pair is the *only* block that crosses the node's physical boundary — it is therefore the *primary* attack surface for over-the-air adversaries.

## 4.3 Routing Protocol Taxonomy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["WSN Routing Protocols"]

    ROOT --> PRO["Proactive\nTable Driven e.g. DSDV"]
    ROOT --> REA["Reactive\nOn Demand e.g. AODV"]
    ROOT --> HIER["Hierarchical / Cluster\nLEACH, PEGASIS, HEED"]
    ROOT --> LOC["Location Based\nGPSR, GEAR"]
    ROOT --> DATA["Data Centric\nSPIN, Directed Diffusion"]

    HIER --> LEACH["LEACH\nRandom CH rotation"]
    HIER --> PEGASIS["PEGASIS\nChain based"]
    HIER --> HEED["HEED\nEnergy aware CH"]

    DATA --> SPIN["SPIN\nMeta data negotiation"]
    DATA --> DD["Directed Diffusion\nInterest based"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024, the **Hierarchical** family (LEACH in particular) is the highest-yield sub-topic because it is energy-aware, has an elegant stochastic analysis, and is the most commonly asked 14-mark design question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment pattern:
> - **Part A**: 3-mark short-answer (typically 2 questions of 3 marks each in ESE; sometimes appear as 3-mark in-series questions).
> - **Part B**: 14-mark descriptive, with **internal choice** between two full questions. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]`
**Define a Wireless Sensor Network. List any three application domains.**

**Model Answer (concise, 3-mark phrasing):**

A Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) is a self-configuring infrastructure comprising spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions and forward their readings via multi-hop wireless links to a central sink.

**Three application domains:**
1. **Military / Battlefield surveillance** — enemy troop movement, nuclear-biological-chemical (NBC) attack detection.
2. **Environmental monitoring** — forest fire detection, flood warning, precision agriculture, wildlife tracking.
3. **Healthcare** — patient body-area monitoring (heart rate, glucose), elderly fall detection.
4. **Industrial IoT** — predictive maintenance of rotating machinery, pipeline leak detection, smart-grid monitoring.

*(Any three well-explained domains get full 3 marks. Naming alone without 1-line application examples loses 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]`
**Differentiate between proactive and reactive routing in WSNs. Give one example protocol of each.**

**Model Answer (3-mark comparison table style):**

| Feature | Proactive (Table-Driven) | Reactive (On-Demand) |
|---|---|---|
| **Route establishment** | Maintained continuously, even before traffic | Built only when a node wants to send |
| **Latency on first packet** | Low (route ready) | High (route discovery delay) |
| **Control overhead** | High (periodic updates) | Low (only when needed) |
| **Energy cost** | High (constant beacons) | Lower for sparse traffic |
| **Example** | DSDV (Destination-Sequenced Distance Vector) | AODV (Ad-hoc On-demand Distance Vector) |
| **Best suited to** | Stable topologies, periodic reporting | Event-driven, bursty traffic |

*(Comparison in *bullet* form is acceptable for 3 marks. Use of a clean table is preferred for visual impact.)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

`[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]`
**a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **internal architecture of a sensor node**. Explain the function of each block and discuss the design challenges imposed by energy, memory, and security constraints. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the **first-order radio energy model** used in WSNs. Derive the optimal number of hops for a multi-hop transmission and discuss its engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Block diagram (2 marks)** — reproduce the sensor node block diagram from Section 4.2 above. Label all six blocks (Sensing, ADC, Processing/MCU, Memory, Radio/Transceiver, Power). Use dashed lines for energy supply.

2. **Function of each block (3 marks)** —
   - **Sensing unit** detects the physical phenomenon (temperature, pressure, motion) and produces an analog voltage.
   - **ADC** converts the analog signal into a digital bit stream.
   - **Processing unit (MCU)** executes the routing protocol, runs the application code, and performs local aggregation.
   - **Memory** stores the program, routing tables, and recently collected samples.
   - **Communication unit (radio + antenna)** transmits and receives packets over the shared wireless medium.
   - **Power unit (battery + harvester)** supplies regulated voltage to all blocks.

3. **Design challenges (2 marks)** —
   - *Energy:* batteries are tiny and non-rechargeable in most field deployments, so every microjoule matters; the radio and the MCU must spend > 99 % of their time in sleep.
   - *Memory:* typically 4–10 KB of RAM and 48–128 KB of flash, so routing tables must be highly compact (e.g., gradient-based rather than full link-state).
   - *Security:* there is no room for full TLS stacks or PKI; lightweight symmetric ciphers (AES-128) and elliptic-curve variants are used.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

1. **State the model (1.5 marks)** — write the two equations for $E_{Tx}(k, d)$ and $E_{Rx}(k)$ as in Section 3.1, Steps 2 and 3.

2. **Multi-hop extension (1.5 marks)** — express $E_{multi-hop}$ for $h$ equal hops as in Step 4.

3. **Derivative and root finding (3 marks)** — set $\frac{dE_{multi-hop}}{dh} = 0$ and arrive at the boxed formula:

$$
h_{opt} = d \cdot \sqrt[n]{\dfrac{(n-1) \cdot \epsilon_{amp}}{E_{elec}}}
$$

4. **Engineering significance (1 mark)** — using multi-hop with $h \approx h_{opt}$ drastically reduces per-node transmit energy because the amplifier term scales with $d^{n}$ and breaking the distance into $h$ smaller pieces reduces the exponent impact. This is the *fundamental justification* for hierarchical routing protocols like LEACH and PEGASIS.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

`[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]`
**a)** What are the **common attacks on WSN routing protocols**? Classify them by layer and describe the **sinkhole attack**, **selective forwarding attack**, and **Sybil attack** in detail with diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** protocol. Discuss cluster-head selection, steady-state operation, and how it improves network lifetime over direct transmission. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Classification of attacks (2 marks):**

| Layer | Common Attacks |
|---|---|
| Physical | Node tampering, node destruction, side-channel |
| MAC / Data Link | Collision, exhaustion, jamming |
| Network / Routing | Sinkhole, blackhole, wormhole, hello flood, sybil, selective forwarding |
| Transport | Flooding, desynchronization |
| Application | Data corruption, clock skewing, false aggregation |

2. **Sinkhole attack (2 marks):** A malicious node advertises a *very attractive* routing metric (e.g., shortest path to sink, highest residual energy, or lowest hop count). All neighbouring nodes forward their data to it, believing it is the optimal next hop. The attacker then either drops the packets (becoming a blackhole) or selectively forwards them. Countermeasures: geographic routing, multi-path routing, authentication of routing metrics.

3. **Selective forwarding attack (1.5 marks):** A compromised node silently drops a *fraction* (not all) of the packets it is supposed to relay, making detection harder. Countermeasures: multi-path routing, watchdog monitors, end-to-end acknowledgements, redundancy coding (e.g., reed-solomon).

4. **Sybil attack (1.5 marks):** A single malicious node presents multiple fake identities to its neighbours, manipulating routing, voting, aggregation, and reputation systems. Countermeasures: identity registration with the base station, radio-resource testing, position verification.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

1. **LEACH overview (1 mark):** LEACH is a *cluster-based, randomized, self-organizing* protocol that rotates the role of Cluster Head (CH) among all nodes to distribute the energy load evenly.

2. **Cluster-Head selection (2 marks):** Each node decides *independently* in round $r$ whether to become a CH by choosing a random number $0 < \mu < 1$ and computing:

$$
T(n) = 
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{P_{CH}}{1 - P_{CH} \cdot (r \bmod \dfrac{1}{P_{CH}})} & \text{if } n \in G \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where $P_{CH} = k / N$ is the desired CH fraction, and $G$ is the set of nodes that have not yet been CH in the last $1/P_{CH}$ rounds.

3. **Steady-state operation (2 marks):** Once clusters are formed, each member node sends its data to its CH using TDMA schedules (issued by the CH), and the CH aggregates and compresses the data, then transmits the aggregate to the sink using spread-spectrum (DS-SS) to conserve energy.

4. **Lifetime improvement (2 marks):** Compared to direct transmission, LEACH achieves roughly an $8 \times$ improvement in first-node-death (FND) lifetime for typical $100\text{ m} \times 100\text{ m}$ deployments. The improvement arises from (i) shortened intra-cluster distances (energy scales as $d^{2}$), (ii) data aggregation at CHs (typically a $5 \times$ reduction in traffic to sink), and (iii) randomized CH rotation (avoids permanent battery drain on any single node).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to define variables** when using formulas like $E_{Tx}(k, d)$. The examiner deducts up to 1 mark if $E_{elec}$, $\epsilon_{amp}$, $n$, and units are not declared.
> 2. **Wrong root exponent** in the optimal-hop derivation: the outer root is the $n$-th root, not $(n-1)$. Cost: 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing PDR with throughput** in question answers. PDR is a *ratio* (0–1), throughput has units of bits/sec.
> 4. **In LEACH questions**, students often forget to explain *why* CH rotation matters (it balances the energy map). Simply stating "it is randomized" is worth only 1 of 2 marks.
> 5. **In attack-classification questions**, do not list attacks *without* stating the layer. Layer + attack + 1-line impact = 1 full mark.
> 6. **Diagrams without labels** receive at most 50 % of the diagram marks. Always label blocks, arrows, and (where relevant) energy flow.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- A **WSN** is a *self-organizing, multi-hop, energy-constrained, data-centric* network of spatially distributed sensor nodes forwarding data to a sink.
- The **sink / base station** is the gateway to the outside world; it has *unlimited* power and compute compared to sensor nodes.
- A **sensor node** has 4 logical units: sensing, processing, communication, power. Modern nodes also have a hardware crypto engine.
- The **first-order radio model** is the canonical energy model: $E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ with $n \in \{2, 4\}$.
- **Multi-hop transmission** is always more energy-efficient than direct transmission beyond a critical distance — this is the foundation of all hierarchical protocols.
- The **optimal number of hops** for minimum transmission energy is $h_{opt} = d \cdot \sqrt[n]{\dfrac{(n-1) \cdot \epsilon_{amp}}{E_{elec}}}$.
- **LEACH** rotates Cluster Heads probabilistically ($P_{CH} = k/N$) and uses data aggregation, yielding roughly an order-of-magnitude lifetime gain.
- **Routing attacks** (sinkhole, blackhole, wormhole, sybil, selective forwarding) target the network layer and degrade PDR, latency, and lifetime.
- **Jamming** targets the physical/MAC layer and crushes SINR and throughput.
- **Security in WSNs** is constrained by energy, memory, and compute — hence the use of lightweight symmetric cryptography (AES-128), elliptic-curve primitives, and key pre-distribution schemes (e.g., EG, q-composite).
- **Defenses** include multi-path routing, watchdog monitors, geographic routing, identity registration, time-synchronization protocols (TPSN, FTSP), and intrusion detection tailored to resource-constrained nodes.
- **Memorize** the six standard WSN metrics: density $\rho$, lifetime $T_L$, latency $D_l$, throughput $\Theta$, PDR $\eta$, energy-per-bit $E_b$.
- **Memorize** the three constants in the energy model: $E_{elec}$ ≈ 50 nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{amp}$ ≈ 10 pJ/bit/$\text{m}^{2}$ (free space) and 0.0013 pJ/bit/$\text{m}^{4}$ (multipath).
- For 14-mark questions, **always include a labeled block diagram** (sensor node OR WSN architecture) and **always derive, not just state**, the energy equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connecting Smart Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Connecting Smart Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Smart Objects (a.k.a. Smart Things)** are autonomous, physical or virtual entities equipped with **sensing, processing, communication, and actuation capabilities** that allow them to perceive their environment, exchange data with other objects or systems, and react intelligently. When these smart objects are interconnected to share sensed data and actuate responses, the process is formally termed **"Connecting Smart Objects."**

According to the IoT reference model, *connecting smart objects* encompasses the **edge tier connectivity layer** — the communication fabric that bridges physical transducers (sensors/actuators) to the network/Internet and ultimately to the application/cloud services. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames this through:

- **Communication criteria** (range, bandwidth, energy, security, topology).
- **Connectivity technologies** (PAN, LAN, WAN, LPWAN).
- **Communication models** (Device-to-Device, Device-to-Cloud, Device-to-Gateway, Back-End Data Sharing).
- **Gateway functions** (protocol translation, local processing, security enforcement).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The official module statement emphasizes four pillars — *IoT Communication Criteria, IoT Connectivity Technologies, Gateways, and Communication Models.* Every answer in this section must map back to one of these.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a smart home. Each "smart object" — a smart bulb, a motion sensor, a smart lock, a thermostat — is like an employee in a large office. On Day 1, every employee speaks a different language:

- The motion sensor *whispers* through ZigBee.
- The smart bulb *shouts* over Wi-Fi.
- The smart meter *mails letters* over LTE-M.
- The smart lock *taps* using NFC.

On their own, they cannot collaborate. You need two things:

1. A **translator** at each desk (a **Gateway / Edge router**).
2. A **common set of rules** for the office to function (a **protocol stack** — MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, etc.).

The act of bringing them all together, plugging them into a common language, and giving them a route to the "central boss" (the cloud/server) is exactly what **Connecting Smart Objects** means in IoT engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** Connecting smart objects is NOT just "putting Wi-Fi in everything." It is the *deliberate engineering choice* of a radio, a protocol, a topology, and a gateway that best matches the **range, data rate, power budget, and security profile** of the use case.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following standard metrics govern every connectivity decision:

| Metric | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Range (d)** | 0.01 m – 1000+ km | m, km |
| **Data Rate (R)** | 1 kbps – 10 Gbps | bps, kbps, Mbps, Gbps |
| **Transmit Power (P_tx)** | -30 dBm – +33 dBm | dBm |
| **Receiver Sensitivity (S_min)** | -85 to -146 dBm | dBm |
| **Path Loss (PL)** | 30 – 130 dB | dB |
| **Latency (L)** | 1 ms – 60 s | ms, s |
| **Duty Cycle (DC)** | ≤ 1 % (Sub-GHz EU) | % |
| **Battery Lifetime** | 1 – 15 years | years |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Friis Free-Space Equation** (used as the fundamental link-budget formula) is:

$$P_r \;=\; P_t \, G_t \, G_r \,\Big(\dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\Big)^{2}$$

where $P_r$ is the received power, $P_t$ the transmitted power, $G_t$ and $G_r$ the antenna gains, $\lambda$ the wavelength, and $d$ the distance. This equation drives the *why* behind every range/coverage calculation a KTU question can demand.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Communication Criteria (the "Why" of Connectivity Choices)

Connecting a smart object is governed by a **multi-criteria optimization problem**. The five canonical criteria are:

### 2.1.1 Range
The maximum distance over which two smart objects can reliably exchange frames. It is a function of **transmit power, antenna gain, frequency, and receiver sensitivity**.

- **Short range (≤ 100 m)** → BLE, ZigBee, RFID, NFC.
- **Medium range (100 m – 1 km)** → Wi-Fi, Z-Wave.
- **Long range (≥ 1 km)** → LoRa, SigFox, NB-IoT, LTE-M, Satellite.

### 2.1.2 Bandwidth (Data Rate)
The volume of data exchanged per unit time. Smart meters may need only **100 bps**; an industrial camera may need **10 Mbps**. Selecting an under- or over-spec technology wastes energy and money.

### 2.1.3 Power / Energy Consumption
Many smart objects are battery-powered for **10+ years**. The energy budget is governed by:

$$E_{frame} \;=\; V_{bat} \cdot I_{tx} \cdot t_{frame} \;+\; V_{bat} \cdot I_{rx} \cdot t_{rx} \;+\; V_{bat} \cdot I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}$$

A device with average current draw $I_{avg}$ from a battery of capacity $C$ (in mAh) lasts:

$$T_{life} \;=\; \dfrac{C}{I_{avg}} \quad \text{(hours)}$$

### 2.1.4 Security
Confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and freshness. Cryptographic cost (AES-128 vs AES-256 vs ECC) directly impacts energy and CPU.

### 2.1.5 Topology & Network Layer
- **Star** (single coordinator/hub).
- **Mesh** (multi-hop, self-healing — ZigBee, Thread).
- **Point-to-Point** (NFC, BLE).
- **Point-to-Multipoint** (LoRaWAN gateways).

---

## 2.2 Communication Models (How Smart Objects Connect)

The **IoT World Forum Reference Model** identifies **four canonical communication models**:

1. **Device-to-Device (D2D)** — Direct, local exchange (BLE pairing, NFC tap, ZigBee peer).
2. **Device-to-Cloud (D2C)** — The object talks directly to an Internet cloud (a Wi-Fi camera → AWS IoT).
3. **Device-to-Gateway (D2G)** — The object talks to a local gateway that bridges to the cloud (BLE sensor → Smartphone → Cloud). This is the **dominant model in industrial and home IoT**.
4. **Back-End Data-Sharing Model** — Multiple cloud services exchange data (AWS IoT ↔ Azure IoT Hub ↔ Google BigQuery).

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | What happens | Why it matters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Sense | Sensor ADC samples physical world | Converts physical → electrical |
| 2. Process | MCU compresses, encrypts, signs | Reduces payload, secures data |
| 3. Transmit | Radio sends a MAC frame | Wireless domain entry |
| 4. Receive | Gateway / peer listens on the channel | Reliable delivery |
| 5. Translate | Gateway converts MQTT ↔ HTTP | Protocol interoperability |
| 6. Forward | Routed to cloud / server | Application processing |
| 7. Actuate | Decision flows back to actuator | Closed-loop control |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most-asked reference** in KTU Module-3 viva and 14-mark questions. Memorize the *boundary values*, not just the names.

| Technology | Standard | Frequency Band | Range (typ.) | Data Rate | Topology | Power | Security |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NFC** | ISO/IEC 14443 / 15693 | 13.56 MHz | ≤ 10 cm | 106 – 424 kbps | P2P | Very Low | ISO 14443-3 |
| **RFID** | ISO/IEC 18000 | 120 kHz – 5.8 GHz | 0.1 – 10 m | 4 – 640 kbps | Tag-Reader | Passive/Active | EPC Gen2 |
| **Bluetooth (Classic)** | IEEE 802.15.1 | 2.4 GHz | ≤ 100 m | 1 – 3 Mbps | Piconet/Scatternet | Low | E0/SAP, AES |
| **BLE** | IEEE 802.15.1 (4.x/5.x) | 2.4 GHz | ≤ 100 m | 125 kbps – 2 Mbps | Star | Ultra-Low | AES-128 |
| **ZigBee** | IEEE 802.15.4 | 2.4 GHz | 10 – 100 m | 250 kbps | Mesh/Star | Low | AES-128 CCM* |
| **Z-Wave** | ITU-T G.9959 | 868/908 MHz | ≤ 30 m | 9.6 – 100 kbps | Mesh | Low | AES-128 |
| **Wi-Fi** | IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | 50 – 200 m | 11 Mbps – 9.6 Gbps | Star | Medium | WPA3 / AES-256 |
| **Thread** | IEEE 802.15.4 + 6LoWPAN | 2.4 GHz | ≤ 100 m | 250 kbps | Mesh | Low | AES-128 |
| **LoRa** | LoRaWAN (R1.x) | 433/868/915 MHz | 2 – 15 km | 0.3 – 50 kbps | Star-of-Stars | Ultra-Low | AES-128 |
| **SigFox** | SigFox proprietary | 868/902 MHz | 10 – 50 km | 100 – 600 bps | Star | Ultra-Low | Lightweight |
| **NB-IoT** | 3GPP Cat-NB1/NB2 | 700 – 900 MHz | ≤ 35 km | 26 – 159 kbps DL | Star | Low | 3GPP LTE Security |
| **LTE-M (Cat-M1)** | 3GPP | 700 – 900 MHz | ≤ 100 km | 300 kbps – 1 Mbps | Star | Low | 3GPP LTE Security |
| **5G eMBB / mMTC / URLLC** | 3GPP NR | Sub-6 GHz / mmWave | ≤ 10 km / NLOS | 1 Gbps+ | Cell | Medium | SUCI/SUPI, 5G-AKA |
| **Satellite IoT** | Iridium / Inmarsat / NB-IoT NTN | L/S/Ku/Ka band | Global | 1 – 100 kbps | Star | Medium | Proprietary |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule of Thumb (used in KTU 14-mark answers):**
> - **PAN** = personal space (~10 m) → BLE, ZigBee, NFC.
> - **LAN** = local building/office → Wi-Fi, Z-Wave.
> - **WAN** = city/global → Cellular (NB-IoT, LTE-M, 5G).
> - **LPWAN** = low-power, long-range → LoRa, SigFox.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensors over **LoRaWAN** (10 km range, 0.3 kbps, 10-year battery).
- **Smart Health (Wearables):** Heart-rate monitor over **BLE** (3 mW peak, 1 Mbps, smartphone gateway).
- **Smart Logistics:** Asset tracking with **RFID** + **NB-IoT** + **Satellite** (multi-modal).
- **Smart Industry (IIoT):** Vibration sensors over **ZigBee mesh** in factories.
- **Smart City:** Streetlights / waste bins over **NB-IoT / LTE-M**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners' Pet Question:** *"Why is ZigBee not used for video streaming?"* — Answer: its **250 kbps data rate and 2.4 GHz interference with Wi-Fi** make it unsuitable. Always justify with **numbers from the cheat-sheet table**.

---

## 2.6 Application Protocols for Smart Object Connectivity

Once the **physical** connection is established, **application protocols** (layer 7) govern data exchange:

| Protocol | Transport | Messaging Model | Header Overhead | Smart Object Fit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **HTTP/1.1** | TCP | Request/Response | ~800 bytes | Low (heavy) |
| **MQTT** | TCP | Pub/Sub (3 QoS levels) | 2-byte fixed | High |
| **CoAP** | UDP | Request/Response + Observe | 4-byte | Very High |
| **AMQP** | TCP | Pub/Sub + Queues | 8-byte | Medium |
| **DDS** | UDP/TCP | Real-time Pub/Sub | Variable | Industrial / Vehicular |
| **LwM2M** | UDP/CoAP | Object model | 4-byte | Massive IoT / NB-IoT |

---

## 2.7 The IoT Gateway — The Hidden Hero

The **gateway** is the device that translates a **PAN / LAN** protocol into a **WAN / IP** protocol. Its functions are:

1. **Protocol translation** (BLE ↔ MQTT).
2. **Local data buffering** (offline resilience).
3. **Security enforcement** (firewall, TLS termination, device attestation).
4. **Edge analytics** (running TinyML / pre-processing).
5. **Network address translation** (NAT / IPv6 ↔ IPv4).
6. **Firmware update orchestration (FOTA)** for connected smart objects.

Common gateway chipsets: **TI CC2652R (ZigBee/BLE/Thread)**, **Qualcomm QCA4020 (Multi-mode)**, **NXP i.MX RT1060 (Edge compute)**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Free-Space Path Loss and Link Budget

The **Friis Transmission Equation** is the bedrock of any range calculation. KTU questions frequently ask: *"Given transmit power, frequency, and receiver sensitivity, find the maximum range."*

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

**Step 1 — Start with the Friis Equation (received power).**

$$P_r \;=\; P_t \, G_t \, G_r \,\Big(\dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\Big)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Re-arrange to isolate the distance $d$.**

Take the square root of both sides:

$$\sqrt{P_r} \;=\; \sqrt{P_t \, G_t \, G_r} \;\cdot\; \dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$.**

$$d \;=\; \dfrac{\sqrt{P_t \, G_t \, G_r} \;\cdot\; \lambda}{4 \pi \, \sqrt{P_r}}$$

**Step 4 — Express as a Path-Loss equation in dB.**

In linear scale, path loss is:

$$PL \;=\; \dfrac{P_t}{P_r} \;\cdot\; G_t G_r$$

Substituting Step 1:

$$PL \;=\; \dfrac{P_t}{P_t G_t G_r \,\big(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\big)^{2}} \;\cdot\; G_t G_r \;=\; \Big(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\Big)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB.**

$$PL_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 10 \,\log_{10}\!\Big(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\Big)^{2} \;=\; 20 \,\log_{10}\!\Big(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\Big)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\lambda = c / f$.**

$$PL_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 20 \,\log_{10}\!\Big(\dfrac{4 \pi d \, f}{c}\Big)$$

**Step 7 — Express with $\log_{10}$ in standard engineering form.**

Using $20 \log_{10}(4\pi / c) = -147.55$ at SI units:

$$PL_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 20 \,\log_{10}(d) \;+\; 20 \,\log_{10}(f) \;-\; 147.55$$

where $d$ is in meters and $f$ is in Hz.

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A smart object transmits at $P_t = +10$ dBm on $f = 2.4$ GHz with antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi. Receiver sensitivity is $S_{min} = -95$ dBm. Find the **maximum free-space range** assuming no other losses.

**Step 1 — Compute the received-power threshold** (must be ≥ sensitivity):

$$P_{r,\min} \;=\; S_{\min} \;=\; -95 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 2 — Convert all quantities to dBm / dBi.**

$$P_t \;=\; +10 \text{ dBm}, \quad G_t = G_r = +2 \text{ dBi}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Friis in dB.**

$$P_{r,\text{dBm}} \;=\; P_{t,\text{dBm}} \;+\; G_{t,\text{dBi}} \;+\; G_{r,\text{dBi}} \;-\; PL_{\text{dB}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum allowable $PL$.**

$$PL_{\max} \;=\; P_t + G_t + G_r - S_{\min}$$

$$PL_{\max} \;=\; 10 + 2 + 2 - (-95) \;=\; 10 + 2 + 2 + 95 \;=\; 109 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $d$ using the FSPL equation.**

$$PL_{\max} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(d) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(f) \;-\; 147.55$$

$$109 \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(d) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(2.4 \times 10^{9}) \;-\; 147.55$$

**Step 6 — Compute the frequency term.**

$$20 \log_{10}(2.4 \times 10^{9}) \;=\; 20 \cdot 9.38 \;=\; 187.6 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and isolate $\log_{10}(d)$.**

$$109 \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(d) \;+\; 187.6 \;-\; 147.55$$

$$109 \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(d) \;+\; 40.05$$

$$20 \log_{10}(d) \;=\; 109 - 40.05 \;=\; 68.95$$

$$\log_{10}(d) \;=\; \dfrac{68.95}{20} \;=\; 3.4475$$

**Step 8 — Compute $d$.**

$$d_{\max} \;=\; 10^{3.4475} \;=\; 2803 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 2.8 \text{ km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **free-space upper bound**. Real-world propagation reduces this by **outdoor/indoor fading margins of 20 – 40 dB**, dropping the practical range to roughly **100 – 300 m** for a 2.4 GHz device — which matches the real-world Wi-Fi/ZigBee specification.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Connectivity Selection Decision

Use this matrix in 14-mark questions to **justify** a connectivity choice:

| Requirement | If High → Use | If Low → Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Range** | LoRa, NB-IoT, Satellite | BLE, NFC, RFID |
| **Data Rate** | Wi-Fi, 5G, LTE-M | LoRa, SigFox, NB-IoT |
| **Battery Life** | BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, NB-IoT | Wi-Fi, Cellular |
| **Mobility** | 5G, LTE-M, Satellite | Wired Ethernet |
| **Mesh** | ZigBee, Thread, Z-Wave | BLE Star, Wi-Fi Star |
| **Indoor Penetration** | NB-IoT (sub-GHz), LoRa (sub-GHz) | mmWave 5G |
| **Cost** | BLE, Wi-Fi (commodity) | Satellite, 5G |
| **Security** | WPA3 (Wi-Fi), 5G-AKA | Bare RFID, NFC |

---

## 3.3 Latency-Bandwidth-Network Model Comparison

For a layered numerical comparison, KTU frequently asks for **justification via delay** in **MQTT vs CoAP vs HTTP**:

| Metric | HTTP | MQTT | CoAP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transport | TCP | TCP | UDP |
| Handshake | 3-way SYN | Existing TCP | None |
| Typical RTT for first message | 3 RTT | 2 RTT | 1 RTT |
| Header size | ~700 B | 2 B | 4 B |
| Energy per message | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| Best for | Cloud APIs | Telemetry | Constrained nodes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation logic (for 14-mark answers):**
> - **TCP Three-Way Handshake (3 RTT):** SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK → first HTTP GET.
> - **MQTT (2 RTT):** Reuses persistent TCP, then CONNECT → CONNACK → first PUBLISH.
> - **CoAP (1 RTT):** Single UDP packet; retransmits handled at the app layer.
>
> This is why CoAP dominates **NB-IoT and LoRa** stacks.
