# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Modified)]`
> **"List the five major criteria used to evaluate IoT connectivity technologies. Which criterion is most decisive for battery-powered sensor nodes and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The five criteria are:

1. **Range** — distance between communicating smart objects.
2. **Bandwidth (Data Rate)** — volume of data exchanged per unit time.
3. **Power / Energy Consumption** — battery drain per byte transmitted.
4. **Security** — confidentiality, integrity, authentication.
5. **Topology & Network Layer Behavior** — star, mesh, P2P.

**Most decisive criterion for battery-powered nodes → Energy Consumption.** Sensor nodes often operate unattended for **5 – 15 years** on a single coin cell. High transmit currents (e.g., Wi-Fi ~250 mA peak) would deplete a small battery in days. Hence technologies like **BLE, ZigBee, and LoRa** are chosen for their ultra-low duty cycles and sleep currents in the **µA range**.

*(Examiner allocation: 1 mark for listing criteria, 1 mark for identifying energy, 1 mark for valid justification with example.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]`
> **"Compare Bluetooth Classic and BLE in terms of data rate, power consumption, and primary use case."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Bluetooth Classic | BLE |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Data Rate | 1 – 3 Mbps | 125 kbps – 2 Mbps |
| Power Consumption | High (~30 mA peak) | Ultra-low (~15 mA peak, µA sleep) |
| Primary Use Case | Audio streaming (headsets, speakers) | Sensor telemetry, wearables, beacons |

**Justification:** BLE's reduced duty cycle and smaller protocol stack (no continuous audio stream) make it ideal for **intermittent, small-packet telemetry**; Classic BR/EDR is engineered for **continuous, high-throughput audio**. The KTU-expected conclusion is that BLE is the *de-facto* smart-object PAN choice today.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🅰️ QUESTION A — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]`

> **"Explain in detail the four communication models used to connect smart objects in IoT systems. With the help of a labeled diagram, illustrate how the Device-to-Gateway model is implemented in a smart-home scenario using BLE sensors, a home gateway, and an MQTT-based cloud broker."**

#### Part (a) — Communication Models (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **IoT World Forum (IWF) Reference Model** defines four canonical communication models:

**1. Device-to-Device (D2D) Model (2 Marks)**
- Two smart objects communicate **directly** without an intermediary server.
- Examples: BLE pairing between a smartwatch and a phone; ZigBee peer-to-peer messaging; NFC tap between a phone and a payment terminal.
- Used when latency must be **ultra-low** and an **Internet connection is unavailable** (e.g., offline device pairing).
- Limitation: no cloud analytics, no remote orchestration.

**2. Device-to-Cloud (D2C) Model (2 Marks)**
- The smart object connects **directly to the public cloud** via TCP/IP.
- Examples: a Wi-Fi camera streaming to AWS IoT; a 4G asset tracker pushing data to Azure IoT Hub.
- Pros: simple architecture, low hardware complexity.
- Cons: requires the device to implement TLS, manage credentials, and handle NAT traversal.

**3. Device-to-Gateway (D2G) Model (2 Marks)**
- The smart object connects to a **local gateway**, which in turn connects to the cloud.
- Examples: BLE heart-rate monitor → smartphone → cloud; ZigBee sensor → smart-home hub → cloud.
- **Most prevalent model in industrial and consumer IoT** because it supports constrained PAN/LAN radios on the device side and rich IP/TCP on the cloud side.

**4. Back-End Data-Sharing Model (1 Mark)**
- Multiple cloud services exchange data via **web service APIs**.
- Example: AWS IoT data mirrored into Google BigQuery for analytics, which then triggers an Azure Logic App workflow.
- Enables enterprise-grade interoperability across vendor clouds.

---

#### Part (b) — Smart-Home Device-to-Gateway Architecture (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **smart-home BLE sensor network** connected via a gateway and MQTT broker is implemented as follows:

**Step 1 — Smart Object (BLE Sensor):**
A temperature/humidity sensor (e.g., **TI CC2652** based) periodically advertises BLE advertisement frames containing sensor data. The device spends **>99 % of time in sleep**, waking every 30 s.

**Step 2 — Gateway (Home Hub):**
A **Raspberry Pi 4** running **BlueZ** (Linux BLE stack) + **Mosquitto MQTT broker** acts as the gateway. Its duties are:
- **BLE scanning** every 5 s.
- **Service discovery** for the environmental-sensing service UUID `0x181A`.
- **GATT characteristic reads** to fetch temperature (°C) and humidity (%RH).
- **Protocol translation:** data wrapped as an MQTT PUBLISH message:

```
Topic: home/livingroom/sensor01/temp
Payload: {"value": 24.6, "unit": "C", "ts": 1730000000}
QoS: 1
```

**Step 3 — Cloud Broker (AWS IoT Core / HiveMQ Cloud):**
The gateway opens an outbound **MQTT-over-TLS (port 8883)** connection to the public broker. Mutual X.509 authentication is used (device cert + CA chain).

**Step 4 — Back-End Services:**
- AWS IoT **Rule Engine** triggers a Lambda function on each message.
- Data is stored in **AWS Timestream**.
- A **mobile dashboard** (Grafana or custom app) subscribes via MQTT and renders the live values.

**Step 5 — Closed-Loop Actuation:**
The mobile app can issue an MQTT PUBLISH on `home/livingroom/ac01/cmd` with payload `{"power":"on"}`, which the gateway subscribes to and forwards over BLE GATT Write to a smart air-conditioner.

**Diagram (already provided in SECTION 4.2 — Smart-Object Connection Architecture).**

**Justification for choosing D2G:**
- The sensor is **too constrained to run TLS + TCP/IP** natively.
- BLE range inside a home is **~10 m**; the gateway handles **Wi-Fi/Ethernet uplink**.
- **Security** is centralized at the gateway; per-device certs are not required.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]`

> **"With a clear comparison table, explain the characteristics of PAN, LAN, and LPWAN technologies for connecting smart objects. For a smart-parking application across a city, justify the most suitable connectivity technology and compute the link-budget feasibility."**

#### Part (a) — Comparison of PAN, LAN, LPWAN (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PAN (BLE / ZigBee) | LAN (Wi-Fi) | LPWAN (LoRa / NB-IoT) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Range** | ≤ 100 m | 50 – 200 m | 2 – 15 km |
| **Data Rate** | 100 kbps – 2 Mbps | 11 Mbps – 9.6 Gbps | 0.3 – 200 kbps |
| **Power** | Ultra-low (µA sleep) | Medium (~250 mA peak) | Ultra-low (10-yr battery) |
| **Topology** | Star / Mesh | Star (AP-centric) | Star-of-stars |
| **Spectrum** | 2.4 GHz ISM | 2.4/5/6 GHz ISM | Sub-GHz (433/868/915 MHz) |
| **Cost/Node** | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Mobility Support** | Limited | Limited (handoff) | High |
| **Examples** | BLE Beacon, ZigBee HA | Wi-Fi camera | LoRa parking sensor |

**Engineering Insight:**

- **PAN** excels where **personal-space, low-power, and short-burst telemetry** are required.
- **LAN** is suited for **high-throughput, mains-powered** smart objects.
- **LPWAN** is the **only viable option** when sensors must operate **for years on a coin cell** and transmit small packets over **multi-kilometer distances**.

---

#### Part (b) — Smart Parking Link-Budget Feasibility (7 Marks)

**Problem Statement:** Each parking slot has a **magnetic (or ultrasonic) sensor** that must report occupancy every 60 s to a **city-wide central server**. Sensors are battery-powered, with **5-year battery life** required. Lots are spread across a **5 km × 5 km urban area**.

**Connectivity Justification (3 Marks):**

- **Reject BLE/ZigBee:** Range < 100 m — would require **thousands of gateways**, infeasible economically.
- **Reject Wi-Fi:** Battery life of 1 – 6 months — does not meet 5-year requirement.
- **Reject Cellular LTE-M/NB-IoT (initially favored):** Spectrum fees + SIM management. Acceptable but expensive.
- **Choose LoRaWAN:** Sub-GHz propagation penetrates buildings, multi-km range, **< 1 % duty cycle**, **10-year battery**, **8 gateways** can cover the entire city.

**Numerical Link-Budget Check (4 Marks):**

Assume: $P_t = +14$ dBm (LoRa EU868 limit), $G_t = G_r = +2$ dBi, $S_{min} = -137$ dBm (SF12, BW=125 kHz), $f = 868$ MHz, $d = 5$ km.

**Step 1 — Compute maximum allowable PL.**

$$PL_{\max} \;=\; P_t + G_t + G_r - S_{\min}$$

$$PL_{\max} \;=\; 14 + 2 + 2 - (-137) \;=\; 155 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute FSPL at 5 km, 868 MHz.**

$$PL_{FSPL} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(5000) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(868 \times 10^{6}) \;-\; 147.55$$

$$PL_{FSPL} \;=\; 20 \cdot 3.699 \;+\; 20 \cdot 8.939 \;-\; 147.55$$

$$PL_{FSPL} \;=\; 73.98 + 178.77 - 147.55 \;=\; 105.20 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute link margin.**

$$\text{Margin} \;=\; PL_{\max} - PL_{FSPL} \;=\; 155 - 105.2 \;=\; 49.8 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract urban losses.**

Urban clutter loss ≈ **20 – 30 dB**. Building penetration loss ≈ **10 – 20 dB** (worst case).

$$\text{Residual Margin} \;=\; 49.8 - 30 - 20 \approx -0.2 \text{ dB} \;\; (\text{at the limit})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** With **SF12** (the highest spreading factor), a LoRa link is **feasible** at 5 km in an urban environment with **small remaining margin**. To improve robustness, designers may:
> - Add **1–2 extra gateways** (SF11 instead of SF12 reduces airtime).
> - Increase **node height** (streetlight pole vs ground).
> - Use **ADR (Adaptive Data Rate)** so closer nodes use SF7 and farther nodes use SF12.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Module 3 (Connecting Smart Objects):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Bluetooth" with "BLE":** They are NOT the same. Classic Bluetooth = audio; BLE = sensor telemetry. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this slip.
> 2. **Wrong frequency units:** Writing "Bluetooth operates at 2.4 GHz" is fine, but writing "5 GHz" or "900 MHz" is **wrong** — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **No link-budget numbers:** When asked "justify LoRa for a 5 km link," many students write prose without numbers. Always **quote at least one dB value, one range, and one power figure**.
> 4. **Mixing protocol layers:** MQTT is **application layer**, not transport. TCP is **transport**. Do not confuse them in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Missing the "Why":** Just listing technology features without mapping them to the use case loses marks. Always say **"because the application requires X, technology Y is chosen"**.
> 6. **Ignoring security:** A complete answer MUST mention **AES-128/256, TLS, device attestation** at least once.
> 7. **Forgetting the gateway:** Device-to-Gateway is the dominant model. Skipping it in diagrams loses up to **2 marks**.
> 8. **Wrong units in path-loss equation:** Use **meters** for distance and **Hz** for frequency unless you have pre-computed constants — examiners check this strictly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Connecting Smart Objects":**

- **Five communication criteria:** Range, Bandwidth, Power, Security, Topology.
- **Four communication models:** D2D, D2C, D2G, Back-End Data Sharing.
- **PAN technologies:** NFC, RFID, Bluetooth Classic, BLE, ZigBee, Z-Wave, Thread.
- **LAN technology:** Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 family).
- **WAN technologies:** 3G, 4G/LTE, 5G (eMBB/mMTC/URLLC).
- **LPWAN technologies:** LoRaWAN, SigFox, NB-IoT, LTE-M.
- **Satellite:** LEO (Iridium), GEO (Inmarsat), NB-IoT NTN.
- **Golden rule:** Lowest energy per byte is the metric to minimize for battery-powered nodes.
- **Golden rule:** Range and data rate are inversely related — doubling range typically halves data rate.
- **Golden rule:** Sub-GHz (sub-1 GHz) propagates farther than 2.4 GHz in urban/indoor environments.
- **Application protocols:** HTTP (heavy), MQTT (medium), CoAP (light), LwM2M (light + device management).
- **Gateway functions:** Protocol translation, security, local buffering, edge analytics, FOTA.
- **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$.
- **FSPL (dB):** $PL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) - 147.55$ (m, Hz).
- **Link budget:** $PL_{\max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - S_{\min}$.
- **Always justify** with **numbers** (range in m/km, rate in kbps/Mbps, power in dBm, lifetime in years).
- **Security baseline:** AES-128 + TLS 1.2/1.3 + device certificates for any cloud-facing connection.
- **Mesh when resilience matters, star when simplicity/cost matters.**
- **Use the comparison table** (Section 2.4) to support every technology choice in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communications Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Communications Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Communications Criteria** in the context of IoT Security refers to the standardized set of **technical, operational, and security benchmarks** that govern how IoT devices transmit, receive, process, and protect data across heterogeneous network infrastructures. These criteria define the *minimum acceptable thresholds* for **range, bandwidth, latency, power consumption, data rate, interoperability, and security** that any IoT communication protocol must satisfy to be considered deployment-ready and resilient against cyber threats.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCCT604 - Module 3)**, communications criteria encompass the **selection parameters, transmission requirements, protocol stack responsibilities, and security enforcement points** that collectively determine whether an IoT communication channel is *fit for purpose* in critical application domains such as healthcare, industrial automation, smart cities, and vehicular networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This topic bridges the OSI/TCP-IP layered model with IoT-specific constraints, requiring students to understand *why* traditional Internet protocols are often unsuitable for resource-constrained IoT endpoints (Class 0, Class 1, Class 2 devices as per RFC 7228).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Postal Service" Model:**
>
> Imagine the **Internet as a global postal system** and **IoT devices as letter-writers** scattered across a village. Now imagine the village has *no electricity*, the *letters are tiny*, the *postmen are slow*, and *bandits intercept the mail*. The "Communications Criteria" are essentially the **rulebook** that the post office (protocol designer) must follow to ensure:
>
> 1. **The letter fits in the mailbox** → *Packet size constraints (MTU)*
> 2. **The postman can find the house** → *Addressing & routing (IPv6, 6LoWPAN)*
> 3. **The letter is not stolen** → *Encryption (TLS, DTLS, AES-128)*
> 4. **The letter arrives in time** → *Latency bounds (real-time requirements)*
> 5. **The postman doesn't get lost** → *Network topology & range (Zigbee, LoRa, BLE)*
> 6. **The mailbox doesn't run out of battery** → *Energy efficiency (Duty cycling, sleep modes)*

In simple terms, **Communications Criteria = the "must-haves" checklist** that determines whether a chosen communication technology is *good enough* to securely and reliably connect an IoT device to the rest of the world.

---

## 1.3 Physical Standards, Constants & Benchmark Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Anchors (bolded for emphasis):**
>
> - **Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU):** Typically **1280 bytes** for 6LoWPAN (vs. 1500 for Ethernet)
> - **ISM Bands:** **2.4 GHz**, **868 MHz (Europe)**, **915 MHz (USA)**
> - **Typical IoT Data Rates:** **20 kbps (LoRa)** to **250 kbps (Zigbee)** to **54 Mbps (Wi-Fi 802.11g)**
> - **Duty Cycle Limit (EU 868 MHz):** **1%** (per ETSI EN 300 220)
> - **AES Block Size:** **128 bits** (NIST FIPS 197)
> - **Standard Range Classification:** *Short (<10 m)*, *Medium (10–100 m)*, *Long (>1 km)*
> - **Frame Error Rate (FER) acceptable threshold:** **< 1%** for industrial IoT
> - **End-to-End Latency bounds:** **< 10 ms (tactile IoT)**, **< 1 s (smart metering)**, **< 1 min (logging)**
> - **Boltzmann's Constant (k):** **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** — used in thermal noise floor calculations for receiver sensitivity

---

## 1.4 Why Communications Criteria Matter in Ethical Hacking

> [!WARNING]
> **From a Security Perspective:** Every communications criterion is a **potential attack surface**. An attacker who knows that a device uses a weak MAC protocol with no authentication can inject forged frames. A system designer who ignores the *latency budget* may inadvertently disable a security handshake (e.g., TLS), creating a Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) opportunity. Ethical hackers must therefore evaluate *each* communication criterion through a **CIA Triad lens** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of IoT Communications Criteria

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes **six interrelated pillars** that govern IoT communication selection. Each pillar is a *design lever* — tightening one (e.g., security) often loosens another (e.g., power budget).

### Pillar 1: **Range & Coverage**
- Defines the maximum physical distance over which two IoT nodes can exchange valid frames.
- **Short-range** (PAN/LAN): BLE, Zigbee, NFC — typically < 100 m
- **Medium-range** (LAN): Wi-Fi, Bluetooth 5.0 — up to 200 m (BLE 5.0 coded PHY)
- **Long-range** (LPWAN): LoRa, Sigfox, NB-IoT — 2 km to 15 km in urban deployments
- **Satellite IoT:** Up to global coverage (LEO constellations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friis Transmission Equation** governs free-space path loss and is the *theoretical foundation* for range calculations. Real-world range is degraded by **multipath fading, obstruction losses, and antenna gain mismatches**.

### Pillar 2: **Data Rate (Throughput)**
- The number of useful bits successfully delivered per unit time, measured in **bps, kbps, Mbps**.
- IoT applications have **highly heterogeneous throughput needs**:
  - Environmental sensors: **0.1 – 10 kbps**
  - Smart metering: **10 – 100 kbps**
  - Video surveillance IoT: **1 – 10 Mbps**
  - Autonomous vehicles V2X: **> 27 Mbps** (DSRC/C-V2X)

### Pillar 3: **Power Consumption & Energy Efficiency**
- The single most restrictive criterion for battery-operated IoT nodes.
- Measured in **Joules per bit (J/bit)** or **milliwatts (mW)** in active/transmit/receive/sleep modes.
- **Class 0 devices** (RFC 7228): < 100 KB RAM, < 100 KB Flash — severely energy-constrained
- **Energy harvesting** (solar, vibration, thermal) is increasingly used to extend lifetime

### Pillar 4: **Latency & Determinism**
- **Latency** = end-to-end delay (source to destination)
- **Jitter** = variation in latency across consecutive packets
- **Determinism** = ability to *guarantee* a worst-case latency bound
- Critical for **real-time control loops** (industrial IoT, vehicle platooning, robotic surgery)

### Pillar 5: **Security & Privacy**
- Encompasses **authentication, confidentiality, integrity, non-repudiation, and privacy preservation**.
- Implemented at one or more layers: *Link (802.15.4 security), Network (IPsec), Transport (DTLS/TLS), Application (MQTT-S, CoAP-DTLS)*
- **Cryptographic overhead** must be quantified — e.g., AES-128-CCM adds ~**20–30 bytes** per frame and consumes **~10 μJ per block** on an 8-bit MCU

### Pillar 6: **Interoperability & Scalability**
- **Interoperability:** Ability to communicate across heterogeneous vendors and protocol stacks
- **Scalability:** Ability to grow from tens to millions of nodes without re-architecting
- **Standardization bodies:** IEEE, IETF, ETSI, 3GPP, oneM2M, W3C (WoT)

---

## 2.2 Layered Mapping of Communications Criteria

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** A common 14-mark question asks students to *"Map the communications criteria to the TCP/IP or OSI layer at which they are enforced."* Memorize the table below.

| **OSI Layer** | **Key Protocols (IoT)** | **Criteria Enforced** | **Security Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7. Application | MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/2, DDS | Interoperability, data semantics | MQTT over TLS, CoAP-DTLS |
| 6. Presentation | CBOR, Protocol Buffers, JSON | Data encoding efficiency | None (handled at lower layers) |
| 5. Session | SMQTT, AMQP sessions | Session resumption, keep-alive | Session tokens |
| 4. Transport | UDP (CoAP), TCP (MQTT), DTLS | Reliability, flow control, encryption | TLS 1.2/1.3, DTLS 1.2 |
| 3. Network | IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL | Addressing, routing, fragmentation | IPsec (AH/ESP) |
| 2. Data Link | IEEE 802.15.4, BLE MAC, LoRaWAN MAC | Frame structure, MAC addressing, ARQ | 802.15.4 security (AES-CCM) |
| 1. Physical | LoRa modulation, O-QPSK, GFSK, OFDM | Range, data rate, spectrum, modulation | Frequency hopping (rare) |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| **#** | **Formula / Relation** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $FSPL(d) = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-space path loss (d in km, f in MHz) | dB |
| 2 | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL - L_{misc}$ | Received power (Friis chain) | dBm |
| 3 | $SNR = \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \dfrac{P_r}{N \cdot B}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dimensionless (dB) |
| 4 | $N = k \cdot T$ | Thermal noise spectral density (k = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K) | W/Hz |
| 5 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR)$ | Shannon-Hartley channel capacity | bps |
| 6 | $E_b \ge \dfrac{P_{tx} \cdot t_{frame}}{L_{frame}}$ | Energy per bit budget (link budget) | Joules/bit |
| 7 | $D_{max} = \dfrac{B \cdot (P_{max} - P_{idle})}{P_{avg}}$ | Device battery lifetime (rough) | hours/days/years |
| 8 | $Throughput = \dfrac{L_{payload} \cdot (1 - FER)}{t_{frame}}$ | Effective data rate accounting for errors | bps |
| 9 | $Jitter = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (d_i - \bar{d})^2}$ | Standard deviation of packet delay | seconds |
| 10 | $O_{crypto} = \dfrac{L_{header\_crypto}}{L_{payload}} \times 100$ | Percentage crypto overhead | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **FSPL** is used by RF engineers to size cell towers, LoRa gateways, and satellite ground stations.
> - **Shannon's capacity (C)** sets the *theoretical ceiling* on data rate — no modulation scheme can exceed it.
> - **Energy-per-bit (E_b)** is the *figure of merit* for low-power IoT radios (e.g., TI CC2650: ~50 nJ/bit in TX mode).

---

## 2.4 Trade-off Analysis: The "Impossible Trinity" of IoT Communications

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Conceptual Question Favorite:** *"Explain why an IoT system cannot simultaneously optimize for Range, Data Rate, and Battery Life."*

| **Optimize for** | **Sacrifice** | **Example Technology** |
|---|---|---|
| **Range** | Data rate, battery | LoRaWAN (15 km, 0.3 kbps, 10 yr battery) |
| **Data Rate** | Range, battery | Wi-Fi HaLow (100+ Mbps, <1 km, hours battery) |
| **Battery Life** | Data rate, range | BLE beacon (months, 100 kbps, <30 m) |
| **Security** | Power, throughput, latency | TLS handshake (heavyweight for Class 0 devices) |
| **Low Latency** | Range, power | UWB / 5G URLLC |

This is the **"Trilemma"** that defines IoT communication design — every protocol makes an explicit or implicit choice about which corner of the triangle to favour.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1: Friis Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) — Complete Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** The Friis transmission equation relates transmitted and received power in a free-space (vacuum-like) environment. We derive the **logarithmic (dB) form** used by virtually every RF engineer.

### Step 1: Define the Isotropic Radiator

An *isotropic antenna* radiates power uniformly in all directions. The power flux density (power per unit area) at distance $d$ is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Explanation:** The total transmitted power $P_t$ spreads over the surface area of an imaginary sphere of radius $d$, whose area is $4\pi d^2$.

---

### Step 2: Introduce Directive Antennas

If both transmitter and receiver use directive antennas with gains $G_t$ and $G_r$ respectively, the received power becomes:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot A_e
$$

where $A_e$ is the *effective aperture* of the receiving antenna, related to its gain and wavelength by:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \cdot \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

**Explanation:** A higher-gain antenna captures more power, modelled by the effective aperture formula from antenna theory.

---

### Step 3: Combine Equations (Friis Transmission Equation)

Substituting $A_e$ into the expression for $P_r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \frac{P_t \cdot G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \cdot \lambda^2}{4\pi} \\[6pt]
P_r &= P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The factor $\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ is the **free-space path loss factor**. Since $\lambda = c/f$ (where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s), the loss increases with frequency.

---

### Step 4: Convert to Decibel Form

Express all powers in dBm and gains in dBi:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r [\text{dBm}] &= P_t [\text{dBm}] + G_t [\text{dBi}] + G_r [\text{dBi}] - FSPL[\text{dB}] \\[6pt]
\text{where } FSPL[\text{dB}] &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The constant **32.44 dB** arises from evaluating $20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ with $c$ in km/s and $f$ in MHz. This is the **most-cited formula in IoT wireless planning**.

---

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

> A LoRaWAN gateway transmits at $P_t = 14$ dBm on $f = 868$ MHz, with $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi. Find $P_r$ at $d = 5$ km.

**Step 5.1 — Compute FSPL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FSPL &= 20 \log_{10}(5) + 20 \log_{10}(868) + 32.44 \\
&= 20(0.699) + 20(2.939) + 32.44 \\
&= 13.98 + 58.78 + 32.44 \\
&= 105.20 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.2 — Compute Received Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= 14 + 2 + 2 - 105.20 \\
&= -87.20 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.3 — Compare with Receiver Sensitivity:**

A typical LoRa SX1276 receiver sensitivity is approximately **−137 dBm** at SF12/BW125. Since $-87.20 > -137$, the link has a margin of:

$$
\text{Link Margin} = -87.20 - (-137.20) = 50.00 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always show units and convert dBm/dBi carefully. A common mistake is to mix dBm with linear Watts in the same equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2: Shannon Capacity & Spectral Efficiency Bound

### Step 1: Start with Shannon's Theorem

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR) \quad [\text{bps}]
$$

**Explanation:** This is the *maximum error-free data rate* achievable over a bandwidth-limited channel with additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN). It is a *theoretical ceiling* — practical systems (Wi-Fi, LoRa) achieve 30–70% of this limit.

---

### Step 2: Express Spectral Efficiency

Divide both sides by $B$:

$$
\eta = \frac{C}{B} = \log_2(1 + SNR) \quad [\text{bits/s/Hz}]
$$

**Explanation:** Spectral efficiency measures how "densely" we pack bits into a unit of bandwidth. LTE achieves ~6 bits/s/Hz; Wi-Fi 6 reaches ~10 bits/s/Hz; 5G targets >30 bits/s/Hz.

---

### Step 3: Convert to Required SNR for a Target Data Rate

Solving for $SNR$:

$$
SNR_{min} = 2^{(R/B)} - 1
$$

For $R = 1$ Mbps over $B = 500$ kHz:

$$
SNR_{min} = 2^{(1{,}000{,}000/500{,}000)} - 1 = 2^2 - 1 = 3 \quad (\approx 4.77 \text{ dB})
$$

---

### Step 4: Relate to Energy per Bit (E_b / N_0)

Since $SNR = \dfrac{E_b \cdot R}{N_0 \cdot B} = \dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \cdot \dfrac{R}{B}$:

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{2^{(R/B)} - 1}{R/B}
$$

**Explanation:** This ratio is the *fundamental energy-efficiency metric* in digital communications. BPSK requires $E_b/N_0 \geq 9.6$ dB; advanced turbo codes can approach the Shannon limit of $-1.6$ dB.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Protocol Selection Decision Tree

| **Application** | **Range Need** | **Data Rate Need** | **Battery Need** | **Recommended Protocol** | **Security Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smart agriculture sensor | Long (2 km) | Very low (1 kbps) | 10+ years | **LoRaWAN (EU868)** | LoRaWAN AppKey + AES-128 |
| Industrial robot control | Short (10 m) | High (1 Mbps) | Wired preferred | **EtherCAT / TSN** | MACsec (802.1AE) |
| Wearable health monitor | Short (5 m) | Low (100 kbps) | 1 week | **BLE 5.0** | LE Secure Connections + AES-CCM |
| Smart home gateway | Medium (30 m) | Medium (10 Mbps) | Wall-powered | **Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax)** | WPA3-SAE |
| Smart electricity meter | Long (urban) | Medium (200 kbps) | 15 years | **NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1)** | LTE security + APN tunneling |
| Vehicle V2X | Short-medium (300 m) | High (27 Mbps) | Car battery | **C-V2X (PC5 sidelink)** | PKI + ECDSA signatures |
| Asset tracking (global) | Global | Very low | 5 years | **Satellite IoT (Astrocast)** | Vendor proprietary + TLS |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Analytical Question Pattern:** *"For a given scenario, justify the choice of communication protocol by mapping it to the six pillars."* Use this matrix as your model.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six-Pillar Framework — Functional Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Choice:** A Mermaid block diagram is used to map the *interactions* between the six communications pillars, since physical drawings of RF propagation are out of scope for a text-rendered schema.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[APPLICATION REQUIREMENTS] --> B[COMMUNICATIONS CRITERIA FRAMEWORK]
    B --> C1[PILLAR 1: RANGE AND COVERAGE]
    B --> C2[PILLAR 2: DATA RATE]
    B --> C3[PILLAR 3: POWER CONSUMPTION]
    B --> C4[PILLAR 4: LATENCY AND JITTER]
    B --> C5[PILLAR 5: SECURITY AND PRIVACY]
    B --> C6[PILLAR 6: INTEROPERABILITY]
    C1 --> D[PROTOCOL SELECTION ENGINE]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E1[LOWER LAYER IEEE 802.15.4 BLE LORA]
    D --> E2[NETWORK LAYER 6LOWPAN RPL IPV6]
    D --> E3[TRANSPORT LAYER UDP DTLS]
    D --> E4[APPLICATION LAYER MQTT COAP HTTP]
    E1 --> F[DEPLOYMENT READY IOT LINK]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    C5 --> G[THREAT MODEL VALIDATION]
    G --> F
```

**Explanation of Flow:** Application-level requirements cascade through the six pillars, which feed the protocol selection engine. The engine maps to one or more layer-specific protocols. Security (Pillar 5) has a side-channel into threat-model validation, ensuring ethical-hacking concerns are addressed.

---

## 4.2 Communication-Criteria Trade-off Trilemma (Mermaid Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LR[Long Range] --- HR[High Data Rate]
    HR --- LB[Long Battery Life]
    LR --- LB
    LR --> T1[LoRaWAN]
    HR --> T2[WiFi HaLow]
    LB --> T3[BLE Beacons]
    T1 -.-> X1[Sacrifices throughput]
    T2 -.-> X2[High power drain]
    T3 -.-> X3[Short range only]
```

**Explanation:** Each corner of the trilemma sacrifices the third property. Protocols can be plotted inside the triangle to visualize their design philosophy.

---

## 4.3 Layered Security Architecture for IoT Communications

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[Layer 7 Application: MQTT CoAP] --> S7[Security: TLS 1.3 Application Tokens]
    L4[Layer 4 Transport: UDP TCP] --> S4[Security: DTLS 1.2 TLS 1.3]
    L3[Layer 3 Network: IPv6 6LoWPAN] --> S3[Security: IPsec AH ESP]
    L2[Layer 2 Data Link: 802.15.4 BLE MAC] --> S2[Security: AES CCM 128 Frame Counter]
    L1[Layer 1 Physical: OQPSK GFSK LoRa] --> S1[Security: Frequency Hopping DSSS]
    S7 --> T[End to End Trust Boundary]
    S4 --> T
    S3 --> T
    S2 --> T
    S1 --> T
    T --> A[Attacker Model Analysis]
```

**Explanation:** Each layer contributes a specific security mechanism. The trust boundary converges at the application endpoint, where ethical-hacking validation (penetration testing) is applied.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flow for Selecting an IoT Communication Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START([Start Selection]) --> Q1{Is device mains powered or battery powered}
    Q1 -- Battery --> Q2{Required operational lifetime}
    Q1 -- Mains --> Q3{Required data rate}
    Q2 -- More than 5 years --> P1[LoRaWAN or Sigfox]
    Q2 -- Between 1 and 5 years --> P2[NB-IoT or LTE-M]
    Q2 -- Less than 1 year --> P3[Zigbee or BLE]
    Q3 -- More than 10 Mbps --> P4[WiFi 6 or 5G]
    Q3 -- Between 1 and 10 Mbps --> P5[WiFi HaLow]
    Q3 -- Less than 1 Mbps --> P6[802.15.4g or Wirepas]
    P1 --> SEC{Security requirement}
    P2 --> SEC
    P3 --> SEC
    P4 --> SEC
    P5 --> SEC
    P6 --> SEC
    SEC -- High --> HSEC[Add DTLS or TLS and Hardware Secure Element]
    SEC -- Medium --> MSEC[Enable Link Layer AES CCM]
    SEC -- Low --> LSEC[Use Pre Shared Key PSK Mode]
    HSEC --> END([Deploy])
    MSEC --> END
    LSEC --> END
```

**Explanation:** This flowchart operationalizes the six pillars into a practical decision tree. KTU students can use it as a model answer for design questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

*Define "Communications Criteria" in the context of IoT security. List any four technical parameters that govern the selection of an IoT communication protocol.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):

**Definition (1 Mark):** Communications Criteria refers to the standardized set of technical, operational, and security benchmarks — such as *range, data rate, power consumption, latency, security, and interoperability* — that determine whether a given communication protocol is suitable for a specific IoT deployment scenario.

**Four Parameters (0.5 Mark each, 2 Marks total):**

1. **Range & Coverage** — Maximum physical distance between two communicating nodes (e.g., 15 km for LoRaWAN).
2. **Data Rate / Throughput** — Number of useful bits transmitted per second (e.g., 250 kbps for Zigbee).
3. **Power Consumption** — Energy required to transmit, receive, and idle, often measured in mW or J/bit.
4. **Security Strength** — Level of cryptographic protection, including key length (e.g., AES-128) and authentication mechanism (e.g., PSK vs. PKI).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Define first, then enumerate with *one example value* per parameter to secure full marks.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

*Explain the meaning of "Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)" and state the Friis transmission formula in dB form.*

#### Model Answer:

**Meaning (1.5 Marks):** Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) is the attenuation of radio signal strength as it propagates through a vacuum or ideal free-space environment with no obstructions, reflection, or absorption. It arises from the *spreading* of electromagnetic energy over an ever-increasing spherical surface as the wavefront travels. FSPL depends on the **distance (d)** between transmitter and receiver and the **operating frequency (f)**.

**Friis Formula in dB (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r[\text{dBm}] &= P_t[\text{dBm}] + G_t[\text{dBi}] + G_r[\text{dBi}] - FSPL[\text{dB}] \\
\text{where } FSPL[\text{dB}] &= 20 \log_{10}(d[\text{km}]) + 20 \log_{10}(f[\text{MHz}]) + 32.44
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the constant **32.44 dB** or mixing units (using meters instead of km). Always declare units in brackets inside the log argument.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module 3 – Internal Choice):** Two questions are provided; the student answers **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the six pillars of communications criteria for IoT. For each pillar, provide one example protocol that excels in that dimension and one trade-off it incurs.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A smart agriculture deployment uses LoRaWAN end devices spread across a 5 km radius. The gateway transmits at 14 dBm at 868 MHz with a 2 dBi antenna. Both gateway and end device use 2 dBi gain. Compute the received power at 5 km and determine the link margin assuming a receiver sensitivity of −137 dBm. Comment on whether the link is viable for SF7/BW125 operation where sensitivity is −123 dBm.*

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Pillar 1: Range & Coverage (1 Mark)**
- *Example:* **LoRaWAN** achieves 10–15 km in rural deployments.
- *Trade-off:* Extremely low data rate (0.3–50 kbps).

**Pillar 2: Data Rate (1 Mark)**
- *Example:* **Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax)** delivers up to 9.6 Gbps.
- *Trade-off:* Short range (~30 m indoors) and high power consumption.

**Pillar 3: Power Consumption (1 Mark)**
- *Example:* **BLE 5.0** in advertising mode draws ~15 µA average.
- *Trade-off:* Limited range (~200 m with coded PHY).

**Pillar 4: Latency & Determinism (1 Mark)**
- *Example:* **5G URLLC** targets 1 ms air-interface latency.
- *Trade-off:* Requires dense small-cell deployment and licensed spectrum.

**Pillar 5: Security & Privacy (1 Mark)**
- *Example:* **MQTT over TLS 1.3** with mutual X.509 authentication.
- *Trade-off:* Handshake overhead (~2–4 RTT) and increased bandwidth use.

**Pillar 6: Interoperability & Scalability (1 Mark)**
- *Example:* **CoAP over 6LoWPAN** uses standard IETF RFC 7252 / RFC 4944.
- *Trade-off:* Constrained to IPv6 and fragmentation overhead.

**Summary Trade-off Diagram (1 Mark):** Mention the IoT communications trilemma (range vs. data rate vs. battery).

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Step 1: Compute FSPL (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FSPL &= 20 \log_{10}(5) + 20 \log_{10}(868) + 32.44 \\
&= 13.98 + 58.78 + 32.44 \\
&= 105.20 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Received Power (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL \\
&= 14 + 2 + 2 - 105.20 \\
&= -87.20 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Link Margins (2 Marks)**

*For SF12/BW125 (sensitivity = −137 dBm):*

$$
\text{Margin}_{SF12} = -87.20 - (-137.20) = 50.00 \text{ dB} \quad \text{✓ Link viable}
$$

*For SF7/BW125 (sensitivity = −123 dBm):*

$$
\text{Margin}_{SF7} = -87.20 - (-123.20) = 36.00 \text{ dB} \quad \text{✓ Link viable}
$$

**Step 4: Comment (1 Mark):**
The link is *highly viable* for both SF7 and SF12, with margins of 36 dB and 50 dB respectively. The 50 dB margin at SF12 is exceptionally robust, allowing operation even in adverse weather, foliage, or interference conditions. The system designer could potentially reduce transmit power to save battery while still maintaining reliable connectivity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often **forget to include antenna gains** in the Friis calculation (losing 1 mark). Also, **do not confuse dBm with dBW** (dBW = dBm + 30). Always show the full equation before substituting numerical values.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of a layered mapping, explain how communications criteria are enforced at each layer of the TCP/IP model for IoT. Identify the dominant security mechanism at the Data Link, Network, and Application layers.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *An industrial IoT (IIoT) sensor must operate on a single coin-cell battery for 8 years, transmitting 50-byte payloads every 60 seconds at 2.4 GHz. The radio consumes 50 mW in TX mode (transmit time = 5 ms per packet) and 5 µW in sleep mode. Compute the average power consumption per day, and verify whether the battery capacity of 2400 mAh (3 V) is sufficient.*

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Layered Mapping Table (4 Marks):**

| **Layer** | **Protocols** | **Criteria Enforced** | **Security Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7. Application | MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/2 | Data format, semantics | TLS 1.3, X.509 |
| 4. Transport | UDP, TCP, DTLS | Reliability, port addressing | DTLS 1.2, TLS 1.3 |
| 3. Network | IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL | Routing, addressing | IPsec (ESP) |
| 2. Data Link | IEEE 802.15.4, BLE MAC, LoRaWAN MAC | Framing, MAC, error control | AES-CCM-128 |
| 1. Physical | O-QPSK, GFSK, LoRa chirp | Modulation, spectrum, range | Frequency hopping |

**Dominant Security Mechanisms (3 Marks):**
- **Data Link Layer:** *IEEE 802.15.4 security* using **AES-CCM-128** with separate keys for confidentiality and integrity. Provides frame-level authentication via MIC.
- **Network Layer:** *IPsec* in **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) mode** with **AES-CBC or AES-GCM** for end-to-end encryption between IPv6 nodes.
- **Application Layer:** *TLS 1.3* (or *DTLS 1.3* for UDP transports like CoAP) with **mutual X.509 certificate authentication** or **pre-shared keys (PSK)** for resource-constrained devices.

**Conclusion:** Security is *defense in depth* — a single compromised layer is contained by the others.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Step 1: Compute Number of Transmissions per Day (1 Mark)**

$$
N_{tx} = \frac{24 \times 60 \times 60}{60} = 1440 \text{ transmissions/day}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Total TX Time per Day (1 Mark)**

$$
t_{tx} = 1440 \times 5 \text{ ms} = 7.2 \text{ s/day}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Energy Consumed in TX Mode (1 Mark)**

$$
E_{tx} = 50 \text{ mW} \times 7.2 \text{ s} = 360 \text{ mJ/day} = 0.360 \text{ J/day}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Energy Consumed in Sleep Mode (1 Mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{sleep} &= 86400 - 7.2 = 86392.8 \text{ s/day} \\
E_{sleep} &= 5 \, \mu\text{W} \times 86392.8 \text{ s} \\
&= 431{,}964 \, \mu\text{J/day} = 0.432 \text{ J/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total Energy per Day (1 Mark)**

$$
E_{total} = 0.360 + 0.432 = 0.792 \text{ J/day}
$$

**Step 6: Convert to Battery Capacity (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{Power} = \frac{0.792 \text{ J}}{86400 \text{ s}} = 9.17 \, \mu\text{W average}
$$

Battery capacity in Joules:

$$
E_{batt} = 2400 \text{ mAh} \times 3 \text{ V} = 7.2 \text{ Wh} = 7.2 \times 3600 = 25{,}920 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 7: Compute Battery Lifetime (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{Lifetime} = \frac{25920 \text{ J}}{0.792 \text{ J/day}} = 32{,}727 \text{ days} \approx 89.66 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The battery is *vastly over-specified* for this duty cycle. The sensor could actually run for ~90 years, not 8. Real-world de-rating factors (self-discharge, temperature, voltage drop) typically reduce this to 8–10 years, validating the original design assumption.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often **forget to convert mW to W or µW to W** consistently. Always show unit conversions explicitly. Also, the sleep current is so small that it is *rounded off* in many solutions; show the µW calculation to earn full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam):**

- **Definition:** Communications Criteria = 6 pillars (Range, Data Rate, Power, Latency, Security, Interoperability).
- **Friis FSPL formula** is the most important equation: $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (with $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz).
- **Shannon Capacity** $C = B \log_2(1 + SNR)$ sets the *theoretical ceiling* on data rate.
- **Energy per bit** ($E_b/N_0$) is the fundamental energy-efficiency metric in digital comms.
- **Layered Security Mapping:** Link (AES-CCM) → Network (IPsec) → Transport (DTLS) → Application (TLS/MQTT).
- **The Trilemma:** Cannot simultaneously optimize Range, Data Rate, and Battery Life — every protocol picks a corner.
- **6LoWPAN MTU** = 1280 bytes; **AES block** = 128 bits; **Duty cycle (868 MHz EU)** = 1%.
- **IoT Device Classes (RFC 7228):** Class 0 (very constrained, ~10 KB RAM), Class 1 (~50 KB), Class 2 (~50–250 KB).
- **5G URLLC** latency target: < 1 ms air interface; **LoRaWAN** range: up to 15 km rural.
- **Security at every layer:** No "one-size-fits-all" — defense in depth is mandatory.
- **Real-world trade-offs:** Higher security = more CPU cycles = more power = shorter battery life.
- **Examiner loves:** Layered mapping tables, numerical examples with FSPL, trilemma explanations.
- **Common pitfalls:** Mixing dBm/dBi/dBW; forgetting antenna gains; using meters instead of km in FSPL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Range × Data Rate × Battery = constant. Every protocol picks two; sacrifices the third. Security is paid for in power and latency."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Access Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** IoT Access Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are IoT Access Technologies?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *IoT Access Technologies* are the **physical (PHY) and medium access control (MAC) layer protocols** that govern how constrained IoT devices — sensors, actuators, RFID tags, smart meters, wearables — connect to gateways, edge nodes, and the broader Internet. They define the **wireless (or wired) air interface**, including modulation, channel access, frame structure, and security primitives that operate at the lowest layers of the OSI/TCP-IP model.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum, these technologies are classified into **three main tiers** based on communication range, data rate, and power consumption, as shown below.

### 1.2 The Three-Tier Classification (KTU Taxonomy)

| Tier | Range Category | Representative Technologies | Typical Use Case |
|------|----------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| **Tier 1 – Short Range (PAN / LAN)** | < 100 m | IEEE 802.15.4, ZigBee, Bluetooth/BLE, NFC, RFID, Z-Wave | Smart home, body-area networks, wearables |
| **Tier 2 – Local Area (WLAN)** | 100 m – 1 km | Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax) | Indoor IoT, IP cameras, smart appliances |
| **Tier 3 – Wide Area (LPWAN)** | 1 km – 50+ km | LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M | Smart city, agriculture, asset tracking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this classification matters in KTU exams:** Questions are frequently framed to test whether the student can *match an application* (e.g., underground water-level monitoring) to the *correct access technology* (e.g., LoRaWAN) based on range, power, and data-rate constraints.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System"

Imagine IoT devices as **houses in a city** and access technologies as the **postal infrastructure**:

- **RFID/NFC** → Like a *handshake* or *neighborhood gossip* — exchange happens within a few centimeters.
- **Bluetooth/BLE** → Like a *neighbor calling out across the street* — personal area communication, low energy.
- **ZigBee** → Like a *mesh of neighbors forwarding letters* — multiple hops, no single point of failure.
- **Wi-Fi** → Like a *broadband fiber to your house* — high speed, but power-hungry.
- **LoRaWAN** → Like a *postal service that sends a small postcard across the country* — tiny payload, enormous range, very low power.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

Every IoT access technology is characterized by **four cardinal metrics** that KTU examiners love to test:

- **Range** — maximum communication distance (m or km)
- **Data Rate** — bits per second (bps, kbps, Mbps)
- **Power Consumption** — typically in mW or mAh; critical for battery-operated nodes
- **Network Topology** — Star, Mesh, Tree, P2P

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the trade-off triangle: **Range ↑ ⇒ Data Rate ↓, Power ↑**. This appears in nearly every KTU Part A question on access technologies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IEEE 802.15.4 — The Foundation of Low-Rate WPANs

**IEEE 802.15.4** is the *parent specification* upon which ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread, and many industrial protocols are built. It defines the **PHY + MAC layers** for Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs).

### Operational Mechanism (Structured Steps)

1. **Channel Access** — Uses **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)**. Before transmitting, a node listens to the channel; if idle for a backoff period, it transmits.
2. **Slotted vs. Unslotted** — In *beacon-enabled mode*, time is divided into slots synchronized by a coordinator's beacon; in *non-beacon mode*, nodes use unslotted CSMA/CA.
3. **Frame Structure** — Maximum MAC frame payload is **127 bytes**, which is critical because it forces upper-layer fragmentation in protocols like 6LoWPAN.
4. **Addressing** — Supports both 16-bit (short) and 64-bit (extended) MAC addresses.
5. **Security** — Offers AES-128 based security at the MAC sublayer (CCM* mode).

### Frequency Bands Supported

| Band | Frequency | Data Rate | Channels |
|------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| 868 MHz (Europe) | 868–868.6 MHz | 20 kbps | 1 |
| 915 MHz (ISM) | 902–928 MHz | 40 kbps | 10 |
| 2.4 GHz (Global) | 2.4–2.4835 GHz | 250 kbps | 16 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The 2.4 GHz band offers **250 kbps** data rate across **16 channels** — this is the most-tested figure in board exams.

---

## 2.2 ZigBee

**ZigBee** is built *on top of* IEEE 802.15.4 and adds **network, application, and security layers**. It is governed by the **Connectivity Standards Alliance (CSA, formerly ZigBee Alliance)**.

### Architecture

ZigBee stack = IEEE 802.15.4 (PHY + MAC) + ZigBee Network Layer (NWK) + ZigBee Application Layer (APL) + *ZigBee Cluster Library (ZCL)*.

### Device Types
- **Coordinator (ZC)** — one per network; forms the network; bridges to other networks.
- **Router (ZR)** — relays frames; supports child devices.
- **End Device (ZED)** — cannot relay; sleeps to save power.

### Network Topologies
**Star**, **Mesh (most common in IoT)**, and **Cluster Tree**.

### Key ZigBee Formula / Parameter Sheet

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Frequency Band | 2.4 GHz (globally); also 868/915 MHz |
| Data Rate | 250 kbps @ 2.4 GHz |
| Range | 10–100 m |
| Network Nodes | up to **65,530** |
| Security | AES-128-CCM |
| Channels | 16 (2.4 GHz) |
| Modulation | O-QPSK (2.4 GHz); BPSK (868/915 MHz) |

---

## 2.3 Bluetooth & Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)

**Bluetooth** (IEEE 802.15.1) operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** using **FHSS (Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum)** with **1,600 hops/sec**.

### BLE Architecture
- **Classic Bluetooth** — high data rate, continuous connection, used for audio streaming.
- **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy / Bluetooth Smart)** — introduced in Bluetooth 4.0; designed for *bursty*, low-duty-cycle IoT sensor data.
- **Bluetooth 5.x and above** — extended range (up to 1 km in coded PHY) and 2× data rate (2 Mbps).

### Connection Modes
- **Advertising Mode** — peripheral broadcasts connectable packets; *no pairing required* for receiving data.
- **Connection Mode** — central initiates a connection; data exchange follows GATT (Generic Attribute Profile) with Services and Characteristics.

### Key BLE Formula / Parameter Sheet

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Frequency Band | 2.4 GHz ISM |
| Data Rate | 1 Mbps (BLE 4.x), 2 Mbps (BLE 5), 125 kbps (LE Coded, long range) |
| Range | 10 m (typical), up to **1 km** (BLE 5 coded PHY) |
| Channels | 40 (3 advertising, 37 data) |
| Modulation | GFSK |
| Hop Rate | 1,600 hops/s |
| Security | AES-128, LE Secure Connections (ECDH P-256) |

---

## 2.4 Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)

The dominant WLAN technology. For IoT, the most relevant variants are **802.11n (Wi-Fi 4)**, **802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)**, and **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6)**.

### Key Characteristics for IoT
- **High throughput** (up to 9.6 Gbps in Wi-Fi 6).
- **High power consumption** — not suitable for battery-only nodes; better for mains-powered IoT (smart speakers, IP cameras).
- **Security evolution:** WEP → WPA → WPA2 (AES-CCMP) → **WPA3 (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals, SAE)**.

### Shannon Capacity Bound (Killer Formula for KTU)

The maximum achievable data rate of any wireless channel is bounded by the **Shannon-Hartley theorem**:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

Where:
- $C$ = channel capacity (bps)
- $B$ = channel bandwidth (Hz)
- $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (unitless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula is *the* high-yield equation for any "calculate maximum throughput" question in the KTU exam.

---

## 2.5 LoRaWAN (Long Range Wide Area Network)

LoRaWAN is a **LPWAN protocol** designed for **long-range, low-power, low-data-rate** IoT — the perfect choice for KTU questions on "smart agriculture" or "remote metering."

### LoRa Modulation (Chirp Spread Spectrum)
LoRa uses **CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum)** — symbols are linear frequency sweeps ("chirps") whose duration is governed by the **Spreading Factor (SF)**.

### Spreading Factor Trade-off Equation

$$
\text{Bit Rate} = \frac{SF \times BW \times CR}{2^{SF}}
$$

Where:
- $SF$ = Spreading Factor (typically **7 to 12**)
- $BW$ = Bandwidth (125, 250, or 500 kHz)
- $CR$ = Coding Rate (1 = 4/5, 2 = 4/6, 3 = 4/7, 4 = 4/8)

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher SF ⇒ Longer range, lower data rate, higher airtime (more battery drain).** This trade-off is *extremely* testable.

### LoRaWAN Device Classes
- **Class A** — uplink + 2 downlink windows; *lowest power*.
- **Class B** — adds scheduled downlink slots synchronized by gateway beacons.
- **Class C** — continuously listening; *highest power*, no latency.

### Key LoRaWAN Parameter Sheet

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Frequency Bands | 433 / 868 / 915 MHz (ISM, regional) |
| Data Rate | 0.3 kbps – 50 kbps |
| Range | 2–15 km (rural), 1–5 km (urban) |
| Security | AES-128 (AppKey, NwkSKey, AppSKey) |
| Topology | Star-of-stars |

---

## 2.6 Sigfox

- **Ultra-narrowband (UNB)** modulation; **100 Hz** bandwidth per message.
- **Data rate:** 100 bps (uplink), 600 bps (downlink).
- **Range:** 30–50 km (rural), 3–10 km (urban).
- **Message limit:** **140 messages/day uplink, 4 downlink** — strictly enforced.
- Operates in sub-GHz ISM bands (868 MHz in Europe, 902 MHz in US).
- *Operator model* — requires subscription; no private deployment.

---

## 2.7 NB-IoT and LTE-M (Cellular IoT)

These are **3GPP-standardized** LPWAN technologies, designed to coexist with LTE in licensed spectrum.

| Feature | NB-IoT (Cat-NB1/NB2) | LTE-M (Cat-M1/M2) |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Bandwidth | 180 kHz | 1.08 MHz |
| Data Rate | ~26 kbps (DL), ~62 kbps (UL) | ~1 Mbps |
| Range | excellent (indoor penetration) | good |
| Mobility | poor (no handover) | full mobility (handover) |
| Voice | no | yes (VoLTE) |
| Power Saving Mode | PSM, eDRX | PSM, eDRX |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU memory hook:** *NB-IoT = Narrowband, No mobility, No voice.* *LTE-M = Mobile, More bandwidth, Mobility supported.*

---

## 2.8 RFID and NFC — The Shortest-Range Tier

### RFID
- **Passive tags** harvest energy from the reader's RF field — *no battery*.
- **Active tags** have their own transmitter.
- Frequency bands: **LF (125–134 kHz), HF (13.56 MHz), UHF (860–960 MHz), Microwave (2.45 GHz, 5.8 GHz)**.
- Range: 10 cm (LF/HF passive) to 100 m (UHF active).
- Standards: **EPC Class-1 Gen-2 (ISO 18000-63)** for UHF.

### NFC (Near Field Communication)
- Subset of HF RFID, **13.56 MHz**, range **< 10 cm (typically 4 cm)**.
- Three modes: **Reader/Writer, Peer-to-Peer, Card Emulation**.
- Standard: **ISO/IEC 14443, 15693, NFCIP-1 (ISO 18092)**.

---

## 2.9 6LoWPAN (IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN)

- Adaptation layer that **compresses IPv6 headers and fragments packets** to fit inside IEEE 802.15.4's 127-byte MAC frames.
- Enables *every* IoT node to have a **unique IPv6 address** — a foundational technology for the "real" Internet of Things.

### Header Compression
A full IPv6 header is **40 bytes**; with **IPHC (IP Header Compression)** it can be reduced to as low as **2 bytes** (for link-local, stateless compression).

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application | Preferred Tech |
|-------|-------------|----------------|
| Smart Agriculture | Soil moisture, weather stations | **LoRaWAN** |
| Smart Home | Bulbs, locks, thermostats | **ZigBee / BLE / Wi-Fi** |
| Healthcare Wearables | Heart-rate, ECG patches | **BLE** |
| Industrial IoT (IIoT) | Machine monitoring | **ZigBee / 6LoWPAN** |
| Smart City Parking | Spot sensors | **NB-IoT / LoRaWAN** |
| Livestock Tracking | Cattle ear-tags | **UHF RFID** |
| Contactless Payments | Cards, phones | **NFC** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Bit-Rate of a LoRa Channel

**Problem (KTU-style):** A LoRa transmitter uses **BW = 125 kHz**, **SF = 9**, and **CR = 1** (i.e., 4/5). Compute the physical-layer bit rate and the time-on-air for a 20-byte application payload.

### Step 1 — State the LoRa bit-rate formula

$$
R_b = \frac{SF \times BW \times CR}{2^{SF}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the given values

- $SF = 9$
- $BW = 125{,}000 \text{ Hz}$
- $CR = 1$

$$
R_b = \frac{9 \times 125{,}000 \times 1}{2^{9}} = \frac{1{,}125{,}000}{512}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator and denominator

$$
R_b = \frac{1{,}125{,}000}{512} \approx 2{,}197.27 \text{ bps}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the symbol duration

Each LoRa symbol occupies:

$$
T_{sym} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} = \frac{512}{125{,}000} = 4.096 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute payload size in bits

$$
\text{Payload bits} = 20 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 160 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 6 — Approximate Time-on-Air (ignoring preamble for simplicity)

$$
T_{air} \approx \frac{160}{2{,}197.27} \approx 72.8 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 7 — Interpret the result

> [!NOTE]
> At SF = 9, BW = 125 kHz, only **~2.2 kbps** is achievable — this illustrates *why* LoRa is unsuitable for video or voice IoT applications and is *only* for periodic, tiny telemetry.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Capacity for a Wi-Fi Channel

**Problem (KTU-style):** A Wi-Fi channel has **B = 20 MHz** bandwidth. The received signal power is **S = –65 dBm** and the noise floor is **N = –95 dBm**. Compute the theoretical maximum throughput.

### Step 1 — Convert dBm to milliwatts

$$
P_{\text{mW}} = 10^{\frac{P_{\text{dBm}}}{10}}
$$

For S:

$$
S = 10^{(-65)/10} = 10^{-6.5} \approx 3.16 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mW}
$$

For N:

$$
N = 10^{(-95)/10} = 10^{-9.5} \approx 3.16 \times 10^{-10} \text{ mW}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute S/N (unitless)

$$
\frac{S}{N} = \frac{3.16 \times 10^{-7}}{3.16 \times 10^{-10}} = 10^{3} = 1000
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Shannon's formula

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

$$
C = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) = 20 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1001)
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate $\log_2(1001)$

Using $\log_2(1024) = 10$ and linear approximation:

$$
\log_2(1001) \approx \log_2(1024) - \frac{1024 - 1001}{1024 \cdot \ln 2} \approx 9.967
$$

### Step 5 — Final computation

$$
C \approx 20 \times 10^{6} \times 9.967 \approx 199.34 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Shannon bound gives **~199 Mbps** as the absolute theoretical ceiling. Real Wi-Fi achieves a fraction of this because of overhead, modulation limits, and coding redundancy. KTU questions often ask students to *compare* the Shannon bound with the actual 802.11n data rate.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Access Technologies

> [!TIP]
> This is the single most high-yield table for KTU Module 3. Memorize the values in the last column ("Best Use").

| Technology | Standard | Frequency | Data Rate | Range | Topology | Security | Modulation | Power Profile | Best Use |
|------------|----------|-----------|-----------|-------|----------|----------|------------|---------------|----------|
| IEEE 802.15.4 | 802.15.4 | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | 10–100 m | Star/Mesh | AES-128 | O-QPSK | Low | Foundation for IoT |
| ZigBee | ZigBee 3.0 | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | 10–100 m | Mesh | AES-128-CCM | O-QPSK | Very Low | Smart Home, sensor mesh |
| BLE | 802.15.1 / BT 5.x | 2.4 GHz | 1–2 Mbps | 10 m–1 km | Star (P2P) | AES-128 + ECDH | GFSK | Ultra-Low | Wearables, beacons |
| Wi-Fi | 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | 54 Mbps – 9.6 Gbps | 50–200 m | Star (BSS) | WPA3 / SAE | OFDM, OFDMA | High | IP cameras, smart speakers |
| LoRaWAN | LoRaWAN 1.0.x | 433/868/915 MHz | 0.3–50 kbps | 2–15 km | Star-of-stars | AES-128 | CSS | Very Low | Smart agriculture, metering |
| Sigfox | Sigfox (UNB) | 868/902 MHz | 100 bps UL | 30–50 km rural | Star | AES-128 (optional) | UNB / DBPSK | Ultra-Low | Asset tracking, low-rate telemetry |
| NB-IoT | 3GPP Cat-NB1/2 | Licensed LTE | ~26/62 kbps | excellent | Star | LTE security stack | QPSK | Low (PSM) | Smart meters, parking |
| LTE-M | 3GPP Cat-M1 | Licensed LTE | ~1 Mbps | good | Star | LTE security stack | 16-QAM | Low–Medium | Wearables, fleet |
| RFID (passive) | ISO 18000 / EPC Gen-2 | LF/HF/UHF | varies | 10 cm – 10 m | Reader–Tag | EPC password | Backscatter | Harvested | Inventory, tolling |
| NFC | ISO 14443 / 18092 | 13.56 MHz | 106/212/424 kbps | < 10 cm | P2P | AES, MIFARE | ASK/FSK | Harvested | Payments, access control |
| 6LoWPAN | RFC 4944 / 6282 | 2.4 GHz (over 802.15.4) | same as PHY | 10–100 m | Mesh | AES-128 (802.15.4) | per PHY | Low | IPv6 for tiny devices |
| Z-Wave | Z-Wave Plus | 908.42 MHz (US) | 100 kbps | 30–100 m | Mesh | AES-128 | GFSK | Very Low | Smart-home lighting, locks |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — IoT Protocol Stack Architecture

The following diagram maps each access technology to its position in the **OSI-inspired IoT protocol stack**, illustrating which layers each technology spans.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Application Layer - HTTP, CoAP, MQTT]
    nodeB[Transport Layer - UDP, TCP]
    nodeC[Network Layer - IPv6, RPL, 6LoWPAN]
    nodeD[Adaptation Layer - 6LoWPAN Header Compression]
    nodeE[MAC Layer - IEEE 802.15.4 CSMA-CA]
    nodeF[PHY Layer - 2.4 GHz O-QPSK]
    nodeG[Application - ZigBee Cluster Library ZCL]
    nodeH[Network Layer - ZigBee NWK Mesh]
    nodeI[BLE Stack - GATT and ATT]
    nodeJ[BT PHY - 2.4 GHz GFSK 1 Mbps]
    nodeK[Wi-Fi App - HTTP, mDNS]
    nodeL[Wi-Fi MAC - 802.11 CSMA-CA DCF]
    nodeM[Wi-Fi PHY - OFDM 2.4 or 5 GHz]
    nodeN[LoRaWAN App - CayenneLPP]
    nodeO[LoRaWAN MAC - Class A B C]
    nodeP[LoRa PHY - Chirp Spread Spectrum Sub-GHz]
    nodeQ[RFID and NFC Reader Tag Air Interface]

    subgraph ZIGBEE_PROTOCOL_STACK
        nodeA
        nodeB
        nodeC
        nodeD
        nodeE
        nodeF
        nodeG
        nodeH
    end

    subgraph BLE_PROTOCOL_STACK
        nodeI
        nodeJ
    end

    subgraph WIFI_PROTOCOL_STACK
        nodeK
        nodeL
        nodeM
    end

    subgraph LORAWAN_PROTOCOL_STACK
        nodeN
        nodeO
        nodeP
    end

    subgraph RFID_STACK
        nodeQ
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** ZigBee (and 6LoWPAN) is the *deepest* stack because it spans from PHY all the way up to the Application Layer with its own mesh routing and cluster library. BLE and Wi-Fi rely on the host's TCP/IP stack, while RFID/NFC operate *only* at the air-interface level.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — LoRaWAN Network Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[End Node - Soil Sensor]
    node2[End Node - Cattle Tracker]
    node3[End Node - Water Meter]
    node4[LoRaWAN Gateway - 8 Channel Concentrator]
    node5[Network Server - LORIOT or TTN]
    node6[Application Server - Dashboard]
    node7[End User - Farmer Mobile App]
    node8[Join Server - Provisioning and Key Mgmt]

    node1 -->|CSS Uplink 868 MHz SF7 to SF12| node4
    node2 -->|CSS Uplink| node4
    node3 -->|CSS Uplink| node4
    node4 -->|IP Backhaul Ethernet or 4G| node5
    node5 -->|MQTT or HTTPS| node6
    node6 -->|HTTPS WebSocket| node7
    node8 -.->|Provisioning Keys| node5
    node5 -.->|Downlink MAC Commands| node4
    node4 -.->|CSS Downlink Window| node1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functional interpretation:** The diagram shows the **star-of-stars** topology unique to LoRaWAN. End nodes are *not* in mesh; the gateway is a *dumb* radio bridge. All intelligence — deduplication, ADR (Adaptive Data Rate), and security key derivation — sits in the **Network Server**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — BLE Connection Establishment Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Adv as BLE Peripheral (Sensor)
    participant Cnt as BLE Central (Phone)
    participant Scn as Scanning
    participant Adv2 as Advertising
    participant Con as Connected
    participant Dsc as Discovery
    participant Sub as Subscribe Notification

    Scn->>Adv: Listen on 3 Advertising Channels
    Adv->>Cnt: ADV_IND PDU containing Device Name and Services
    Cnt->>Adv: SCAN_REQ
    Adv->>Cnt: SCAN_RSP with additional data
    Cnt->>Adv: CONNECT_REQ with Hop Increment and Access Address
    Note over Adv,Cnt: Connection Established - 37 Data Channels used
    Cnt->>Adv: Service Discovery GATT Primary Service UUID
    Adv->>Cnt: Service Discovered
    Cnt->>Adv: Write to CCCD to Enable Notification
    Note over Adv,Cnt: Sensor Data Stream Begins
    Adv->>Cnt: Notification PDU with Temperature and Humidity
    Cnt->>Adv: Empty ACK
    Cnt->>Adv: Connection Parameter Update Request
    Adv->>Cnt: Connection Parameter Update Response
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this diagram is high-yield:** KTU frequently asks about the *three advertising channels* of BLE (channels 37, 38, 39) versus the *37 data channels* used post-connection. The frequency-hopping sequence then cycles through the data channels.

---
