- Variable header: 2 bytes (topic length) + topic string (e.g., "home/room1/temp" = 17 bytes)
- Total MQTT overhead: $H_{MQTT} = 2 + 2 + 17 = 21$ bytes

TCP+IP overhead: $T_{MQTT} = 40$ bytes

$$B_{MQTT} = 4 + 21 + 40 = 65 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4: Calculate CoAP Overhead**

CoAP (RFC 7252):
- Header: 4 bytes
- Token: 1 byte (Token Length = 1)
- Options: URI-path "temp" + Content-Format "application/json" ≈ 6 bytes
- Payload marker: 1 byte (0xFF)
- Total CoAP overhead: $H_{CoAP} = 4 + 1 + 6 + 1 = 12$ bytes

UDP+IP overhead: $T_{CoAP} = 20_{IP} + 8_{UDP} = 28$ bytes

$$B_{CoAP} = 4 + 12 + 28 = 44 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5: Compute the Overhead Efficiency Ratio**

Define the **Protocol Efficiency Metric** as:

$$\eta = \frac{P}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$$

For HTTP:
$$\eta_{HTTP} = \frac{4}{174} \times 100\% \approx 2.30\%$$

For MQTT:
$$\eta_{MQTT} = \frac{4}{65} \times 100\% \approx 6.15\%$$

For CoAP:
$$\eta_{CoAP} = \frac{4}{44} \times 100\% \approx 9.09\%$$

**Step 6: Annualized Bandwidth Comparison (Over 1 Year)**

At 1 transmission/minute = **525,600 transmissions/year**:

$$B_{HTTP,\ annual} = 174 \times 525600 = 91,454,400 \text{ bytes} \approx 87.2 \text{ MB}$$

$$B_{MQTT,\ annual} = 65 \times 525600 = 34,164,000 \text{ bytes} \approx 32.6 \text{ MB}$$

$$B_{CoAP,\ annual} = 44 \times 525600 = 23,126,400 \text{ bytes} \approx 22.1 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 7: Cost Impact (assuming cellular data at ₹0.50/MB)**

$$\text{Cost}_{HTTP} = 87.2 \times 0.50 = ₹43.60 \text{ per device per year}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{MQTT} = 32.6 \times 0.50 = ₹16.30 \text{ per device per year}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{CoAP} = 22.1 \times 0.50 = ₹11.05 \text{ per device per year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a deployment of **10,000 IoT devices**, the choice of CoAP over HTTP saves approximately **₹3,25,500/year** in cellular data costs alone — a critical factor in massive IoT (MIoT) deployments.

## 3.2 MQTT QoS 2 Handshake — Exact-Once Delivery Derivation

QoS 2 is a 4-step handshake guaranteeing exactly-once delivery. Let us derive the message flow state machine:

**Step 1:** Publisher sends `PUBLISH` (Packet ID = X, DUP = 0)

$$\text{State}_{Publisher}: PUBLISH\_SENT \rightarrow \text{awaiting PUBREC}$$

**Step 2:** Broker responds with `PUBREC` (Packet ID = X)

$$\text{State}_{Broker}: PUBLISH\_STORED \rightarrow \text{awaiting PUBREL}$$

**Step 3:** Publisher sends `PUBREL` (Packet ID = X)

$$\text{State}_{Publisher}: PUBREC\_RECEIVED \rightarrow \text{awaiting PUBCOMP}$$

**Step 4:** Broker responds with `PUBCOMP` (Packet ID = X)

$$\text{State}_{Broker}: RELEASED \rightarrow \text{message delivered exactly once}$$

The **4-way handshake overhead** can be expressed as:

$$O_{QoS2} = 4 \times (H_{MQTT} + T_{TCP/IP})$$

$$O_{QoS2} = 4 \times (2 + 40) = 168 \text{ bytes of control overhead per logical message}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Selection Heuristic

| Scenario | Recommended Protocol | Justification |
|----------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Battery-powered sensor, lossy link** | MQTT QoS 0 or CoAP NON | Minimize energy per transmission |
| **Critical actuator control (valve, lock)** | MQTT QoS 2 | Exactly-once guarantee |
| **RESTful device management** | CoAP with LwM2M | HTTP semantics over UDP |
| **Real-time industrial robotics** | DDS | Sub-millisecond latency, broker-less |
| **Chat-based smart home control** | XMPP | Presence + encryption built-in |
| **Enterprise message persistence** | AMQP | Durable queues, transactions |
| **Web dashboard integration** | HTTP/REST | Browser-native, no extra client lib |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — MQTT Publish/Subscribe Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Pub1[Publisher Sensor 1] -->|PUBLISH temp/city1| Broker
    Pub2[Publisher Sensor 2] -->|PUBLISH temp/city2| Broker
    Pub3[Publisher Actuator] -->|PUBLISH cmd/valve| Broker
    Broker[MQTT Broker Mosquitto]
    Broker -->|Fanout to subscribers| Sub1[Subscriber Mobile App]
    Broker -->|Fanout to subscribers| Sub2[Subscriber Cloud Analytics]
    Broker -->|Fanout to subscribers| Sub3[Subscriber Database Logger]
    Broker -.->|LWT triggered on disconnect| LWTC[Last Will Topic]
    style Broker fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style LWTC fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — CoAP Request/Response & Observe Pattern

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CoAP Client
    participant S as CoAP Server Sensor
    C->>S: CON GET /temperature (Token T1, Observe 0)
    S-->>C: ACK 2.05 Content (Token T1, Observe 1)
    Note over S: Temperature changes
    S->>C: CON 2.05 Content (Token T2, Observe 2)
    C->>S: ACK (Token T2)
    Note over C,S: Asynchronous push continues...
    Note over S: Client cancels observation
    C->>S: CON GET /temperature (Token T3, Observe 1 with R)
    S-->>C: ACK 2.05 Content (Token T3, no Observe)
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Protocol Stack Comparison (OSI Layers)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph MQTTStack
        MQTT[MQTT Layer 7] --> MQTTT[TCP] --> MQTTIP[IP] --> MQTTPHY[Radio]
    end
    subgraph CoAPStack
        COAP[CoAP Layer 7] --> COAPD[DTLS Optional] --> COAPU[UDP] --> COAPIP[IP] --> COAPPHY[Radio]
    end
    subgraph HTTPStack
        HTTP[HTTP Layer 7] --> HTTPT[TLS Optional] --> HTTPC[TCP] --> HTTPIP[IP] --> HTTPPHY[Radio]
    end
    style MQTT fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style COAP fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style HTTP fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — MQTT QoS 2 Four-Way Handshake State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PublishSent
    PublishSent --> PubRecReceived: Receive PUBREC
    PublishSent --> PublishSent: Timeout Resend PUBLISH
    PubRecReceived --> PubcompReceived: Send PUBREL Receive PUBCOMP
    PubRecReceived --> PubRecReceived: Timeout Resend PUBREL
    PubcompReceived --> [*]: Message Delivered Exactly Once
    note right of PubcompReceived: 4 packet exchanges guarantee no duplicates
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — IoT Protocol Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[New IoT Application] --> Q1{Need Real-time less than 1ms latency?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| DDS[DDS Protocol]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Device is severely constrained RAM less than 50KB?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| COAP[CoAP Protocol]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need message persistence and queues?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| AMQP[AMQP Protocol]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need presence and chat like features?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| XMPP[XMPP Protocol]
    Q4 -->|No| MQTT[MQTT Default Choice]
    style DDS fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style COAP fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style AMQP fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style XMPP fill:#e377c2,color:#fff
    style MQTT fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between MQTT and CoAP with respect to transport protocol, message overhead, and typical use cases.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|-----------|------|------|
| Transport Protocol | TCP (connection-oriented, reliable) | UDP (connectionless, lightweight) |
| Message Overhead | 2-byte fixed header + topic string | 4-byte base header + options |
| Default Port | 1883 (8883 for TLS) | 5683 (5684 for DTLS) |
| Communication Pattern | Publish/Subscribe (broker-mediated) | Request/Response + Observe |
| Typical Use Case | Continuous telemetry, cloud messaging | Resource-constrained RESTful devices |
| QoS Support | 3 levels (0, 1, 2) | 2 levels (CON/NON) |

**Conclusion:** MQTT is preferred for **stable, broker-mediated, cloud-backed** telemetry, while CoAP excels in **resource-constrained, RESTful, low-overhead** M2M communication.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the role of a Broker in MQTT? Explain the Last Will and Testament (LWT) feature.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Broker** is the central server in an MQTT deployment that acts as a message router. It accepts message publications from publishers, filters them by topic, and delivers them to all interested subscribers. The broker **decouples** publishers from subscribers, enabling asynchronous, many-to-many communication without direct device-to-device connections.

**Last Will and Testament (LWT):**
- When a client connects to the broker, it can optionally register an LWT message associated with a specific **LWT topic**.
- If the broker detects an **abnormal disconnection** (e.g., network failure, device crash, missed keep-alive), it automatically publishes the LWT message on behalf of the disconnected client.
- **Use Case:** A smart home motion sensor registers LWT as "offline" on topic `home/security/sensor1/status`. If the sensor fails, the home automation system immediately receives this notification and triggers an alarm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose 3–5 marks by:
> 1. Confusing **QoS levels** between MQTT and CoAP (MQTT has 3, CoAP has 2).
> 2. Writing only definitions without **architectural diagrams** (mandatory for 7+ mark sub-questions).
> 3. Failing to mention **port numbers** and **default transport**.
> 4. Ignoring the **security layer** (TLS/DTLS) when discussing protocol stacks.
> 5. Mixing up **broker-based** (MQTT, AMQP) vs **brokerless** (DDS) protocols.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat architecture diagram, explain the working of MQTT protocol. Discuss all three Quality of Service (QoS) levels with their packet exchange sequences.**

#### **Part (a) — MQTT Architecture and Working (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture Components:**

1. **Publisher:** The IoT device (e.g., temperature sensor) that generates data and publishes it to a topic on the broker.
2. **Subscriber:** The consumer (e.g., mobile app, cloud service) that subscribes to one or more topics of interest.
3. **Broker:** The central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX) that mediates communication.

**Working Flow:**

1. **Connection Establishment:** The client opens a TCP connection to the broker on port 1883 (or 8883 for TLS). It sends a `CONNECT` packet containing Client ID, Clean Session flag, Keep Alive timer, and optional credentials.
2. **Connection Acknowledgment:** The broker responds with `CONNACK`, indicating success or failure (with return codes 0–5).
3. **Subscription:** Subscribers send a `SUBSCRIBE` packet specifying topics and desired QoS levels. The broker confirms with a `SUBACK`.
4. **Publication:** Publishers send `PUBLISH` packets with the topic and payload. The broker routes them to all matching subscribers.
5. **Heartbeat:** Clients send `PINGREQ` packets within the Keep Alive interval; broker responds with `PINGRESP`.
6. **Disconnection:** A clean disconnect uses a `DISCONNECT` packet; otherwise, the broker assumes failure.

**Architectural Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    Dev1[Temp Sensor Publisher] -->|CONNECT/PUBLISH| Broker
    Dev2[Humidity Sensor Publisher] -->|CONNECT/PUBLISH| Broker
    Broker[MQTT Broker]
    Broker -->|SUBSCRIBE topic home/+/data| App[Mobile App]
    Broker -->|SUBSCRIBE topic home/floor1/#| Cloud[AWS IoT Core]
    Broker -->|SUBSCRIBE cmd/valve| Ctrl[Actuator Controller]
    style Broker fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
```

#### **Part (b) — MQTT QoS Levels with Packet Exchanges (7 Marks)**

**QoS 0 — At Most Once:**
- Packet Exchange: `PUBLISH` (single packet)
- Guarantee: Best-effort delivery, no acknowledgment
- Use Case: Lossy sensor telemetry (e.g., environmental temperature)

**QoS 1 — At Least Once:**
- Packet Exchange: `PUBLISH` → `PUBACK`
- Guarantee: Message will arrive, but duplicates are possible (publisher retransmits on timeout if no `PUBACK` received)
- Use Case: Smart metering, general telemetry

**QoS 2 — Exactly Once (4-way handshake):**
- Packet Exchange: `PUBLISH` → `PUBREC` → `PUBREL` → `PUBCOMP`
- Guarantee: Message arrives exactly once, no duplicates
- Use Case: Critical control commands (e.g., industrial valve shutoff, medical device actuation)

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | QoS 0 | QoS 1 | QoS 2 |
|---------|-------|-------|-------|
| Packet Count | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| Reliability | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Latency | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Bandwidth Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Idempotency Required | N/A | Yes (server) | N/A (broker handles) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Explain the Constrained Application Protocol (CoAP) in detail. Compare it with HTTP and discuss the Observe extension.**

#### **Part (a) — CoAP Architecture, Message Format, and Methods (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** CoAP (RFC 7252) is an IETF-standardized application protocol designed for resource-constrained IoT devices. It provides HTTP-like RESTful semantics (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) but operates over **UDP** with a **4-byte binary header**, reducing overhead by approximately **75%** compared to HTTP.

**Message Format:**

A CoAP message consists of:

1. **Header (4 bytes):**
   - Ver (2 bits): Version = 1
   - T (2 bits): Type (CON, NON, ACK, RST)
   - TKL (4 bits): Token Length (0–8)
   - Code (8 bits): Class.Detail (e.g., 2.05 = Content)
   - Message ID (16 bits): For duplicate detection and reliability

2. **Token (0–8 bytes):** Correlates responses to requests.

3. **Options (variable):** URI-path, URI-query, Content-Format, Observe, etc. Each option is TLV-encoded.

4. **Payload (variable):** Marked by the 0xFF byte, followed by binary content.

**CoAP Methods:**

| Method | Code | Purpose |
|--------|------|---------|
| GET | 0.01 | Retrieve resource representation |
| POST | 0.02 | Create a new resource or trigger action |
| PUT | 0.03 | Update an existing resource |
| DELETE | 0.04 | Remove a resource |

**Confirmable vs Non-Confirmable:**
- **CON:** Stop-and-wait ARQ with retransmission. Receiver must reply with ACK or RST.
- **NON:** Fire-and-forget, no acknowledgment. Used for repeated sensor data where occasional loss is acceptable.

#### **Part (b) — CoAP vs HTTP Comparison and Observe Extension (7 Marks)**

**Detailed Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | HTTP/1.1 | CoAP |
|-----------|----------|------|
| **Transport** | TCP (reliable, heavy) | UDP (lightweight, unreliable) |
| **Header Size** | 200 bytes – 2 KB | 4 bytes (base) |
| **Encoding** | Text/ASCII | Binary |
| **Connection Model** | Connection-per-request (or persistent) | Stateless, message-based |
| **Security** | TLS (heavy handshake) | DTLS (lightweight equivalent) |
| **Push Capability** | None (needs WebSockets) | Native via Observe |
| **Caching** | HTTP caching proxies | CoAP caching (max-age option) |
| **Discovery** | Limited | Built-in (/.well-known/core) |
| **Typical Use** | Web, browsers | Constrained IoT devices |

**Observe Extension (RFC 7641):**

The Observe pattern transforms CoAP from a pure request/response protocol into a **server-push** capable protocol:

1. The client sends a `GET` request with the **Observe option set to 0** (registration).
2. The server registers the client's interest and immediately responds with the current resource state and Observe option = 1.
3. **Whenever the resource state changes**, the server asynchronously sends a notification (CON) with an incremented Observe sequence number.
4. The client sends ACK, and the loop continues.
5. To deregister, the client sends another `GET` with the Observe option and the `R` (Re-Reset) flag set, or simply lets the registration expire.

**Advantages of Observe:**
- Eliminates polling overhead
- Reduces network traffic by ~60–80% in event-driven IoT
- Provides sub-second notification latency
- Maintains RESTful semantics

**Real-world Deployment:** CoAP with Observe is the foundation of **LwM2M (Lightweight M2M)** device management, used by **Oma SpecWorks** and major cellular IoT providers for firmware updates, sensor monitoring, and remote diagnostics.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision for KTU ESE — Memorize These Points:**

- ✅ MQTT operates over **TCP Port 1883** (plain) and **8883** (TLS). It uses a **broker** (e.g., Mosquitto) and supports **3 QoS levels** (0, 1, 2).
- ✅ CoAP operates over **UDP Port 5683** (plain) and **5684** (DTLS). It uses **4-byte binary header** and supports **CON/NON** message types.
- ✅ QoS 2 uses a **4-way handshake**: PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP (exactly-once delivery).
- ✅ LWT (Last Will and Testament) is an MQTT feature for **fault detection** on abnormal disconnection.
- ✅ The **Observe extension** (RFC 7641) enables CoAP server-push, eliminating polling.
- ✅ **DDS is broker-less** and uses peer-to-peer RTPS — ideal for real-time critical systems (autonomous vehicles, robotics).
- ✅ **AMQP** provides durable, transactional queues — used in enterprise IoT.
- ✅ **XMPP** is XML-based with native presence — used for chat-style IoT interfaces.
- ✅ HTTP/REST has the **highest overhead** (~200B–2KB headers) — unsuitable for constrained devices.
- ✅ Protocol Efficiency η = P / B_total × 100%; **CoAP is ~4× more efficient** than HTTP for small payloads.
- ✅ MQTT topics use `/` hierarchy; `+` is single-level wildcard, `#` is multi-level wildcard.
- ✅ The **broker decouples** publishers from subscribers — they do not need to know each other's IP/port.
- ✅ **Security:** MQTT uses TLS; CoAP uses DTLS; XMPP supports OMEMO E2E encryption; AMQP supports SASL+TLS.
- ✅ **KTU's "BIG 4" focus:** MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, and one of (XMPP/AMQP/DDS) for descriptive questions.
- ✅ **Numerical question pattern:** Calculate bytes-on-wire, overhead ratio, or annual bandwidth for a given IoT scenario.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "MQTT-C-HAD":**
> **M**QTT (Pub/Sub), **C**oAP (Req/Resp), **H**TTP (Req/Resp), **A**MQP (Queues), **D**DS (Real-time) — these are the **5 protocols** most likely to appear in KTU Module 3 questions.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Transport Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Transport Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Transport Layer** is the **Layer 4** of both the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** and the **TCP/IP Protocol Suite**. It is fundamentally responsible for providing **end-to-end logical communication, process-to-process delivery, and data transport services** between a source application and a destination application running on remote hosts. While the Network Layer (Layer 3) ensures host-to-host packet delivery, the Transport Layer refines this by ensuring that data is correctly addressed to the **specific software process (application)** on each host through the concept of **ports and sockets**.

In the context of **Ethical Hacking & IoT Security (PBCCT604)**, the Transport Layer is the primary attack surface for threats such as **TCP SYN Floods, UDP Amplification Attacks, Port Scanning, Session Hijacking, and Transport Layer DDoS**. It is also the layer where critical defensive protocols like **TLS (Transport Layer Security)** and **DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security for IoT)** operate to provide confidentiality and integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Transport Layer is examined heavily in Module 3 of PBCCT604, with focus on **TCP, UDP, the Three-Way Handshake, Port Addressing, common transport-layer attacks, and IoT-specific transport protocols (MQTT, CoAP, DTLS)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Service

Imagine the **Internet** as a giant international postal system:

| Internet Concept | Real-World Analogy |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Host / Server** | A large apartment building with thousands of flats |
| **IP Address (Network Layer)** | The building's street address (e.g., "12 Baker Street") |
| **Port Number (Transport Layer)** | The specific flat number inside the building (e.g., "Flat 80") |
| **Socket** | The complete address: `Building + Flat` (e.g., `12 Baker Street, Flat 80`) |
| **TCP** | **Registered Post with Acknowledgment** — sender requires a signed receipt and ensures the letter arrives in order, intact |
| **UDP** | **Ordinary Postcard** — sender drops it into the mailbox, no guarantee of delivery, but extremely fast |

The Transport Layer is the **"Flat Number Inspector"** of the Internet — it takes a packet delivered to the correct building (IP address) and delivers it to the precise resident (application process) who is expecting it.

## 1.3 The Two Core Transport Layer Protocols

The Internet primarily uses **two** transport protocols, both standardized by the **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)** in **RFC 793** (TCP) and **RFC 768** (UDP):

- **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** — A **connection-oriented**, **reliable**, **byte-stream** protocol. It guarantees ordered, error-checked, and duplicate-suppressed delivery of data through mechanisms like the **Three-Way Handshake**, **sequence numbers**, **acknowledgments**, and **flow control**. The standard header length is **20 bytes** (minimum).

- **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** — A **connectionless**, **unreliable**, **best-effort** protocol. It sends **datagrams** with minimal overhead and no handshake. Its fixed header is only **8 bytes**, making it ideal for **latency-sensitive IoT and real-time applications** (DNS queries, video streaming, VoIP, MQTT).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **TCP Header Minimum Size:** **20 bytes**
> - **UDP Header Fixed Size:** **8 bytes**
> - **Total Port Range:** **0 to 65535** (2¹⁶ possible ports)
> - **Well-Known Ports:** **0–1023**
> - **Registered Ports:** **1024–49151**
> - **Dynamic/Private Ports:** **49152–65535**

## 1.4 Socket Addressing — The Complete Delivery Endpoint

A **socket address** is the unique endpoint identifier formed by the concatenation of an **IP address** and a **port number**, written in standard notation as:

$$\text{Socket} = \langle \text{IP Address}, \text{Port Number} \rangle$$

For example, a web server connection might be represented as `192.168.1.10:443`, meaning host **192.168.1.10** on port **443** (HTTPS). This four-tuple `(Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port)` uniquely identifies **every active TCP conversation** on the Internet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Transport Layer Functions

The Transport Layer performs **five core logical functions**. Each is a high-yield KTU theory point:

- **1. Process-to-Process Addressing (Port Multiplexing):**
  - **Why:** A single host runs hundreds of applications (browser, email, IoT sensor). Without port numbers, the OS cannot know which application should receive an incoming packet.
  - **How:** Each application process binds to a unique 16-bit port number. The Transport Layer inspects the destination port in the incoming segment and forwards the payload to the correct socket buffer.

- **2. Connection Establishment & Termination:**
  - **TCP** uses the **Three-Way Handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK)** to establish a virtual connection before any data flows.
  - **TCP** uses the **Four-Way Termination (FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK)** to gracefully close the connection.
  - **UDP** performs **no handshake** — it is "fire-and-forget".

- **3. Reliable Data Transfer (TCP only):**
  - Implemented through **sequence numbers**, **acknowledgments (ACKs)**, **retransmission timers**, and **checksums**.
  - Guarantees that data is delivered **exactly once, in order, without corruption**.

- **4. Flow Control:**
  - Prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver.
  - **TCP** uses a **sliding window protocol** where the receiver advertises its available buffer size in the **Window Size field** of the TCP header.

- **5. Congestion Control:**
  - Prevents the network itself from being overwhelmed.
  - **TCP** employs algorithms like **Slow Start**, **Congestion Avoidance**, **Fast Retransmit**, and **Fast Recovery** (the modern standard is **TCP Reno / TCP CUBIC**).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every transport-layer formula and metric required for solving KTU numerical and analytical questions.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Socket Address** | $\text{Socket} = \langle \text{IP}, \text{Port} \rangle$ | Tuple | Uniquely identifies a session endpoint |
| **Maximum Port Value** | $2^{16} - 1 = 65535$ | Dimensionless | Range is $0 \le \text{Port} \le 65535$ |
| **Well-Known Port Range** | $0 \le P \le 1023$ | Dimensionless | Reserved for IANA / privileged services |
| **Registered Port Range** | $1024 \le P \le 49151$ | Dimensionless | Assigned by IANA on request |
| **Dynamic Port Range** | $49152 \le P \le 65535$ | Dimensionless | Ephemeral / private client ports |
| **TCP Header Min Size** | $H_{TCP} = 20 \text{ bytes}$ | Bytes | Without Options/Padding |
| **TCP Header Max Size** | $H_{TCP}^{max} = 60 \text{ bytes}$ | Bytes | With 40 bytes of Options field |
| **UDP Header Size** | $H_{UDP} = 8 \text{ bytes}$ | Bytes | Fixed, no options |
| **TCP Sequence Number Range** | $0 \le S \le 2^{32} - 1$ | Dimensionless | Wraps around modulo $2^{32}$ |
| **TCP Window Size (Max)** | $W_{max} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65535$ | Bytes | Without Window Scaling option |
| **TCP Window (with Scaling)** | $W_{scaled} = W_{advertised} \times 2^{S}$ | Bytes | $S$ is the Shift Count (0–14) |
| **Effective Throughput (Stop-and-Wait)** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{L}{RTT + T_f}$ | Bytes/sec | $L$ = packet size, $T_f$ = transmission time |
| **Effective Throughput (Sliding Window)** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{W \times L}{RTT}$ | Bytes/sec | $W$ = window size in packets |
| **Maximum Achievable Throughput** | $\text{Bandwidth-Delay Product: } BDP = BW \times RTT$ | Bits | Theoretical pipe capacity |
| **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $RTT = 2 \times T_p + T_f + T_q$ | Seconds | $T_p$ = prop delay, $T_f$ = frame tx time, $T_q$ = queueing |
| **Checksum (1's complement sum)** | $\Sigma_{16} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{word}_i \quad (\text{mod } 2^{16})$ | Bits | Wrap-around carry added back |
| **TCP Pseudo-Header Length** | $96 \text{ bits}$ (12 bytes) | Bytes | Used for checksum computation only |
| **MTU Impact on TCP** | $MSS = MTU - H_{IP} - H_{TCP} = 1500 - 20 - 20 = 1460$ | Bytes | Standard Ethernet MSS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to compute **throughput or bandwidth-delay product**, always show units in your final answer. Examiners award the **methodology mark** only if you explicitly state the formula before substitution.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In **Ethical Hacking & IoT Security**, mastery of the Transport Layer is non-negotiable for the following engineering tasks:

- **Penetration Testing:** Tools like **Nmap** operate by sending crafted TCP SYN/ACK/FIN packets and analyzing responses to fingerprint operating systems and discover open ports.
- **IoT Protocol Design:** Lightweight IoT protocols such as **MQTT (port 1883)** run over TCP for reliability, while **CoAP (port 5683)** runs over UDP for low-power, lossy networks (LLNs).
- **DDoS Mitigation:** Distinguishing between **SYN Floods** (half-open TCP connections) and **UDP Floods** (volumetric UDP amplification) requires a deep understanding of how each protocol establishes sessions.
- **Secure Transport Design:** **TLS 1.3** encrypts data at the Transport Layer (sitting above TCP), while **DTLS 1.2** provides equivalent security for UDP-based IoT traffic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: TCP Three-Way Handshake State Transitions

The **TCP Three-Way Handshake** synchronizes **Initial Sequence Numbers (ISN)** between a client and server. Let us derive the complete sequence-number state at every step.

### Step 1 — Define Variables and Initial State

Let:
- $C$ = Client (Initiator)
- $S$ = Server (Listener)
- $x$ = Client's chosen Initial Sequence Number (ISN)
- $y$ = Server's chosen Initial Sequence Number (ISN)
- $SEQ$ = Sequence Number field value
- $ACK$ = Acknowledgment Number field value
- $\text{ACK flag} = 1$ denotes acknowledgment is valid

**Initial state:** Both $C$ and $S$ are in the **CLOSED** state. $S$ transitions to **LISTEN** (passive open).

### Step 2 — Message 1: Client Sends SYN

Client $C$ sends a TCP segment with:
- $SYN = 1$ (synchronize flag set)
- $SEQ = x$ (client's chosen ISN)
- $ACK$ flag is **not** set (so ACK field is invalid)

State transition: $C$ moves from **CLOSED** to **SYN-SENT**.

**Logical meaning:** $C$ is announcing, *"I want to open a connection, and my starting byte will be numbered $x$."*

### Step 3 — Message 2: Server Responds with SYN + ACK

Server $S$ receives the SYN and responds with a single segment carrying **two simultaneous flags**:
- $SYN = 1$ (server also synchronizes)
- $ACK = 1$ (server acknowledges the client's SYN)
- $SEQ = y$ (server's chosen ISN)
- $ACK = x + 1$ (acknowledging receipt of $x$, expecting next byte to be $x+1$)

State transition: $S$ moves from **LISTEN** to **SYN-RECEIVED**.

**Logical meaning:** $S$ is announcing, *"I acknowledge your $x$, my next expected byte from you is $x+1$. My starting byte will be numbered $y$."*

### Step 4 — Message 3: Client Sends Final ACK

Client $C$ receives the SYN+ACK and sends the final acknowledgment:
- $SYN$ flag is **not** set
- $ACK = 1$
- $SEQ = x + 1$ (next byte from client)
- $ACK = y + 1$ (acknowledging receipt of $y$, expecting $y+1$ from server)

State transition: $C$ moves from **SYN-SENT** to **ESTABLISHED**.

Upon receipt by the server: $S$ moves from **SYN-RECEIVED** to **ESTABLISHED**.

### Step 5 — Consolidated State Transition Equation

The connection establishment can be summarized as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C: \quad & \text{CLOSED} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN}(x)} \text{SYN-SENT} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK}(y+1)} \text{ESTABLISHED} \\
S: \quad & \text{LISTEN} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN-ACK}(y, x+1)} \text{SYN-RECEIVED} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK}(x+1)} \text{ESTABLISHED}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Total Packets Exchanged

$$
P_{3WH} = 3 \text{ packets} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{setup} = 1.5 \times RTT
$$

The **connection setup latency** equals **one and a half round-trip times** because the ACK can be piggy-backed onto the first data packet, eliminating a fourth round-trip.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: TCP Connection Termination (Four-Way Handshake)

Unlike the 3-way handshake, TCP termination requires **four** steps because TCP supports **half-close** operation.

### Step 1 — Active Close Initiated by Client

Client $C$ sends:
- $FIN = 1, ACK = 1$
- $SEQ = u$ (current client sequence)
- $ACK = v$ (current server acknowledgment)

State: $C \to \text{FIN-WAIT-1}$

### Step 2 — Server Acknowledges the FIN

Server $S$ sends:
- $ACK = 1$ (no FIN yet — server may still have data to send)
- $SEQ = v, ACK = u + 1$

State: $S \to \text{CLOSE-WAIT}$, $C \to \text{FIN-WAIT-2}$

**Why two ACKs instead of one?** Because TCP is **full-duplex**, the client-to-server direction must close independently of the server-to-client direction.

### Step 3 — Server Sends Its Own FIN

After sending remaining data, $S$ sends:
- $FIN = 1, ACK = 1$
- $SEQ = w, ACK = u + 1$

State: $S \to \text{LAST-ACK}$

### Step 4 — Client Sends Final ACK

Client $C$ sends:
- $ACK = 1$
- $SEQ = u + 1, ACK = w + 1$

State: $C \to \text{TIME-WAIT} \xrightarrow{2 \times MSL} \text{CLOSED}$

Where **MSL = Maximum Segment Lifetime**, typically **2 minutes** (RFC 793) or **60 seconds** in modern Linux.

### Final Equation — Total Packets for Termination

$$
P_{4WH} = 4 \text{ packets} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{teardown} = 2 \times RTT
$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Sliding Window Throughput Calculation

Consider a TCP sender with window size $W$ packets, link bandwidth $BW$ bps, and round-trip time $RTT$ seconds. Each packet has length $L$ bytes.

### Step 1 — Identify the Bottleneck

If the sender is constrained only by the **receiver's advertised window** (ignoring network congestion), the maximum number of unacknowledged bytes in flight equals $W \times L$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Pipeline Throughput Formula

During one round-trip time, the sender can transmit exactly one full window's worth of data:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Data Delivered per RTT}}{\text{Time per RTT}}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{W \times L \text{ bytes}}{RTT \text{ seconds}}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Bits per Second for Consistency with Bandwidth

$$
\text{Throughput (bps)} = \frac{W \times L \times 8}{RTT}
$$

### Step 4 — Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) Constraint

For the window to fully utilize the link, it must be at least equal to the BDP:

$$
W_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{BW \times RTT}{L \times 8} \right\rceil \text{ packets}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A TCP connection has window size $W = 64$ KB, $RTT = 100$ ms, and packet length $L = 1$ KB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{W \times L}{RTT} \\
&= \frac{64 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{0.1 \text{ seconds}} \\
&= \frac{524288 \text{ bits}}{0.1 \text{ s}} \\
&= 5{,}242{,}880 \text{ bps} \approx 5.24 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Transport Layer Attack Surface

This matrix maps each protocol state/field to its corresponding **ethical hacking attack vector**, forming a high-yield analytical tool for KTU theory questions.

| Transport Mechanism | Legitimate Function | Associated Attack Vector | Attack Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SYN Flood** | Half-open connection queue | Attacker sends massive SYN packets, never sends final ACK | **DDoS / DoS** |
| **UDP Flood** | Stateless data delivery | Attacker floods random ports with UDP packets | **Volumetric DDoS** |
| **Port Scanning (SYN Scan)** | Server port listening | Half-open TCP scans to identify open ports | **Reconnaissance** |
| **Session Hijacking** | Stateful TCP session | Predicting TCP sequence numbers to inject data | **MITM / Spoofing** |
| **UDP Reflection** | Stateless query/response | Small request generates large response (DNS, NTP) | **Amplification Attack** |
| **Slowloris** | HTTP keep-alive over TCP | Holding TCP connections open with partial headers | **Low-and-Slow DoS** |
| **TCP Reset Attack** | Abrupt connection tear-down | Injecting spoofed RST packets to drop legitimate sessions | **Denial of Service** |
| **CoAP over UDP** | IoT sensor data | Sniffing or injecting unauthenticated CoAP messages | **IoT-Specific Attack** |
| **MQTT over TCP** | IoT pub/sub messaging | Subscribing to default topics or stealing credentials | **IoT Credential Theft** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Transport Layer Architecture in the TCP/IP Stack

This diagram illustrates the precise position and functional role of the Transport Layer within the **TCP/IP 4-Layer Model**, as required by KTU Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app1[Application Layer: HTTP HTTPS FTP SSH DNS MQTT COAP]
    app2[Application Layer: SMTP SNMP Telnet]
    trans1[Transport Layer: TCP UDP TLS DTLS]
    trans2[Transport Layer: Port Addressing 3 Way Handshake Flow Control]
    net1[Network Layer: IP ICMP ARP IPv6]
    net2[Network Layer: Routing Fragmentation Addressing]
    link1[Link Layer: Ethernet WiFi PPP]
    link2[Link Layer: MAC Framing Error Detection]

    app1 --> trans1
    app2 --> trans2
    trans1 --> net1
    trans2 --> net2
    net1 --> link1
    net2 --> link2

    style trans1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style trans2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — TCP Three-Way Handshake State Machine

This block-level functional diagram shows the complete **state transitions** between Client and Server during connection establishment.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> LISTEN: Server Opens Socket
    LISTEN --> SYNRECEIVED: SYN Y SEQ ACK X plus 1

    CLOSED --> SYNSENT: Client Sends SYN X
    SYNSENT --> ESTABLISHED: Client Receives SYN ACK Sends ACK Y plus 1

    SYNRECEIVED --> ESTABLISHED: Server Receives Final ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FINWAIT1: Client Sends FIN
    FINWAIT1 --> FINWAIT2: Client Receives ACK
    FINWAIT2 --> TIMEWAIT: Client Receives FIN Sends ACK
    TIMEWAIT --> CLOSED: After 2 MSL Timeout
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSEWAIT: Server Receives FIN
    CLOSEWAIT --> LASTACK: Server Sends FIN
    LASTACK --> CLOSED: Server Receives Final ACK
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Transport Layer Attack Mapping

This functional flow maps **attack vectors** against their **target mechanisms** within the Transport Layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    attacker[Malicious Actor]
    synflood[Attack Vector: SYN Flood]
    udpflood[Attack Vector: UDP Flood]
    portscan[Attack Vector: Port Scan]
    hijack[Attack Vector: Session Hijack]
    synqueue[Target: Half Open Connection Queue]
    udpstack[Target: Stateless UDP Stack]
    ports[Target: Listening Sockets]
    seqno[Target: TCP Sequence Number]

    attacker --> synflood
    attacker --> udpflood
    attacker --> portscan
    attacker --> hijack

    synflood --> synqueue
    udpflood --> udpstack
    portscan --> ports
    hijack --> seqno

    synqueue --> impact1[Outcome: Server Resource Exhaustion DoS]
    udpstack --> impact2[Outcome: Bandwidth Saturation DDoS]
    ports --> impact3[Outcome: Service Enumeration Recon]
    seqno --> impact4[Outcome: Unauthorized Session Injection MITM]

    style synflood fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style udpflood fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style portscan fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style hijack fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style impact1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style impact2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style impact3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style impact4 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — IoT Transport Protocol Decision Flow

A practical diagram showing how an IoT system designer selects between TCP-based and UDP-based transport protocols.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[IoT Application Requirement]
    q1{Need Guaranteed Delivery?}
    q2{Power or Bandwidth Constrained?}
    q3{Need Pub Sub Messaging?}

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|Yes| tcp[Use TCP]
    q1 -->|No| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| q3
    q2 -->|No| udp[Use UDP]
    q3 -->|Yes| mqtt[Use MQTT over TCP Port 1883]
    q3 -->|No| coap[Use CoAP over UDP Port 5683]

    tcp --> dtls[Add TLS for Security]
    udp --> dtls2[Add DTLS for Security]
    mqtt --> mqtts[Add MQTT over TLS Port 8883]
    coap --> coaps[Add CoAP over DTLS Port 5684]

    style tcp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style udp fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style mqtt fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style coap fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Explain the function of the Transport Layer in the TCP/IP model. Differentiate between TCP and UDP in terms of reliability, header size, and connection establishment.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Transport Layer** is the fourth layer of the TCP/IP model and is responsible for **process-to-process delivery** of data between applications running on different hosts. It achieves this through **port-based multiplexing** and offers two main protocols: **TCP** and **UDP**.

| Feature | TCP | UDP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Connection Type** | Connection-oriented (uses 3-way handshake) | Connectionless (no handshake) |
| **Reliability** | Reliable — guarantees delivery via ACKs and retransmissions | Unreliable — best-effort, no ACK |
| **Header Size** | **20 bytes** minimum (up to 60 bytes) | **8 bytes** fixed |
| **Speed** | Slower due to overhead | Faster due to low overhead |
| **Use Case** | Web (HTTP), Email (SMTP), File transfer (FTP) | DNS, VoIP, Video streaming, IoT (CoAP) |
| **Flow Control** | Yes (sliding window) | No |
| **Ordering** | Guaranteed in-order delivery | No ordering guarantee |

> **Note:** In ethical hacking, TCP is the target of **SYN floods and session hijacking**, while UDP is the target of **UDP floods and amplification attacks**.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — December 2023]
**What is a socket? Explain port numbers and their classification with examples of well-known ports used in IoT.**

**Model Answer:**

A **socket** is an endpoint of a two-way communication link between two programs running on a network. It is uniquely identified by the combination of an **IP address** and a **port number**, written as `(IP, Port)`.

**Port Number Classification:**

- **Well-Known Ports (0–1023):** Reserved for standard services.
  - `20/21` — FTP
  - `22` — SSH
  - `23` — Telnet
  - `25` — SMTP
  - `53` — DNS
  - `80` — HTTP
  - `443` — HTTPS

- **Registered Ports (1024–49151):** Assigned by IANA to specific applications.
  - `1883` — MQTT (IoT messaging over TCP)
  - `5683` — CoAP (IoT constrained protocol over UDP)
  - `8883` — MQTT over TLS

- **Dynamic/Private Ports (49152–65535):** Used as ephemeral client-side ports during temporary connections.

> **IoT Relevance:** MQTT on port `1883` and CoAP on port `5683` are the **two most important IoT transport-layer ports** for KTU exam questions.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **Part (a) — 7 marks** and **Part (b) — 7 marks**. Students answer **one full question** (either Q. A or Q. B).

### 📘 QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the TCP Three-Way Handshake with a neat diagram. Why is it called a "three-way" handshake and not a two-way handshake?**

**Model Solution:**

The TCP Three-Way Handshake is the procedure used to establish a TCP connection between a client and server. It is called "three-way" because **three segments** are exchanged between the two endpoints, ensuring **synchronization of sequence numbers in BOTH directions** simultaneously.

**Step 1: SYN (Client → Server)**
- The client sends a segment with the **SYN flag = 1** and an initial sequence number $x$.
- Client state: `CLOSED → SYN-SENT`.
- Meaning: *"I want to connect. My first byte will be numbered $x$."*

**Step 2: SYN + ACK (Server → Client)**
- The server responds with **both SYN and ACK flags set**.
- Sequence number $y$ (server's own ISN), acknowledgment number $= x + 1$.
- Server state: `LISTEN → SYN-RECEIVED`.
- Meaning: *"I acknowledge your $x$. My first byte will be numbered $y$. Please send your next byte as $x+1$."*

**Step 3: ACK (Client → Server)**
- Client sends ACK with sequence $= x + 1$ and acknowledgment $= y + 1$.
- Both sides transition to `ESTABLISHED`.

**Why not two-way?** A two-way handshake would only allow the client to confirm the server's sequence number, but would leave the server **uncertain** whether the client has received its initial SYN. The third ACK **closes this ambiguity**, which is why three segments are mandatory.

**Diagram (already provided in Section 4.2):**
Client → SYN(x) → Server
Server → SYN(y) + ACK(x+1) → Client
Client → ACK(y+1) → Server

> **Examiner's Note:** A common mistake students make is drawing the third ACK with the wrong acknowledgment number. Always remember: **ACK = Received SEQ + 1**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the structure of the TCP header. Explain the function of at least six fields in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

The TCP header is **20 bytes** minimum and consists of the following fields:

| Field | Size (bits) | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Source Port** | 16 | Identifies the sending application's port |
| **Destination Port** | 16 | Identifies the receiving application's port |
| **Sequence Number** | 32 | Byte-stream position of the first byte in this segment |
| **Acknowledgment Number** | 32 | Next expected byte from the other side (only if ACK=1) |
| **Data Offset** | 4 | Length of TCP header in 32-bit words (min 5, max 15) |
| **Reserved** | 6 | Reserved for future use; must be zero |
| **Flags (URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN)** | 6 | Control bits governing connection state |
| **Window Size** | 16 | Receiver's advertised buffer space (for flow control) |
| **Checksum** | 16 | 1's-complement checksum over header + data + pseudo-header |
| **Urgent Pointer** | 16 | Offset from sequence number indicating urgent data end |
| **Options + Padding** | 0–320 | Optional features like MSS, Window Scaling, Timestamps |

**Six Field Explanations:**

1. **Sequence Number:** Allows the receiver to **reassemble segments in correct order** and detect duplicate segments.
2. **Acknowledgment Number:** Enables **cumulative acknowledgment** — the sender learns which bytes have been successfully received.
3. **Flags:** The **SYN, FIN, RST** flags manage connection lifecycle; **ACK** validates the acknowledgment field; **PSH** requests immediate push to application; **URG** marks urgent data.
4. **Window Size:** Critical for **flow control** — without it, a slow receiver would be overwhelmed.
5. **Checksum:** Mandatory error detection — calculated over a **96-bit pseudo-header**, the TCP header, and payload.
6. **Urgent Pointer:** Used for out-of-band signaling (e.g., terminating a Telnet session via Ctrl+C).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Confusing UDP and TCP header sizes** — UDP is **8 bytes fixed**, TCP is **20 bytes minimum**.
> - **Forgetting the pseudo-header** in checksum calculation (worth 1 mark if missed).
> - **Wrong sequence number derivation** — the ACK always equals `received SEQ + 1`, NOT the received SEQ itself.
> - **Missing the role of Flags field** — many students omit that ACK flag must be set for the acknowledgment number to be valid.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model Paper]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the common security attacks targeting the Transport Layer. Explain SYN Flood and UDP Flood attacks in detail with mitigation strategies.**

**Model Solution:**

The Transport Layer is a prime target because it is **stateful** (in TCP) and **always reachable** from the public Internet. The major attack categories are:

**1. TCP SYN Flood Attack:**

A **denial-of-service attack** that exploits the half-open state of the TCP three-way handshake.

- **Mechanism:** The attacker sends a flood of **SYN packets** with **spoofed source IP addresses** to the victim's port. The server responds with SYN+ACK and allocates a **TPCB (Transmission Control Block)** in its connection table for each request.
- **Result:** The attacker never sends the final ACK. The server's connection table fills up with **half-open connections**, exhausting memory and CPU.
- **Code Impact:**
  - If the connection queue size is $Q_{max}$ and attack rate is $R$ SYN/sec, the queue saturates when $R \times T_{timeout} \ge Q_{max}$.

- **Mitigation Strategies:**
  - **SYN Cookies:** The server does NOT allocate state upon SYN; instead it encodes the state into the sequence number of the SYN+ACK response, validating only after the final ACK arrives.
  - **Increase backlog queue size.**
  - **Firewall filtering** of spoofed source addresses (egress filtering, BCP 38).
  - **Rate-limiting** SYN packets per source IP.

**2. UDP Flood Attack:**

A **volumetric DDoS attack** that sends massive numbers of UDP packets to random ports on the victim.

- **Mechanism:** The attacker sends high-bandwidth UDP traffic. The victim must:
  1. Check for listening applications on each destination port.
  2. Respond with **ICMP Destination Unreachable** packets for closed ports.
- **Result:** Bandwidth saturation, CPU exhaustion, and network congestion.

- **Mitigation Strategies:**
  - **Rate-limiting UDP traffic** at the firewall.
  - **Filtering unused UDP ports.**
  - **Disabling ICMP unreachable responses** to reduce amplification.
  - **Anycast network scrubbing** via upstream DDoS protection services (e.g., Cloudflare, AWS Shield).

**Additional Attacks (Briefly):**

- **Port Scanning:** Reconnaissance attack using half-open SYN scans.
- **Session Hijacking:** Injection of spoofed packets using predicted TCP sequence numbers.
- **Slowloris:** Holding TCP connections open with slow, partial HTTP headers.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the role of the Transport Layer in IoT security. Compare MQTT and CoAP in terms of transport protocol, port, security mechanism, and typical use cases.**

**Model Solution:**

The Transport Layer is **critical to IoT security** because most IoT communication protocols operate directly at this layer. The choice between TCP and UDP determines the entire security posture of an IoT deployment.

**Comparison: MQTT vs CoAP**

| Feature | **MQTT** | **CoAP** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transport Protocol** | TCP | UDP |
| **Default Port** | `1883` (plaintext), `8883` (TLS) | `5683` (plaintext), `5684` (DTLS) |
| **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe (broker-mediated) | Request/Response (client-server) |
| **Security Mechanism** | TLS 1.2/1.3 over TCP | DTLS 1.2 over UDP |
| **Header Overhead** | 2-byte fixed header | 4-byte base header |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (TCP keep-alive overhead) | Lower (ideal for battery devices) |
| **Typical Use Case** | Smart home hubs, industrial telemetry | Constrained sensor networks (6LoWPAN, LoRa) |
| **Reliability** | Built-in (3 QoS levels: 0, 1, 2) | Optional (Confirmable vs Non-confirmable messages) |

**IoT Security Concerns at the Transport Layer:**

1. **Default credentials on MQTT brokers** — a common attack vector where attackers subscribe to `#` (wildcard topic) and exfiltrate all sensor data.
2. **Unencrypted CoAP** — when deployed without DTLS, sensor data is transmitted in plaintext over UDP, enabling sniffing attacks.
3. **Lack of device authentication** — Transport-layer protocols must verify device identity via certificates or pre-shared keys (PSKs).
4. **Resource exhaustion** — IoT devices have constrained buffers, making them vulnerable to transport-layer floods even at low traffic rates.

**Best Practice:** For any production IoT deployment, **TLS 1.3 over TCP or DTLS 1.2 over UDP must be enforced**, default credentials must be rotated, and broker/servers must require client certificate authentication.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PBCCT604 Transport Layer Questions:**
>
> 1. **Misstating TCP header size:** TCP minimum is **20 bytes**, not 24. UDP is exactly **8 bytes**, not 20.
> 2. **Confusing "Three-Way Handshake" with "Three ACK packets":** It is **3 segments**, not 3 ACKs.
> 3. **Wrong acknowledgment formula:** $ACK = SEQ + 1$ always. Never write $ACK = SEQ$.
> 4. **Omitting pseudo-header in checksum:** TCP/UDP checksum computation must include the 12-byte IP pseudo-header.
> 5. **Confusing well-known ports:** MQTT is **1883** (TCP), CoAP is **5683** (UDP). Mixing these up loses marks.
> 6. **Treating UDP as "useless":** UDP is **deliberately chosen** for low-latency IoT/streaming — explain its trade-offs, not just its weaknesses.
> 7. **Ignoring TIME_WAIT state:** Many students skip the **2 × MSL wait** in the four-way termination. This state is mandatory and examinable.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for The Transport Layer (Module 3):**

- ✅ The Transport Layer provides **process-to-process delivery** via **port multiplexing**.
- ✅ The complete socket address is `(IP, Port)`, a 4-tuple uniquely identifies a TCP session.
- ✅ Port range is **0 to 65535**; Well-known = `0–1023`, Registered = `1024–49151`, Dynamic = `49152–65535`.
- ✅ **TCP = Connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream, 20-byte header minimum**.
- ✅ **UDP = Connectionless, unreliable, datagram, 8-byte header**.
- ✅ Three-Way Handshake: **SYN(x) → SYN-ACK(y, x+1) → ACK(y+1)** — sequence numbers MUST be acknowledged with +1.
- ✅ Four-Way Termination uses **FIN/ACK pairs in both directions**, ending in **TIME-WAIT for 2 × MSL**.
- ✅ Sliding Window throughput: $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{W \times L \times 8}{RTT}$ bps.
- ✅ Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) defines the minimum window size: $W_{min} = \lceil \dfrac{BW \times RTT}{L \times 8} \rceil$.
- ✅ **SYN Flood** = half-open connection exhaustion → mitigate with **SYN Cookies, rate limiting, BCP 38**.
- ✅ **UDP Flood** = volumetric DDoS → mitigate with **firewall filtering, ICMP suppression**.
- ✅ IoT protocols: **MQTT (TCP, 1883)** and **CoAP (UDP, 5683)**; secure variants use **TLS (8883)** and **DTLS (5684)**.
- ✅ **TCP Checksum** uses a 12-byte pseudo-header (Source IP, Dest IP, Zero, Protocol, TCP Length).
- ✅ Maximum TCP Window = $2^{16}-1 = 65535$ bytes; **Window Scaling option** extends this by $2^{S}$ where $S \in [0, 14]$.
- ✅ Standard Ethernet MSS = MTU - IP Header - TCP Header = 1500 - 20 - 20 = **1460 bytes**.
- ✅ Modern congestion control algorithms: **TCP Reno, TCP CUBIC, TCP BBR**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Application Transport Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IoT Application Transport Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**IoT Application Transport Methods** refer to the standardized suite of communication protocols and mechanisms operating at the **Application Layer (OSI Layer 7)** and the **Transport Layer (OSI Layer 4)** that govern how data packets, control signals, and management messages are exchanged between heterogeneous IoT devices, edge gateways, cloud brokers, and application servers. These methods define the **message semantics**, **packet structure**, **reliability guarantees**, **security primitives**, and **Quality of Service (QoS)** tiers that enable scalable, low-power, and constrained-environment IoT communication over IP (Internet Protocol) networks.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, IoT Application Transport Methods encapsulate two protocol families:

1. **Application Layer Protocols** — Define *what* the data means and *how* devices interact (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP, DDS, WebSockets).
2. **Transport Layer Protocols** — Define *how* data is delivered end-to-end (TCP, UDP, DTLS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCCT604 – Module 3):** Students must be able to differentiate between **request-response**, **publish-subscribe**, and **queue-based** messaging paradigms, and explain why specific transport choices (TCP vs UDP) are coupled with specific application protocols in IoT.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Postal System"

Imagine a smart city where thousands of sensors, traffic lights, and home appliances need to "talk" to a central control room.

- **TCP** is like a **registered post with tracking** — every letter is signed for, tracked, and guaranteed to arrive. Slow but reliable.
- **UDP** is like a **drop-in-the-mailbox flyer** — fast, no tracking, if it gets lost, you send another one.
- **MQTT** is a **newspaper subscription system** — devices *subscribe* to topics (e.g., "Temperature/Room5") and a *broker* pushes updates only when something changes. Bandwidth-efficient.
- **CoAP** is a **lightweight web request** — like HTTP, but for tiny devices that only have the memory of a calculator.
- **AMQP** is a **banking queue system** — messages line up in queues, ensuring strict ordering and guaranteed delivery, like financial transactions.
- **DDS** is a **real-time peer-to-peer radio network** — devices talk directly to each other with microsecond latency, no central server.
- **WebSockets** is a **persistent phone call** — once connected, both sides can talk freely in real time.
- **DTLS** is the **sealed envelope** — it encrypts UDP traffic so prying eyes can't read your drop-in-the-mailbox flyers.

## 1.3 Core Categorical Breakdown

| Paradigm | Representative Protocols | Typical Use Case in IoT |
|---|---|---|
| **Publish–Subscribe (Pub/Sub)** | **MQTT**, AMQP, DDS | Telemetry, smart home, industrial sensors |
| **Request–Response** | **CoAP**, HTTP/HTTPS | Device configuration, firmware updates |
| **Streaming / Real-time** | **WebSockets**, DDS | Dashboards, control loops, robotics |
| **Queue-based** | **AMQP** | Reliable messaging, enterprise IoT |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **MQTT** runs on **TCP port 1883** (unsecured) and **8883** (TLS-secured).
> - **CoAP** runs on **UDP port 5683** (unsecured) and **DTLS-secured equivalent port 5684**.
> - **AMQP** runs on **TCP port 5672** (unsecured) and **5671** (TLS-secured).
> - **DDS** uses **UDP/TCP** on ports **7400+** with **RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe)** protocol.
> - **HTTP** traditionally on **TCP port 80**, **HTTPS** on **443**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Layer Stack Decomposition

IoT transport is a **two-layer concern**. Understanding the distinction is critical for KTU exams.

### Layer A — Transport Layer (OSI Layer 4)

This layer handles **end-to-end data delivery**, flow control, error recovery, and port multiplexing.

#### 2.1.1 TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)
- **Connection-oriented**: Establishes a session via the 3-way handshake (**SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK**).
- **Reliable**: Uses sequence numbers, acknowledgments (ACK), and retransmission timers.
- **Ordered**: Receives data in the same order it was sent.
- **Heavyweight**: Header size is **20 bytes** (minimum), stateful, higher power consumption.
- **Flow control**: Uses sliding window protocol.
- **Congestion control**: Implements **cubic**, **Reno**, or **BBR** algorithms.

#### 2.1.2 UDP (User Datagram Protocol)
- **Connectionless**: Fire-and-forget, no handshake.
- **Unreliable**: No built-in ACK, no retransmission.
- **Unordered**: Datagrams may arrive out of sequence.
- **Lightweight**: Header size is **8 bytes** only.
- **Low latency**: Ideal for real-time and lossy-tolerable IoT data.

#### 2.1.3 DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security)
- **TLS equivalent for UDP** — provides encryption, authentication, integrity for datagrams.
- Used by **CoAP** (CoAPs), **DDS-Security**, and **Secure MQTT over UDP variants**.
- Uses a modified handshake (cookie-based to prevent DoS).

### Layer B — Application Layer (OSI Layer 7)

#### 2.1.4 MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)
**Standardized** by **OASIS** and **ISO/IEC 20922**.
- Operates over **TCP** (and optionally WebSockets).
- Follows the **publish/subscribe** pattern with a central **broker**.
- Three **Quality of Service (QoS)** levels:
  - **QoS 0** — At most once (fire-and-forget).
  - **QoS 1** — At least once (PUBACK confirms).
  - **QoS 2** — Exactly once (4-way handshake: PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP).
- **Last Will and Testament (LWT)** — broker publishes a predefined message if a client disconnects ungracefully.
- **Retain flag** — broker stores the last message of a topic for new subscribers.
- **Keep-alive ping** — client sends PINGREQ to maintain session.
- **Topic hierarchy** — uses `/` delimiter, e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`.

#### 2.1.5 CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)
**Standardized** by **IETF RFC 7252**.
- Operates over **UDP** (and optionally DTLS for security).
- Follows the **request/response** model similar to HTTP (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE).
- Header is only **4 bytes** (compact binary header).
- Supports **CON (Confirmable)**, **NON (Non-confirmable)**, **ACK**, **RST** message types — analogous to TCP reliability over UDP.
- Supports **observe pattern** — server pushes resource state changes to subscribed client.
- Supports **block-wise transfer** for large payloads over constrained links.

#### 2.1.6 HTTP / HTTPS
- The classic **web protocol** adapted for IoT (e.g., RESTful APIs).
- Request/response model, text-based headers (heavy for tiny devices).
- HTTPS adds **TLS 1.2/1.3** for encryption.
- Used in IoT primarily by **cloud-facing APIs** and **less constrained gateways**.

#### 2.1.7 AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)
**Standardized** by **OASIS** (v1.0).
- Operates over **TCP**.
- Provides **queue-based** messaging with **exchanges**, **bindings**, and **queues**.
- Three delivery guarantees: **at-most-once**, **at-least-once**, **exactly-once**.
- Heavier than MQTT, more suited to **enterprise IoT backends**.

#### 2.1.8 DDS (Data Distribution Service)
**Standardized** by **OMG (Object Management Group)**.
- Uses **RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe)** protocol — typically over **UDP** (multicast) for ultra-low latency.
- **Broker-less** architecture — peer-to-peer.
- Supports **20+ QoS policies** (reliability, deadline, latency budget, liveliness, ownership).
- Used in **autonomous vehicles**, **aerospace**, **defense**, **medical robotics**.

#### 2.1.9 WebSockets
- **Full-duplex** communication channel over a single **TCP** connection.
- Starts as an HTTP upgrade handshake.
- Enables **real-time bidirectional** data — used for IoT dashboards and control UIs.

## 2.2 Why Specific Pairings Exist — The Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **The TCP/UDP vs Application Protocol Coupling Rule of Thumb:**
> - Protocols needing **reliable, ordered delivery** (MQTT, AMQP, HTTPS) → use **TCP**.
> - Protocols needing **low-latency, lightweight delivery** (CoAP, DDS) → use **UDP**.
> - When UDP is used but security is needed → wrap with **DTLS**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table

| Protocol | Layer | Transport | Header Size | Messaging Model | Security | Default Port | Key Strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MQTT** | Application | TCP | 2 bytes (fixed) | Pub/Sub | TLS (optional) | **1883 / 8883** | Lightweight pub/sub |
| **CoAP** | Application | UDP | 4 bytes | Request/Response | DTLS (optional) | **5683 / 5684** | Tiny constrained devices |
| **HTTP** | Application | TCP | Variable (text) | Request/Response | TLS (HTTPS) | **80 / 443** | Universal web interop |
| **AMQP** | Application | TCP | 8 bytes (frame) | Queue-based | TLS (optional) | **5672 / 5671** | Enterprise reliability |
| **DDS** | Application | UDP/TCP | ~12 bytes (RTPS) | Broker-less Pub/Sub | DTLS (optional) | **7400+** | Real-time peer-to-peer |
| **WebSockets** | Application | TCP | 2–14 bytes (frame) | Full-duplex | TLS (wss://) | **80 / 443** | Real-time bidirectional |
| **TCP** | Transport | IP | 20 bytes | Stream | TLS (optional) | — | Reliable ordered |
| **UDP** | Transport | IP | 8 bytes | Datagram | DTLS (optional) | — | Lightweight, fast |
| **DTLS** | Transport (security) | UDP | 13+ bytes | Datagram | Built-in | — | Secures UDP |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture**: MQTT over TCP via cellular (NB-IoT) for soil sensors in remote fields — minimal bandwidth.
- **Smart Metering**: CoAP over UDP via 6LoWPAN for in-home energy meters — minimal power, constrained memory.
- **Connected Vehicles (V2X)**: DDS over UDP multicast for sub-10ms inter-vehicle coordination.
- **Industrial SCADA**: AMQP for guaranteed delivery of critical process commands.
- **Smart Home Dashboards**: WebSockets for live control panels over home Wi-Fi.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since IoT Application Transport Methods is fundamentally a **protocol-engineering topic** rather than a closed-form mathematical derivation topic, this section substitutes algebraic derivations with **exhaustive protocol message flow analysis**, **header byte-by-byte breakdowns**, **QoS handshake derivations**, and **comparative analytical matrices** that KTU examiners frequently test.

## 3.1 MQTT QoS Handshake — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis

### QoS 0 — At Most Once (No Acknowledgment)

**Step 1:** The publisher (client) constructs a `PUBLISH` packet with the following structure:

$$ \text{PUBLISH}_{QoS0} = \{ \text{Fixed Header (1B)}, \text{Variable Header}, \text{Payload} \} $$

**Step 2:** The fixed header byte encodes: packet type `0011` (PUBLISH = 3) in the upper 4 bits, and flags (DUP, QoS, RETAIN) in the lower 4 bits. For QoS 0, the QoS field is `00`.

$$ \text{Fixed Header Byte}_{QoS0} = 0b0011\,0000 = 0x30 $$

**Step 3:** The packet is transmitted to the broker. **No acknowledgment** is expected or sent.

**Step 4:** If the packet is lost in transit, it is **never retransmitted**. The message is delivered *at most once*.

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the fastest and lightest mode — equivalent to UDP semantics over a TCP transport. Suitable for non-critical telemetry like ambient temperature readings.

### QoS 1 — At Least Once (PUBACK Acknowledgment)

**Step 1:** The publisher sends a `PUBLISH` packet with `DUP=0`, `QoS=01`, `Packet Identifier (PID)=0x0001` (a unique 2-byte ID for tracking).

**Step 2:** The broker receives the packet, delivers it to subscribers, and sends a `PUBACK` packet back to the publisher containing the same PID.

$$ \text{PUBACK} = \{ \text{Type}=0x40, \text{Remaining Length}=0x02, \text{PID}=0x0001 \} $$

**Step 3:** If the publisher does not receive a `PUBACK` within a configurable timeout, it **retransmits** the `PUBLISH` packet with `DUP=1` (duplicate flag set) and the same PID.

**Step 4:** This process repeats until the `PUBACK` is received. The message is delivered *at least once* — duplicates are possible.

> **Conversion Logic:** QoS 1 trades off duplicate delivery for guaranteed receipt. The publisher cannot assume ordering of acknowledgments and must deduplicate by PID on its side.

### QoS 2 — Exactly Once (4-Way Handshake)

This is the most complex level and is **frequently asked in KTU exams**.

**Step 1: PUBLISH** — The publisher sends a `PUBLISH` packet with `QoS=10` and `PID=0x000A`.

**Step 2: PUBREC (Publish Received)** — The broker acknowledges receipt of the PUBLISH by sending a PUBREC packet with the same PID.

$$ \text{PUBREC} = \{ \text{Type}=0x50, \text{Remaining Length}=0x02, \text{PID}=0x000A \} $$

**Step 3: PUBREL (Publish Release)** — The publisher, upon receiving the PUBREC, sends a PUBREL packet to release the message for delivery.

$$ \text{PUBREL} = \{ \text{Type}=0x60, \text{Remaining Length}=0x02, \text{PID}=0x000A \} $$

**Step 4: PUBCOMP (Publish Complete)** — The broker confirms the release by sending PUBCOMP, and the message is finally considered delivered exactly once.

$$ \text{PUBCOMP} = \{ \text{Type}=0x70, \text{Remaining Length}=0x02, \text{PID}=0x000A \} $$

> **Conversion Logic:** The 4-way handshake guarantees that the message is acknowledged at *both* the publish and release phases, preventing duplicates even across retransmissions. This is the only MQTT level that ensures exactly-once semantics.

### Summary Table of MQTT QoS Levels

| QoS Level | Handshake | Retransmission | Duplicates | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | None | No | Possible (loss) | Non-critical telemetry |
| **QoS 1** | PUBLISH → PUBACK | Yes | Possible | Reliable telemetry |
| **QoS 2** | PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP | Yes | **None** | Billing, command control |

## 3.2 CoAP Message Format — Byte-by-Byte Exhaustive Breakdown

The CoAP message is encoded in a compact binary format (RFC 7252). The total minimum size is just **4 bytes**.

**Step 1: Version and Type (1 byte)**

$$ \text{Byte 0} = \underbrace{VV}_{2\,\text{bits (Ver=01)}} \mid \underbrace{T}_{2\,\text{bits (Type)}} \mid \underbrace{TKL}_{4\,\text{bits (Token Length, 0--8)}} $$

**Conversion Logic:**
- `V=01` means CoAP version 1.
- `TT` can be: `00` (CON = Confirmable), `01` (NON = Non-confirmable), `10` (ACK), `11` (RST = Reset).

**Step 2: Code (1 byte)** — Class (upper 3 bits) and Detail (lower 5 bits).

$$ \text{Byte 1} = \underbrace{CCC}_{3\,\text{bits}} \mid \underbrace{DDDDD}_{5\,\text{bits}} $$

**Conversion Logic:** A `GET` request is encoded as class `0`, detail `01` → `0x01`. A `POST` is class `0`, detail `02` → `0x02`. Response codes mirror HTTP: `2.05` (Content) → class `2`, detail `05` → `0xA5`.

**Step 3: Message ID (2 bytes)** — Matches CON with ACK, used for duplicate detection and reliability over UDP.

**Step 4: Token (0–8 bytes)** — Optional client-generated identifier for matching responses to requests (when multiple requests are in flight).

**Step 5: Options (variable)** — URI path, content format, max-age, etc. Each option is `Delta-Length-Value` encoded.

**Step 6: Payload (variable)** — Marked by a `0xFF` marker byte, followed by the actual data.

> **Conversion Logic:** This 4-byte baseline is what makes CoAP ideal for RFC 7228 constrained-node networks (Class 0 devices with < 10 KB RAM).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Selection Criteria

This matrix is **exam-ready** and shows the engineering trade-offs that KTU examiners expect you to justify.

| Selection Criterion | MQTT | CoAP | HTTP | AMQP | DDS | WebSockets |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Constrained Device Friendly** | High | **Very High** | Low | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | High | **Very High** | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Real-Time Capability** | Medium | Medium | Low | Low | **Very High** | **High** |
| **Reliability Guarantees** | 3 QoS levels | CON/ACK | TCP-based | 3 levels | 20+ QoS | TCP-based |
| **Security Maturity** | TLS (mature) | DTLS (mature) | TLS (mature) | TLS (mature) | DTLS (evolving) | TLS (mature) |
| **Scalability (devices)** | **Very High** (millions via broker) | High | Low (per-connection) | High | **Very High** (P2P) | Low |
| **Topology** | Broker-centric | Server-client | Server-client | Broker-centric | Broker-less P2P | Server-client |
| **Power Consumption** | Medium | **Low** | High | High | Medium | High |
| **Best For** | Telemetry, smart home | Constrained sensors, smart meters | Cloud APIs, dashboards | Enterprise IoT, fintech | V2X, robotics, defense | Live dashboards, control UIs |

## 3.4 DTLS Handshake — Exhaustive Step Analysis

DTLS is essentially **TLS over UDP**, with modifications to handle datagram unreliability.

**Step 1: ClientHello** — The client sends a `ClientHello` to the server (over UDP).
**Step 2: HelloVerifyRequest** — The server responds with a `HelloVerifyRequest` containing a **cookie** — this is a **DoS countermeasure** to ensure the client is reachable.
**Step 3: ClientHello (with cookie)** — The client resends the `ClientHello` with the cookie attached.
**Step 4: ServerHello, Certificate, ServerKeyExchange, ServerHelloDone** — Server responds with cryptographic parameters.
**Step 5: ClientKeyExchange, ChangeCipherSpec, Finished** — Client sends its key share and signals encryption is active.
**Step 6: ChangeCipherSpec, Finished** — Server confirms, and the secure channel is established.

> **Conversion Logic:** The cookie exchange ensures the attacker cannot spoof IP addresses to amplify traffic — critical for IoT gateways exposed to the internet.

## 3.5 Transport Selection Decision Tree

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision Flow for KTU Exam Answers:**
> 1. **Need real-time + P2P?** → DDS over UDP.
> 2. **Need pub/sub + many constrained devices?** → MQTT over TCP.
> 3. **Need request/response + very constrained device?** → CoAP over UDP + DTLS.
> 4. **Need enterprise-grade queue reliability?** → AMQP over TCP.
> 5. **Need real-time dashboard updates?** → WebSockets over TCP.
> 6. **Need broad web compatibility?** → HTTP/HTTPS over TCP.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 MQTT QoS 2 — Exactly-Once 4-Way Handshake Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Publisher Client
    participant B as MQTT Broker

    P->>B: PUBLISH (QoS=2, PID=000A)
    Note over P,B: Step 1: Publisher sends message
    B->>P: PUBREC (PID=000A)
    Note over P,B: Step 2: Broker acknowledges receipt
    P->>B: PUBREL (PID=000A)
    Note over P,B: Step 3: Publisher releases for delivery
    B->>P: PUBCOMP (PID=000A)
    Note over P,B: Step 4: Broker confirms completion
    Note over P,B: Message delivered EXACTLY ONCE
```

## 4.4 CoAP Request/Response with DTLS

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CoAP Client (Sensor)
    participant S as CoAP Server (Gateway)
    participant D as DTLS Security Layer

    C->>D: ClientHello (with cookie)
    D->>C: HelloVerifyRequest (cookie)
    C->>D: ClientHello (with cookie attached)
    D->>C: ServerHello, Certificate, KeyExchange
    C->>D: ClientKeyExchange, Finished
    D->>C: Finished (Secure channel established)
    C->>S: CON GET /sensors/temp
    S->>C: ACK (2.05 Content) with payload
    Note over C,S: Reliable delivery over UDP
```
