# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the **three Quality of Service (QoS) levels** in MQTT and identify the level that provides **exactly-once** delivery semantics.

**Model Answer:**

MQTT defines three QoS levels for message delivery between publisher, broker, and subscriber:

1. **QoS 0 — At Most Once:** The message is delivered once with no acknowledgment. No retransmission occurs. Fastest but lossy.
2. **QoS 1 — At Least Once:** A `PUBACK` is required. The publisher retransmits if no acknowledgment is received within the timeout. Duplicates are possible.
3. **QoS 2 — Exactly Once:** A 4-way handshake (PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP) guarantees the message is delivered **exactly once** with no duplicates.

The level that provides **exactly-once delivery** is **QoS Level 2**, and it uses a 4-way handshake to ensure no duplicate delivery even under network failures.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between **TCP** and **UDP** as transport layer protocols for IoT. State **two use cases** where each is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless (no handshake) |
| Reliability | Reliable (ACK + retransmit) | Unreliable (no ACK) |
| Header Size | **20 bytes** (min) | **8 bytes** |
| Ordering | Ordered delivery | No ordering guarantee |
| Congestion Control | Yes (Cubic, Reno, BBR) | None |
| Latency | Higher (handshake overhead) | Lower (immediate send) |
| Power Consumption | Higher | Lower |

**TCP preferred for:** MQTT brokers, HTTPS REST APIs, firmware OTA updates, AMQP queues — where guaranteed delivery matters.
**UDP preferred for:** CoAP on constrained sensors, DDS for real-time V2X, audio/video streaming — where low latency and bandwidth efficiency matter.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **MQTT protocol architecture** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the **publish-subscribe model**, the role of the **broker**, and the **topic hierarchy**. Discuss any **two advanced features** of MQTT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a sequence diagram, describe the **MQTT QoS Level 2** handshake. Explain why this level is used for **critical IoT applications** like billing or industrial command-and-control. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**MQTT Architecture:**

MQTT follows a **hub-and-spoke publish/subscribe** topology. Three components exist:

1. **Publisher** — IoT devices or applications that publish messages tagged with a topic.
2. **Broker** — Central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX) that receives all messages, filters by topic, and distributes to subscribers.
3. **Subscriber** — Applications or devices that subscribe to one or more topics and receive matching messages.

**Topic Hierarchy:**

Topics are UTF-8 strings separated by `/`, e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`. Wildcards are supported:
- `+` — single-level wildcard, e.g., `home/+/temperature`.
- `#` — multi-level wildcard, e.g., `home/#`.

**Two Advanced Features:**

1. **Last Will and Testament (LWT):** When a client connects, it can register an LWT message with a topic. If the broker detects an ungraceful disconnect (no keep-alive ping within `1.5 × keepalive` interval), it publishes the LWT on behalf of the dead client — useful for fault detection.

2. **Retained Messages:** A publisher can set the `RETAIN` flag to `1`. The broker then stores the last message of that topic and delivers it immediately to any new subscriber — useful for state queries (e.g., "what is the current temperature?").

#### Model Solution for (b)

**QoS Level 2 Handshake (refer Section 3.1.3 of these notes for full byte-level detail):**

**Step 1 — PUBLISH:** Publisher sends `PUBLISH` with `QoS=10`, `PID=0x000A`.
**Step 2 — PUBREC:** Broker acknowledges receipt with `PUBREC` carrying the same PID.
**Step 3 — PUBREL:** Publisher releases the message for downstream delivery with `PUBREL`.
**Step 4 — PUBCOMP:** Broker confirms completion with `PUBCOMP`. The message is now considered **delivered exactly once**.

**Why Critical for IoT Billing / Industrial Control:**

In billing, duplicate meter readings would inflate invoices. In industrial SCADA, duplicate "open valve" commands could cause equipment damage or safety incidents. QoS 2's 4-way handshake guarantees that the receiver processes the message **exactly once** by requiring acknowledgment at both the *receipt* (PUBREC) and *release* (PUBREL) phases, eliminating duplicate processing across retransmission cycles.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the **CoAP protocol** in detail. Explain its **message format**, **message types** (CON, NON, ACK, RST), and the **observe pattern**. Compare CoAP with HTTP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** and **AMQP** as IoT application protocols. Highlight their **architectural differences**, **transport choices**, and **ideal IoT use cases** for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**CoAP Protocol:**

CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol, RFC 7252) is a specialized **web transfer protocol** designed for **constrained nodes** and constrained networks (RFC 7228). It mimics HTTP semantics but uses a **4-byte binary header** and runs over **UDP** to minimize overhead.

**Message Format:** A CoAP message consists of:
- **Ver (2 bits)** — version, always `01`.
- **T (2 bits)** — type: `00` CON, `01` NON, `10` ACK, `11` RST.
- **TKL (4 bits)** — token length (0–8 bytes).
- **Code (8 bits)** — request method or response code (e.g., `0.01` = GET, `2.05` = Content).
- **Message ID (16 bits)** — used to match CON with ACK.
- **Token (0–8 bytes)** — matches request to response.
- **Options (variable)** — URI path, content format, etc.
- **Payload marker `0xFF` + Payload** — actual data.

**Message Types:**
- **CON (Confirmable):** Receiver must send ACK. Provides reliability over UDP.
- **NON (Non-confirmable):** No ACK expected — fire-and-forget, like UDP.
- **ACK:** Acknowledges a CON message.
- **RST (Reset):** Indicates the recipient cannot process the message.

**Observe Pattern (RFC 7641):** A client can register an interest in a resource with the `Observe` option. The server then *pushes* updates whenever the resource state changes — converting CoAP from a pure pull model to a push model, analogous to MQTT pub/sub but lighter.

**CoAP vs HTTP Comparison:**

| Parameter | CoAP | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | UDP | TCP |
| Header Size | 4 bytes | Variable, typically hundreds of bytes |
| Stack Size | ~10 KB RAM | ~100 KB+ RAM |
| Message Format | Binary | Text |
| Methods | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE | Same |
| Security | DTLS | TLS |
| Caching | Built-in (max-age option) | Via headers |
| Push Support | Observe pattern | SSE / WebSockets |

#### Model Solution for (b)

**DDS (Data Distribution Service):**

DDS is an **OMG-standardized** middleware protocol using the **RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe)** wire protocol. It is **broker-less** — every node is a peer that can publish and subscribe directly, using **UDP multicast** for ultra-low latency. DDS offers **20+ QoS policies**, including deadline, latency budget, reliability, liveliness, ownership strength, and transport priority. It is the de facto standard for **autonomous vehicles (V2X)**, **aerospace flight control**, **defense systems**, **medical robotics**, and **smart grid synchrophasor** networks where **microsecond-level latency** is required.

**AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol):**

AMQP is an **OASIS-standardized** binary protocol that runs over **TCP** and provides a **queue-based** messaging model. Its architecture comprises **producers**, **exchanges** (direct, topic, fanout, headers), **bindings**, and **queues**. Messages are stored in queues until consumers acknowledge processing. AMQP supports **three delivery guarantees**: at-most-once, at-least-once, and exactly-once (via transactions). It is widely used in **enterprise IoT backends**, **banking/fintech messaging**, **logistics tracking**, and **healthcare HL7/FHIR integration** where **strict message ordering and durability** are mandatory.

**Architectural Differences:**

| Parameter | DDS | AMQP |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Broker-less (P2P) | Broker-centric |
| Transport | UDP (multicast) or TCP | TCP |
| Latency | Microseconds | Milliseconds |
| QoS Policies | 20+ fine-grained | 3 coarse levels |
| Discovery | Built-in (RTPS endpoints) | Requires configuration |
| Best For | Real-time, V2X, robotics | Enterprise, fintech, healthcare |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "exactly-once" semantics:** Many students write that QoS 1 or QoS 2 both "guarantee delivery." Only **QoS 2** guarantees *exactly-once* — QoS 1 only guarantees *at-least-once* (duplicates possible). Examiner will deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Mixing up transport and application layers:** CoAP is an *application layer* protocol (it runs *over* UDP). Do not call UDP an application protocol. DTLS is *not* a transport protocol — it is a *security layer* that operates on top of UDP.
> 3. **Forgetting default ports:** Always cite the default port numbers (MQTT 1883, CoAP 5683, AMQP 5672). Examiners frequently award 1 mark specifically for this.
> 4. **Confusing LWT with Retain:** Last Will and Testament is published on **disconnect**. Retain stores the **last message** for new subscribers. These are different features.
> 5. **Skipping the topic hierarchy:** When drawing the MQTT broker diagram, always show at least one **example topic** like `home/room5/temp` — examiners expect this in the diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **MQTT** = Pub/Sub, **TCP**, broker-centric, ports **1883 / 8883**, header ~2 bytes fixed.
- **CoAP** = Request/Response, **UDP**, ports **5683 / 5684**, header **4 bytes**, supports Observe.
- **HTTP/HTTPS** = Request/Response, **TCP**, ports **80 / 443**, text headers, high overhead.
- **AMQP** = Queue-based, **TCP**, ports **5672 / 5671**, exchanges/queues/bindings, enterprise use.
- **DDS** = Broker-less P2P, **UDP multicast**, RTPS protocol, real-time V2X/aerospace/defense.
- **WebSockets** = Full-duplex, **TCP**, port 80/443 (wss:// for TLS), live dashboards.
- **TCP** = 20-byte header, reliable, ordered, connection-oriented, 3-way handshake.
- **UDP** = 8-byte header, unreliable, connectionless, low latency, low power.
- **DTLS** = TLS for UDP, used to secure CoAP, uses **cookie-based** handshake for DoS defense.
- **MQTT QoS 0** = No ACK (at most once).
- **MQTT QoS 1** = PUBACK (at least once, duplicates possible).
- **MQTT QoS 2** = 4-way handshake PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP (exactly once).
- **MQTT LWT** = message published by broker on ungraceful client disconnect.
- **MQTT Retain** = broker stores last message of a topic for new subscribers.
- **CoAP Message Types** = CON (reliable), NON (fire-and-forget), ACK, RST.
- **CoAP Observe** = RFC 7641, server pushes resource state changes to subscribed client.
- **DDS QoS** = 20+ policies including deadline, latency budget, reliability, liveliness.
- **Selection rule:** Pub/Sub many constrained devices → MQTT; Request/Response very constrained → CoAP; Real-time P2P → DDS; Live UI → WebSockets; Enterprise queue → AMQP; Cloud API → HTTPS.
- **Security pairing:** TCP + TLS = secure TCP; UDP + DTLS = secure UDP.
- **KTU mantra:** Always mention the **default port number**, **transport layer choice**, and **one use case** for any protocol you discuss — this consistently earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OCTAVE and FAIR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCCT604 - ETHICAL HACKING & IOT SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** OCTAVE and FAIR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is OCTAVE?

> [!NOTE]
> **OCTAVE (Operationally Critical Threat, Asset, and Vulnerability Evaluation)** is a security evaluation methodology and risk-based strategic assessment framework developed by the **Software Engineering Institute (SEI) at Carnegie Mellon University** under the CERT Coordination Center. It is used to identify, prioritize, and manage information security risks from an *operational* (business) perspective rather than purely a technical one.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** OCTAVE is a self-directed, organization-wide assessment where an *internal team* drives the risk evaluation, distinguishing it from consultant-driven frameworks like NIST or ISO 27005.

#### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Health Check"
Think of OCTAVE as a **comprehensive annual health check-up for an organization**:
- Instead of a doctor checking your blood pressure, a cross-functional team (not just IT!) examines the "organizational body."
- The team looks at **what matters most** (critical assets like patient records = *valuable information assets*).
- They identify **what could hurt those assets** (cyber-attacks, insider threats, natural disasters = *threats*).
- They pinpoint **weak spots that make attacks possible** (unpatched servers, untrained staff = *vulnerabilities*).
- Finally, they **prescribe a treatment plan** (risk mitigation strategy) — not just technical patches, but also policy and procedural changes.

---

### 1.2 What is FAIR?

> [!NOTE]
> **FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk)** is a taxonomy and *quantitative* risk analysis framework developed by **Jack Jones** and later formalized through the **Open Group**. It provides a structured way to measure, model, and quantify information risk in **financial terms** (e.g., monetary loss per year).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** FAIR is the world's first and only standard *quantitative* information risk model, allowing organizations to answer: "How much risk do we have, in dollars, and is that acceptable?"

#### Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Insurance Premium"
FAIR is like how an **insurance company calculates your car premium**:
- They don't just say "high risk" or "low risk" — they assign **a number** (₹8,500 per year).
- They break down risk into pieces: *How often will you crash?* (**Loss Event Frequency**) × *How bad will it be when you do?* (**Loss Magnitude**).
- They consider many *factors*: your age, car model, driving history, location, even weather.
- The result is a **defensible, defendable dollar figure** executives can understand and budget for.

---

### 1.3 Core Philosophical Difference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OCTAVE vs. FAIR — One-Liner Distinction:**
> - **OCTAVE = Qualitative + Strategic** (tells you *what* to protect and *why*).
> - **FAIR = Quantitative + Financial** (tells you *how much* the risk costs in money).

Both are *complementary*: OCTAVE identifies and prioritizes risks; FAIR measures them precisely.

---

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Meaning | KTU Board Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **OCTAVE Allegro** | Streamlined version of OCTAVE suitable for mid-sized organizations | High (frequently asked) |
| **OCTAVE-S** | Small organization variant (≤100 employees) | Medium |
| **FAIR-CAM** | FAIR + Controls Analysis Model (added 2020) | Medium |
| **Primary Risk** | Direct loss from a threat event | High |
| **Secondary Risk** | Indirect losses (reputation, legal) | High |
| **Risk = LEF × LM** | Core FAIR equation (Loss Event Frequency × Loss Magnitude) | **Very High** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** SEI Technical Report CMU/SEI-2007-TR-012 (OCTAVE Allegro) and ISO/IEC 27005-aligned FAIR taxonomy. No fictional references used.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 OCTAVE Framework — Three-Phase Deep Dive

OCTAVE Allegro is structured into **four phases** (note: the original OCTAVE has 3 phases, but Allegro has 4; the KTU module emphasizes Allegro). Each phase has clear deliverables.

#### **Phase 1: Establish Drivers**
- **Goal:** Understand organizational context before diving into assets.
- **Steps:**
  1. Define **risk measurement criteria** (impact areas: reputation, financial, productivity, fines, safety).
  2. Develop an **information asset profile** container (where you will later store each asset's data).
  3. Identify **areas of concern** (broad anxieties from management like "what if our customer DB leaks?").
- **Why it matters:** Without business context, security is just wasted budget.

#### **Phase 2: Profile Assets**
- **Goal:** Identify and rank *critical information assets*.
- **Steps:**
  1. Identify assets (people, information, systems, software, hardware, intangible IP).
  2. Pick the **most critical 5–10 assets** for deep dive (focused prioritization).
  3. Document each asset's:
     - **Owner**
     - **Security requirements** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the *CIA Triad*)
     - **Most important security attribute** (called the "critical attribute").
- **Why it matters:** You cannot protect everything; prioritization is a board-level skill.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad Mapping in OCTAVE:** Each asset's "critical attribute" is one of **C, I, or A** — typically only ONE is treated as critical to keep analysis tractable.

#### **Phase 3: Identify Threats**
- **Goal:** For each critical asset, identify realistic threat scenarios.
- **Steps:**
  1. Identify **threat sources** (deliberate human, accidental human, system, natural, environmental).
  2. Map threats to assets via **threat trees** (visual flow of *how* an attack could unfold).
  3. Score threats against the **risk measurement criteria** (Phase 1 output).
- **Why it matters:** Threats without business impact = noise. OCTAVE forces business-impact thinking.

#### **Phase 4: Identify and Mitigate Risks**
- **Goal:** Convert threat scenarios into actionable mitigation plans.
- **Steps:**
  1. Map **vulnerabilities** to threat scenarios (what weaknesses enable the threat?).
  2. Score risks using a **risk score matrix** (impact × likelihood).
  3. Develop **mitigation strategy**:
     - **Mitigate** (apply control) — most common
     - **Accept** (risk is too low to act on)
     - **Defer** (act later)
     - **Transfer** (insurance, outsourcing)
- **Why it matters:** A risk register without mitigation is a confession of failure.

---

### 2.2 OCTAVE Risk Scoring Mechanism

OCTAVE uses a qualitative 3×3 or 5×5 matrix (KTU most commonly tests the **3×3 variant**):

$$
\text{Risk Score} = \text{Impact Value} \times \text{Likelihood Value}
$$

Where:
- **Impact** ranges from **Low (1)** → **Medium (2)** → **High (3)**.
- **Likelihood** ranges from **Low (1)** → **Medium (2)** → **High (3)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **score range is 1 to 9**. Scores ≥ 6 typically require immediate mitigation.

---

### 2.3 FAIR Framework — The 10 Core Factors

FAIR is hierarchical. The **top-level decomposition** is:

$$
\text{Risk} = \underbrace{\text{Prob(Event Occurs)}}_{\text{Threat Event Frequency (TEF)}} \times \underbrace{\text{Prob(Event Impacts Asset)}}_{\text{Vulnerability (Vuln)}} \times \underbrace{\text{Loss per Event}}_{\text{Loss Magnitude (LM)}}
$$

Or simply:
$$
\text{Risk} = \text{LEF} \times \text{LM}
$$

Where:
- **LEF** (Loss Event Frequency) = how often a loss event happens per year.
- **LM** (Loss Magnitude) = monetary impact per loss event.

#### The 10 FAIR Factors (in detail)

| # | Factor | What it measures | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Asset** | What is at risk? | Item description |
| 2 | **Threat** | Who/what can harm it? | Threat type |
| 3 | **Vulnerability** | Weakness that enables the threat | Probability (0 to 1) |
| 4 | **Threat Event Frequency (TEF)** | How often the threat acts | Events/year |
| 5 | **Loss Event Frequency (LEF)** | How often a loss results | Events/year |
| 6 | **Primary Loss (PL)** | Direct, immediate financial hit | Currency/year |
| 7 | **Secondary Loss (SL)** | Indirect losses (reputation, lawsuits) | Currency/year |
| 8 | **Loss Magnitude (LM)** | Total per-event cost = PL + SL | Currency |
| 9 | **Control Strength (CS)** | How effective a control is | Probability reduction |
| 10 | **Risk** | Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) | **Currency/year** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The famous FAIR equation** is the **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** — the single number executives care about:
> $$\text{Risk (ALE)} = \text{LEF} \times \text{LM}$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Used In | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{Risk} = \text{LEF} \times \text{LM}$ | Core FAIR definition of annualized loss | FAIR calculation | **Very High** |
| $\text{LEF} = \text{TEF} \times \text{Vuln}$ | Loss event frequency = threat frequency × vulnerability | FAIR derivation | **High** |
| $\text{LM} = \text{PL} + \text{SL}$ | Loss magnitude = primary + secondary losses | FAIR derivation | **High** |
| $\text{Risk Score} = I \times L$ | OCTAVE qualitative 3×3 matrix | OCTAVE Phase 4 | **High** |
| $\text{Risk Tolerance} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{ALE}_i$ | Total portfolio risk for n assets | FAIR reporting | Medium |
| $\text{Residual Risk} = \text{Inherent Risk} - \text{Control Effect}$ | Risk remaining after mitigation | Both frameworks | Medium |
| $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF}$ | Single Loss Expectancy (legacy) | Sometimes mixed in | Low |
| $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Annualized Loss Expectancy (legacy NIST view) | Legacy vs FAIR debate | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction for KTU:** Traditional risk formulas (ALE = SLE × ARO) use a *flat* loss value. FAIR's **two-dimensional loss** (Primary + Secondary) is a major upgrade because it captures reputation, regulatory, and legal fallout — which often exceed direct loss.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** OCTAVE helps hospitals decide which medical IoT devices to patch first. FAIR helps quantify the cost of a ransomware-induced patient-data breach (often $10M+ per incident).
- **Banking:** FAIR is used by **JPMorgan Chase, Citi, and Bank of America** to defend cybersecurity budgets in dollar terms to the Board.
- **Critical Infrastructure (IoT):** A water-treatment plant uses OCTAVE to identify SCADA system as critical asset, and FAIR to estimate the financial cost of a poisoned-process attack.
- **Insurance:** Cyber-insurance underwriters use FAIR-compatible models (e.g., RiskLens) to price premiums for ransomware coverage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: OCTAVE Qualitative Risk Scoring

> **Scenario:** A B.Tech college has identified *student examination database* as a critical asset (Integrity-critical). One threat scenario: *"A malicious insider modifies marks during database write."*
> - **Impact if it happens:** High (legal action, reputation damage, academic chaos) → **Impact = 3**
> - **Likelihood (past 2 years, 1 incident):** Medium → **Likelihood = 2**

**Step 1:** Apply the OCTAVE risk formula.

$$
\text{Risk Score} = \text{Impact} \times \text{Likelihood}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
\text{Risk Score} = 3 \times 2
$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$
\boxed{\text{Risk Score} = 6}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the mitigation policy.

Since **6 ≥ 6 (threshold)**, this risk **must be mitigated immediately**. Possible mitigations:
- Role-based access control (RBAC) on the database.
- Cryptographic hash verification of mark entries.
- Four-eyes approval for grade changes.
- Audit logging with daily review.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: FAIR Quantitative Risk Calculation

> **Scenario:** A server hosting customer credit-card data is analyzed using FAIR.
> - **Threat Event Frequency (TEF):** Sophisticated external attacker attempts compromise on average **2 times per year** (TEF = 2).
> - **Vulnerability (Vuln):** Probability the attack succeeds given an attempt = **25%** (Vuln = 0.25).
> - **Primary Loss (PL):** Direct costs (forensics, notification, fines) = **$200,000 per event**.
> - **Secondary Loss (SL):** Reputational damage, customer churn, lawsuits = **$800,000 per event**.

**Step 1:** Compute Loss Event Frequency (LEF).

$$
\text{LEF} = \text{TEF} \times \text{Vuln}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
\text{LEF} = 2 \times 0.25
$$

**Step 3:** Compute LEF.

$$
\boxed{\text{LEF} = 0.5 \text{ events per year}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Loss Magnitude (LM).

$$
\text{LM} = \text{PL} + \text{SL}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute values.

$$
\text{LM} = 200{,}000 + 800{,}000
$$

**Step 6:** Compute LM.

$$
\boxed{\text{LM} = \$1{,}000{,}000 \text{ per event}}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute the Annualized Loss Expectancy (Risk).

$$
\text{Risk (ALE)} = \text{LEF} \times \text{LM}
$$

**Step 8:** Substitute.

$$
\text{Risk} = 0.5 \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 9:** Final result.

$$
\boxed{\text{Risk} = \$500{,}000 \text{ per year}}
$$

**Step 10:** Interpret for the Board.

The organization faces an *expected loss of $500,000 per year* from this threat scenario. Any control costing less than $500,000/year that reduces the risk by at least 50% is a **positive ROI investment**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: OCTAVE vs. FAIR Comparison

| Dimension | OCTAVE (Allegro) | FAIR |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Qualitative + Process | Quantitative + Model |
| **Output** | Ranked risk register with mitigation plans | Dollar-denominated Annual Loss Expectancy |
| **Perspective** | Operational / Business | Financial / Risk-analytical |
| **Time to perform** | Weeks (one full cycle) | Days to weeks (per asset) |
| **Best for** | Setting up the risk program from scratch | Defending budgets to executives / insurance pricing |
| **Skill required** | Cross-functional team (IT + business) | Trained risk analyst + statistics |
| **KTU Exam frequency** | **High** (process steps asked) | **High** (FAIR formula asked) |
| **Origin** | Carnegie Mellon SEI / CERT (2007) | Jack Jones (2005), Open Group |
| **Math** | $I \times L$ matrix | $\text{LEF} \times \text{LM}$ financial model |
| **Complementarity** | Front-end: identifies what to measure | Back-end: precisely measures it |

---

### 3.4 Combined Workflow: OCTAVE → FAIR Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When answering "Compare OCTAVE and FAIR" questions, draw the *pipeline* showing how OCTAVE identifies critical assets, and FAIR quantifies the financial risk of losing them.

$$
\text{Step 1: OCTAVE Phase 2} \rightarrow \text{Identify Critical Asset (e.g., credit-card DB)}
$$
$$
\text{Step 2: OCTAVE Phase 3} \rightarrow \text{Map Threat Scenarios}
$$
$$
\text{Step 3: FAIR Modeling} \rightarrow \text{Assign TEF, Vuln, PL, SL to each scenario}
$$
$$
\text{Step 4: FAIR Calculation} \rightarrow \text{Compute ALE per scenario}
$$
$$
\text{Step 5: OCTAVE Phase 4} \rightarrow \text{Prioritize mitigations by ALE vs. cost}
$$

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D3 --> E[Phase 4: Identify and Mitigate Risks]

    E3c --> F

```

> [!NOTE]

---

### 4.2 FAIR — 10-Factor Hierarchical Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Annualized Loss Expectancy] --> LEF
    R --> LM
    LEF[Loss Event Frequency] --> TEF
    LEF --> VULN
    TEF[Threat Event Frequency] --> TS1[Threat Type]
    TEF --> CS1[Control Strength Impact]
    VULN[Vulnerability Probability] --> D1[Threat Capability]
    VULN --> D2[Resistance Strength]
    LM[Loss Magnitude] --> PL
    LM --> SL
    PL[Primary Loss] --> P1[Direct Financial Impact]
    PL --> P2[Asset Replacement Cost]
    SL[Secondary Loss] --> S1[Reputation Damage]
    SL --> S2[Regulatory Fines]
    SL --> S3[Customer Churn and Lawsuit]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading guide:** The two main branches (LEF on the left, LM on the right) multiply to give total Risk (ALE). This mirrors the formula $\text{Risk} = \text{LEF} \times \text{LM}$.

---

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end
    subgraph FAIR_BLOCK [FAIR Quantification Layer]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions**

#### **Question 1: Define OCTAVE Allegro. Mention its origin.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
OCTAVE Allegro is a streamlined risk-based information security assessment methodology developed by the **Software Engineering Institute (SEI) at Carnegie Mellon University** under the **CERT Coordination Center**, published as technical report CMU/SEI-2007-TR-012. It enables organizations to identify, prioritize, and manage information security risks from an *operational* standpoint using a self-directed, cross-functional team approach, without requiring extensive external consultant involvement.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for definition, 1 for origin/context, 0.5 for relevance)*

---

#### **Question 2: What is the core FAIR risk equation? State each term.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The core FAIR risk equation is:

$$
\boxed{\text{Risk (ALE)} = \text{LEF} \times \text{LM}}
$$

Where:
- **ALE** = Annualized Loss Expectancy (the financial risk per year, in currency).
- **LEF** = Loss Event Frequency (expected number of loss events per year).
- **LM** = Loss Magnitude (financial impact per loss event, equal to Primary Loss + Secondary Loss).

*(3 marks — 1.5 for formula, 1.5 for explaining all three terms)*

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A: OCTAVE — Comprehensive Study** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A. (a)** Explain the four phases of OCTAVE Allegro in detail. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 1 — Establish Drivers (2 marks)**
This phase builds the business context for the assessment. The team:
- Establishes the **risk measurement criteria** specific to the organization. The most common five impact areas are: (1) *Reputation and customer confidence*, (2) *Financial*, (3) *Productivity*, (4) *Fines and legal penalties*, (5) *Safety and health*. Each is given a relative weight.
- Creates an **information asset profile worksheet** to be populated in Phase 2.
- Documents **areas of concern** — broad statements of concern from senior management about what could go wrong (e.g., "loss of customer credit-card data could lead to lawsuits"). These are *not* yet detailed threat scenarios.

**Phase 2 — Profile Assets (2 marks)**
- Lists all information assets in five categories: people, information, systems, software, hardware, and intangible (IP, brand).
- Selects the **5–10 most critical assets** based on Phase 1 criteria. This is essential because deep-diving 100+ assets is impractical.
- For each critical asset, documents: *owner*, *security requirements (CIA)*, the *most important security attribute* (called the *critical attribute* — e.g., Integrity for exam database, Confidentiality for credit-card DB), and a brief description of its business purpose.

**Phase 3 — Identify Threats (1.5 marks)**
- Lists **threat sources** in five categories: deliberate human (insider, hacker), accidental human (employee mistake), system (hardware/software failure), natural (flood, earthquake), and environmental (power loss, fire).
- Constructs a **threat tree** for each critical asset: a graphical flowchart from the threat source → access point → attack vector → outcome.
- Scores each threat scenario against the Phase 1 risk criteria to create a *prioritized list* of threats.

**Phase 4 — Identify and Mitigate Risks (1.5 marks)**
- Maps each threat scenario to the **vulnerabilities** that enable it (e.g., unpatched server, weak password).
- Computes a **risk score** = Impact × Likelihood (3×3 matrix, range 1–9). Scores ≥ 6 require immediate mitigation.
- Develops a **mitigation strategy** with four options: *Mitigate* (apply a control), *Accept* (acknowledge and budget), *Defer* (act later), *Transfer* (insurance, outsource).
- Produces the final **Risk Register and Mitigation Plan** — the document delivered to the Board.

---

**A. (b)** Discuss the role of cross-functional teams in OCTAVE. Why is OCTAVE considered a "self-directed" framework? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Cross-functional team composition (3 marks):**
OCTAVE is *not* an IT-only exercise. The assessment team typically includes:
- A **sponsor** (executive, e.g., CIO or CISO) — provides authority, resources, and access.
- A **core team** of 3–5 members drawn from multiple business units: IT, Operations, Finance, Legal, HR, and the asset owners themselves.
- **Extended team members** — individuals consulted for specific assets (e.g., a database admin for the customer DB, a facility manager for the data center).

**Why cross-functional? (2 marks):**
- Many critical assets are *not* technical (e.g., proprietary research, employee trust, brand reputation). Only non-IT staff can assess these.
- Risk is a *business problem*, not just a tech problem. Mitigation costs (e.g., changing a business process) require buy-in from business owners, not just IT.
- It produces a **shared mental model** of risk across the organization, increasing compliance with mitigation plans.

**Why "self-directed" (2 marks):**
- The team drives the analysis internally — *no external consultant is required* to perform OCTAVE Allegro (unlike ISO 27005 or NIST RMF which often need external auditors).
- This reduces cost, builds *internal capacity*, and produces a risk register that reflects *true organizational reality* rather than a checklist filled in for an auditor.
- The methodology provides **worksheets and templates** (in the OCTAVE Allegro documentation) that guide the team step-by-step, making the process repeatable without external help.

---

#### **Question B: FAIR — Comprehensive Study** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**B. (a)** Explain the FAIR framework in detail. Describe its 10 core factors and the hierarchical decomposition. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**FAIR Overview (1 mark):**
FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk) is a quantitative, financially-oriented risk model developed by Jack Jones (2005) and standardized through the **Open Group**. It uniquely decomposes risk into measurable factors, allowing risk to be expressed in dollars/year (Annualized Loss Expectancy).

**The Top-Level Decomposition (1.5 marks):**
$$
\text{Risk} = \text{LEF} \times \text{LM}
$$
where LEF is computed from TEF and Vulnerability, and LM is computed from Primary and Secondary Losses.

**The 10 Factors (4 marks, ~0.4 each):**

1. **Asset** — the thing of value (data, system, person, IP).
2. **Threat** — entity that can act against the asset (insider, malware, flood).
3. **Vulnerability** — probability (0–1) that a threat event results in loss, given the event occurs.
4. **Threat Event Frequency (TEF)** — estimated number of threat events per year.
5. **Loss Event Frequency (LEF)** — estimated number of actual loss events per year = TEF × Vuln.
6. **Primary Loss (PL)** — direct, immediate financial impact per event (e.g., replacing hardware, paying a fine).
7. **Secondary Loss (SL)** — indirect, downstream impact (e.g., reputation loss, customer churn, lawsuits).
8. **Loss Magnitude (LM)** — total per-event cost = PL + SL.
9. **Control Strength (CS)** — effectiveness of an existing or proposed control (probability reduction).
10. **Risk (ALE)** — annualized loss = LEF × LM, expressed in currency per year.

**Hierarchical structure (0.5 marks):**
The factors form a tree — Risk is the root, branching into LEF and LM; LEF branches into TEF and Vulnerability; LM branches into Primary and Secondary. The hierarchy allows each high-level factor to be derived from lower-level measurable inputs.

---

**B. (b)** An e-commerce server is analyzed using FAIR. Threat Event Frequency = 3 per year, Vulnerability = 0.20, Primary Loss = ₹3,00,000, Secondary Loss = ₹7,00,000. Calculate the annual risk in INR. Also explain how this result guides the Board. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply LEF formula.

$$
\text{LEF} = \text{TEF} \times \text{Vuln}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute.

$$
\text{LEF} = 3 \times 0.20
$$

**Step 3 (0.5 mark):** Compute.

$$
\boxed{\text{LEF} = 0.6 \text{ events per year}}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply LM formula.

$$
\text{LM} = \text{PL} + \text{SL}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute.

$$
\text{LM} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6 (0.5 mark):** Compute.

$$
\boxed{\text{LM} = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000 \text{ per event}}
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Apply the FAIR risk equation.

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{LEF} \times \text{LM}
$$

**Step 8 (0.5 mark):** Final calculation.

$$
\text{ALE} = 0.6 \times 10{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ALE} = \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}}
$$

**Board-level interpretation (1.5 marks):**
- The organization faces an *expected loss of ₹6 lakhs per year* from this threat.
- Any mitigation (e.g., Web Application Firewall, WAF) costing less than ₹6 lakhs/year that reduces the LEF by at least 30% (bringing ALE down to ≤ ₹4.2 lakhs) is a **net positive ROI**.
- If insurance for the same risk costs ₹2 lakhs/year, transferring the risk becomes attractive because the residual exposure (₹4 lakhs) plus premium is still less than the inherent ₹6 lakhs.
- This **defensible financial number** is what FAIR provides — a number executives can debate and budget against, unlike vague "high-medium-low" qualitative labels.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TEF and LEF** — TEF is *attempts*, LEF is *successful losses*. Mixing them up = wrong answer, ~3 marks lost.
> 2. **Forgetting Secondary Loss** in FAIR — many students compute only PL. Secondary Loss (reputation, lawsuits) often *exceeds* PL in real incidents.
> 3. **Mixing OCTAVE phases with FAIR factors** — these are *separate frameworks*. Don't say "FAIR Phase 2" — it has no phases, only factors.
> 4. **Writing qualitative output for FAIR** — "high risk" is NOT a FAIR answer; you MUST produce a *numeric* ALE.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the originator** — SEI/Carnegie Mellon for OCTAVE; Jack Jones/Open Group for FAIR. The KTU Board awards 0.5–1 mark specifically for correct origin.
> 6. **Skipping the LEF sub-derivation** — if a problem gives TEF and Vuln, you MUST show the LEF step before computing ALE, even if the question asks for final ALE.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** 📋

- ✅ **OCTAVE** = **O**perationally **C**ritical **T**hreat, **A**sset, **V**ulnerability **E**valuation — by **SEI / Carnegie Mellon**.
- ✅ OCTAVE Allegro has **4 phases** (not 3 like the original): Establish Drivers → Profile Assets → Identify Threats → Mitigate Risks.
- ✅ OCTAVE is **qualitative**, **self-directed**, and **cross-functional**.
- ✅ Risk score in OCTAVE: $\text{Score} = I \times L$ (range 1–9; threshold 6).
- ✅ Four mitigation options: **Mitigate, Accept, Defer, Transfer** (M-A-D-T).
- ✅ **FAIR** = **F**actor **A**nalysis of **I**nformation **R**isk — by **Jack Jones** / **Open Group**.
- ✅ FAIR is **quantitative** and produces a **financial number** (ALE in currency/year).
- ✅ Core FAIR formula: $\text{ALE} = \text{LEF} \times \text{LM}$.
- ✅ Sub-derivation: $\text{LEF} = \text{TEF} \times \text{Vuln}$ and $\text{LM} = \text{PL} + \text{SL}$.
- ✅ FAIR decomposes risk into **10 factors** (Asset, Threat, Vuln, TEF, LEF, PL, SL, LM, Control Strength, Risk/ALE).
- ✅ **OCTAVE identifies, FAIR quantifies** — they are *complementary*, not competitors.
- ✅ Always show **all intermediate steps** in FAIR calculations — partial credit depends on showing LEF and LM separately.
- ✅ Always cite the **originator** (SEI or Jack Jones) — Board allocates marks for this.
- ✅ Real-world users of FAIR: **JPMorgan Chase, Citi, Bank of America**; cyber-insurance industry (RiskLens).
- ✅ Key limitation of OCTAVE: subjective scoring; key limitation of FAIR: requires trained analysts and good data.
